topics.title: Awareness on IS Codes - IS 732
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT752 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Awareness on IS Codes - IS 732

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition of IS 732

**IS 732** is the **Indian Standard Code of Practice for Electrical Wiring Installations**, formulated and published by the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)**, the national body responsible for standardization in India under the BIS Act, 2016. The code prescribes the **minimum requirements** and **good engineering practices** governing the **design, selection of materials, installation, inspection, and testing** of low-voltage (LV) electrical wiring installations in residential, commercial, and industrial premises.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Syllabus Focus**
> The course **PEEOT752 – Electrical System Design** mandates that every student in the Electrical & Electronics Engineering (EEE) stream develop **working awareness** of the **hierarchy of Indian Standards (IS)** governing electrical system design, of which **IS 732 is the foundational standard** for any wiring installation.

In the broader context of electrical standardization in India, IS 732 sits at the apex of a **family of related Indian Standards** that work in conjunction. It is harmonized closely with the international standard **IEC 60364** (International Electrotechnical Commission – Low-voltage electrical installations), making Indian practice globally compatible.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchy of Relevant Indian Standards**
> - **IS 732** – Code of Practice for Electrical Wiring Installations (the *master wiring code*).
> - **IS 3043** – Code of Practice for Earthing.
> - **IS 694** – PVC insulated cables for working voltages up to and including 1100 V.
> - **IS 1554 (Part 1)** – PVC insulated (heavy duty) electric cables.
> - **IS 9537 (Part 1 to 4)** – Conduits for electrical installations.
> - **IS 3854** – Switches for domestic and similar purposes.
> - **IE Rules 1956** – Indian Electricity Rules framed under the Electricity Act, 2003.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are building a house. You do not lay bricks randomly; you follow a *blueprint* written by structural engineers. Similarly, when an electrician wires a building, the wiring is not done on intuition or guesswork. **IS 732 is the official "blueprint book"** that tells the electrical engineer:

- *Which type of wire to use?*
- *How deep should the wiring be inside the wall?*
- *How many sockets can be on one circuit?*
- *How to protect a person from electric shock?*
- *How to test the installation before energising?*

If an engineer follows IS 732, the installation will be **safe**, **reliable**, and **legally compliant** under the **Central Electricity Authority (CEA) Regulations** and the **Electricity Act, 2003**. If IS 732 is ignored, the installation may be unsafe, may not pass electrical inspector approval, and may even void property insurance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy**
> Think of **IS 732 as the "Traffic Rules" for electricity inside a building**. Traffic rules keep vehicles (electrons) flowing in an orderly, safe manner. IS 732 does the same — it keeps electricity flowing safely, preventing "collisions" (short circuits), "overspeeding" (overcurrent), and "accidents" (electric shock, fire).

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following physical and standard metrics are referenced throughout IS 732:

- **Standard low-voltage band in India:** **250 V (single-phase)** and **415 V (three-phase)**, 50 Hz AC.
- **Standard reference ambient temperature for derating:** **45 °C** (as per Indian conditions, hotter than the IEC default of 30 °C).
- **Standard earthing resistance values (as per IS 3043, referenced by IS 732):** ≤ **1 Ω** for large substations; ≤ **2 Ω** for standard domestic/industrial installations; ≤ **5 Ω** for minor installations and equipment.
- **Touch voltage safety limit:** **50 V AC** (under dry conditions) is the conventional prospective touch voltage limit below which exposed-conductive-parts need not be connected to protective conductors (per IS 732).
- **Minimum insulation resistance:** **1 MΩ** measured with a 500 V DC megger between each conductor and earth (for a 250 V installation).
- **Conductor cross-sectional area (Cu) limits:** Lighting circuit minimum **1.5 mm²**; Power circuit minimum **2.5 mm²** (per IS 732 / IS 4648).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these numbers matter**
> Every numerical value in IS 732 is the result of decades of empirical field data, fire-incident investigations, and laboratory testing. Memorising the **boundary values** is essential for KTU exams and for on-site decision-making.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Scope of IS 732

The standard is applicable to **electrical installations in buildings** and covers the following areas in a structured way:

- **Selection of wiring methods** (conduit, trunking, cable tray, etc.).
- **Selection of conductors and cables** (Cu / Al, PVC / XLPE insulation, armoured / unarmoured).
- **Protection against electric shock** (basic and fault protection).
- **Protection against thermal effects** (overload, short circuit, fire).
- **Protection against overcurrent** (fuses, MCBs, MCCBs).
- **Earthing arrangements and equipotential bonding.**
- **Isolation, switching, and control.**
- **Inspection and testing** of completed installations.

## 2.2 General Requirements (Section-by-Section Logical Walkthrough)

The reasoning behind each major requirement of IS 732 is broken down as follows:

### 2.2.1 Safety-First Design Philosophy
- The fundamental principle is that **safety to life is paramount**, followed by **safety to property**.
- Every wiring choice must satisfy: (i) protection against **electric shock**, (ii) protection against **fire**, (iii) **reliable operation** of the connected load, and (iv) **ease of maintenance**.

### 2.2.2 Classification of Voltage Bands
- **Extra-Low Voltage (ELV):** ≤ 50 V AC or ≤ 120 V DC — used for safety-critical circuits (e.g., electronic bell, water-level controller).
- **Low Voltage (LV):** 50 V to 1000 V AC — the normal domestic / industrial range.
- **High Voltage (HV):** > 1000 V AC — covered by separate standards (IS 7098 for cables, etc.).
- IS 732 explicitly addresses only the **LV band**.

### 2.2.3 Types of Wiring Systems Recognised by IS 732
- **Concealed conduit wiring** (PVC / rigid steel conduit embedded in walls and ceilings) — the *most common* in modern residential and commercial buildings.
- **Surface conduit wiring** — used in industrial sheds, basements, and where chasing walls is not feasible.
- **PVC-insulated and sheathed (flexible) wiring** — for temporary installations and small residential buildings.
- **Cable tray / ladder wiring** — for heavy industrial feeders.
- **Trench / duct wiring** — for under-floor or road-crossing cable runs.

### 2.2.4 Protection Against Electric Shock (Two-Pronged Strategy)
- **Basic Protection (Protection against direct contact):**
  Insulation, enclosures, barriers, and out-of-reach placement of live parts.
- **Fault Protection (Protection against indirect contact):**
  Earthing, automatic disconnection (via MCB / ELCB / RCCB), supplementary equipotential bonding.

### 2.2.5 Protection Against Overcurrent
- The conductor must be protected by an **Overcurrent Protective Device (OCPD)** — typically an **MCB (Miniature Circuit Breaker)**.
- The rating of the OCPD ($I_n$) must satisfy: $I_n \leq I_z$ (the current-carrying capacity of the cable after derating).

### 2.2.6 Earthing — The Heart of IS 732
- IS 732 mandates **three distinct earth electrodes** for typical installations:
  - **Body Earth** (for the metal body of equipment).
  - **Neutral Earth** (for the star-point neutral of the transformer / generator).
  - **Equipment Earth** (for sensitive electronic equipment, separate low-impedance earth).
- The earth conductor sizing is governed by **IS 3043**, which IS 732 cross-references.

### 2.2.7 Inspection and Testing
- **Pre-energising checks:** continuity, insulation resistance, earth resistance, polarity.
- **Periodic re-testing** recommended every **5 years** for domestic and **2 years** for industrial installations.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The table below is the most-tested content from Module 1 of PEEOT752.** KTU exam questions frequently derive from these exact relationships.

| **S.No.** | **Quantity / Concept** | **Formula / Relation** | **Unit** | **Significance in IS 732** |
|:---------:|:-----------------------|:----------------------|:--------:|:---------------------------|
| 1 | Voltage drop (single-phase) | $\Delta V = \dfrac{2 \cdot I \cdot L \cdot \rho}{A}$ | Volts (V) | Limits the run length of a feeder |
| 2 | Voltage drop (three-phase) | $\Delta V = \dfrac{\sqrt{3} \cdot I \cdot L \cdot \rho}{A}$ | Volts (V) | For 415 V industrial feeders |
| 3 | Permissible voltage drop (IS 732) | $\Delta V \leq 3\% \text{ of declared voltage}$ | V (or %) | Final-circuit lighting and socket-outlet limit |
| 4 | Permissible voltage drop (total) | $\Delta V_{total} \leq 5\% \text{ of declared voltage}$ | V (or %) | From transformer secondary to farthest load |
| 5 | Derated current capacity | $I_z = I_{z0} \cdot k_1 \cdot k_2 \cdot k_3$ | Ampere (A) | $k_1$ = temp, $k_2$ = grouping, $k_3$ = soil/duct |
| 6 | Conductor cross-section from current | $A = \dfrac{\rho \cdot L \cdot I}{\Delta V / 2}$ (1-$\phi$) | mm² | Sizing phase conductor |
| 7 | Earth resistance (rod electrode) | $R = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[\ln\left(\dfrac{4L}{d}\right) - 1\right]$ | Ohm ($\Omega$) | Sizing a single earth rod |
| 8 | Touch voltage limit | $V_T \leq 50 \text{ V (dry, AC)}$ | V | Safety threshold for indirect contact |
| 9 | Insulation resistance minimum | $R_{ins} \geq 1 \text{ M}\Omega$ at 500 V DC | M$\Omega$ | Mandatory pre-energising test |
| 10 | OCPD coordination | $I_n \leq I_z$ and $I_2 \leq 1.45 \, I_z$ | A | MCB rating vs. cable ampacity |
| 11 | Short-circuit current (impedance method) | $I_{sc} = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{R^2 + X^2}}$ | A | For protective device discrimination |
| 12 | Loop impedance for disconnection | $Z_s = Z_e + (R_1 + R_2)$ | $\Omega$ | Used to verify auto-disconnection time |

> **Notation used in the table:** $I$ = load current, $L$ = length, $\rho$ = resistivity, $A$ = cross-sectional area, $I_n$ = nominal protective device current, $I_z$ = effective current-carrying capacity of cable, $I_2$ = conventional tripping current of MCB, $Z_s$ = earth fault loop impedance, $Z_e$ = external earth-fault loop impedance, $R_1, R_2$ = phase and CPC resistance.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

In the professional practice of a **licensed electrical contractor or a BEE-certified Energy Manager**, IS 732 is consulted daily when:

- Designing the **single-line diagram (SLD)** of a building.
- Computing the **short-circuit level** at each busbar to select the **breaking capacity (in kA)** of MCBs.
- Preparing the **Earthing Layout Drawing**.
- Conducting the **Insulation Resistance Test** before issuing the *Electrical Completion Certificate* to the utility.
- Undertaking an **Energy Audit** for a large commercial building, where IS 732 dictates the **power factor correction** and **harmonics management** rules.

For KTU 2024-scheme students, IS 732 awareness is the **gateway to the design-related elective** and to professional practice in the **Central Public Works Department (CPWD)**, **PWD (Kerala)**, **KSEB**, and **Kerala State Electricity Board-approved contractor** roles.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Voltage Drop Formula (Single-Phase)

The voltage drop across a cable is the single most commonly tested calculation in KTU module examinations. Let us derive it rigorously.

### 3.1.1 Setup and Assumptions
Consider a single-phase AC circuit supplying a load at the far end of a 2-core cable of total length $L$ (i.e., the *go* and *return* conductor together). Let the load current be $I$ amperes, the conductor cross-section be $A$ mm², and the material resistivity be $\rho$ $\Omega \cdot$mm²/m.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Resistance of the two-core loop.**
The resistance of one conductor of length $L$ metres is:

$$
R_{\text{one}} = \frac{\rho \cdot L}{A}
$$

For a single-phase two-core cable, the current must travel through *both* conductors (phase + neutral), so the total loop resistance is **twice** the single-conductor resistance:

$$
R_{\text{loop}} = 2 \cdot R_{\text{one}} = \frac{2 \rho L}{A}
$$

**Step 2 — Voltage drop (Ohm's Law).**
The current $I$ flowing through the loop resistance produces a voltage drop $\Delta V$:

$$
\Delta V = I \cdot R_{\text{loop}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substituting.**
Insert the expression for $R_{\text{loop}}$ into Ohm's Law:

$$
\Delta V = I \cdot \frac{2 \rho L}{A}
$$

**Step 4 — Final form (single-phase).**

$$
\boxed{\Delta V_{1\phi} = \frac{2 I L \rho}{A}}
$$

**Conversion logic:** The factor of $2$ comes from the *out-and-back* current path; the linear term $L$ reflects that longer cables accumulate more resistance; the inverse dependence on $A$ reflects that thicker cables have lower resistance.

### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Derivation (Three-Phase)

For a balanced 3-phase load, the current returns through the *line-to-line* voltage difference. The relevant loop length is $L$ (one-way only) and the voltage is the line-to-line value $V_L = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{ph}$.

**Step 1 — Resistance of one phase conductor.**

$$
R_{\text{phase}} = \frac{\rho L}{A}
$$

**Step 2 — Voltage drop in one phase.**

$$
\Delta V_{\text{phase}} = I \cdot R_{\text{phase}} = \frac{I \rho L}{A}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to line-to-line drop using the $\sqrt{3}$ factor.**

$$
\Delta V_{LL} = \sqrt{3} \cdot \Delta V_{\text{phase}}
$$

**Step 4 — Final form (three-phase).**

$$
\boxed{\Delta V_{3\phi} = \frac{\sqrt{3} \cdot I L \rho}{A}}
$$

**Conversion logic:** The factor $\sqrt{3}$ arises because the line-to-line voltage is $\sqrt{3}$ times the phase-to-neutral voltage in a balanced star or delta system, and IS 732 specifies the *line-to-line* drop as the design quantity.

## 3.2 Derivation of OCPD Coordination (The $1.45$ Rule)

### 3.2.1 The Underlying Problem
A cable's "current-carrying capacity" $I_z$ is defined as the maximum continuous current it can carry without exceeding its rated conductor temperature. The **MCB** placed upstream has a *nominal rating* $I_n$ and a *conventional non-tripping current* $I_1$ (typically $1.13 \, I_n$) and a *conventional tripping current* $I_2$ (typically $1.45 \, I_n$).

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Condition 1: MCB rating must not exceed cable capacity.**

$$
I_n \leq I_z
$$

**Step 2 — Condition 2: For a small overload, the cable must trip before overheating.**
The cable's *final operating current* is $1.45 \, I_z$ (per IS 732 Table 8, Clause 7.4.1.2). The MCB must trip at a current *no greater than* this final operating current:

$$
I_2 \leq 1.45 \, I_z
$$

**Step 3 — Combine both conditions.**

$$
\boxed{I_n \leq I_z \quad \text{AND} \quad I_2 \leq 1.45 \, I_z}
$$

**Conversion logic:** $1.13$ is the *non-tripping threshold* (the MCB will not trip below this current for 1 hour); $1.45$ is the *tripping threshold* (the MCB *must* trip at this current within 1 hour). The cable's adiabatic heating limit is anchored to the value $1.45 \, I_z$. Therefore, designing the MCB at $I_2 \leq 1.45 \, I_z$ guarantees that the cable *cannot* exceed its temperature limit.

## 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Typical Pattern)

**Problem:** A single-phase 230 V, 50 Hz feeder supplies a load of **10 A** at a distance of **50 m** using a **4 mm² copper conductor** ($\rho = 0.0172 \, \Omega \cdot \text{mm}^2/\text{m}$). Verify the voltage drop and check compliance with IS 732.

### Step 1 — Compute voltage drop.

$$
\Delta V = \frac{2 I L \rho}{A}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
\Delta V = \frac{2 \cdot 10 \cdot 50 \cdot 0.0172}{4}
$$

$$
\Delta V = \frac{17.2}{4}
$$

$$
\Delta V = 4.3 \text{ V}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute percentage drop.

$$
\% \Delta V = \frac{\Delta V}{V} \times 100 = \frac{4.3}{230} \times 100
$$

$$
\% \Delta V = 1.87\%
$$

### Step 3 — Compare with IS 732 limit.
IS 732 allows a maximum of **3% drop** for the final sub-circuit. Since $1.87\% < 3\%$, the feeder **complies** with IS 732.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** Always quote the IS 732 clause number (Clause **5.6 / Table 7** for voltage drop limits) in your answer to score the full 14 marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Hierarchy of Indian Standards for Electrical Installations

The following **Mermaid block diagram** illustrates the hierarchy and inter-relationships between IS 732 and the other supporting Indian Standards that govern a typical LV electrical installation. This is a frequent 7-mark question in KTU module tests.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[BIS Bureau of Indian Standards] --> B[IS 732 Master Wiring Code]
    B --> C[IS 3043 Earthing]
    B --> D[IS 694 PVC Cables]
    B --> E[IS 1554 Heavy Duty Cables]
    B --> F[IS 9537 Conduit Systems]
    B --> G[IS 3854 Switches]
    B --> H[IS 12640 RCCB Residual Current]
    A --> I[CEA Regulations 2010]
    A --> J[IE Rules 1956]
    A --> K[National Building Code NBC 2016]
    I --> B
    J --> B
    K --> B
    C --> L[Installation Design]
    D --> L
    E --> L
    F --> L
    G --> L
    H --> L
    L --> M[Safe Electrical Installation]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of an IS 732-Compliant Wiring Installation

The diagram below maps the **functional architecture flow** of a single domestic electrical installation designed as per IS 732. It shows the *logical sequence* of protection, switching, and earthing components.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Utility Service Line 11kV] --> T0[Distribution Transformer 11kV to 415V]
    T0 --> M1[Main Energy Meter KSEB]
    M1 --> SP1[Main MCB 63A 3P]
    SP1 --> E1[Main Earth Electrode IS 3043]
    SP1 --> ELCB1[ELCB RCCB 63A 30mA]
    ELCB1 --> DB1[Distribution Board]
    DB1 --> M2[MCB 16A Lighting Circuit]
    DB1 --> M3[MCB 20A Socket Circuit]
    DB1 --> M4[MCB 25A AC Geyser]
    M2 --> L1[Light Loads Phase L1]
    M3 --> S1[Socket Outlets Phase L1]
    M4 --> G1[Geyser Load Phase L1]
    DB1 --> S2[Switchgear and Isolator]
    S2 --> MR1[Motor Load if any]
    E1 --> EBD[Equipotential Bonding of all metal parts]
    EBD --> S1
    EBD --> G1
    EBD --> MR1
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **Main MCB** (SP1) provides short-circuit and overload protection for the entire building.
- The **ELCB / RCCB** (ELCB1) is the *life-saving device* that detects leakage currents of 30 mA and disconnects within 40 ms (per IS 732).
- Each sub-circuit has its own **MCB** (M2, M3, M4) sized per the load it serves.
- The **Main Earth Electrode** (E1) is bonded to all exposed metal parts of the installation to create a *common equipotential zone*.

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart for OCPD Selection (As Per IS 732)

This flowchart captures the *decision logic* a designer follows when selecting an MCB for a given sub-circuit.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Identify Load in Ampere I] --> B[Determine Cable Current Capacity Iz0 from IS 3961]
    B --> C{Determine Derating Factors k1 k2 k3}
    C --> D[Compute Effective Capacity Iz equals Iz0 times k1 k2 k3]
    D --> E[Select MCB Nominal In]
    E --> F{Condition In less than or equal to Iz}
    F -- No --> G[Choose larger cable and restart from B]
    F -- Yes --> H{Condition I2 less than or equal to 1.45 Iz}
    H -- No --> I[Choose larger cable and restart from B]
    H -- Yes --> J[MCB selection finalised]
    J --> K[End]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 marks)**
**Q: Define IS 732. State any two of its scope items.**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**
IS 732 is the Indian Standard Code of Practice for Electrical Wiring Installations, published by the Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS).

*Scope (any two):*
- (i) Design and selection of materials for low-voltage electrical wiring installations in residential, commercial, and industrial premises.
- (ii) Protection against electric shock, fire hazards, and overcurrent.
- (iii) Inspection and testing of completed installations.
- (iv) Selection of wiring systems such as conduit, trunking, and cable tray.

---

### **Question 2 (3 marks)**
**Q: What is the touch voltage limit prescribed by IS 732, and what device provides protection against indirect contact?**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**
The touch voltage limit prescribed by IS 732 is **50 V AC** under dry conditions. This is the maximum prospective touch voltage below which exposed conductive parts need not be connected to protective conductors.

The protective device that provides automatic disconnection against *indirect contact* is the **RCCB (Residual Current Circuit Breaker)** / **ELCB**, typically rated at **30 mA** sensitivity, which disconnects the supply within **40 ms**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Part-B questions in PEEOT752 carry 14 marks, are module-internal-choice based, and typically have sub-parts (a) of 7 marks and (b) of 7 marks. Two completely independent questions (Question A and Question B) are provided below; the student answers ONE.

---

### **Question A (14 marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the scope of IS 732. List the general requirements that govern an electrical wiring installation as per IS 732.

**(b) [7 marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a typical IS 732-compliant residential wiring installation, identifying the roles of the main MCB, ELCB, sub-circuit MCBs, and the earth electrode.

#### **Model Solution to (a):**

**Definition and Scope of IS 732:**
IS 732 is the Indian Standard Code of Practice for Electrical Wiring Installations. It is published by the Bureau of Indian Standards and is harmonized with the international standard IEC 60364. The scope of IS 732 includes:

1. *General Principles:* The code lays down the general principles governing the design, erection, and verification (inspection and testing) of LV electrical installations.
2. *Selection of Wiring Methods:* Recognised methods include PVC-insulated sheathed wiring, conduit wiring (concealed or surface), and cable-tray / ladder wiring.
3. *Selection of Cables and Conductors:* Specifies current-carrying capacities, voltage drops, and derating factors based on ambient temperature and grouping.
4. *Protection Against Electric Shock:* Specifies basic protection (insulation, enclosures) and fault protection (earthing, automatic disconnection).
5. *Protection Against Fire and Overcurrent:* Specifies the use of MCBs, fuses, and coordination rules.
6. *Earthing Arrangements:* References IS 3043 for earth electrode sizing, equipotential bonding, and earth resistance limits.
7. *Inspection and Testing:* Prescribes pre-energising tests such as insulation resistance (≥ 1 MΩ), earth continuity, and polarity.

**General Requirements (any five with explanation):**

- *Safety-First Design:* All designs must prioritise life safety, followed by property protection.
- *Voltage Classification:* Installations must be classified as ELV (≤ 50 V AC), LV (50–1000 V AC), or HV (> 1000 V AC).
- *Conductor Sizing:* Cross-sectional area must be chosen based on current rating, voltage drop, and short-circuit withstand.
- *OCPD Selection:* Overcurrent protective devices must be coordinated with cable ampacity as per the $I_n \leq I_z$ rule.
- *Earthing:* All exposed-conductive-parts must be bonded to a low-impedance earth.
- *Accessibility:* All switchgear must be easily accessible for operation and maintenance.
- *Documentation:* Single-line diagrams, layout drawings, and test certificates must be maintained.

#### **Model Solution to (b):**

**Functional Architecture (with reference to the diagram in Section 4.2):**

- **Service Line → Distribution Transformer (11 kV to 415 V):** The utility-supplied 11 kV is stepped down by the distribution transformer to 415 V three-phase, 50 Hz.
- **Main Energy Meter:** The KSEB / utility meter records total energy consumption.
- **Main MCB (3-pole, 63 A typical):** Provides short-circuit and overload protection for the entire installation; isolating the building during faults.
- **Main Earth Electrode:** As per IS 3043, an earth rod of GI pipe (or copper-bonded rod) is driven into the ground; soil resistivity decides the depth.
- **ELCB / RCCB (63 A, 30 mA):** This is the *life-saving device*; it detects any earth-leakage current exceeding 30 mA and disconnects the supply in < 40 ms, protecting humans from electric shock.
- **Distribution Board (DB):** Splits the main feed into multiple sub-circuits, each protected by its own MCB.
- **Sub-Circuit MCBs:** A 16 A MCB for the lighting circuit, 20 A for the 5 A socket circuit, 25 A for the geyser or AC, etc. Each MCB is sized to match the connected load and the cable size.
- **Equipotential Bonding:** All metal parts of the building (water pipes, gas pipes, air-conditioner outdoor units) are bonded together to ensure no potential difference exists between them, eliminating shock hazard.

**Block Diagram (as in Section 4.2 of the notes):**
[The student is expected to redraw the Mermaid diagram in graphical form for full marks.]

---

### **Question B (14 marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the voltage drop formula for a single-phase 2-wire AC feeder. A 230 V, single-phase feeder of length 60 m supplies a load of 8 A. The cable is 2.5 mm² copper. Find the percentage voltage drop and verify IS 732 compliance. (Resistivity of copper = 0.0172 $\Omega \cdot$mm²/m).

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the OCPD–cable coordination rule ($I_n \leq I_z$, $I_2 \leq 1.45 I_z$) with the significance of the 1.45 factor. A 6 mm² copper cable has a current-carrying capacity of 32 A in the installed conditions. Select the appropriate MCB rating.

#### **Model Solution to (a):**

**Derivation:**
The resistance of one conductor of length $L$ and cross-section $A$ is:

$$
R = \frac{\rho L}{A}
$$

In a single-phase, 2-wire system, current returns through the neutral; hence the loop resistance is:

$$
R_{\text{loop}} = \frac{2 \rho L}{A}
$$

By Ohm's Law, voltage drop is:

$$
\Delta V = I \cdot R_{\text{loop}} = \frac{2 I L \rho}{A}
$$

**Numerical Solution:**
Given: $I = 8$ A, $L = 60$ m, $A = 2.5$ mm², $\rho = 0.0172 \, \Omega \cdot$mm²/m, $V = 230$ V.

$$
\Delta V = \frac{2 \times 8 \times 60 \times 0.0172}{2.5}
$$

$$
\Delta V = \frac{16.512}{2.5}
$$

$$
\Delta V = 6.6048 \text{ V}
$$

Percentage drop:

$$
\% \Delta V = \frac{6.6048}{230} \times 100 = 2.87\%
$$

**IS 732 Compliance:** The permissible limit is **3%** for final sub-circuits. Since $2.87\% < 3\%$, the feeder **complies** with IS 732.

#### **Model Solution to (b):**

**The OCPD Coordination Rule:**
To ensure that a cable is fully protected against overload and short-circuit:

- **Condition 1:** $I_n \leq I_z$ — The MCB nominal rating must not exceed the effective current-carrying capacity of the cable (after derating).
- **Condition 2:** $I_2 \leq 1.45 \, I_z$ — The conventional tripping current of the MCB must not exceed 1.45 times the cable's effective capacity.

**Significance of 1.45:**
- The MCB has two thresholds: $1.13 \, I_n$ (non-tripping) and $1.45 \, I_n$ (tripping) over 1 hour.
- The cable's *final* operating limit (the current at which it reaches its temperature limit in 1 hour) is $1.45 \, I_z$.
- Hence, designing the MCB such that $I_2 \leq 1.45 \, I_z$ guarantees that the cable *never* exceeds its temperature rating.

**Numerical Solution:**
Given: $I_z = 32$ A. We need to find $I_n$ (standard MCB rating) such that $I_n \leq 32$ A and $I_2 = 1.45 \, I_n \leq 1.45 \times 32 = 46.4$ A.

**Standard MCB ratings (IS 8828):** 6 A, 10 A, 16 A, 20 A, 25 A, 32 A, 40 A, 50 A, 63 A.

Choosing $I_n = 32$ A:
- Condition 1: $32 \leq 32$ ✓
- Condition 2: $1.45 \times 32 = 46.4 \leq 46.4$ ✓

**Answer:** The appropriate MCB rating is **32 A**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor 2 in single-phase drop.** Many students write $\Delta V = \frac{IL\rho}{A}$ and lose 2 of 7 marks.
> 2. **Confusing line-to-line and line-to-neutral drop.** A three-phase drop is $\sqrt{3} \cdot IL\rho / A$, not $3 \cdot IL\rho / A$.
> 3. **Quoting IS 732 clause numbers incorrectly.** Always say "Clause 5.6 / Table 7 of IS 732" for voltage drop limits.
> 4. **Forgetting to include derating factors.** If the question specifies ambient temperature or grouping, multiply by $k_1, k_2$ to get the *effective* $I_z$, otherwise the MCB coordination is wrong.
> 5. **Not sketching the block diagram.** A question that asks "with a neat diagram" without one gets only 3–4 marks out of 7, even if the written explanation is perfect.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Module 1 – IS 732 Awareness)**

- [x] **IS 732** = Code of Practice for **Electrical Wiring Installations**, published by **BIS**, harmonized with **IEC 60364**.
- [x] Standard LV band in India = **230 V single-phase, 415 V three-phase, 50 Hz**.
- [x] IS 732 covers **scope, selection, protection, earthing, inspection, and testing**.
- [x] Related standards: **IS 3043 (Earthing), IS 694 (PVC Cables), IS 1554 (HD Cables), IS 9537 (Conduits), IS 8828 (MCBs)**.
- [x] **Touch voltage limit:** **50 V AC** (dry); **30 mA RCCB** disconnects in < **40 ms** for life protection.
- [x] **Voltage drop limit:** **3%** for final sub-circuit; **5%** total from transformer to farthest load.
- [x] **Single-phase drop formula:** $\Delta V = \dfrac{2 I L \rho}{A}$.
- [x] **Three-phase drop formula:** $\Delta V = \dfrac{\sqrt{3} I L \rho}{A}$.
- [x] **OCPD coordination rule:** $I_n \leq I_z$ and $I_2 \leq 1.45 \, I_z$.
- [x] **Insulation resistance minimum:** $\geq$ **1 MΩ** at **500 V DC** (megger test).
- [x] **Earth resistance limit:** $\leq$ **1–5 Ω** depending on installation type (refer to IS 3043).
- [x] **Conductor cross-section minima:** Lighting = **1.5 mm² Cu**; Power sockets = **2.5 mm² Cu**.
- [x] **Three types of earth:** Body Earth, Neutral Earth, Equipment Earth.
- [x] **Conduit wiring** is the *most common* method recognised by IS 732 in modern buildings.
- [x] Always cite the **IS 732 clause / table number** in KTU answers to fetch full marks.
- [x] IS 732 is the **legal basis** for issuing the *Electrical Completion Certificate* under the **Electricity Act, 2003** and **CEA Safety Regulations 2010**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IS 3043
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT752 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** IS 3043

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# IS 3043 — Code of Practice for Earthing

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**IS 3043:1987** is the Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) *Code of Practice for Earthing*, prescribed by the **APJ Abdul Kalam Technological University (KTU)** under the **2024 Scheme** for the design, installation, and maintenance of **protective earthing systems** in electrical installations, substations, generating stations, and industrial networks.

The standard formally defines the following critical entities:

| Term | Formal Definition (as per IS 3043) |
|---|---|
| **Earth** | The conductive mass of the earth, whose electric potential at any point is conventionally taken as **zero (0 V)**. |
| **Earth Electrode** | A conductor or group of conductors in intimate contact with the earth, providing an electrical connection to it. |
| **Earthing (Grounding)** | The process of making a deliberate electrical connection to the general mass of earth, primarily for **safety of personnel and equipment**. |
| **Earth Resistance** | The ohmic resistance between the earth electrode and the remote earth (a point where soil potential gradient becomes negligibly small), measured in **ohms (Ω)**. |
| **Soil Resistivity (ρ)** | The electrical resistance of a unit cube of soil, expressed in **ohm-metres (Ω·m)**. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** IS 3043 is treated as the **primary governing standard** for Module 1 of *Electrical System Design*. Questions frequently appear in Part A (3 marks) on definitions, and in Part B (14 marks) on electrode resistance calculations and touch/step potential limits.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are holding a charged capacitor (say, a high-voltage appliance's metal chassis that has developed a fault voltage). Your body is now at, say, **230 V** with respect to true earth. If you touch a water tap (which is connected to the earth via metallic plumbing), a lethal current will flow through you to reach the earth.

Now, suppose a thick copper wire — the **earth electrode** — has been driven deep into the moist soil of your garden, and the appliance's faulty chassis is connected to it. The fault current will flow through this wire (which offers a much **lower resistance path** than your body, typically **< 1 Ω** vs. your body's ~1000 Ω) and safely dissipate into the vast mass of earth. The protective fuse/MCB will then trip instantly, isolating the circuit.

> **In simple words:** Earthing is like giving electricity a **"fast emergency exit"** so it does not have to escape *through* you. The earth acts as a **giant, zero-resistance reservoir** that can absorb any amount of electrical charge.

### Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Maximum permissible earth electrode resistance** (for large substations): **≤ 0.5 Ω**
- **Maximum permissible earth electrode resistance** (for small installations/equipment): **≤ 5 Ω to 10 Ω**
- **Permissible body current** (IEC 479 / IS 3043): **≤ 10 mA** for indefinite exposure
- **Standard soil resistivity (typical clay/loam):** **10 Ω·m to 100 Ω·m**; rocky soil may exceed **1000 Ω·m**.
- **Touch potential limit** (substations, typical): **≤ 430 V** for fault clearance time of **0.5 s**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Zero Potential Reference:** By universal convention, the surface of the earth is taken as the **absolute zero electrical potential reference**. Every voltage in an electrical system is measured *with respect to earth* unless explicitly stated otherwise.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Purpose & Scope of Earthing (Why IS 3043 Exists)

IS 3043 codifies earthing practice to achieve the following engineering objectives, in the order of KTU priority:

1. **Personnel Safety** — To limit **touch potential** and **step potential** to safe human-body tolerance levels during earth faults.
2. **Equipment Protection** — To provide a low-impedance fault-current return path so that overcurrent protective devices (MCBs, MCCBs, relays) operate discriminatively and quickly.
3. **Overvoltage / Surge Protection** — To safely discharge lightning surges, switching transients, and static electricity to the earth mass.
4. **System Voltage Stabilization** — To provide a stable neutral reference and reduce electromagnetic interference (EMI).
5. **Equipotential Bonding** — To ensure all exposed conductive parts remain at the **same potential**, eliminating shock hazards.

## 2.2 Classification of Earthing (IS 3043 Framework)

IS 3043 recognizes two principal *functional* categories of earthing, each with a distinct design intent:

- **System Earthing (Functional Earthing):** Connecting the *neutral point* of a power system (star-connected generator/transformer winding) to earth to stabilize line-to-earth voltages and provide a reference.
- **Equipment Earthing (Protective Earthing):** Connecting the *non-current-carrying metallic parts* of equipment (chassis, enclosures, frames, cable armour) to earth, so that dangerous touch voltages do not persist during insulation failure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Many students confuse *system earthing* with *equipment earthing*. The mnemonic is: **"Neutral = System; Frame = Equipment."**

## 2.3 Types of Earth Electrodes (Per IS 3043, Clause 7)

The standard specifies the following permissible electrode types, each suited to specific soil/site conditions:

- **Pipe Electrode** — A galvanized iron (GI) or copper pipe, typically **40 mm to 50 mm diameter**, buried vertically with its top at least **2.75 m** to **3.0 m** below ground level. *Most common in Indian practice.*
- **Plate Electrode** — A copper or GI plate (size typically **60 cm × 60 cm** for GI, **60 cm × 60 cm × 3.15 mm** for copper) buried vertically at a depth of **≥ 1.5 m** below ground.
- **Rod Electrode** — Solid copper or copper-clad steel rod, **12.5 mm to 25 mm** diameter, driven vertically; suitable for rocky terrain where excavation is impractical.
- **Strip Electrode** — A copper or GI strip **25 mm × 1.6 mm** (copper) or **25 mm × 6 mm** (GI) buried in horizontal trenches, typically used for substations and for equipotential bonding.
- **Mat (Grid) Electrode** — A network of horizontal buried conductors forming a mesh, installed in large substations to control step and touch potentials.

## 2.4 Soil Resistivity — The Master Variable

The single most important factor in earth electrode design is the **soil resistivity (ρ)**, since it directly governs electrode resistance. IS 3043 lists approximate values:

| Soil Type | Typical Resistivity (Ω·m) |
|---|---|
| Wet organic soil / marshy ground | **5 – 10** |
| Loam, clay, damp humus | **10 – 100** |
| Sand and gravel (moist) | **50 – 500** |
| Sand and gravel (dry) | **1000 – 10000** |
| Bedrock (granite, limestone) | **> 10000** |
| Sea water | **~ 0.2** |

**Factors influencing ρ** (frequently asked in KTU 3-mark questions):
- **Moisture content** (ρ decreases exponentially with moisture).
- **Temperature** (ρ rises sharply below 0 °C as water freezes).
- **Salt/mineral content** (dissolved salts drastically reduce ρ).
- **Grain size and compaction** of soil.

> [!NOTE]
> **Soil Treatment (Clause 11 of IS 3043):** When natural ρ is too high, the soil immediately surrounding the electrode is treated with **charcoal + salt (NaCl)** layers to keep moisture and lower resistivity. Typical treatment mass: **50–75 kg of charcoal + 5–10 kg of salt per electrode**, renewed every 1–2 years.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

The following table consolidates all exam-critical formulas from IS 3043. **Memorize this table — it solves 80% of the Part B numerical questions.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables & Units | Validity / Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Resistance of **pipe / rod electrode** | $$R = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[ \ln\left(\frac{4L}{d}\right) - 1 \right]$$ | ρ = soil resistivity (Ω·m), L = buried length (m), d = electrode diameter (m) | Approximate; valid when L >> d |
| 2 | Resistance of **plate electrode** | $$R = \frac{\rho}{8S} + \frac{\rho}{4\pi d}$$ | S = perimeter of plate ≈ 2(a+b) for a×b plate (m), d = depth of burial (m) | First term dominates; second term is the depth correction |
| 3 | Resistance of **strip / horizontal conductor** | $$R = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[ \ln\left(\frac{2L}{w}\right) + \ln\left(\frac{L}{h}\right) \right]$$ | L = strip length (m), w = strip width (m), h = burial depth (m) | For long horizontal runs; w << L |
| 4 | Resistance of **grid (mat) electrode** (Sverak's simplified formula) | $$R_g = \rho \left[ \frac{1}{4r} + \frac{1}{L_T} \right]$$ | r = radius of equivalent circular area of grid (m), L_T = total buried conductor length (m) | Used in substation design |
| 5 | **Effective** resistance of **n identical electrodes in parallel** | $$R_n = \frac{R_1}{n} \cdot \eta$$ | R_1 = resistance of single electrode, n = number, η = utilization factor (0 < η < 1) | η depends on spacing; spacing ≥ 2 × electrode length gives η ≈ 0.9 |
| 6 | **Soil resistivity** (Wenner 4-pin method) | $$\rho = 2\pi a R$$ | a = spacing between adjacent electrodes (m), R = measured resistance (Ω) | Most common field test; uses 4 equally-spaced probes in a straight line |
| 7 | **Schlumberger method** soil resistivity | $$\rho = \pi \cdot \frac{(b^2 - a^2)}{2a} \cdot R$$ | a, b = electrode spacings (m) | Variant of Wenner; inner electrodes measure potential |
| 8 | **Touch potential (E_touch)** | $$E_{touch} = I_g \cdot R_t$$ | I_g = ground-fault current (A), R_t = resistance of human body + contact path (Ω) | Conservative limit per IS 3043: **E_touch ≤ (1000 + 1.5 C_s) Ω · I_g** with C_s = surface layer resistivity factor |
| 9 | **Step potential (E_step)** | $$E_{step} = I_g \cdot R_{step}$$ | R_step = impedance between two foot contacts | Limit: **E_step ≤ (1000 + 6 C_s) Ω · I_g** |
| 10 | **Maximum permissible electrode potential rise (GPR)** | $$GPR = I_g \cdot R_e$$ | R_e = total earth-electrode resistance (Ω) | GPR = I_g × R_e; must satisfy touch and step criteria |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipe vs Plate:** In KTU numericals, if the problem does not specify the electrode type, the **pipe electrode formula (R = ρ/2πL · [ln(4L/d) − 1])** is the default, since it is the most prevalent in India.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Resistance of a Pipe (Vertical Rod) Earth Electrode

This is a **favourite 7-mark KTU question**. The derivation proceeds from the classical expression for the resistance of a hemisphere electrode and extends it to a finite-length rod.

### Step 1: Starting Point — Resistance of a Point Source (Hemisphere) in Infinite Soil

Imagine a single hemispherical electrode of radius **r** buried with its flat face flush with the earth's surface. Current **I** flows radially outward into an infinite uniform soil of resistivity **ρ**.

The current density at radius **x** is:

$$J(x) = \frac{I}{2\pi x^2}$$

The electric field at radius **x** is, by Ohm's law in the soil:

$$E(x) = \rho \cdot J(x) = \frac{\rho \cdot I}{2\pi x^2}$$

The potential difference between radius **r** and an infinite "remote earth" point is the line integral of the field:

$$V(r) = \int_{r}^{\infty} E(x)\, dx = \int_{r}^{\infty} \frac{\rho I}{2\pi x^2}\, dx = \frac{\rho I}{2\pi} \left[ -\frac{1}{x} \right]_{r}^{\infty} = \frac{\rho I}{2\pi r}$$

By Ohm's law, R = V/I, hence the resistance of a hemisphere is:

$$R_{hemisphere} = \frac{\rho}{2\pi r}$$

### Step 2: Extension to a Finite-Length Vertical Pipe Electrode

A vertical pipe of length **L** and diameter **d** (= 2r) is much longer than its radius. Each infinitesimal disc of length **dx** along the pipe acts as a hemisphere of radius equal to the distance from that disc to the remote earth.

Summing the contributions (parallel-resistor integral) of all such discs along the length L:

$$R = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L} \int_{0}^{L} \frac{dx}{x + \sqrt{x^2 + r^2}}$$

> **Physical interpretation:** The denominator √(x² + r²) is the slant distance from a disc located at depth **x** to the earth's surface, capturing the "current spreading" geometry.

### Step 3: Evaluating the Integral

This integral is a standard form. The result, for L >> r, is:

$$\int_{0}^{L} \frac{dx}{x + \sqrt{x^2 + r^2}} = \ln\left(\frac{4L}{d}\right) - 1$$

> **Key point:** The "1" in the bracket is the *self-correction* for the electrode's own geometry; without it, the formula overestimates the resistance by a small amount.

### Step 4: Final Result

Substituting back, the resistance of a **pipe/rod earth electrode** is:

$$\boxed{R = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[ \ln\left(\frac{4L}{d}\right) - 1 \right]}$$

This is the formula tabulated in Clause 9 of IS 3043. All KTU problems are solved using this expression.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical (KTU Past-Paper Pattern, 14 Marks)

> **Problem:** A substation is to be earthed using **vertical cast-iron pipes, each 3.0 m long and 100 mm in diameter**, buried in soil of resistivity **80 Ω·m**. The maximum allowable earth resistance is **2 Ω**. How many pipes are required if the utilization factor is **0.85**?

**Given:**
- L = 3.0 m; d = 0.10 m (i.e., 100 mm)
- ρ = 80 Ω·m
- R_allowable = 2 Ω
- η = 0.85

**Step 1 — Resistance of a single pipe** (apply the IS 3043 formula):

$$R_1 = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[ \ln\left(\frac{4L}{d}\right) - 1 \right]$$

Compute the argument of the log first:

$$\frac{4L}{d} = \frac{4 \times 3.0}{0.10} = 120$$

$$\ln(120) = 4.7875$$

Therefore:

$$R_1 = \frac{80}{2\pi \times 3.0} \left[ 4.7875 - 1 \right] = \frac{80}{18.8496} \times 3.7875$$

$$R_1 = 4.2441 \times 3.7875 = 16.075\ \Omega$$

> Note: This high single-electrode resistance is typical — soil resistivity in the problem is high.

**Step 2 — Parallel combination with utilization factor:**

For n pipes in parallel (with utilization factor η):

$$R_n = \frac{R_1 \cdot \eta}{n} \quad \Rightarrow \quad n = \frac{R_1 \cdot \eta}{R_n} = \frac{16.075 \times 0.85}{2.0}$$

$$n = \frac{13.664}{2.0} = 6.83$$

**Step 3 — Round up to the next whole number:**

$$n_{required} = 7\ \text{pipes}$$

> **Answer:** *7 pipe electrodes* must be installed in parallel to achieve a combined earth resistance of ≤ 2 Ω.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Electrode Type

| Site Condition | Recommended Electrode (per IS 3043) | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **Deep, moist soil** (e.g., Kerala laterite with high water table) | **Pipe electrode** | Easy to drive; cost-effective; deep burial accesses lower-ρ layers |
| **Shallow bedrock or rocky terrain** | **Rod electrode** (driven with mechanical hammer) | No excavation needed; reaches fractures that may hold moisture |
| **Large outdoor substation** (132 kV, 220 kV) | **Mat/Grid electrode** with vertical rods | Controls step and touch potentials across a wide area |
| **Dry, sandy, high-ρ soil** | **Plate + chemical treatment** (charcoal + salt) | Increases contact area; chemical treatment maintains moisture |
| **Urban indoor installations** (rising mains, building earth pits) | **Pipe electrode** in a constructed earth pit with masonry enclosure | Compact, easy to maintain, allows periodic watering |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kerala-Specific Note:** Kerala laterite soil has typical ρ ≈ **30–200 Ω·m** depending on moisture. A single 3 m pipe usually gives 5–20 Ω. For installations requiring ≤ 1 Ω, multiple pipes spaced at least 6 m apart are essential.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Earth-Pit Construction Flow (Block-Level Architecture)

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture** of a standard pipe-electrode earth-pit installation, as specified in IS 3043 (Clauses 8–11). It captures every mandatory component, from the equipment to the soil-treatment layers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[EQUIPMENT METAL ENCLOSURE OR NEUTRAL POINT] --> B[PROTECTIVE EARTH CONDUCTOR GI OR COPPER STRIP]
    B --> C[FUNNEL ASSEMBLY FOR PERIODIC WATERING]
    C --> D[CAST IRON PIPE ELECTRODE 40MM TO 50MM DIAMETER]
    D --> E[BURIED IN EARTH PIT 3.0 M TO 4.0 M DEEP]
    E --> F[CHARCOAL LAYER 50MM TO 75MM THICK]
    F --> G[SALT LAYER NaCl 25MM THICK]
    G --> H[ALTERNATE LAYERS OF CHARCOAL AND SALT SURROUNDING PIPE]
    H --> I[BRICK MASONRY ENCLOSURE CI PIPE SLEEVE AT TOP]
    I --> J[TEST LINK FOR MEASURING EARTH RESISTANCE]
    J --> K[REMOTE EARTH SOIL MASS ZERO POTENTIAL]
    K -.->|REFERENCE| A
```

**Reading the diagram (for KTU description answers):**

- The **equipment** at the top connects to the **earth conductor** (GI/copper strip), which carries the fault current.
- The conductor terminates at the **earth pit**, which contains a **vertical pipe** surrounded by alternating layers of **charcoal and salt** to maintain low resistivity.
- A **funnel** at the top allows periodic manual watering of the pit to keep the surrounding soil moist.
- A **test link** provides a disconnect point so that an earth tester (fall-of-potential method) can be connected without removing the conductor.
- The current finally disperses into the **remote earth** (the bulk soil mass), whose potential is taken as zero.

## 4.2 Fall-of-Potential Method — Earth Resistance Measurement

IS 3043 prescribes the **fall-of-potential (FOP) method** as the standard field test for measuring earth resistance. The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the test setup.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph E[EARTH SYSTEM UNDER TEST]
    E1[ELECTRODE UNDER TEST E]
    end
    subgraph C[CURRENT INJECTION]
    C1[CURRENT ELECTRODE C PLACED 30M TO 50M AWAY]
    end
    subgraph P[POTENTIAL PROBE]
    P1[POTENTIAL ELECTRODE P MOVED ALONG LINE EC]
    end
    subgraph D[MEASURING INSTRUMENT]
    D1[EARTH TESTER OR BRIDGE]
    end
    E1 -- CABLE1 --> D1
    C1 -- CABLE2 --> D1
    D1 -- CABLE3 --> P1
    P1 -- CABLE4 --> E1
```

> **Procedure (textual explanation for KTU 3-mark answer):**
> 1. The **Current Electrode (C)** is driven into the ground at a distance of **30–50 m** from the earth electrode under test **(E)**.
> 2. The **Potential Electrode (P)** is driven into the ground *in between* E and C.
> 3. Current is injected through C, and the voltage is measured between E and P.
> 4. The resistance R = V/I is plotted as P is moved. A **plateau region** in the R-vs-distance curve gives the true earth resistance. Outside the plateau, the reading is contaminated by mutual resistance between electrodes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "fall-of-potential"?** The voltage between E and P drops (falls) as P is moved away from E, eventually levelling off when P enters the region of zero potential gradient (remote earth). This *levelling-off resistance* is the true earth-electrode resistance.

## 4.3 Equipotential Bonding Schematic for a Substation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[HIGH VOLTAGE EQUIPMENT 1 TRANSFORMER NEUTRAL] --> M[EQUIPOTENTIAL EARTHING MAT GRID OF COPPER CONDUCTORS]
    B[HIGH VOLTAGE EQUIPMENT 2 CIRCUIT BREAKER FRAME] --> M
    C[HIGH VOLTAGE EQUIPMENT 3 ISOLATOR STRUCTURE] --> M
    D[HV EQUIPMENT 4 CT PT JUNCTION BOX] --> M
    E[LV PANEL METAL ENCLOSURE] --> M
    F[LIGHTNING ARRESTER EARTH TERMINAL] --> M
    G[BUILDING STEEL STRUCTURE] --> M
    M --> H[MULTIPLE PIPE ELECTRODES N IN PARALLEL]
    H --> I[REMOTE EARTH SOIL RESISTIVITY RHO]
```

> **Engineering Insight:** The **equipotential mat** ensures that during a fault, *all metal parts rise to the same potential* (the GPR). Since there is **no potential difference** between, say, the transformer frame and the operator's feet, the touch potential is **zero by construction**, regardless of the fault current magnitude. This is the **gold standard** of personnel safety in EHV substations.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]**
*Define the following terms as per IS 3043: (i) Earth electrode, (ii) Soil resistivity, (iii) Step potential.*

**Model Answer:**

(i) **Earth Electrode:** A conductor or group of conductors in intimate contact with the earth, providing a direct electrical connection between the electrical installation and the general mass of earth. Common types include pipes, plates, rods, and strips.

(ii) **Soil Resistivity (ρ):** The electrical resistance offered by a unit cube (1 m × 1 m × 1 m) of soil, measured between opposite faces. It is expressed in **ohm-metres (Ω·m)**. It is the single most important parameter governing the resistance of an earth electrode. Typical values range from **5 Ω·m** (wet organic soil) to over **10 000 Ω·m** (dry rocky terrain).

(iii) **Step Potential:** The potential difference between the feet of a person standing on the ground near an earthed structure during a fault, considering a step length of **1 metre**. It is given by $E_{step} = I_g \times R_{step}$, where $R_{step}$ includes the resistance of the two foot contacts, the soil between them, and the body. Per IS 3043, the safe limit is $E_{step} \le (1000 + 6 C_s) \times 0.116$ for the typical body-current assumption.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]**
*List the various types of earth electrodes mentioned in IS 3043. State any three factors affecting soil resistivity.*

**Model Answer:**

The various types of earth electrodes specified in IS 3043 (Clause 7) are:

1. **Pipe electrode** — vertical GI/copper pipe, 40–50 mm diameter, 2.75–3.0 m long.
2. **Plate electrode** — copper or GI plate, typically 60 cm × 60 cm, buried vertically at depth ≥ 1.5 m.
3. **Rod electrode** — solid copper or copper-clad steel rod, 12.5–25 mm diameter, driven vertically.
4. **Strip electrode** — copper or GI strip, 25 mm × 1.6 mm (Cu) or 25 mm × 6 mm (GI), buried horizontally in trenches.
5. **Mat (Grid) electrode** — interconnected horizontal conductors forming a mesh, used in substations.

**Three factors affecting soil resistivity:**

1. **Moisture content** — resistivity drops dramatically (often by an order of magnitude) as moisture increases from 0% to 30%.
2. **Temperature** — below 0 °C, the soil water freezes and resistivity increases sharply.
3. **Salt / mineral content** — dissolved electrolytes (NaCl, CaCl₂) provide ionic conduction paths and substantially lower ρ.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1, Question II.a]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the resistance of a vertical pipe earth electrode as given in IS 3043. State the assumptions used.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A substation uses **8 vertical cast-iron pipe electrodes, each 3.0 m long and 100 mm in diameter**, installed in parallel at 6 m spacing in soil of resistivity **120 Ω·m**. The utilization factor is **0.90**. Calculate: (i) the resistance of a single electrode, and (ii) the combined earth resistance of the installation.

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Assumptions:**

1. The soil is a **homogeneous, isotropic, semi-infinite medium** of uniform resistivity ρ.
2. The remote earth is at **zero potential**, at an effectively infinite distance.
3. The electrode is **vertical**, fully embedded, and its length L is much greater than its diameter d (L >> d).
4. The electrode behaves as a **linear, ohmic conductor** (no ionization or non-linear soil effects).
5. The current disperses **radially** into the soil; symmetry is exploited.

**Derivation:**

The derivation is given in full in **Section 3.1** of these notes. Briefly:

1. For a **hemispherical** electrode of radius r, current I spreads radially into infinite soil. The current density is $J = I / (2\pi x^2)$ at distance x, and the electric field is $E = \rho J = \rho I / (2\pi x^2)$.
2. Integrating the field from r to ∞ gives the potential at the hemisphere surface: $V = \rho I / (2\pi r)$, hence $R = \rho / (2\pi r)$.
3. For a **finite-length vertical pipe** of length L, summing the parallel contributions of all differential disc elements along the pipe gives the integral $R = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L} \int_{0}^{L} \frac{dx}{x + \sqrt{x^2 + r^2}}$.
4. Evaluating this integral (using the substitution and small-r approximation) yields:

$$\boxed{R = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[ \ln\left(\frac{4L}{d}\right) - 1 \right]}$$

where d = 2r is the diameter of the pipe.

> **Key points to mention in the answer (for full 7 marks):**
> - Mention all 5 assumptions.
> - Show the hemispherical case as the building block.
> - Write the integral for the vertical pipe.
> - State the result of integration and the final expression.
> - Note that this formula is in **Clause 9.1 of IS 3043**.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given:**

- Number of pipes: n = 8
- L = 3.0 m
- d = 0.10 m
- ρ = 120 Ω·m
- η = 0.90

**(i) Resistance of a single pipe** (apply IS 3043 formula):

$$R_1 = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[ \ln\left(\frac{4L}{d}\right) - 1 \right]$$

$$\frac{4L}{d} = \frac{4 \times 3.0}{0.10} = 120$$

$$\ln(120) = 4.7875$$

$$R_1 = \frac{120}{2\pi \times 3.0} \times (4.7875 - 1) = \frac{120}{18.8496} \times 3.7875$$

$$R_1 = 6.3662 \times 3.7875 = 24.110\ \Omega$$

**(ii) Combined resistance of 8 pipes in parallel** (with utilization factor):

$$R_n = \frac{R_1 \times \eta}{n} = \frac{24.110 \times 0.90}{8} = \frac{21.699}{8} = 2.712\ \Omega$$

> **Final Answer:**
> (i) R_1 = **24.11 Ω**
> (ii) R_n = **2.71 Ω**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Common Mark-Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Students often enter L in cm and d in mm in the same formula, producing wildly wrong answers. **Always convert to SI units (metres) before substituting.**
> 2. **Forgetting the "− 1" in the bracket:** This single omission costs ~1.5 marks. Write out the bracket fully.
> 3. **Forgetting the utilization factor η:** For n electrodes in parallel, it is **R_n = R_1 × η / n**, NOT R_n = R_1 / n. A common trap is to treat them as ideal parallel resistors, ignoring mutual screening.
> 4. **Logarithm base:** All logs in IS 3043 are natural (base *e*). Do not use log₁₀.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2, Question II.b]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **fall-of-potential method** for measurement of earth resistance. Sketch the test setup and explain how the true earth resistance is determined from the test results.

**(b) [7 Marks]** The soil resistivity at a site is measured using the **Wenner 4-pin method** with electrode spacing **a = 6 m**. The measured resistance is **R = 12.5 Ω**. A **plate electrode of size 60 cm × 60 cm** is to be installed at a depth of **2.0 m** at this site. Calculate the expected earth resistance of the plate.

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Apparatus:** Earth tester (megger-type), two auxiliary electrodes — **Current electrode C** and **Potential electrode P**.

**Test setup (refer to Section 4.2 Mermaid diagram):**

1. The **electrode under test (E)** is disconnected from the installation at the test link.
2. The **Current electrode C** is driven into the soil at a distance of **30 m to 50 m** from E (typically 4× the largest dimension of E).
3. The **Potential electrode P** is driven into the soil on the straight line between E and C, at a distance of about **20% of the E–C spacing** initially.
4. The earth tester injects a known current I between E and C, and measures the potential V between E and P.
5. The resistance is R = V / I.
6. **P is then moved** along the line E–C in steps (e.g., 10%, 20%, 30%, …, 90% of EC), and the resistance is recorded at each position.

**Interpretation of the curve:**

- When P is close to E, the resistance is low (P is still in the high-potential zone around E).
- As P moves toward C, the resistance reading rises.
- The curve becomes **flat (plateau)** in the middle region — this plateau value is the **true earth resistance of E**.
- Beyond ~80% of EC, the curve rises again because P enters the influence zone of C.
- The plateau value is reported as the earth resistance.

> **Key points for full marks:** apparatus identification, layout, measurement procedure, plateau interpretation.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute soil resistivity from the Wenner measurement:**

The Wenner formula is:

$$\rho = 2\pi a R$$

$$\rho = 2\pi \times 6 \times 12.5 = 471.24\ \Omega \cdot m$$

**Step 2 — Compute the perimeter of the plate electrode:**

For a square plate of side 0.6 m, the perimeter is:

$$S = 4 \times 0.6 = 2.4\ m$$

**Step 3 — Apply the plate-electrode resistance formula:**

$$R = \frac{\rho}{8S} + \frac{\rho}{4\pi d}$$

Substituting:

$$R = \frac{471.24}{8 \times 2.4} + \frac{471.24}{4\pi \times 2.0}$$

$$R = \frac{471.24}{19.2} + \frac{471.24}{25.133}$$

$$R = 24.544 + 18.750$$

$$\boxed{R = 43.29\ \Omega}$$

> **Interpretation:** The plate electrode gives a higher resistance than expected. To achieve the typical target of ≤ 1 Ω, the design must either: (a) use **multiple plates in parallel**, (b) use a **larger plate**, or (c) treat the soil with **salt and charcoal** to reduce ρ locally. For the same site, switching to a **3.0 m vertical pipe electrode** (R ≈ **47 Ω**, but the resistance is similar in this dry soil) would still be insufficient — so **soil treatment is mandatory** at such high-ρ sites.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Wenner formula trap:** Students frequently write $\rho = 2\pi a R$ but then plug in R in **kΩ** (because their instrument displays resistance in kilo-ohms). Always check units.
> 2. **Plate perimeter:** The variable S is the *perimeter*, not the *area* or *side*. Many students incorrectly use S = 0.6 m (the side) instead of S = 2.4 m.
> 3. **Two-term formula:** The plate formula has **two terms** — the resistance of the plate itself (ρ/8S) and a depth-correction term (ρ/4πd). Omitting the second term underestimates R by ~40%.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Quick-Revision Checklist (Print This and Pin to Your Wall Before the Exam)**

- **IS 3043** = BIS code for *Code of Practice for Earthing* (1987 edition, still current).
- The two main objectives of earthing are **personnel safety** and **equipment protection**.
- Two functional categories: **System earthing** (neutral) and **Equipment earthing** (frames).
- Five electrode types: **Pipe, Plate, Rod, Strip, Mat/Grid**.
- The **default formula** for vertical pipe/rod resistance is:
$$R = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\left(\frac{4L}{d}\right) - 1\right]$$
- The **plate formula** has two terms: $R = \rho/8S + \rho/4\pi d$.
- The **Wenner 4-pin formula** is: $\rho = 2\pi a R$ (most common soil-resistivity test).
- The **fall-of-potential method** is the standard earth-resistance test (current electrode at 30–50 m).
- For **n electrodes in parallel**, the formula is $R_n = R_1 \eta / n$, NOT $R_1 / n$ — the **utilization factor η** is critical.
- The **safe touch potential limit** (IS 3043) is $E_{touch} \le (1000 + 1.5 C_s) \times 0.116$ (typical body resistance × permissible current).
- The **safe step potential limit** is $E_{step} \le (1000 + 6 C_s) \times 0.116$.
- **Typical earth resistance targets:** ≤ 0.5 Ω (large substations), ≤ 5 Ω (small commercial), ≤ 10 Ω (domestic).
- **Soil resistivity** decreases with moisture and salt; it increases with depth in dry soil and on bedrock.
- **SI units always** — convert cm to m, mm to m, before substituting into any formula.
- **Natural logarithms** (base *e*) throughout IS 3043 — not log₁₀.
- The standard soil treatment is **charcoal + salt (NaCl)**, applied in alternating layers around the electrode, and recharged every 1–2 years.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Mantra for IS 3043:**
> *"ρ is the master variable. The pipe formula has a '−1' you must NOT forget. The Wenner formula is ρ = 2πaR. The parallel formula has a utilization factor. Touch potential × step potential — both must be ≤ their limits. SI units and natural logs — always."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IS 2026, IS 3646 Part 1 & 2, IS 5216 Part 1 & 2, Electricity Supply Code 2014
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT752 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** IS 2026, IS 3646 Part 1 & 2, IS 5216 Part 1 & 2, Electricity Supply Code 2014

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Role of Indian Standards (IS) in Electrical System Design

**Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** is the national standards body of India, formerly known as the Indian Standards Institution (ISI). For an electrical design engineer, IS codes are not just bureaucratic documents — they are the **codified engineering wisdom** accumulated over decades. They ensure **safety, interoperability, performance, and reliability** of every component from a 6 A switch to a 500 MVA power transformer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping (CO1):** This topic directly addresses the learning outcome *"Recall the relevant Indian Standards and statutory regulations applicable to electrical system design."* — a high-weightage concept for Part A (3 marks) and frequently tested in Part B (14 marks).

## 1.2 The Four Pillars of Module 1

| Code | Full Title | Primary Domain |
|---|---|---|
| **IS 2026** | Power Transformers (multi-part) | Equipment Specification |
| **IS 3646 (P-1 & P-2)** | Code of Practice for Interior Illumination | Lighting Design |
| **IS 5216 (P-1 & P-2)** | Safety Procedures & Practices in Electrical Work | Installation Safety |
| **KSERC Supply Code 2014** | Kerala Electricity Supply Code | Statutory / Utility Interface |

## 1.3 Intuitive Overview — The "Traffic Rules" Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of these standards as the **traffic rules of the electrical world**. IS 2026 is the **vehicle fitness certificate** (your transformer must meet certain build quality). IS 3646 is the **street lighting standard** (how bright a road should be at night). IS 5216 is the **driving license rules & safety gear** (how electricians should work safely). The Electricity Supply Code 2014 is the **Highway Act** (the legal contract between you, the road builder — DISCOM, and the regulator — KSERC).

- **Why these four together?** An electrical design is incomplete if you specify the **equipment** (IS 2026), design the **lighting** (IS 3646), ignore the **safety of workers** (IS 5216), and bypass the **statutory compliance** (Supply Code 2014).
- **Statutory Authority:** BIS codes are **voluntary** unless mandated by law. The Electricity Act 2003 makes adherence to BIS standards mandatory for licensed electrical installations in India.

## 1.4 The Standardization Pyramid

The hierarchy of standards for any electrical design flows as:

$$\text{Statutory Law (Electricity Act 2003)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Regulations (CEA / KSERC Supply Code)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Indian Standards (IS)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Designer's Best Practice}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Statutory Constants to Remember (bold for high-yield):**
> - **Standard Frequency:** **50 Hz**
> - **Standard Low-Voltage (LV):** **230 V (1-φ)** and **415 V (3-φ)**
> - **Standard Wiring Voltage:** **250 V grade** for domestic wiring
> - **Earth Electrode Resistance (recommended):** **≤ 1 Ω for large substations, ≤ 5 Ω for small installations**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 IS 2026 — Power Transformers (Multi-Part Standard)

IS 2026 is the **bedrock standard** for oil-immersed and dry-type power transformers used in transmission and distribution networks. It is structurally equivalent to the international **IEC 60076** series.

### 2.1.1 Part-wise Breakdown

| Part | Title | Design Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Part 1** | General Requirements | Rating, vector group, tap range |
| **Part 2** | Temperature-Rise Requirements | Cooling class selection (ONAN, ONAF, OFAF) |
| **Part 3** | Insulation Levels & Dielectric Tests | BIL (Basic Insulation Level) selection |
| **Part 4** | Terminal Markings, Tappings & Connections | Vector group (Dyn11, Yyn0, etc.) |
| **Part 5** | Ability to Withstand Short-Circuit | Through-fault withstand verification |
| **Part 7** | Loading Guide for Oil-Immersed Transformers | Overload capability estimation |
| **Part 8** | Application Guide | Specification writing |
| **Part 10** | Determination of Sound Levels | Noise limit (typically **≤ 75 dB(A)** for 100 kVA) |
| **Part 11** | Application Guide for Dry-Type Transformers | Cast-resin transformer selection |

### 2.1.2 Critical Design Parameters (from IS 2026)

- **Standard kVA Ratings:** 16, 25, 63, 100, 160, 250, 400, 500, 630, 800, 1000, 1250, 1600, 2000, 2500 kVA (and above).
- **Maximum Permissible Temperature Rise (over 50 °C ambient):**
  - **Oil (top):** **50 °C** (ONAN), **60 °C** (forced cooling)
  - **Winding (average):** **55 °C** (Class A), **65 °C** (Class E), **70 °C** (Class B), **90 °C** (Class F)
- **Vector Group Convention:** **Dyn11** is the most common for distribution transformers (Δ-primary, Y-secondary with neutral brought out, +30° phase shift).
- **Standard Tap Range:** **±5 %** in steps of **2.5 %** (5 taps total) on HV side.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The 14-mark question on transformer design almost always requires knowledge of IS 2026 Part 2 (temperature rise) and Part 5 (short-circuit withstand). Always cite the *clause number* in your answer.

## 2.2 IS 3646 — Code of Practice for Interior Illumination (Parts 1 & 2)

IS 3646 governs **artificial lighting design** for indoor spaces. It is the Indian equivalent of the CIE (Commission Internationale de l'Éclairage) recommendations.

### 2.2.1 Part 1: Principles of Good Lighting & Aspects of Design

Part 1 establishes the **5 Quantitative Principles of Good Lighting:**

1. **Illumination Level** (sufficient lux for the task)
2. **Uniformity of Illumination** (ratio of minimum to average ≥ 0.8 for general areas)
3. **Glare Control** (limiting Direct Glare Index)
4. **Colour Rendering** (CRI ≥ 80 for offices, ≥ 60 for streets)
5. **Shadows & Flicker** (avoid stroboscopic effects with 50 Hz supply)

### 2.2.2 Part 2: Schedule of Illumination & Glare Index

Part 2 gives the **tabular data** of recommended lux values for every conceivable indoor task. This is **the most-frequently-reproduced table** in KTU exams.

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Lux Values (must be memorized):**

| Task / Location | Recommended Illuminance (lux) | Glare Index Limit |
|---|---|---|
| Corridors, passages, lobbies | **50 – 100** | 19 |
| Stairs, lifts | **100 – 150** | 19 |
| Stores, stockrooms | **100 – 200** | 22 |
| General office work | **300 – 500** | 16 |
| Detailed drawing office | **750 – 1000** | 16 |
| Watch-making, jewellery | **1500 – 3000** | 10 |
| Hospital operating theatre | **5000 – 10000** | 10 |
| Classrooms | **300 – 500** | 16 |
| Workshops (general) | **300 – 750** | 22 |
| Roads (residential) | **30 – 50 lux** | – |
| Roads (main traffic) | **50 – 100 lux** | – |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Glare Index** is computed using the **Glare Index Formula** which considers the position, luminance, and solid angle of every luminaire visible in the field of view. IS 3646 Part 2 prescribes the maximum permissible values.

## 2.3 IS 5216 — Safety Procedures & Practices in Electrical Work (Parts 1 & 2)

IS 5216 is the **Bible of Electrical Safety** for Indian practice. It is the operational counterpart of IS 732 (Wiring) and the Electricity Act 2003 (Statute).

### 2.3.1 Part 1: Operating Procedures

This part covers **live-line work, switching, isolation, and permit systems:**

- **Rule of Thumb:** *"No person shall work on any electrical apparatus or conductor unless it is dead and isolated."* — Clause 4.1
- **Permit-to-Work (PTW) System:** A documented written authorization issued by an authorized person before any work begins on HV/LV apparatus.
- **Lock-Out & Tag-Out (LOTO) Procedure:** The isolator is mechanically locked in the OFF position and a tag is affixed indicating *"DO NOT OPERATE — MEN AT WORK."*
- **Minimum Approach Distances (for live-line work, glove work):**
  - LV (< 250 V): **No live work permitted** without special authorization
  - 11 kV: **≥ 0.5 m** (glove method)
  - 33 kV: **≥ 0.9 m**
  - 110 kV: **≥ 1.5 m**
  - 220 kV: **≥ 2.5 m**
  - 400 kV: **≥ 3.5 m**

### 2.3.2 Part 2: Maintenance Procedures

- **Earthing of Conductors:** All conductors that have been isolated but might pick up induced voltage (e.g., long overhead lines) must be **earthed and short-circuited** using a **discharge rod** (earthing rod) before work.
- **Sequence of Operations (De-energization):** **Switch OFF → Isolate → Lock → Test for Dead → Earth & Short.**
- **Re-energization Sequence:** **Remove Earths & Shorts → Remove Locks → Remove Tags → Switch ON.**
- **Personal Protective Equipment (PPE):** Insulating gloves (Class 0 to Class 4), arc-flash suit, helmet with face shield, safety shoes.
- **Classification of Persons:** *Authorized Person*, *Competent Person*, *Instructed Person*, *Ordinary Person* (Clauses 3.4 – 3.7).

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Trap:** Examiners specifically look for the *correct sequence* of isolation and the *use of LOTO* in safety-related questions. A common student error is reversing the earthing step.

## 2.4 Electricity Supply Code 2014 (KSERC)

The **Kerala State Electricity Regulatory Commission (KSERC)** issued this Code under Section 181 of the Electricity Act, 2003. It governs the relationship between the **Distribution Licensee (KSEB Ltd.)** and the **Consumer**.

### 2.4.1 Salient Chapters

| Chapter | Subject | Key Provisions |
|---|---|---|
| I | General & Definitions | Consumer categories: LT-I to LT-VII, HT-I to HT-IV |
| II | Classification of Consumers | Tariff slabs, applicability |
| III | Procedure for New Connection | Application, feasibility, agreement |
| IV | Standards of Performance | **Time-bound service guarantees** |
| V | Quality of Supply | Voltage, frequency, harmonics limits |
| VI | Metering | Ownership, accuracy class (0.5 / 0.2) |
| VII | Billing & Payment | Cycle, dispute redressal |
| VIII | Disconnection & Reconnection | Notice, reasons, procedure |
| IX | Safety | Earthing, clearances, statutory norms |
| X | Penal Provisions & Compensation | Failure to meet SOP → compensation |

### 2.4.2 Quality of Supply Limits (Most-Tested)

$$\text{Nominal Voltage: } V_n = 230 \text{ V (1-φ)}, \; 415 \text{ V (3-φ)}$$

| Parameter | Permissible Variation | Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| Voltage (LV) | **+10 % to –10 %** of nominal (i.e., 207 V – 253 V) | Sustained variation |
| Voltage (11 kV / 33 kV) | **± 6 %** to **± 9 %** (depending on load) | |
| Frequency | **49.5 Hz to 50.5 Hz** | Normal operation |
| Total Harmonic Distortion (Voltage) | **THD ≤ 8 %** (LV), **≤ 5 %** (HV) | As per IEEE 519 cross-reference |
| Power Factor | **≥ 0.85 lag** (penalty below) | At consumer's interface |
| Voltage Unbalance | **≤ 3 %** | Negative-sequence component |

### 2.4.3 Standards of Performance (Time-Bound Guarantees)

| Service | Time Limit (from request) |
|---|---|
| New LT connection (feeder ready) | **7 days** |
| New HT connection (where no extension needed) | **30 days** |
| Restoration of supply after fuse-off | **4 hours** (urban), **8 hours** (rural) |
| Restoration after line breakdown | **8 hours** (urban), **24 hours** (rural) |
| Meter complaint rectification | **30 days** |
| Voltage complaint investigation | **60 days** |
| Bill complaint redressal | **30 days** |

> Failure to meet these attracts **automatic compensation** as per the Code, a unique feature of KSERC regulations.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Variable Definitions | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Illuminance (E)** | $E = \dfrac{\Phi}{A}$ | $\Phi$ = luminous flux (lm), $A$ = area (m²) | Direct calculation |
| **Luminous Flux** | $\Phi = I \cdot \omega$ | $I$ = intensity (cd), $\omega$ = solid angle (sr) | Photometric fundamentals |
| **Number of Lamps** | $N = \dfrac{E \cdot A}{\Phi_L \cdot UF \cdot MF}$ | $UF$ = utilization factor, $MF$ = maintenance factor | Luminaire selection |
| **Lumen Method Flux** | $\Phi_{total} = \dfrac{E \cdot A}{UF \cdot MF}$ | Total lumens required | Initial estimate |
| **Maintenance Factor** | $MF = \dfrac{1}{d \cdot (1 + R)}$ | $d$ = dirt factor, $R$ = lamp lumen depreciation ratio | Design margin |
| **Glare Index (British)** | $GI = 10 \log_{10} \left[ \dfrac{0.45}{\pi} \sum \dfrac{L^2 \omega}{p^2} \right]$ | $L$ = luminance, $\omega$ = solid angle, $p$ = Guth's position index | Glare rating |
| **Specific Load** | $SL = \dfrac{\text{Total lux} \times \text{Area}}{\text{Total wattage}}$ | W/m² per 100 lux | Energy check |
| **Transformer kVA** | $S = \sqrt{3} \, V_L I_L$ | $V_L$, $I_L$ = line values | Rating |
| **Voltage Regulation** | $VR\% = \dfrac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100$ | No-load vs full-load voltage | IS 2026 verification |
| **% Impedance** | $Z\% = \dfrac{V_{sc}}{V_{rated}} \times 100$ | Short-circuit test result | Fault current calculation |
| **Short-Circuit Current** | $I_{sc} = \dfrac{I_{FL}}{Z\%/100} = \dfrac{100 \, S}{\sqrt{3} \, V \cdot Z\%}$ | At transformer terminals | Switchgear selection |
| **Earth Resistance (Rod)** | $R_e = \dfrac{\rho}{2 \pi L} \left[ \ln\left(\dfrac{8L}{d}\right) - 1 \right]$ | $\rho$ = soil resistivity (Ω-m), $L$ = length, $d$ = diameter | IS 5216 earthing |
| **Touch Voltage** | $V_t = I_{body} \cdot R_{e,fault}$ | Step-and-touch calculation | Safety |
| **Voltage Drop (3-φ)** | $\Delta V = \sqrt{3} \, I L (R \cos\phi + X \sin\phi)$ | Per conductor length | Conductor sizing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** This combination of codes is used by:
> 1. **KSEB / DISCOMs** for HT consumer billing, quality compliance, and new connection approvals.
> 2. **Consulting Engineers (PWD, CPWD, MES)** for tender specifications, lighting design, and transformer selection.
> 3. **Safety Auditors** for verification of LOTO procedures and PPE in industrial plants.
> 4. **Accredited Test Labs (NABL)** for transformer type tests per IS 2026 and lighting photometry per IS 3646.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Number of Lamps Required for a Specified Illuminance

This is the **most repeated numerical problem** in KTU exams for the IS 3646 module.

### Given Data
- Room: $A \times B = 12 \text{ m} \times 8 \text{ m} = A \text{ m}^2$ of working plane
- Required illuminance: $E = 300 \text{ lux}$ (general office)
- Lamp: Fluorescent tube, $\Phi_L = 3000 \text{ lumens each}$
- Luminaire type: Industrial reflector, $UF = 0.5$
- Maintenance schedule: $MF = 0.8$
- Each luminaire houses 2 lamps

### Step 1 — Total Luminous Flux Required

The total flux that must reach the working plane to produce the required illuminance is given by the basic definition of illuminance:

$$\Phi_{required} = E \times A$$

Substituting the values:

$$\Phi_{required} = 300 \;\text{lux} \times 96 \;\text{m}^2 = 28800 \;\text{lumens}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** One lux is defined as one lumen per square metre, so the product of $E$ (lm/m²) and $A$ (m²) gives the total flux in lumens that must strike the working plane.

### Step 2 — Total Luminous Flux to be Emitted by Lamps

In real installations, not all flux emitted by the lamp reaches the working plane. Two correction factors apply:
- **UF (Utilization Factor):** Ratio of flux reaching the working plane to the total flux emitted.
- **MF (Maintenance Factor):** Accounts for lamp lumen depreciation and luminaire dirt accumulation.

So the lamp-side flux must be:

$$\Phi_{lamps} = \dfrac{\Phi_{required}}{UF \times MF} = \dfrac{28800}{0.5 \times 0.8}$$

Calculating the denominator:

$$UF \times MF = 0.5 \times 0.8 = 0.4$$

Therefore:
