$$\Phi_{lamps} = \dfrac{28800}{0.4} = 72000 \;\text{lumens}$$

### Step 3 — Number of Lamps

Each lamp emits $\Phi_L = 3000 \text{ lm}$. Each luminaire houses 2 lamps, so one luminaire contributes:

$$\Phi_{luminaire} = 2 \times 3000 = 6000 \;\text{lumens}$$

Total number of luminaires required:

$$N_{lum} = \dfrac{\Phi_{lamps}}{\Phi_{luminaire}} = \dfrac{72000}{6000} = 12 \;\text{luminaires}$$

Since each luminaire has 2 lamps:

$$N_{lamps} = 12 \times 2 = 24 \;\text{lamps}$$

### Step 4 — Verification (Specific Load Check)

Total installed wattage = $24 \times 40 \text{ W} = 960 \text{ W}$ (assuming 40 W fluorescent tubes).

$$\text{Specific Load} = \dfrac{\text{Wattage}}{\text{Area}} = \dfrac{960}{96} = 10 \;\text{W/m}^2$$

This is within the acceptable range of **8 – 12 W/m²** for office lighting using fluorescent tubes, confirming the design is energy-efficient.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Link to IS 3646:** The MF value used (0.8) is typical for *moderately clean* industrial environments. For *clean* offices, MF = 0.9; for *dirty* foundries, MF = 0.6. This directly comes from IS 3646 Part 1.

## 3.2 Derivation: Short-Circuit Current at LV Terminals (IS 2026 Part 5)

When selecting a switchgear (MCCB, ACB, fuse) at the LT bus of a transformer, the breaking capacity must exceed the **prospective short-circuit current (PSCC)** at that point.

### Given Data
- Transformer rating: $S = 1000 \text{ kVA}$
- Rated LV voltage: $V_L = 415 \text{ V}$ (3-phase)
- Percentage impedance from IS 2026 nameplate: $Z\% = 6\%$

### Step 1 — Full-Load Current on LV Side

For a 3-phase transformer:

$$I_{FL} = \dfrac{S}{\sqrt{3} \, V_L} = \dfrac{1000 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times 415}$$

Computing the denominator:

$$\sqrt{3} \times 415 = 1.732 \times 415 \approx 718.9 \;\text{V}$$

Therefore:

$$I_{FL} = \dfrac{1000000}{718.9} \approx 1390 \;\text{A}$$

### Step 2 — Short-Circuit Current at LV Terminals

The short-circuit current equals the full-load current divided by the per-unit impedance:

$$I_{sc} = \dfrac{I_{FL}}{Z_{pu}} = \dfrac{I_{FL}}{Z\%/100}$$

Substituting:

$$I_{sc} = \dfrac{1390}{0.06} \approx 23167 \;\text{A} \approx 23.17 \;\text{kA}$$

### Step 3 — Switchgear Selection Rule

The **breaking capacity** of the downstream ACB/MCCB must satisfy:

$$I_{brk} \geq I_{sc} + \text{safety margin (typically 20\%)}$$

$$I_{brk} \geq 1.2 \times 23.17 \approx 27.8 \;\text{kA}$$

So we select a **35 kA** ACB — the next standard rating.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap:** Students often forget to convert **Z% to per-unit** (divide by 100) and write $I_{sc} = I_{FL} \times Z\%$, which is **dimensionally wrong** and costs full marks.

## 3.3 Derivation: Earth Resistance of a Driven Rod (IS 5216 / IS 3043)

The **single-rod earth electrode** is the most common earthing method for small installations. Its resistance in homogeneous soil is:

$$R_e = \dfrac{\rho}{2 \pi L} \left[ \ln\left(\dfrac{8L}{d}\right) - 1 \right]$$

where:
- $\rho$ = soil resistivity (Ω-m)
- $L$ = length of rod in the ground (m)
- $d$ = diameter of rod (m)

### Numerical Example

Given:
- $\rho = 100 \;\Omega\text{-m}$ (typical red loam)
- $L = 3 \text{ m}$
- $d = 0.05 \text{ m}$ (50 mm GI pipe)

### Step 1 — Evaluate the Logarithmic Term

$$\dfrac{8L}{d} = \dfrac{8 \times 3}{0.05} = \dfrac{24}{0.05} = 480$$

$$\ln(480) = \ln(4.8 \times 10^2) = \ln(4.8) + 2\ln(10)$$

Using natural logarithm values:

$$\ln(4.8) \approx 1.5686, \quad \ln(10) \approx 2.3026$$

$$\ln(480) \approx 1.5686 + 2 \times 2.3026 = 1.5686 + 4.6052 = 6.1738$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Bracketed Factor

$$\ln\left(\dfrac{8L}{d}\right) - 1 = 6.1738 - 1 = 5.1738$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Pre-Factor

$$\dfrac{\rho}{2 \pi L} = \dfrac{100}{2 \times \pi \times 3} = \dfrac{100}{18.85} \approx 5.305 \;\Omega$$

### Step 4 — Final Earth Resistance

$$R_e = 5.305 \times 5.1738 \approx 27.45 \;\Omega$$

### Step 5 — Conclusion

This single rod (27.45 Ω) is well above the required **≤ 5 Ω** for an installation earth. To reduce the resistance:

$$\text{Options: } \begin{cases} \text{Increase rod length } L \\ \text{Use multiple rods in parallel} \\ \text{Treat soil with salt \& charcoal} \\ \text{Use earth plates instead of rods} \end{cases}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world insight:** The 27.45 Ω is for *homogeneous* soil. In layered soil, the effective resistance is lower. IS 3043 provides correction factors for layered earth models.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — IS Code Selection Decision Tree

| Design Activity | Applicable IS Code | Key Clause | What to Extract |
|---|---|---|---|
| Specifying 11 kV/0.415 kV distribution transformer | **IS 2026** | Part 1, Part 2 | kVA rating, vector group, Z%, temperature rise |
| Designing hospital corridor lighting | **IS 3646** | Part 2, Table 1 | Lux = 100–150, GI limit = 19 |
| Issuing permit to work on 11 kV breaker | **IS 5216** | Part 1, Clause 5 | PTW format, isolation sequence |
| Earth-rod sizing for an industry | **IS 3043** (cross-ref) | Clause 8.2 | Earth resistance formula |
| Consumer complaining of low voltage | **Supply Code 2014** | Chapter V | Voltage limits, 60-day redressal |
| Selecting ACB for 1000 kVA transformer | **IS 13947 / IS 2026** | Part 5 | Short-circuit current calculation |
| Disconnecting a defaulting consumer | **Supply Code 2014** | Chapter VIII | 15-day notice, reconnection procedure |
| Glare calculation for an office | **IS 3646** | Part 2, Appendix A | Glare index formula, BRS tables |

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Standards Hierarchy in Electrical Design

The following Mermaid block illustrates how the four codes fit into the engineering workflow — from the regulatory umbrella to the actual field installation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    law["ELECTRICITY ACT 2003 (Statute)"] --> rules["CEA Safety Regulations 2010"]
    rules --> supply["KSERC SUPPLY CODE 2014"]
    supply --> consumer["Consumer Interface LT / HT Metering"]
    law --> bis["Bureau of Indian Standards BIS"]
    bis --> is2026["IS 2026 POWER TRANSFORMERS"]
    bis --> is3646["IS 3646 INTERIOR ILLUMINATION"]
    bis --> is5216["IS 5216 ELECTRICAL SAFETY"]
    is2026 --> spec["Equipment Specification"]
    is3646 --> light["Lighting Design"]
    is5216 --> install["Installation Workmanship"]
    spec --> plant["SUBSTATION / PLANT"]
    light --> plant
    install --> plant
    supply --> plant
    consumer --> plant
    plant --> energise["SAFE ENERGISED SYSTEM"]
```

> **Reading Guide:** Every component on the right is governed by at least one of the four standards, all of which trace back to the parent statute (the Electricity Act 2003). This is the single most important conceptual flow to internalize for KTU exams.

## 4.2 Lighting Design Process (IS 3646 Workflow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Identify Task & Location"] --> B{"Refer IS 3646 Part 2 Schedule"}
    B --> C["Extract Required Lux Level E"]
    C --> D["Measure Area A in sq m"]
    D --> E["Select Luminaire Type"]
    E --> F["Read UF from IS 3646 Table"]
    F --> G["Assume MF from IS 3646 Table"]
    G --> H["Compute Total Lumen Required"]
    H --> I["Choose Lamp Wattage and Flux"]
    I --> J["Calculate Number of Lamps N"]
    J --> K["Check Glare Index GI Limit"]
    K --> L{"GI within Limit?"}
    L -- No --> M["Re-select Luminaire or Add Diffuser"]
    M --> K
    L -- Yes --> N["Verify Specific Load W per sq m"]
    N --> O["Final Layout Drawing"]
```

> **Reading Guide:** This is the exact Lumen Method procedure tested in 14-mark questions. The glare check (Step K) is the *non-negotiable* second-order verification that examiners specifically look for.

## 4.3 Safety Procedure Sequence (IS 5216 De-energization)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Receive Work Permit Request"] --> B["Identify All Energy Sources"]
    B --> C["Switch OFF Main Isolator"]
    C --> D["Lock Isolator in OFF Position"]
    D --> E["Affix Danger Tag DO NOT OPERATE"]
    E --> F["Test for Dead Using Approved Tester"]
    F --> G{"Dead Confirmed?"}
    G -- No --> H["STOP Investigate Further"]
    G -- Yes --> I["Apply Earths and Short Circuits"]
    I --> J["Issue Permit to Work PTW"]
    J --> K["Work Commences Under PTW"]
    K --> L["On Completion Remove Tools and Personnel"]
    L --> M["Remove Earths and Shorts"]
    M --> N["Remove Locks and Tags"]
    N --> O["Switch ON and Restore Supply"]
    O --> P["Cancel PTW and Archive Record"]
```

> **Reading Guide:** Note the **strict order**: switch off → lock → tag → test → earth. Reversing the *earth-before-test* step is a fatal error and a guaranteed 0-marks answer in any safety question.

## 4.4 KSERC Supply Code 2014 — Consumer Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Consumer Applies for New Connection"] --> B["Feasibility Study by KSEB"]
    B --> C{"Feasible?"}
    C -- No --> D["Reject with Reasons in 30 days"]
    C -- Yes --> E["Estimate and Demand Note"]
    E --> F["Consumer Pays and Signs Agreement"]
    F --> G["Service Connection Released"]
    G --> H["Meter Installed Accuracy 0 5"]
    H --> I["Billing Cycle Begins Monthly"]
    I --> J["Quality of Supply Monitored"]
    J --> K{"Voltage / Frequency within Limits?"}
    K -- No --> L["Penalty and Compensation to Consumer"]
    K -- Yes --> M["Continued Supply"]
    M --> N{"Consumer Defaults or Violates?"}
    N -- Yes --> O["15 Day Disconnection Notice"]
    O --> P["Disconnection and Dues Recovery"]
    N -- No --> M
    L --> Q["DISCOM Corrective Action"]
    Q --> M
```

> **Reading Guide:** This lifecycle covers the *complete legal and commercial journey* of an electrical consumer. The **15-day notice** and **automatic compensation** clauses are unique features of the KSERC Code and are heavily tested.

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 marks)

### Question 1 (3 marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q: List any **three** important provisions of the Electricity Supply Code 2014 (KSERC) with respect to (a) standards of performance for new connections, and (b) quality of supply voltage limits.**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Standards of Performance for New Connections (Supply Code 2014, Chapter IV):**
1. **New LT connection** (when the existing LT feeder is available and no extension is required): to be released within **7 days** from the date of payment of estimate and execution of agreement.
2. **New HT connection** (where no system extension is needed): to be released within **30 days** from the date of payment of the estimate and execution of the agreement.
3. **Failure to comply:** If the licensee (KSEB Ltd.) fails to meet the above time limits, it is liable to pay **automatic compensation** to the consumer as prescribed in the Code (typically a fixed monetary amount per day of delay, subject to a cap).

**(b) Quality of Supply — Voltage Limits (Supply Code 2014, Chapter V):**
1. The licensee shall maintain the **voltage at the consumer's terminals** within the following limits, measured as a 10-minute average RMS value:
   - **Low Voltage (230 V / 415 V):** **+10 % to –10 %** of nominal (i.e., 207 V to 253 V on single-phase; 359 V to 440 V between phases on three-phase).
   - **High Voltage (11 kV, 22 kV, 33 kV):** **± 6 %** to **± 9 %** of nominal depending on the loading condition.
2. The licensee shall also ensure that the **frequency** is maintained within **49.5 Hz to 50.5 Hz** during normal operating conditions.
3. **Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)** of the supply voltage shall not exceed **8 %** at the LV consumer interface and **5 %** at the HV consumer interface.

> **Key Mark-Winning Points:** Mention the *specific numerical values* (7 days, 30 days, ±10 %, 49.5–50.5 Hz). Generic answers without numbers fetch only 1 out of 3.

---

### Question 2 (3 marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q: Differentiate between IS 3646 Part 1 and IS 3646 Part 2 in terms of their scope and content.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | **IS 3646 Part 1** | **IS 3646 Part 2** |
|---|---|---|
| **Title** | Code of Practice for Interior Illumination — *Principles of Good Lighting and Aspects of Design* | Code of Practice for Interior Illumination — *Schedule of Illumination and Glare Index* |
| **Scope** | Establishes the **qualitative and quantitative principles** that govern the design of any interior lighting scheme | Provides the **tabular data** (schedules) of recommended illuminance values and glare index limits for specific tasks and locations |
| **Content** | Covers the 5 principles of good lighting: (i) illumination level, (ii) uniformity, (iii) glare control, (iv) colour rendering, (v) absence of flicker. Also explains utilization factor, maintenance factor, and design methods (Lumen Method, Point-by-Point Method) | Contains detailed **schedules** of recommended lux for over 100 task types (offices, hospitals, factories, schools, etc.) along with the corresponding maximum permissible **Glare Index** values |
| **User Application** | Used to *understand the philosophy* of lighting design and the underlying design formulae | Used as a *ready reference table* during detailed design to look up the lux value for the specific task at hand |
| **Key Tables/Formulae** | UF tables, MF tables, lumen method equation, glare index formula (introduction) | Schedule of illumination (Table 1), Glare Index Schedule (Table 2) |

> **Key Mark-Winning Points:** Stating the *correct titles* of both parts and giving *at least one numerical example* (e.g., "Part 2 specifies 300–500 lux for general office work") is the key to full marks.
