(7 Marks) --- #### Model Solution to Question A: **Part (a) — TPDT Changeover Switch (7 Marks)** **Construction (3 marks):** - Three identical poles, each consisting of a **fixed contact** and a **movable contact**. - The movable contact is mounted on a common shaft rotated by an external lever or motorised actuator. - Each pole has **two stable positions**: - **Position I (Normal):** Connected to **EB supply** (incoming 1). - **Position II (Emergency):** Connected to **DG supply** (incoming 2). - Mechanical **interlock** prevents both sources from being connected simultaneously (essential for safety). - The shaft can be manually operated (manual changeover switch, MCOS) or motor-driven (Automatic Transfer Switch, ATS). **Working (3 marks):** - Under **normal condition**, all three phases are connected to the EB bus via Position I. The DG is in standby. - When EB fails, the ATS controller senses loss of voltage → starts the DG → waits for DG voltage/frequency stabilisation → **actuates the motorised shaft** to Position II → load is now fed by DG. - When EB returns, the ATS controller confirms restoration → after a preset delay → switches back to Position I → DG is cooled down and stopped. - The mechanical interlock ensures a **break-before-make** operation, preventing paralleling of unsynchronised sources. **Application (1 mark):** Used in hospitals, data centres, high-rise buildings, and any installation requiring **100% power availability** as per **IE Rule 47** and **IEEE 446** (Orange Book). --- **Part (b) — Switch Rating Calculation (7 Marks)** **Step 1: Compute full-load current of motor.** $$ \begin{aligned} I_{FL} &= \frac{P}{\sqrt{3} \times V_L \times \eta \times \cos\phi} \\ &= \frac{7500}{\sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 0.86 \times 0.85} \\ &= \frac{7500}{506.66} \\ &= 14.80 \text{ A} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Compute DOL starting current.** $$ I_{start} = 6 \times I_{FL} = 6 \times 14.80 = 88.8 \text{ A} $$ **Step 3: Apply 1.25 safety factor for switch rating.** $$ I_{n,\text{min}} = 1.25 \times 14.80 = 18.5 \text{ A} $$ **Step 4: Select next standard rating → 25 A (from R10 series).** $$ I_{n,\text{selected}} = 25 \text{ A (TPST, 415 V, AC-23 duty)} $$ **Step 5: Voltage-drop check (IE Rule 48 — limit 3% of 400 V = 12 V).** For 3-φ balanced load, $V_d = \sqrt{3} \times I \times R \times \cos\phi$. Assume cable area $A = 4$ mm², $\rho = 0.0172$ Ω·mm²/m, $L = 30$ m. $$ \begin{aligned} R &= \frac{\rho L}{A} = \frac{0.0172 \times 30}{4} = 0.129 \; \Omega \\ V_d &= \sqrt{3} \times 14.80 \times 0.129 \times 0.85 \\ &= 2.81 \text{ V} \quad (< 12 \text{ V}) \quad \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6: Conclusion.** $$ \boxed{\text{Select a 25 A, 415 V, TPST industrial switch (AC-23), 4 mm² Cu cable, } V_d = 2.81 \text{ V (within limit)}.} $$ --- ### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice > **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]** **a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **SPST, SPDT, and DPDT switch** configurations and their typical applications. (7 Marks) **b)** A single-phase 230 V sub-circuit feeds the following loads in a domestic installation: (i) 5 × 60 W lamps, (ii) 1 × 1000 W heater, (iii) 2 × 5 A socket outlets. Determine the **suitable switch rating** and comment on the necessity of an additional MCB. (7 Marks) --- #### Model Solution to Question B: **Part (a) — SPST / SPDT / DPDT (7 Marks)** | Type | Diagram (Symbol) | Application | |---|---|---| | **SPST** | Two terminals, one movable contact — simple ON/OFF | Domestic light switch, fan regulator input | | **SPDT** | Three terminals (COM, NO, NC) — changeover | Selector switch for two speed fans, two-source low-power circuits | | **DPDT** | Two coupled SPDT — operates in tandem | Forward-reverse of small DC motors, polarity reversal | **Diagrams (3 marks):** (Students should draw IEC standard symbols with terminals marked.) **Description and Application (4 marks):** - **SPST:** Simplest form. Two contacts, one pair closes, the other opens. Used in domestic light/fan circuits as per **IE Rule 73**. - **SPDT:** Has one input and two outputs. Common in **selector switches** for choosing between two supply sources for low-power loads. - **DPDT:** Two SPDT switches mechanically ganged. Used in **forward-reverse starters** for small motors, and **inverter bypass** circuits. --- **Part (b) — Domestic Sub-Circuit Sizing (7 Marks)** **Step 1: Compute total connected load.** $$ \begin{aligned} L_{lamps} &= 5 \times 60 = 300 \text{ W} \\ L_{heater} &= 1 \times 1000 = 1000 \text{ W} \\ L_{sockets} &= 2 \times 5 \times 230 = 2300 \text{ W} \quad (\text{assuming sockets fully loaded}) \\ L_{total} &= 300 + 1000 + 2300 = 3600 \text{ W} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Compute total current.** $$ I_{total} = \frac{3600}{230} = 15.65 \text{ A} $$ **Step 3: Apply 1.25 factor.** $$ I_{n,\text{min}} = 1.25 \times 15.65 = 19.57 \text{ A} $$ **Step 4: Select next standard → 20 A modular switch.** $$ I_{n,\text{selected}} = 20 \text{ A (SPST modular switch)} $$ **Step 5: Necessity of MCB (3 marks).** > [!IMPORTANT] > **An MCB is MANDATORY** as per **IE Rule 48, IS 732, and IS 8828** for the following reasons: > 1. **Overload protection** — if all loads run simultaneously beyond 20 A, the switch will overheat. > 2. **Short-circuit protection** — a switch alone CANNOT break fault currents safely; an MCB rated 20 A, 10 kA will. > 3. **Earth-fault coordination** — combined with RCCB, provides full protection as per **IE Rule 61A**. > 4. **Regulatory compliance** — NEC + IS 732 require MCB/ELCB in every sub-circuit. **Step 6: Final Selection.** $$ \boxed{\text{20 A SPST switch backed by 20 A, 10 kA, C-curve MCB (single-pole + N).}} $$ --- > [!WARNING] > **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Conflating SPST and DPST:** SPST switches *only* the line. DPST switches *both* line and neutral simultaneously. Both are required in many KTU questions. > 2. **Forgetting the 1.25 de-rating factor:** If you select a switch exactly equal to $I_{load}$, you **lose 1 mark**. Always apply 1.25. > 3. **Confusing utilization categories:** AC-21 ≠ AC-22 ≠ AC-23. Selecting AC-21 for a motor load will fail and is a common reason for losing 2 marks. > 4. **Skipping unit-conversion steps:** Always show kW → W and A → mA conversions explicitly. > 5. **Ignoring voltage-drop in switch sizing questions:** IE Rule 48 caps the drop at 3% — examiners deduct 1–2 marks if not verified. > 6. **Writing "switch can break short-circuit current":** Strictly false. Switches are not fault-rated. > 7. **Forgetting mechanical interlock** while describing TPDT changeover: This is a 1-mark question in disguise. --- ## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST - [x] **Definition:** A switch is a manual mechanical device for making/breaking *normal* currents only — **not** fault currents. - [x] **Pole-Throw:** SPST, SPDT, DPST, DPDT, TPST, TPDT — memorize each symbol and use case. - [x] **Utilization Categories:** AC-21 (resistive), AC-22 (mixed), AC-23 (motor/inductive). - [x] **Rating Formula:** $I_n = 1.25 \times I_{load}$, then round up to standard R10 size. - [x] **Standards:** IS 13947 Part 3 (switches), IS 8828 (MCB), IEC 60947-3. - [x] **Construction Types:** Knife (obsolete), Toggle, Push-button, Rotary, Slide, Limit, Micro, Float, Isolator, SDF. - [x] **Switch-Disconnector:** Combines switch + isolator function; visible-break in OFF state. - [x] **Position in circuit:** Always **after** overcurrent protection (MCB/Fuse) and **before** the load. - [x] **Voltage drop limit:** ≤ 3% of declared voltage per **IE Rule 48**. - [x] **ATS / Changeover:** TPDT with mechanical interlock; break-before-make operation. - [x] **Endurance:** Mechanical $\geq 10^5$ ops, Electrical $\geq 10^4$ ops. - [x] **Difference from Circuit Breaker:** CB trips automatically; switch is manual; CB can break fault currents. - [x] **Domestic application:** 6 A (lighting), 16 A (socket), 25 A (heater/geyser), 32 A (AC unit). - [x] **Industrial application:** TPST 50–800 A rotary cam switch, AC-23 duty. - [x] **MCB Always Required:** Switch alone does not provide overcurrent or short-circuit protection. --- > ✅ **End of Module 1 — Topic: Switches | PEEOT752 — Electrical System Design** > 📚 *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020, and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (Levels L1–L4).*

topics.title: Distribution Box
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT752 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Distribution Box

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Distribution Box (DB) – Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Distribution Box (DB)**, also called a **Distribution Board** or **Consumer Unit**, is an electrical enclosure that receives electrical power from the main supply (via the energy meter) and **distributes it to multiple sub-circuits** within a building, industry, or installation, while providing **overload, short-circuit, and earth-leakage protection** through protective devices such as **Miniature Circuit Breakers (MCBs)**, **Residual Current Circuit Breakers (RCCBs)**, and **Moulded Case Circuit Breakers (MCCBs)**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, a DB is the **nerve-centre of any low-voltage (LV) electrical installation** — typically rated up to **415 V, 50 Hz, three-phase** — and is governed by **IS 13032 (Panel Boards)**, **IS 8623 / IEC 61439-1 (Low-voltage Switchgear Assemblies)**, and the **Central Electricity Authority (CEA) Regulations 2010**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Distinction (KTU Board Pattern)**
> - **Distribution Box (DB)** → Final distribution to loads
> - **Main Distribution Board (MDB)** → Main power control point in a building
> - **Sub-Distribution Board (SDB)** → Branch distribution from MDB
> - **Power Distribution Board (PDB)** → Used for high-power 3-phase loads (HVAC, lifts, etc.)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **large apartment building with 50 flats**:
- **One huge water pipe** enters the building (this is the *incoming supply cable*).
- The water must reach **every flat**, but if a pipe bursts in Flat 23, we don't want the entire building to lose water.

**The Distribution Box is exactly like the building's water control room:**
- The **main inlet valve** = the **Main Switch / Isolator** (cuts power to the whole building).
- The **branch pipes with individual valves** = the **MCBs / RCCBs** (each protects one flat/circuit).
- The **water pressure gauge** = the **busbar** (the main conductor that carries current).
- The **leak detector** = the **ELCB/RCCB** (cuts supply on earth leakage).
- The **metal cabinet housing it all** = the **enclosure** (protects against dust, water, accidental touch).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Examiners frequently test whether students understand that a DB is **not just a box**, but an *assembled switchgear system* consisting of enclosure, busbars, switching devices, and protective devices — all working as a coordinated unit to ensure **selectivity, discrimination, and safety**.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Metrics & Ratings

The following metrics are **always in bold** because they are the most-examined numerical parameters:

- **Rated Operational Voltage (Ue):** **415 V AC, 3-phase, 4-wire** OR **240 V AC, 1-phase, 2-wire**
- **Rated Frequency:** **50 Hz** (Indian standard)
- **Rated Current (In):** From **32 A** (residential) up to **800 A** (industrial MDBs)
- **Short-Circuit Withstand Capacity (Icw):** **10 kA – 50 kA** for 1 second
- **Ingress Protection (IP) Rating:** **IP 43** (indoor), **IP 55 / IP 65** (outdoor / industrial)
- **Form of Internal Separation:** **Form 1, 2, 3, or 4** (as per IEC 61439-2)
- **Standard Colour Coding:** **Red, Yellow, Blue** for phases; **Black** for neutral; **Green/Yellow** for earth

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Architecture – What Happens Inside a DB?

A Distribution Box performs **five simultaneous, coordinated functions** during its operational life:

1. **Power Reception:** The incoming supply cable (from the energy meter or upstream MDB) terminates at the **Main Incoming Device** — either a **Switch-Disconnecter (Isolator)** or an **MCCB**.

2. **Power Distribution:** The incoming current flows through the **main busbar assembly** (made of electrolytic grade copper or aluminium, tin-plated), which then feeds each outgoing branch circuit.

3. **Overcurrent Protection:** Each outgoing circuit has an **MCB (Single-Pole or Triple-Pole)** sized to the cable's current-carrying capacity. This protects against:
   - **Overload** (sustained low-level overcurrent)
   - **Short-circuit** (instantaneous high fault current)

4. **Earth-Fault / Residual Current Protection:** An **RCCB (or RCBO)** monitors the difference between line and neutral currents. If the difference exceeds **30 mA (for human protection) or 300 mA (for fire prevention)**, the device trips within **< 40 milliseconds**.

5. **Isolation & Maintenance:** The main switch allows the **entire DB to be made dead** for maintenance, ensuring **Lockout-Tagout (LOTO)** safety.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarises all the **high-yield formulas** required to solve KTU numerical problems on Distribution Box design, sizing, and protection coordination.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notation convention:** $\vert$ symbols are rendered using `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity. Do not use the pipe character `$\vert$` directly in table cells.

| S.No. | Parameter | Formula | Standard Unit | Application Context |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total Connected Load | $P_{total} = \sum P_i$ | kW | Summation of all branch loads |
| 2 | Full-Load Current (1-φ) | $I_{FL} = \dfrac{P \times 1000}{V \times \cos\phi}$ | Amperes | Single-phase load current |
| 3 | Full-Load Current (3-φ) | $I_{FL} = \dfrac{P \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \times V_L \times \cos\phi}$ | Amperes | Three-phase balanced load |
| 4 | Voltage Drop (3-φ) | $V_{d} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3} \times I \times L \times (R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)}{1000}$ | Volts | Cable sizing verification |
| 5 | Allowable Voltage Drop | $V_{d} \le 3\%$ of $V_L$ (lighting) OR $5\%$ of $V_L$ (power) | Volts | IS 732 / NEC compliance |
| 6 | MCB Rating Selection | $I_{MCB} \ge I_{FL}$ and $I_{MCB} \le 1.45 \times I_{z}$ | Amperes | Coordination with cable |
| 7 | Energy Let-Through (I²t) | $I^{2}t = K^{2} \times S^{2}$ | A²s | Discrimination check |
| 8 | Earth-Fault Loop Impedance | $Z_{s} = Z_{e} + Z_{1} + R_{1} + R_{2}$ | Ohms | Maximum value $\le 0.4 \Omega$ (230 V) |
| 9 | Prospective Short-Ckt Current | $I_{psc} = \dfrac{U_{0}}{Z_{s}}$ | kA | Must be $\le$ MCB breaking capacity |
| 10 | Busbar Cross-Section | $A = \dfrac{I}{\delta_{max}}$ | mm² | $\delta_{max} = 1.6$ A/mm² for Cu |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Distribution Box concept is the **backbone of every LV electrical design** in the following sectors:

- **Residential:** Flat/apartment DBs with 6-way to 18-way configurations.
- **Commercial:** Floor-wise SDBs feeding lighting, AC units, and plug loads.
- **Industrial:** Power Distribution Boards (PDBs) feeding motors, PLC panels, and process lines.
- **Data Centres:** High-availability PDUs with **Form 4 segregation** for maintenance without shutdown.
- **Renewables:** AC DBs in solar PV systems, connecting inverter outputs to the building.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A common question is *"Explain the difference between MCB, MCCB, RCCB, and ELCB."* Memorise the table above and you will score full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Selection of MCB Rating from Cable Current Capacity

This derivation is the **most-tested numerical in KTU board exams** for Electrical System Design.

**Given:**
- A sub-circuit feeds a load of $P = 4$ kW at $V = 230$ V single-phase, $\cos\phi = 0.9$.
- The cable used is 2.5 mm² copper, PVC insulated, with current-carrying capacity $I_z = 18$ A (as per IS 3961, Method C — enclosed in conduit).

**Step 1: Calculate the Full-Load Current (FLC)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{FL} &= \frac{P \times 1000}{V \times \cos\phi} \\
&= \frac{4000}{230 \times 0.9} \\
&= \frac{4000}{207.0} \\
I_{FL} &= 19.32 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the Coordination Rule (IS 732 / IEC 60364)**

The MCB rating $I_n$ must satisfy **two simultaneous conditions**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Condition A: } & I_n \ge I_{FL} \\
\text{Condition B: } & I_n \le 1.45 \times I_z
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Evaluate Condition B**

$$
\begin{aligned}
1.45 \times I_z &= 1.45 \times 18 \\
&= 26.1 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Select the Standard MCB Rating**

From standard MCB ratings (6, 10, 16, 20, 25, 32, 40, 50, 63 A), we need $I_n$ such that $19.32 \le I_n \le 26.1$.

**Selected MCB: 20 A (C-curve)**

$$
\boxed{I_n = 20 \text{ A, Type C, 10 kA breaking capacity}}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Busbar Sizing for a 3-Phase MDB

**Given:** A Main Distribution Board (MDB) feeds a total balanced 3-phase load of **150 kVA** at **415 V, 50 Hz**, with $\cos\phi = 0.85$. The DB is to be designed using **tin-plated electrolytic copper busbars** with maximum current density $\delta_{max} = 1.6$ A/mm².

**Step 1: Total Line Current**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{total} &= \frac{kVA \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \times V_L} \\
&= \frac{150 \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \times 415} \\
&= \frac{150000}{718.7} \\
I_{total} &= 208.7 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Required Cross-Sectional Area**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{req} &= \frac{I_{total}}{\delta_{max}} \\
&= \frac{208.7}{1.6} \\
A_{req} &= 130.4 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Standard Busbar Selection**

Standard copper busbar sizes: 12 × 5 mm (60 mm²), 25 × 5 mm (125 mm²), 30 × 5 mm (150 mm²), 40 × 5 mm (200 mm²), 50 × 5 mm (250 mm²).

**Selected: 30 × 5 mm (150 mm²) — gives 1.39 A/mm² (safe margin)**

$$
\boxed{\text{Busbar: 30 \times 5 mm Cu, 1-phase per bar + 1 N + 1 E}}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Earth-Fault Loop Impedance (EFLI) Verification

**Given:** A 230 V circuit protected by a 16 A Type B MCB. The maximum disconnection time per IS 732 is **0.4 seconds** (for fixed equipment) or **0.2 seconds** (for socket outlets).

**Step 1: From MCB Type B Characteristics, $I_a$ (trip current) for 0.4 s ≈ $5 \times I_n$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_a &= 5 \times 16 = 80 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Maximum Allowable EFLI**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_{s, max} &= \frac{U_0}{I_a} \\
&= \frac{230}{80} \\
Z_{s, max} &= 2.875 \text{ \Omega}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compare with Actual Measured EFLI**

If actual measured $Z_s = 0.85 \text{ \Omega}$, then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_s &\le Z_{s, max} \\
0.85 &\le 2.875 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

The installation is **SAFE** for ADS (Automatic Disconnection of Supply).

## 3.4 KTU Numerical Practice Matrix

| Problem Type | Key Formula Tested | Marks Weightage | Frequency in Past Papers |
|---|---|---|---|
| MCB rating selection | $I_n \ge I_{FL}$ and $I_n \le 1.45 I_z$ | 7 marks | Very High (4/5 years) |
| Voltage drop calculation | $V_d = \sqrt{3} I L (R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)/1000$ | 7 marks | High |
| Busbar sizing | $A = I/\delta_{max}$ | 7 marks | Medium |
| EFLI verification | $Z_{s, max} = U_0 / I_a$ | 7 marks | High |
| DB layout (descriptive) | Form of separation, IP rating | 7-14 marks | Very High |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Single-Line Diagram (SLD) of a Typical Building DB Network

The diagram below shows the **complete hierarchy** from the utility supply to the final load, including all protective devices inside each DB.

```mermaid
graph TD
    UTIL[UTILITY SUPPLY 11 kV] --> TR[STEP-DOWN TRANSFORMER 11kV 415V]
    TR --> METER[HT LT METERING PANEL]
    METER --> MCCB1[MAIN MCCB 400A 36kA]
    MCCB1 --> MDB1[MAIN DISTRIBUTION BOARD MDB]
    MDB1 --> SDB1A[FLOOR 1 SDB TYPE A LIGHTING]
    MDB1 --> SDB1B[FLOOR 1 SDB TYPE B POWER]
    MDB1 --> SDB1C[FLOOR 1 SDB TYPE C AC UPS]
    SDB1A --> MCB1A1[MCB 10A LIGHTS]
    SDB1A --> MCB1A2[MCB 10A LIGHTS]
    SDB1A --> MCB1A3[MCB 10A LIGHTS]
    SDB1B --> MCB1B1[MCB 20A SOCKETS]
    SDB1B --> MCB1B2[MCB 20A SOCKETS]
    SDB1B --> RCCB1B1[RCCB 40A 30mA SOCKET PROTECTION]
    SDB1C --> MCB1C1[MCCB 63A AC UNITS]
    SDB1C --> MCB1C2[MCB 32A UPS BYPASS]

    UTIL ~~~ TR
    TR ~~~ METER
```

## 4.2 Internal Architecture of a Single-Phase Distribution Box

The block diagram below illustrates the **internal functional architecture** of a typical residential single-phase DB, showing the path of current from incoming to outgoing.

```mermaid
graph LR
    IN[INCOMING SUPPLY 230V PH N] --> MAINSW[MAIN SWITCH ISOLATOR 63A DOUBLE POLE]
    MAINSW --> RCCB1[RCCB 63A 30mA]
    RCCB1 --> BUS[COPPER BUSBAR PHASE]
    BUS --> MCB1[MCB 10A 1P C CURVE]
    BUS --> MCB2[MCB 16A 1P C CURVE]
    BUS --> MCB3[MCB 20A 1P C CURVE]
    BUS --> MCB4[MCB 25A 1P C CURVE]
    MCB1 --> L1[LIGHTING CIRCUIT 1]
    MCB2 --> L2[LIGHTING CIRCUIT 2]
    MCB3 --> L3[SOCKET OUTLET 16A]
    MCB4 --> L4[GEYSER AC POINT]
    BUS ~~~ N
    N[NEUTRAL LINK] --> RCCB1
    N --> L1
    N --> L2
    N --> L3
    N --> L4
```

## 4.3 Form-of-Separation Block Diagram (IEC 61439-2)

The following diagram explains the **four forms of internal separation** defined by IEC 61439-2, which determine the safety and maintainability of a DB.

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph FORM1[FORM 1 NO SEPARATION]
        F1A[MAIN SWITCH]
        F1B[BRANCH MCBS]
        F1A --- F1B
    end
    subgraph FORM2[FORM 2 SEPARATION BETWEEN BUSBARS AND FUNCTIONAL UNITS]
        F2A[MAIN SWITCH]
        F2B[BRANCH MCBS]
        F2C[BUSBAR COMPARTMENT]
        F2C --- F2A
        F2C --- F2B
    end
    subgraph FORM3[FORM 3 SEPARATION OF BUSBARS FROM FUNCTIONAL UNITS AND BETWEEN FUNCTIONAL UNITS TERMINALS]
        F3A[MAIN SWITCH]
        F3B[BRANCH MCBS]
        F3C[BUSBAR]
        F3D[TERMINAL COMPARTMENT]
        F3C --- F3A
        F3C --- F3B
        F3D --- F3A
        F3D --- F3B
    end
    subgraph FORM4[FORM 4 SEPARATION OF TERMINALS FROM BUSBARS AND BETWEEN FUNCTIONAL UNITS]
        F4A[MAIN SWITCH]
        F4B[BRANCH MCBS]
        F4C[BUSBAR]
        F4D[TERMINAL AREA]
        F4C --- F4A
        F4C --- F4B
        F4D --- F4A
        F4D --- F4B
        F4A --- F4B
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Pearl:** The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly lists *"Forms of internal separation"* as a Module 1 learning outcome. Drawing this hierarchy correctly in the exam earns **3 bonus marks** above the regular answer.

## 4.4 IP Rating Reference Block

| IP Code | First Digit (Solid) | Second Digit (Water) | Typical DB Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| IP 20 | Fingers only | None | Indoor, dry locations |
| IP 43 | Tools, wires > 1 mm | Spraying water | Indoor, damp areas |
| IP 54 | Dust-protected | Splashing water | Industrial indoor |
| IP 55 | Dust-protected | Water jets | Outdoor semi-sheltered |
| IP 65 | Dust-tight | Powerful jets | Outdoor, coastal, heavy industry |
| IP 66 | Dust-tight | Heavy seas | Marine, transformer yards |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]`

**Define a Distribution Box. List any four essential components housed inside it.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A Distribution Box is a metal-enclosed low-voltage panel that receives incoming power and distributes it to multiple outgoing sub-circuits, while providing protection against overcurrent and earth faults.

**Four essential components (½ mark each):**
1. **Main Isolator / Switch-Disconnector**
2. **Miniature Circuit Breakers (MCBs)** for overcurrent protection
3. **Residual Current Circuit Breaker (RCCB)** for earth-leakage protection
4. **Copper busbars** for current distribution
5. *(Bonus: Neutral link, Earth bar, DIN rail, Enclosure)*

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between MCB, MCCB, and RCCB in a tabular form.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | MCB | MCCB | RCCB |
|---|---|---|---|
| Full form | Miniature Circuit Breaker | Moulded Case Circuit Breaker | Residual Current Circuit Breaker |
| Current range | 0.5 A – 125 A | 100 A – 1600 A | 16 A – 125 A |
| Protection | Overload + Short-circuit | Overload + Short-circuit | Earth-leakage only |
| Trip sensitivity | Magnetic + Thermal | Electronic / Thermal-magnetic | Residual current (30 mA / 300 mA) |

---

## 5.2 PART B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Design a Distribution Box for a residential flat having the following loads. Show the complete single-line diagram, MCB selection, and cable sizing.**

**Load Schedule:**
- 6 lighting points × 60 W = 360 W
- 4 ceiling fans × 75 W = 300 W
- 2 socket outlets (general) × 100 W = 200 W
- 1 socket outlet (kitchen) × 2000 W
- 1 AC point × 1500 W
- 1 geyser × 2000 W
- Supply: 230 V, 1-phase, 50 Hz, $\cos\phi = 0.9$

#### (a) Construct the Single-Line Diagram and identify the number of ways in the DB. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Total Connected Load**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{total} &= 360 + 300 + 200 + 2000 + 1500 + 2000 \\
&= 6360 \text{ W} = 6.36 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Grouping into Sub-Circuits (as per IS 732)**

| Circuit No. | Load Description | Load (W) | MCB Rating (A) | Cable |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Lighting (6 pts) | 360 | 6 A (10 A) | 1.5 mm² Cu |
| 2 | Fans (4 nos) | 300 | 6 A (10 A) | 1.5 mm² Cu |
| 3 | General sockets | 200 | 16 A | 2.5 mm² Cu |
| 4 | Kitchen socket | 2000 | 20 A | 4.0 mm² Cu |
| 5 | AC point | 1500 | 16 A | 2.5 mm² Cu |
| 6 | Geyser | 2000 | 20 A | 4.0 mm² Cu |

**Step 3: DB Configuration**

**Selected: 8-way SPN DB** with one 63 A DP isolator + one 63 A, 30 mA RCCB + 6 outgoing MCBs (1 spare way).

#### (b) Verify the MCB selection for the kitchen socket circuit. (7 Marks)

**Given:** $P = 2000$ W, $V = 230$ V, $\cos\phi = 0.9$, cable 4 mm² Cu ($I_z = 27$ A as per IS 3961, Method C).

**Step 1: Full-Load Current**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{FL} &= \frac{P \times 1000}{V \times \cos\phi} \\
&= \frac{2000 \times 1000}{230 \times 0.9} \\
I_{FL} &= 9.66 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply MCB Coordination Rule**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{FL} &\le I_n \le 1.45 \times I_z \\
9.66 &\le I_n \le 1.45 \times 27 \\
9.66 &\le I_n \le 39.15
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Select Standard MCB**

**Selected: 20 A, C-curve, 10 kA MCB**

**Verification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_n (20\text{ A}) &\ge I_{FL} (9.66\text{ A}) \quad \checkmark \\
I_n (20\text{ A}) &\le 39.15 \text{ A} \quad \checkmark \\
V_d &\approx 1.2\% \text{ of 230 V (within 3\%)} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Explain with neat sketches the various types of Distribution Boards (SPN, TPN, MCB DB, MCCB DB) and their applications in different installations.**

#### (a) Classify Distribution Boards with diagrams and write their applications. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Type 1: SPN (Single Pole and Neutral) DB**
- **Description:** Contains one phase (P) and one neutral (N) busbar; uses single-pole MCBs.
- **Application:** Residential flats, small offices, single-phase lighting circuits.
- **Rating:** 230 V, up to 100 A, 6 to 18 ways.

**Type 2: TPN (Triple Pole and Neutral) DB**
- **Description:** Contains three phase (R, Y, B) and one neutral busbar; uses triple-pole + neutral MCBs.
- **Application:** Three-phase residential buildings, small commercial loads, lift supply.
- **Rating:** 415 V, up to 400 A, 4-way to 12-way.

**Type 3: MCB Distribution Board**
- **Description:** Uses Miniature Circuit Breakers only (no fuse); DIN-rail mounted.
- **Application:** Modern residential, IT offices, hospitals.
- **Advantage:** Reusable after tripping; no replacement cost.

**Type 4: MCCB Distribution Board / Panel**
- **Description:** Uses Moulded Case Circuit Breakers for higher ratings.
- **Application:** Industrial feeders, motor control centres, large commercial.
- **Rating:** 100 A to 1600 A, 36 kA to 70 kA breaking capacity.

#### (b) Explain IP ratings and forms of internal separation as applicable to DBs. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**IP Rating System (IEC 60529):**
- **IP** = Ingress Protection
- **First digit (0-6):** Protection against solid foreign objects
- **Second digit (0-9):** Protection against water

**Examples:**
- **IP 43:** Protected against tools/wires > 1 mm and spraying water — **typical indoor residential DB**
- **IP 55:** Dust-protected + water jet resistant — **typical outdoor industrial DB**

**Forms of Internal Separation (IEC 61439-2):**
- **Form 1:** No internal separation — basic only.
- **Form 2:** Busbars separated from functional units — **most common for residential DBs**.
- **Form 3:** Busbars separated AND functional units separated from each other — **commercial SDBs**.
- **Form 4:** Busbars, functional units, AND terminals all separated — **data centres, hospitals**.

**Application selection logic:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Form} \propto \text{Criticality of Load}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout – Common Mark Loss Points**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to convert kW to W** in the formula $I = P/(V \cos\phi)$ — this single mistake loses **3 of 7 marks**.
> 2. **Not using $\sqrt{3}$ for 3-phase** full-load current — loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Writing MCB rating without verifying the $1.45 \times I_z$ condition** — board examiners specifically look for this coordination.
> 4. **Confusing MCB and MCCB breaking capacities** — MCBs are typically 10 kA, MCCBs 36 kA.
> 5. **Not drawing the neutral and earth links** in the SLD — 1 mark lost.
> 6. **Forgetting units** (A, V, kW, mm²) — Board instructions explicitly demand SI units.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Distribution Box = Incoming device + Busbar + Outgoing protective devices + Enclosure.**
- **Most common DB type in KTU questions: SPN (residential) and TPN (commercial).**
- **MCB sizing rule:** $I_{FL} \le I_n \le 1.45 \times I_z$ — memorize this verbatim.
- **Always use $\sqrt{3}$ for 3-phase** and **1 for 1-phase** current formulas.
- **Standard MCB ratings to remember:** **6, 10, 16, 20, 25, 32, 40, 50, 63 A**.
- **Standard RCCB rating for human protection:** **30 mA** (trip time < 40 ms).
- **Maximum voltage drop allowed:** **3% for lighting**, **5% for power** (per IS 732).
- **IP 43** is the *de facto* minimum for indoor DBs; **IP 55** for outdoor.
- **Forms 1 → 4** represent increasing levels of internal separation; remember *terminal separation* is the key differentiator for Form 4.
- **Busbar current density:** **1.6 A/mm² (Cu)** and **1.0 A/mm² (Al)** — direct formula in board exams.
- **Colour code:** **R-Y-B** for phases, **Black** for neutral, **Green-Yellow** for earth.
- **Two-letter mnemonic for DB components:** **S-M-R-B** → **S**witch, **M**CB, **R**CCB, **B**usbar.
- **Always state the breaking capacity** (e.g., 10 kA) when specifying an MCB in the answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Metering System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT752 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Metering System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Metering System (per KTU 2024 PEEOT752 syllabus):** A metering system is an integrated assembly of current transformers (CTs), potential transformers (PTs), measuring instruments (kWh meters, kVAh meters, maximum demand indicators, power factor meters), connecting wiring, test blocks, and a metering panel that together measure, record, and monitor the electrical energy and power consumed (or generated) by a consumer or utility installation, in accordance with **IS 14697**, **CBIP Technical Report 88**, and the **Central Electricity Authority (CEA) Regulations 2006**.

In an electrical installation, the metering system is the **"financial nerve centre"** of the power system. While protection schemes (relays, MCBs, ACBs) deal with *safety*, the metering system deals with *commerce and accounting* — measuring how much energy has been delivered, at what power factor, and at what maximum demand.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — The Petrol Pump Analogy:**
> Think of an electrical metering system exactly like the **dispenser at a petrol (gas) pump**.
> - The **CT** is like the **flow sensor** (measures how fast fuel is flowing).
> - The **PT** is like the **pressure gauge** (measures the "push" behind the flow — analogous to voltage).
> - The **kWh meter** is the **litres-to-rupees calculator** (adds up total energy).
> - The **Maximum Demand Indicator (MDI)** is like the **"peak litres/minute" indicator** — the highest flow rate observed during the visit.
> - The **metering panel** is the entire **pump cabinet** housing, wiring, and display.
> - The **Test Block** is the **calibration port** used by the inspector to verify accuracy without disconnecting the meter.
>
> Just as you cannot trust a fuel station without a calibrated meter, a utility cannot bill a consumer, and a consumer cannot verify their bill, without a properly designed metering system.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **System Frequency:** **50 Hz** (India standard, per **IS 14697**)
- **Standard CT Secondary Current:** **5 A** (or **1 A** for high accuracy long-distance metering)
- **Standard PT Secondary Voltage:** **110 V / √3** (line-to-neutral) → **110 V** (line-to-line)
- **Standard Accuracy Classes for Metering CTs:** **0.2, 0.2S, 0.5, 0.5S, 1.0** (per **IS 2705**)
- **Standard Accuracy Classes for PTs:** **0.2, 0.5, 1.0** (per **IS 3156**)
- **Standard Energy Meter Accuracy Class:** **0.2S, 0.5S, 1.0** (per **IS 14697**)
- **Reference Standard Burden for Metering CT:** **5 VA, 10 VA, 15 VA, 30 VA**
- **Insulation Level (11 kV system):** **12 kV / 28 kV** (power frequency / impulse)

### 1.4 Why Metering System is Critical in Electrical System Design

1. **Revenue Billing:** All HT consumers are billed on kVAh (apparent energy) — measured directly by metering system.
2. **Load Management:** MDI helps consumer avoid penalty for exceeding contract demand.
3. **Power Quality Monitoring:** Modern meters log voltage, frequency, harmonics, and PF.
4. **Audit & Statutory Compliance:** CEA, KSERC (Kerala), and KSEB require calibrated, sealed meters.
5. **Energy Accounting:** For industrial consumers with multiple feeders, accurate energy balance is mandatory.
6. **SCADA/AMR Interface:** Modern meters have RS-485 / PLC / RF / GPRS communication ports.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight (Syllabus Mapping):** The Metering System topic is a **CO1 (Apply) and CO2 (Analyse)** mapped module in PEEOT752, and questions frequently appear in ESE Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks). The student is expected to be able to **select** CTs/PTs/meters and **calculate burdens and accuracies**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Block Architecture of a Typical HT Metering System

A standard HT consumer metering system (e.g., 11 kV / 433 V, 500 kVA consumer) consists of the following components in series from primary to display:

1. **HT Switchgear (VCB or SF6)** — isolation device
2. **Current Transformers (CTs)** — step-down current (e.g., 400/5 A)
3. **Potential Transformers (PTs)** — step-down voltage (e.g., 11000/110 V)
4. **Test Terminal Block (TTB)** — provides isolation for meter testing
5. **Multi-Function Meter (MFM)** — measures kWh, kVAh, kVArh, MD, PF, V, I, Hz
6. **MDI (Maximum Demand Indicator)** — mechanical or electronic
7. **Metering Cubicle/Panel** — physical enclosure
8. **Sealing Arrangement** — by utility (KSEB) to prevent tampering

### 2.2 Current Transformer (CT) — Operating Principle for Metering

A CT for metering is designed to operate in the **"near short-circuit" condition** (very low burden impedance). The secondary current is **almost exactly proportional** to primary current.

**Key Metering CT Characteristics:**

- **Ratio Error:** Difference between actual and marked ratio.
- **Phase Error:** Phase displacement between primary and secondary currents (must be low for accurate PF measurement).
- **Composite Error:** Vector sum of ratio and phase errors.
- **Burden:** The total impedance (VA) connected to CT secondary.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pearl:** A **metering CT should NEVER saturate within its range**, unlike a **protection CT** which should saturate beyond its knee-point voltage to maintain coordination. A saturated metering CT will give a *false low reading* — this is a major billing dispute cause in India.

### 2.3 Potential Transformer (PT) — Operating Principle

A PT is a **step-down transformer** with the secondary wound for **110 V / √3 = 63.5 V** (line-to-neutral) or **110 V** (line-to-line), depending on connection.

**Types Used in Metering:**

- **Electromagnetic PT (EMPT):** Conventional wound-type, used up to 33 kV.
- **Capacitor Voltage Transformer (CVT):** Used for EHV (>132 kV) where EMPT becomes uneconomical; combines a capacitor divider with an intermediate transformer.
- **Resistor-Capacitor Divider:** Used in some electronic meters.

### 2.4 Multi-Function Meter (MFM) — Modern Metering Heart

A modern MFM samples voltage and current waveforms, computes True RMS, active/reactive/apparent power and energy, and stores data in memory. Standards: **IS 14697, IEC 62053-22/23, CBIP 325**.

**Key Features:**
- **4-Quadrant Measurement** (Import/Export, Lag/Lead)
- **Time-of-Day (TOD) Tariff** — 6 time zones
- **Demand Integration Period** — usually **15 minutes / 30 minutes** (sliding block)
- **Load Survey** — 30/60 min intervals for 35–60 days
- **Communication:** Optical port, RS-485 (Modbus/DLMS), PLC, GPRS
- **Memory:** Non-volatile (retains for 3 years even without power)
- **Anti-tamper features:** Reverse energy detection, magnetic tamper, neutral disturbance

### 2.5 Maximum Demand (MD) — Concept and Importance

**Maximum Demand** is the highest average kVA (or kW) drawn by the consumer over a specified **demand integration period** (DIP) — typically **15 minutes** for HT consumers in Kerala (KSEB).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kerala State Electricity Regulatory Commission (KSERC) Regulation:** For HT consumers, **billing demand = max{85% of contract demand, actual recorded MD}**. This means even if you don't draw your full contract demand, you pay for 85% of it!

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | CT Ratio | $N_{CT} = \dfrac{I_{primary}}{I_{secondary}}$ | — | $I_{sec}$ is **5 A** or **1 A** |
| 2 | PT Ratio | $N_{PT} = \dfrac{V_{primary}}{V_{secondary}}$ | — | $V_{sec}$ is **110 V** (L-L) or **63.5 V** (L-N) |
| 3 | Total Multiplication Factor (TMF) | $TMF = N_{CT} \times N_{PT}$ | — | Used in TOD energy display |
| 4 | Actual Energy | $E_{actual} = M_{reading} \times TMF$ | kWh | — |
| 5 | CT Burden (per phase) | $B_{CT} = I_{s}^{2} \times Z_{total}$ | VA | $Z_{total} = Z_{meter} + Z_{wire} + Z_{contact}$ |
| 6 | CT Voltage (Vsec) | $V_{sec} = I_{s} \times (Z_{meter} + Z_{wire})$ | V | Must be less than **Accuracy Limit Voltage (ALV)** |
| 7 | Accuracy Limit Factor (ALF) | $ALF = \dfrac{ALV}{I_{s} \times Z_{sec}}$ | — | For metering CT, ALF ≤ **1.2 to 1.5** |
| 8 | Metering Constant | $K_{meter} = \dfrac{No.\ of\ impulses}{kVArh}$ | imp/kVArh | Test bench verification |
| 9 | True Power | $P = \sqrt{3} \times V_{L} \times I_{L} \times \cos\phi$ | W | For balanced 3-phase |
| 10 | Apparent Power | $S = \sqrt{3} \times V_{L} \times I_{L}$ | VA | — |
| 11 | Reactive Power | $Q = \sqrt{3} \times V_{L} \times I_{L} \times \sin\phi$ | VAr | — |
| 12 | Power Factor | $\cos\phi = \dfrac{P}{S}$ | — | Lagging/leading depends on quadrant |
| 13 | Maximum Demand (kVA) | $MD = \dfrac{\text{Energy in DIP (kVAh)}}{\text{DIP (h)}}$ | kVA | DIP = 15/30 min = 0.25/0.5 h |
| 14 | Three-Phase Energy | $E_{3\phi} = \sqrt{3} \times V_{L} \times I_{L} \times t \times \cos\phi$ | Wh | For balanced load over time $t$ |
| 15 | Accuracy Class Error | $\epsilon = \pm \dfrac{(E_{measured} - E_{true})}{E_{true}} \times 100\%$ | % | Per IS 14697 |

### 2.7 Real-World Utility of Metering System

1. **Utility Billing** — KSEB, Tata Power, BSES, etc., rely entirely on metering.
2. **Captive Power Plants (CPP)** — Need export/import metering for wheeling banking.
3. **Solar/Wind Net-Metering** — Bi-directional (4-quadrant) meters mandatory per KSERC.
4. **Industrial Energy Auditing** — BEE Grade audits depend on logged data.
5. **Penalty Avoidance** — Low PF penalty, high MD penalty, TOD peak penalty.
6. **Data Centre Power Usage Effectiveness (PUE)** — computed from metering data.

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern Trend (KTU 2024):** ABDLMS (Advanced Battery-Discharge-type Limited Meters) and **Smart Meters (IS 16444, Part 1/2)** with **RF/HPLC/PLC** communication are now statutory for all consumers >20 kW.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Total CT Burden Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A 3-phase HT consumer has CTs of ratio **400/5 A**, connected to a multi-function meter through **30 metres** of **2.5 sq.mm copper cable** per phase. The meter burden is **2 VA per phase**. Calculate:
1. Total CT burden per phase
2. Actual secondary voltage at full load
3. Verify whether the CT is within its accuracy class

**Given Data:**

$$
I_{p} = 400\ A,\quad I_{s} = 5\ A,\quad L = 30\ m,\quad A = 2.5\ mm^{2},\quad \text{Copper},\quad B_{meter} = 2\ VA/phase
$$

**Step 1: Calculate resistance of connecting wire (round trip = 2L for loop)**

$$
R_{wire} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot 2L}{A} = \dfrac{1.724 \times 10^{-8} \times 2 \times 30}{2.5 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

> Note: $\rho_{Cu} = 1.724 \times 10^{-8}\ \Omega \cdot m$ at 20°C. (Use $2.2 \times 10^{-8}$ for 90°C operation.)

$$
R_{wire} = \dfrac{1.0344 \times 10^{-6}}{2.5 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.4138\ \Omega
$$

**Step 2: Wire burden at full load (5 A secondary)**

$$
B_{wire} = I_{s}^{2} \cdot R_{wire} = 5^{2} \times 0.4138 = 10.345\ VA
$$

**Step 3: Total CT burden per phase**

$$
B_{total} = B_{meter} + B_{wire} = 2.0 + 10.345 = 12.345\ VA
$$

> Per IS 2705, standard burdens are **5, 10, 15, 30 VA**. Our 12.345 VA is **between 10 VA and 15 VA**. We should select a **15 VA** rated CT.

**Step 4: Actual secondary voltage at full load (with 15 VA CT)**

$$
V_{sec} = I_{s} \cdot Z_{sec}
$$

If we assume CT secondary winding impedance ≈ 0.1 Ω (typical for 400/5 A), then:

$$
Z_{total} = Z_{winding} + R_{wire} = 0.1 + 0.4138 = 0.5138\ \Omega
$$

$$
V_{sec} = 5 \times 0.5138 = 2.569\ V
$$

**Step 5: Accuracy Limit Voltage (ALV) for 0.5 class metering CT with ALF = 1.2**

$$
ALV = ALF \cdot I_{s} \cdot Z_{burden(at\ rated\ burden)}
$$

Assuming rated burden = 15 VA = $Z = 15/5^{2} = 0.6\ \Omega$:

$$
ALV = 1.2 \times 5 \times 0.6 = 3.6\ V
$$

Since $V_{sec} = 2.569\ V < ALV = 3.6\ V$, **the CT operates within its accuracy class.** ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trick (often asked):** If the cable length is doubled, the burden doubles, and at very long distances the CT **loses accuracy**. Solution: use **1 A secondary CTs** — burden becomes ¼ for the same cable (since $V \propto I$).

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Energy Calculation from Meter Reading

**Problem Statement:** A 3-phase HT consumer has CTs of **200/5 A** and PTs of **11000/110 V**. The meter reads **1234.567 kVAh** at the start of the month and **1567.890 kVAh** at the end. Calculate:
1. Total Multiplication Factor
2. Actual kVAh consumed
3. Bill amount at ₹7.50 per kVAh (kerala industrial tariff approx.)

**Step 1: CT Ratio**

$$
N_{CT} = \dfrac{200}{5} = 40
$$

**Step 2: PT Ratio**

$$
N_{PT} = \dfrac{11000}{110} = 100
$$

**Step 3: TMF**

$$
TMF = N_{CT} \times N_{PT} = 40 \times 100 = 4000
$$

**Step 4: Difference in meter reading**

$$
\Delta M = 1567.890 - 1234.567 = 333.323\ kVAh
$$

**Step 5: Actual kVAh consumed**

$$
E_{actual} = \Delta M \times TMF = 333.323 \times 4000 = 1{,}333{,}292\ kVAh
$$

> In practical units: $E_{actual} = 1333.292\ MVAh = 1.333\ million\ kVAh$

**Step 6: Bill amount**

$$
\text{Bill} = 1{,}333{,}292 \times 7.50 = \text{₹}\ 99{,}99{,}690 \approx \text{₹}\ 1\ \text{Crore (1 million INR)}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Maximum Demand Penalty Calculation (Kerala Pattern)

**Problem Statement:** A factory has:
- **Contract Demand (CD):** 500 kVA
- **Recorded MD (during month):** 480 kVA
- **Recorded PF:** 0.85 lag
- **Energy consumed:** 2,00,000 kVAh

Calculate the **billing demand** and **PF penalty** (KSEB rule: PF penalty if PF < 0.90).

**Step 1: Billing Demand (KSERC Regulation)**

$$
MD_{billing} = \max\left[0.85 \times CD,\ MD_{recorded}\right]
$$

$$
MD_{billing} = \max[0.85 \times 500,\ 480] = \max[425,\ 480] = 480\ kVA
$$

> The consumer is billed for **480 kVA** since it exceeds 85% of CD.

**Step 2: PF Penalty (KSEB formula)**

For PF below 0.90 lag, surcharge = 1% of energy charges per 0.01 PF below 0.90, capped at 16% (for PF = 0.85).

$$
\Delta PF = 0.90 - 0.85 = 0.05
$$

> Number of 0.01 steps below 0.90 = 5
>
> $$\text{Surcharge} = 5 \times 1\% = 5\%$$

**Step 3: Energy charge**

$$
\text{Energy charge} = 2{,}00{,}000 \times 7.50 = \text{₹}\ 15{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 4: PF Surcharge**

$$
\text{Surcharge} = 5\% \times 15{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}\ 75{,}000
$$

> The consumer pays **₹75,000 extra** for a 0.05 lag PF shortfall! This is why PF correction is critical in design.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — CT vs PT vs Meter Sizing

| Parameter | For 100 kVA Load | For 500 kVA Load | For 2500 kVA Load |
|-----------|------------------|------------------|-------------------|
| Full Load Current (433 V) | $\dfrac{100 \times 10^{3}}{\sqrt{3} \times 433} = 133\ A$ | $\dfrac{500 \times 10^{3}}{\sqrt{3} \times 433} = 667\ A$ | $\dfrac{2500 \times 10^{3}}{\sqrt{3} \times 433} = 3334\ A$ |
| Recommended CT Ratio | **200/5 A** (1.5× FLC) | **800/5 A** (1.2× FLC) | **4000/5 A** (1.2× FLC) |
| CT Accuracy Class | 0.5 (Class 0.5 metering) | 0.5 or 0.2 | 0.2S (for billing) |
| PT Ratio (11 kV) | 11000/110 V | 11000/110 V | 11000/110 V |
| Recommended Burden | 10 VA | 15 VA | 15 VA + check |
| Meter Type | Trivector MFM | Trivector MFM (TOD) | Trivector + AMR |
| DIP | 15 min | 15 min | 15 min |

### 3.5 Worked Example — Energy Meter Accuracy Verification

A **0.5S class** energy meter is tested at 5% of rated current and unity PF. Reference energy = 100 kWh. Meter reads 100.6 kWh. Determine compliance.

$$
\%\ Error = \dfrac{E_{measured} - E_{true}}{E_{true}} \times 100 = \dfrac{100.6 - 100}{100} \times 100 = +0.6\%
$$

For **Class 0.5S**, the allowed error at 5% In is **±1.0%** (per IS 14697). Since $0.6\% < 1.0\%$, the meter **passes**. ✓

> If error were $1.2\%$, the meter would **fail** and require re-calibration or replacement.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — HT Metering System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph GRID["HT INCOMING 11 kV / 22 kV SUPPLY"]
        BUS1[HT Bus / VCB Incoming]
    end

    subgraph CTBLOCK["CT SECTION - Step Down Current"]
        CTA[CT Phase R 400/5A]
        CTB[CT Phase Y 400/5A]
        CTC[CT Phase B 400/5A]
    end

    subgraph PTBLOCK["PT SECTION - Step Down Voltage"]
        PTA[PT Phase R 11000/110V]
        PTB[PT Phase Y 11000/110V]
        PTC[PT Phase B 11000/110V]
    end

    subgraph TTB["TEST TERMINAL BLOCK"]
        TTB1[TTB With Shorting Links]
    end

    subgraph MFM["MULTI-FUNCTION METER MFM"]
        PROC[DSP Microprocessor]
        MEM[Non-Volatile Memory]
        MDI[Max Demand Indicator 15 min]
        DISP[LCD Display]
    end

    subgraph COMM["COMMUNICATION MODULE"]
        RS485[RS-485 Port]
        GSM[GPRS Modem]
        AMR[AMR Data Logger]
    end

    BUS1 --> CTA
    BUS1 --> CTB
    BUS1 --> CTC
    BUS1 --> PTA
    BUS1 --> PTB
    BUS1 --> PTC
    CTA --> TTB1
    CTB --> TTB1
    CTC --> TTB1
    PTA --> TTB1
    PTB --> TTB1
    PTC --> TTB1
    TTB1 --> PROC
    PROC --> MEM
    PROC --> MDI
    PROC --> DISP
    PROC --> RS485
    RS485 --> GSM
    GSM --> AMR
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Metering System Signal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[HT 3-Phase Supply] -->|Voltage Primary| B[Potential Transformer PT]
    A -->|Current Primary| C[Current Transformer CT]
    B -->|110V Secondary| D[Test Terminal Block TTB]
    C -->|5A Secondary| D
    D --> E[Signal Conditioning and A to D Conversion]
    E --> F[Microprocessor Based Calculation]
    F --> G1[kWh Active Energy]
    F --> G2[kVAh Apparent Energy]
    F --> G3[kVArh Reactive Energy]
    F --> G4[Max Demand MD in kVA]
    F --> G5[Power Factor PF]
    F --> G6[Frequency Hz]
    F --> H[Memory Storage Load Survey 60 days]
    F --> I[Display and Communication]
    I --> J[AMR SCADA Billing]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — 4-Quadrant Metering Concept

```mermaid
quadrantChart
    title 4-Quadrant Energy Metering Concept
    x-axis Real Power kW Negative to Positive
    y-axis Reactive Power kVAr Negative to Positive
    quadrant-1 Q2 Import Inductive Lagging
    quadrant-2 Q1 Export Inductive Lagging
    quadrant-3 Q3 Export Capacitive Leading
    quadrant-4 Q4 Import Capacitive Leading
    Q1: [0.7, 0.6]
    Q2: [0.7, 0.85]
    Q3: [0.3, 0.15]
    Q4: [0.3, 0.4]
```

> **Interpretation of Quadrants (per IS 14697 / IEC 62053):**
> - **Q1:** Power **imported**, Reactive **exported** (Inductive load — typical industrial consumer).
> - **Q2:** Power **exported**, Reactive **exported** (Generator supplying inductive load).
> - **Q3:** Power **exported**, Reactive **imported** (Generator over-excited).
> - **Q4:** Power **imported**, Reactive **imported** (Capacitive load — rarely seen in practice).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define a Current Transformer used for metering. Why is a metering CT different from a protection CT?**

**Model Answer:**

A Current Transformer (CT) for metering is an instrument transformer designed to step down the primary current to a standardized secondary current of **5 A or 1 A** while maintaining high **ratio accuracy** and **low phase error** over its normal operating range.

**Differences from Protection CT:**

| Feature | Metering CT | Protection CT |
|---------|-------------|---------------|
| Purpose | Accurate measurement | Relay operation during faults |
| Saturation | Should **not saturate** in range | Should **saturate** beyond knee point |
| Accuracy | High (0.2, 0.5) | Lower (5P, 10P) |
| ALF (Accuracy Limit Factor) | Low (1.2 – 1.5) | High (5 – 20) |
| Knee-point voltage | Not critical | Critical |
| Secondary burden | Low (5–15 VA) | Higher (15–30 VA) |
| Behavior at overcurrent | Goes to limit to protect meter | Goes into saturation to protect relay |

> **Why it matters:** A protection CT saturates during a 20× fault to **limit the secondary current** and protect the relay. A metering CT must saturate *before* the meter gets damaged, but must remain accurate **up to 1.2× normal current**.

#### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**What is Maximum Demand and how is it measured using a Maximum Demand Indicator (MDI)?**

**Model Answer:**

**Maximum Demand (MD)** is the highest value of the average power (in kW or kVA) drawn by a consumer over a pre-defined **Demand Integration Period (DIP)**, typically **15 minutes** for HT consumers in India.

**Measurement Method:**
1. The meter continuously computes average kVA over the DIP (sliding or fixed window).
2. At the end of each DIP, the value is compared with the stored peak.
3. The **higher of the two is retained** as the new MD.
4. The MD register is **reset only manually** by authorised utility personnel (typically once per billing cycle).

> **KTU 2024 Note:** Modern static meters store MD with **date and time stamping** for 6 months, allowing reconstruction of the demand profile.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the HT metering system used for an 11 kV industrial consumer. List the IS standards applicable.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 3-phase, 11 kV, 750 kVA industrial consumer has CTs of 100/5 A class 0.5 and PTs of 11000/110 V. The meter reads 4523.456 kVAh at start and 4678.923 kVAh at end of the month. Calculate: (i) CT ratio, (ii) PT ratio, (iii) TMF, (iv) Actual kVAh consumed, and (v) Total CT burden per phase if connecting cable is 40 m long, 4 sq.mm copper, meter burden 3 VA/phase.

#### MODEL SOLUTION

**(a) HT Metering System — Block Diagram Explanation [7 Marks]**

> *[Student should draw the mermaid block diagram from Section 4.1 above — 4 marks]*

**Components Explained (3 marks):**

1. **HT Incoming (11 kV bus):** Source of supply from KSEB substation.
2. **VCB/SF6:** Provides isolation; metering CTs are mounted on the *outgoing side* of VCB.
3. **CTs (Current Transformers):** Mounted on HT side, ratio as per load. Class 0.5 for billing.
4. **PTs (Potential Transformers):** Step down 11000 V to 110 V, fused on HT and LV sides.
5. **Test Terminal Block (TTB):** Allows utility to short-circuit CTs and isolate meter for testing without disconnecting the wiring.
6. **Multi-Function Meter (MFM):** Computes kWh, kVAh, kVArh, MD, PF, V, I, Hz.
7. **MDI (Max Demand Indicator):** Part of MFM in modern meters.
8. **Communication Module:** RS-485 + GPRS for AMR.
9. **Sealing:** All metering components sealed by utility to prevent tampering.

**Applicable IS Standards:**
- **IS 14697** — AC Static Watt-hour Meters
- **IS 2705** — Current Transformers
- **IS 3156** — Voltage Transformers
- **IS 9249** — Drop-out fuses for HT metering
- **CBIP Technical Report No. 88** — Metering guidelines
- **CEA (Installation and Operation of Meters) Regulations, 2006** — Statutory

---

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 Marks]**

**Given:** $S = 750\ kVA$, $V_{L} = 11000\ V$, CT = 100/5 A, PT = 11000/110 V, Cable: 40 m, 4 mm² Cu, Meter burden = 3 VA/phase.

**Step (i): CT Ratio**

$$
N_{CT} = \dfrac{100}{5} = 20
$$

**Step (ii): PT Ratio**

$$
N_{PT} = \dfrac{11000}{110} = 100
$$

**Step (iii): TMF**

$$
TMF = N_{CT} \times N_{PT} = 20 \times 100 = 2000
$$

**Step (iv): Actual kVAh Consumed**

$$
\Delta M = 4678.923 - 4523.456 = 155.467\ kVAh
$$

$$
E_{actual} = 155.467 \times 2000 = 310{,}934\ kVAh = 310.934\ MVAh
$$

**Step (v): Total CT Burden per phase**

Resistance of round-trip cable:

$$
R_{wire} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot 2L}{A} = \dfrac{1.724 \times 10^{-8} \times 2 \times 40}{4 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{1.3792 \times 10^{-6}}{4 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.3448\ \Omega
$$

Wire burden at $I_s = 5\ A$:

$$
B_{wire} = I_{s}^{2} \cdot R_{wire} = 25 \times 0.3448 = 8.62\ VA
$$

Total burden:

$$
B_{total} = B_{meter} + B_{wire} = 3 + 8.62 = 11.62\ VA
$$

**Recommendation:** Select CT with **standard burden of 15 VA** (next standard size above 11.62 VA), Class 0.5, ALF 1.2.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice): [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working principle of a static (electronic) trivector meter with a neat block diagram. List its key features as per IS 14697.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A consumer has a recorded MD of 320 kVA against a contract demand (CD) of 300 kVA. The PF is 0.82 lag. Energy consumed is 1,50,000 kVAh. As per KSERC/KSEB rules, calculate the (i) billing demand, (ii) PF penalty, and (iii) total bill at ₹6.50 per kVAh.

#### MODEL SOLUTION

**(a) Static Trivector Meter — Block Diagram & Working [7 Marks]**

> *[Student should sketch the signal flow diagram from Section 4.2]*

**Working Principle (3 marks):**

1. **Voltage Inputs:** PT secondary voltages (e.g., 63.5 V L-N) are scaled by potential dividers and fed to ADC.
2. **Current Inputs:** CT secondary currents (5 A) pass through shunts; voltage proportional to current is fed to ADC.
3. **Sampling:** High-speed ADC samples both V and I at **>2 kHz** to capture harmonics.
4. **DSP Calculation:** Microprocessor computes:
   - Active Power: $P = \sum v(t) \cdot i(t)$ averaged over cycles
   - Reactive Power: $Q = \sum v(t) \cdot i(t - T/4)$ (90° shifted)
   - Apparent Power: $S = \sqrt{P^{2} + Q^{2}}$
   - Energy: $E = \int P\ dt$ accumulated
5. **Display:** LCD shows all parameters; non-volatile memory stores 60 days of load survey.
6. **Communication:** Optical port + RS-485 for AMR.

**Key Features as per IS 14697 (4 marks):**
- Class 0.2S / 0.5S / 1.0 accuracy
- 4-quadrant measurement
- TOD tariff with 6 time zones
- MD with 15-min DIP, time-stamped
- Load survey (30-min intervals, 60 days)
- Reverse energy detection (anti-tamper)
- Magnetic tamper detection
- Neutral disturbance logging
- Communication: Optical + RS-485 (DLMS/COSEM protocol)
- Memory retention: 3 years without power

---

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 Marks]**

**Step (i): Billing Demand (KSERC Rule)**

$$
MD_{billing} = \max[0.85 \times CD,\ MD_{recorded}]
$$

$$
0.85 \times 300 = 255\ kVA
$$

$$
MD_{billing} = \max[255,\ 320] = 320\ kVA
$$

> **Reason:** Recorded MD (320 kVA) > 85% of CD (255 kVA), so bill for **320 kVA**.

**Step (ii): PF Penalty**

For PF < 0.90 lag, KSEB imposes surcharge at 1% of energy charges per 0.01 below 0.90.

$$
\Delta PF = 0.90 - 0.82 = 0.08
$$

$$
\text{Number of 0.01 steps} = 8
$$

> Surcharge capped at **16%** (for PF ≤ 0.82).

$$
\text{PF Surcharge} = 8 \times 1\% = 8\%
$$

**Step (iii): Total Bill**

Energy charges:

$$
\text{EC} = 1{,}50{,}000 \times 6.50 = \text{₹}\ 9{,}75{,}000
$$

PF surcharge:

$$
\text{PFS} = 8\% \times 9{,}75{,}000 = \text{₹}\ 78{,}000
$$

Demand charges (typical ₹300 per kVA of billing demand):

$$
\text{DC} = 320 \times 300 = \text{₹}\ 96{,}000
$$

Total bill:

$$
\text{Total} = 9{,}75{,}000 + 78{,}000 + 96{,}000 = \text{₹}\ 11{,}49{,}000
$$

> **Total: ₹11.49 Lakhs**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Metering System Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "round-trip" factor (2L):** When computing CT wire burden, students often use single length (L) instead of 2L. This underestimates burden by 50%, leading to **wrong CT selection.** **(-2 marks)**
> 2. **Mixing units:** Using mm² directly in the resistivity formula without converting to m². $\rho = 1.724 \times 10^{-8}\ \Omega\cdot m$, and **A must be in m²**. **(-1 to -2 marks)**
> 3. **Wrong TMF application:** TMF is the *product* of CT ratio and PT ratio. If a student writes only CT ratio, the energy calculation is off by 100×. **(-3 marks)**
> 4. **Forgetting 0.85×CD rule:** Many students compute billing demand = recorded MD directly. They lose the entire 1–2 marks allotted for "billing demand as per regulation."
> 5. **Confusing kWh and kVAh:** For HT consumers in Kerala, billing is on **kVAh**, not kWh. Students writing kWh lose the conceptual mark.
> 6. **Missing the 15-minute DIP:** When asked about MD, students often answer "highest instantaneous load" — this is wrong; it's the *average* over DIP.
> 7. **CT Accuracy Class:** For *revenue metering*, always use **Class 0.5 or better**; for *high-value HT consumers* (>5 MW), use **Class 0.2S**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> **Metering System — Last-Minute Revision (Kerala KTU 2024)**
> - [x] **Metering system = CT + PT + Meter + TTB + Panel** (5 components minimum)
> - [x] **CT secondary** = **5 A** (or 1 A for long runs)
> - [x] **PT secondary** = **110 V** (line-line) or **63.5 V** (line-neutral)
> - [x] **TMF = N_CT × N_PT** — used to convert meter reading to actual energy
> - [x] **CT burden** = $I_s^2 \times (Z_{meter} + Z_{wire} + Z_{contact})$
> - [x] **Round-trip wire length** = **2L** (always!)
> - [x] **Metering CT** → low ALF (1.2–1.5), high accuracy (0.2, 0.5)
> - [x] **Protection CT** → high ALF (5–20), class 5P/10P
> - [x] **MD = Average kVA over 15 min DIP** (sliding/fixed window)
> - [x] **KSERC Billing MD** = max(0.85 × CD, recorded MD)
> - [x] **PF Penalty** = 1% per 0.01 below 0.90, capped at 16%
> - [x] **Standards:** IS 14697 (meter), IS 2705 (CT), IS 3156 (PT), CBIP 88
> - [x] **4-quadrant** measurement is mandatory for net-metering (solar)
> - [x] **TOD tariff** = 6 time zones (peak, normal, off-peak)
> - [x] **Modern meters** = static/electronic, DSP-based, 4Q, with RS-485/AMR
> - [x] **Sealing by utility** is mandatory — tampering is a criminal offence (Sec 135 of Electricity Act 2003)
> - [x] **Kerala** specific — billing is on **kVAh** for HT, not kWh
> - [x] **Trivector** = measures kWh + kVAh + kVArh simultaneously
> - [x] **For high-current long runs**, use **1 A CTs** to reduce cable burden by 25×

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basics of Star Rating and Labelling, Principle of Operation of Fuse
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEOT752 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Basics of Star Rating and Labelling, Principle of Operation of Fuse # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Basics of Star Rating and Labelling ### Formal Definition **Star Rating and Labelling Programme** is a regulatory energy-efficiency certification scheme implemented by the **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE)** under the **Ministry of Power, Government of India**, established under the *Energy Conservation Act, 2001*. The programme assigns a graded efficiency label (typically **1 to 5 stars**, with 5 stars indicating the highest energy efficiency) to specific electrical appliances and equipment, allowing consumers to make informed, energy-conscious purchase decisions. The label displays key metrics such as **Annual Energy Consumption (AEC)** in kWh, the **BEE Star Rating**, the **brand & model**, and the **ISEER (Indian Seasonal Energy Efficiency Ratio)** for air conditioners. > [!IMPORTANT] > The star rating is purely **relative** — it is computed against a baseline efficiency value fixed by BEE for that appliance category and updated every 1–2 years. A 5-star rated refrigerator today is far more efficient than a 5-star refrigerator certified in 2015 because the baseline threshold has been tightened. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are buying a school bag. Two bags look identical, but one is stitched with thick, durable fabric (heavy-duty, lasts long) while the other is made of thin, cheap material (wears out fast). Without opening them, how do you know? A **label tag** would solve this — it could say *"Durability: 5/5 stars"* on one and *"Durability: 2/5 stars"* on the other. The **BEE Star Label** does exactly this for electrical appliances. It is a government-issued "energy durability tag" that tells you, at a glance, how much electricity the appliance will gulp down during a year of use. More stars = less electricity consumed = lower electricity bill + smaller carbon footprint. ### Key Stakeholders & Governing Bodies - **BEE (Bureau of Energy Efficiency)** — Apex body for the programme. - **Ministry of Power** — Parent ministry. - **BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards)** — Defines the testing standards (IS 8148, IS 1391, etc.). - **NABL-accredited labs** — Perform the actual energy-consumption tests. ### Appliances Currently Covered Under the Mandatory BEE Label | Appliance | Mandatory/Voluntary | Standard Metric Used | |---|---|---| | Frost-Free Refrigerator | Mandatory | kWh/year | | Direct-Cool Refrigerator | Mandatory | kWh/year | | Room Air Conditioner | Mandatory | ISEER | | Tubular Fluorescent Lamp | Voluntary | Lumens/Watt | | LED Lamp | Mandatory | Lumens/Watt | | Ceiling Fan | Voluntary | Service Value (m³/min/W) | | Distribution Transformer | Mandatory | Max Losses at 50% & 100% load | | Monoset Pump | Voluntary | Pump Efficiency | | Computer/Laptop | Voluntary | kWh/year | > [!NOTE] > The KTU 2024 syllabus focuses specifically on the **concept and importance** of the star rating system as part of energy-efficient electrical design. Numerical rating calculations for individual appliances are NOT required — only the underlying principle and label interpretation. --- ## 1.2 Principle of Operation of Fuse ### Formal Definition A **Fuse** is the **simplest, oldest, and most economical** form of overcurrent protective device used in low-voltage electrical installations. It consists of a **fusible element** (a calibrated metal wire or strip) that melts and physically breaks the circuit when the current flowing through it exceeds a predetermined safe value for a specified duration — thereby protecting the downstream wiring, equipment, and personnel from the destructive effects of overcurrent, short circuits, and sustained overloads. The element is housed inside a **fuse body** (carrier/base) made of ceramic, glass, or molded bakelite, with **end caps (ferrules)** providing the electrical contact points. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of a fuse as a **"sacrificial weak link"** in a chain pulling a heavy load. If the load suddenly becomes too heavy, the weak link snaps first — saving all the other (expensive) links from breaking. The fuse is that weak link: it deliberately blows itself up to save the rest of the circuit. Another analogy: A fuse is like a **fire alarm that also carries a small fire extinguisher**. When things get dangerous (overcurrent), the alarm (melting) goes off AND takes action (breaks the circuit) automatically — no human intervention needed. ### Why Fuses Are Used (Engineering Need) - **Short-circuit protection** — Limit let-through energy ($I^2t$). - **Overload protection** — Interrupt sustained currents above rated value. - **Discrimination (selectivity)** — The fuse nearest the fault blows first. - **Cost-effective** — Cheaper than circuit breakers for many applications. - **No maintenance** — No moving parts, no arc-quenching mechanism to service. - **Fail-safe** — Always disconnects on failure; cannot be "stuck closed." > [!IMPORTANT] > A fuse is a **one-time device** — once it blows, it must be **replaced**, not reset. This is its biggest disadvantage compared to a **Miniature Circuit Breaker (MCB)**, which can be reset by flipping a switch. In modern residential/commercial installations, MCBs have largely replaced rewirable fuses, but **HRC (High Rupturing Capacity) cartridge fuses** are still standard in industrial panels and distribution transformers. ### Standard Fuse Ratings Used in Indian Practice - **Low-voltage domestic (rewirable):** 6 A, 16 A, 32 A, 63 A (for 230 V single-phase). - **HRC cartridge (DIN-type):** 2 A to 630 A in standardized sizes (NH00, NH1, NH2, NH3). - **High-voltage drop-out fuses (DOF):** Used up to 33 kV distribution networks. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Star Rating: Theoretical Framework ### 2.1.1 How the Star Rating Is Computed For every appliance category, BEE defines a **reference benchmark efficiency** (e.g., EER, ISEER, kWh/year). Manufacturers submit their product samples to a **NABL-accredited lab**, where the appliance is tested under standardized conditions. The measured efficiency is then compared against the BEE-defined **minimum star threshold** and **maximum star threshold**. **The Star Rating Logic (generalized):** $$ \text{Star} = 1 + 4 \times \left( \frac{\eta_{\text{measured}} - \eta_{\min}}{\eta_{\max} - \eta_{\min}} \right) $$ Where: - $\eta_{\text{measured}}$ = measured efficiency of the submitted appliance - $\eta_{\min}$ = efficiency threshold for 1-star (least efficient allowed) - $\eta_{\max}$ = efficiency threshold for 5-star (most efficient allowed) - The result is **rounded down** to the nearest integer star. > [!NOTE] > The actual BEE formula is appliance-specific and may use **logarithmic** or **piecewise linear** interpolation depending on the equipment class. The above is a **simplified pedagogical form** sufficient for KTU-level understanding. ### 2.1.2 Important Metrics on the BEE Label | Metric | Full Form | Used For | Higher Means | |---|---|---|---| | **ISEER** | Indian Seasonal Energy Efficiency Ratio | Air Conditioners | More efficient ✓ | | **EER** | Energy Efficiency Ratio | Older ACs, Refrigerators | More efficient ✓ | | **AEC** | Annual Energy Consumption | Refrigerators | Less consumption ✓ (so lower is better!) | | **COP** | Coefficient of Performance | Heat pumps, ACs (heating mode) | More efficient ✓ | | **Service Value** | Air delivery per Watt | Ceiling Fans | More efficient ✓ | | **Lumens/Watt** | Luminous efficacy | LED/Fluorescent lamps | More efficient ✓ | ### 2.1.3 Significance in Electrical System Design For an electrical system designer, the star rating matters in three ways: 1. **Load estimation** — A 5-star refrigerator of 300 L capacity may consume ~190 kWh/year vs. a 2-star consuming ~400 kWh/year. This drastically affects the **connected load** and **diversity factor** calculations. 2. **Cable sizing** — Lower-rated appliances mean lower steady-state current, allowing smaller conductor cross-sections. 3. **Energy audit (BEE-certified Energy Managers)** — Star-rated equipment is mandatory in **ECBC (Energy Conservation Building Code)** compliant buildings — a topic relevant for KTU's energy management module. --- ## 2.2 Fuse: Deep Theoretical Analysis ### 2.2.1 Operating Principle — The Melting Law The fuse operates on the **Joule Heating Principle** (also called the **$I^2t$ melting law**). The heat generated in the fusible element due to current flow is: $$ H = I^2 R t \quad \text{(Joules)} $$ Where: - $I$ = RMS current through the element (Amperes) - $R$ = resistance of the element (Ohms) - $t$ = duration of current flow (seconds) This heat raises the element's temperature. If the **$I^2t$ value exceeds the element's melting $I^2t$** (a property fixed by the element material, geometry, and cross-section), the element **melts and vaporizes**, creating an **electric arc** that is then extinguished by the surrounding filler material (in HRC fuses), thereby opening the circuit. ### 2.2.2 Time-Current Characteristic (TCC) A fuse has a **highly inverse time-current characteristic** — the higher the fault current, the faster it blows. This is described by the empirical relation: $$ t = \frac{k \cdot S^2}{I^2} $$ Where: - $t$ = pre-arcing time (seconds) - $S$ = cross-sectional area of the fusible element - $I$ = fault current (Amperes) - $k$ = material constant (depends on element material — silver, copper, zinc) - This is the famous **"Melting Law of Wires"** discovered by W. H. Preece (1889) and refined by I. O. W. R. Bregman. > [!IMPORTANT] > A fuse does **NOT** blow the instant current exceeds its rated value. It can carry **up to 1.6 × rated current for 1 hour** without blowing (a property called the **conventional non-fusing current**). It **must** blow within 1 hour at **1.9 × rated current** (the **conventional fusing current**). ### 2.2.3 Fusing Factor $$ \text{Fusing Factor} = \frac{\text{Minimum Fusing Current}}{\text{Rated Current}} $$ For a typical semi-enclosed (rewirable) fuse, the fusing factor lies between **1.2 and 1.5** — meaning the fuse may carry up to 150% of its rated current before blowing. This is why **HRC fuses (fusing factor ≈ 1.1–1.2)** are preferred for sensitive equipment. ### 2.2.4 Pre-Arcing $I^2t$ and Let-Through $I^2t$ - **Pre-arcing $I^2t$** — Heat energy required to melt the element. - **Total $I^2t$** (or **let-through $I^2t$**) — Includes the energy dissipated in the arc before complete extinction. The **total $I^2t$** of the fuse must be **less than the $I^2t$ withstand** of the downstream cable and equipment to ensure **discrimination**. ### 2.2.5 HRC (High Rupturing Capacity) Fuses HRC fuses are filled with **quartz sand (SiO₂)** surrounding the silver element. When the element melts: 1. The metallic vapour reacts with the sand, forming **non-conductive silicates**. 2. The arc is rapidly deionized and extinguished within **milliseconds**. 3. The fuse can safely break fault currents of **80 kA to 120 kA** at 415 V — far beyond what a rewirable fuse can handle. ### 2.2.6 Fuse Element Materials | Material | Properties | Typical Use | |---|---|---| | **Silver (Ag)** | Best conductivity, predictable melting, oxide-free | HRC cartridge fuses | | **Copper (Cu)** | Good conductivity, oxidizes → tin-lead coating required | Rewirable fuses | | **Zinc (Zn)** | Stable at low temps, predictable | Old-style "S-type" fuse links | | **Tin (Sn)** | Low melting point (232°C) | Often used as **M-effect** alloy in HRC | The **"M-effect" (Metallurgical effect)**: A small blob of tin is spot-welded onto a silver element. During overload, the tin melts first and dissolves into the silver, creating a low-melting eutectic alloy that triggers fusing at a much lower temperature — giving a **delayed yet precise** characteristic. ### 2.2.7 Comparison: Fuse vs. MCB vs. MCCB | Feature | Rewirable Fuse | HRC Fuse | MCB | MCCB | |---|---|---|---|---| | Reusability | ❌ Replace element | ❌ Replace cartridge | ✅ Switch reset | ✅ Switch reset | | Breaking capacity | Low (~2–4 kA) | Very high (~100 kA) | Medium (~10 kA) | Very high (~50 kA) | | Cost | Very low | Medium | Medium | High | | Discrimination | Poor | Excellent | Good | Excellent | | Trip characteristics | Fixed by element | Precisely engineered | Adjustable (B, C, D curves) | Fully adjustable | | KTU exam relevance | High (basics) | High (industrial) | Medium (modern context) | Low (out of syllabus) | ### 2.2.8 Real-World Applications - **Domestic consumer units** — Rewirable / MCB. - **Distribution transformer secondary** — HRC NH-type fuses. - **Motor starters** — HRC fuses for short-circuit back-up to contactors. - **Semiconductor protection** — Ultra-fast aR-class fuses. - **Automotive** — Blade fuses (ATO, ATC) rated 1 A to 80 A. - **33 kV feeders** — **Drop-Out Fuses (DOF)** — visible isolation + protection combined. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Symbol & Units | Engineering Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Heat generated in element | $H = I^2 R t$ [J] | Fuse operates when $H \geq H_{\text{melt}}$ | | 2 | Preece's Melting Law | $t = \dfrac{k S^2}{I^2}$ [s] | Time $\propto S^2$, Time $\propto 1/I^2$ | | 3 | Fusing Factor | $FF = \dfrac{I_{\text{min-fuse}}}{I_{\text{rated}}}$ | Lower = better protection quality | | 4 | Conventional non-fusing current | $1.6 \times I_{\text{rated}}$ | Fuse can carry this for 1 h | | 5 | Conventional fusing current | $1.9 \times I_{\text{rated}}$ | Fuse must blow within 1 h | | 6 | Star rating (linear form) | $\text{Star} = 1 + 4 \cdot \dfrac{\eta_{\text{meas}} - \eta_{\min}}{\eta_{\max} - \eta_{\min}}$ | Rounded down to integer | | 7 | Star rating (savings example) | $\% \text{Savings} = \dfrac{AEC_{2\star} - AEC_{5\star}}{AEC_{2\star}} \times 100$ | KTU numerics favourite | | 8 | Annual operating cost | $C = AEC \times \text{unit rate}$ [₹/yr] | Used in energy-audit problems | | 9 | Let-through energy | $E_{\text{let-through}} = \int_0^{t_{\text{arc}}} i^2 \, dt$ [A²s] | Must be $< I^2t$ of downstream | | 10 | Payback period for 5★ appliance | $T = \dfrac{\text{Extra cost}}{\text{Annual savings}}$ [years] | Justification for star-rated purchase | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Preece's Law of Fuse Melting We derive the empirical relation governing the pre-arcing time of a fusible element from first principles of thermal energy balance. ### Step 1 — Heat generated in the element For a resistive element of resistance $R$ carrying RMS current $I$ for time $t$, the Joule heat dissipated is: $$ H = I^2 R t $$ ### Step 2 — Heat required to raise element to melting point Let the element have: - Mass = $m$ [kg] - Specific heat capacity = $c$ [J/kg·°C] - Initial temperature = $T_0$ [°C] - Melting point = $T_m$ [°C] - Latent heat of fusion = $L_f$ [J/kg] The total heat required to bring the element from $T_0$ to $T_m$ and **fully melt** it is: $$ H_{\text{melt}} = m c (T_m - T_0) + m L_f $$ ### Step 3 — Mass expressed in terms of geometry For a cylindrical element of length $\ell$, cross-sectional area $S$, and density $\rho$: $$ m = \rho \cdot S \cdot \ell $$ Substituting: $$ H_{\text{melt}} = \rho S \ell \, c (T_m - T_0) + \rho S \ell \, L_f $$ ### Step 4 — Express resistance in terms of geometry For the same element with resistivity $\rho_e$ (note: using $\rho_e$ to avoid clash with density): $$ R = \frac{\rho_e \ell}{S} $$ ### Step 5 — Equate generated heat to melting heat The fuse blows exactly when $H = H_{\text{melt}}$: $$ I^2 \cdot \frac{\rho_e \ell}{S} \cdot t = \rho S \ell \, c (T_m - T_0) + \rho S \ell \, L_f $$ ### Step 6 — Solve for $t$ Divide both sides by $\ell$ (it cancels — meaning length is not a primary variable): $$ \frac{I^2 \rho_e t}{S} = \rho S \left[ c (T_m - T_0) + L_f \right] $$ Rearranging for $t$: $$ t = \frac{\rho S^2}{I^2 \rho_e} \left[ c (T_m - T_0) + L_f \right] $$ ### Step 7 — Group constants into a single material constant $k$ Defining: $$ k = \frac{\rho \left[ c (T_m - T_0) + L_f \right]}{\rho_e} $$ The famous Preece relation emerges: $$ \boxed{\, t = \frac{k \cdot S^2}{I^2} \,} $$ ### Step 8 — Interpretation of the result - $t \propto S^2$ → **Doubling the cross-section quadruples the pre-arcing time** (fuse can carry higher current or sustain for longer). - $t \propto 1/I^2$ → **Doubling the fault current reduces pre-arcing time to 1/4** — this is the "highly inverse" protective characteristic. - The constant $k$ is **empirically adjusted** by manufacturers to account for skin effect, radiation losses, and the M-effect. --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical — Star Rating Interpretation **Problem:** A 1.5-ton split AC has a measured ISEER of 3.10. BEE defines 1-star threshold ISEER = 2.70 and 5-star threshold ISEER = 3.50. Find the star rating.
