# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**State any three general requirements that an electrical installation in a residential building must satisfy as per IS 732.**

**Model Answer:**

> The three cardinal general requirements per IS 732:2019 are:
> 1. **Safety of personnel** from electric shock — ensured by RCCB (30 mA), double insulation, and earthing.
> 2. **Adequacy of capacity** — the installation must carry the present load plus 20% future load without exceeding the temperature rating of conductors.
> 3. **Voltage drop limitation** — total voltage drop from meter to farthest load must not exceed **4%** of the nominal supply voltage (230 V or 415 V).

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between an RCCB and an MCB. Why is an RCCB mandatory in residential installations?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | MCB (Miniature Circuit Breaker) | RCCB (Residual Current Circuit Breaker) |
|---|---|---|
| Function | Overload + short-circuit protection | Earth-leakage / residual current protection |
| Operates on | Thermal + magnetic trip | Sum of currents in phase and neutral ≠ 0 |
| Sensitivity | Several amps to kiloamps | Typically 30 mA (residential), 100/300 mA (mains) |
| Does not protect against | Earth leakage | Overload / short circuit |

> **RCCB is mandatory** in residential installations per IS 732:2019 and CEA Measures 2010 because **a human body comes into contact with a live conductor during a fault**, and at 230 V, a current as low as 30 mA through the body for > 40 ms can cause **ventricular fibrillation** (lethal). The MCB alone does NOT detect small earth-leakage currents.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / May 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the **general requirements for electrical installations in a residential building** as per IS 4648 and IS 732, covering: (i) service connection and metering, (ii) distribution boards and final sub-circuits, (iii) protective devices. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A single-phase 230 V residential load of **3 kW** at **0.85 power factor lagging** is to be fed through a Cu cable from a sub-distribution board. The cable run length is **40 m**. Determine: (i) the design current, (ii) the minimum cable size to limit voltage drop to **3%**, (iii) the appropriate MCB rating. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**1. Service Connection and Metering:**
- KSEB LT service at **230 V, 50 Hz, 1-phase, 2-wire**.
- Overhead service cable (2C × 6 mm² Al XLPE, weather-proof) from service pole to building.
- **Main Service Cut-Out (HRC fuse)** at the point of entry — KSEB sealed.
- **Energy meter** in a meter box at **1.5 m to 1.8 m above ground level**.
- Earth electrode pit within 1 m of meter for body earth.

**2. Distribution Boards:**
- **Main Distribution Board (MDB)** at the entrance lobby or meter room.
- **Sub-Distribution Boards (SDBs)** at strategic points — one per floor for a two-storey house.
- MDB height: **1.5 m to 2.0 m** above floor level.
- Each board has an **isolator + RCCB (63 A, 30 mA) + MCBs**.
- ECC (Earth Continuity Conductor) bus bar of Cu inside the board.

**3. Final Sub-Circuits (per IS 4648):**
- **Lighting circuit:** Max 10 points, total 800 W, **1.5 mm² Cu**, 6 A MCB.
- **Fan circuit:** Max 10 fans, **1.5 mm² Cu**, 6 A MCB.
- **5 A socket circuit:** Max 6 sockets, **1.5 mm² Cu**, 6 A MCB.
- **15 A socket circuit:** Max 2 sockets, **2.5 mm² Cu**, 16 A MCB.
- **AC / Geyser circuit:** 1 point, **4 mm² Cu**, 20–25 A MCB.
- **Spare:** Minimum 20% of total ways.

**4. Protective Devices (chain):**
- Service cut-out → Isolator MCB (50/63 A) → RCCB (63 A, 30 mA) → MCBs (per circuit) → Load.
- **Earth-fault loop impedance check:** $Z_s \leq U_0 / I_a$ where $I_a$ = MCB trip current at 0.4 s.

**5. Earthing:**
- **IS 3043:2018** compliant earth pit (GI pipe 40 mm dia × 2.5 m, charcoal + salt).
- **Earth resistance ≤ 5 Ω** for residential LT.
- **Two-pole earthing** — one for body, one for neutral (where applicable).

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Design current:**

$$I_b = \dfrac{P}{V \times \cos\phi} = \dfrac{3000}{230 \times 0.85} = 15.35 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2 — Allowed voltage drop:**

$$\Delta V_{\text{allowed}} = \dfrac{3}{100} \times 230 = 6.9 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Minimum cable area from VD formula (1-φ):**

$$A_{\min} = \dfrac{2 \times I_b \times L \times \rho \times \cos\phi}{\Delta V_{\text{allowed}}}$$

$$A_{\min} = \dfrac{2 \times 15.35 \times 40 \times 0.0225 \times 0.85}{6.9}$$

$$A_{\min} = \dfrac{23.49}{6.9} = 3.40 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 4 — Select next standard Cu size:** **$A = 4 \text{ mm}^2$**.

**Step 5 — Verify ampacity:**
From IS 3961, 4 mm² Cu 2-core PVC, installation method B1: $I_z = 32$ A (30 °C).
Derated for 45 °C ambient: $I_{z,\text{derated}} = 32 \times 0.87 = 27.8$ A.

**Coordination check (IS 732):**

$$I_z (27.8) \geq I_n (20\,\text{A MCB}) \geq I_b (15.35\,\text{A}) \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 6 — MCB rating selection:** **20 A Type-C MCB** (next standard above $I_b$).

**Step 7 — Voltage drop verification with selected size:**

$$\Delta V_{\text{actual}} = \dfrac{2 \times 15.35 \times 40 \times 0.0225 \times 0.85}{4} = 5.87 \text{ V} \quad (2.55\,\%) \quad \checkmark$$

**Final Selection:**

| Component | Specification |
|---|---|
| Cable | 4 mm² Cu, 2-core + ECC (1.5 mm² Cu), PVC insulated, armoured |
| Conduit | 25 mm PVC rigid |
| MCB | 20 A, Type-C, 10 kA, 250 V AC |
| RCCB (upstream) | 63 A, 30 mA, 4-pole (in 1-φ mode) |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Compare the **general requirements of electrical installations in commercial and high-rise buildings**, with at least **six (6) points of distinction**. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A high-rise commercial complex has a **total connected load of 850 kVA** at **0.9 power factor**, with a **standby essential load of 250 kVA** at 0.8 power factor. Determine: (i) the **HT supply arrangement**, (ii) the **transformer(s) sizing** (N+1 redundancy), (iii) the **DG set rating**, and (iv) the **main bus-duct current rating** at 415 V. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Comparative Table (six points, expanded for 7 marks):**

1. **Supply Voltage:** Commercial: 415 V 3-φ directly from KSEB. High-rise: 11 kV HT incoming with own substation (load > 500 kVA).

2. **Earthing:** Commercial: ≤ 2 Ω with 3–4 earth pits. High-rise: ≤ 1 Ω using **earth mat** of 50 × 6 mm GI flat in 1 m × 1 m grid plus multiple rod electrodes.

3. **Standby Power:** Commercial: UPS for IT loads. High-rise: Full **DG set** (250 kVA to 2 MVA) with AMF/ATS and synchronizing panel if multiple DGs.

4. **Wiring Method:** Commercial: PVC conduit + cable tray on surface or underfloor. High-rise: **Bus-duct rising mains** in dedicated fire-rated vertical shaft; cable trays in ceiling voids.

5. **Fire Survival:** Commercial: Standard XLPE cables. High-rise: **2-hour fire-survival cables** (mica-taped) for fire pumps, lifts, emergency lighting per NBC 2016.

6. **Lightning Protection:** Commercial: Required if building height > 15 m. High-rise: **Mandatory ESE / Franklin system** per IS/IEC 62305 with down conductors and dedicated earth pits.

7. **Metering & Monitoring:** Commercial: Multi-tenant meters. High-rise: **Centralized BMS** with SCADA, smart meters (Modbus), power-quality analyzers.

8. **Approval:** Commercial: CEIG (if > 5 kW). High-rise: CEIG + Fire Department + Local Municipality + Lift Inspector.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given Data:**
- Total connected load: 850 kVA at 0.9 PF
- Essential load: 250 kVA at 0.8 PF
- Diversity factor: 0.8 (assumed)

**Step 1 — HT supply arrangement:**
- Load > 500 kVA → **Dedicated 11 kV HT supply from KSEB** with HT metering cubicle.
- **HT VCB panel: 11 kV, 630 A, 25 kA short-circuit rating**, with single incoming + metering + two outgoing (one per transformer).

**Step 2 — Maximum demand after diversity:**

$$\text{MD} = 850 \times 0.8 = 680 \text{ kVA}$$

**Step 3 — Transformer sizing (N+1 redundancy):**

Use **2 transformers**, each rated for ≥ 100% of the diversified load:

$$\text{Per transformer rating} \geq 680 \text{ kVA}$$

Standard sizes: 750 kVA, 1000 kVA, 1250 kVA, 1500 kVA.

**Select 2 × 1000 kVA, 11 kV / 433 V, Dyn11, ONAN** (with N+1 redundancy, the system can supply full load with one transformer out, since 1 × 1000 > 680).

Optional alternative: 2 × 750 kVA (load sharing 340 kVA each — closer to 50% loading which is efficient; but 1 × 750 < 680, so N+1 fails). Therefore **2 × 1000 kVA is the safe selection**.

**Step 4 — DG set rating (essential load only):**

$$kW_{\text{essential}} = kVA_{\text{essential}} \times \cos\phi = 250 \times 0.8 = 200 \text{ kW}$$

Apply 25% margin:

$$kW_{\text{DG}} = 1.25 \times 200 = 250 \text{ kW}$$

DG rating in kVA at 0.8 PF:

$$kVA_{\text{DG}} = \dfrac{250}{0.8} = 312.5 \text{ kVA}$$

**Select standard: 320 kVA DG set** (Caterpillar / Kirloskar / Mahindra Powerol).

**Step 5 — Main bus-duct current at 415 V:**

Total diversified current:

$$I_{\text{MDB}} = \dfrac{kVA_{\text{total}} \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \times 415} = \dfrac{850 \times 1000}{1.732 \times 415}$$

$$I_{\text{MDB}} = \dfrac{850000}{718.78} = 1182.5 \text{ A}$$

Apply 25% future-load margin:

$$I_{\text{bus-duct}} = 1.25 \times 1182.5 = 1478 \text{ A}$$

**Select standard 1600 A TP + N bus duct** (Schneider / Legrand / C&S).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing ELCB with RCCB** — older IS 732 used "ELCB". Since 2019, the correct term is **RCCB**. Examiners deduct 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting the "2" in 1-phase VD formula** — the loop length is 2L. Many students write L. Cost: 1 mark.
> 3. **Not applying ambient temperature derating** for Kerala (45 °C). Cost: 1 mark.
> 4. **Wrong standard conductor size** — selecting 3.5 mm² instead of 4 mm². Always use IS standard: 1.5, 2.5, 4, 6, 10, 16, 25, 35, 50, 70, 95, 120 mm².
> 5. **Ignoring power factor** in VD calculation for inductive loads (motors, ACs). Cost: 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Missing earth-fault loop impedance check** — this is a frequently asked sub-part.
> 7. **N+1 redundancy error** — selecting two transformers each of 340 kVA instead of 1000 kVA. Each transformer must individually carry the full diversified load.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> **Memorize the following bullet list before entering the exam hall:**

- ☐ **Standards to recall:** IS 732:2019, IS 4648:1968, IS 3043:2018, IS 3961, IS/IEC 62305, NEC 2011, NBC 2016, CEA 2010.
- ☐ **Standard supplies:** 230 V 1-φ, 415 V 3-φ, 11 kV HT (Kerala/KSEB).
- ☐ **Voltage drop limit:** ≤ 4% total (IS 732) — 2% service + 2% internal preferred.
- ☐ **Standard Cu conductor sizes (mm²):** 1.5, 2.5, 4, 6, 10, 16, 25, 35, 50, 70, 95, 120.
- ☐ **Resistivity to use in KTU problems:** Cu = 0.0225 Ω·mm²/m; Al = 0.036 Ω·mm²/m.
- ☐ **RCCB mandatory sensitivity:** 30 mA (residential/final), 100/300 mA (mains).
- ☐ **Earth resistance limits:** ≤ 1 Ω HT, ≤ 5 Ω LT.
- ☐ **Insulation resistance:** ≥ 1 MΩ per kV (≥ 50 MΩ for 230 V domestic).
- ☐ **Ambient temperature for Kerala:** 45 °C (derating factor ≈ 0.87 for PVC).
- ☐ **1-phase VD formula:** $\Delta V = \dfrac{2 I L \rho \cos\phi}{A}$. **3-phase VD formula:** $\Delta V = \dfrac{\sqrt{3} I L \rho \cos\phi}{A}$.
- ☐ **Earth-fault loop impedance limit:** $Z_s \leq U_0 / I_a$ where $I_a$ = MCB trip current at 0.4 s.
- ☐ **Final sub-circuit rules (IS 4648):** Lighting 800 W / 10 points; Power 1 kW / 2 sockets.
- ☐ **Spare capacity rule:** ≥ 20% residential, 25% commercial, 30% high-rise.
- ☐ **High-rise threshold:** ≥ 15 m (NBC), ≥ 24 m (CEIG special), fire survival cables = 2 h rating.
- ☐ **DG sizing formula:** kVA_DG = 1.25 × kW_essential / 0.8.
- ☐ **Coordination check:** $I_z \geq I_n \geq I_b$ (cable ampacity ≥ MCB rating ≥ design current).
- ☐ **Earth mat for high-rise:** 50 × 6 mm GI flat in 1 m × 1 m grid at substation plinth.
- ☐ **Rising mains:** Bus duct in dedicated fire-rated shaft, tap-off at each floor.
- ☐ **TN-S earthing:** Separate neutral and body earth electrodes; equipotential bonding of all metal services.
- ☐ **Lightning protection threshold:** Height > 15 m → mandatory per IS/IEC 62305.
- ☐ **N+1 transformer redundancy:** Each transformer must carry the full diversified MD independently.

---

> **End of Module 2 Notes — General Requirements for Electrical Installations**
> *Next Module Preview: Module 3 — Design of Electrical Installations for Small Industries / Public Buildings / Commercial Complexes.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Method of Load Survey for Electrical Installation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT752 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Method of Load Survey for Electrical Installation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Load Survey** is a systematic, methodical, and structured process of identifying, quantifying, classifying, and recording every electrical load present within a proposed or existing electrical installation. It forms the **foundational design input** upon which the entire electrical system — from the selection of conductor cross-sectional areas, protective device ratings, transformer sizing, bus-bar capacities, and voltage-drop calculations to the layout of distribution boards — is engineered.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with **IS 732:2019 (Code of Practice for Electrical Wiring Installations)** and the **National Electrical Code (NEC)**, the load survey is the very first technical deliverable of the design engineer. It is the *quantitative bridge* between an architect's floor plan and an electrical contractor's bill of materials (BoM).

Mathematically, a load survey seeks to establish the **Connected Load (CL)** of the installation, which is then transformed, via correction factors, into the **Maximum Demand (MD)**:

$$MD = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(CL_i \times DF_i \times \text{Utilization Factor}_i)}{1000} \quad \text{(in kW or kVA)}$$

where $DF_i$ is the **Demand Factor** of the $i^{th}$ load group, $CL_i$ is the connected load of the $i^{th}$ load group in watts, and the summation runs over all $n$ identified load groups.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The load survey is the *prerequisite* for every subsequent design activity in this module. Skipping or shortcutting the load survey is the single most common cause of undersized installations in KTU student projects.

> [!NOTE]
> **Distinction to memorize for 3-mark questions:** *Connected Load* = sum of nameplate ratings of all appliances. *Maximum Demand* = the actual load that the system is expected to supply simultaneously after applying diversity and demand factors.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Bucket" Approach

Imagine you are planning a wedding banquet dinner for 500 guests. The caterer doesn't simply pour 500 individual plates of food onto one giant tray — that would be wasteful and chaotic. Instead, the caterer asks:
1. *How many guests prefer vegetarian versus non-vegetarian?* → **Load classification**
2. *What is the average appetite of each guest?* → **Diversity factor**
3. *Will everyone eat rice, roti, and dessert all at the exact same moment?* → **Demand factor**
4. *How many cooking burners must run simultaneously?* → **Maximum demand**

The caterer is, in effect, conducting a **load survey** of the dining hall. The electrical design engineer does the *exact same thing* for a building, except the "appetite" is measured in **watts (W)** and **volt-amperes (VA)**, and the "cooking burners" are circuit breakers, cables, and transformers.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Memory Aid:** "C.L.E.A.R." — **C**lassify loads → **L**ist ratings → **E**stimate diversity → **A**pply demand factor → **R**ecord maximum demand. This 5-step mnemonic is a KTU favourite.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants and units are used universally in KTU-evaluated load survey problems:

| Constant / Metric | Symbol | Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Standard domestic supply voltage (India) | $V_{LL}$ | **415 V** (3-phase) or **240 V** (1-phase) |
| Standard residential phase-to-neutral voltage | $V_{LN}$ | **230 V ± 10%** |
| Power factor — incandescent lamp | $\cos \phi$ | **1.0** (unity) |
| Power factor — fluorescent / LED | $\cos \phi$ | **0.85 – 0.95** |
| Power factor — motors (general purpose) | $\cos \phi$ | **0.75 – 0.85** |
| Power factor — air-conditioners | $\cos \phi$ | **0.80 – 0.90** |
| Power factor — IT / data loads | $\cos \phi$ | **0.95 – 1.0** |
| Standard reference frequency | $f$ | **50 Hz** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Objectives of Conducting a Load Survey

The load survey is performed to satisfy **seven critical engineering objectives**:

1. **Quantification of Total Connected Load** — Establishes the sum of nameplate ratings of every appliance, luminaire, motor, and outlet.
2. **Determination of Maximum Demand** — After applying diversity and demand factors, yields the realistic peak load the system must serve.
3. **Voltage Drop Verification** — Provides the current values to be substituted into the voltage-drop equation $\Delta V = I \cdot R \cdot L$ to ensure compliance with the **5% (lighting) / 10% (power)** IS 732 limits.
4. **Protective Device Selection** — MCB/MCCB ratings are chosen as $\geq 1.25 \times$ circuit design current.
5. **Conductor Sizing** — Cross-sectional area of cables depends directly on the design current.
6. **Earthing and Neutral Sizing** — Neutral conductor must carry the maximum unbalanced load, which the load survey explicitly reveals.
7. **Cost Estimation and Energy Auditing** — Enables accurate BoM generation and subsequent annual energy forecasting.

## 2.2 Classification of Electrical Loads

Loads are classified along **three orthogonal axes** in a KTU design report:

### Axis 1 — By Nature of Consumption
- **Lighting Loads** (resistive, unity power factor) — LED panels, fluorescent tubes, incandescent lamps, downlighters.
- **Heating Loads** (resistive, unity power factor) — geysers, ovens, toasters, irons, space heaters.
- **Motor Loads** (inductive, lagging power factor) — lifts, escalators, water pumps, AHU fans, compressors.
- **Electronic Loads** (non-linear, harmonic-generating) — computers, servers, UPS, SMPS, LED drivers, televisions.

### Axis 2 — By Priority and Criticality
- **Essential / Life-Safety Loads** — Fire pumps, smoke exhaust, emergency lighting, ICU equipment. Backed by DG/IPS.
- **Important Loads** — Lifts, data centres, server rooms. May be on standby power.
- **General / Non-Essential Loads** — Office fans, corridor lighting, pantry equipment. Tripped during DG operation.

### Axis 3 — By Spatial Distribution
- **Floor-wise grouping** — Each floor is surveyed as a sub-distribution zone.
- **Function-wise grouping** — HVAC loads separated from lighting loads for independent metering.

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Methodology of a Load Survey

The KTU-recognised **7-stage load survey methodology** is as follows:

1. **Obtain Architectural Drawings** — Floor plans, elevations, reflected ceiling plans (RCP), and site plans.
2. **Identify Rooms and Functional Zones** — Living room, bedroom, kitchen, lobby, AHU room, server room, parking, etc.
3. **Determine Lighting Layout per Room** — Lux level as per IS 3646, luminaire type, mounting height, and lumens per fixture.
4. **Identify Socket-Outlet Positions** — 6A and 16A outlets as per IS 732 conventions.
5. **Identify Fixed Equipment** — AC units, geysers, pumps, lifts, exhaust fans.
6. **Assign Wattage per Item** — Using manufacturer data, IS standards, or thumb rules.
7. **Tabulate and Sum to get Connected Load** — Categorized by phase, by floor, and by switchboard.

## 2.4 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **definitive formula sheet** for any KTU Part-B question on this topic. Memorize the boundary conditions and units in **bold**.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Units | Key Boundary / Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Connected Load (per appliance) | $P_i = V \times I_i \times \cos \phi_i$ | W (watts) | $V$ in volts, $I$ in amperes |
| 2 | Total Connected Load | $CL = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i$ | W | Linear sum of all nameplate ratings |
| 3 | Demand Factor | $DF = \dfrac{MD}{CL}$ | dimensionless (0 < DF ≤ 1) | Always **less than 1** for grouped loads |
| 4 | Maximum Demand (per group) | $MD_i = CL_i \times DF_i$ | W | Applied **per sub-circuit or per category** |
| 5 | Diversity Factor (between groups) | $DivF = \dfrac{\sum MD_i}{MD_{system}}$ | dimensionless (DivF ≥ 1) | Inverse concept to DF |
| 6 | 3-Phase Apparent Power | $S = \sqrt{3} \times V_{LL} \times I_L$ | VA | $V_{LL}$ = **415 V** in India |
| 7 | 1-Phase Apparent Power | $S = V_{LN} \times I$ | VA | $V_{LN}$ = **240 V** |
| 8 | Real Power from Apparent Power | $P = S \times \cos \phi$ | W | Use individual load PF |
| 9 | Design Current of a 3-Phase Load | $I_b = \dfrac{P}{\sqrt{3} \times V_{LL} \times \cos \phi}$ | A | Used for cable sizing |
| 10 | Design Current of a 1-Phase Load | $I_b = \dfrac{P}{V_{LN} \times \cos \phi}$ | A | Used for branch circuit sizing |
| 11 | Energy Consumption (annual) | $E = MD \times DF_{annual} \times 8760$ | kWh | $8760$ = hours per year (**bold constant**) |
| 12 | MCB/MCCB Rating Selection | $I_n \geq 1.25 \times I_b$ | A | Per IS 732:2019 Cl. 4.3 |
| 13 | Voltage Drop (3-Phase, balanced) | $\Delta V = \sqrt{3} \times I_b \times L \times (R \cos\phi + X \sin\phi)$ | V | Limit: **5%** for lighting, **10%** for power |
| 14 | Per-Unit Voltage Drop | $\Delta V_{\%} = \dfrac{\Delta V}{V_{LL}} \times 100$ | % | Must be ≤ 5 or 10 per IS 732 |
| 15 | Lighting Load (Lumen Method) | $N = \dfrac{E \times A}{\eta \times \text{CU} \times \text{LLF}}$ | lamps | $\eta$ = luminaire efficiency, CU = coefficient of utilization, LLF = light loss factor |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The load survey is the **decision-support backbone** for:
- **Electrical Consultants** preparing single-line diagrams (SLDs).
- **Contractors** preparing tender quotations and BoMs.
- **Builders and Architects** verifying that sanctioned load from the DISCOM (e.g., KSEB) is adequate.
- **Energy Auditors** (BEE-certified) computing the Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) in kWh/m²/year.
- **Insurance Surveyors** validating that the installation is not under- or over-rated for risk underwriting.

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Insight:** A properly executed load survey reduces over-design by 15–25% (and hence project cost) and prevents dangerous under-design that leads to nuisance tripping or, in extreme cases, fire hazards.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Connected Load to Maximum Demand

The transformation from raw nameplate data to engineering design data follows a precise mathematical chain. Below is the **exhaustive, step-by-step derivation** that a KTU examiner expects to see in a full 14-mark answer.

**Given:**
- A residential installation with $n$ categories of loads.
- For each category $i$, the sum of nameplate wattages is $CL_i$ (in W).
- The demand factor recommended by IS 732 / standard thumb rules for category $i$ is $DF_i$ (a decimal between 0 and 1).
- The diversity factor applicable between two categories $i$ and $j$ is $DivF_{ij}$ (a number ≥ 1).

**Step 1: Compute Maximum Demand of Each Category Independently**

For each load category $i$:

$$MD_i = CL_i \times DF_i$$

This is the demand that the load category would draw *if it were the only category in the building*. The demand factor accounts for the fact that not all appliances in a category operate simultaneously at full rating.

**Step 2: Sum the Category Demands (Pre-Diversity)**

$$MD_{agg} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} MD_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (CL_i \times DF_i)$$

This aggregated maximum demand assumes that *all* categories peak at the *exact same instant*. In reality, this is statistically improbable.

**Step 3: Apply Diversity Factor to Reflect Temporal Staggering**

$$MD_{system} = \frac{MD_{agg}}{DivF_{total}}$$

where $DivF_{total}$ is the compound diversity factor across all $n$ categories. For a small residential building, $DivF_{total}$ is typically 1.1 to 1.3. For a large commercial complex, it can be as high as 1.5 to 2.0.

**Step 4: Convert Watts to Volt-Amperes for 3-Phase Sizing**

$$S_{system} = \frac{MD_{system}}{\cos \phi_{overall}}$$

where $\cos \phi_{overall}$ is the *weighted average* power factor of the installation. For a mixed residential load, this is typically **0.85**.

**Step 5: Convert Volt-Amperes to Full-Load Current for Cable and Breaker Sizing**

$$I_{FL} = \frac{S_{system}}{\sqrt{3} \times V_{LL}} = \frac{MD_{system}}{\sqrt{3} \times V_{LL} \times \cos\phi_{overall}}$$

**Step 6: Select Protective Device Rating**

$$I_{n(MCB)} \geq 1.25 \times I_{FL}$$

**Step 7: Verify Voltage Drop**

$$\Delta V = \sqrt{3} \times I_{FL} \times L \times (R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)$$

$$\Delta V_{\%} = \frac{\Delta V}{V_{LL}} \times 100 \leq 5\% \text{ (for lighting) or 10% (for power)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** The factor **1.25** in Step 6 is non-negotiable in KTU valuations. Examiners specifically look for this multiplier to test whether the student has internalised IS 732 Cl. 4.3.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> A 2-BHK residential flat has the following connected loads. Compute the total connected load, the maximum demand, the design current, and recommend the MCB rating. Use $V_{LN} = 240$ V, $V_{LL} = 415$ V, and $\cos\phi_{overall} = 0.85$.

**Load Schedule (Given Data):**

| Sl. No. | Load Description | Quantity | Rating Each (W) | $\cos\phi$ | DF |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | LED ceiling light | 8 | 12 | 0.95 | 0.8 |
| 2 | Wall socket (6A) | 6 | 100 | 0.85 | 0.3 |
| 3 | Wall socket (16A) | 3 | 1000 | 0.85 | 0.3 |
| 4 | Air-conditioner (1.5T split) | 2 | 1800 | 0.85 | 0.8 |
| 5 | Geyser | 1 | 2000 | 1.0 | 0.5 |
| 6 | Refrigerator | 1 | 250 | 0.7 | 0.6 |
| 7 | Television + set-top box | 1 | 150 | 0.9 | 0.5 |
| 8 | Kitchen mixer-grinder | 1 | 750 | 0.8 | 0.2 |
| 9 | Washing machine | 1 | 500 | 0.85 | 0.3 |
| 10 | Exhaust fan | 2 | 60 | 0.7 | 0.6 |

**Step 1: Compute Connected Load per Category**

$$CL_1 = 8 \times 12 = 96 \text{ W}$$
$$CL_2 = 6 \times 100 = 600 \text{ W}$$
$$CL_3 = 3 \times 1000 = 3000 \text{ W}$$
$$CL_4 = 2 \times 1800 = 3600 \text{ W}$$
$$CL_5 = 1 \times 2000 = 2000 \text{ W}$$
$$CL_6 = 1 \times 250 = 250 \text{ W}$$
$$CL_7 = 1 \times 150 = 150 \text{ W}$$
$$CL_8 = 1 \times 750 = 750 \text{ W}$$
$$CL_9 = 1 \times 500 = 500 \text{ W}$$
$$CL_{10} = 2 \times 60 = 120 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2: Total Connected Load**

$$CL_{total} = 96 + 600 + 3000 + 3600 + 2000 + 250 + 150 + 750 + 500 + 120 = 11066 \text{ W} \approx 11.07 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3: Maximum Demand per Category**

$$MD_1 = 96 \times 0.8 = 76.8 \text{ W}$$
$$MD_2 = 600 \times 0.3 = 180 \text{ W}$$
$$MD_3 = 3000 \times 0.3 = 900 \text{ W}$$
$$MD_4 = 3600 \times 0.8 = 2880 \text{ W}$$
$$MD_5 = 2000 \times 0.5 = 1000 \text{ W}$$
$$MD_6 = 250 \times 0.6 = 150 \text{ W}$$
$$MD_7 = 150 \times 0.5 = 75 \text{ W}$$
$$MD_8 = 750 \times 0.2 = 150 \text{ W}$$
$$MD_9 = 500 \times 0.3 = 150 \text{ W}$$
$$MD_{10} = 120 \times 0.6 = 72 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4: Aggregated Maximum Demand**

$$MD_{agg} = 76.8 + 180 + 900 + 2880 + 1000 + 150 + 75 + 150 + 150 + 72 = 5633.8 \text{ W} \approx 5.63 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5: Apply Diversity Factor (assumed DivF = 1.15 for a typical flat)**

$$MD_{system} = \frac{5633.8}{1.15} = 4899.0 \text{ W} \approx 4.90 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6: Convert to kVA**

$$S_{system} = \frac{4.90 \text{ kW}}{0.85} = 5.76 \text{ kVA}$$

**Step 7: Design Current (Single-Phase, since residential)**

$$I_{FL} = \frac{S_{system}}{V_{LN}} = \frac{5760}{240} = 24.0 \text{ A}$$

**Step 8: MCB Rating**

$$I_{n(MCB)} \geq 1.25 \times 24.0 = 30.0 \text{ A}$$

**Recommended:** A standard **32 A single-pole + neutral (SPN) MCB** with **C-curve** characteristics.

**Step 9: Voltage Drop Check (assume 30 m cable run, $R = 0.727$ Ω/km for 4 mm² Cu, $X$ negligible)**

$$\Delta V = 2 \times 24.0 \times 0.030 \times 0.727 = 1.047 \text{ V}$$
$$\Delta V_{\%} = \frac{1.047}{240} \times 100 = 0.44\% \quad \ll 5\% \quad \therefore \text{Compliant}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A raw connected load of 11.07 kW was successfully refined to a 4.90 kW maximum demand — a reduction of **56%**. This is the engineering value of a load survey.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Standard Demand Factor Reference Table

The following table provides the **KTU-recommended Demand Factors** for rapid use in exam problems. These values are aligned with IS 732:2019 and NEC Table 220.

| Load Category | Typical DF | Sub-Demand Factor (NEC 220) |
|---|---|---|
| Lighting (residential) | **0.75 – 0.90** | First 3000 W @ 100%, rest @ 35% |
| Lighting (commercial/office) | **0.80 – 0.95** | 100% of VA rating |
| Convenience outlets (6A) | **0.20 – 0.40** | First 10 outlets @ 1000 VA each, rest @ 180 VA |
| Power outlets (16A) | **0.30 – 0.50** | 100% of largest + 50% of others |
| Air-conditioners (residential) | **0.80 – 0.90** | 100% of nameplate |
| Geyser / water heater | **0.50 – 0.70** | 100% of largest + 50% of others |
| Lifts (elevators) | **0.60 – 0.80** | 125% of largest motor |
| Water pumps | **0.60 – 0.80** | 125% of largest motor |
| Kitchen equipment (commercial) | **0.50 – 0.70** | 100% of largest + 50% of rest |
| Server rooms / data centres | **0.90 – 1.00** | 100% with redundancy factor |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Load Survey Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step A: Architectural Drawings] --> B[Step B: Identify Zones and Rooms]
    B --> C[Step C: Classify Loads by Type]
    C --> C1[Lighting Loads]
    C --> C2[Heating Loads]
    C --> C3[Motor Loads]
    C --> C4[Electronic Loads]
    C --> C5[Socket Outlet Loads]
    C1 --> D[Step D: Assign Wattage per Item]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    D --> E[Step E: Tabulate Connected Load]
    E --> F[Step F: Apply Demand Factor]
    F --> G[Step G: Aggregate Maximum Demand]
    G --> H[Step H: Apply Diversity Factor]
    H --> I[Step I: Convert to kVA and Design Current]
    I --> J[Step J: Select MCB MCCB and Cable Size]
    J --> K[Step K: Verify Voltage Drop Compliance]
    K --> L{Final Design Output}
    L --> L1[Single Line Diagram SLD]
    L --> L2[Bill of Materials BoM]
    L --> L3[Load Schedule Register]
    L --> L4[Sanctioned Load Application to DISCOM]
```

## 4.2 Hierarchy of Load Survey Documentation

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Master Load Schedule] --> P2[Floor-wise Load List]
    P1 --> P3[Phase-wise Load List]
    P1 --> P4[Category-wise Load List]
    P2 --> P2A[Ground Floor]
    P2 --> P2B[First Floor]
    P2 --> P2C[Second Floor]
    P3 --> P3A[Phase R]
    P3 --> P3B[Phase Y]
    P3 --> P3C[Phase B]
    P4 --> P4A[Lighting]
    P4 --> P4B[Power Sockets]
    P4 --> P4C[Air Conditioning]
    P4 --> P4D[Heating and Geyser]
    P4 --> P4E[Motor and Pump]
```

## 4.3 Information Flow from Survey to Design

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT
        S1[Architectural Plan]
        S2[Owner Load List]
        S3[IS Standards]
    end
    subgraph PROCESS
        LS[Load Survey]
        CL[Connected Load Calc]
        MD[Maximum Demand Calc]
        VL[Voltage Drop Check]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT
        O1[SLD Single Line Diagram]
        O2[Bill of Quantities]
        O3[Energy Estimate]
        O4[Sanctioned Load Form]
    end
    S1 --> LS
    S2 --> LS
    S3 --> LS
    LS --> CL
    CL --> MD
    MD --> VL
    VL --> O1
    VL --> O2
    VL --> O3
    VL --> O4
```

## 4.4 Decision Logic for Demand Factor Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{What type of load group?} --> Q1A[Lighting]
    Q1 --> Q1B[Socket Outlets]
    Q1 --> Q1C[Fixed Equipment]
    Q1A --> R1A[Apply DF = 0.80 to 0.95]
    Q1B --> R1B[Apply DF = 0.20 to 0.40]
    Q1C --> R1C{Is it a motor?}
    R1C --> R1CYes[Apply DF = 0.60 to 0.80]
    R1C --> R1CNo[Apply DF = 0.50 to 0.70]
    R1A --> S1[Sum to get MD]
    R1B --> S1
    R1CYes --> S1
    R1CNo --> S1
    S1 --> T1[Aggregate MD]
    T1 --> U1{Apply Diversity?}
    U1 --> U1Yes[Divide by DivF = 1.10 to 2.00]
    U1 --> U1No[Use MD directly]
    U1Yes --> V1[Final MD]
    U1No --> V1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** 
**Define the term "Load Survey" as applied to electrical installation design. List any four objectives of conducting a load survey.**

**Model Answer (10 lines):**

A load survey is the systematic process of identifying, classifying, and quantifying every electrical load — lighting, heating, motor, socket-outlet, and electronic — within a proposed installation, along with its nameplate rating, quantity, and operational diversity.

**Four key objectives are:**
1. To determine the **total connected load** of the installation from architectural and equipment data.
2. To compute the **maximum demand** by applying demand and diversity factors to the connected load.
3. To provide the **design current** input required for cable sizing, MCB/MCCB selection, and bus-bar sizing.
4. To enable **accurate estimation of sanctioned load** to be applied for from the local DISCOM (e.g., KSEB), and to facilitate cost estimation and energy audit.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** 
**Distinguish between "Connected Load", "Demand Factor", and "Diversity Factor" with one-line definitions.**

**Model Answer (6 lines):**

- **Connected Load (CL):** The algebraic sum of the nameplate wattage ratings of all appliances, luminaires, and equipment installed in the premises. Unit: watts (W) or kilowatts (kW).
- **Demand Factor (DF):** The ratio of the maximum demand of a load group to its connected load. It is always less than or equal to 1.
- **Diversity Factor (DivF):** The ratio of the sum of individual maximum demands of several load groups to the maximum demand of the whole installation considered as a single load. It is always greater than or equal to 1.

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — July 2024]

A small commercial office building has the following electrical loads. Prepare the load schedule, compute the total connected load, the maximum demand (assume an overall diversity factor of 1.20), and the full-load current. Recommend the MCCB rating for the main panel. Use $V_{LL} = 415$ V, $V_{LN} = 240$ V, and an overall $\cos\phi = 0.85$.

| Sl. No. | Load Description | Quantity | Rating Each (W) | DF |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | LED panel light (600×600 mm) | 40 | 36 | 0.90 |
| 2 | 6A socket outlets (workstations) | 30 | 100 | 0.25 |
| 3 | 16A socket outlets (printers, etc.) | 8 | 1000 | 0.30 |
| 4 | Split AC (1.5T) | 10 | 1800 | 0.85 |
| 5 | Server rack | 2 | 3000 | 0.95 |
| 6 | Lift motor (7.5 kW) | 1 | 7500 | 0.75 |
| 7 | Water pump (3 HP) | 1 | 2240 | 0.60 |

#### Part (a): Prepare the Load Schedule and Compute Connected Load & Maximum Demand. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Connected Load per category**

$$CL_1 = 40 \times 36 = 1440 \text{ W}$$
$$CL_2 = 30 \times 100 = 3000 \text{ W}$$
$$CL_3 = 8 \times 1000 = 8000 \text{ W}$$
$$CL_4 = 10 \times 1800 = 18000 \text{ W}$$
$$CL_5 = 2 \times 3000 = 6000 \text{ W}$$
$$CL_6 = 1 \times 7500 = 7500 \text{ W}$$
$$CL_7 = 1 \times 2240 = 2240 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2: Total Connected Load**

$$CL_{total} = 1440 + 3000 + 8000 + 18000 + 6000 + 7500 + 2240 = 46180 \text{ W} \approx 46.18 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3: Maximum Demand per category**

$$MD_1 = 1440 \times 0.90 = 1296 \text{ W}$$
$$MD_2 = 3000 \times 0.25 = 750 \text{ W}$$
$$MD_3 = 8000 \times 0.30 = 2400 \text{ W}$$
$$MD_4 = 18000 \times 0.85 = 15300 \text{ W}$$
$$MD_5 = 6000 \times 0.95 = 5700 \text{ W}$$
$$MD_6 = 7500 \times 0.75 = 5625 \text{ W}$$
$$MD_7 = 2240 \times 0.60 = 1344 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4: Aggregated Maximum Demand (Pre-Diversity)**

$$MD_{agg} = 1296 + 750 + 2400 + 15300 + 5700 + 5625 + 1344 = 32415 \text{ W} \approx 32.42 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5: Apply Diversity Factor DivF = 1.20**

$$MD_{system} = \frac{32415}{1.20} = 27012.5 \text{ W} \approx 27.01 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6: Convert to kVA**

$$S_{system} = \frac{27.01}{0.85} = 31.78 \text{ kVA}$$

#### Part (b): Compute Full-Load Current and Recommend MCCB Rating. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Full-Load Current on 3-Phase Supply**

$$I_{FL} = \frac{S_{system}}{\sqrt{3} \times V_{LL}} = \frac{31780}{\sqrt{3} \times 415} = \frac{31780}{718.78} = 44.21 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2: MCCB Rating (Standard Rule)**

$$I_{n(MCCB)} \geq 1.25 \times I_{FL} = 1.25 \times 44.21 = 55.26 \text{ A}$$

**Recommended MCCB:** A standard **63 A, 3-Pole MCCB** with adjustable thermal-magnetic trip settings.

**Step 3: Sanctioned Load to be Applied for (rounded up)**

$$MD_{system} = 27.01 \text{ kW} \implies \text{Apply for } \mathbf{30 \text{ kW}} \text{ sanctioned load from KSEB}$$

**Step 4: Voltage Drop Sanity Check (assume 50 m feeder run, 16 mm² Cu cable, $R \approx 1.15$ Ω/km)**

$$\Delta V = \sqrt{3} \times 44.21 \times 0.050 \times 1.15 = 4.40 \text{ V}$$
$$\Delta V_{\%} = \frac{4.40}{415} \times 100 = 1.06\% \quad \ll 5\% \quad \therefore \text{Compliant}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:** Many students forget the **1.25 safety multiplier** for breaker selection. Marks are deducted in two places — once for not multiplying, and once for choosing a non-standard breaker size. Always pick the *next higher standard size* (i.e., 63 A here, not 55 A).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

Explain, with the help of a neat flowchart, the **step-by-step methodology of conducting a load survey** for a residential building. State clearly the difference between **demand factor** and **diversity factor**, and explain why connected load is *not* used directly for transformer sizing.

#### Part (a): Step-by-Step Methodology with Flowchart (Mermaid to be drawn on answer script). **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer Outline:**

The methodology consists of **seven sequential steps:**

1. **Gather Architectural Inputs:** Floor plans, elevations, reflected ceiling plans, electrical layout drawings (if available), and the owner's load list.
2. **Identify Functional Zones:** Living room, bedrooms, kitchen, utility, balcony, lobby, staircase, parking, common area. Mark them on a copy of the floor plan.
3. **Determine Lighting Positions and Ratings:** Use IS 3646 for lux levels (e.g., 100–150 lux for living room, 300 lux for kitchen, 200 lux for study). Compute number of luminaires using the Lumen Method: $N = \frac{E \times A}{\eta \times CU \times LLF}$.
4. **Mark Socket-Outlet Positions:** 6A sockets at 1.8 m height every 4–5 m in living areas; 16A sockets near AC and kitchen appliance points. Provide minimum 1 socket per 10 m² in habitable rooms.
5. **List Fixed Equipment:** AC indoor units, geysers, water pumps, range hoods, exhaust fans, washing machines, refrigerators.
6. **Tabulate the Load Schedule:** Format: Sl. No. | Description | Qty | Rating (W) | Total W | $\cos\phi$ | DF. Sum the totals.
7. **Apply Diversity and Compute Maximum Demand:** Use category-wise DFs (from table) and a global DivF (1.10–1.30 for residential). Derive MD and then kVA and full-load current.

**Flowchart (to be drawn in pen-and-paper exam):**

```
[Architectural Plan] → [Identify Rooms]
        ↓
[Mark Lighting Points] → [Mark Socket Points]
        ↓
[Mark Fixed Equipment]
        ↓
[Tabulate: Description | Qty | Watts | Total]
        ↓
[Sum → Connected Load]
        ↓
[Apply Demand Factor per category]
        ↓
[Aggregate → Aggregated MD]
        ↓
[Apply Diversity Factor]
        ↓
[System MD → kVA → Full-Load Current]
        ↓
[Select MCB, Cable, MCCB]
```

#### Part (b): Differentiate DF vs DivF, and Explain Why CL ≠ Transformer Size. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | Demand Factor (DF) | Diversity Factor (DivF) |
|---|---|---|
| **Applied to** | A single load group (e.g., all 6A sockets) | Between multiple load groups (e.g., sockets vs AC vs lighting) |
| **Numerical range** | $0 < DF \leq 1$ | $DivF \geq 1$ |
| **Reduces or increases apparent value** | Reduces (multiplier) | Reduces (divisor) |
| **Definition** | $DF = \frac{MD \text{ of group}}{CL \text{ of group}}$ | $DivF = \frac{\sum MD_i}{MD_{system}}$ |
| **Example** | A 16A socket may have DF = 0.30 | A 10-flat apartment has DivF ≈ 1.3 |

**Why Connected Load cannot be used directly for transformer sizing:**

Using the connected load as the design input would result in **massive over-sizing** of the transformer. The reasoning is statistical and economic:

1. **Statistical Reality of Simultaneity:** In a residential building, it is *extremely improbable* that every light, every socket, every AC, and every geyser will operate at full rating at the exact same instant. Studies show that for a 50-flat apartment, the simultaneous demand is only 30–50% of the summed connected load.

2. **Economic Penalty:** A 500 kVA transformer sized at full connected load of 1500 kW (hypothetical) would cost three times more than a properly sized 500 kVA unit, and would operate at low efficiency (high iron losses, low copper losses), increasing no-load losses throughout its 25-year life.

3. **Capital and Space Wastage:** Larger transformers require larger rooms, heavier cabling, more robust switchgear, and higher civil costs. The load survey is what allows the engineer to specify the *smallest transformer that still safely serves the realistic maximum demand*.

4. **Voltage Regulation and Harmonics:** An oversized lightly-loaded transformer has poor voltage regulation and can develop ferroresonance issues with non-linear loads.

Therefore, the **load survey → connected load → demand factor → maximum demand → transformer kVA** chain is an essential economic and technical optimization.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:** A common error is to confuse "demand factor" with "diversity factor" or to state that both are "less than 1". Recall: **DF ≤ 1**, but **DivF ≥ 1**. Examiners frequently test this distinction. Also, never write "DF = 1/DivF" — they are conceptually inverse but numerically *not* reciprocals of each other in general.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Definition:** Load Survey = systematic identification, classification, and quantification of all electrical loads.
- [x] **First deliverable** of the electrical design process; prerequisite for cable sizing, breaker selection, and transformer sizing.
- [x] **Connected Load (CL):** Sum of nameplate ratings (in W). Always use this for "raw" energy budgeting.
- [x] **Maximum Demand (MD):** CL × DF (per group), then aggregated and divided by DivF. Used for sizing hardware.
- [x] **DF ≤ 1** (always); **DivF ≥ 1** (always). They are *not* reciprocals.
- [x] **Standard Voltages:** $V_{LL}$ = 415 V, $V_{LN}$ = 240 V (3-phase 4-wire Indian system).
- [x] **Breaker Rating Rule:** $I_n \geq 1.25 \times I_{FL}$ (non-negotiable IS 732 multiplier).
- [x] **Voltage Drop Limits:** 5% for lighting, 10% for power (from origin of installation to the load).
- [x] **7-Stage Methodology:** Plans → Zones → Lighting → Sockets → Equipment → Tabulate → Apply DF/DivF.
- [x] **Three Classification Axes:** Nature (lighting/heating/motor/electronic), Priority (essential/important/general), and Spatial (floor/function).
- [x] **C.L.E.A.R. Mnemonic:** Classify → List → Estimate diversity → Apply DF → Record MD.
- [x] **Industry thumb rule:** Demand factor for residential lighting ≈ 0.80–0.90; for 6A sockets ≈ 0.25–0.35; for AC units ≈ 0.85; for geysers ≈ 0.50.
- [x] **KSEB Sanctioned Load:** Always round up MD to the next standard sanctioned slab (e.g., 3 kW, 5 kW, 7 kW, 10 kW for residential).
- [x] **Connected load overestimates by 40–70%** in most buildings — the load survey's job is to reveal the realistic figure.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Diversity Factor, Sizing and Selection of Wires
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT752 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Diversity Factor, Sizing and Selection of Wires

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Diversity Factor (DF)

### Formal Academic Definition
The **Diversity Factor** is defined as the ratio of the sum of the individual maximum demands of various parts of an electrical installation (or sub-divisions of the system) to the **coincident maximum demand** of the whole system (or the part of the system under consideration).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Standard Definition (As per IS 732 & NEC):**
> $$\text{Diversity Factor (DF)} = \frac{\sum \text{Individual Maximum Demands of Loads}}{\text{Maximum Demand of the Entire System}}$$

### Key Characteristics
- **Always Greater Than or Equal to Unity** ($\text{DF} \geq 1$).
- A higher DF indicates that the loads are **less likely to peak simultaneously**.
- Directly affects the **installed capacity** of transformers, switchgear, and busbars.
- Inversely related to the **Coincidence Factor (CCF)**:

$$\text{Diversity Factor} = \frac{1}{\text{Coincidence Factor}}$$

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!TIP]
> **The Water-Tap Analogy:**
> Imagine a building has **10 flats**, each rated with a maximum possible water demand of **1000 litres/hour** (total = 10,000 litres/hour). However, in practice, it is highly improbable that all 10 families will shower, wash clothes, and fill tanks at the *exact* same minute. The actual peak demand observed on the main inlet pipe might only be **5000 litres/hour**.
>
> Here, Diversity Factor = 10,000 / 5,000 = **2.0**. The plumber can therefore safely use a smaller diameter incoming pipe (lower installed capacity) than if he had summed every individual maximum.
>
> *In electrical systems, this principle allows us to "right-size" transformers, cables, and switchgear, leading to massive capital cost savings without compromising reliability.*

---

## 1.2 Sizing and Selection of Wires (Cables)

### Formal Academic Definition
**Wire sizing** is the engineering process of determining the minimum required cross-sectional area of an electrical conductor such that it can safely carry the design current without exceeding permissible temperature limits, while maintaining the voltage drop at the load terminals within statutory limits.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> A properly designed cable must satisfy **three mandatory criteria**:
> 1. **Current Carrying Capacity (Ampacity)** ≥ Full-load current
> 2. **Voltage Drop** ≤ Permissible limit (typically **3%** for a single circuit, **5%** from transformer to consumer end per IS 732)
> 3. **Short-Circuit Withstand Capacity** ≥ Prospective short-circuit current
>
> The final selected size is the **largest** of all three.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!TIP]
> **The Highway Analogy:**
> A wire is like a highway. If too many cars (current) try to squeeze into a 2-lane road (thin wire), traffic jams (voltage drop) occur, the road overheats (I²R losses), and accidents (insulation failure/fire) can happen. The "permissible load" is the safe number of cars the road can handle per hour. The "voltage drop" is the time delay between entry and exit. A good electrical designer sizes the highway wide enough for safe, fast, cool operation — not just to barely fit the cars.

### Physical & Standards Constants
- **Reference ambient temperature for cables in India (IS 3961)**: **40°C** (for XLPE) and **45°C** (for PVC).
- **Standard reference depth of laying (for underground cables)**: **0.9 m (3 feet)**.
- **Soil thermal resistivity (standard)**: **150 °C·cm/W**.
- **Frequency of supply in India**: **50 Hz**.
- **Permissible voltage drop**: **3% per circuit** (as recommended by IS 732).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Diversity Factor — Deep Theoretical Breakdown

The Diversity Factor is **not a constant**; it varies with:
- Type of load (residential, commercial, industrial).
- Time of day / season.
- Number of consumers in a group.
- Operating pattern (manual vs. automatic).

### Logical Steps of DF Application
1. **Step 1 — Load Survey:** Identify every load in the installation and tag its individual rating (in kW or kVA).
2. **Step 2 — Demand Factor (DF_req):** Multiply each load by its respective *demand factor* to obtain its *maximum demand*:

$$\text{Maximum Demand of Load } i = \text{Connected Load}_i \times \text{Demand Factor}_i$$

3. **Step 3 — Aggregation:** The *coincident maximum demand* of the group is obtained by:

$$\text{Group Maximum Demand} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{MD}_i}{\text{Diversity Factor of the group}}$$

4. **Step 4 — Capacity Fixing:** The transformer/switchgear capacity is chosen $\geq$ this group MD.

### Engineering Utility
- **Over-sizing Prevention:** Prevents the costly mistake of installing a transformer sized to the algebraic sum of all loads.
- **Economic Optimization:** Reduces initial capital cost (transformer, cables, busbars).
- **Operational Efficiency:** Right-sized equipment operates closer to its optimal loading (typically 60–80%).

---

## 2.2 Wire Sizing — Step-by-Step Logic

A systematic KTU-grade wire selection procedure involves **6 sequential checks**:

### Step 1 — Determine Full-Load Current ($I_{FL}$)
For 3-phase systems:

$$I_{FL} = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3} \times V_L \times \cos\phi}$$

For 1-phase systems:

$$I_{FL} = \frac{P}{V \times \cos\phi}$$

### Step 2 — Apply Diversification & Grouping
- Sum maximum demands of grouped loads.
- Apply Diversity Factor for upstream feeder sizing.

### Step 3 — Apply Derating Factors
The *nominal* current carrying capacity ($I_z$) from the manufacturer's table (e.g., IS 3961) must be derated to account for:

$$I_{z,\text{derated}} = I_{z,\text{table}} \times k_1 \times k_2 \times k_3 \times k_4$$

where the correction factors are:

| Symbol | Factor | Typical Range | Source Standard |
|:------:|:-------|:-------------:|:---------------:|
| $k_1$ | Ambient Temperature Correction | 0.80 – 1.10 | IS 3961 |
| $k_2$ | Soil Thermal Resistivity (for buried cables) | 0.70 – 1.05 | IS 3961 |
| $k_3$ | Depth of Laying (for buried cables) | 0.90 – 1.05 | IS 3961 |
| $k_4$ | Grouping / Number of Circuits | 0.50 – 1.00 | IS 3961 / NEC |

### Step 4 — Select Conductor for Ampacity
Required: $I_{z,\text{derated}} \geq I_{FL} \times 1.25$ (for motor circuits as per IS 732, the multiplier is 1.25 for continuous loads).

### Step 5 — Check Voltage Drop
Voltage drop is calculated as:

$$\Delta V = I_{FL} \times (R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi) \times L \times 2 \quad \text{(for single-phase, 2-wire)}$$

$$\Delta V = \sqrt{3} \times I_{FL} \times (R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi) \times L \quad \text{(for three-phase)}$$

Voltage drop percentage:

$$\%\text{VD} = \frac{\Delta V}{V_{rated}} \times 100$$

### Step 6 — Check Short-Circuit Withstand
Required cross-section for short-circuit:

$$A = \frac{I_{sc} \times \sqrt{t}}{k}$$

where:
- $A$ = Cross-sectional area in mm²
- $I_{sc}$ = RMS short-circuit current in A
- $t$ = Duration of fault in seconds
- $k$ = Material constant (**115** for copper, **76** for aluminium, with PVC insulation)

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula | Description | Unit | Boundary / Note |
|:-:|:--------|:------------|:----:|:----------------|
| 1 | $\text{DF} = \frac{\sum \text{Individual MDs}}{\text{Coincident MD}}$ | Diversity Factor | — | $\text{DF} \geq 1$ |
| 2 | $\text{CCF} = \frac{\text{Group MD}}{\sum \text{Individual MDs}}$ | Coincidence Factor | — | $\text{CCF} \leq 1$ |
| 3 | $\text{DF} \times \text{CCF} = 1$ | Reciprocal Relation | — | Always |
| 4 | $I_{FL,3\phi} = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3} \, V_L \cos\phi}$ | 3-Phase Full Load Current | A | $P$ in W, $V_L$ in V |
| 5 | $I_{FL,1\phi} = \frac{P}{V \cos\phi}$ | 1-Phase Full Load Current | A | $P$ in W, $V$ in V |
| 6 | $I_{z,\text{design}} = I_z \cdot k_1 \cdot k_2 \cdot k_3 \cdot k_4$ | Derated Ampacity | A | $k \leq 1$ typically |
| 7 | $\Delta V_{3\phi} = \sqrt{3} I (R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi) L$ | 3-Phase Voltage Drop | V | Per phase loop simplified |
| 8 | $\Delta V_{1\phi} = 2 I (R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi) L$ | 1-Phase Voltage Drop | V | Factor 2 for return path |
| 9 | $A_{sc} = \frac{I_{sc} \sqrt{t}}{k}$ | Short-Circuit Cross-section | mm² | $k=115$ Cu/PVC, 76 Al/PVC |
| 10 | $\%\text{VD} = \frac{\Delta V}{V_{\text{rated}}} \times 100$ | Voltage Drop Percentage | % | $\leq 3\%$ per circuit |

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility (Real-World Significance)

- **Power System Planning:** DF is used by electricity boards (KSEB, TNEB) to forecast the maximum demand of entire colonies/zones.
- **Cost Optimization:** A hospital with 100 air-conditioners (each 1.5 kW) would have a *connected load* of 150 kW, but with a typical DF of 1.5–1.8, the *transformer* is sized for ~83–100 kVA instead of 150 kVA.
- **Reliability Engineering:** Correct cable sizing prevents insulation failure — one of the leading causes of electrical fires in Kerala's commercial buildings.
- **IS 732 Compliance:** The Bureau of Indian Standards mandates the 3% / 5% voltage drop rule, and any deviation must be justified with technical documentation during KSEB inspections.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Diversity Factor from First Principles

Let us consider a system containing **$n$** individual loads with individual maximum demands $\text{MD}_1, \text{MD}_2, \ldots, \text{MD}_n$.

### Step 1 — Sum of Individual Maximum Demands
The arithmetic sum of all individual maximum demands is:

$$S_{ind} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{MD}_i = \text{MD}_1 + \text{MD}_2 + \text{MD}_3 + \ldots + \text{MD}_n$$

*This represents the theoretical "worst case" if every load peaked at exactly the same instant.*

### Step 2 — Observed Coincident Maximum Demand
In practice, the actual maximum demand measured at the upstream bus (e.g., transformer secondary) is $\text{MD}_{\text{system}}$. This is always less than or equal to $S_{ind}$ because of **diversification of load peaks in time**.

$$\text{MD}_{\text{system}} \leq S_{ind}$$

### Step 3 — Forming the Ratio
By definition, the Diversity Factor is the ratio of these two quantities:

$$\text{DF} = \frac{S_{ind}}{\text{MD}_{\text{system}}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{MD}_i}{\text{MD}_{\text{system}}}$$

### Step 4 — Lower Bound
Since $\text{MD}_{\text{system}} \leq S_{ind}$, the denominator is always less than or equal to the numerator, yielding:

$$\text{DF} \geq 1$$

### Step 5 — Reciprocal Relation with Coincidence Factor
The **Coincidence Factor (CCF)** is defined as the fraction of load groups that peak simultaneously:

$$\text{CCF} = \frac{\text{MD}_{\text{system}}}{S_{ind}}$$

Multiplying DF and CCF:

$$\text{DF} \times \text{CCF} = \frac{S_{ind}}{\text{MD}_{\text{system}}} \times \frac{\text{MD}_{\text{system}}}{S_{ind}} = 1$$

Hence:

$$\boxed{\text{DF} = \frac{1}{\text{CCF}}}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Diversity Factor Application

**Problem:** A residential complex has three feeders with the following data:

| Feeder | Connected Load (kW) | Demand Factor | Number of Consumers |
|:------:|:------------------:|:-------------:|:-------------------:|
| A | 200 | 0.8 | 50 |
| B | 350 | 0.7 | 80 |
| C | 500 | 0.6 | 120 |

The Diversity Factor for the entire complex is given as **1.45**. Find the transformer rating to be installed.

### Step 1 — Compute Individual Maximum Demand for each feeder
For Feeder A:

$$\text{MD}_A = 200 \times 0.8 = 160 \text{ kW}$$

For Feeder B:

$$\text{MD}_B = 350 \times 0.7 = 245 \text{ kW}$$

For Feeder C:

$$\text{MD}_C = 500 \times 0.6 = 300 \text{ kW}$$

### Step 2 — Sum of Individual Maximum Demands
Adding the three individual maximum demands:

$$S_{ind} = 160 + 245 + 300 = 705 \text{ kW}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Diversity Factor
The coincident (system) maximum demand is:

$$\text{MD}_{\text{system}} = \frac{S_{ind}}{\text{DF}} = \frac{705}{1.45} = 486.21 \text{ kW}$$

### Step 4 — Apply Power Factor
Assuming an overall power factor of **0.85 lagging**:

$$S_{\text{system}} = \frac{P_{\text{system}}}{\cos\phi} = \frac{486.21}{0.85} = 571.95 \text{ kVA}$$

### Step 5 — Select Standard Transformer Size
The next standard transformer size above 571.95 kVA is **630 kVA**.

$$\boxed{\text{Selected Transformer Rating} = 630 \text{ kVA}}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Cable Sizing with Derating

**Problem:** A 3-phase, 415 V, 50 Hz motor rated **15 kW** with $\cos\phi = 0.85$ and efficiency $\eta = 0.90$ is to be supplied by an **XLPE armoured cable** run on a perforated cable tray in a factory. Conditions:
- Ambient temperature: **45°C** (above the standard 40°C for XLPE)
- **6** such motor circuits are bunched together in a group.
- Cable length: **80 m**.
- Permissible voltage drop: **3%**.
- Cable to be run in **air** on a tray.

Find the required cable cross-section.

### Step 1 — Full-Load Current
First, compute the input power:

$$P_{in} = \frac{P_{\text{out}}}{\eta} = \frac{15{,}000}{0.90} = 16{,}666.67 \text{ W}$$

Then the full-load current:

$$I_{FL} = \frac{P_{in}}{\sqrt{3} \times V_L \times \cos\phi} = \frac{16{,}666.67}{\sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 0.85}$$

$$I_{FL} = \frac{16{,}666.67}{611.13} = 27.27 \text{ A}$$

### Step 2 — Apply Motor Loading Multiplier
For continuous motor loads, IS 732 recommends a **1.25 multiplier**:

$$I_{\text{design}} = 1.25 \times 27.27 = 34.09 \text{ A}$$

### Step 3 — Apply Derating Factors
From IS 3961 (XLPE, 90°C, on perforated tray):

- Ambient temperature factor at 45°C: $k_1 = 0.94$
- Grouping factor for 6 circuits: $k_2 = 0.76$

The required nominal ampacity from the cable table:

$$I_{z,\text{required}} = \frac{I_{\text{design}}}{k_1 \times k_2} = \frac{34.09}{0.94 \times 0.76} = \frac{34.09}{0.7144} = 47.72 \text{ A}$$

### Step 4 — Select Cable from Table
From the XLPE cable table (on perforated tray, 90°C):

| Cable Size (mm²) | Ampacity (A) |
|:----------------:|:------------:|
| 4 | 32 |
| **6** | **41** |
| **10** | **57** |
| 16 | 76 |

The smallest cable whose ampacity exceeds 47.72 A is **10 mm²** (57 A).

$$\boxed{\text{Selected cable (by ampacity)} = 10 \text{ mm}^2 \text{ (3-core XLPE)}}$$

### Step 5 — Check Voltage Drop
For 10 mm² XLPE cable (copper):
- Resistance: $R \approx 1.83 \,\Omega/\text{km}$ (AC value at 90°C)
- Reactance: $X \approx 0.073 \,\Omega/\text{km}$

Voltage drop:

$$\Delta V = \sqrt{3} \times I_{FL} \times (R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi) \times L$$

With $\cos\phi = 0.85$ and $\sin\phi = 0.527$:

$$\Delta V = \sqrt{3} \times 27.27 \times (1.83 \times 0.85 + 0.073 \times 0.527) \times 0.080$$

$$\Delta V = 1.732 \times 27.27 \times (1.5555 + 0.0385) \times 0.080$$

$$\Delta V = 1.732 \times 27.27 \times 1.5940 \times 0.080$$

$$\Delta V = 6.024 \text{ V}$$

Voltage drop percentage:

$$\%\text{VD} = \frac{6.024}{415} \times 100 = 1.45\%$$

Since $1.45\% < 3\%$, the voltage drop criterion is **satisfied**.

$$\boxed{\text{Final Selected Cable Size} = 10 \text{ mm}^2 \text{ (3-core, XLPE, Copper)}}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — KTU Frequently Asked Cases

| Scenario | Key Consideration | Governing Standard |
|:---------|:-----------------|:-------------------|
| Motor feeder (continuous) | Use 1.25× multiplier on FLI | IS 732 |
| Single-phase lighting | Apply 2× factor for return path in VD | IS 732 |
| Buried underground cable | Derate for soil thermal resistivity | IS 3961 (Part 5) |
| High ambient (>40°C) | Apply temperature derating | IS 3961 |
| Grouped circuits in conduit | Apply grouping factor (0.5–1.0) | IS 3961 (Part 6) |
| Diesel generator backup | Use 1.10× derating for non-linear loads | IEEE 141 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow — Wire Selection Procedure

The following Mermaid block renders a comprehensive block-level functional architecture flow mapping the complete wire selection procedure as per KTU 2024 / IS 3961 standards.

```mermaid

    stepA1 --> stepA2{Step 2: Continuous or Non-Continuous Load?}

    stepA2 -->|Non-Continuous| stepA4[Use I_FL as is]

    stepA4 --> stepA5[Step 3: Identify Installation Method]
    stepA5 --> stepA6[Step 4: Apply Derating Factors k1, k2, k3, k4]
    stepA6 --> stepA7[Step 5: Compute Required Nominal Ampacity I_z_req]
    stepA7 --> stepA8[Step 6: Select Cable from IS 3961 Table]
    stepA8 --> stepA9{Step 7: Voltage Drop within 3 percent?}
    stepA9 -->|No| stepA10[Upsize Cable and Recheck]
    stepA9 -->|Yes| stepA11{Step 8: Short-Circuit Withstand OK?}
    stepA10 --> stepA8
    stepA11 -->|No| stepA10
```
