# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, 3 Marks]**
**Define Diversity Factor and Coincidence Factor. Show their relationship.**

#### Model Answer
- **Diversity Factor (DF):** It is the ratio of the sum of individual maximum demands of various parts of an electrical installation to the coincident maximum demand of the whole system.
- **Coincidence Factor (CCF):** It is the ratio of the coincident maximum demand of a group to the sum of the individual maximum demands of the group.
- **Relationship:** $\text{DF} = 1 / \text{CCF}$.
- DF $\geq 1$, whereas CCF $\leq 1$.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, 3 Marks]**
**List the factors affecting the current carrying capacity of a cable.**

#### Model Answer
The current carrying capacity of a cable is affected by the following factors:
1. Ambient temperature of the surroundings.
2. Number of cables grouped together (mutual heating).
3. Depth of laying (for buried cables).
4. Thermal resistivity of soil (for buried cables).
5. Method of installation (in air, in conduit, buried, on tray, etc.).
6. Type of insulation (PVC max 70°C, XLPE max 90°C).
7. Frequency of current (skin effect at high frequency).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, 14 Marks]**
**(a)** A commercial building has the following loads on its main feeder:
- Lighting: 20 kW
- Air-conditioning: 80 kW
- Lifts: 30 kW
- General Power Sockets: 25 kW

If the demand factors for the respective loads are 0.9, 0.8, 1.0, and 0.6, and the overall Diversity Factor is 1.3, find the maximum demand on the transformer. Assume a power factor of 0.85. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a flowchart the procedure for sizing a cable as per IS 3961, mentioning the various derating factors. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution to (a):

**Step 1 — Compute Individual Maximum Demand (MD) for each load group:**

For Lighting:

$$\text{MD}_{\text{light}} = 20 \times 0.9 = 18 \text{ kW}$$

For Air-Conditioning:

$$\text{MD}_{AC} = 80 \times 0.8 = 64 \text{ kW}$$

For Lifts:

$$\text{MD}_{\text{lift}} = 30 \times 1.0 = 30 \text{ kW}$$

For General Sockets:

$$\text{MD}_{\text{socket}} = 25 \times 0.6 = 15 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 — Sum of Individual Maximum Demands:**

$$S_{ind} = 18 + 64 + 30 + 15 = 127 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Diversity Factor:**

$$\text{MD}_{\text{system}} = \frac{127}{1.3} = 97.69 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to kVA using Power Factor:**

$$S_{\text{transformer}} = \frac{97.69}{0.85} = 114.93 \text{ kVA}$$

**Step 5 — Select Standard Transformer Size:**
The next standard rating above 114.93 kVA is **125 kVA**.

$$\boxed{\text{Selected Transformer Rating} = 125 \text{ kVA}}$$

---

#### Solution to (b):

**Step-by-step cable sizing procedure as per IS 3961:**

1. **Determine Full-Load Current ($I_{FL}$):** For 3-phase, $I_{FL} = P / (\sqrt{3} V \cos\phi)$.

2. **Apply Continuous Load Multiplier:** If the load is continuous (≥3 hours), multiply $I_{FL}$ by 1.25.

3. **Identify the Installation Method:** Air / Conduit / Tray / Buried — this affects the base ampacity table to be used.

4. **Apply Derating Factors:**
   - $k_1$: Ambient temperature correction
   - $k_2$: Soil thermal resistivity (for buried)
   - $k_3$: Depth of laying (for buried)
   - $k_4$: Grouping of circuits

5. **Compute the Required Nominal Ampacity:**

$$I_{z,\text{req}} = \frac{I_{\text{design}}}{k_1 \cdot k_2 \cdot k_3 \cdot k_4}$$

6. **Select Cable from IS 3961 Table:** Pick the next standard size whose ampacity $\geq I_{z,\text{req}}$.

7. **Voltage Drop Check:** Confirm $\%\text{VD} \leq 3\%$. If not, upsize.

8. **Short-Circuit Check:** Confirm cross-section $\geq I_{sc}\sqrt{t}/k$. If not, upsize.

9. **Final Selection:** The largest of the three computed sizes is the final selected size.

---

### Question B — Option 2 (Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, 14 Marks]**
**(a)** A 3-phase, 400 V, 50 Hz induction motor rated 11 kW with $\cos\phi = 0.85$ and efficiency $\eta = 0.88$ is to be supplied by an XLPE cable of length 50 m, bunched in a group of 4 in conduit at an ambient temperature of 45°C. Determine the suitable cable size using IS 3961 derating principles. Check the voltage drop for your selection. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A feeder supplies the following loads simultaneously at their peak times:
- 10 kW (DF = 0.9)
- 25 kW (DF = 0.7)
- 15 kW (DF = 0.6)
- 5 kW (DF = 0.5)

If the system Diversity Factor is 1.5, determine the kVA rating of the transformer. Take $\cos\phi = 0.9$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution to (a):

**Step 1 — Compute Full-Load Current:**

$$I_{FL} = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3} \times V \times \cos\phi \times \eta} = \frac{11{,}000}{\sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 0.85 \times 0.88}$$

$$I_{FL} = \frac{11{,}000}{516.86} = 21.29 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Continuous Motor Multiplier (1.25):**

$$I_{\text{design}} = 1.25 \times 21.29 = 26.61 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Derating Factors:**
- Ambient temperature factor at 45°C: $k_1 = 0.94$
- Grouping of 4 circuits in conduit: $k_2 = 0.65$

**Step 4 — Compute Required Nominal Ampacity:**

$$I_{z,\text{req}} = \frac{26.61}{0.94 \times 0.65} = \frac{26.61}{0.611} = 43.55 \text{ A}$$

**Step 5 — Select Cable from XLPE/Conduit Table:**

| Size (mm²) | Ampacity (A) |
|:----------:|:------------:|
| 4 | 28 |
| **6** | **36** |
| **10** | **49** |
| 16 | 65 |

Selecting **10 mm²** (49 A $\geq$ 43.55 A) ✓

**Step 6 — Voltage Drop Check (10 mm² XLPE, copper):**
- $R = 1.83 \,\Omega/\text{km}$ (at 90°C)
- $X = 0.073 \,\Omega/\text{km}$

$$\Delta V = \sqrt{3} \times 21.29 \times (1.83 \times 0.85 + 0.073 \times 0.527) \times 0.050$$

$$= 1.732 \times 21.29 \times 1.5940 \times 0.050$$

$$= 2.94 \text{ V}$$

$$\%\text{VD} = \frac{2.94}{400} \times 100 = 0.735\%$$

Since $0.735\% < 3\%$, the voltage drop criterion is satisfied.

$$\boxed{\text{Selected cable: 10 mm}^2 \text{ (3-core, XLPE, Cu)}}$$

---

#### Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Individual Maximum Demands:**

$$\text{MD}_1 = 10 \times 0.9 = 9 \text{ kW}$$

$$\text{MD}_2 = 25 \times 0.7 = 17.5 \text{ kW}$$

$$\text{MD}_3 = 15 \times 0.6 = 9 \text{ kW}$$

$$\text{MD}_4 = 5 \times 0.5 = 2.5 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 — Sum of Individual MDs:**

$$S_{ind} = 9 + 17.5 + 9 + 2.5 = 38 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Diversity Factor:**

$$\text{MD}_{\text{system}} = \frac{38}{1.5} = 25.33 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to kVA:**

$$S = \frac{25.33}{0.9} = 28.15 \text{ kVA}$$

**Step 5 — Select Standard Transformer:**
Next standard size above 28.15 kVA is **50 kVA**.

$$\boxed{\text{Selected Transformer Rating} = 50 \text{ kVA}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 1.25 multiplier** for motor (continuous) loads. This is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction in Part B.
> 2. **Using base table ampacity directly** without applying derating factors ($k_1$ to $k_4$). Always compute $I_{z,\text{req}} = I_{\text{design}} / (k_1 k_2 k_3 k_4)$ — do **not** multiply the table value.
> 3. **Confusing Diversity Factor (DF) with Demand Factor:** Demand factor is per individual load ($\leq 1$); Diversity factor is per group ($\geq 1$).
> 4. **Voltage drop in single-phase:** Students forget the factor of **2** (for the return path) — resulting in wrong answer by exactly 2×.
> 5. **Units mismatch:** $R$ is in $\Omega/\text{km}$, $L$ in **m or km** — must be consistent. KTU students often mix units, leading to a 1-mark cut.
> 6. **No diversity for a single load:** DF = 1 if there is only one load group under consideration. Many students write DF = 1.5 even for a single motor.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Notes for Last-Minute KTU Prep:**

- **Diversity Factor:** Always $\geq 1$; reduces the installed capacity; inverse of Coincidence Factor.
- **Demand Factor:** Always $\leq 1$; applied to individual loads.
- **Three Mandatory Sizing Criteria:** Ampacity ✓ | Voltage Drop $\leq 3\%$ ✓ | Short-Circuit Withstand ✓.
- **1.25 Multiplier** is mandatory for **continuous / motor** loads (per IS 732).
- **Derating Formula:** $I_{z,\text{req}} = I_{\text{design}} / (k_1 k_2 k_3 k_4)$.
- **Three-Phase FLI:** $I_{FL} = P / (\sqrt{3} V_L \cos\phi)$.
- **Voltage Drop (3-$\phi$):** $\Delta V = \sqrt{3} \, I (R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi) L$.
- **Voltage Drop (1-$\phi$):** $\Delta V = 2 \, I (R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi) L$ — **do not forget the 2**.
- **Short-Circuit Cross-Section:** $A = I_{sc} \sqrt{t} / k$ with $k = 115$ (Cu/PVC) or $76$ (Al/PVC).
- **Standard Reference Conditions for IS 3961:** Ambient 40°C (XLPE) / 45°C (PVC); Soil resistivity 150°C·cm/W; Depth 0.9 m.
- **XLPE Max Conductor Temp:** 90°C; **PVC Max Conductor Temp:** 70°C.
- **KSEB / IS 732 Voltage Drop Rule:** 3% per circuit; 5% total from transformer to consumer.
- **Memorize Standard Transformer Ratings:** 25, 50, 100, 160, 250, 315, 400, 500, 630, 1000, 1250, 1600, 2000 kVA.
- **Memorize Standard Cable Sizes:** 1.5, 2.5, 4, 6, 10, 16, 25, 35, 50, 70, 95, 120, 150, 185, 240, 300, 400 mm².
- **Rule of Thumb:** For a 415 V, 3-phase motor, **1 kW ≈ 2 A** (at $\cos\phi \cdot \eta = 0.85$).
- **Cost Implication:** Aluminium is cheaper than copper (per kg) but requires **1.6× larger** cross-section for the same ampacity.
- **Skin Effect:** Negligible at 50 Hz for sizes up to 240 mm² — do not over-engineer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DB and Protection Devices, Design Steps in Electrical Wiring
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT752 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** DB and Protection Devices, Design Steps in Electrical Wiring

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Distribution Board (DB) — Formal Definition

A **Distribution Board (DB)** is a metallic or insulated enclosure that houses one or more protective devices (such as MCBs, MCCBs, RCCBs/ELCBs, isolators, and busbars) along with neutral and earth terminals. Its primary function is to **receive incoming electrical power from the main supply/meter board and distribute it safely to multiple sub-circuits or final sub-circuits**, while providing overcurrent, short-circuit, and earth-fault protection in accordance with **IS 732:2019** and the **Central Electricity Authority (CEA) Regulations 2010**.

### 1.2 Protection Devices — Formal Definition

A **Protection Device** is a piece of electrical equipment installed in a circuit to automatically disconnect the supply under abnormal operating conditions such as **overload, short circuit, earth fault, or over-voltage**, thereby preventing damage to equipment, fire hazards, and risk to human life. Common devices include MCBs, MCCBs, fuses, RCCBs, ELCBs, RCBOs, and SPDs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Protection devices are graded as per **IS 8826 (MCBs)**, **IS 12640 (RCCBs)**, and coordinated with **IEC 60947-2 (MCCBs)**. Students must remember the standard fault-disconnection times: **≤ 0.4 s for final circuits (≤ 32 A)** and **≤ 5 s for distribution circuits** as per IS 732.

### 1.3 Types of Distribution Boards

| S. No. | Type of DB | Location | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Main Distribution Board (MDB)** | After energy meter | Receives supply from utility/meter and feeds SDBs |
| 2 | **Sub Distribution Board (SDB)** | Between MDB and final circuits | Further sub-division of load to localised groups |
| 3 | **Final Distribution Board (FDB)** | Near load centre | Directly feeds final sub-circuits (lighting, sockets) |

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — "The Heart and the Watchmen"**
> Imagine a large apartment building. The **MDB** is like the **main water tank inlet** — it receives the entire supply. From there, water flows to smaller tanks on each floor — these are the **SDBs**. Finally, individual pipes go to taps in each flat — these are the **final sub-circuits**.
> Now, what if a pipe bursts in one flat? We don't want the entire building to lose water. That's why each pipe has a **valve (MCB)** that shuts off only that flat. And what if a tap leaks due to a cracked pipe (earth fault)? A **sensitive current sensor (RCCB)** detects the leak and shuts it off in milliseconds. So the DB is essentially a **"control junction"** with built-in **"watchmen"** (protection devices) guarding every circuit.

### 1.5 Classification of Protection Devices

- **Overcurrent Protection Devices**: MCB, MCCB, HRC Fuse
- **Earth-Fault Protection Devices**: RCCB, ELCB, RCBO
- **Over-Voltage Protection**: Surge Protection Device (SPD)
- **Switching Devices**: Isolator, Switch-Disconnector, Contactor
- **Combined Devices**: RCBO (RCCB + MCB in one unit)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Earth-Fault Trip Current Ratings (I∆n) for RCCBs:** **30 mA** (personal protection against electric shock), **100 mA / 300 mA** (fire protection), **500 mA** (machine/equipment protection only).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Miniature Circuit Breaker (MCB)

An MCB is a **thermo-magnetic** (bimetallic + solenoid) operated, single-pole/multi-pole, automatically resettable protective device used in LV (Low Voltage) installations up to **415 V AC, 50 Hz**, with current ratings from **0.5 A to 125 A** and short-circuit capacity typically **6 kA / 10 kA / 15 kA**.

**Operating Principle:**
- **Overload (slow trip):** Bimetallic strip bends due to I²R heating → mechanical latch releases → contacts open.
- **Short Circuit (instant trip):** High current energises solenoid plunger → striker hits the trip mechanism.

**Trip Curves (per IS 8826 / IEC 60898):**

| Curve Type | Instantaneous Trip Range | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **B Curve** | **3 In to 5 In** | Purely resistive loads — lighting, heating |
| **C Curve** | **5 In to 10 In** | Slightly inductive — fans, small motors, sockets |
| **D Curve** | **10 In to 20 In** | Highly inductive — large motors, transformers, X-ray |

### 2.2 Moulded Case Circuit Breaker (MCCB)

An MCCB is a heavy-duty protective device for currents **up to 1600 A** (sometimes 2500 A) and breaking capacities **up to 100 kA** at 415 V. It offers **adjustable overload, short-circuit, and instantaneous trip settings**, and is used at MDBs and large SDBs.

### 2.3 Residual Current Circuit Breaker (RCCB) vs. ELCB

| Feature | **RCCB (Current Operated)** | **ELCB (Voltage Operated)** |
|---|---|---|
| Principle | Senses **difference** between line & neutral currents | Senses **voltage** on earth wire |
| Trip Current | Typically **30 mA, 100 mA, 300 mA** | Depends on earth electrode resistance |
| Sensitivity | Very high, reliable | Less sensitive, obsolete |
| Standard | **IS 12640 (Part 1 & 2)** | Largely replaced by RCCB |
| Application | Modern installations | Rare, legacy systems |

**Working of RCCB:** Under healthy conditions, current in line wire = current in neutral wire (vector sum = 0). Under earth fault, a small current leaks to earth → imbalance detected by a **Core Balance Current Transformer (CBCT)** → trip coil energised → contacts open in **< 40 ms**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **An RCCB does NOT provide overcurrent or short-circuit protection.** It must always be used **in series with an MCB** (or as a combined RCBO).

### 2.4 RCBO (Residual Current Breaker with Overcurrent)

A **single compact device that combines the functions of an MCB and an RCCB**, offering protection against **overload, short circuit, and earth fault simultaneously**. Widely used in modern final sub-circuits to save DIN-rail space.

### 2.5 Surge Protection Device (SPD)

A device that **limits transient overvoltages** caused by lightning strikes or switching surges, diverting the surge current to earth. Classified as **Type 1, Type 2, and Type 3** per IEC 61643-11. Installed at MDB (Type 1/2) and near sensitive equipment (Type 3).

### 2.6 HRC (High Rupturing Capacity) Fuse

A fuse element enclosed in a ceramic body filled with **quartz sand**, capable of safely interrupting very high fault currents (typically **80 kA to 120 kA**). The sand **quenches the arc** and absorbs the energy. Used in **SFU (Switch Fuse Units)** and as backup protection with MCBs in cascading schemes.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| S.No. | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I = \dfrac{P}{\sqrt{3} \times V_L \times \cos\phi}$ | 3-phase full-load current (P in W) |
| 2 | $I = \dfrac{P}{V \times \cos\phi}$ | Single-phase full-load current |
| 3 | $V_d = \dfrac{\rho \cdot L \cdot I \cdot 2}{A}$ (1-φ) | Voltage drop (single phase, 2-wire) |
| 4 | $V_d = \dfrac{\rho \cdot L \cdot I \cdot \sqrt{3}}{A}$ (3-φ) | Voltage drop (three phase, 3-wire) |
| 5 | $V_{d\%} = \dfrac{V_d}{V_{rated}} \times 100$ | Percentage voltage drop |
| 6 | $I_{sc} = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{R^2 + X^2}}$ | Short-circuit current at point of fault |
| 7 | $t_{min} = \dfrac{k^2 \cdot A^2}{I_{sc}^2}$ | Minimum time before conductor damage (I²t let-through) |
| 8 | $I_{disc} = k \cdot I_n$ | Disconnection time factor (k = 1 for < 5 s, 1.45 for < 0.4 s) |
| 9 | $E_f = I^2 \cdot R \cdot t$ | Energy let-through (fuse/MCB discrimination) |
| 10 | $I_{d} \cdot t \leq k^2 \cdot s^2$ | Adiabatic equation for conductor protection (IEC 60364) |

> Where: $A$ = conductor cross-section (mm²), $L$ = length (m), $\rho$ = resistivity (Ω·mm²/m ≈ 0.0172 for Cu at 20°C, 0.0282 for Al), $k$ = material factor (**115 for Cu/PVC, 76 for Al/PVC, 143 for Cu/XLPE**), $I_n$ = protective device rated current.

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Domestic wiring:** MCBs (C-curve, 6 A/16 A/20 A/32 A) for lighting and sockets, **RCCB 30 mA 63 A** at the SDB.
- **Industrial MDB:** MCCBs with adjustable trip units (LSIG: Long-time, Short-time, Instantaneous, Ground fault).
- **Solar PV installations:** Type 1+2 SPD mandatory; special **DC-rated MCBs** required on PV side.
- **Hospital ICUs:** **IT earthing + 10 mA RCCBs** (medical IT system per IS 13450/IEC 61557-8).
- **Discrimination / Selectivity:** A downstream 6 A MCB must trip first before the upstream 63 A MCB — this is **graded protection**, a high-weightage KTU topic.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Selection of MCB Rating from Load Current

**Problem Statement:** A single-phase 230 V, 50 Hz sub-circuit feeds a group of 8 fluorescent lamp fittings, each rated **40 W**, 0.86 p.f. (with power factor correction capacitor). Determine the MCB rating.

**Step 1 — Calculate Total Connected Load:**

$$P_{total} = n \times P_{lamp} = 8 \times 40 = 320 \; \text{W}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Diversity Factor (for lighting, IS 732 allows 1.0 for ≤ 100 points, but conservatively 0.9):**

$$P_{design} = P_{total} \times D_f = 320 \times 0.9 = 288 \; \text{W}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Full-Load Current:**

$$I_{FL} = \frac{P_{design}}{V \times \cos\phi} = \frac{288}{230 \times 0.86}$$

$$I_{FL} = \frac{288}{197.8} = 1.456 \; \text{A}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Safety Margin (1.25 to 1.5 of I_FL, per IS 732 Cl. 4.4.1):**

$$I_{MCB} \geq 1.25 \times I_{FL} = 1.25 \times 1.456 = 1.82 \; \text{A}$$

**Step 5 — Select the next standard MCB rating from the preferred range (IS 8826):**

Preferred MCB ratings → **2 A, 6 A, 10 A, 16 A, 20 A, 25 A, 32 A, 40 A, 50 A, 63 A**

$$I_{MCB, \text{standard}} = 6 \; \text{A} \; (\text{C-curve})$$

> The next higher rating (6 A) is selected to provide **at least 1.25× overload margin** and to allow **inrush tolerance** for fluorescent lamp chokes.

### 3.2 Derivation: Cable Sizing Using Voltage-Drop Constraint

**Problem:** A 3-phase, 415 V, 50 Hz induction motor draws **30 A** at 0.85 p.f., located **45 m** from the SDB. Determine the minimum cable cross-section so that voltage drop ≤ **3%** (recommended by IS 732 for feeders).

**Step 1 — Permissible Voltage Drop:**

$$V_{d,\text{max}} = 0.03 \times 415 = 12.45 \; \text{V}$$

**Step 2 — Cross-Section from Voltage Drop Formula (3-phase):**

$$A = \frac{\rho \cdot L \cdot I \cdot \sqrt{3}}{V_{d,\text{max}}}$$

For copper conductors, $\rho_{Cu} = 0.0172 \; \Omega \cdot \text{mm}^2/\text{m}$ (at 70°C operating temp, $\rho_{Cu} \approx 0.0212$).

$$A = \frac{0.0212 \times 45 \times 30 \times 1.732}{12.45}$$

$$A = \frac{49.59}{12.45} = 3.98 \; \text{mm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Apply Current-Carrying Capacity (Derating) Check:**

From IS 3961 (Part II) for 3-core PVC insulated Cu cable, installed in conduit, **4 mm² Cu** carries approximately **27 A** (Method C). Since the motor current is **30 A**, this is **inadequate**.

**Step 4 — Select Next Higher Standard Size:**

Select **6 mm² Cu** → CCC ≈ **36 A** (with installation method correction factors). This satisfies both VD and CCC.

**Step 5 — Verify Voltage Drop with Selected Cable:**

$$V_d = \frac{0.0212 \times 45 \times 30 \times 1.732}{6} = 8.27 \; \text{V} \; (< 12.45 \; \text{V} \;\checkmark)$$

$$V_{d\%} = \frac{8.27}{415} \times 100 = 1.99\% \;\; (< 3\% \;\checkmark)$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Verification of Protection Coordination (Adiabatic Equation)

For the above motor cable (6 mm² Cu, PVC), check if the upstream MCB protects the cable under short-circuit.

**Step 1 — Determine Short-Circuit Current at DB (assume transformer impedance-limited):**

$$I_{sc} = \frac{V}{\sqrt{R^2 + X^2}} = \frac{230}{0.05} = 4600 \; \text{A} \; (\text{assumed 50 kVA transformer, single-phase short circuit level})$$

**Step 2 — Maximum Allowable Disconnection Time (Adiabatic Equation):**

$$t_{max} = \frac{k^2 \cdot A^2}{I_{sc}^2}$$

For Cu/PVC: $k = 115$

$$t_{max} = \frac{115^2 \times 6^2}{4600^2} = \frac{13225 \times 36}{21{,}160{,}000} = 0.0225 \; \text{s} = 22.5 \; \text{ms}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with MCB Trip Time:**

A 6 A C-curve MCB at **4600 A** (i.e., ~767× rated) trips in **< 5 ms** (instantaneous region) — which is well within **22.5 ms**. ✔ **Coordination achieved.**

> [!NOTE]
> If $t_{MCB} > t_{max}$, the cable insulation will be damaged **before** the MCB trips. This is the **fundamental "protection coordination" check** demanded by KTU questions.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Design Steps in Electrical Wiring

The complete design procedure, aligned with **IS 732:2019** and **IS 4648:1968**, is given below:

| Step | Design Activity | Governing Code | Key Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | Determine **total connected load** & apply **diversity factor** | IS 732 Cl. 4.1 | Design load (kW) |
| **2** | Decide **type of wiring system** (conduit, trunking, MI, casing-capping) | IS 732 Cl. 5.1 | Wiring layout |
| **3** | **Divide load into sub-circuits** (light/socket/power separation) | IS 732 Cl. 4.4 | Number of circuits |
| **4** | Calculate **full-load current per circuit** | $I = P/V$ or $I = P/(\sqrt{3}V\cos\phi)$ | I_FL (A) |
| **5** | **Select MCB/MCCB rating** ≥ 1.25 × I_FL | IS 8826 / IEC 60947-2 | Protective device |
| **6** | **Select cable size** (consider CCC, VD ≤ 3–5%, grouping derating) | IS 3961 (Pt II), IS 732 Cl. 5.5 | Conductor A (mm²) |
| **7** | **Select RCCB** (30 mA for personal, 100/300 mA for fire) | IS 12640 | Earth-fault device |
| **8** | **Earth-fault loop impedance check** ($Z_s \leq 50/U_0$) | IS 732 Cl. 6 | $Z_s$ verification |
| **9** | **Discrimination / Selectivity** study (upstream vs. downstream) | IEC 60364-5-53 | Trip-time grading |
| **10** | **Earthing design** (plate/rod, soil resistivity, conductor size) | IS 3043 | Earth resistance < 1 Ω (IT) / 5 Ω (TN) |
| **11** | **DB layout & schedule** (single-line diagram, phase balance) | IS 732 | Final SLDB |
| **12** | **Documentation** (GA drawing, wiring diagram, DB schedule, BOM) | KSEB / CEA | Statutory approval |

### 3.5 Standard Sub-Circuit Limits (IS 732 Cl. 4.4)

| Sub-Circuit Type | Max No. of Points | Max Load (W) | MCB Rating |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Lighting (5 A)** | 10 (or 800 W) | 800 | 6 A |
| **5 A Socket** (non-dedicated) | 2 | 500 | 6 A |
| **15 A Socket** (dedicated) | 1 | 1000 | 16 A / 20 A |
| **Power sub-circuit** | 1 | As per load | 20–32 A |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Phase-Balancing Rule:** When designing the DB, the total load on each of the three phases (R, Y, B) must be balanced within **± 5%** of each other. This minimises neutral current, reduces voltage unbalance, and is a **recurring KTU exam question**.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Typical LV Distribution with Protection

```mermaid
graph TD
    M1[Utility Supply 11kV] --> T1[Distribution Transformer 11kV 415V]
    T1 --> M2[Meter Board kWh MFM]
    M2 --> M3[Main Distribution Board MDB]
    M3 --> MCB1[MCCB 200A 25kA Triple Pole Main]
    MCB1 --> BUS[Copper Bus Bar 200A TP]
    BUS --> SB1[Sub DB 1 Lighting 16A MCBs]
    BUS --> SB2[Sub DB 2 Power 20A MCBs]
    BUS --> SB3[Sub DB 3 AC UPS 32A MCBs]
    SB1 --> RCCB1[RCCB 63A 30mA]
    SB2 --> RCCB2[RCCB 63A 30mA]
    SB3 --> RCCB3[RCCB 40A 30mA]
    RCCB1 --> MCB2[MCB 6A C Curve Light Circuit]
    RCCB2 --> MCB3[MCB 16A C Curve 5A Sockets]
    RCCB2 --> MCB4[MCB 20A C Curve 15A Sockets]
    RCCB3 --> MCB5[MCB 32A C Curve AC 1.5Ton]
    SB1 --> SPD1[Type 2 SPD 40kA 275V]
    M3 --> EARTH[Earthing System IS3043 Pipe Plate 5 Ohm]
    EARTH --> BUS
    EARTH --> SB1
    EARTH --> SB2
    EARTH --> SB3
```

> **Interpretation of Diagram:** This block diagram shows the hierarchical flow of power from the utility HT side through transformer, meter, MDB, MCCB, and busbar. The busbar feeds three Sub-DBs, each protected by a dedicated RCCB (for earth-fault protection) and downstream MCBs (for overload/short-circuit). A Type-2 SPD is installed at the MDB to arrest switching surges. A single earth pit (per IS 3043) is bonded to the MDB, Sub-DBs, and equipment earth terminals to provide a common equipotential zone.

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Logic of MCB Internal Trip Mechanism

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Current Flow through MCB] --> B{Bimetal Strip Heated by I Squared R}
    A --> C{Solenoid Energised by High Current}
    B -->|Slow Overload| D[Trip Latch Released]
    C -->|Instant Short Circuit| D
    D --> E[Contacts Open within 0.4s]
    E --> F[Arc Quenched by Arc Chute]
    F --> G[Circuit Isolated and De-energised]
```

### 4.3 SLD of a Typical 3-Phase DB Layout

```mermaid
graph TD
    IN[Incoming MCCB 100A TP] --> P1[Phase R Bus]
    IN --> P2[Phase Y Bus]
    IN --> P3[Phase B Bus]
    IN --> N[Neutral Bus]
    IN --> E[Earth Bus]
    P1 --> MCB1[MCB 20A R Phase Lighting]
    P2 --> MCB2[MCB 20A Y Phase Lighting]
    P3 --> MCB3[MCB 20A B Phase Lighting]
    P1 --> MCB4[MCB 16A R Phase 5A Sockets]
    P2 --> MCB5[MCB 16A Y Phase 5A Sockets]
    P3 --> MCB6[MCB 16A B Phase 5A Sockets]
    MCB1 --> RCCB1[RCCB 40A 30mA Lighting]
    MCB4 --> RCCB2[RCCB 40A 30mA Sockets]
    RCCB1 --> L1[Light Load R]
    RCCB1 --> L2[Light Load Y]
    RCCB1 --> L3[Light Load B]
    RCCB2 --> S1[Socket Load R]
    RCCB2 --> S2[Socket Load Y]
    RCCB2 --> S3[Socket Load B]
    N --> N1[Common Neutral Link]
    E --> E1[Common Earth Link]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The MCCB at the DB incomer feeds three phase buses (R, Y, B) and a common neutral + earth bus. Lighting MCBs (20 A C-curve) and socket MCBs (16 A C-curve) are distributed across the three phases to maintain **phase balance**. Each group is protected by a common 30 mA RCCB to provide earth-fault safety for personal protection.

### 4.4 Discrimination / Selectivity Study

```mermaid
graph TD
    UT[Utility 415V] --> M1[Main MCCB 250A Adjustable LSIG]
    M1 --> M2[Sub MCCB 100A Adjustable]
    M2 --> SDB[Sub DB MCB 40A C Curve]
    SDB --> F1[Final MCB 16A C Curve]
    SDB --> F2[Final MCB 10A C Curve]
    SDB --> F3[Final MCB 6A C Curve]
    F1 --> L1[AC Load 1.5 Ton]
    F2 --> L2[Geyser Load]
    F3 --> L3[Light Load]
    M1 -.->|Trip Time 0.5s| M1
    M2 -.->|Trip Time 0.15s| M2
    SDB -.->|Trip Time 0.05s| SDB
    F1 -.->|Trip Time 0.01s| F1
```

> **Interpretation:** A fault at **L1** should be cleared by **F1 (16 A MCB)** in ~10 ms, *without* tripping M2, SDB, or M1. The trip-time grading is achieved through **current discrimination** (inverse-time characteristics) and **time-grading** of upstream devices — a **discipline vital for KTU numerical problems**.
