<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

> **Define luminous intensity. State its SI unit and explain the term "steradian."**

**Model Answer:**

Luminous intensity is defined as the **luminous flux emitted by a source per unit solid angle in a specified direction**. It is given by:

$$
I = \frac{d\Phi}{d\Omega}
$$

**SI Unit:** The SI unit of luminous intensity is the **candela (cd)**, which is one of the seven SI base units. One candela equals one lumen per steradian ($1 \text{ cd} = 1 \text{ lm/sr}$).

**Steradian (sr):** A steradian is the SI unit of **solid angle**. It is defined as the solid angle subtended at the centre of a sphere by a surface area equal to the square of the radius of the sphere:

$$
\Omega = \frac{A}{r^2}
$$

The total solid angle around a point in three-dimensional space is $4\pi$ steradians (the full sphere).

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

> **Distinguish between Mean Spherical Candle Power (MSCP) and Mean Horizontal Candle Power (MHCP). State the relation between them for a rotationally symmetric source.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | MSCP (Mean Spherical Candle Power) | MHCP (Mean Horizontal Candle Power) |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Average of luminous intensity over the entire sphere ($4\pi$ sr) | Average of luminous intensity in the horizontal plane ($2\pi$ sr) |
| **Formula** | $I_{mscp} = \dfrac{\Phi_{total}}{4\pi}$ | $I_{mhcp} = \dfrac{\Phi_{horizontal}}{2\pi}$ |
| **Symmetry** | All directions weighted equally | Only the equatorial plane |
| **Use** | Used to compute total flux of a source | Used when the source has rotational symmetry |

**Relation for a rotationally symmetric source:**

$$
I_{mscp} = \frac{1}{2} \, I_{mhcp}
$$

This relation holds because the source's intensity depends only on the polar angle $\theta$, so the horizontal-plane intensity (at $\theta = 90°$) is twice the average over the full sphere.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and is split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. **Internal choice** is provided — you must answer either **Question A** or **Question B**.

---

### ❑ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

> **(a)** Derive the relationship between **luminous flux and luminous intensity** for an isotropic point source. A 100 W incandescent lamp emits a total luminous flux of 1500 lumens uniformly in all directions. Find its mean spherical candle power and the solid angle subtended by a cone of half-angle $25°$ at the centre. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A road lantern is mounted at a height of 8 m above the road. The lantern has a luminous intensity of 900 cd in a direction making $30°$ with the vertical (i.e., towards a road point). Calculate the illuminance on the road surface directly under that direction. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution to A(a):

**Derivation (4 marks):**

For an isotropic (uniform in all directions) point source, the total luminous flux $\Phi$ is emitted uniformly into a total solid angle of $4\pi$ steradians. By the definition of luminous intensity:

$$
I = \frac{d\Phi}{d\Omega}
$$

Integrating over the full sphere:

$$
\Phi = \int I \, d\Omega = I \int_{0}^{4\pi} d\Omega = I \cdot 4\pi
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\,I = \frac{\Phi}{4\pi}\,}
$$

**Calculation (3 marks):**

Given $\Phi = 1500$ lm:

$$
I_{mscp} = \frac{1500}{4\pi} = \frac{1500}{12.5664} \approx 119.37 \text{ cd}
$$

Solid angle of a cone of half-angle $\theta = 25°$:

$$
\omega = 2\pi (1 - \cos 25°) = 2\pi (1 - 0.9063) = 2\pi \times 0.0937 \approx 0.5887 \text{ sr}
$$

#### Solution to A(b):

**Given:**
- Mounting height $h = 8$ m
- Luminous intensity in the direction of interest: $I = 900$ cd
- Angle with vertical: $\theta = 30°$

**Distance from lantern to road point** (slant distance $d$):

$$
d = \frac{h}{\cos\theta} = \frac{8}{\cos 30°} = \frac{8}{0.8660} \approx 9.238 \text{ m}
$$

**Illuminance** using $E = \dfrac{I \cos\alpha}{d^2}$, where $\alpha$ is the angle between the light ray and the normal to the road surface. Since the road normal is vertical, and the light ray makes $30°$ with the vertical, $\alpha = 30°$:

$$
E = \frac{I \cos\theta}{d^2} = \frac{900 \times \cos 30°}{(9.238)^2} = \frac{900 \times 0.8660}{85.34} = \frac{779.4}{85.34} \approx 9.13 \text{ lux}
$$

**Verification using horizontal distance $x$:**

Horizontal distance: $x = h \tan\theta = 8 \tan 30° = 8 \times 0.5774 \approx 4.619$ m

Slant distance: $d = \sqrt{h^2 + x^2} = \sqrt{64 + 21.33} = \sqrt{85.33} \approx 9.238$ m ✓

**Final Answer for A(b):** $E \approx 9.13$ lux.

---

### ❑ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 — Alternative]`

> **(a)** Explain the concept of **solid angle**. Show that the total solid angle around a point in three-dimensional space is $4\pi$ steradians. A lamp has a Mean Horizontal Candle Power (MHCP) of 250 cd. If the lamp is rotationally symmetric, find its Mean Spherical Candle Power (MSCP) and total luminous flux. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Define **luminous intensity** and **illuminance**. A lamp of luminous intensity 500 cd in all directions is suspended 6 m above a floor. Find (i) the illuminance directly below the lamp, and (ii) the illuminance at a point on the floor 8 m away horizontally from the point directly below the lamp. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution to B(a):

**Concept of solid angle (2 marks):**

A solid angle is the three-dimensional analogue of a plane angle. It is the measure of the "spread" or "cone" of directions in 3D space, viewed from a point. The SI unit is the **steradian (sr)**. The solid angle subtended at the centre of a sphere of radius $r$ by a surface element of area $dA$ on the sphere is:

$$
d\Omega = \frac{dA}{r^2}
$$

**Proof that total solid angle = $4\pi$ (2 marks):**

The total surface area of a sphere of radius $r$ is $A = 4\pi r^2$. Substituting:

$$
\Omega_{total} = \frac{A_{total}}{r^2} = \frac{4\pi r^2}{r^2} = 4\pi \text{ sr}
$$

**Numerical calculation (3 marks):**

Given $I_{mhcp} = 250$ cd, and the source is rotationally symmetric:

$$
I_{mscp} = \frac{I_{mhcp}}{2} = \frac{250}{2} = 125 \text{ cd}
$$

Total luminous flux:

$$
\Phi = I_{mscp} \cdot 4\pi = 125 \times 4\pi = 125 \times 12.5664 \approx 1570.8 \text{ lm}
$$

#### Solution to B(b):

**Definitions (2 marks):**

- **Luminous intensity** ($I$): The luminous flux emitted by a source per unit solid angle in a given direction. SI unit: candela (cd).
- **Illuminance** ($E$): The luminous flux incident per unit area of a surface. SI unit: lux (lx) = lm/m².

**Part (i) — Illuminance directly below (2 marks):**

For a point source, illuminance on a surface perpendicular to the ray is:

$$
E_1 = \frac{I}{h^2} = \frac{500}{6^2} = \frac{500}{36} \approx 13.89 \text{ lux}
$$

(The floor is horizontal; the ray goes straight down, so it is perpendicular to the floor.)

**Part (ii) — Illuminance 8 m horizontally away (3 marks):**

Slant distance: $d = \sqrt{h^2 + x^2} = \sqrt{6^2 + 8^2} = \sqrt{36 + 64} = \sqrt{100} = 10$ m.

The angle $\theta$ between the ray and the vertical (i.e., the floor normal) satisfies:

$$
\cos\theta = \frac{h}{d} = \frac{6}{10} = 0.6
$$

Illuminance:

$$
E_2 = \frac{I \cos\theta}{d^2} = \frac{500 \times 0.6}{100} = \frac{300}{100} = 3.0 \text{ lux}
$$

**Final Answers for B(b):** (i) $E_1 \approx 13.89$ lux, (ii) $E_2 = 3.0$ lux.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes — Where Students Lose Marks in KTU ESE:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $\cos\theta$ factor in illuminance** (loses 2–3 marks). When light strikes a surface at an angle, the *projected area* is smaller, so illuminance is $E = \frac{I \cos\theta}{d^2}$, **not** $\frac{I}{d^2}$. Students frequently write the latter even when the geometry is oblique.
>
> 2. **Mixing up MHCP and MSCP**. MHCP is the average over the **horizontal plane only** ($2\pi$ sr), while MSCP is the average over the **whole sphere** ($4\pi$ sr). They are **not the same** for asymmetric sources. Use $I_{mscp} = \frac{1}{2} I_{mhcp}$ **only when the source is rotationally symmetric**.
>
> 3. **Wrong unit conversion of solid angle**. The total solid angle is $4\pi$ sr, not $4\pi$ "radians" and not $360°$. Cones use $\omega = 2\pi(1-\cos\theta)$, not $\pi r^2 \theta^2$ (the latter is a small-angle approximation).
>
> 4. **Confusing candela with lumen**. Candela measures *directional* intensity; lumen measures *total* flux. A laser pointer can have $10$ cd but only $0.01$ lm because the beam is so narrow.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to write units**. KTU examiners explicitly deduct for missing SI units. Always write **cd, lm, lx, sr, m, m²** at the end of every numerical answer.
>
> 6. **Not stating "isotropic" assumption**. If a problem says "a lamp emits X lumens" without giving a polar distribution, you must explicitly **state** the assumption of isotropy before using $I = \frac{\Phi}{4\pi}$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Luminous Intensity: 1-Page Rapid Revision**

- **Definition:** $I = \dfrac{d\Phi}{d\Omega}$ — flux per unit solid angle in a direction.
- **SI Unit:** **candela (cd)**, an SI base unit; $1 \text{ cd} = 1 \text{ lm/sr}$.
- **Solid angle:** $\Omega = \dfrac{A}{r^2}$, SI unit **steradian (sr)**.
- **Total solid angle around a point:** $\Omega_{total} = 4\pi$ sr.
- **Isotropic point source:** $I = \dfrac{\Phi}{4\pi}$ (uniform in all directions).
- **Cone of half-angle $\theta$:** solid angle $\omega = 2\pi(1 - \cos\theta)$.
- **MSCP** = average intensity over the full sphere $= \dfrac{\Phi_{total}}{4\pi}$.
- **MHCP** = average intensity in horizontal plane $= \dfrac{\Phi_{horiz}}{2\pi}$.
- **Symmetric source relation:** $I_{mscp} = \dfrac{1}{2} I_{mhcp}$ *(only for rotationally symmetric lamps)*.
- **Illuminance (point source):** $E = \dfrac{I}{d^2}$ on perpendicular surface; $E = \dfrac{I \cos\theta}{d^2}$ on inclined surface.
- **Inverse Square Law:** illuminance falls as the **square of distance** from a point source.
- **Real-world applications:** road lighting design, headlight aiming, projector design, goniophotometric measurements, luminaire I-tables.
- **Common pitfalls:** forgetting $\cos\theta$ in oblique incidence, mixing up cd vs lm, ignoring isotropy assumption, omitting SI units, and using MHCP/MSCP relations for asymmetric sources.
- **Numericals to practice:** (i) Given total lumens of isotropic lamp → find candela; (ii) Given MHCP of symmetric lamp → find total lumens via MSCP; (iii) Given height, horizontal distance, and intensity → find illuminance on a horizontal surface.
- **Examiner's most-asked concepts:** Definition of candela, relation $I = \Phi / 4\pi$, MSCP/MHCP distinction, derivation of total solid angle = $4\pi$ sr.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT752 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Illuminance, Illumination Calculation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Illuminance (Illumination)

**Illuminance (E)** is the quantity of luminous flux (Φ) incident per unit area on a surface. It quantifies *how brightly a surface is lit*, irrespective of the direction from which light arrives.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> Illuminance is defined as the **luminous flux received per unit area** of a surface. Mathematically,
>
> $$E = \dfrac{d\Phi}{dA}$$
>
> where $d\Phi$ is the luminous flux (in lumens) falling on an infinitesimal area $dA$ (in m²).

### Standard SI Unit of Illuminance
- **SI Unit:** **lux (lx)** = 1 lumen / m² = 1 lm/m²
- **CGS / FPS Unit:** **foot-candle (fc)** or **lumen/ft²**
- **Conversion:** 1 foot-candle ≈ **10.76 lux**

| Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit | Symbol of Unit |
|----------|--------|---------|----------------|
| Luminous Flux | $\Phi$ | lumen | lm |
| Luminous Intensity | $I$ | candela | cd |
| **Illuminance** | $E$ | **lux** | **lx** |
| Luminance | $L$ | candela/m² | cd/m² |

> [!NOTE]
> **Recommended Illuminance Levels (IS 3646 / IS 6665 Standards):**
> - Offices / Classrooms : **300 – 500 lux**
> - Drawing offices : **500 – 750 lux**
> - Corridors / Stairs : **100 – 150 lux**
> - Street lighting : **15 – 30 lux**
> - Industrial (fine work) : **500 – 1000 lux**
> - Hospital operation theatre : **1000+ lux**

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: Illuminance vs. Luminous Flux

**Conceptual Analogy (The Water-Pouring Analogy):**
Imagine luminous flux is **rain water** falling from the sky, and the floor of your room is the **surface**. 

- **Luminous flux (Φ)** is the *total amount of water* that pours down (measured in litres, just as flux is in lumens).
- **Illuminance (E)** is the *intensity of water collected per square metre of floor* (measured as litres/m², just as illuminance is in lumens/m² = lux).

If you pour 1000 litres of water onto a small 1 m² area, the surface gets drenched — high *illuminance*. Spread the same 1000 litres over 100 m² and the floor just gets damp — low *illuminance*. Same flux, different illuminance, depending on the area over which it is spread.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** 
> - **Flux** = Total light "produced" or "delivered" (lm) 
> - **Illuminance** = Light "arriving" on a surface per unit area (lux = lm/m²)
> - Flux is the **cause**, illuminance is the **effect** on the surface.

### Why Illuminance Matters in Design
The human eye perceives brightness not by flux alone, but by how much light actually **falls on the task surface** (a book, a lathe, a road). Therefore, every lighting design — indoor, outdoor, industrial — begins by specifying the *required illuminance in lux* on the working plane.

---

## 1.3 Why Illumination Calculation is Essential in Electrical System Design

In KTU's *Electrical System Design* course, the **lighting design load** is one of the major contributors to a building's total electrical load. An accurate **illumination calculation** is required to:
1. Determine the **number, type, and wattage of lamps** to be installed.
2. Estimate the **lighting load in watts** to size MCBs, cables, and bus-bars.
3. Verify **uniformity of illumination** across the working plane.
4. Optimise **energy consumption** and ensure compliance with the Energy Conservation Building Code (ECBC).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 scheme specifically tests the student's ability to (a) apply the **Inverse Square Law** and **Lambert's Cosine Law** to compute illuminance at a point, and (b) use the **Lumen Method** to size the number of luminaires for a room. Both numerical and descriptive questions appear regularly in the End Semester Examination (ESE).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Laws of Illumination

The behaviour of light emitted by a **point source** is governed by two foundational laws, which form the mathematical backbone of every illumination calculation.

### 2.1.1 The Inverse Square Law

**Statement:** *The illuminance at a point on a surface is inversely proportional to the square of the distance between the point source and the surface*, provided the light ray strikes the surface normally (perpendicular).

**Derivation Logic:**
1. Consider a point source $S$ emitting light uniformly in all directions.
2. The luminous flux $\Phi$ passes through a sphere of radius $r$ centred on $S$. The total surface area of the sphere is $4\pi r^2$.
3. Flux per unit area at distance $r$ (which equals the illuminance $E$ on a surface normal to the rays at that point) is:
4. As $r$ doubles, the same flux spreads over $4 \times$ the area, so illuminance drops to $1/4^{th}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Inverse Square Law — Governing Equation:**
> $$E = \dfrac{I}{d^2}$$
> where $I$ = luminous intensity (cd) along the direction of the ray, and $d$ = distance (m) from source to point.

**The "Why" Behind It:** This is purely a *geometry of spheres* effect. Since a sphere's surface area scales with $r^2$, the same flux must thin out as $1/r^2$ to cover the larger spherical surface.

### 2.1.2 Lambert's Cosine Law (Law of Incidence)

**Statement:** *The illuminance on a surface is proportional to the cosine of the angle of incidence* $\theta$ between the incident ray and the normal to the surface.

**Why It Happens:** When a light ray strikes a surface at an angle $\theta$ from the normal, the *effective area* intercepting the beam becomes larger by a factor of $1/\cos\theta$, so illuminance on the *actual* (tilted) surface is reduced by $\cos\theta$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lambert's Cosine Law — Governing Equation:**
> $$E = \dfrac{I \cdot \cos\theta}{d^2}$$
> where $\theta$ = angle between the incident ray and the normal to the illuminated surface.

**Combined Effect (Most Frequently Tested Form):**
$$E_p = \dfrac{I \cdot \cos\theta}{d^2}$$

This single equation is the workhorse of the **point-by-point method** of illumination design.

---

## 2.2 Illumination Calculation Methods

There are two principal methods taught in the KTU syllabus:

| Method | Best Suited For | Inputs Required | Output |
|--------|----------------|-----------------|--------|
| **Lumen Method (Flux Method)** | Indoor, uniform, area lighting | Room area, mounting height, lamp lumens, CU, MF | Number of lamps / luminaires |
| **Point-by-Point Method** | Outdoor / spot / street / task lighting | Lamp intensity, positions, distances, angles | Illuminance at specific points |

---

## 2.3 The Lumen Method — Step-by-Step Logic

The Lumen Method (also called the **Lumen Output Method**) is used when *uniform general illumination* is required over a horizontal working plane (e.g., an office floor, a workshop).

**Step 1 — Specify the required illuminance $E$** (lux) on the working plane.
**Step 2 — Compute the area to be illuminated** $A$ (m²).
**Step 3 — Compute the total lumens required** to fall on the working plane:
$$\Phi_{required} = E \times A \;(\text{lumens})$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Coefficient of Utilization (CU or UF)** to account for light absorbed by walls, ceiling, and the geometry of the room:
$$\Phi_{lamp\ output} = \dfrac{E \times A}{CU}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Maintenance Factor (MF)** to compensate for lamp lumen depreciation, dirt accumulation on luminaires, and reduced reflectance over time:
$$\Phi_{lamp\ output} = \dfrac{E \times A}{CU \times MF}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the number of lamps:**
$$N = \dfrac{\Phi_{lamp\ output}}{n \times \Phi_L}$$
where $n$ = number of lamps per luminaire, and $\Phi_L$ = rated lumens per lamp.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Lumen Method Formula (most-tested):**
> $$\boxed{N = \dfrac{E \times A}{n \times \Phi_L \times CU \times MF}}$$

### 2.3.1 Key Factors Explained

**(a) Coefficient of Utilization (CU) / Utilization Factor (UF):**
$$CU = \dfrac{\text{Lumens received on the working plane}}{\text{Lumens emitted by the lamps}}$$
Typical values: **0.4 to 0.8**, depending on room index, wall reflectance, ceiling reflectance.

**(b) Maintenance Factor (MF):**
$$MF = \dfrac{\text{Illuminance after depreciation}}{\text{Initial illuminance}} = \dfrac{1}{1 + \text{depreciation fraction}}$$
Typical values: **0.7 to 0.8** for clean offices, **0.6 to 0.7** for industrial/dirty environments.

**(c) Depreciation Factor (DF):**
$$DF = \dfrac{1}{MF}$$
So $MF = 0.8$ implies $DF = 1.25$ — meaning the *initial* lamp output must be 1.25× the *maintained* value to ensure the illuminance never falls below the design level before relamping.

**(d) Space-to-Height Ratio (SHR or SHR):**
The ratio of the spacing between luminaires to their mounting height above the working plane. The *maximum permissible SHR* is specified by the luminaire manufacturer to ensure acceptable uniformity. A typical value is **1.0 to 1.5** for direct lighting.

---

## 2.4 The Point-by-Point Method — Step-by-Step Logic

Used for **outdoor lighting** (streets, floodlights) or when a specific point on the working plane must meet a stated illuminance.

**Step 1 —** Identify the position of the lamp (height $h$, horizontal offset $x$ from the point $P$).
**Step 2 —** Compute the slant distance: $d = \sqrt{h^2 + x^2}$
**Step 3 —** Compute the angle of incidence: $\cos\theta = h/d$
**Step 4 —** Apply the combined law: $E = \dfrac{I \cdot \cos\theta}{d^2} = \dfrac{I \cdot h}{(h^2 + x^2)^{3/2}}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Point-by-Point Master Equation:**
> $$E = \dfrac{I \cdot h}{(h^2 + x^2)^{3/2}}$$
> where $h$ = vertical mounting height (m), $x$ = horizontal distance (m) of point $P$ from the foot of the lamp pole, and $I$ = luminous intensity (cd) of the lamp in the direction of $P$.

For **multiple lamps**, the total illuminance at a point is the *scalar sum*:
$$E_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \dfrac{I_i \cdot \cos\theta_i}{d_i^{\,2}}$$

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Office / Classroom design:** Lumen method → ensures uniform 300–500 lux on desks.
- **Street lighting:** Point-by-point method → ensures minimum 15–30 lux on the road.
- **Stadium / Floodlighting:** Combines both methods with symmetrical/asymmetrical intensity distributions.
- **Industrial inspection:** Higher illuminance (1000 lux) with local task lighting supplements the general design.
- **Energy audits:** Comparing actual lux measurements with design lux reveals the *maintenance factor* in real installations.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---------|---------------|-------|
| 1 | $E = \Phi / A$ | Average illuminance on a surface | lux |
| 2 | $E = I / d^2$ | Inverse square law (point source, normal incidence) | lux |
| 3 | $E = I \cos\theta / d^2$ | Lambert's cosine law combined with inverse square | lux |
| 4 | $E = I h / (h^2 + x^2)^{3/2}$ | Illuminance at horizontal offset $x$ below lamp at height $h$ | lux |
| 5 | $E_{total} = \sum I_i \cos\theta_i / d_i^{\,2}$ | Superposition from multiple sources | lux |
| 6 | $N = E A / (n \Phi_L \cdot CU \cdot MF)$ | Number of lamps (Lumen Method) | lamps |
| 7 | $CU = \Phi_{working\ plane} / \Phi_{lamps}$ | Coefficient of utilization | dimensionless |
| 8 | $MF = 1 / DF$ | Maintenance factor vs. depreciation factor | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\Phi_{lamp} = E A / (CU \times MF)$ | Total lamp lumens required | lumens |
| 10 | $SHR = S / h$ | Space-to-height ratio for luminaire spacing | dimensionless |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Shortcut:** In the Lumen Method, students often confuse $MF$ and $DF$. 
> - **MF < 1** (e.g., 0.8) → *reduces* the effective lumens, so you need *more* lamps. 
> - **DF > 1** (e.g., 1.25) → the *initial* lumens must exceed *maintained* by 25%. 
> Use whichever is given; if only depreciation is mentioned, the Lumen Method formula uses **MF in the denominator**.

---

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Inverse Square Law

**Starting point:** A point source $S$ emits total luminous flux $\Phi$ uniformly in all directions (i.e., it is an *isotropic* point source with luminous intensity $I$ in every direction).

**Step 1 — Luminous intensity of an isotropic source:**
By definition, the luminous intensity is the flux emitted per unit solid angle:
$$I = \dfrac{\Phi}{4\pi} \;\;\;\; \text{(cd)}$$

**Step 2 — Flux intercepted by a small area on a sphere of radius $d$:**
A sphere of radius $d$ has surface area $A_{sphere} = 4\pi d^2$. The flux per unit area on this sphere (which is exactly the illuminance on a small patch *normal* to the rays) is:
$$E = \dfrac{\Phi}{4\pi d^2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the intensity from Step 1:**
$$E = \dfrac{4\pi I}{4\pi d^2} = \dfrac{I}{d^2}$$

**Conclusion:** 
$$\boxed{E = \dfrac{I}{d^2} \;\;\;\; \text{(lux)}}$$

**Dimensional check:** $I$ is in cd = lm/sr. Since $4\pi$ sr is a full sphere, $I$ in lm/sr × sr/m² simplifies to lm/m² = lux. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Lambert's Cosine Law

**Setup:** A point source $S$ at distance $d$ from a small plane surface of area $A$, with the line $SA$ making an angle $\theta$ with the normal $n$ to the surface.

**Step 1 — Flux falling on the area (ray perpendicular to it):**
If the surface were *normal* to the ray, illuminance would be $E_\perp = I / d^2$, and flux received would be:
$$\Delta\Phi_\perp = E_\perp \cdot A = \dfrac{I \cdot A}{d^2}$$

**Step 2 — Tilt the surface by angle $\theta$:**
When the surface is tilted, the *projected* (effective) area as seen from the source becomes $A \cos\theta$. The flux that this tilted surface intercepts is therefore:
$$\Delta\Phi = E_\perp \cdot (A \cos\theta) = \dfrac{I \cdot A \cos\theta}{d^2}$$

**Step 3 — Illuminance on the tilted surface:**
$$E_\theta = \dfrac{\Delta\Phi}{A} = \dfrac{I \cos\theta}{d^2}$$

**Conclusion:**
$$\boxed{E_\theta = \dfrac{I \cos\theta}{d^2} \;\;\;\; \text{(lux)}}$$

**Physical interpretation:** At grazing incidence ($\theta \to 90°$), $\cos\theta \to 0$, so $E \to 0$ — light is reflected away or barely interacts. At normal incidence ($\theta = 0°$), $\cos\theta = 1$ and we recover the inverse square law.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Horizontal-Offset Illuminance Formula

This is the **most-tested form** in KTU point-by-point problems.

**Setup:** A lamp at height $h$ above the ground. A point $P$ on the ground is at horizontal distance $x$ from the foot of the pole. The ray $SP$ has length $d$ and makes an angle $\theta$ with the vertical (i.e., with the normal to the horizontal ground).

**Step 1 — Slant distance from geometry:**
By Pythagoras:
$$d = \sqrt{h^2 + x^2}$$

**Step 2 — Cosine of the incidence angle:**
The vertical $h$ is adjacent to the angle $\theta$ in the right triangle, and the hypotenuse is $d$. Hence:
$$\cos\theta = \dfrac{h}{d} = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{h^2 + x^2}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into Lambert's cosine + inverse square equation:**
$$E = \dfrac{I \cos\theta}{d^2} = \dfrac{I}{d^2} \cdot \dfrac{h}{d} = \dfrac{I \cdot h}{d^3}$$

**Step 4 — Replace $d$ with $\sqrt{h^2 + x^2}$:**
$$E = \dfrac{I \cdot h}{\left(\sqrt{h^2 + x^2}\right)^3} = \dfrac{I \cdot h}{(h^2 + x^2)^{3/2}}$$

**Final Master Equation:**
$$\boxed{E = \dfrac{I \cdot h}{(h^2 + x^2)^{3/2}} \;\;\;\; \text{(lux)}}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the Lumen Method Formula

**Step 1 — Required flux on the working plane:**
For an area $A$ requiring an average illuminance $E$:
$$\Phi_{req} = E \times A$$

**Step 2 — Account for utilisation losses:**
Only a fraction $CU$ of the lamp flux reaches the working plane. Hence the *lamp flux* $\Phi_L^{total}$ must be:
$$\Phi_L^{total} \times CU = \Phi_{req} \;\Rightarrow\; \Phi_L^{total} = \dfrac{E \cdot A}{CU}$$

**Step 3 — Account for maintenance / depreciation:**
Over the lamp's life, the maintained flux is $MF$ times the initial flux. So the *initial* lamp flux must be larger:
$$\Phi_L^{total} \times MF = \dfrac{E \cdot A}{CU} \;\Rightarrow\; \Phi_L^{total} = \dfrac{E \cdot A}{CU \times MF}$$

**Step 4 — Convert lamp flux to number of lamps:**
If each luminaire has $n$ lamps, and each lamp emits $\Phi_L$ lumens, then the number of luminaires is:
$$N = \dfrac{\Phi_L^{total}}{n \times \Phi_L} = \dfrac{E \cdot A}{n \times \Phi_L \times CU \times MF}$$

**Conclusion:**
$$\boxed{N = \dfrac{E \cdot A}{n \cdot \Phi_L \cdot CU \cdot MF}}$$

---

## 3.5 Solved Analytical Example (Worked-Out, KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A classroom $10 \text{ m} \times 8 \text{ m}$ requires an average illuminance of **300 lux**. Each fluorescent luminaire holds **2 lamps** rated at **2500 lumens each**. Given $CU = 0.5$ and $MF = 0.8$, determine (a) the number of luminaires required, and (b) the total lamp load in watts if each lamp is 40 W.

**Step 1 — Area:**
$$A = 10 \times 8 = 80 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Total lumens required on working plane:**
$$\Phi_{req} = E \times A = 300 \times 80 = 24{,}000 \text{ lumens}$$

**Step 3 — Apply CU:**
$$\Phi_{lamp\ output} = \dfrac{24{,}000}{0.5} = 48{,}000 \text{ lumens}$$

**Step 4 — Apply MF (initial design lumens):**
$$\Phi_{lamp\ output} = \dfrac{48{,}000}{0.8} = 60{,}000 \text{ lumens}$$

**Step 5 — Number of luminaires:**
Each luminaire has $n = 2$ lamps, each producing $\Phi_L = 2500$ lm, so:
$$\text{Lumens per luminaire} = 2 \times 2500 = 5000 \text{ lm}$$
$$N = \dfrac{60{,}000}{5{,}000} = 12 \text{ luminaires}$$

**Step 6 — Total lamp load:**
$$\text{Total lamps} = 12 \times 2 = 24 \text{ lamps}$$
$$\text{Load} = 24 \times 40 = 960 \text{ W}$$

**Answers:** (a) **12 luminaires**, (b) **960 W**.

---

## 3.6 Solved Point-by-Point Example

**Problem:** A street lamp is mounted at a height of **8 m** with a luminous intensity of **300 cd** in the direction of a point $P$ located **6 m** horizontally from the foot of the pole. Find the illuminance at $P$.

**Step 1 — Slant distance:**
$$d = \sqrt{8^2 + 6^2} = \sqrt{64 + 36} = \sqrt{100} = 10 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Cosine of incidence angle:**
$$\cos\theta = \dfrac{h}{d} = \dfrac{8}{10} = 0.8$$

**Step 3 — Apply the master equation (Method A):**
$$E = \dfrac{I \cdot \cos\theta}{d^2} = \dfrac{300 \times 0.8}{10^2} = \dfrac{240}{100} = 2.4 \text{ lux}$$

**Step 3 (alt) — Direct master formula (Method B):**
$$E = \dfrac{I \cdot h}{(h^2 + x^2)^{3/2}} = \dfrac{300 \times 8}{(100)^{3/2}} = \dfrac{2400}{1000} = 2.4 \text{ lux} \;\;\; \checkmark$$

**Answer:** Illuminance at $P$ = **2.4 lux**.

---

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture: Illumination Calculation Workflow

The diagram below maps the **end-to-end decision flow** that an electrical designer follows when computing illumination — from problem statement to final lamp quantity.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start Lighting Design Task"] --> B["Identify Working Plane and Area A in m2"]
    B --> C["Specify Required Illuminance E in lux from IS 3646"]
    C --> D{"Uniform Area Lighting OR Single Point Check"}
    D -- Uniform Area --> E["Apply Lumen Method"]
    D -- Single Point --> F["Apply Point by Point Method"]
    E --> E1["Compute Phi required = E times A"]
    E1 --> E2["Adjust for CU and MF"]
    E2 --> E3["Phi lamp output = E A divided by CU times MF"]
    E3 --> E4["Choose lamp wattage and lumens per lamp"]
    E4 --> E5["Number of luminaires N = Phi lamp output divided by n times Phi L"]
    E5 --> E6["Verify Space to Height Ratio for spacing S"]
    E6 --> E7["Compute Lighting Load in kW and size cable"]
    F --> F1["Find slant distance d = sqrt h2 plus x2"]
    F1 --> F2["Compute cos theta = h divided by d"]
    F2 --> F3["Apply E = I cos theta divided by d squared"]
    F3 --> F4{"Multiple lamps at point"}
    F4 -- Yes --> F5["Sum contributions E total = Sigma I cos theta divided by d squared"]
    F4 -- No --> F6["Report single lamp illuminance at point"]
    F5 --> F7["Compare with IS 3646 minimum and conclude"]
    F6 --> F7
    E7 --> G["Final Design Output"]
    F7 --> G
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Point-by-Point Geometry

The block-level architecture below shows how **geometric inputs** ($h$, $x$, $I$) flow through the **inverse square law** and **cosine law** blocks to produce the illuminance at point $P$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H["Mounting height h metres"] --> G1["Geometry block d equals sqrt of h2 plus x2"]
    X["Horizontal offset x metres"] --> G1
    G1 --> D1["Slant distance d"]
    D1 --> CB["Cosine law block cos theta equals h divided by d"]
    H --> CB
    CB --> CT["cos theta value"]
    I["Luminous intensity I candela"] --> ISQ["Inverse square block 1 divided by d squared"]
    D1 --> ISQ
    ISQ --> IT["1 divided by d squared"]
    CT --> ML["Master law multiplier E equals I cos theta divided by d squared"]
    IT --> ML
    ML --> OUT["Illuminance at P in lux"]
```

---

## 4.3 Comparative Schematic: Lumen Method vs Point-by-Point Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    LM["Lumen Method"] --> LM1["Average illuminance over an area"]
    LM1 --> LM2["Inputs: A, E, Phi L, CU, MF"]
    LM2 --> LM3["Output: Number of luminaires N"]
    LM3 --> LM4["Best for indoor general lighting"]
    PP["Point by Point Method"] --> PP1["Illuminance at a specific point"]
    PP1 --> PP2["Inputs: I, h, x or d and theta"]
    PP2 --> PP3["Output: E at one location in lux"]
    PP3 --> PP4["Best for outdoor street and flood lighting"]
    LM4 --> SUM["Both feed into final cable and MCB sizing"]
    PP4 --> SUM
```

---

## 4.4 Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Method

| Design Situation | Recommended Method | Primary Output |
|------------------|--------------------|----------------|
| Office / classroom uniform lighting | Lumen Method | Number of luminaires |
| Workshop / assembly hall | Lumen Method + check by Point-by-Point | Number of luminaires + uniformity |
| Street lighting design | Point-by-Point Method | Illuminance along the road |
| Stadium / floodlight | Point-by-Point Method (with grid) | Iso-illuminance contours |
| Task lighting at a machine | Point-by-Point Method | Local lux on the task |

---

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEEET753 equivalent module]**
**State and explain the inverse square law of illumination. Mention any two assumptions made in deriving the law.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement (1 mark):** The inverse square law states that the illuminance $E$ at a point on a surface, due to a point source, is *inversely proportional to the square of the distance* $d$ between the source and the point, provided the light falls normally on the surface.

**Equation (1 mark):** $E = I / d^2$ where $I$ is the luminous intensity (cd) and $d$ is the distance (m).

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. The source is a true *point source* (negligible physical dimensions).
2. The light is emitted uniformly in all directions (isotropic).
3. The receiving surface is *normal* to the incident ray.
4. There is no absorption or scattering in the medium (e.g., clean air).

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2]**
**Define the term *coefficient of utilization*. What is its typical range?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** The coefficient of utilization (CU), also called the *utilization factor*, is defined as the ratio of the *total luminous flux reaching the working plane* to the *total luminous flux emitted by the lamps*.

$$CU = \dfrac{\text{Lumens received on the working plane}}{\text{Lumens emitted by the lamps}}$$

**Typical range (1 mark):** $CU$ generally lies between **0.4 and 0.8** for most practical indoor installations, depending on the room index, luminaire type, and the reflectance of walls and ceiling.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> In Part B questions, students *frequently lose 2–3 marks* for:
> 1. **Confusing MF and DF** — remember, the Lumen Method formula uses $MF$ in the denominator, *not* $DF$.
> 2. **Forgetting to multiply $n$ (lamps per luminaire)** — the formula gives the number of *luminaires*, but if the question asks for *number of lamps*, you must multiply by $n$.
> 3. **Unit mismatch in inverse square law** — $d$ must be in *metres*; if the question gives $d$ in cm, convert it first.
> 4. **Mixing up the two cosine laws** — $\cos\theta$ in Lambert's law uses the angle from the *normal*, not from the surface.
> 5. **Not summing contributions from multiple lamps** in point-by-point problems — every lamp within range contributes, and the illuminances are *added* (superposition).

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, PEEOT752 Module 2]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* State and derive the **Lambert's cosine law of illumination**. A lamp with a luminous intensity of **500 cd** in a particular direction is located at a distance of **4 m** from a surface. Calculate the illuminance produced on the surface when the light is incident at an angle of **60°** to the normal.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* An office room of dimensions **15 m × 10 m × 3.5 m** (height) is to be illuminated to a level of **450 lux**. Using **fluorescent tube luminaires**, each having **3 lamps of 40 W** with an output of **3000 lumens per lamp**, and assuming a coefficient of utilization of **0.6** and a maintenance factor of **0.75**, determine:
- (i) the number of luminaires required,
- (ii) the total connected load, and
- (iii) the spacing between luminaires if they are arranged in a square pattern.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

### Part (a) — Lambert's Cosine Law

**Statement (2 marks):** Lambert's cosine law states that the illuminance $E$ on a surface is *directly proportional to the cosine of the angle of incidence* $\theta$ between the incident ray and the *normal* to the surface, other factors being constant.

**Derivation (3 marks):**

Consider a point source $S$ at distance $d$ from a small plane area $A$ whose normal $n$ makes an angle $\theta$ with the line $SA$.

If the surface were *normal* to the ray, illuminance would be:
$$E_\perp = \dfrac{I}{d^2}$$

When the surface is tilted, only its *projection* along the ray direction intercepts light. The projected (effective) area is $A \cos\theta$. Hence the flux intercepted is:
$$\Delta\Phi = E_\perp \cdot (A \cos\theta) = \dfrac{I \cdot A \cos\theta}{d^2}$$

Illuminance on the tilted surface:
$$E = \dfrac{\Delta\Phi}{A} = \dfrac{I \cos\theta}{d^2}$$

**Numerical problem (2 marks):**
Given $I = 500 \text{ cd}$, $d = 4 \text{ m}$, $\theta = 60°$, so $\cos 60° = 0.5$.
$$E = \dfrac{500 \times 0.5}{4^2} = \dfrac{250}{16} = 15.625 \text{ lux}$$

---

### Part (b) — Lumen Method

**Given:**
- Room area: $A = 15 \times 10 = 150 \text{ m}^2$
- Required illuminance: $E = 450 \text{ lux}$
- $n = 3$ lamps per luminaire
- $\Phi_L = 3000$ lumens per lamp
- $CU = 0.6$, $MF = 0.75$

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Total lumens required on working plane:**
$$\Phi_{req} = E \times A = 450 \times 150 = 67{,}500 \text{ lumens}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Apply CU and MF:**
$$\Phi_{lamp\ output} = \dfrac{67{,}500}{0.6 \times 0.75} = \dfrac{67{,}500}{0.45} = 150{,}000 \text{ lumens}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Lumens per luminaire:**
$$n \times \Phi_L = 3 \times 3000 = 9000 \text{ lumens/luminaire}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Number of luminaires:**
$$N = \dfrac{150{,}000}{9000} = 16.67 \approx 17 \text{ luminaires}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Total connected load:**
$$\text{Total lamps} = 17 \times 3 = 51 \text{ lamps}$$
$$\text{Load} = 51 \times 40 = 2040 \text{ W} = 2.04 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6 (2 marks) — Spacing (square pattern):**
For 17 luminaires in a square pattern, the nearest perfect square is **16 = 4 × 4** (acceptable for uniform distribution) or 17 arranged as **4 × 4 + 1**.

Using 16 luminaires in 4 rows × 4 columns:
- Spacing along length: $S_L = 15 / 4 = 3.75 \text{ m}$
- Spacing along width: $S_W = 10 / 4 = 2.5 \text{ m}$

*Average spacing* (for 17 fittings, treating as 4×4 grid + 1 spare):
$$S \approx 2.74 \text{ m (rounded)}$$

Verify against space-to-height ratio: working plane is at ~0.85 m above floor, so mounting height $\approx 3.5 - 0.85 = 2.65$ m. Required $SHR \le 1.5$, so max $S = 1.5 \times 2.65 \approx 3.97$ m. Our spacing of 2.74 m is well within limit. ✓

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, PEEOT752 Module 2]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Two lamps $L_1$ and $L_2$ are mounted at the **same height of 6 m** above the ground and are separated by a **horizontal distance of 12 m**. Lamp $L_1$ has an intensity of **400 cd** and $L_2$ has **600 cd**, both directed vertically downward. Calculate the **total illuminance on the ground at a point $P$ that is 4 m from $L_1$** (along the line joining the two lamps).

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A drawing office of area **20 m × 12 m** requires a maintained illuminance of **750 lux**. Three-lamp fluorescent luminaires, each lamp rated at **36 W, 3200 lumens**, are to be used. If the coefficient of utilization is **0.55** and the maintenance factor is **0.7**, find:
- (i) the number of luminaires required,
- (ii) the spacing if luminaires are arranged in a regular rectangular pattern with 2:1 length-to-width ratio.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

### Part (a) — Superposition with Two Lamps

**Setup:** $h = 6$ m. Point $P$ is 4 m from $L_1$ along the line $L_1 L_2$. So $P$ is at distance $|12 - 4| = 8$ m from $L_2$.

**Contribution from $L_1$ (2 marks):**
$$d_1 = \sqrt{h^2 + x_1^{\,2}} = \sqrt{36 + 16} = \sqrt{52} \approx 7.211 \text{ m}$$
$$\cos\theta_1 = \dfrac{h}{d_1} = \dfrac{6}{7.211} \approx 0.8322$$
$$E_1 = \dfrac{I_1 \cos\theta_1}{d_1^{\,2}} = \dfrac{400 \times 0.8322}{52} \approx 6.40 \text{ lux}$$

**Contribution from $L_2$ (2 marks):**
$$d_2 = \sqrt{h^2 + x_2^{\,2}} = \sqrt{36 + 64} = \sqrt{100} = 10 \text{ m}$$
$$\cos\theta_2 = \dfrac{6}{10} = 0.6$$
$$E_2 = \dfrac{600 \times 0.6}{100} = 3.6 \text{ lux}$$

**Total illuminance (1 mark):**
$$E_{total} = E_1 + E_2 = 6.40 + 3.6 \approx 10.0 \text{ lux}$$

**Conclusion (2 marks):** Total illuminance at $P$ ≈ **10 lux**. This is sufficient for corridor/street lighting but inadequate for office work, indicating the need for closer-spaced or higher-intensity lamps.

---

### Part (b) — Lumen Method (Drawing Office)

**Given:**
- Area $A = 20 \times 12 = 240 \text{ m}^2$
- $E = 750$ lux
- $n = 3$ lamps/luminaire, $\Phi_L = 3200$ lm/lamp
- $CU = 0.55$, $MF = 0.7$

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Lumens required:**
$$\Phi_{req} = 750 \times 240 = 180{,}000 \text{ lumens}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Apply CU × MF:**
$$\Phi_{lamp\ output} = \dfrac{180{,}000}{0.55 \times 0.7} = \dfrac{180{,}000}{0.385} \approx 467{,}532 \text{ lumens}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Number of luminaires:**
$$N = \dfrac{467{,}532}{3 \times 3200} = \dfrac{467{,}532}{9600} \approx 48.7 \approx 49 \text{ luminaires}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Rectangular pattern, 2:1 ratio:**
Let the grid be $2k \times k$ luminaires. Then $2k \times k = 48$ (nearest practical, taking 48 instead of 49 for a clean grid).
- $k^2 = 24 \Rightarrow k = \sqrt{24} \approx 4.9$
- Round to $2k = 10$, $k = 5$, giving $10 \times 5 = 50$ luminaires (slightly over-designed, but acceptable for uniform distribution).

**Spacing:**
- Spacing along length (20 m): $S_L = 20 / 10 = 2.0$ m
- Spacing along width (12 m): $S_W = 12 / 5 = 2.4$ m

**Step 5 (2 marks) — Verification:**
Mounting height above working plane $\approx 3.5 - 0.85 = 2.65$ m. Maximum SHR $\approx 1.5$, so maximum $S = 1.5 \times 2.65 = 3.98$ m. Both $S_L = 2.0$ m and $S_W = 2.4$ m are within limit. ✓

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print / Memorise Before ESE):**

- **Illuminance** $E$ is *flux per unit area* — unit **lux (lm/m²)**. Not to be confused with luminous intensity (cd) or luminance (cd/m²).
- **Inverse Square Law:** $E = I / d^2$ — valid only for *point sources* striking a surface *normally*. If the source is far away but not a true point, the error is small.
- **Lambert's Cosine Law:** $E = I \cos\theta / d^2$ — $\theta$ is measured from the *normal* to the surface. At grazing incidence ($\theta = 90°$), illuminance is zero.
- **Master horizontal-offset equation:** $E = I h / (h^2 + x^2)^{3/2}$ — use this directly for street/floodlight problems.
- **Lumen Method formula:** $N = E A / (n \Phi_L \cdot CU \cdot MF)$ — gives number of *luminaires*; multiply by $n$ for total *lamps*.
- **CU (Utilization Factor)** ranges 0.4 to 0.8. It accounts for the light *absorbed by walls, ceiling, and luminaire itself*. It is read from manufacturer tables, given the *room index* $K = (L \times W) / [h \times (L + W)]$.
- **MF (Maintenance Factor)** is the ratio of *maintained* to *initial* illuminance; its value is < 1 (typically 0.6–0.8). Its reciprocal is the **Depreciation Factor (DF)**.
- **Space-to-Height Ratio (SHR)** = $S / h_{working}$. Luminaire manufacturer specifies a *maximum permissible SHR*; the actual spacing must be ≤ this value to ensure uniformity.
- **Superposition principle:** For multiple lamps, total illuminance at a point is the *arithmetic sum* of individual contributions. Always include *every* lamp whose light reaches the point.
- **Recommended lux levels (IS 3646):** Offices 300–500, Drawing 500–750, Industrial 500–1000, Street 15–30, Operation Theatre 1000+. Memorise at least the office and street values.
- **Practical design steps:** (1) Find $E$ from standards, (2) compute $A$, (3) compute lumens required, (4) divide by $CU \times MF$, (5) choose lamp wattage/lumens, (6) compute $N$, (7) verify SHR, (8) compute total load for cable sizing.
- **Common mistake to avoid:** In the Lumen Method, do NOT confuse $MF$ with $DF$. If the problem gives *depreciation = 25%*, then $MF = 0.75$ (not 1.25), and $MF$ goes in the denominator.
- **Conversion to remember:** 1 foot-candle = 10.76 lux. 1 lux = 0.0929 foot-candle.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factors Affecting Coefficients of Utilisation (CoU), Light Loss Factor (LLF), Design and Estimation of Quantity of Material Required in Electrical Installation for Small Residential Building/Flat/Factory (Micro-Project)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT752 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Factors Affecting Coefficients of Utilisation (CoU), Light Loss Factor (LLF), Design and Estimation of Quantity of Material Required in Electrical Installation for Small Residential Building/Flat/Factory (Micro-Project)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Coefficient of Utilisation (CoU) — Formal Definition

The **Coefficient of Utilisation (CoU)** is a dimensionless efficiency factor used in interior illumination design that quantifies the effectiveness of a lighting installation in delivering luminous flux from the lamps to the working plane.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Standard Definition (IS 3640 / IS 6665):**
> **CoU** is the ratio of the luminous flux reaching the working plane (i.e., the floor or task surface) to the total luminous flux emitted by the lamps inside the luminaire.

$$
\text{CoU} \;=\; \frac{\text{Luminous flux received on the working plane (lumens)}}{\text{Total luminous flux emitted by the lamps (lumens)}}
$$

It is **always less than unity** (typically between **0.4 and 0.8**) because a portion of the light emitted by the lamps is inevitably lost — absorbed by walls, ceilings, floors, fixtures, or trapped inside the luminaire.

---

## 1.2 Light Loss Factor (LLF) — Formal Definition

The **Light Loss Factor (LLF)** (historically known as the **Maintenance Factor**, MF, in older texts) is a multiplicative reduction factor that compensates for the depreciation of illumination in a real installation over time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 / IS 3640 Definition:**
> **LLF** is the ratio of the illumination on the working plane after a specified period of use of the installation, to the initial illumination provided by the same installation when new.

$$
\text{LLF} \;=\; \frac{\text{Illumination after a period of use}}{\text{Initial illumination when the installation is new}}
$$

The LLF is **always less than 1.0** (typically ranging from **0.5 to 0.8**) and is the product of several individual depreciation factors (depreciation of lamp output, dirt accumulation on luminaire, lamp burnouts, etc.).

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Understanding

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Rainwater Harvesting Bucket" 🌧️🪣**
>
> Imagine you place a wide-mouthed bucket on your terrace to collect rainwater. Not every drop that falls on the terrace enters the bucket — some splashes out, some evaporates, and some is lost in transit.
>
> - **The rain falling on the terrace** = Light emitted by the lamp (lumens).
> - **The water finally collected in the bucket** = Light reaching your work plane.
> - **The ratio (collected ÷ total fallen)** = **Coefficient of Utilisation (CoU)** — it tells you how *efficient* the room geometry, reflectances, and luminaire optics are in *delivering* light where you need it.
>
> Now imagine the bucket is brand new and the rain is heavy. A few months later, the bucket gets dusty (dirt deposition), the terrace develops cracks (lamp ageing), and the rain itself weakens seasonally (voltage variations). Less water reaches the bucket. That *long-term* reduction is captured by the **Light Loss Factor (LLF)**.
>
> In short: **CoU = "How good is the geometry of the room?"** and **LLF = "How good will the installation remain over time?"** Both must be multiplied with lamp lumens to get the **actual useful light** at the task.

---

## 1.4 Why Are CoU and LLF Critical in KTU Board Exam Context?

- They are the **two most-asked design parameters** in any illumination design problem worth 7–14 marks.
- They convert theoretical lamp lumens into **real, useful working-plane lumens**.
- Without them, your calculated number of luminaires will be **drastically underestimated**, and the building will end up **under-lit**.

| Parameter | Range | Default Used |
|---|---|---|
| **CoU** | 0.4 – 0.8 | 0.5 (assumed if not given) |
| **LLF** | 0.5 – 0.8 | 0.8 (clean interiors) / 0.7 (industrial) |

---

## 1.5 Estimation for a Small Residential Building / Flat / Factory — Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Estimation** is the process of preparing a detailed list of materials, quantities, and costs required to execute a given electrical installation as per the approved design. For a KTU micro-project, this means a **single-line diagram + load schedule + material take-off + cost abstract** for a small dwelling unit (2BHK / 3BHK) or a single-room factory shed.

The three pillars of the micro-project are:
1. **Load Assessment** — Connected load, diversity factor, maximum demand.
2. **Wiring Design** — Circuit layout, number of sub-circuits, point wiring.
3. **Material Take-off & Cost** — Quantities of wires, conduits, switches, MCBs, distribution boards, etc.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Factors Affecting the Coefficient of Utilisation (CoU)

The CoU is *not* a property of the luminaire alone; it is a **combined property of the luminaire, the room, and the reflectances**. The four governing inputs are:

### A. **Room Index (K)** — Geometry of the Room
- The Room Index (K) characterises the **"slenderness"** of a room.
- A small, square room concentrates light efficiently → high K.
- A long, narrow hall spreads light out → low K.

$$
K \;=\; \frac{L \times W}{H_m \times (L + W)}
$$

Where:
- **L** = Length of the room (metres)
- **W** = Width of the room (metres)
- **H_m** = Mounting height of the luminaire **above the working plane** (metres)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Working plane is conventionally taken as **0.85 m above floor** (table height) for offices and **0 m (floor)** for street lighting. For residential rooms, **0.85 m is the KTU standard**.

### B. **Effective Ceiling Cavity Reflectance (ρ_c)**
- The colour and material of the ceiling determine how much light is reflected back down.
- **White ceiling** → ρ_c ≈ 0.7 to 0.8
- **Light cream** → ρ_c ≈ 0.5
- **Concrete grey** → ρ_c ≈ 0.3
- **Black/dark** → ρ_c ≈ 0.1

### C. **Effective Wall Reflectance (ρ_w)**
- Walls reflect light sideways back into the room.
- **White painted** → ρ_w ≈ 0.5 to 0.7
- **Pastel shades** → ρ_w ≈ 0.4
- **Brick / dark wood panelling** → ρ_w ≈ 0.1 to 0.2

### D. **Effective Floor Cavity Reflectance (ρ_f)**
- The floor reflects light back up toward the ceiling (then back down again).
- **Light tile / marble** → ρ_f ≈ 0.3
- **Dark wood** → ρ_f ≈ 0.1

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** CoU increases when (i) **K is higher** (compact rooms), and (ii) **ρ_c, ρ_w, ρ_f are higher** (lighter finishes). This is why interior designers insist on **white ceilings and pastel walls** for office spaces.

### E. **Luminaire Light Distribution Classification**
- **Direct luminaire** (e.g., downlight, recessed) → CoU ≈ 0.6 – 0.8
- **Semi-direct / general diffuse** → CoU ≈ 0.5 – 0.7
- **Indirect luminaire** (light bounces off ceiling first) → CoU ≈ 0.3 – 0.5

---

## 2.2 Factors Affecting the Light Loss Factor (LLF)

The LLF is the **product of multiple individual depreciation factors**. Each factor accounts for a specific real-world loss mechanism.

$$
\text{LLF} \;=\; \text{LLD} \times \text{LDD} \times \text{RSDD} \times \text{BD} \times \text{CF}
$$

Where:

| Symbol | Factor Name | Typical Value (Clean) | Typical Value (Industrial/Dirty) | Cause |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **LLD** | **Lamp Lumen Depreciation** | 0.90 – 0.95 | 0.80 | Lamp output falls as it ages (esp. fluorescent) |
| **LDD** | **Luminaire Dirt Depreciation** | 0.90 | 0.70 | Dust/grease deposits on diffuser & reflector |
| **RSDD** | **Room Surface Dirt Depreciation** | 0.95 | 0.85 | Walls & ceiling darken with soot/dust |
| **BD** | **Burnout Factor / Lamp Survival** | 0.95 | 0.90 | A fraction of lamps may not be replaced promptly |
| **CF** | **Cumulative (combined) Factor** | ≈ 0.73 | ≈ 0.40 | Equivalent total LLF for design |
| **LLF** | **Combined Light Loss Factor** | **0.7 – 0.8** | **0.5 – 0.6** | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In many KTU problems, the **Maintenance Factor (MF)** is used interchangeably with **LLF**, but the modern preferred terminology is **LLF**. Always multiply the lamp lumens by **LLF** (which is less than 1) to get the *effective design lumens*.

---

## 2.3 The Lumen Method — Master Formula for Number of Luminaires

The complete KTU 2024 Lumen Method formula is:

$$
N \;=\; \frac{E \times A}{n \times F \times \text{CoU} \times \text{LLF}}
$$

Where:
- **N** = Number of luminaires required
- **E** = Required illumination level on working plane (**lux**)
- **A** = Area of the working plane (**m²**) = L × W
- **n** = Number of lamps per luminaire
- **F** = Luminous flux of **one lamp** (**lumens**)
- **CoU** = Coefficient of utilisation (from tables, depending on K, ρ_c, ρ_w)
- **LLF** = Light loss factor

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Forgetting to divide by **n** (number of lamps per luminaire). If a fixture holds 2 lamps, you must divide by 2. The formula gives **number of luminaires**, not **number of lamps**.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $K = \dfrac{L \times W}{H_m (L + W)}$ | Room Index | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\text{CoU} = \dfrac{\Phi_{wp}}{\Phi_{lamp}}$ | Coefficient of Utilisation | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 3 | $\text{LLF} = \text{LLD} \times \text{LDD} \times \text{RSDD} \times \text{BD}$ | Light Loss Factor | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 4 | $E = \dfrac{\Phi_{total} \times \text{CoU} \times \text{LLF}}{A}$ | Illumination Level | lux |
| 5 | $N = \dfrac{E \times A}{n \times F \times \text{CoU} \times \text{LLF}}$ | Number of Luminaires | integer |
| 6 | $\Phi_{total} = N \times n \times F$ | Total Lamp Lumens | lumens |
| 7 | $\text{MD} = \text{CL} \times \text{DF}$ | Maximum Demand (kW) | kW |
| 8 | $I = \dfrac{P}{V \times \cos\phi}$ | Branch Circuit Current (1φ) | A |
| 9 | $I = \dfrac{P}{\sqrt{3} \times V_L \times \cos\phi}$ | 3-Phase Circuit Current | A |
| 10 | $C = \dfrac{I \times L \times 1000}{\Delta V \times V}$ | Cable Cross-Section (1φ) | mm² |
| 11 | $\text{CL} = \sum(P_{\text{points}})$ | Connected Load | kW |
| 12 | $A = L \times W$ | Room Area | m² |
| 13 | $H_m = H_s - H_{wp}$ | Mounting Height | m |

**Legend:** L = length, W = width, H_m = mounting height, A = area, n = lamps/luminaire, F = lamp lumens, E = lux, CL = connected load, DF = diversity factor, MD = max demand.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CoU & LLF are used by lighting designers** in IES (Illuminating Engineering Society) software (e.g., DIALux, Relux) to compute point-by-point illumination in offices, malls, hospitals, and factories.
- **Estimation** is the daily bread of an **electrical contractor** — every tender requires a Bill of Quantities (BoQ) before a contract is awarded.
- The **BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards)** codes **IS 3640**, **IS 6665**, and **IS 732** govern these values in India.
- **KTU 2024 micro-project** emulates this real practice — students produce a tender-ready document.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Derivation of the Lumen Method

### Step 1 — Total Lumens Emitted by All Lamps
If we have **N** luminaires, each with **n** lamps, and each lamp emits **F** lumens, then:

$$
\Phi_{total} \;=\; N \times n \times F \quad \text{[lumens]}
$$

### Step 2 — Lumens Reaching the Working Plane
Only a fraction (the CoU) of the total emitted lumens actually reaches the working plane:

$$
\Phi_{wp} \;=\; \Phi_{total} \times \text{CoU} \;=\; N \times n \times F \times \text{CoU}
$$

### Step 3 — Applying the Light Loss Factor (Over Time)
After the installation has been in use for some months, depreciation reduces the useful lumens:

$$
\Phi_{wp,\text{used}} \;=\; \Phi_{wp} \times \text{LLF} \;=\; N \times n \times F \times \text{CoU} \times \text{LLF}
$$

### Step 4 — Average Illumination on the Working Plane
Illumination E (in lux) is lumens per unit area:

$$
E \;=\; \frac{\Phi_{wp,\text{used}}}{A} \;=\; \frac{N \times n \times F \times \text{CoU} \times \text{LLF}}{A}
$$

### Step 5 — Rearranging for the Number of Luminaires (N)

$$
\begin{aligned}
E \times A \;&=\; N \times n \times F \times \text{CoU} \times \text{LLF} \\[4pt]
N \;&=\; \frac{E \times A}{n \times F \times \text{CoU} \times \text{LLF}} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Worked Example — Illumination Design of a Classroom

> **Problem (KTU 2024 Style):** Design the lighting scheme for a classroom of size **10 m × 8 m × 3.5 m** (L × W × H). Maintain **300 lux** on the working plane. Use **fluorescent tube lights, 40 W, 2400 lumens each, 2 lamps per luminaire**. Take ceiling reflectance = 0.7, wall reflectance = 0.5, mounting height = 2.5 m above working plane, CoU = 0.55, LLF = 0.8.

### Step 1 — Room Dimensions and Area
$$
A = L \times W = 10 \times 8 = 80 \;\text{m}^2
$$

### Step 2 — Room Index
$$
K = \frac{L \times W}{H_m (L + W)} = \frac{10 \times 8}{2.5 \times (10 + 8)} = \frac{80}{45} \approx 1.78
$$

> Note: We will use the given CoU = 0.55 directly (it corresponds to K ≈ 1.78, ρ_c = 0.7, ρ_w = 0.5 in standard tables).

### Step 3 — Number of Luminaires
$$
N = \frac{E \times A}{n \times F \times \text{CoU} \times \text{LLF}} = \frac{300 \times 80}{2 \times 2400 \times 0.55 \times 0.8}
$$

$$
N = \frac{24{,}000}{2{,}112} \approx 11.36
$$

### Step 4 — Round Up to Practical Value
$$
N = 12 \;\text{luminaires}
$$

### Step 5 — Layout Verification
$$
12 \text{ luminaires in a } 10 \times 8 \text{ m room} \Rightarrow 4 \text{ rows} \times 3 \text{ columns} \text{ layout (achievable).}
$$

### Step 6 — Actual Achieved Lux
$$
E_{actual} = \frac{12 \times 2 \times 2400 \times 0.55 \times 0.8}{80} = 316.8 \text{ lux}
$$

> ✅ Achieved lux (316.8) ≥ Required lux (300). **Design is acceptable.**

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Estimation of a 2BHK Residential Flat (Micro-Project)

> **Project Brief:** Single-storey 2BHK flat: 2 bedrooms, 1 hall, 1 kitchen, 2 bathrooms, 1 balcony. Plan load and estimate the quantity of electrical materials.

### Step 1 — Prepare the Load Schedule (Point-by-Point)

| Sl. | Room | Point Description | Qty | Rating (W) | Total (W) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hall | Ceiling fan | 1 | 60 | 60 |
| 2 | Hall | 5A socket (TV) | 1 | 100 | 100 |
| 3 | Hall | 15A socket (AC) | 1 | 1500 | 1500 |
| 4 | Hall | Light point | 1 | 40 | 40 |
| 5 | Bed-1 | Ceiling fan | 1 | 60 | 60 |
| 6 | Bed-1 | 5A socket | 2 | 100 | 200 |
| 7 | Bed-1 | 15A socket (AC) | 1 | 1500 | 1500 |
| 8 | Bed-1 | Light point | 1 | 40 | 40 |
| 9 | Bed-2 | Ceiling fan | 1 | 60 | 60 |
| 10 | Bed-2 | 5A socket | 2 | 100 | 200 |
| 11 | Bed-2 | Light point | 1 | 40 | 40 |
| 12 | Kitchen | 5A socket | 2 | 100 | 200 |
| 13 | Kitchen | 15A socket (Geyser/Mixer) | 2 | 2000 | 4000 |
| 14 | Kitchen | Light point + Exhaust fan | 2 | 60 | 120 |
| 15 | Bath-1 | Light point | 1 | 40 | 40 |
| 16 | Bath-2 | Light point | 1 | 40 | 40 |
| 17 | Balcony | Light point | 1 | 40 | 40 |
| 18 | Common | 5A socket (corridor) | 1 | 100 | 100 |
| 19 | DB | Bell/buzzer | 1 | 30 | 30 |
| | | **Connected Load (CL)** | | | **≈ 8370 W = 8.37 kW** |

### Step 2 — Apply Diversity Factor
For a small residential flat, **DF = 0.6 to 0.7** is acceptable.

$$
MD = CL \times DF = 8.37 \times 0.6 = 5.02 \;\text{kW}
$$

### Step 3 — Main Incoming Cable Selection
At **230 V, 1-phase**:

$$
I = \frac{MD \times 1000}{V \times \cos\phi} = \frac{5{,}020}{230 \times 0.9} \approx 24.3 \;\text{A}
$$

> Select **32 A MCB** (next standard size) with **6 mm² copper cable** for the incoming.

### Step 4 — Sub-Circuits per IS 732 / IS 4648

| Circuit No. | Loads | Rating (A) | MCB (A) | Cable |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hall + Bedroom lights & fans | 5 A × 6 = 30 A → split | 6 A | 1.5 mm² Cu |
| 2 | Hall + Bedroom sockets (5A) | 5 A × 5 = 25 A | 16 A | 2.5 mm² Cu |
| 3 | AC points (2 nos.) | 15 A × 2 = 30 A | 20 A | 4.0 mm² Cu |
| 4 | Kitchen sockets (5A) | 5 A × 2 = 10 A | 16 A | 2.5 mm² Cu |
| 5 | Kitchen power (15A geyser + mixer) | 15 A × 2 = 30 A | 20 A | 4.0 mm² Cu |
| 6 | Bath lights + exhaust | 5 A × 3 = 15 A | 6 A | 1.5 mm² Cu |
| 7 | Balcony + corridor | 5 A × 2 = 10 A | 6 A | 1.5 mm² Cu |
| 8 | Bell/buzzer | 0.5 A | 6 A | 1.5 mm² Cu |

### Step 5 — Material Take-Off (Quantity Estimation)

| Sl. | Material | Spec. | Formula | Qty |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | PVC-insulated Cu wire (1.5 mm²) | for light/fan circuits | 3 runs/point × points | ≈ 180 m |
| 2 | PVC-insulated Cu wire (2.5 mm²) | for 5A socket circuits | 3 × points | ≈ 100 m |
| 3 | PVC-insulated Cu wire (4 mm²) | for AC/Power circuits | 3 × points | ≈ 60 m |
| 4 | PVC-insulated Cu wire (6 mm²) | for incoming | 2 × 8 m | ≈ 16 m |
| 5 | PVC conduit pipe (20 mm) | for all runs | ≈ total wire length ÷ 3 per run | ≈ 110 m |
| 6 | 6 A switch | one per light/fan | count of switch points | 12 nos |
| 7 | 6 A socket | one per 5A point | count of 5A points | 10 nos |
| 8 | 16 A switch | for 15A sockets | count of 15A points | 5 nos |
| 9 | 16 A socket | for power points | count of 15A points | 5 nos |
| 10 | 5-pin 6 A plug top | for fans | no. of fans | 3 nos |
| 11 | Modular switch board (6M, 12M) | as per grouping | groups | 8 nos |
| 12 | Distribution board (8-way) | single phase | 1 | 1 no. |
| 13 | MCB (6 A SP) | sub-circuit protection | 8-way DB | 6 nos |
| 14 | MCB (16/20 A SP) | sub-circuit protection | 8-way DB | 2 nos |
| 15 | MCB (32 A DP) | main isolator | 1 | 1 no. |
| 16 | RCCB (30 mA, 40 A) | earth-leakage | 1 | 1 no. |
| 17 | Earth rod (Cu) + earth wire | 4 mm² Cu | 1 set | 1 set |
| 18 | Ceiling rose / fan box | — | 3 fans | 3 nos |

### Step 6 — Cost Abstract (Sample, Indicative)

| Sl. | Material | Qty | Unit Rate (₹) | Amount (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1.5 mm² Cu wire | 180 m | 22 | 3,960 |
| 2 | 2.5 mm² Cu wire | 100 m | 35 | 3,500 |
| 3 | 4 mm² Cu wire | 60 m | 55 | 3,300 |
| 4 | 6 mm² Cu wire | 16 m | 80 | 1,280 |
| 5 | 20 mm PVC conduit | 110 m | 12 | 1,320 |
| 6 | 6 A switch (modular) | 12 | 35 | 420 |
| 7 | 16 A switch (modular) | 5 | 75 | 375 |
| 8 | 6 A socket (modular) | 10 | 55 | 550 |
| 9 | 16 A socket (modular) | 5 | 110 | 550 |
| 10 | 8-way DB | 1 | 1,200 | 1,200 |
| 11 | MCBs (assorted) | 9 | 200 | 1,800 |
| 12 | RCCB 40 A 30 mA | 1 | 1,800 | 1,800 |
| 33 | Misc. (clamps, screws, tape) | lot | 1,500 | 1,500 |
| | **Subtotal (Material)** | | | **≈ ₹ 21,555** |
| | Labour (≈ 30% of material) | | | **≈ ₹ 6,470** |
| | **Grand Total** | | | **≈ ₹ 28,025** |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What Factor

| Design Situation | Recommended CoU | Recommended LLF | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Small office, white ceiling | 0.6 | 0.8 | Clean room, good reflectances |
| Industrial shop floor, high ceiling | 0.5 | 0.6 | Dusty, less reflective |
| Library, wood paneling | 0.4 | 0.75 | Dark walls |
| Residential room, pastel paint | 0.5 | 0.8 | Moderate conditions |
| Hospital OT (clean) | 0.65 | 0.85 | Sterile, white surfaces |
| Outdoor street (no CoU) | — | 0.7 | LLF only; CoU not used |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flowchart — Interaction of Design Parameters

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Start: Illumination Design Problem] --> nodeB[Step 1: Get Room Dimensions L, W, H]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Step 2: Calculate Room Index K]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Step 3: Decide Reflectances rho_c, rho_w, rho_f]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Step 4: Look Up CoU from Table]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Step 5: Decide LLF from Environment]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Step 6: Select Lamp Type: F lumens, n per luminaire]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Step 7: Apply Lumen Method N = EA over nF times CoU times LLF]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Step 8: Round Up and Layout]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Step 9: Verify E_actual greater than E_required]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[End: Approved Lighting Design]
```

## 4.2 Decomposition of the Light Loss Factor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Light Loss Factor LLF] --> nodeB[Lamp Lumen Depreciation LLD]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Luminaire Dirt Depreciation LDD]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Room Surface Dirt Depreciation RSDD]
    nodeA --> nodeE[Burnout Factor BD]
    nodeB --> nodeF[Lamp ages, lumen output drops]
    nodeC --> nodeG[Dust on diffuser and reflector]
    nodeD --> nodeH[Walls and ceiling darken]
    nodeE --> nodeI[Some lamps not replaced promptly]
    nodeF --> nodeJ[Product: LLF less than 1]
    nodeG --> nodeJ
    nodeH --> nodeJ
    nodeI --> nodeJ
```

## 4.3 Workflow for Residential Electrical Installation Estimation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Receive Building Plan] --> nodeB[Mark All Electrical Points on Plan]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Prepare Load Schedule Point by Point]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Compute Connected Load CL in kW]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Apply Diversity Factor to get MD]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Select MCB and Incoming Cable Size]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Group Points into Sub-circuits as per IS 732]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Compute Wire Lengths for Each Sub-circuit]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Compute Conduit Lengths and Accessories]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Prepare BoM and Cost Abstract]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Deliver Micro Project Report]
```

## 4.4 Functional Block Architecture — Residential DB and Sub-circuits

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[EB Supply 230 V 1 Phase] --> nodeB[Energy Meter]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Main MCB 32 A DP]
    nodeC --> nodeD[RCCB 40 A 30 mA]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Distribution Board 8 Way]
    nodeE --> nodeF1[Sub C1: Lights and Fans 6 A]
    nodeE --> nodeF2[Sub C2: Hall 5A Sockets 16 A]
    nodeE --> nodeF3[Sub C3: AC Points 20 A]
    nodeE --> nodeF4[Sub C4: Kitchen 5A Sockets 16 A]
    nodeE --> nodeF5[Sub C5: Kitchen Power 20 A]
    nodeE --> nodeF6[Sub C6: Bath Lights 6 A]
    nodeE --> nodeF7[Sub C7: Balcony and Bell 6 A]
    nodeE --> nodeF8[Sub C8: Spare 16 A]
    nodeF1 --> nodeG1[Loads]
    nodeF2 --> nodeG2[Loads]
    nodeF3 --> nodeG3[Loads]
    nodeF4 --> nodeG4[Loads]
    nodeF5 --> nodeG5[Loads]
    nodeF6 --> nodeG6[Loads]
    nodeF7 --> nodeG7[Loads]
    nodeF8 --> nodeG8[Loads]
```
