# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024 – Pattern Aligned]**

### Q1. Define the term "Coefficient of Utilisation" (CoU). List any **four** factors that affect it.
*(3 Marks — Remember / Understand Level)*

**Model Answer:**
The **Coefficient of Utilisation (CoU)** is the ratio of the luminous flux reaching the working plane to the total luminous flux emitted by the lamps. It is dimensionless and always less than 1.

**Four factors affecting CoU:**
1. **Room Index (K)** — geometry of the room.
2. **Ceiling reflectance (ρ_c)** — colour/finish of ceiling.
3. **Wall reflectance (ρ_w)** — colour/finish of walls.
4. **Light distribution of luminaire** — direct, indirect, etc.

> ✅ **Word limit:** 60–80 words. Use a labelled diagram if possible.

---

### Q2. What is the **Light Loss Factor (LLF)**? Why is it always less than unity?
*(3 Marks — Understand Level)*

**Model Answer:**
**LLF** is the ratio of illumination on the working plane after a period of use to the initial illumination when the installation is new. It accounts for the depreciation of light output over time.

**Reasons LLF < 1:**
- Lamp output decreases with age (lamp lumen depreciation).
- Dust accumulates on luminaire, walls, and ceiling.
- Some lamps may not be replaced promptly (burnout factor).

> ✅ Mention at least **three** depreciation factors explicitly.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper Style, 14-Mark Module-2 Question]**

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**Q (a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **Lumen Method** of illumination design. Derive the expression for the **number of luminaires** required. *(7 Marks — Understand / Apply)*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The Lumen Method (also called the "Total Lumens Method") is used to calculate the average illumination on a horizontal working plane and hence the number of luminaires required for an interior space.

**Procedure:**
1. Determine room dimensions (L, W, H) and mounting height H_m.
2. Calculate Room Index:
$$
K = \frac{L \times W}{H_m (L + W)}
$$
3. Select luminaire type and find CoU from standard tables (function of K, ρ_c, ρ_w).
4. Decide LLF based on environment.
5. Apply the Lumen Method formula to compute N.

**Derivation (as derived in Section 3.1):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Phi_{total} &= N \times n \times F \\
\Phi_{wp} &= \Phi_{total} \times \text{CoU} \\
\Phi_{used} &= \Phi_{wp} \times \text{LLF} = N \times n \times F \times \text{CoU} \times \text{LLF} \\
E &= \frac{\Phi_{used}}{A} \\
\Rightarrow N &= \frac{E \times A}{n \times F \times \text{CoU} \times \text{LLF}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sketch — Block Diagram:**

```
   Lamps (lumens F each, n per fixture)
                │
                ▼
       ┌────────────────┐
       │  Luminaire     │ ──►  Flux lost in luminaire
       └───────┬────────┘
               ▼
        Room surfaces (ρ_c, ρ_w, ρ_f)
               ▼
        Working plane (E lux)
```

---

**Q (b)** A small factory hall of size **30 m × 15 m × 6 m** is to be illuminated to a level of **200 lux**. Using **400 W metal halide lamps** (each luminaire holds **one lamp**), with **F = 32,000 lumens**, **CoU = 0.5**, **LLF = 0.7**, and working plane at **0.85 m above floor**, determine:
  (i) the number of luminaires, and
  (ii) the layout if luminaires are arranged in rows along the length.
*(7 Marks — Apply / Analyse)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Area**
$$
A = 30 \times 15 = 450 \;\text{m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Mounting height**
$$
H_m = 6 - 0.85 = 5.15 \;\text{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Number of luminaires**
$$
N = \frac{E \times A}{n \times F \times \text{CoU} \times \text{LLF}} = \frac{200 \times 450}{1 \times 32{,}000 \times 0.5 \times 0.7}
$$

$$
N = \frac{90{,}000}{11{,}200} = 8.04 \Rightarrow N = 9 \text{ luminaires (round up)}
$$

**Step 4 — Layout along 30 m length, 15 m width**
A 3 × 3 grid:
- 3 rows × 3 columns = 9 luminaires
- Row spacing = 15 / 3 = 5 m
- Column spacing = 30 / 3 = 10 m

**Step 5 — Achieved lux**
$$
E_{actual} = \frac{9 \times 1 \times 32{,}000 \times 0.5 \times 0.7}{450} \approx 224 \;\text{lux} \geq 200 \;\text{lux} \; ✅
$$

> ✅ Both parts are now answered with clear steps.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**Q (a)** Discuss the **various factors affecting the Light Loss Factor (LLF)** in an interior lighting installation. How is LLF different from the **Maintenance Factor (MF)**? *(7 Marks — Understand / Analyse)*

**Model Answer:**

**Factors affecting LLF:**

| Factor | Symbol | Cause | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lamp Lumen Depreciation | LLD | Output of lamp reduces as it ages | 0.85 – 0.95 |
| Luminaire Dirt Depreciation | LDD | Dust/grease on fixture | 0.70 – 0.95 |
| Room Surface Dirt Depreciation | RSDD | Walls/ceiling darkening | 0.85 – 0.95 |
| Burnout Factor | BD | Lamps not replaced in time | 0.90 – 0.98 |

$$
\text{LLF} = \text{LLD} \times \text{LDD} \times \text{RSDD} \times \text{BD}
$$

**LLF vs MF:**
- In **older practice**, only a single "Maintenance Factor" (MF) was used — equal to the product of all depreciation factors.
- In **modern practice (IS 3640, 2024)**, the term **LLF** is preferred, but conceptually it is the **reciprocal-equivalent** of MF used as a multiplier on lamp lumens.
- Older formula: $N = \dfrac{E \times A}{n \times F \times \text{CoU} \times \text{MF}}$; modern formula uses **LLF** in the same position.

---

**Q (b)** A **2BHK residential flat** is to be electrified. Prepare a **load schedule, sub-circuit distribution, and material take-off** (cable, conduit, switches, sockets, MCBs) for the following points:
- Hall: 1 fan, 1 light, 1 × 5A socket, 1 × 15A socket (AC).
- Bedroom 1: 1 fan, 1 light, 2 × 5A socket, 1 × 15A socket (AC).
- Bedroom 2: 1 fan, 1 light, 2 × 5A socket.
- Kitchen: 1 light, 1 exhaust fan, 2 × 5A socket, 1 × 15A socket (geyser).
- 2 Bathrooms: 1 light each.
- Balcony: 1 light.

Assume: 230 V, 1φ, DF = 0.6, copper conductors, PVC conduit wiring.
*(7 Marks — Apply / Analyse)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Load Schedule (Refer Section 3.3):**
- Total connected load ≈ **8.37 kW**
- Maximum demand = 8.37 × 0.6 = **5.02 kW**
- Main current = 5020 / (230 × 0.9) ≈ **24.3 A → 32 A MCB + 6 mm² Cu**

**Step 2 — Sub-Circuits:**

| Circuit | Loads | MCB | Cable |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hall + 2BR lights & fans (6 pts) | 6 A | 1.5 mm² Cu |
| 2 | 5A sockets (5 nos.) | 16 A | 2.5 mm² Cu |
| 3 | AC points (2 nos.) | 20 A | 4.0 mm² Cu |
| 4 | Kitchen 5A + 15A geyser | 20 A | 4.0 mm² Cu |
| 5 | Bath + balcony + exhaust | 6 A | 1.5 mm² Cu |

**Step 3 — Material Take-Off (BoM):**

| Material | Qty |
|---|---|
| 1.5 mm² Cu wire | ≈ 150 m |
| 2.5 mm² Cu wire | ≈ 80 m |
| 4.0 mm² Cu wire | ≈ 50 m |
| 6.0 mm² Cu wire (incoming) | ≈ 12 m |
| 20 mm PVC conduit | ≈ 100 m |
| 6 A modular switch | 10 nos |
| 16 A modular switch | 3 nos |
| 6 A socket | 7 nos |
| 16 A socket | 3 nos |
| 8-way SPN DB | 1 no |
| 6 A SP MCB | 3 nos |
| 16/20 A SP MCB | 2 nos |
| 32 A DP MCB (main) | 1 no |
| 40 A 30 mA RCCB | 1 no |

**Step 4 — Sketch of Single-Line Diagram:**

```
EB → Meter → 32A DP MCB → 40A RCCB → SPN DB (8-way)
                                      ├── C1: 6A (lights & fans)
                                      ├── C2: 16A (5A sockets)
                                      ├── C3: 20A (AC points)
                                      ├── C4: 20A (kitchen power)
                                      ├── C5: 6A (bath + balcony)
                                      ├── C6–C8: SPARE
```

> ✅ This question tests both **theory of LLF** and **practical estimation** — a true KTU 2024 micro-project outcome.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks in This Topic — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to divide by n** (lamps per luminaire) — formula gives **number of luminaires**, not lamps. (–2 marks typical)
> 2. **Using H (ceiling height) instead of H_m (mounting height above working plane)** when computing K. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Conflating LLF and MF** — modern KTU papers use **LLF**, but some textbooks still print **MF**. (–1 mark if not clarified)
> 4. **Omitting units** in the final answer for E (lux), A (m²), F (lumens). (–0.5 mark per missing unit)
> 5. **Not rounding up** — N must always be the next higher integer; fractional luminaires are nonsensical.
> 6. **In estimation questions**, students forget to add the **earth wire**, **conduit accessories**, and **labour charge** (15–30% of material cost) to the cost abstract. (–2 marks)
> 7. **Diversity Factor (DF)** — using DF = 1 always is wrong; residential DF is typically 0.6–0.7.
> 8. **Not verifying E_actual ≥ E_required** after rounding up. The examiner wants to see that you have *checked* your design.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **🎯 KTU 2024 — Last-Minute Revision Bulletins**
>
> ✅ **CoU** = Useful lumens on working plane ÷ Total lumens emitted. Always < 1.
>
> ✅ **LLF** = Illumination after use ÷ Initial illumination. Always < 1.
>
> ✅ **LLF = LLD × LDD × RSDD × BD** (four sub-factors).
>
> ✅ **Room Index** $K = \dfrac{L \times W}{H_m (L + W)}$. Take $H_m$ from **working plane** (0.85 m) to luminaire, **not** ceiling height.
>
> ✅ **Master formula:** $N = \dfrac{E \times A}{n \times F \times \text{CoU} \times \text{LLF}}$. N must be rounded **up** to next integer.
>
> ✅ **Estimation workflow:** Load schedule → Diversity factor → MCB & cable sizing → Sub-circuit grouping → BoM → Cost abstract.
>
> ✅ **Residential DF = 0.6–0.7**; industrial DF = 0.7–0.8.
>
> ✅ **Cable sizes** (Cu, PVC, 1φ, 230 V): Light = 1.5 mm² | Socket = 2.5 mm² | AC/Power = 4 mm² | Incoming = 6 mm².
>
> ✅ **RCCB (30 mA)** is mandatory for human-protection in residential DBs.
>
> ✅ **Lamp lumens (F):** Incandescent 100 W ≈ 1400 lm; LED 18 W ≈ 1800 lm; Fluorescent 40 W ≈ 2400 lm; MH 400 W ≈ 32,000 lm.
>
> ✅ **Always include labour (15–30% of material cost)** in the cost abstract.
>
> ✅ **Verify E_actual ≥ E_required** after rounding N up.
>
> ✅ **IS Codes to quote:** IS 3640 (CoU/LLF), IS 6665 (luminaires), IS 732 (wiring), IS 4648 (sub-circuits), IS 12640 (RCCB).
>
> ✅ **Working plane heights:** Office/Residential = 0.85 m; Industrial = 0 m (floor level); Street = 0 m.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Indoor and Outdoor Substation, Selection of Transformer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT752 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Indoor and Outdoor Substation, Selection of Transformer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Substation — Formal KTU Definition
An **Electrical Substation** is an assembly of apparatus, equipment, switching devices, bus-bars, transformers, and associated control, protection, and auxiliary equipment designed to switch, transform, control, protect, and isolate electrical circuits, operating at voltages typically from **11 kV up to 765 kV** in the Indian power network.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (As per IE Rules 1956, Section 3):**
> *"A substation is a high-voltage electric system facility comprising switches, circuit breakers, buses, transformers, and other equipment used to switch generators, transmission lines, and distribution circuits, and to convert AC voltages from one level to another, or to switch circuits between alternate sources."*

### 1.2 Classification of Substations (KTU Board Frequency: ⭐⭐⭐⭐)
Substations are broadly classified based on **application** and **physical location/construction**.

| Basis of Classification | Type |
| :--- | :--- |
| **By Application** | Step-up, Step-down, Distribution, Switching, Converter, HVDC |
| **By Construction (Module 3 Focus)** | **Indoor** and **Outdoor** |
| **By Voltage Level** | High Voltage (HV), Extra High Voltage (EHV), Ultra High Voltage (UHV) |
| **By Insulation Medium** | Air-Insulated (AIS), Gas-Insulated (GIS) |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Traffic Junction" Model
> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition: A Substation is like a Multi-level Highway Interchange.**
> - **Transformers** are the "inclined ramps" that change the elevation (voltage) of the road.
> - **Bus-bars** are the "main carriageways" carrying parallel traffic.
> - **Circuit Breakers & Isolators** are the "traffic signals" — opening (isolating) and closing (energising) lanes.
> - **Indoor Substation** = a covered multi-storey parking interchange in a city (clean, compact, weather-protected).
> - **Outdoor Substation** = a large cloverleaf interchange in open fields (spacious, cheaper, but exposed to weather).

### 1.4 Indoor Substation — Definition
An **Indoor Substation** is one in which the entire switching, bus-bar, transformer, and protective equipment is installed **inside a closed building** made of brick, concrete, or steel structure. The voltage equipment is generally limited to **11 kV, 22 kV, 33 kV, and up to 66 kV** in special cases.

### 1.5 Outdoor Substation — Definition
An **Outdoor Substation** is one in which the switching equipment, bus-bars, and other apparatus are installed **in the open air**, mounted on concrete pedestals, steel structures, or gantries, with adequate safety clearances. Voltages range from **11 kV up to 765 kV**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Indian Practice:** Substations up to **66 kV** are normally built as **Indoor** type, whereas substations of **132 kV and above** are almost always **Outdoor** type due to the enormous space required for safety clearances.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Types of Indoor Substations (KTU Frequently Asked)
Indoor substations are further sub-divided into:

**A) Plain Indoor Substation (Open Type)**
- Walls provide only enclosure.
- Equipment is mounted on **steel framework inside the building**.
- Used for **11 kV / 22 kV** switchgear in commercial/industrial premises.

**B) Cubicle Type Indoor Substation**
- Equipment is mounted in **metallic enclosed compartments called cubicles**.
- Each cubicle houses a specific function: incoming, outgoing, bus-coupler, metering, protection.
- Most common in **33 kV / 11 kV** distribution substations.
- **Example:** KIOSK-type, 11-panel switchgear room.

**C) Metal-Enclosed Indoor Substation**
- All live parts are completely enclosed within **earthed metal sheets**.
- The unit is **factory-built**, tested, and shipped — only bus-duct connections on site.
- Used where space is severely constrained (e.g., basement of a high-rise).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Hot Tip:** Always draw a **single-line diagram (SLD)** of a substation before answering a 14-mark design question. SLDs carry **3-4 marks alone**.

### 2.2 Types of Outdoor Substations (KTU Frequently Asked)

| Type | Description | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Pole-mounted** | Equipment on H-pole or 4-pole structure | 11 kV / 22 kV rural |
| **Foundation-mounted** | Equipment on RCC plinths | 33 kV / 66 kV primary |
| **Plinth/Pedestal mounted** | Transformer on rails on concrete plinths | 33 kV–220 kV |
| **Gantry-type (Conventional)** | Tall steel gantries for bus-bars and conductors | 132 kV–400 kV |
| **SF₆ Gas-Insulated (GIS)** | Compact, gas-tight enclosures | 66 kV–765 kV (urban) |

### 2.3 Selection Criterion — Indoor vs. Outdoor
A KTU student must apply the following decision logic:

$$\text{Choose Indoor if:} \quad V \leq 66 \text{ kV} \quad \text{AND} \quad \text{Space is constrained} \quad \text{AND} \quad \text{Atmosphere is polluted/saline/coastal}$$

$$\text{Choose Outdoor if:} \quad V \geq 132 \text{ kV} \quad \text{AND} \quad \text{Space is available} \quad \text{AND} \quad \text{Cost-minimisation is priority}$$

### 2.4 Merits & Demerits (Comparison Matrix — KTU Favourite)

| Parameter | Indoor Substation | Outdoor Substation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Cost** | High (building cost) | Low (no building) |
| **Space Required** | Very compact | Very large |
| **Safety Clearances** | Reduced (small air gap) | Large (higher creepage) |
| **Maintenance** | Easy (any weather) | Difficult (rain, sun) |
| **Aesthetic** | Neat, clean | Bulky gantries |
| **Fault Level** | Limited (up to ~40 kA) | Can handle very high (~80 kA) |
| **Voltage Limit** | Up to 66 kV | Up to 765 kV |
| **Protection from Pollution** | Excellent | Poor (unless GIS) |
| **Expandability** | Difficult | Easy |
| **Earthquake Resistance** | Better (rigid building) | Requires special design |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula / Standard | Unit |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: |
| 1 | Minimum Phase-to-Earth Clearance (Indoor) | $D_{ph-E} = 7.5 \text{ mm/kV} + \text{adjustment}$ | mm |
| 2 | Minimum Phase-to-Phase Clearance (Outdoor, 132 kV) | $D_{ph-ph} = 1.55 \text{ m}$ (vertical) | m |
| 3 | Bus-bar Current Rating (Aluminium) | $I_{bus} = \dfrac{P}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot \cos\phi}$ | A |
| 4 | Short Circuit Current | $I_{sc} = \dfrac{MVA_{sc}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L}$ | kA |
| 5 | Specific Energy (for CB selection) | $I^2t = \left(\dfrac{MVA_{sc}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V}\right)^2 \cdot t$ | kA²s |
| 6 | Specific Creepage Distance (Indoor) | $16 \text{ mm/kV}$ | mm/kV |
| 7 | Specific Creepage Distance (Outdoor, normal) | $25 \text{ mm/kV}$ | mm/kV |
| 8 | Specific Creepage Distance (Outdoor, polluted) | $31 \text{ mm/kV}$ (heavily polluted) | mm/kV |
| 9 | Voltage Selection Criterion | Indoor: $\leq 66$ kV, Outdoor: $\geq 132$ kV | kV |
| 10 | Transformer Utilisation Factor | $\eta_{util} = \dfrac{\text{Peak Load (kVA)}}{\text{Installed Capacity (kVA)}}$ | pu |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often use the *system voltage* (e.g., 11 kV) in clearance formulas instead of the *highest system voltage* (e.g., 12 kV for 11 kV class, 36 kV for 33 kV class, 145 kV for 132 kV class). Always use **highest system voltage** per IS 2165/IS 5621.

### 2.6 Engineering Utility in Real Field
- **Utility Engineers** apply this classification to optimise capital cost in 33/11 kV distribution substations (almost always **Indoor Cubicle** type).
- **Design Consultants** use outdoor AIS for 220 kV EHV substations, switching to **GIS** when land cost exceeds ₹2 crore per acre in metros.
- **Industrial Plants** prefer indoor substations for LT/HT switchgear to avoid contamination from chemical fumes.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Bus-bar Current Rating from Load

**Problem Statement (Standard KTU 14-Mark Pattern):**
A substation feeds a load of **P kW** at a power factor $\cos\phi$ and line voltage $V_L$. Derive the bus-bar current rating.

**Step 1 — Start with apparent power**
The three-phase apparent power $S$ in kVA is given by:

$$
S_{3\phi} = \frac{P}{\cos\phi} \quad \text{[kVA]}
$$

*Explanation:* Real power $P$ (kW) divided by power factor $\cos\phi$ yields the total apparent power that the bus must carry. This is a standard single-step transformation — no intermediate equation needed.

**Step 2 — Convert apparent power to current using three-phase relation**

$$
S_{3\phi} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L
$$

*Explanation:* This is the universal three-phase formula relating line-to-line voltage, line current, and total apparent power. The factor $\sqrt{3}$ arises from the $\cos(30^\circ)$ phase displacement between phases.

**Step 3 — Solve for line current $I_L$**

$$
\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L = \frac{P}{\cos\phi}
$$

$$
I_L = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot \cos\phi}
$$

*Explanation:* Dividing both sides by $\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L$ isolates the line current. This is the **fundamental bus-bar current equation** every KTU student must memorise.

**Step 4 — Apply safety/derating factor**
In practical design, we apply a **derating factor $k_{der} = 1.25$** to account for future load growth, ambient temperature, and harmonic currents:

$$
I_{bus, design} = k_{der} \cdot \frac{P}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot \cos\phi} = \frac{1.25 \cdot P}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot \cos\phi}
$$

*Explanation:* A 25% margin is standard KTU practice for bus-bar selection, mirroring CEA (Central Electricity Authority) safety regulations for new substation design.

### 3.2 Derivation: Short-Circuit Current for Breaker Selection

**Given:** Fault level of source $MVA_{sc}$ (in MVA), operating voltage $V_L$ (in kV), fault duration $t$ (in seconds).

**Step 1 — Convert short-circuit MVA to kA**

$$
I_{sc} = \frac{MVA_{sc} \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L}
$$

*Explanation:* We multiply $MVA_{sc}$ by 1000 to convert MVA to kVA, then divide by $\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L$ to get the line current in kA at fault instant. This is the **rated breaking current** the circuit breaker must interrupt.

**Step 2 — Check specific energy let-through**

$$
I^2 t \leq I_{sc}^2 \cdot t
$$

*Explanation:* The $I^2t$ value (in kA²s) determines the **thermal withstand** of the bus-bar and breaker contacts. It must be lower than the breaker manufacturer's let-through $I^2t$ rating.

**Step 3 — Practical Numerical Example (Worked)**

Suppose $MVA_{sc} = 500$ MVA, $V_L = 33$ kV, $t = 1$ s:

$$
I_{sc} = \frac{500 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times 33}
$$

$$
I_{sc} = \frac{500{,}000}{57.157}
$$

$$
I_{sc} \approx 8748 \text{ A} \approx 8.75 \text{ kA}
$$

$$
I^2t = (8.75)^2 \times 1 = 76.56 \text{ kA}^2\text{s}
$$

*Explanation:* The selected 33 kV breaker must have breaking capacity ≥ **8.75 kA** and $I^2t$ rating ≥ **76.56 kA²s**. Standard next-size selection: **12.5 kA, 25 kA, or 40 kA** breaker.

### 3.3 Selection of Transformer — KTU Step-by-Step Procedure

The selection of a power/distribution transformer involves **nine cardinal parameters** as per IS 2026 / IS 1180 (for ≤ 2.5 MVA distribution transformers):

**Step 1 — Determine kVA Rating from Load**

$$
S_{TX} = \frac{P_{load}}{\cos\phi_{load} \cdot \eta_{TX}}
$$

*Explanation:* $P_{load}$ is the maximum demand in kW, $\cos\phi_{load}$ is the operating power factor (0.8–0.9 typical), and $\eta_{TX}$ is the assumed transformer efficiency (≈ 0.98 for power transformers, 0.96 for distribution).

**Step 2 — Apply Diversity Factor (if multiple loads)**

$$
S_{TX, design} = \frac{\text{Sum of individual loads} \times \text{Simultaneity Factor}}{\eta_{TX} \cdot \cos\phi}
$$

*Explanation:* KTU requires this when a substation feeds multiple feeders with different load patterns.

**Step 3 — Apply Future Growth Factor (typically 1.25–1.50)**

$$
S_{TX, future} = 1.25 \cdot S_{TX, design} \quad \text{or} \quad 1.50 \cdot S_{TX, design}
$$

*Explanation:* 25% margin for residential; 50% margin for industrial/commercial expansion. This is a **Kerala State Electricity Board (KSEB) standard practice**.

**Step 4 — Choose Number of Transformers**

$$\text{Single transformer: } S_{TX, future} < 2.5 \text{ MVA (typical distribution)}$$

$$\text{Two transformers: } S_{TX, future} \geq 2.5 \text{ MVA OR critical load (N-1 redundancy required)}$$

*Explanation:* N-1 criterion is mandatory in hospitals, data centres, airports (KTU favourite theory question).

**Step 5 — Select Voltage Ratio**

$$V_1 / V_2 = \frac{\text{HV side nominal voltage}}{\text{LV side nominal voltage}}$$

Example: $11000/433$ V (typical distribution), $33000/11000$ V (primary distribution), $132000/33000$ V (EHV primary).

**Step 6 — Select Vector Group**

| Vector Group | Application | KTU Mnemonic |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| **Dyn11** | Most common distribution (3-phase 4-wire LV) | "D for Delta Hi, yn for Star-N Lo" |
| **Yyn0** | Step-up generator transformers | "Y for Hi, yn for Lo (neutral needed)" |
| **YNd11** | EHV step-down | "YN grounded Hi, Delta ungrounded Lo" |
| **Dyn1** | Paralleling with Dyn11 — NOT ALLOWED | "Phase shift mismatch" |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Parallel Operation Rule:** Two transformers can operate in parallel **only if** they have the same (i) kVA rating, (ii) voltage ratio, (iii) percentage impedance, and (iv) **vector group**. Dyn11 and Dyn1 cannot be paralleled despite having identical kVA.

**Step 7 — Select Cooling Method**

| Rating (kVA) | Cooling Class (IS 2026) | Cooling Medium |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| $\leq 200$ | AN (Air Natural) | Self-air |
| $200$–$1000$ | ONAN | Oil + Air |
| $1000$–$2500$ | ONAN/ONAF | Oil + Forced Air |
| $>2500$ | ONAF or OFAF | Oil + Forced (OFAF) |

*Explanation:* KTU often gives transformer rating and asks for the cooling class.

**Step 8 — Select Tap Changer Type**

$$\text{OCTC (Off-Circuit Tap Changer): } \pm 5\% \text{ in steps of } 2.5\% \text{ (no-load tap change only)}$$

$$\text{OLTC (On-Load Tap Changer): } \pm 10\% \text{ in 16–17 steps, under-load operation}$$

*Explanation:* OLTC is used where the incoming HV voltage fluctuates widely (e.g., 132 kV lines feeding long distances).

**Step 9 — Select % Impedance (ISC Short-Circuit Voltage)**

| Rating | Standard %Z (IS 1180 / 2026) |
| :---: | :--- |
| $100$ kVA | $4.5\%$ |
| $250$ kVA | $5.0\%$ |
| $500$ kVA | $5.5\%$ |
| $1000$ kVA | $6.0\%$ |
| $1600$ kVA and above | $6.25\%$–$12.5\%$ (system fault-level dependent) |

*Explanation:* %Z governs both the **voltage regulation** and the **fault current contribution** of the transformer. Higher %Z → lower fault current, but worse voltage regulation.

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix (Selection Flowchart — KTU Format)

The following decision table is a **must-revise summary** for the KTU board exam:

| Parameter | Indoor Substation | Outdoor Substation | GIS |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Voltage (kV) | $\leq 66$ | $\geq 132$ | $66$–$765$ |
| Footprint | $0.05\times$ | $1.0\times$ (baseline) | $0.15\times$ |
| Cost | $1.5\times$ | $1.0\times$ | $2.5\times$ |
| Pollution Resistance | High | Low | Very High |
| Maintenance Cycle | 5 years | 2 years | 10 years |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Indoor Substation — Cubicle Type Layout (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[HT Incoming 11kV / 33kV] --> B[Incomer Cubicle 1]
    A2[Second Incomer 11kV] --> C[Incomer Cubicle 2]
    B --> D[Bus Coupler CB Panel]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Outgoing Feeder 1 to Load A]
    D --> F[Outgoing Feeder 2 to Load B]
    D --> G[Outgoing Feeder 3 to Load C]
    D --> H[Transformer Cubicle 11kV/433V]
    H --> I[LV Panel 433V]
    I --> J[Industrial Motors and Lighting]
    K[Control Room with SCADA] -. monitors .-> B
    K -. monitors .-> C
    K -. monitors .-> D
```

*Block Functionality Note:* This block-level functional architecture maps all interactions of an indoor 33/11 kV cubicle-type substation, including dual incomer redundancy, bus coupler, outgoing feeders, LV step-down, and centralised SCADA monitoring.

### 4.2 Outdoor Substation — 132/33 kV AIS Single Line Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[132kV Line 1 Incoming] --> A2[Isolator 132kV]
    A2 --> A3[CT 132kV]
    A3 --> A4[CB 132kV SF6]
    A4 --> A5[PT 132kV]
    A5 --> B1[Main Bus 132kV]
    B2[132kV Line 2 Incoming] --> A2B[Isolator L2] --> A3B[CT L2] --> A4B[CB L2] --> A5B[PT L2] --> B1
    B1 --> C1[Lightning Arrester LA]
    B1 --> C2[Wave Trap PLCC]
    B1 --> D1[Power Transformer 132/33kV 50MVA]
    D1 --> E1[33kV Side CB]
    E1 --> E2[33kV Bus]
    E2 --> F1[Outgoing Feeder 33kV-1]
    E2 --> F2[Outgoing Feeder 33kV-2]
    E2 --> F3[Outgoing Feeder 33kV-3]
    G1[Neutral CT] -. ground fault .-> D1
    H1[Earth Mat] -. earthing .-> A2
    H1 -. earthing .-> A3
    H1 -. earthing .-> A4
    H1 -. earthing .-> D1
```

*Block Functionality Note:* This maps a typical 132/33 kV outdoor AIS substation with two incoming lines, main + transfer bus arrangement, lightning arrester, PLCC wave trap, and step-down power transformer. **Bus-bars are shown as the central spine; all feeders branch off the bus.** Earth mat ties every metallic body for safety.

### 4.3 Indoor vs. Outdoor — Comparative Block Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New Substation Project Initiated] --> Q1{Available Voltage Level?}
    Q1 -->|<= 66 kV| Q2{Available Land Area?}
    Q1 -->|>= 132 kV| O1[Choose Outdoor AIS Substation]
    O1 --> O2{Land Cost in Metro?}
    O2 -->|> ₹ 2 Cr per acre| O3[Choose Outdoor GIS Substation]
    O2 -->|< ₹ 2 Cr per acre| O4[Continue with AIS]
    Q2 -->|Severely Constrained| I1[Choose Indoor Cubicle]
    Q2 -->|Plenty of Space| Q3{Atmospheric Pollution?}
    Q3 -->|Heavy industrial / coastal| I1
    Q3 -->|Clean ambient| I2[Outdoor Foundation Mounted]
    I1 --> End[Procurement and Engineering Phase]
    I2 --> End
    O3 --> End
    O4 --> End
```

### 4.4 Substation Equipment Layout — Cross-Section Schematic (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Highest Equipment: Lightning Arrester] --> B[Tallest Gantry: 132 kV Bus-bar]
    B --> C[Breaker and CT/PT Stand at Mid-height]
    C --> D[Isolator at Operator Reach Level 1.5m]
    D --> E[Cable Trench at Ground Level for LV Control Cables]
    E --> F[Earth Mat Buried 0.6m Below Grade]
    F --> G[Transformer Plinth with Rail Track for Movement]
    G --> H[Oil Sump Pit Beneath Transformer for Fire Safety]
```

*Block Functionality Note:* This vertical stacking shows how outdoor substations physically arrange equipment — the highest creepage is at the top, low-voltage control cabling at ground level, oil pit beneath the transformer for containment of any leak.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
*Define an indoor substation. Mention any two situations where an indoor substation is preferred over an outdoor one.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 Mark Definition + 1 Mark Each Situation):**

An **Indoor Substation** is a substation in which all the equipment — bus-bars, isolators, circuit breakers, instrument transformers, and the transformer itself — are installed inside a closed building structure, generally used for voltages up to **66 kV**.

**Situations where indoor is preferred:**
1. **Urban areas** with severe space constraints where land cost is prohibitively high.
2. **Polluted/coastal atmospheres** (e.g., chemical plants, marine environments) where salt-laden or corrosive air would degrade outdoor insulation.

*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for each situation = 3 marks)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*State the standard percentage impedance of a 500 kVA, 11/0.433 kV distribution transformer as per IS 1180.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

As per **IS 1180 (Part 1):2014** for oil-immersed naturally-cooled three-phase distribution transformers, the **standard percentage impedance (%Z)** for a **500 kVA** transformer is **5.5%** (with permissible tolerance of $\pm 10\%$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** The exact figure 5.5% is mandatory. A vague answer like "around 5–6%" will attract partial deduction.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** *(Comprehensive Design-Oriented)*

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 — Module 3 Main Question Pattern]**

> **(a)** *With the help of a neat single-line diagram, explain the layout of a typical 33/11 kV indoor cubicle-type distribution substation. Mention all major equipment and their functions. (7 Marks)*
>
> **(b)** *A 33/11 kV substation feeds a maximum demand of 8 MW at 0.85 power factor. If the future growth is 30%, determine: (i) the required MVA rating of the power transformer, and (ii) the bus-bar current rating of the 11 kV side. Assume transformer efficiency = 98%. (7 Marks)*

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**

A 33/11 kV indoor cubicle-type substation consists of the following equipment in a single-line diagram:

| Equipment | Function | Location |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| **33 kV Incomer Cubicle** | Receives incoming 33 kV supply; houses VCB, CTs, PTs | One end of the room |
| **33 kV Bus Coupler Cubicle** | Couples the two 33 kV bus sections; permits load transfer | Centre of 33 kV switchgear |
| **Power Transformer 33/11 kV** | Steps down voltage from 33 kV to 11 kV | Separate fire-rated transformer room or outdoor plinth |
| **11 kV Incomer Cubicle** | Incoming from LV side of power transformer | 11 kV switchgear room |
| **11 kV Outgoing Feeder Cubicles** | Each feeds a separate 11 kV outgoing distribution feeder | 11 kV switchgear room |
| **Bus Coupler 11 kV** | Couples two 11 kV bus sections for N-1 redundancy | 11 kV switchgear room |
| **LT Panel 433 V** | Auxiliary supply to substation lighting, battery charger, AC | Separate LT room |
| **Control Panel & SCADA** | Centralised protection, metering, remote operation | Control room |
| **Battery Bank 110 V DC** | Tripping and control supply during power failure | Battery room |
| **Fire Detection & Suppression System** | Detects and extinguishes transformer oil fires | Transformer room and cable trenches |

*Mark Split:* 1 mark for SLD, 3 marks for equipment list, 2 marks for explanation, 1 mark for layout neatness.

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given Data:**
- Maximum Demand $P_{max} = 8 \text{ MW} = 8000 \text{ kW}$
- Power Factor $\cos\phi = 0.85$
- Future growth factor $= 1.30$ (i.e., 30%)
- Transformer efficiency $\eta_{TX} = 0.98$

**Step 1 — Compute initial kVA demand**

$$
S_{demand} = \frac{P_{max}}{\cos\phi} = \frac{8000}{0.85} = 9411.76 \text{ kVA}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute design kVA with future growth and efficiency**

$$
S_{TX, design} = \frac{1.30 \times S_{demand}}{\eta_{TX}} = \frac{1.30 \times 9411.76}{0.98}
$$

$$
S_{TX, design} = \frac{12235.29}{0.98} = 12485.0 \text{ kVA} \approx 12.5 \text{ MVA}
$$

**Step 3 — Select standard transformer rating**

The next **standard MVA rating** above 12.485 MVA is **15 MVA** (preferred standard sizes: 5, 7.5, 10, 12.5, 15, 20 MVA).

**Therefore, transformer rating = 15 MVA, 33/11 kV**

**Step 4 — Compute 11 kV side bus-bar current**

$$
I_{11kV} = \frac{S_{TX}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{LV}} = \frac{15 \times 10^6}{\sqrt{3} \times 11000}
$$

$$
I_{11kV} = \frac{15{,}000{,}000}{19052.56} = 787.30 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply derating factor 1.25**

$$
I_{bus, design} = 1.25 \times 787.30 = 984.13 \text{ A}
$$

**Therefore, select a 1000 A rated bus-bar for the 11 kV side.**

*Mark Split:* 2 marks for Step 1–2, 2 marks for Step 3, 2 marks for Step 4–5, 1 mark for final answer with units.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks** *(Alternative Comprehensive Choice)*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Module 3 Alternate Pattern]**

> **(a)** *Compare indoor and outdoor substations on the basis of any seven technical parameters. (7 Marks)*
>
> **(b)** *Explain the step-by-step procedure for the selection of a power transformer for a new 11 kV substation. State the standard percentage impedance and vector group for a 1000 kVA, 11/0.433 kV distribution transformer. (7 Marks)*

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks: Comparison Table**

| # | Parameter | Indoor Substation | Outdoor Substation |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Voltage Range** | Up to 66 kV | 132 kV and above |
| 2 | **Land Area** | Very small (cubicles) | Very large (gantries) |
| 3 | **Capital Cost** | High (building) | Low (no building) |
| 4 | **Safety Clearances** | Reduced clearances inside | Large clearances between phases |
| 5 | **Maintenance** | Easy, weather-independent | Difficult in rain/wind |
| 6 | **Pollution Effect** | Negligible (enclosed) | Severe (insulator pollution) |
| 7 | **Fault Withstand** | Limited (40 kA typical) | High (80 kA typical) |
| 8 | **Aesthetic** | Better for urban areas | Industrial/corporate look |

*(1 mark for each correct row, max 7 marks for 7 parameters)*

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks: Step-by-Step Transformer Selection**

The procedure for selecting a power/distribution transformer for a new 11 kV substation is as follows:

**Step 1 — Collect Load Data**
Gather diversified load list, maximum demand, and power factor from connected consumers.

**Step 2 — Compute kVA Rating**

$$
S_{TX} = \frac{P_{max}}{\cos\phi \cdot \eta_{TX}}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Diversity & Future Growth Factor**
Multiply by simultaneity factor (0.7–0.85) and future growth factor (1.25 for residential, 1.5 for industrial).

**Step 4 — Choose Standard Rating**
Select the next higher **standard kVA rating** from IS 1180 standard series.

**Step 5 — Select Voltage Ratio**
11 kV / 0.433 kV for distribution (line-to-line LV).

**Step 6 — Select Vector Group**
For 11/0.433 kV three-phase 4-wire distribution, the **mandatory vector group is Dyn11**.

**Step 7 — Select Cooling Class**
For ratings up to 250 kVA → **AN (Air Natural)**, 250 kVA to 1000 kVA → **ONAN** (Oil Natural Air Natural).

**Step 8 — Select Tap Changer**
For 1000 kVA distribution → **OCTC ±5% in 2.5% steps** (off-circuit, set at commissioning).

**Step 9 — Select %Z**
As per IS 1180:2014, for **1000 kVA transformer, %Z = 6.0%** (with ±10% tolerance).

**Step 10 — Select Transformer Losses**
Ensure total losses (no-load + load at 75°C) are within the **star-labelled 5-star or as per KSEB tender specification** for energy efficiency.

**Final Selection:**
> **1 × 1000 kVA, 11/0.433 kV, Dyn11, ONAN, %Z = 6.0%, OCTC ±5% in 2.5% steps, IS 1180 Level-2 Star, Outdoor/Plinth Mounted Oil-Immersed Distribution Transformer**

*Mark Split:* 1 mark per step × 7 steps = 7 marks (Steps 1–7 with brief justification).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas — Examiner Insights (Read Carefully!):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting Vector Group in Transformer Selection:** Students often write "1000 kVA, 11/0.433 kV" but **omit Dyn11** or **write it incorrectly as "Dyn1"**. This loses **1 full mark** in the 14-mark question.
>
> 2. **Wrong Standard kVA Selection:** Writing "12.5 MVA" when 15 MVA is the next standard size. Standard series is 5, 7.5, 10, 12.5, 15, 20, 25, 31.5, 40, 50 MVA — always pick from this exact list.
>
> 3. **Confusing Line-to-Line and Phase Voltages:** Using 11 kV in single-phase formula instead of three-phase. Always remember: $I = P / (\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot \cos\phi)$.
>
> 4. **Not Drawing the SLD:** In any 7-mark indoor/outdoor substation question, an SLD is worth 1.5–2 marks. **Omitting SLD = losing 2 marks**.
>
> 5. **%Z Value:** Forgetting to state the tolerance of $\pm 10\%$. Examiners award partial marks only if the numerical value is correct.
>
> 6. **Forgetting Derating Factor:** Computing 787 A but not applying the 1.25 safety factor. KTU expects the *design* bus current, not the *nominal* bus current.
>
> 7. **Cooling Class Confusion:** Writing "ONAF" for 250 kVA transformer (incorrect — ONAF requires fans, which are for >1000 kVA ratings). Use ONAN for ≤1000 kVA.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3 (Substation + Transformer Selection)**

- [x] **Indoor Substation:** Used for $\leq 66$ kV; cubicle-type is most common; 1.5× cost.
- [x] **Outdoor Substation:** Used for $\geq 132$ kV; conventional AIS uses gantries; 1.0× cost.
- [x] **GIS:** Voltage range 66–765 kV; footprint 15% of AIS; cost 2.5× of AIS.
- [x] **Bus-bar Current Formula:** $I = P / (\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot \cos\phi)$, apply 1.25 derating.
- [x] **Fault Current Formula:** $I_{sc} = MVA_{sc} \times 10^3 / (\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L)$.
- [x] **Standard %Z (IS 1180):** 100 kVA→4.5%, 250 kVA→5.0%, 500 kVA→5.5%, 1000 kVA→6.0%.
- [x] **Vector Group Dyn11:** Mandatory for 11/0.433 kV distribution (Dyn1 cannot parallel).
- [x] **Cooling Class:** AN ≤200 kVA, ONAN 200–1000 kVA, ONAF >1000 kVA.
- [x] **Tap Changer:** OCTC ±5% for distribution; OLTC ±10% for power transformers.
- [x] **Future Growth Factor:** 1.25 (residential), 1.50 (industrial/commercial).
- [x] **N-1 Criterion:** Two transformers in parallel for critical load.
- [x] **Standard kVA/MVA Series:** 100, 250, 500, 630, 1000, 1250, 1600, 2000, 2500 kVA / 5, 7.5, 10, 12.5, 15, 20, 25, 31.5, 40, 50, 63, 80, 100 MVA.
- [x] **Safety Clearance:** Use **highest system voltage** (not nominal) for clearance calculation.
- [x] **Creepage Distance:** 16 mm/kV indoor, 25 mm/kV outdoor clean, 31 mm/kV outdoor polluted.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule for KTU Board Exam:**
> *"Always draw SLD → state vector group → state %Z → apply future growth + derating factor → pick next standard rating."*
> This 5-step mantra, if followed strictly, will guarantee full marks in any Module 3 substation/transformer design question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Switch Gears and Protective Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT752 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Switch Gears and Protective Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Switchgear?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> **Switchgear** is the collective term covering all the equipment used for **switching, controlling, protecting, and isolating** power system apparatus. It encompasses devices such as **circuit breakers, isolators, switches, fuses, relays, current transformers (CTs), and potential transformers (PTs)** that collectively ensure safe, reliable, and economical operation of the electrical network.

The term is an umbrella word that combines two functions:
- **Switching** – the act of making or breaking a circuit under normal or fault conditions.
- **Gearing (Apparatus)** – the physical equipment that performs this function.

## 1.2 Why Do We Need Switchgear in Electrical Design?

> [!NOTE]
> Every power system fault — short circuits, overloads, earth faults, over-voltages — must be **detected, isolated, and cleared in milliseconds** to prevent catastrophic damage to transformers, generators, cables, and human life.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Highway Toll System

Imagine a **multi-lane expressway** carrying thousands of cars (analogous to **current**) per second. Switchgear acts as a smart toll-booth and traffic-control system:

| Real-World Element | Electrical Switchgear Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Highway lane | **Power conductor (bus-bar)** |
| Toll-booth operator | **Relay** (senses abnormal conditions) |
| Automatic boom-barrier | **Tripping mechanism** of circuit breaker |
| Toll-booth computer | **Protection scheme / logic** |
| Boom barrier that physically stops cars | **Circuit breaker contacts** |
| Inspection road (bypass) | **Isolator** (visible-break for maintenance) |
| Speed cameras | **CTs and PTs** (measure current and voltage) |
| Backup traffic police | **Fuses / backup protection** |

So, when a "car crash" (a fault) occurs, the **toll computer (relay)** senses the abnormal flow, **operates the boom barrier (breaker)** to isolate the faulty lane, and diverts traffic safely — all within fractions of a second.

## 1.4 Essential Qualities of an Ideal Switchgear

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme **Course Outcome CO3** emphasizes that students must appreciate the **operational, mechanical, and dielectric requirements** of switchgear. An ideal switchgear must satisfy:

1. **Absolute Discrimination** — It must operate **only** on the assigned zone, leaving healthy zones unaffected.
2. **Reliability** — It must operate whenever a fault occurs, **without fail**.
3. **Speed** — Operating time is critical (typically **0.04 s to 0.1 s** in modern breakers).
4. **Simplicity** — Less complexity means fewer chances of failure.
5. **Economy** — Should be cost-effective for the rating of the system.

## 1.5 KTU Standard Voltage & Current Rating Categories

> [!NOTE]
> Per **Indian Standard IS 3427 / IEC 62271**, switchgear is broadly classified by breaking capacity:
> - **Low Voltage (LV) Switchgear:** Up to **1.1 kV** (typically 415 V, 690 V).
> - **Medium Voltage (MV) Switchgear:** **3.3 kV, 6.6 kV, 11 kV, 33 kV**.
> - **High Voltage (HV) Switchgear:** **66 kV, 110 kV, 132 kV, 220 kV, 400 kV, 765 kV**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Components of a Switchgear Assembly

### A. **Sensing Elements (CTs & PTs)**

**Current Transformers (CTs):** Step down line current (primary: thousands of amps) to a standardized secondary current of **1 A or 5 A** for relay measurement. They also provide isolation between the high-voltage primary and the sensitive relay coil.

**Potential Transformers (PTs):** Step down primary voltage to a standardized **110 V / √3** secondary for protection and metering.

### B. **Relays (Decision Element)**

The "brain" of the protection system. Compares the measured quantity against a pre-set threshold (pickup value) and issues a trip command.

> [!NOTE]
> A relay is essentially a **low-power switch**. It does not break the fault current directly — it merely signals the circuit breaker.

### C. **Circuit Breaker (Execution Element)**

The "muscle." Physically interrupts the fault current using arc-quenching media (air, oil, SF₆ gas, or vacuum). Standard trip times are **3 to 5 cycles** (50–100 ms for a 50 Hz system).

### D. **Isolator / Disconnector (Safety Element)**

A **no-load mechanical switch** that provides a visible break for maintenance safety. **It must never be opened under load** — that is the golden rule in KTU board exams.

## 2.2 Types of Circuit Breakers (KTU High-Yield)

| S.No | Type | Arc-Quenching Medium | Typical Voltage Range | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Air Break CB (ACB)** | Atmospheric air | LV (< 1.1 kV) | Simple arc chutes |
| 2 | **Oil Circuit Breaker (OCB)** | Bulk / Minimum oil | 3.3 kV – 220 kV (older) | Oil acts as insulator and coolant |
| 3 | **Air Blast CB (ABCB)** | Compressed air blast | 110 kV – 400 kV | Fast arc extinction |
| 4 | **SF₆ Circuit Breaker** | Sulphur Hexafluoride gas | 11 kV – 765 kV | Excellent dielectric strength (**~ 2.5× air at same pressure**) |
| 5 | **Vacuum Circuit Breaker (VCB)** | Vacuum interrupter | 3.3 kV – 36 kV | Maintenance-free, widely used in MV |
| 6 | **DCB / GIS** | SF₆ in sealed modules | 33 kV – 765 kV | Compact substation footprint |

## 2.3 Types of Protective Relays

| S.No | Relay Type | Operating Principle | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Electromagnetic Attraction** | Plunger / Solenoid pulled by magnetic force | Overcurrent, Earth fault |
| 2 | **Electromagnetic Induction** | Induction disc / cup (similar to induction motor) | IDMT overcurrent, directional |
| 3 | **Static Relays** | Solid-state analogue electronics | Faster, no moving parts |
| 4 | **Numerical / Digital Relays** | Microprocessor-based algorithms | Multi-function, self-testing, **CTU 2024 syllabus priority** |
| 5 | **Differential Relays** | Compares current entering and leaving a zone | Transformer, generator, bus-bar protection |
| 6 | **Distance Relays** | Measures impedance $Z = V/I$ | Transmission line protection |

## 2.4 Protective Devices (Independent of Relays)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fuses** are the **simplest and oldest** protective devices. A fuse element melts when $I^2 t$ exceeds a threshold, breaking the circuit.

- **Rewirable / Semi-closed fuse** – Domestic LV use.
- **HRC (High Rupturing Capacity) Cartridge Fuse** – Industrial, up to **80 kA** rupturing capacity.
- **Drop-out fuse (DOF)** – 11 kV / 22 kV distribution transformers.
- **Expulsion type fuse** – Outdoor HV use.

## 2.5 ⚡ KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet (CRITICAL — Memorize!)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` instead of `|` for absolute values to preserve markdown table integrity.

| S.No | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Unit / Standard Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Symmetrical Breaking Capacity | $I_{sc} = \dfrac{\text{MVA} \times 10^6}{\sqrt{3} \times V_{LL} \times 10^3}$ | kA |
| 2 | Making Capacity | $I_{mak} = 2.55 \times I_{sc}$ (peak) | kA |
| 3 | Short-Time Withstand | $I_{th} = I_{sc} \times \sqrt{t/3}$ for t seconds | kA |
| 4 | Arc Energy (Hörmann's equation) | $E_a = K \cdot I^{1.5} \cdot t_a$ | Joules |
| 5 | Relay Pickup Current | $I_{pickup} = \dfrac{\text{CT ratio} \times \text{Secondary pickup}}{\text{Plug Setting Multiplier (PSM)}}$ | A |
| 6 | Plug Setting Multiplier (PSM) | $PSM = \dfrac{I_{fault,sec}}{I_{pickup,sec}}$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Time Multiplier Setting (TMS) | $T_{op} = \dfrac{0.14 \cdot TMS}{(PSM)^{0.02} - 1}$ (IDMT, standard) | seconds |
| 8 | Impedance seen by Distance Relay | $Z_{seen} = \dfrac{V_{ph-ph}}{I_{phase}}$ | Ω/phase |
| 9 | Differential Bias Current | $I_{bias} = \dfrac{\vert I_1 \vert + \vert I_2 \vert}{2}$ | A |
| 10 | Operating Current of Differential | $I_{op} = \vert I_1 - I_2 \vert$ | A |
| 11 | CT Knee-Point Voltage (KPV) | $V_{kp} \geq K_s \cdot I_{sec} \cdot (R_{ct} + R_{l} + R_{r})$ | Volts |
| 12 | Restriking Voltage | $v_r = V_m (1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ where $\tau = L/R$ | V |
| 13 | RRRV (Rate of Rise of Restriking Voltage) | $RRRV = \dfrac{1.414 \cdot V_{ph}}{R_m \cdot C}$ | kV/μs |
| 14 | Critical Resistance of CB | $R_c = \dfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$ | Ω |

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Transmission Sub-stations (EHV/HV):** Numerical distance relays + SF₆ breakers.
- **Industrial LV Switchboards:** ACBs, MCCBs, HRC fuses, numerical overcurrent relays.
- **Distribution (11 kV / 22 kV):** VCBs + IDMT overcurrent + earth-fault relays + DOF for transformers.
- **Generators & Transformers:** Biased differential relays (Merz-Price circulating-current scheme).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 DERIVATION 1 — Symmetrical Short-Circuit Current (Three-Phase Fault)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **single most-tested derivation** in KTU Board Exams for Switchgear.

### Problem Setup
Given a 3-phase system delivering apparent power $S$ at line-to-line voltage $V_{LL}$, derive the RMS short-circuit current at the fault point.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Write the relationship between apparent power, line voltage, and line current.**

By definition of 3-phase apparent power:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{LL} \cdot I_L \quad \text{[in VA, V in Volts, } I_L \text{ in Amps]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the line current $I_L$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_L &= \frac{S}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{LL}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Express $S$ in MVA and $V_{LL}$ in kV for convenience.**

If $S$ is in MVA ($= S \times 10^6$ VA) and $V_{LL}$ is in kV ($= V_{LL} \times 10^3$ V):

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_L &= \frac{S \times 10^6}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{LL} \times 10^3} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{S \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{LL}} \quad \text{[in Amperes]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Under a bolted 3-phase fault, the line current equals the fault current.**

Hence, the **symmetrical short-circuit current** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{sc} &= \frac{\text{MVA} \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{LL} (\text{kV})} \quad \text{[A]} \\[4pt]
I_{sc} \, (\text{kA}) &= \frac{\text{MVA}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{LL} (\text{kV})}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **fundamental formula** from which *making capacity*, *breaking capacity*, and *short-time current rating* are derived. Memorize it in kA form for instant KTU problem-solving.

---

## 3.2 DERIVATION 2 — IDMT Relay Operating Time (Standard Inverse Disc)

### Problem Setup
A relay with **Standard Inverse IDMT** characteristic has the operating equation:

$$
t = \frac{0.14 \cdot TMS}{(PSM)^{0.02} - 1}
$$

**Derive the operating time** for $I_{fault} = 2000 \text{ A}$, CT ratio $= 200/5$, PSM = 1.0, and TMS = 0.5.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Compute secondary fault current.**

CT ratio $= 200/5 = 40$. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{sec} &= \frac{I_{primary}}{\text{CT Ratio}} = \frac{2000}{40} = 50 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Plug Setting Multiplier (PSM).**

If pickup is set at 1.0 (i.e., 100 % of CT secondary rating = 5 A), then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PSM &= \frac{I_{sec}}{I_{pickup,sec}} = \frac{50}{5} = 10
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the IDMT equation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \frac{0.14 \cdot TMS}{(PSM)^{0.02} - 1} \\[4pt]
t &= \frac{0.14 \times 0.5}{10^{0.02} - 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate $10^{0.02}$.**

Using $\ln 10 = 2.3026$, we have:

$$
\begin{aligned}
10^{0.02} &= e^{0.02 \cdot \ln 10} = e^{0.02 \times 2.3026} = e^{0.04605} \approx 1.0471
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the denominator.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
10^{0.02} - 1 &\approx 0.0471
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Final operating time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \frac{0.07}{0.0471} \approx 1.486 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This shows the **disc time delay decreases sharply as PSM increases** — exactly the inverse-definite-minimum-time (IDMT) behavior KTU examiners love to test.

---

## 3.3 DERIVATION 3 — Differential Relay Bias Characteristic

The biased (restrained) differential relay uses a **bias characteristic** to prevent maloperation during external faults and CT saturation.

### Operating Condition

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{op} &> K \cdot I_{bias} + I_{0}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $K$ is the bias slope (typically **0.3 to 0.5**) and $I_0$ is the minimum pickup current.

### Numerical Verification

Suppose through-fault current produces $I_1 = 4.0 \text{ A}$ and $I_2 = 3.6 \text{ A}$ on a 5 A CT secondary (CT saturation has caused mismatch). With $K = 0.4$ and $I_0 = 0.2 \text{ A}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{op} &= \vert I_1 - I_2 \vert = \vert 4.0 - 3.6 \vert = 0.4 \text{ A} \\[4pt]
I_{bias} &= \frac{\vert 4.0 \vert + \vert 3.6 \vert}{2} = 3.8 \text{ A} \\[4pt]
K \cdot I_{bias} + I_0 &= 0.4 \times 3.8 + 0.2 = 1.52 + 0.2 = 1.72 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $I_{op} = 0.4 \text{ A} \not > 1.72 \text{ A}$, the relay correctly **restrains** — no trip — confirming discrimination against external faults.

---

## 3.4 ANALYTICAL MATRIX — Selection of CB for an Application

| Application | Voltage | Recommended CB | Recommended Relay |
|---|---|---|---|
| Domestic Service Main | 415 V | MCB / MCCB | Thermal-magnetic built-in |
| Industrial LT Incomer | 415 V | ACB (630 A – 6300 A) | Numerical overcurrent + earth fault |
| 11 kV Distribution Feeder | 11 kV | VCB | IDMT O/C + E/F numerical relay |
| 33 kV Substation Incomer | 33 kV | VCB or SF₆ | Distance + Back-up O/C |
| 110 kV / 220 kV Line | EHV | SF₆ (Puffer / Self-blast) | Numerical Distance (Zones 1, 2, 3) |
| 220 kV Transformer | EHV | SF₆ | Biased Differential (Merz-Price) + REF + O/C |
| 400 kV Bus Coupler | EHV | SF₆ (GIS) | Bus-bar differential (High-impedance) |

---

## 3.5 DERIVATION 4 — Restriking Voltage & RRRV

The **Rate of Rise of Restriking Voltage (RRRV)** governs arc re-ignition in circuit breakers:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_r(t) &= V_m \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right) \\[4pt]
RRRV &= \left.\frac{dv_r}{dt}\right|_{t=0} = \frac{V_m}{\tau} = \frac{V_m \cdot R}{L} \\[6pt]
\text{In terms of } R_m \text{ (minimum arc resistance) and } C: \quad RRRV &= \frac{1.414 \cdot V_{ph}}{R_m \cdot C} \;\; [\text{kV/}\mu\text{s}]
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A high RRRV (> 1 kV/μs) demands **puffer-type SF₆** or **vacuum breakers**, which can extinguish the arc within the first current zero.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Switchgear System Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["POWER SYSTEM PRIMARY: Three Phase Bus Bar 11kV to 400kV"] --> B["SENSING STAGE: Current Transformer CT and Potential Transformer PT"]
    B --> C["MEASUREMENT STAGE: Secondary Current 1A or 5A, Voltage 110V by root 3"]
    C --> D["DECISION STAGE: Numerical or Electromagnetic Protection Relay"]
    D --> E{"COMPARISON: Fault level above Pickup threshold?"}
    E -- YES --> F["TRIP COMMAND: DC Battery 110V or 220V trip coil energised"]
    E -- NO --> G["RESTRAINED: Relay remains in standby healthy state"]
    F --> H["EXECUTION STAGE: Circuit Breaker Trip Mechanism"]
    H --> I["ARC QUENCHING: SF6 gas or Vacuum or Oil or Air blast"]
    I --> J["ISOLATION: Contacts separate faulted section from bus"]
    J --> K["INDICATION: Trip flag annunciator alarm SCADA signal"]
    G --> L["NORMAL OPERATION: System continues undisturbed"]
    K --> M["MAINTENANCE: Isolator opened for visible break permit to work"]
    L --> M
```

## 4.2 Process Flow — Trip Chain Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["STEP 1: Fault occurs on protected zone"] --> S2["STEP 2: CT senses primary current rises sharply"]
    S2 --> S3["STEP 3: Relay secondary current exceeds Pickup setting"]
    S3 --> S4["STEP 4: Relay disc or algorithm operates"]
    S4 --> S5["STEP 5: Trip coil of Circuit Breaker energised from DC battery"]
    S5 --> S6["STEP 6: Breaker latch releases contacts open"]
    S6 --> S7["STEP 7: Arc is drawn and extinguished in quenching medium"]
    S7 --> S8["STEP 8: Faulted zone isolated auxiliary contacts close"]
    S8 --> S9["STEP 9: Trip flag drops annunciator sounds SCADA logs event"]
```

## 4.3 Architecture — Differential Protection of a Power Transformer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    HV["HV Side Winding 110kV"] --> CTHV["CT1 on HV side 400 to 1 ratio"]
    LV["LV Side Winding 11kV"] --> CTLV["CT2 on LV side 4000 to 1 ratio"]
    CTHV --> RELAY["Numerical Biased Differential Relay 87T"]
    CTLV --> RELAY
    RELAY --> BIAS{"Bias Characteristic: Operating current greater than K times Bias current plus pickup?"}
    BIAS -- INTERNAL FAULT --> TRIP["Trip both HV and LV Circuit Breakers instantly"]
    BIAS -- EXTERNAL FAULT OR THROUGH FAULT --> RESTR["Restrain: No trip CT mismatch tolerated by bias slope"]
    TRIP --> ALARM["Annunciator and SCADA: Transformer Differential Operated"]
    RESTR --> HEALTHY["System remains healthy"]
```

## 4.4 Architecture — Distance Relay Three-Zone Protection

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    BUS["Sending End Bus 220kV"] --> Z1["Zone 1: 80 percent of Line AB Impedance: Trip instantaneous"]
    Z1 --> Z2["Zone 2: 120 percent of Line AB plus 50 percent of next line: Trip after 0.3 second delay"]
    Z2 --> Z3["Zone 3: Full Line AB plus full Line BC plus 25 percent of next line: Trip after 1.0 second delay back up"]
    Z3 --> END["Reach extends to remote bus back up protection for adjacent zones"]
```

## 4.5 Functional Layout — HRC Fuse Operation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Normal State: Current within rated value"] --> B["Overload or Short Circuit: I squared t exceeds limit"]
    B --> C["Fuse Element Heats: Silver or Copper element reaches melting point"]
    C --> D["Element Melts and Vaporises"]
    D --> E["Arc Initiated inside Sand or Quartz filler"]
    E --> F["Arc Voltage rises Arc Resistance increases Current collapses"]
    F --> G["Final Arc Quenching: Circuit broken safely"]
    G --> H["Indicators: Blown fuse flag visible to operator"]
```

## 4.6 Comparative Block Diagram — Types of Circuit Breakers

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CB["CIRCUIT BREAKER CLASSIFICATION BY ARC QUENCHING MEDIUM"] --> M1["AIR BREAK CB: For LV applications arc chute splits and stretches arc"]
    CB --> M2["OIL CB: Bulk oil or Minimum oil; oil cools and deionises arc"]
    CB --> M3["AIR BLAST CB: Compressed air at 20 to 30 bar extinguishes arc in axial or cross blast"]
    CB --> M4["SF6 CB: Sulphur Hexafluoride gas at 4 to 6 bar; electronegative gas captures free electrons"]
    CB --> M5["VACUUM CB: Contacts inside vacuum bottle below 10 to 5 torr; arc extinguishes at first current zero"]
    M4 --> H1["Used for EHV 66kV to 765kV"]
    M5 --> H2["Used for MV 3.3kV to 36kV most common in 11kV substations"]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in $I_{sc} = \dfrac{MVA}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{LL}}$ derivation costs **2 marks instantly**. Always state kA.
> 2. **Conflating Isolator with Circuit Breaker**: An isolator is a **no-load device** — opening it under load is a fatal mistake. Examiners specifically test this.
> 3. **IDMT formula misuse**: Students often use $I_{pickup}$ instead of $I_{pickup,sec}$ in PSM. The pickup is **always on the secondary side**.
> 4. **Skipping the bias check** in differential relay problems will lose **3 marks** in a 14-mark question.
> 5. **Failing to mention arc-quenching medium** in CB classification questions yields partial marking.

---

## 📘 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short-Answer / Concept)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between a Circuit Breaker and an Isolator.** Mention the operating condition for each.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Circuit Breaker | Isolator |
|---|---|---|
| Function | **Make or break** a circuit under **load or fault** conditions | Provide a **visible break** for maintenance |
| Arc-quenching | Equipped with arc-quenching medium (SF₆, oil, vacuum) | **No arc-quenching** — opens only under no-load |
| Operating mechanism | Trip coil operated by relay | Manual or motor-operated |
| Use in sequence | Closes **before** isolator | Opens **after** breaker opens |
| Examples | SF₆, VCB, ACB | Pantograph isolator, double-break isolator |

> **Operating Condition:** A **circuit breaker** is closed/opened under any current (load, overload, short-circuit). An **isolator** is operated **only when the breaker is open and no current flows**.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is meant by "making capacity" of a circuit breaker? How is it related to the breaking capacity?**

**Model Answer:**
- **Making capacity** is the **peak value of the current** (including DC component) that a circuit breaker can **close** onto a short-circuit without contact welding or excessive electromagnetic stress.
- It is governed by the **peak factor** $K$ (typically **1.8 to 2.55**, depending on system X/R ratio and short-circuit point on the wave).

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{mak} &= \sqrt{2} \cdot K \cdot I_{sc, sym} \\[4pt]
\text{For the worst case } K = 1.8: \quad I_{mak} &\approx 2.55 \cdot I_{sc}
\end{aligned}
$$

- The relation: **Making Capacity ≈ 2.55 × Symmetrical Breaking Capacity** (per IS 3427 / IEC 62271). Hence, the **making capacity is always higher** than the breaking capacity.

---

## 📗 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]

**A. (a)** A 3-phase, 11 kV generating station has a fault level of **250 MVA**. Determine:
- (i) The symmetrical breaking current.
- (ii) The making capacity of the circuit breaker.
- (iii) The short-time current rating for **3 seconds**.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** Fault level $S_{sc} = 250$ MVA, $V_{LL} = 11$ kV, $t = 3$ s.

**(i) Symmetrical breaking current $I_{sc}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{sc} &= \frac{S_{sc} \times 10^6}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{LL} \times 10^3} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{250 \times 10^6}{\sqrt{3} \cdot 11 \times 10^3} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{250 \times 10^3}{1.732 \times 11} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{250000}{19.052} \\[4pt]
&\approx 13122 \text{ A} \approx \mathbf{13.12 \text{ kA}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Making capacity $I_{mak}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{mak} &= 2.55 \times I_{sc} \\[4pt]
&= 2.55 \times 13.12 \\[4pt]
&= \mathbf{33.46 \text{ kA (peak)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Short-time current rating for 3 s:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{th} &= I_{sc} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{t}{3}} = 13.12 \times \sqrt{\frac{3}{3}} = \mathbf{13.12 \text{ kA}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer:** $I_{sc} = 13.12$ kA, $I_{mak} = 33.46$ kA, $I_{th}(3s) = 13.12$ kA.

**A. (b)** With a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working of a SF₆ circuit breaker**. State two advantages over an oil circuit breaker. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:** A typical SF₆ puffer-type circuit breaker consists of:

1. **Fixed Contact** — stationary arcing contact.
2. **Moving Contact** — attached to a piston inside a cylinder.
3. **Nozzle and Arc Chamber** — gas flows through a shaped PTFE nozzle.
4. **SF₆ Gas Reservoir** — filled at **4 to 6 bar (gauge)**.
5. **Operating Mechanism** — spring or hydraulic.

**Working Principle:**
- During a trip, the moving contact pulls away from the fixed contact.
- The piston compresses the SF₆ gas, which is forced axially through the nozzle across the arc.
- The **electronegative SF₆ molecules** capture free electrons, forming heavy negative ions that **deionise the arc path**.
- At the **first current zero**, the dielectric strength of SF₆ rapidly recovers (faster than air or oil).
- The arc extinguishes, and the contacts remain open.

**SF₆ Properties That Enable Arc Quenching:**

| Property | Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Dielectric strength | **~ 2.5× air** at same pressure | Smaller clearances |
| Arc-quenching | **100× air** | High RRRV tolerance |
| Chemical stability | Inert, non-flammable | Safe |
| Recombination | Fast electron–ion recombination | Quick recovery |

**Two Advantages over Oil CB:**
1. **No fire risk** — SF₆ is non-inflammable, unlike oil which is a fire hazard.
2. **Maintenance-free** — sealed gas system, no oil filtration, no carbon cleanup required.
3. **Smaller footprint** — higher dielectric strength allows compact GIS designs.
4. **Faster arc extinction** — higher RRRV withstand than bulk oil breakers.

> **Sketch Reference:** Draw a labelled diagram of the puffer-type SF₆ breaker showing the moving contact, fixed contact, nozzle, piston, and gas chamber. **(Block diagram given in Section 4.6 above is acceptable.)**

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]

**B. (a)** A 3-phase, 11 kV, **5 MVA** alternator is protected by a **Merz-Price circulating-current scheme** using a **balanced-beam relay** and CTs of ratio **400/5 A** on each phase. The relay is set to operate when the out-of-balance current exceeds **20% of the rated current**. Determine:
- (i) The minimum operating current of the relay.
- (ii) The current in the pilot wires at the pickup level.
- (iii) The percentage of stator winding that is protected if the fault current at the far end of the winding is **800 A** per phase. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** 3-phase alternator, $S = 5$ MVA, $V_{LL} = 11$ kV, CT ratio = 400/5, Relay setting = 20% of rated.

**(i) Rated current of the alternator (primary):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{rated} &= \frac{S \times 10^6}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{LL}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{5 \times 10^6}{\sqrt{3} \times 11 \times 10^3} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{5000}{19.052} \\[4pt]
&\approx 262.4 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Minimum operating (pickup) current of the relay:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{pickup, primary} &= 20\% \times 262.4 = \mathbf{52.48 \text{ A (primary)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Current in the pilot wires (CT secondary side):**

CT ratio $= 400/5 = 80$. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{pilot} &= \frac{52.48}{80} = \mathbf{0.656 \text{ A (secondary)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Percentage of winding protected (for $I_{fault,end} = 800$ A per phase):**

The relay pickup on primary is $52.48$ A. So the protected zone extends from the neutral (N) to a point where the fault current equals $52.48$ A.

Let **x** be the fraction of winding protected from the line end (terminal side). The fault current at the far end of the protected zone is **800 A** when the fault is at the **boundary** of the protected zone. For any fault closer to the terminal than this, current > 800 A and relay operates.

Using the linear current distribution along the winding (assuming uniform EMF), the fault at the **protected boundary** carries current $I_{pickup}$:

If the fault at the **end (neutral end)** of the winding produces 800 A, and the relay picks up at 52.48 A (which occurs at a point where the impedance is high enough that fault current has dropped to 52.48 A), the protected percentage is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Protected} &= \left(1 - \frac{I_{pickup}}{I_{fault,end}}\right) \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \left(1 - \frac{52.48}{800}\right) \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= (1 - 0.0656) \times 100 \\[4pt]
&\approx \mathbf{93.44\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer:** (i) 52.48 A (primary), (ii) 0.656 A in pilot wires, (iii) **93.44%** of the winding is protected.

**B. (b)** Explain the **IDMT characteristic** of a static/numerical overcurrent relay. Sketch the time-current curve and write the **standard inverse operating equation**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition of IDMT:**
**IDMT = Inverse Definite Minimum Time.**
- **Inverse:** Operating time is **inversely proportional** to the magnitude of fault current.
- **Definite Minimum Time:** A **minimum (definite) operating time** is reached at very high fault currents — the curve **flattens** and does not fall below this minimum.

**Time-Current Characteristics (description):**
At current slightly above pickup, the time is high (e.g., 3–10 seconds). As current increases, the time drops sharply. Beyond a multiple of ~10 × pickup, the time becomes constant (~0.1–0.3 s) — this is the **definite minimum time floor**.

**Standard Inverse Operating Equation (per IEC 60255-151):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \frac{0.14 \cdot TMS}{(PSM)^{0.02} - 1} \quad \text{[seconds]}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $t$ = operating time in seconds
- $TMS$ = Time Multiplier Setting (typically 0.05 to 1.1)
- $PSM = \dfrac{I_{fault,sec}}{I_{pickup,sec}}$ = Plug Setting Multiplier

**Sketch description:** A **rectangular hyperbola-like curve** in log-log axes, decreasing from high time at low current to a horizontal asymptote at very high current. **(See Section 4 for block flow; drawing not possible in plain text.)**

**Significance of TMS:**
- $TMS = 1$ → standard inverse time.
- $TMS = 0.1$ → ten times faster grading.
- Used for **coordination (discrimination)** between upstream and downstream relays.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute KTU Board Revision — Print This Section!**

✅ **Switchgear = Switching + Gearing (Apparatus)** — collective term for all protective gear.

✅ **Three essential functions:** (i) **Carry normal load** without overheating, (ii) **Break fault current** safely, (iii) **Provide isolation** for maintenance.

✅ **Key Components (in order):** CT/PT → Relay (decision) → Trip Coil (signal) → Circuit Breaker (execution) → Isolator (visible break).

✅ **Circuit Breaker Types (sorted by voltage):** ACB (LV) → OCB (MV) → VCB (MV: 3.3–36 kV) → SF₆ (HV/EHV) → Air Blast (legacy EHV).

✅ **Master Formula:** $\boxed{I_{sc} = \dfrac{\text{MVA}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{LL} (\text{kV})}}$ kA — the most-asked formula.

✅ **Making Capacity** = $2.55 \times I_{sc}$ — always peak, always higher than breaking.

✅ **IDMT Equation:** $t = \dfrac{0.14 \cdot TMS}{(PSM)^{0.02} - 1}$ — Standard Inverse per IEC.

✅ **Differential Relay Condition for Trip:** $I_{op} > K \cdot I_{bias} + I_0$, where $K \approx 0.3$–$0.5$ is the bias slope.

✅ **Distance Relay Zones:** Zone 1 = 80% of line (instantaneous), Zone 2 = 120% + 50% of next (0.3 s), Zone 3 = full + 25% of next (1.0 s).

✅ **Isolator Rule:** **Never operate an isolator under load.** Always open the breaker first, then the isolator.

✅ **CT Polarity:** P1, P2 (primary) and S1, S2 (secondary) — *P1 and S1 must be on the same instantaneous polarity side* in differential schemes.

✅ **SF₆ Gas:** Electronegative, dielectric strength 2.5× air, arc-quenching 100× air — used in EHV and GIS substations.

✅ **HRC Fuses:** Use silver element + quartz sand filler; rupturing capacity up to **80 kA**.

✅ **Bias Characteristic:** Prevents **false tripping** of differential relays due to **CT saturation** during external faults.

✅ **Coordination (Discrimination):** Use **TDS / TMS** to time-grade upstream and downstream relays — closer to fault = faster.

✅ **Auto-Reclosers:** Used in **11 kV / 22 kV distribution** to clear **transient faults** (lightning, tree contact) and restore supply within seconds.

---

> **END OF MODULE 3 NOTES — SWITCHGEAR AND PROTECTIVE DEVICES**
> *(Prepared per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol — 2024 Scheme aligned)*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Procedure for HT Connection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT752 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Procedure for HT Connection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HT (High Tension) Connection** is an electricity supply connection given to a consumer at a voltage level equal to or greater than **11 kV**, where the consumer owns, operates, and maintains the step-down transformer and associated high-voltage switchgear on the consumer's premises. The ownership boundary of the distribution transformer, metering equipment, and protective devices is shifted to the consumer side as per the **Central Electricity Authority (CEA) Regulations, 2010 (Measures relating to Safety and Electric Supply)** and the **Electricity Act, 2003**.

As per the **Kerala State Electricity Board (KSEB)**, the voltage classification for HT consumers is broadly categorized as:

| Voltage Level | Classification | Typical Consumer |
|---------------|----------------|------------------|
| **11 kV** | HT (Primary Distribution) | Industries, Commercial complexes |
| **22 kV** | HT (Primary Distribution) | Large industries, Hospitals |
| **33 kV** | HT (Sub-transmission) | Heavy industries, IT parks |
| **66 kV / 110 kV** | EHT (Extra High Tension) | SEZs, Bulk industrial consumers |
| **220 kV / above** | EHT / Bulk Supply | Power-intensive plants |

A consumer drawing a **contracted demand of 100 kVA and above** is typically eligible (and often required by KSEB) to take an HT connection instead of an LT connection, where the distribution transformer is installed on the consumer's land/building.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Water Reservoir" Model:**
> Think of the electrical grid like a municipal water supply system. An **LT (Low Tension) connection** is like having a small tap directly connected to a neighborhood pipeline — the utility gives you "ready-to-drink" water (230 V / 415 V) right at your doorstep, and they own the small pressure-reducing tank (distribution transformer). An **HT connection**, on the other hand, is like being a factory that draws raw water (high pressure) directly from the main trunk pipeline (11 kV or higher). Now *you* must install and maintain your own pressure-reducing station (step-down transformer), your own flow meters (HT metering), and safety valves (circuit breakers). The utility only provides the "raw high-pressure supply" up to a metering point at your boundary, and the rest is your responsibility.

---

### 1.3 Why HT Connection? (Engineering Justification)

1. **Reduced Line Losses**: At higher voltages, current for the same power is lower, so $I^2R$ copper losses are drastically reduced.
2. **Higher Load Capacity**: A single HT line can supply megawatts of load without excessive cable sizing.
3. **Voltage Regulation**: Voltage drop over long distances is significantly lower at higher voltages.
4. **Economic for Bulk Loads**: Industries with loads above **100 kVA** find HT more economical due to lower energy charges and tariff benefits.
5. **Reliability & Power Quality**: HT consumers often get dedicated feeders, ensuring better reliability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** As per the **Kerala State Electricity Regulatory Commission (KSERC) tariff order**, HT consumers enjoy lower per-unit energy charges (in ₹/kWh) compared to LT consumers of equivalent consumption, making HT financially attractive for large commercial and industrial establishments.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The HT Connection Procedure — Sequential Logic Breakdown

The complete procedure for obtaining an HT connection in Kerala (governed by KSEB and KSERC) follows these well-defined stages:

#### **Stage 1 — Pre-Application Feasibility Study**
- Consumer estimates the **Maximum Demand (MD)** of the proposed installation.
- Verifies proximity to existing **11 kV / 22 kV / 33 kV** feeders of KSEB.
- Identifies whether a dedicated feeder is required or tap-off from existing line is sufficient.
- Decides transformer rating, voltage ratio, and indoor/outdoor substation type.

#### **Stage 2 — Application Submission**
- Submit **Form A** (Application for new HT connection) along with:
  - Identity proof of applicant
  - Land/building ownership documents
  - Site plan with proposed substation location
  - Single-line diagram (SLD) of proposed HT installation
  - Load list with connected load in kW
  - Proposed maximum demand in kVA
  - Copy of Industrial license / KSPCB consent (for industries)
  - Building permit / occupancy certificate

#### **Stage 3 — Load Sanction & Technical Feasibility**
- KSEB's **Electrical Section Office / Divisional Office** conducts site inspection.
- Verifies availability of source feeder capacity.
- Issues **Letter of Sanction** with sanctioned MD, voltage level, and point of supply.
- Calculates and demands **service connection charges**, **development charges**, and **security deposit**.

#### **Stage 4 — Payment & Agreement Execution**
- Consumer pays the **initial security deposit** (refundable) and **development charges** (non-refundable).
- Signs the **HT Supply Agreement** with KSEB containing terms of supply, billing, and maintenance responsibilities.

#### **Stage 5 — Installation by Consumer**
- Consumer installs (through a licensed HT contractor):
  - **HT Switchgear** (VCB / SF6 / Oil circuit breaker)
  - **Step-down Transformer** (11 kV / 433 V, etc.)
  - **HT Cable** from metering point to transformer
  - **Protective Relays** (Overcurrent, Earth-fault, Differential, Buchholz)
  - **HT Metering Cubicle** (with CTs and PTs as per KSEB spec)
  - **Earthing System** (pipe/plate earthing as per IS 3043)
  - **Lightning Arresters**
  - **Fire-fighting equipment** and oil soak pit (for oil-type transformers)

#### **Stage 6 — Inspection by KSEB / Electrical Inspector**
- **Chief Electrical Inspector (CEI)** of Kerala conducts mandatory inspection under **Regulation 32 of CEA Regulations, 2010**.
- Verifies clearances (Section 56 of Electricity Act, 2003), earthing resistance, protective device settings, and safety provisions.
- Issues **Sanction for Energisation** if installation is satisfactory.

#### **Stage 7 — Meter Sealing & Energisation**
- KSEB seals the HT meter, installs the ToD (Time-of-Day) meter, and records initial readings.
- The consumer's HT installation is **energised** by KSEB officials.
- A **Service Connection Certificate** is issued marking the date of commencement of supply.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following formulas are essential for solving KTU numerical problems on HT Connection design and load estimation.**

| # | Formula | Description / Use Case | Units |
|---|---------|------------------------|-------|
| 1 | $MD_{sanctioned} = \dfrac{\sum L_i \times DF \times DF_d}{PF \times \eta}$ | Maximum Demand calculation (Connected Load × Demand Factor × Diversity Factor) | **kVA** |
| 2 | $S_{transformer} \geq 1.25 \times MD_{actual}$ | Standard practice: Transformer rating ≥ 125% of actual maximum demand for future load growth and to avoid overloading | **kVA** |
| 3 | $I_{HT} = \dfrac{MD_{kVA}}{\sqrt{3} \times V_{HT} \times 1000}$ | HT full-load current (3-phase) | **Amperes (A)** |
