| 4 | $I_{LT} = \dfrac{MD_{kVA}}{\sqrt{3} \times V_{LT} \times 1000}$ | LT full-load current (3-phase) on transformer secondary | **Amperes (A)** |
| 5 | $S_{sc} = \dfrac{V^2}{Z_{pu}} \times 1000$ | Short-circuit apparent power at any bus (per unit impedance method) | **MVA** |
| 6 | $I_{sc} = \dfrac{I_{FL}}{Z_{pu \text{ (transformer)}}}$ | Short-circuit current calculation (approximation: $I_{sc} = \dfrac{V}{Z_{sys}}$) | **kA** |
| 7 | $R_{earth} \leq \dfrac{V_{touch \text{ limit}}}{I_{fault \text{ max}}}$ | Maximum permissible earth electrode resistance for safety (IS 3043) | **Ohms ($\Omega$)** |
| 8 | $V_{drop \%} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3} \times I \times L \times (R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)}{V \times 10}$ | Percentage voltage drop in HT/LT line/cable | **%** |
| 9 | $E_{consumer} = \dfrac{MD_{kVA}}{MD_{total}} \times E_{fault}$ | Energy at consumer bus from system fault level | **kVA or MVA** |

> **Symbols:** $L_i$ = individual load, $DF$ = Demand Factor, $DF_d$ = Diversity Factor, $PF$ = Power Factor, $\eta$ = Efficiency, $V$ = Line voltage, $Z_{pu}$ = Per-unit impedance, $L$ = Length, $R$ = Resistance/km, $X$ = Reactance/km.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Utility of HT Connection Systems

- **Industrial Manufacturing Plants** (steel, cement, textiles) — primary beneficiaries.
- **Hospitals & IT Parks** requiring 24×7 reliable power with dedicated backup.
- **Shopping Malls & Multiplexes** with centralized HVAC and elevator loads.
- **Renewable Energy Generators** connecting to the grid at 11 kV / 22 kV.
- **Electric Vehicle (EV) Charging Hubs** with bulk load requirements.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Maximum Demand (MD) for HT Application

**Problem Setup:** A proposed HT consumer has the following connected loads in its industrial facility. Compute the **Maximum Demand in kVA** to be applied for, and the **recommended transformer rating**.

| Load | Quantity | Rating per unit | Total kW |
|------|----------|-----------------|----------|
| 3-Phase Induction Motors (75 kW each) | 4 | 75 kW | 300 kW |
| Lighting Load (single-phase aggregated) | – | – | 50 kW |
| HVAC Load (Centralized) | 2 chillers | 60 kW each | 120 kW |
| Welding Transformers (intermittent) | 3 | 25 kVA each | 60 kW |
| Office Equipment | – | – | 20 kW |

Given data:
- Overall **Demand Factor ($DF$)** = 0.75
- **Diversity Factor ($DF_d$)** = 1.15
- **Average Power Factor ($PF$)** = 0.85 (lagging)
- **System efficiency ($\eta$)** = 0.92

---

**Step 1 — Total Connected Load**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{connected} &= P_{motors} + P_{lighting} + P_{HVAC} + P_{welding} + P_{office} \\
P_{connected} &= 300 + 50 + 120 + 60 + 20 \\
P_{connected} &= 550 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* Sum all connected loads irrespective of diversity or demand factor to get the **installed capacity** of the facility.

---

**Step 2 — Apply Demand Factor**

The **Demand Factor** is the ratio of maximum demand to connected load. It accounts for the fact that not all loads operate simultaneously at full rating.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{max \, demand} &= P_{connected} \times DF \\
P_{max \, demand} &= 550 \times 0.75 \\
P_{max \, demand} &= 412.5 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* This represents the peak real power the consumer is realistically expected to draw.

---

**Step 3 — Convert to kVA (Apparent Power)**

Since the load is inductive (motors, transformers), we use the **Power Factor** to convert real power to apparent power.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{MD} &= \dfrac{P_{max \, demand}}{PF \times \eta} \\
S_{MD} &= \dfrac{412.5}{0.85 \times 0.92} \\
S_{MD} &= \dfrac{412.5}{0.782} \\
S_{MD} &\approx 527.5 \text{ kVA}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* The utility bills and contracts are made in **kVA**, not kW, because apparent power accounts for the burden on the network including reactive component and system losses.

---

**Step 4 — Apply Diversity Factor (Optional, for feeder design)**

The Diversity Factor accounts for the fact that multiple loads within a group do not all peak together.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{MD,\,sanctioned} &= S_{MD} \times DF_d \\
S_{MD,\,sanctioned} &= 527.5 \times 1.15 \\
S_{MD,\,sanctioned} &\approx 606.6 \text{ kVA}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* The utility often grants **Diversity Factor allowance** only if the consumer can demonstrate well-subdivided loads (e.g., motors with sequential starting). Otherwise, MD = 527.5 kVA is applied for.

---

**Step 5 — Apply Standard Safety Margin for Transformer Selection**

As per KSEB / industry practice, transformer rating should be **at least 25% above** the sanctioned MD.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{transformer} &\geq 1.25 \times S_{MD,\,sanctioned} \\
S_{transformer} &\geq 1.25 \times 606.6 \\
S_{transformer} &\geq 758.3 \text{ kVA}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** A **standard 750 kVA or 1000 kVA** transformer would be selected. Choosing the next standard rating (**1000 kVA**) provides headroom for future expansion.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — HT vs LT Connection Comparison

| Parameter | LT Connection | HT Connection |
|-----------|---------------|---------------|
| Supply Voltage | 230 V / 415 V | **≥ 11 kV** |
| Minimum Demand | < 100 kVA | ≥ 100 kVA |
| Transformer Owner | KSEB (utility) | **Consumer** |
| Meter Type | LT kWh meter | HT CT-PT operated ToD meter |
| Tariff | Higher per-unit rate | Lower per-unit rate |
| Maintenance Responsibility | Utility | Consumer (with KSEB supervision) |
| Approval Authority | Assistant Engineer | Chief Electrical Inspector (CEI) |
| Safety Compliance | CEA Reg. (basic) | CEA Reg. 2010 (full) |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Complete HT Connection Procedure — Workflow Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Consumer Identifies HT Load Requirement] --> B[Stage 1: Pre-Application Feasibility Study]
    B --> B1[Estimate Maximum Demand in kVA]
    B --> B2[Identify Nearby 11kV / 22kV Feeder]
    B --> B3[Decide Substation Type Indoor or Outdoor]
    B1 --> C[Stage 2: Submit Application Form A to KSEB]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> C1[Attach Site Plan, SLD, Load List, Documents]
    C1 --> D[Stage 3: KSEB Technical Feasibility Study]
    D --> D1[Check Feeder Capacity]
    D --> D2[Issue Letter of Sanction with MD and Voltage]
    D1 --> E[Stage 4: Pay Security Deposit and Development Charges]
    D2 --> E
    E --> E1[Sign HT Supply Agreement with KSEB]
    E1 --> F[Stage 5: Consumer Installs HT Equipment]
    F --> F1[HT Switchgear VCB or SF6]
    F --> F2[Step-down Transformer 11kV to 433V]
    F --> F3[HT Cable, CT PT Metering, Earthing, LA]
    F1 --> G[Stage 6: Inspection by Chief Electrical Inspector]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> G1{Inspection Satisfactory?}
    G1 -- No --> F[Rectify Defects and Resubmit]
    G1 -- Yes --> H[Stage 7: Meter Sealing by KSEB]
    H --> H1[KSEB Issues Energisation Order]
    H1 --> I[End: HT Supply Commenced Service Certificate Issued]
```

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Typical HT Consumer Installation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    KSEBfeeder[11 kV KSEB Feeder] --> HTmeter[HT Metering Cubicle CT PT and TOD Meter]
    HTmeter --> VCB[HT VCB Vacuum Circuit Breaker]
    VCB --> Trans[Step-down Power Transformer 11kV to 433V]
    Trans --> LTpanel[Main LT Panel 433V]
    LTpanel --> Bus1[Distribution Bus A Industrial Loads]
    LTpanel --> Bus2[Distribution Bus B Commercial Loads]
    LTpanel --> Bus3[Distribution Bus C HVAC and Lighting]
    Trans -. Earth .-> Earth[Earth Pit System Pipe and Plate as per IS 3043]
    VCB -. Protection .-> Relay[Protective Relays OCEF Buchholz Differential]
    Trans -. Surge .-> LA[Lightning Arresters]
    Bus1 --> Motor1[3-Phase Induction Motors]
    Bus2 --> Office[Office and Commercial Loads]
    Bus3 --> HVAC[Central HVAC Chillers]
```

---

### 4.3 Single-Line Diagram (Conceptual) — HT Consumer Substation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Source[11 kV Source from KSEB] --> Isolator1[Isolator 11 kV]
    Isolator1 --> CTmeter[CT for Metering]
    CTmeter --> VCBmain[VCB 11 kV Main Breaker]
    VCBmain --> LA1[Lightning Arrester]
    VCBmain --> Cable[HT Cable XLPE to Transformer]
    Cable --> Trans11to433[Power Transformer 11 kV / 433 V]
    Trans11to433 -.Buchholz.--> Buch[Buchholz Relay]
    Trans11to433 -.Earth.--> Earth2[Earth Mat]
    Trans11to433 --> ACB[LT Air Circuit Breaker 433 V Main]
    ACB --> LTbus[LT Distribution Bus 433 V]
    LTbus --> OutgoingFeeders[Outgoing Feeders to Loads]
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

> Define an **HT connection** as per KSEB / CEA regulations. State the **minimum demand threshold** at which an HT connection becomes mandatory.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> An **HT (High Tension) connection** is a high-voltage electricity supply connection provided at voltages **11 kV and above**, where the consumer owns, installs, and maintains the step-down transformer, HT switchgear, and associated protective equipment on the consumer premises, as per the **CEA (Measures relating to Safety and Electric Supply) Regulations, 2010** and the **Electricity Act, 2003**.
>
> As per KSEB regulations, a consumer with a **contracted maximum demand of 100 kVA or more** is generally required to apply for an HT connection. For loads below this threshold, an LT connection (230 V / 415 V) is provided, with the distribution transformer owned and maintained by the utility.
>
> *(1 mark each for: definition, voltage level, threshold value.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

> List **any six essential documents** required to be submitted along with the HT connection application form (Form A) to KSEB.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The following six documents are mandatory:

1. **Identity proof** of the applicant (Aadhaar / PAN / Company registration).
2. **Land / building ownership documents** (sale deed / lease agreement).
3. **Site plan** showing proposed substation location and approach road.
4. **Single-Line Diagram (SLD)** of the proposed HT installation.
5. **Load list** with connected load in kW and proposed maximum demand in kVA.
6. **Statutory approvals** — KSPCB consent (for industries), factory license, building permit, and fire department NOC (if applicable).

*(½ mark each, 6 × ½ = 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**Q.A.** An industrial unit plans to set up an HT connection. The connected loads are as follows:

| Load Description | Quantity | Unit Rating |
|------------------|----------|-------------|
| 3-Phase Induction Motors | 6 | 90 kW each |
| Lighting Load | – | 75 kW total |
| HVAC (Centralized) | 3 chillers | 50 kW each |
| Welding Sets (intermittent) | 4 | 30 kVA each |
| Office Equipment | – | 25 kW total |

Given:
- Demand Factor = 0.80
- Power Factor = 0.88 (lag)
- System Efficiency = 0.90
- Diversity Factor = 1.20

**Tasks:**
**(a)** Calculate the **Maximum Demand in kVA** to be applied for. (7 marks)
**(b)** Select a **standard transformer rating**, justify the selection, and compute the **HT full-load current** at 11 kV. Also, explain the **complete procedure** (in 6 stages) for obtaining HT connection approval from KSEB. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**Part (a) — Maximum Demand Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Total Connected Load

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{motors} &= 6 \times 90 = 540 \text{ kW} \\
P_{lighting} &= 75 \text{ kW} \\
P_{HVAC} &= 3 \times 50 = 150 \text{ kW} \\
P_{welding} &= 4 \times 30 = 120 \text{ kW (assuming PF=1)} \\
P_{office} &= 25 \text{ kW} \\
P_{connected} &= 540 + 75 + 150 + 120 + 25 = 910 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *(1 mark for computing each sub-total correctly.)*

**Step 2:** Apply Demand Factor

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{max \, demand} &= P_{connected} \times DF \\
P_{max \, demand} &= 910 \times 0.80 = 728 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *(1 mark)*

**Step 3:** Convert to kVA

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{MD} &= \dfrac{P_{max \, demand}}{PF \times \eta} \\
S_{MD} &= \dfrac{728}{0.88 \times 0.90} = \dfrac{728}{0.792} \\
S_{MD} &= 919.19 \text{ kVA}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *(2 marks)*

**Step 4:** Apply Diversity Factor

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{MD,\,sanctioned} &= 919.19 \times 1.20 = 1103.03 \text{ kVA}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final MD to be applied:** ≈ **1103 kVA**

> *(1 mark for concluding answer with units.)*

---

**Part (b) — Transformer Selection + HT Current + Procedure (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Apply 25% margin for transformer rating

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{transformer} &\geq 1.25 \times 1103 \\
S_{transformer} &\geq 1379 \text{ kVA}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Selected Standard Rating:** **1500 kVA, 11 kV / 433 V, 3-phase Dyn11 transformer.**

> **Justification:** Standard KSEB-approved ratings are 500, 750, 1000, **1500**, 2000 kVA. The next rating above 1379 kVA is 1500 kVA, which also provides headroom for future expansion. Connection: **Delta (primary) – Star (secondary)** with neutral brought out for LT distribution. *(2 marks)*

**Step 2:** Compute HT Full-Load Current at 11 kV

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{HT} &= \dfrac{S_{transformer}}{\sqrt{3} \times V_{HT}} \\
I_{HT} &= \dfrac{1500 \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \times 11000} \\
I_{HT} &= \dfrac{1{,}500{,}000}{19052.56} \\
I_{HT} &\approx 78.7 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *(2 marks)*

**Step 3 — 6 Stages of HT Connection Procedure:** *(3 marks — distribute ½ mark per stage)*

1. **Feasibility Study** by consumer — estimate MD, locate feeder.
2. **Application submission** to KSEB with Form A and documents.
3. **Load sanction** — KSEB inspects, issues Letter of Sanction with MD and voltage.
4. **Payment and Agreement** — security deposit, development charges, sign HT supply agreement.
5. **Installation by consumer** — transformer, HT switchgear, metering, earthing, LA.
6. **Inspection by CEI** — issue energisation sanction, meter sealing, HT supply commences.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model — Alternative Choice]**

**Q.B.** **(a)** With the help of a neat flowchart, explain the **step-by-step procedure for obtaining HT connection approval from KSEB** in Kerala. Mention the role of the **Chief Electrical Inspector (CEI)** in this process. (7 marks)

**(b)** An HT consumer has a **contracted demand of 2000 kVA** at **11 kV supply**. The **per-unit impedance ($Z_{pu}$)** of the upstream system and transformer combined is **0.08 p.u.** Compute the **short-circuit kVA** at the consumer bus and the **prospective short-circuit current**. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**Part (a) — Flowchart + CEI Role (7 marks):**

A complete flowchart with **7 sequential stages** must be drawn — refer to the diagram in **Section 4.1** of these notes. *(5 marks for flowchart)*

**Role of the Chief Electrical Inspector (CEI):** *(2 marks)*

> The **Chief Electrical Inspector of Kerala** is the statutory authority appointed under the **Electricity Act, 2003** and **CEA Regulations, 2010**. The CEI:
> - Conducts mandatory inspection of the HT installation **before first energisation**.
> - Verifies **safety clearances**, earthing resistance (≤ 1 Ω for HT), protective device coordination, fire-fighting provisions, and oil-soak pit (for oil-filled transformers).
> - Issues the **Sanction for Energisation** certificate under **Regulation 32 of CEA Regulations, 2010**.
> - Has the power to **disconnect** any non-compliant installation even after energisation.

---

**Part (b) — Short Circuit Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Base kVA ($S_{base}$) = 2000 kVA = 2 MVA
- Per-unit impedance ($Z_{pu}$) = 0.08

**Step 1 — Short-circuit kVA at the consumer bus:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{sc} &= \dfrac{S_{base}}{Z_{pu}} \\
S_{sc} &= \dfrac{2000}{0.08} \\
S_{sc} &= 25{,}000 \text{ kVA} = 25 \text{ MVA}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *(3 marks)*

**Step 2 — Prospective short-circuit current:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{sc} &= \dfrac{S_{sc}}{\sqrt{3} \times V_{HT}} \\
I_{sc} &= \dfrac{25{,}000 \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \times 11{,}000} \\
I_{sc} &= \dfrac{25{,}000{,}000}{19052.56} \\
I_{sc} &\approx 1312.2 \text{ A} \approx 1.31 \text{ kA}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *(3 marks)*

**Step 3 — Interpretation:** *(1 mark)*

> The HT switchgear (VCB) must have a **breaking capacity ≥ 1.31 kA**, and the busbars must be rated for **at least 25 MVA short-time withstand**. Practical breaker ratings are selected as **25 kA** to provide a **20× safety margin**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Efficiency ($\eta$) term** in the Maximum Demand formula — KTU examiners specifically test $S_{MD} = P / (PF \times \eta)$. Missing $\eta$ costs **2 full marks**.
> 2. **Confusing Demand Factor and Diversity Factor.** Demand factor < 1; Diversity factor > 1. Many students apply them in the wrong direction.
> 3. **Forgetting to state the Standard Transformer rating** — examiners expect the next-higher standard value (e.g., 1500 kVA, not 1379 kVA).
> 4. **Omitting the role of CEI** in HT approval — this is a **recurring KTU question** and missing it costs 2 marks.
> 5. **Wrong unit in HT current** — answer must be in **Amperes (A)**, not kA or mA.
> 6. **Skipping the flowchart in procedure questions** — a labelled flowchart is **mandatory** for full marks in 7-mark procedure questions.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for HT Connection:**

- **Definition:** HT connection = supply at **≥ 11 kV** with consumer-owned transformer.
- **Threshold:** **≥ 100 kVA** contracted demand → HT mandatory (KSEB).
- **Application Form:** **Form A** submitted to KSEB Section Office.
- **Statutory Authority:** **CEA Regulations, 2010** + **Electricity Act, 2003** + **KSERC Tariff Order**.
- **Inspection:** **Chief Electrical Inspector (CEI)** issues energisation sanction.
- **Formula — Maximum Demand:** $S_{MD} = \dfrac{P_{connected} \times DF \times DF_d}{PF \times \eta}$
- **Formula — Transformer Sizing:** $S_{TX} \geq 1.25 \times MD$
- **Formula — HT Full-Load Current:** $I_{HT} = \dfrac{kVA}{\sqrt{3} \times V_{HT}}$ in Amperes
- **Formula — Short-circuit kVA:** $S_{sc} = S_{base} / Z_{pu}$
- **Formula — HT Short-circuit Current:** $I_{sc} = \dfrac{S_{sc} \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \times V_{HT}}$ in Amperes
- **7 Stages of Procedure:** Feasibility → Application → Sanction → Payment → Installation → Inspection → Energisation.
- **Key Equipment:** VCB / SF6 Breaker, Transformer (Dyn11), HT Cable (XLPE), CT-PT Metering, LA, Earthing (≤ 1 Ω), Buchholz Relay.
- **Tariff Benefit:** HT consumers pay **lower ₹/kWh** than LT.
- **Documentation:** Site plan, SLD, Load list, Ownership proof, KSPCB consent, Building permit, ID proof.
- **Exam Tip:** Always state **units**, **standard transformer ratings**, and **draw a flowchart** for procedure questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design and Estimation of Quantity of Material Required for Substations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT752 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design and Estimation of Quantity of Material Required for Substations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **substation** is an assembly of apparatus installed at a selected point in an electrical power system, comprising switching, controlling, transforming, and protection equipment, along with associated civil and structural works, whose primary function is the **receipt, transformation, switching, and dispatch of electrical energy** at specified voltage levels while maintaining power quality, reliability, and operational safety.

**Material estimation** in substation engineering is the **systematic quantitative assessment** of all electrical, mechanical, and civil engineering materials (measured in metres, kilograms, cubic metres, or numbers) required to complete the substation as per the approved single-line diagram, layout drawing, and technical specifications, in compliance with **IS 802 (Part I to XI), IS 3043, IS 5613, IS 1180, IEC 60076, and CEA (Measures Relating to Safety and Electric Supply) Regulations, 2010**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Substation material estimation is a deterministic, drawing-based engineering exercise — every quantity must be **traceable back to a drawing dimension, a schedule entry, or a rated parameter of equipment**. Estimation is the financial and engineering backbone of any 11 kV / 33 kV / 110 kV / 220 kV substation project.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a substation as the **"kitchen of a high-rise building"**:

- The **incoming HT line** is the raw water/gas pipeline coming from the municipal supply.
- The **transformer** is the pressure regulator / cooking stove that converts the input to a usable form.
- The **bus bars** are the main distribution pipes running across the kitchen ceiling.
- The **circuit breakers** are the safety valves and isolation taps.
- The **CTs and PTs** are the meters and gauges measuring flow and pressure.
- The **earthing system** is the safety drainage system.
- The **control room and relay panels** are the chef's dashboard.
- The **fencing, gravel, and civil works** are the building's walls, flooring, and foundation.

Just as a contractor must prepare a **Bill of Quantities (BoQ)** before building a kitchen, the substation design engineer must prepare a **Material Take-Off (MTO) sheet** before procurement. If you miss a gas valve in your kitchen estimate, the project halts mid-construction. Similarly, missing a single **post insulator** or **earthing rod** can stall substation commissioning.

## 1.3 Classification of Substations (Relevant to Material Estimation)

| Type | Voltage Range | Typical Application | Material Dominance |
|------|--------------|---------------------|--------------------|
| **Pole-mounted (PMS)** | Up to 11 kV | Rural distribution | Steel pole, platform, HT/LT cables |
| **Indoor (GIS-ready)** | 11 kV / 33 kV | Urban, space-constrained | SF₆ breakers, bus duct, panel boards |
| **Outdoor (AIS)** | 33 kV / 110 kV / 220 kV / 400 kV | EHT substations | Steel structures, ACSR conductors, long creepage insulators |
| **Hybrid (HGIS / MTS)** | 66 kV and above | Coastal Kerala sites | Mix of GIS bays + outdoor transformer bays |

> [!NOTE]
> Kerala's KSEB standard practice is predominantly **outdoor Air-Insulated Substation (AIS)** layout up to 220 kV, with **GIS** being adopted in space-constrained city substations (e.g., Thiruvananthapuram, Ernakulam).

## 1.4 Components Whose Material Quantities Must Be Estimated

The engineer must itemise quantities for the following **eight major heads**:

1. **Civil & structural works** — foundations, control room, cable trench, fencing, gravel, roads.
2. **Steel structures** — gantries, equipment supports, beam columns.
3. **Bus bar system and conductors** — tubular/ACSR, jumpers, droppers.
4. **Insulators** — post, disc, pin, long-rod composite.
5. **Power transformer** (as a complete unit, with oil, radiators, conservator, Buchholz).
6. **Switchgear** — circuit breakers, isolators, earth switches, CTs, PTs, LAs.
7. **Earthing system** — earth pits, earth strip, MS rod, Backfill Compound (BFC).
8. **Cabling, control panels, batteries, and auxiliary supplies** — control cables, battery bank, battery charger, ACDB/DCDB, fire-fighting, lighting.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic of Substation Material Estimation

The estimation process follows a **top-down, drawing-driven workflow**:

1. **Receive the approved single-line diagram (SLD)** — this fixes the **number of bays** (incoming, outgoing, bus coupler, transformer bays).
2. **Develop the general layout (plan + elevation)** — fixes **span, height, and bay width** between gantries.
3. **Identify the bill of equipment (BoE)** — each equipment item carries a **rated current, dynamic short-circuit current, creepage, BIL, and weight** from the manufacturer.
4. **Compute the electrical quantities** using the formulas below.
5. **Compute the civil quantities** from the layout drawing (concrete volume, steel tonnage, fencing length).
6. **Apply wastage / wastage factors** (typically **2.5 % to 5 %** for conductors, **5 % to 7.5 %** for cables, **3 % to 5 %** for steel structures).
7. **Prepare the Abstract Estimate / BoQ** — the document submitted to KSEB / utility for sanction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why estimation accuracy matters:**
> - **Under-estimation** → procurement shortage → commissioning delay.
> - **Over-estimation** → blocked capital, idle inventory, audit objections from the Kerala State Electricity Regulatory Commission.
> KTU 2024 stresses that estimation must be **defensible, drawing-traceable, and code-compliant**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> **All equations follow KTU standard exam convention. Use `\vert` for absolute value to avoid breaking markdown tables.**

| # | Quantity to Estimate | Governing Formula | Units | Code Reference |
|---|----------------------|-------------------|-------|----------------|
| 1 | **Full-load current of transformer** | $I_{FL} \;=\; \dfrac{S}{\sqrt{3} \times V}$ | A | IS 1180 |
| 2 | **Cross-section of current-carrying conductor (by current density)** | $A \;=\; \dfrac{I}{\delta}$ | mm² | IS 802-I |
| 3 | **Minimum conductor area (by short-circuit withstanding)** | $A_{min} \;=\; \dfrac{I_{sc} \times \sqrt{t}}{K}$ | mm² | IS 802-I |
| 4 | **Short-circuit current (Ohmic/Impedance method)** | $I_{sc} \;=\; \dfrac{kVA_{base}}{\sqrt{3} \times V \times Z_{pu}}$ | kA | IEC 60909 |
| 5 | **Specific weight of bus bar** | $W_{bus} \;=\; n \times L \times A \times \rho_{material}$ | kg | — |
| 6 | **Concrete volume of foundation** | $V_c \;=\; L \times B \times D$ | m³ | IS 456 |
| 7 | **Steel reinforcement weight** | $W_s \;=\; 0.00785 \times d^2 \times L$ per bar | kg | IS 2502 |
| 8 | **Number of disc insulators in a string** | $n \;=\; \dfrac{V_{LL}}{V_{string}}$ | nos. | IS 731 |
| 9 | **Specific creepage distance** | $SCD \;=\; \dfrac{creepage\;mm}{V_{LL}}$ | mm/kV | IS 13134 |
| 10 | **Earth resistance (rod electrode)** | $R \;=\; \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\left(\dfrac{8L}{d}\right)-1\right]$ | Ω | IS 3043 |
| 11 | **Number of earth pits required** | $n_{pits} \;=\; \dfrac{R_{single}}{R_{required}}$ | nos. | IS 3043 |
| 12 | **Length of earth strip** | $L_{strip} \;=\; 2 \times (L \times B) + perimeter$ | m | IS 3043 |
| 13 | **Cable length per run** | $L_{cable} \;=\; 1.10 \times (route\;length) + sag + termination$ | m | — |
| 14 | **Galloping / sag allowance** | $sag \;\approx\; \dfrac{w \times l^2}{8T}$ | m | IS 802-II |
| 15 | **Wind pressure on structures** | $P \;=\; 0.6 \times V_w^2$ | kg/m² | IS 802-I |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard parameters used in KTU problems:**
> - Current density $\delta$ for copper bus = **1.6 A/mm²** (normal), **2.0 A/mm²** (emergency).
> - Current density $\delta$ for aluminium bus = **1.0 A/mm²** (normal), **1.3 A/mm²** (emergency).
> - Density of copper $\rho$ = **8.89 g/cm³** = **8960 kg/m³**.
> - Density of aluminium $\rho$ = **2.71 g/cm³** = **2710 kg/m³**.
> - Specific earth resistance $\rho$ for laterite soil of Kerala $\approx$ **100–300 Ω·m** (use **200 Ω·m** as default).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Material estimation is the **gateway document** for:
- **Tender invitations** by KSEB / Kerala State Electricity Board Limited.
- **Capital budgeting** under DPR (Detailed Project Report) submission to KTU-EMP / NITI Aayog.
- **Inventory planning** for islanded Kerala substations in Idukki / Wayanad where logistics add 8–12 % to material cost.
- **Disaster-resilient design** — additional 12–15 % material provisioning is now mandatory for cyclonic coastal districts (Alappuzha, Kollam) per KERI 2023 guidelines.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example (KTU 2024 Scheme Style):**
> *Design a 33/11 kV, 5 MVA outdoor substation and estimate the quantity of: (i) ACSR Panther conductor for incoming 33 kV side, (ii) 11 kV disc insulator string, (iii) quantity of concrete for transformer foundation, (iv) number of earth pits, and (v) total length of 50×6 mm GI earth strip.*

---

## 3.1 Step 1 — Compute Full-Load Current on the 33 kV Side

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{FL,\;33\,kV} &= \frac{S}{\sqrt{3} \times V} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{5 \times 10^6}{\sqrt{3} \times 33 \times 10^3} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{5 \times 10^6}{1.732 \times 33 \times 10^3} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{5 \times 10^6}{57.156 \times 10^3} \\[4pt]
&\approx 87.5 \;\; \text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** A 5 MVA transformer at 33 kV draws only ~87.5 A, but the line must be **rated for short-circuit current**, not full-load current. This is a common KTU trap.

---

## 3.2 Step 2 — Size the ACSR Panther Conductor (Cross-Section Verification)

**ACSR Panther rating:** Current capacity ≈ **150 A** (at 45 °C ambient with 0.6 m/s wind, per IS 802-I).
Cross-section = **21.0 mm² Al + 7/3.00 mm steel** = total **31.5 mm²** equivalent copper area.

**Required area by current density (Al, δ = 1.0 A/mm²):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{req} &= \frac{I_{FL}}{\delta} = \frac{87.5}{1.0} = 87.5 \;\; \text{mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Required area by short-circuit (assume $I_{sc} = 10$ kA, $t = 1$ s, $K = 75$ for Al):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{min,\,sc} &= \frac{I_{sc} \times \sqrt{t}}{K} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{10 \times 10^3 \times \sqrt{1}}{75} \\[4pt]
&\approx 133.3 \;\; \text{mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Since the short-circuit criterion ($A_{min,sc} = 133.3$ mm²) governs over the current-density criterion ($A_{req} = 87.5$ mm²), the design must upgrade to a **larger conductor**, typically **ACSR Zebra (54/3.18 mm Al + 7/3.18 mm St, A = 428 mm² Al, rating 245 A)** which is the KSEB standard for 33 kV incoming lines.

**Final conductor selected:** **ACSR Zebra**, 1 run per phase, 3 phases = 3 conductors.

**Length calculation** (assume substation span = 30 m, gantry-to-gantry):

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{per\;phase} &= 1.05 \times (span + sag + termination) \\[4pt]
&= 1.05 \times (30 + 1.2 + 1.5) \\[4pt]
&\approx 33.7 \;\; \text{m/phase}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{total} &= 3 \times L_{per\;phase} \approx 101.1 \;\; \text{m} \;\; \rightarrow \;\; \textbf{round to 110 m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> The 1.05 multiplier is the **wastage + jumper + sag factor** mandated in KSEB estimates.

---

## 3.3 Step 3 — Number of Disc Insulators for 33 kV String

**Governing choice:** 33 kV side operates at 36 kV max (with 5 % over-voltage), so a **single suspension string of 3 discs (90 kN electromechanical rating)** is the KSEB standard.

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{discs} &= \frac{V_{LL}}{V_{per\;disc}} = \frac{33}{11} \approx 3 \;\; \text{discs}
\end{aligned}
$$

Each string has **3 disc insulators**, plus **2 corona control rings**, plus **2 arcing horns** and **suspension hardware**.

**Number of strings** (3 incoming gantries × 3 phases + 1 transformer dead-end × 3 phases + bus coupler × 3) ≈ **18 strings** → Total discs = 18 × 3 = **54 nos.**

---

## 3.4 Step 4 — Concrete Volume of Power Transformer Foundation

Assume transformer dimensions **3.5 m × 2.5 m** (footprint) with **plinth height 0.5 m** and **depth 1.5 m** below GL (with 0.3 m PCC below RCC):

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= 4.0 \;\; \text{m (1.5 m beyond X-mer on each side for oil soak pit)} \\
B &= 3.0 \;\; \text{m (1.5 m + 1.0 m + 0.5 m)} \\
D &= 1.8 \;\; \text{m (0.3 m PCC + 1.5 m RCC)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{concrete} &= L \times B \times D = 4.0 \times 3.0 \times 1.8 = 21.6 \;\; \text{m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Steel reinforcement (assume 80 kg/m³ of concrete for foundations):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{steel} &= 80 \times 21.6 = 1728 \;\; \text{kg} \approx 1.73 \;\; \text{tonnes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Add rail track, soak pit, fire wall quantities** (oil containment wall of 0.3 m thick × 1.0 m high running around the plinth):

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{wall} &= 2 \times (4.0 + 3.0) = 14 \;\; \text{m} \\
V_{wall} &= 14 \times 0.3 \times 1.0 = 4.2 \;\; \text{m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total concrete for transformer plinth + fire wall = 21.6 + 4.2 = 25.8 m³**, plus 100 mm gravel layer over 4 × 3 = 12 m² × 0.1 m = **1.2 m³ gravel**.

---

## 3.5 Step 5 — Number of Earth Pits (IS 3043 Method)

**Required earth resistance** for HV substation: **≤ 0.5 Ω** (combined mesh value).

**Single-pit resistance** (rod 3 m long, 50 mm dia, soil $\rho$ = 200 Ω·m):

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{single} &= \frac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\left(\frac{8L}{d}\right) - 1\right] \\[4pt]
&= \frac{200}{2\pi \times 3}\left[\ln\left(\frac{8 \times 3}{0.05}\right) - 1\right] \\[4pt]
&= \frac{200}{18.85}\left[\ln(480) - 1\right] \\[4pt]
&= 10.61 \times [6.173 - 1] \\[4pt]
&= 10.61 \times 5.173 \\[4pt]
&\approx 54.9 \;\; \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**Number of pits (in parallel, with spacing ≥ 6 m to avoid overlap):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{pits} &= \frac{R_{single}}{R_{required}} = \frac{54.9}{0.5} = 109.8 \;\; \rightarrow \;\; \textbf{practically 25–40 pits in a mesh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Direct division over-estimates; in practice, a **grid of 30 pits** connected by 50×6 mm GI earth strip (forming a mesh) achieves ≤ 0.5 Ω — this is the KSEB-standard.

---

## 3.6 Step 6 — Total Length of 50×6 mm GI Earth Strip

**Main earth grid** (assumed 60 m × 50 m substation area):

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{main\;grid} &= 2 \times (60 + 50) = 220 \;\; \text{m} \;\; \text{(spaced 5 m apart, 12 lines each way)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — for a 5 m × 5 m mesh: 60/5 = 12 lines × 50 m each = 600 m, plus 12 × 60 m = 720 m, total = **1320 m**.

**Connections to equipment** (X-mer neutral, LA base, GB base, PT neutral, panel earth) ≈ **40 drops × 5 m each = 200 m**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{total\,strip} &= 1320 + 200 = 1520 \;\; \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Add 7.5 % wastage:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{final} &= 1.075 \times 1520 \approx 1634 \;\; \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final ordered quantity: ~1650 m of 50×6 mm GI earth strip + 30 nos. earth pits with 50 mm dia, 3 m long GI rod + 30 × 25 kg Back Fill Compound (BFC) bags.**

---

## 3.7 Step 7 — Master Material Take-Off (MTO) Summary Table

| Sl. | Material Description | Unit | Qty (Estimated) |
|-----|----------------------|------|-----------------|
| 1 | ACSR Zebra conductor | m | 110 |
| 2 | 90 kN disc insulator | nos. | 54 |
| 3 | Suspension hardware | sets | 18 |
| 4 | M30 concrete for X-mer foundation | m³ | 25.8 |
| 5 | Gravel (40 mm, oil-soak pit) | m³ | 1.2 |
| 6 | 50×6 mm GI earth strip | m | 1650 |
| 7 | 50 mm dia × 3 m GI earth rod | nos. | 30 |
| 8 | Back Fill Compound (BFC) | bags | 30 |
| 9 | LT/HT control cable (2.5 mm², 4C) | m | 600 |
| 10 | 110 V DC battery (200 Ah VRLA) | set | 1 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Substation Material Estimation Workflow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Receive Approved SLD and Layout Drawing] --> B0[Identify Number of Bays and Equipment List]
    B0 --> C0[Compute Electrical Quantities using IS 802 and IEC 60909]
    C0 --> D0[Compute Conductor Bus and Insulator Quantities]
    D0 --> E0[Compute Civil Quantities Concrete Steel and Fencing]
    E0 --> F0[Compute Earthing System Quantities per IS 3043]
    F0 --> G0[Compute Cabling and Aux Supply Quantities]
    G0 --> H0[Apply Wastage Factors 5 to 7 percent]
    H0 --> I0[Prepare MTO Sheet and Abstract Estimate]
    I0 --> J0[Submit to KSEB for Technical Sanction]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram of a Typical 33/11 kV Substation Showing Equipment to be Estimated

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INCOMING[33 kV INCOMING LINE BAY]
        A1[33 kV Line A] --> A2[Tension String Disc Insulators]
        A2 --> A3[Isolator with Earth Switch]
        A3 --> A4[33 kV CT for Metering and Protection]
        A4 --> A5[Vacuum Circuit Breaker 630 A 25 kA]
    end

    subgraph BUS[MAIN BUS 33 kV]
        A5 --> BUS1[ACSR Zebra Tubular Bus 1 Run per Phase]
        BUS1 --> BUS2[Bus PT for Voltage Metering]
    end

    subgraph XMER[POWER TRANSFORMER BAY]
        BUS1 --> T1[33 11 kV 5 MVA Power Transformer]
        T1 --> T2[Neutral CT and LA on LV Side]
    end

    subgraph OUTGO[11 kV OUTGOING BAY]
        T1 --> O1[11 kV Outgoing Feeder 1]
        T1 --> O2[11 kV Outgoing Feeder 2]
        T1 --> O3[11 kV Outgoing Feeder 3]
    end

    subgraph EARTHEARTH[EARTHING AND PROTECTION]
        G1[GI Earth Strip 50x6 mm Mesh] -.-> A3
        G1 -.-> A4
        G1 -.-> T1
        G1 -.-> O1
    end

    subgraph CONTROL[CONTROL ROOM]
        C1[Relay and Control Panel] --- A5
        C1 --- O1
        C2[Battery Bank 110 V DC] --- C1
        C3[ACDB and DCDB Panel] --- C2
    end
```

## 4.3 Material Estimation Process Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[Substation Material Estimation]

    ROOT --> CAT1[Category 1 Electrical]
    ROOT --> CAT2[Category 2 Civil and Structural]
    ROOT --> CAT3[Category 3 Earthing and Safety]
    ROOT --> CAT4[Category 4 Auxiliary and Control]

    CAT1 --> E1[1 Power Transformer]
    CAT1 --> E2[2 Circuit Breakers]
    CAT1 --> E3[3 Isolators and Earth Switches]
    CAT1 --> E4[4 Current and Potential Transformers]
    CAT1 --> E5[5 Lightning Arresters]
    CAT1 --> E6[6 Conductors and Bus Bars]
    CAT1 --> E7[7 Insulators]
    CAT1 --> E8[8 Control and Power Cables]

    CAT2 --> C1[1 Transformer Foundation]
    CAT2 --> C2[2 Equipment Plinths]
    CAT2 --> C3[3 Cable Trenches]
    CAT2 --> C4[4 Control Room Building]
    CAT2 --> C5[5 Fencing and Gates]
    CAT2 --> C6[6 Gravel Spreading and Roads]
    CAT2 --> C7[7 Steel Gantry Structures]

    CAT3 --> G1[1 Earth Pits and Rods]
    CAT3 --> G2[2 Earth Strip and Connectors]
    CAT3 --> G3[3 BFC and Charcoal]
    CAT3 --> G4[4 Lightening Protection Mast]

    CAT4 --> A1[1 Battery and Charger]
    CAT4 --> A2[2 Relay Panels and SCADA]
    CAT4 --> A3[3 Fire Fighting Equipment]
    CAT4 --> A4[4 Illumination and Receptacles]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]
**List any six quantities of civil engineering materials required for the construction of a 110 kV substation.**

**Model Answer:**
The major civil engineering material quantities to be estimated for a 110 kV substation are:
1. **M25/M30 grade concrete** (in m³) — for transformer plinth, equipment foundations, control room, cable trench, fire wall, fencing post foundations.
2. **Reinforcement steel (Fe 415 / Fe 500)** (in kg/tonnes) — typically 60–80 kg per m³ of concrete.
3. **Brick masonry / fly-ash brickwork** (in m³) — for control room walls.
4. **RCC and stone aggregate** (in m³) — for road works and sub-station yard.
5. **50 mm–75 mm size gravel** (in m³) — spread 100 mm thick over the entire yard area for fire safety and oil spillage.
6. **Chain-link / barbed-wire fencing with MS angle posts** (in running metres) — for substation boundary, typically 1.8 m height with 0.5 m concertina coil.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]
**Define "current density" and state the standard values used for sizing copper and aluminium bus bars in substations.**

**Model Answer:**
**Current density** is defined as the amount of current flowing per unit cross-sectional area of a conductor, expressed in A/mm². It governs the cross-section of bus bars and jumpers.

$$
\delta = \frac{I}{A}
$$

**Standard values (per IS 802 Part I):**

| Material | Normal Operation | Emergency Operation |
|----------|------------------|---------------------|
| Copper   | 1.6 A/mm²        | 2.0 A/mm²           |
| Aluminium| 1.0 A/mm²        | 1.3 A/mm²           |

The actual cross-section required is the **maximum** of the two criteria — **current density criterion** and **short-circuit withstanding criterion**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Design the earthing system for a 33/11 kV, 5 MVA substation to be constructed at Ernakulam, where the soil resistivity is 250 Ω·m. Compute the (a) number of earth pits and (b) total length of 50×6 mm GI strip required, with full derivation. (7+7 = 14 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Number of Earth Pits (7 marks)

**Given:** $\rho$ = 250 Ω·m, $L$ = 3 m (rod length), $d$ = 50 mm = 0.05 m, Required $R$ ≤ 0.5 Ω.

**Step 1:** Compute single-pit resistance using the standard rod formula (per IS 3043):

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{single} &= \frac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\left(\frac{8L}{d}\right) - 1\right] \\[4pt]
&= \frac{250}{2\pi \times 3}\left[\ln\left(\frac{8 \times 3}{0.05}\right) - 1\right] \\[4pt]
&= \frac{250}{18.85}\left[\ln(480) - 1\right] \\[4pt]
&= 13.26 \times [6.173 - 1] \\[4pt]
&= 13.26 \times 5.173 \\[4pt]
&\approx 68.6 \;\; \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply reduction factor for parallel pits in a mesh. In practice, 30 pits connected by a strip mesh achieve the required 0.5 Ω in Kerala's laterite soil.

**Answer:** **30 nos. of 50 mm dia × 3 m GI rod earth pits** with BFC backfill and connected by 50×6 mm GI strip in a 5 m × 5 m mesh.

#### Part (b) — Total Length of Earth Strip (7 marks)

**Substation area:** Assume 50 m × 40 m = 2000 m².

**Step 1:** Mesh formation at 5 m × 5 m:
- Lines along length (50 m direction): 40/5 + 1 = 9 lines
- Lines along width (40 m direction): 50/5 + 1 = 11 lines
- Total mesh length = 9 × 50 + 11 × 40 = 450 + 440 = **890 m**

**Step 2:** Equipment drops (transformer neutral, LA, body earth, CT/PT neutral, panel earth — 40 drops × 6 m each):

$$
L_{drops} = 40 \times 6 = 240 \;\; \text{m}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply 5 % wastage:

$$
L_{total} = 1.05 \times (890 + 240) = 1.05 \times 1130 = 1186.5 \;\; \text{m}
$$

**Answer:** **~1200 m of 50×6 mm GI earth strip** + **30 nos. of 50 mm × 3 m GI earth rods** + **30 bags of 25 kg BFC**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Estimate the quantity of: (a) concrete (M30 grade) and (b) ACSR Zebra conductor required for a 33/11 kV, 5 MVA outdoor substation. Assume: 1 power transformer foundation, 4 outgoing feeder foundations, 1 bus coupler foundation, span = 30 m, bay width = 8 m. (7+7 = 14 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Concrete Quantity (7 marks)

**Foundation dimensions per IS 4091 (transformer):** 4.0 m × 3.0 m × 1.8 m (including PCC).
**Equipment foundation standard (breaker/isolator/LA):** 1.5 m × 1.0 m × 1.5 m deep.
**Control room:** 8 m × 5 m × 0.4 m thick slab + 0.3 m × 0.3 m × 8 m plinth beam (4 sides).

**Step 1:** Transformer foundation

$$
V_1 = 4.0 \times 3.0 \times 1.8 = 21.6 \;\; \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 2:** Equipment foundations (4 outgoing + 1 bus coupler + 1 incoming = 6 nos., each 1.5×1.0×1.5 m):

$$
V_2 = 6 \times 1.5 \times 1.0 \times 1.5 = 13.5 \;\; \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 3:** Control room slab + plinth beams:

$$
V_3 = (8 \times 5 \times 0.4) + (4 \times 8 \times 0.3 \times 0.3) = 16.0 + 2.88 = 18.88 \;\; \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 4:** Cable trench (1.0 m wide × 0.8 m deep, length = 4 × 30 m):

$$
V_4 = 4 \times 30 \times 0.8 = 96 \;\; \text{m}^3 \;\; \text{(concrete is only for base 100 mm + walls 100 mm thick)}
$$

$$
V_{trench} = (30 \times 1.0 \times 0.1) \times 4 + (4 \times 30 \times 0.8 \times 0.1 \times 2) = 12.0 + 19.2 = 31.2 \;\; \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 5:** Total (excluding cable trench for compactness) ≈ **84.4 m³**; round to **85 m³ of M30 concrete**.

**Steel reinforcement @ 75 kg/m³:** 85 × 75 = 6375 kg ≈ **6.4 tonnes of TMT Fe 500 steel**.

#### Part (b) — ACSR Zebra Conductor (7 marks)

**Full-load current at 33 kV side:**

$$
I_{FL} = \frac{5 \times 10^6}{\sqrt{3} \times 33 \times 10^3} = 87.5 \;\; \text{A}
$$

**ACSR Zebra rating (per IS 802):** 245 A continuous, 1 run sufficient.

**Length per phase** = span + droppers + jumper + termination + wastage (5 %):

$$
L_{phase} = 1.05 \times (30 + 3 + 2 + 1) = 1.05 \times 36 = 37.8 \;\; \text{m/phase}
$$

**Total for 3 phases:** $3 \times 37.8 \approx 113.4$ m, round to **115 m ACSR Zebra**.

**Plus jumper lengths** (6 jumpers × 4 m each) = **24 m**, and **earth wire (GI 7/3.15 mm, 1 run along gantry)** = **40 m**.

**Final ordered quantity: 140 m ACSR Zebra + 50 m GI earth wire.**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose 2–4 Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to apply the short-circuit criterion** — students often size conductors by current density alone, losing 2 marks when the examiner expects $A_{min, sc} = I_{sc}\sqrt{t}/K$ to govern.
> 2. **Not adding wastage/contingency** — KSEB/KTU estimators always add 2.5 % to 7.5 % on raw quantities; quoting bare quantities is treated as an incomplete estimate.
> 3. **Confusing ACSR Panther (150 A) with ACSR Zebra (245 A)** — a classic 1-mark trap in 33 kV substation questions.
> 4. **Missing the BFC (Back Fill Compound) bag in earth-pit BoQ** — for Kerala's laterite soil, 25 kg BFC per pit is mandatory per CEA Regulations 2010.
> 5. **Sizing earth strip by intuition rather than mesh calculation** — must show 5 m × 5 m mesh layout to earn full 7 marks.
> 6. **Forgetting the neutral CT, lightning arrester, and conservator** in transformer BoQ — each transformer has ~12 sub-items, not just the tank.
> 7. **Not specifying unit** in the MTO sheet — KTU deducts 0.5 mark per missing unit.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 3 — Substation Material Estimation):**

- ☐ A substation's **material list has 8 heads**: civil, steel structures, bus bars, insulators, switchgear, transformer, earthing, control/aux.
- ☐ **Current density (δ)** for Cu = 1.6 A/mm² (normal), Al = 1.0 A/mm² (normal).
- ☐ **Short-circuit conductor sizing** is ALWAYS mandatory — formula $A_{min, sc} = I_{sc} \sqrt{t} / K$, with $K_{Cu} = 142$ and $K_{Al} = 75$.
- ☐ **33 kV disc insulator string** = 3 nos. per string (KSEB standard), 90 kN E&M rating.
- ☐ **ACSR Panther (150 A)** for distribution, **ACSR Zebra (245 A)** for 33 kV substation incoming, **ACSR Moose (460 A)** for 110 kV.
- ☐ **Earth pit formula (IS 3043)** is $R = \rho/(2\pi L) \times [\ln(8L/d) - 1]$ for rod electrodes.
- ☐ **Required earth resistance**: ≤ 0.5 Ω for HV substations (combined mesh), ≤ 1 Ω for body earth, ≤ 5 Ω for lightning protection.
- ☐ **Concrete grade**: M25 for general plinth, M30 for transformer foundation, with PCC 1:4:8 below.
- ☐ **Steel reinforcement**: 60–80 kg per m³ of concrete (use **75 kg/m³** as KTU default).
- ☐ **Cable trench**: typically 1.0 m wide × 0.8 m deep, **100 mm RCC base + 100 mm RCC walls**.
- ☐ **Wastage factors**: 2.5 % for steel, 5 % for conductors, 7.5 % for cables, 5 % for earth strip.
- ☐ **Standards to quote in answers**: IS 802 (Part I–XI), IS 3043, IS 1180, IS 5613, IS 13134, IS 4091, IS 1180, IEC 60076, IEC 60909, CEA Safety Regulations 2010.
- ☐ **Unit discipline**: every MTO entry must show a unit (m, kg, m³, nos., set).
- ☐ **Kerala-specific**: laterite soil $\rho$ = 200–300 Ω·m, BFC mandatory, monsoon-seasonal earthing resistance rise by 30 % (oversize by 1.3×).

> **One-line Memory Trick (KTU Special):**
> *"**C**ivil, **S**teel, **B**us, **I**nsulator, **S**witchgear, **T**ransformer, **E**arth, **C**able" → **CSBSITEC** → the 8 heads of substation material estimation.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pre-commissioning Tests for Transformers, Industrial Loads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT752 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pre-commissioning Tests for Transformers, Industrial Loads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Pre-Commissioning Tests for Transformers

**Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
Pre-commissioning tests are a series of systematic, standardized electrical, mechanical, and chemical checks performed on a power transformer **after installation but before it is energized and connected to the operational network**. These tests verify that the transformer has not been damaged during transit, storage, or erection, and that it conforms to the design specifications and relevant IS/IEC standards (e.g., **IS 2026**, **IS 1180**, **IEC 60076**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Emphasis:** In the KTU 2024 ESE pattern, pre-commissioning tests are treated as a **high-weightage, application-oriented topic** in Electrical System Design because they bridge the gap between theoretical design and field-ready installation. Examiners frequently test the *purpose, instruments used, and acceptance criteria* of these tests.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine buying a brand-new car after it has been shipped across the ocean. Before you start the engine and drive it on a highway, you (or the dealer) would:
1. Check the tyre pressure (mechanical integrity),
2. Check the oil level (lubrication / chemical state),
3. Test the brakes (safety),
4. Check the electrical battery (insulation),
5. Listen for unusual sounds during a short idle (no-load test).

Pre-commissioning tests on a transformer are **exactly the same logic** — they are the *road-worthiness certificate* for the transformer before it joins the live electrical grid. Skipping them is like driving blindfolded on a national highway.

### Classification of Pre-Commissioning Tests

| Category | Purpose | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Electrical Tests** | Verify dielectric & conductive integrity | Megger, Ratio, Polarity, HV Test |
| **Mechanical Tests** | Verify structural soundness | Tightness of bolts, oil leaks, gasket check |
| **Chemical Tests** | Verify insulating medium quality | Oil BDV, DGA, Moisture content |
| **Thermal Tests** | Verify cooling performance | Temperature rise, Oil/Winding temp. |
| **Protection Tests** | Verify safety devices operate | Buchholz, OLTC, PRD, OTI/WTI |

## 1.2 Industrial Loads

**Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
An **industrial load** refers to the total electrical power consumed by equipment and machinery in a manufacturing, processing, or commercial enterprise. It encompasses a wide variety of devices — motors, heaters, lighting, welding sets, furnaces, and HVAC systems — each with distinct electrical characteristics (resistive, inductive, non-linear).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Industrial Loads Matter in Electrical System Design:**
> The selection of transformer rating, switchgear, busbar sizing, and cable cross-section is *entirely* driven by the nature of the industrial load it feeds. A 500 kVA transformer feeding a purely resistive load behaves very differently from one feeding a large induction motor or an arc furnace.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an industrial load as a **"customer's appetite"** at a buffet. A tea-drinking customer (resistive load like a heater) is steady, predictable, and easy to serve. A college student athlete (inductive motor load) eats huge portions at lunch (high inrush current) but is steady afterward. A diabetic patient (sensitive electronic load) needs special care — even a small voltage dip can damage them.

The **electrical designer** is the buffet manager. He must:
- Size the kitchen (transformer),
- Decide the menu (voltage level),
- Schedule service (peak demand management),
- And ensure no customer goes hungry during rush hour (load factor).

### Broad Classification of Industrial Loads

1. **Resistive Loads** — Electric heaters, ovens, electric furnaces (resistance type), incandescent lighting. Power factor = **1.0**.
2. **Inductive Loads** — Induction motors, transformers at no-load, chokes. Power factor = **lagging** (typically 0.7–0.85).
3. **Capacitive Loads** — Capacitor banks, synchronous motors (over-excited), long cable networks. Power factor = **leading**.
4. **Non-Linear Loads** — Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs), rectifiers, UPS, SMPS, LED drivers. Generate **harmonics**.
5. **Intermittent / Cyclic Loads** — Welding transformers, cranes, hoists, elevators. Have very high **demand factor**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Theory of Pre-Commissioning Tests

### Test 1: Insulation Resistance (IR) Test — The "Megger Test"

**Objective:** To measure the resistance of insulation between windings and between each winding and earth (tank).

**Instrument:** A **500 V DC Megger** for LV transformers and a **2500 V / 5000 V DC Megger** for HV transformers (as per **IS 2026 Part 1**).

**Procedure:**
1. Isolate the transformer from the supply.
2. Short-circuit the HV terminals together, short-circuit the LV terminals together, and connect the Megger between HV-E, LV-E, and HV-LV.
3. Apply the test voltage for **60 seconds** (standard duration; values are read at 60 s to allow absorption current to decay).
4. Record the IR value in **MΩ**.

**Acceptance Criterion (KTU/IS Standard):**
The IR value must be greater than:

$$
IR_{min} \text{ (M}\Omega\text{)} = \frac{kV_{rating} \text{ of winding} + 1}{1.0} \quad \text{(for old transformers)}
$$

A more practical formula used in the field is:

$$
IR_{min} \text{ (M}\Omega\text{)} = \frac{C \times kV_{rating}}{kVA_{rating}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pi Factor (Dielectric Absorption Ratio):** For transformers above **1 MVA**, IR is recorded at **15 s and 60 s**, and the **Polarization Index (PI) = IR(60s) / IR(15s)** is calculated. A healthy transformer has **PI > 1.5**; a value < 1.0 indicates wet or contaminated insulation.

**Temperature Correction of IR:**
IR must be corrected to a reference temperature of **30°C** (some standards use 40°C):

$$
IR_{30} = IR_{T} \times 2^{(30 - T)/10}
$$

where $T$ is the winding temperature in °C at the time of measurement.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Utility:** The Megger test is the *first* test conducted on-site because it requires no power supply of its own, is non-destructive, and gives a quick snapshot of the transformer's dielectric health.

---

### Test 2: Transformer Turns Ratio (TTR) Test

**Objective:** To verify that the turns ratio of the transformer matches the nameplate specification within tolerance.

**Principle:** Apply a low-voltage AC supply to the HV winding, measure the induced voltage on the LV winding, and calculate the ratio.

**Formula:**

$$
R = \frac{V_{HV}}{V_{LV}} = \frac{N_{HV}}{N_{LV}}
$$

**Percentage Error (Acceptance):**

$$
\% \text{ Error} = \frac{R_{measured} - R_{nameplate}}{R_{nameplate}} \times 100
$$

Acceptable limit as per **IS 2026**: **± 0.5%** for distribution transformers and **± 0.1%** for power transformers.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake (KTU Board Trap):** Students often write "ratio" without specifying the direction (HV:LV or LV:HV). Always quote the ratio in the **HV : LV** form unless specifically asked otherwise.

---

### Test 3: Polarity & Phase Displacement Test

**Objective:** To ensure the relative polarity of HV and LV terminals is correct, and that the phase displacement matches the vector group symbol.

**Test Setup:** Connect the HV and LV windings of the same phase in series with a voltmeter across them. Apply a low AC voltage to the HV winding.

- If voltmeter reads **V1 − V2** (subtractive polarity) → standard for distribution transformers.
- If voltmeter reads **V1 + V2** (additive polarity) → reverse connection needed.

**Vector Group:** For Indian power transformers, the standard groups are **Dyn11** (most common distribution) and **Yyn0** (older installations). Phase displacement for Dyn11 = **+30°** (LV leads HV by 30°).

---

### Test 4: Oil Dielectric Strength (Breakdown Voltage — BDV) Test

**Objective:** To measure the dielectric strength of the transformer oil (insulating oil).

**Instrument:** **Oil BDV Test Kit** with standardized electrodes (spherical or mushroom-shaped, gap = 2.5 mm).

**Procedure:** Fill the test cell with oil, apply AC voltage at a rate of rise of **2 kV/sec** until breakdown occurs. Repeat **6 times** with 1-minute gaps. Discard the first reading; **average the next 5 readings**.

**Acceptance (IS 335):**

| Voltage Class (kV) | Minimum BDV (kV, RMS) |
|---|---|
| ≤ 72.5 kV | **30 kV** |
| 72.5 – 245 kV | **50 kV** |
| > 245 kV | **60 kV** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Tip:** A low BDV value (< 30 kV) is the **single most common reason** for transformer rejection during pre-commissioning in Kerala, owing to high humidity and coconut-oil contamination in storage.

---

### Test 5: Winding Resistance Test

**Objective:** To detect loose connections, broken strands, or contact resistance issues in the tap changer.

**Instrument:** **Digital Micro-Ohm Meter** or **Kelvin Double Bridge**.

**Formula:**

$$
R_{w} = \frac{\rho \times L}{A} \quad \text{(fundamental resistance of conductor)}
$$

**Temperature Correction to 75°C (reference):**

$$
R_{75} = R_{T} \times \frac{234.5 + 75}{234.5 + T}
$$

**Acceptance:** All three-phase windings must be **balanced within 5%** at the same tap.

---

### Test 6: Short-Circuit (Impedance) Test

**Objective:** To measure the percentage impedance voltage of the transformer, which determines short-circuit current and voltage regulation.

**Procedure (on LV side, HV shorted):** Apply reduced voltage to circulate **full-load current**. Record the applied voltage $V_{sc}$.

$$
\%Z = \frac{V_{sc}}{V_{rated}} \times 100
$$

$$
Z_{pu} = \frac{\%Z}{100}
$$

$$
I_{sc, \text{full voltage}} = \frac{I_{full-load}}{\%Z \text{ (pu)}}
$$

---

### Test 7: No-Load Loss and Current Test

**Objective:** To measure core loss (iron loss) and magnetizing current.

**Procedure:** Apply **rated voltage and frequency** to the LV winding with HV open. Measure input power $P_0$ and input current $I_0$.

$$
P_0 = \text{No-load loss (W)} \approx P_{core} = P_{hysteresis} + P_{eddy}
$$

$$
I_0 \% = \frac{I_0}{I_{rated}} \times 100
$$

Typical $I_0$ values: **2% – 5%** of full-load current for distribution transformers.

---

### Test 8: Buchholz Relay Test

**Objective:** To verify the operation of the gas-actuated protection relay mounted in the pipe between the tank and the conservator.

**Procedure:** Release air slowly into the relay through the petcock. The **first gas collection chamber** should give an **alarm**, and accumulated gas should give a **trip**.

**Gas Color Diagnosis (Modern Diagnostic Aid):**
- **Yellow** → insulation paper decomposition (overheating).
- **White** → moisture ingress.
- **Black** → oil decomposition (arc / severe fault).
- **Grey** → tap changer contact erosion.

---

### Test 9: Other Essential Tests (Quick Reference)

| Test | Standard Reference | Acceptance |
|---|---|---|
| Earth Resistance | IS 3043 | < **1 Ω** for HV installations |
| Phasing Out Test | Field practice | Phasors must match grid |
| Tap Changer Operation | IS 8468 | Smooth across all taps |
| Cooling Equipment Check | IS 2026 | Fans / pumps rated current within ±10% |
| Noise Level | NEMA TR-1 | < **75 dB(A)** for 1 MVA+ units |
| DGA (Dissolved Gas Analysis) | IEC 60599 | H₂, CH₄, C₂H₂ within IEEE C57.104 limits |

---

## 2.2 Deep Theory of Industrial Loads

### A. Load Characteristics

Every industrial load has three defining characteristics that govern system design:

1. **Magnitude (kW / kVA)** — the demand it places on the source.
2. **Power Factor (cos φ)** — the reactive component of current.
3. **Duty Cycle (Hours / Intermittence)** — the time pattern of consumption.

### B. Load Factor (LF)

$$
LF = \frac{\text{Average Load over a period}}{\text{Peak (Maximum) Load in the same period}}
$$

> A **high LF** indicates steady, continuous operation (textile mills, steel plants). A **low LF** indicates intermittent heavy use (welding shops, rolling mills).

### C. Demand Factor (DF)

$$
DF = \frac{\text{Maximum Demand of Load}}{\text{Connected Load}}
$$

> This factor is **always < 1** because, in practice, all connected loads never operate at full rating simultaneously. Indian Standard **IS 732** provides typical DF values:

| Load Type | Typical DF |
|---|---|
| Lighting (Commercial) | 0.80 – 1.0 |
| Lighting (Industrial) | 0.60 – 0.80 |
| Motors (Individual) | 0.75 – 0.85 |
| Motors (Group) | 0.60 – 0.70 |
| Welding Sets | 0.25 – 0.50 |
| HVAC Systems | 0.70 – 0.85 |
| Resistive Ovens | 0.80 – 0.90 |

### D. Diversity Factor (DivF)

$$
DivF = \frac{\text{Sum of Individual Max Demands}}{\text{Simultaneous Maximum Demand of the Group}}
$$

> A **DivF > 1** means the loads are diverse (don't peak at the same time). Used to **right-size the upstream transformer**.

### E. Coincidence Factor

$$
CF = \frac{1}{DivF} = \frac{\text{Group Maximum Demand}}{\text{Sum of Individual Max Demands}}
$$

### F. Utilisation Factor (UF)

$$
UF = \frac{\text{Maximum Demand on Equipment}}{\text{Rated Capacity of Equipment}}
$$

> Reflects how well a transformer / cable is utilised. KTU often asks: "A transformer rated 1000 kVA feeding a maximum demand of 750 kVA — find the UF." Answer: **UF = 0.75 or 75%**.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Typical Range / Acceptance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Insulation Resistance Min | $IR_{min} = C \cdot kV / kVA$ | MΩ | Depends on $C$ (10–1600) |
| 2 | Temperature Corrected IR | $IR_{30} = IR_T \cdot 2^{(30-T)/10}$ | MΩ | At reference 30°C |
| 3 | Polarization Index | $PI = IR_{60s} / IR_{15s}$ | — | > 1.5 (good); < 1.0 (bad) |
| 4 | Turns Ratio | $R = N_1 / N_2 = V_1 / V_2$ | — | ± 0.5% (dist.) |
| 5 | Winding R correction | $R_{75} = R_T \cdot (309.5 / (234.5 + T))$ | Ω | To 75°C |
| 6 | % Impedance | $\%Z = (V_{sc} / V_{rated}) \times 100$ | % | 4% – 12.5% |
| 7 | Short Circuit Current | $I_{sc} = I_{fl} / Z_{pu}$ | A | For protection grading |
| 8 | Earth Resistance | $R_E = \rho / (2 \pi L) \cdot \ln(L/d)$ | Ω | < 1 Ω (HV yard) |
| 9 | Load Factor | $LF = E_{consumed} / (P_{max} \times T)$ | — | 0.40 – 0.90 |
| 10 | Demand Factor | $DF = MD / CL$ | — | 0.25 – 1.0 |
| 11 | Diversity Factor | $DivF = \sum MD_i / MD_{group}$ | — | 1.0 – 2.5 |
| 12 | Utilisation Factor | $UF = MD / Rated Cap.$ | — | 0.50 – 0.90 |
| 13 | Active Power | $P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$ | W | For 3-phase |
| 14 | Apparent Power | $S = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L$ | VA | — |
| 15 | kVA Rating of Motor | $kVA = (P_{mech} / \eta \cdot \cos\phi) \cdot 1000$ | kVA | Std. catalogue |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Almost every numerical problem in this module involves at least **two of the above formulas** in a chain. Practice combining DF + DivF + kVA to size a transformer for a mixed industrial load.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

1. **Substation Design Engineers** use these tests to issue "fit-for-charging" certificates before transformer energization.
2. **Industrial Consultants** use load factor / demand factor data to **right-size transformers**, saving crores of rupees in CAPEX (capital expenditure).
3. **Utility Engineers** apply diversity factor across a feeder to **avoid over-specification** of upstream switchgear.
4. **Insurance and Safety Auditors** require pre-commissioning test reports (Megger, BDV, TTR) before approving an installation for commercial use.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Derivation Mandate:** Every algebraic step is shown explicitly. No mental arithmetic shortcuts are used.

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Temperature Correction of Insulation Resistance

### Statement
The measured insulation resistance of a transformer winding is dependent on temperature. Higher temperature → lower IR. We must correct the measured value to a standard reference temperature of 30°C.

### Derivation

Insulation resistance of a dielectric follows an **exponential inverse law with temperature**, given empirically by:

$$
R_T = R_{30} \cdot k^{(T - 30)}
$$

where $k$ is the temperature coefficient of resistance (empirically ≈ 0.5, i.e., resistance halves for every 10°C rise).

Rewriting $k = (1/2) = 2^{-1}$, and substituting:

$$
R_T = R_{30} \cdot 2^{-(T - 30)/10}
$$

$$
R_T = \frac{R_{30}}{2^{(T - 30)/10}}
$$

Solving for $R_{30}$ (the corrected value):

$$
R_{30} = R_T \cdot 2^{(T - 30)/10}
$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $-1$ in the exponent (so the convention is "at 30°C vs measured"):

$$
IR_{30} = IR_T \cdot 2^{(30 - T)/10}
$$

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Given:** A transformer's HV winding IR is measured as **120 MΩ** at a winding temperature of **55°C**. The oil temperature is **50°C** (assume average 52.5°C for the dielectric).

**Step 1:** Apply the formula

$$
IR_{30} = IR_T \cdot 2^{(30 - T)/10}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values

$$
IR_{30} = 120 \times 2^{(30 - 55)/10}
$$

**Step 3:** Simplify the exponent

$$
(30 - 55)/10 = -25/10 = -2.5
$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate $2^{-2.5}$

$$
2^{-2.5} = \frac{1}{2^{2.5}} = \frac{1}{2^2 \times 2^{0.5}} = \frac{1}{4 \times 1.4142} = \frac{1}{5.6568} = 0.1768
$$

**Step 5:** Compute the final corrected IR

$$
IR_{30} = 120 \times 0.1768 = 21.21 \text{ M}\Omega
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students forget that the **measured value is LOWER** than the corrected value at cold reference. Always check: if T > 30°C, the exponent is negative, so $2^{neg} < 1$, meaning the **corrected IR (at 30°C) is LESS than measured** when the equipment is hot. This is counter-intuitive but correct.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Percentage Error in Turns Ratio

### Statement
A 500 kVA, 11 kV / 433 V transformer has a nameplate turns ratio of 25.4. On test, the measured ratio is 25.6. Compute the percentage error.

### Derivation

$$
R_{nameplate} = \frac{V_{HV}}{V_{LV}} = \frac{11000}{433} = 25.404 \approx 25.4
$$

$$
R_{measured} = 25.6 \text{ (given)}
$$

Percentage error formula:

$$
\% \text{ Error} = \frac{R_{measured} - R_{nameplate}}{R_{nameplate}} \times 100
$$

Substituting:

$$
\% \text{ Error} = \frac{25.6 - 25.4}{25.4} \times 100
$$

Step-by-step:

$$
= \frac{0.2}{25.4} \times 100
$$

$$
= 0.007874 \times 100
$$

$$
= 0.7874 \%
$$

### Acceptance Check
As per **IS 2026**, permissible tolerance is **± 0.5%**. Here we have **+0.787%**, which **fails the test**. The transformer must be re-tapped or sent back for repair.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Short-Circuit Current and Breaker Selection

### Statement
A **2 MVA, 11 kV / 433 V, Dyn11** transformer has a measured percentage impedance of **6.5%**. Compute the full-voltage short-circuit current on the LV side.

### Step-by-Step

**Step 1:** Full-load current on LV side

$$
I_{fl,LV} = \frac{S_{rated}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{LV}} = \frac{2 \times 10^6}{\sqrt{3} \times 433}
$$

$$
= \frac{2 \times 10^6}{1.732 \times 433} = \frac{2 \times 10^6}{749.96}
$$

$$
= 2666.67 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 2:** Per-unit impedance

$$
Z_{pu} = \frac{\%Z}{100} = \frac{6.5}{100} = 0.065
$$

**Step 3:** Short-circuit current at full voltage

$$
I_{sc} = \frac{I_{fl}}{Z_{pu}} = \frac{2666.67}{0.065}
$$

$$
= 41,025 \text{ A} \approx 41.03 \text{ kA}
$$

**Step 4:** Implication for breaker selection

The LV ACB (Air Circuit Breaker) must have a **breaking capacity ≥ 41 kA** with an adequate **making capacity** (typically 2.55 × breaking capacity for IEC standards).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering:** This calculation is the **basis of switchgear rating** in any industrial substation. A breaker with insufficient breaking capacity will **explode** when called upon to interrupt a fault — the single most catastrophic failure mode in an electrical system.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Transformer Sizing for a Mixed Industrial Load

### Statement (Typical KTU University Problem)
A small-scale industry has the following connected loads:

| Equipment | Qty | Rating (kW) | PF | DF |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Induction Motors (Group) | 10 | 15 each | 0.80 lag | 0.70 |
| Lighting (Fluorescent) | — | 20 kW | 0.90 lag | 0.85 |
| Welding Set | 2 | 25 kVA each | 0.55 lag | 0.30 |
| Resistive Oven | 1 | 30 kW | 1.00 | 0.90 |

Compute the **transformer kVA rating** assuming a Diversity Factor of **1.4** between the motor group and the rest.

### Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Connected Load per Equipment**

Induction Motors (Group): $CL = 10 \times 15 = 150 \text{ kW}$ at pf 0.80
Lighting: $CL = 20 \text{ kW}$ at pf 0.90
Welding Sets: $CL = 2 \times 25 = 50 \text{ kVA}$ at pf 0.55
Resistive Oven: $CL = 30 \text{ kW}$ at pf 1.00

**Step 2: Maximum Demand (per equipment) = CL × DF**

Motors: $MD_1 = 150 \times 0.70 = 105 \text{ kW}$
Lighting: $MD_2 = 20 \times 0.85 = 17 \text{ kW}$
Welding: $MD_3 = 50 \times 0.30 = 15 \text{ kVA}$
Oven: $MD_4 = 30 \times 0.90 = 27 \text{ kW}$

**Step 3: Convert all to kVA (since DF applies to kW for unity-pf loads, but for mixed pf loads we use the kVA form for S):**

For motor group:
$$
kVA_1 = \frac{kW_1}{pf_1} = \frac{105}{0.80} = 131.25 \text{ kVA}
$$

For lighting:
$$
kVA_2 = \frac{17}{0.90} = 18.89 \text{ kVA}
$$

For welding (already in kVA, kW = kVA × pf):
$$
kW_3 = 15 \times 0.55 = 8.25 \text{ kW}
$$

For oven (unity pf):
$$
kVA_4 = kW_4 = 27 \text{ kVA}
$$

**Step 4: Sum of individual max demands in kVA**

$$
\sum MD_i = 131.25 + 18.89 + 15 + 27 = 192.14 \text{ kVA}
$$

**Step 5: Apply Diversity Factor**

$$
MD_{group} = \frac{\sum MD_i}{DivF} = \frac{192.14}{1.4} = 137.24 \text{ kVA}
$$

**Step 6: Apply a Design Margin of 20% (Standard Practice)**

$$
S_{transformer} = MD_{group} \times 1.20 = 137.24 \times 1.20 = 164.69 \text{ kVA}
$$

**Step 7: Select Next Standard Rating (as per IS 1180 / IEC)**

Available standard ratings: 100, 160, 200, 250, 315, 400, 500 kVA.

**Selected rating: 200 kVA** (next size above 164.69 kVA).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** The 20% margin accommodates future load growth (typically 5–10 years), voltage regulation losses, and the thermal de-rating of transformers above 30°C ambient (Kerala conditions).

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: BDV of Transformer Oil — Why 2.5 mm Gap?

The standard electrode gap of **2.5 mm** in oil BDV testing is not arbitrary. It is derived from the **Weidmann-Fröhlich oil gap theory**:

The breakdown strength of a thin oil film under uniform AC field is given by:

$$
V_b = k \cdot d^{0.5}
$$

where $d$ is the gap (mm) and $k$ is the oil quality constant (≈ 30 kV for standard mineral oil).

For $d = 2.5$ mm:

$$
V_b = 30 \times (2.5)^{0.5} = 30 \times 1.581 = 47.43 \text{ kV}
$$

This matches the empirical observation that new transformer oil breaks down at **40–50 kV** under standard test conditions, validating the 2.5 mm gap selection by IEC.

---

## 3.6 KTU Analytical Matrix — "Which Test For Which Problem?"

| Suspected Fault | Recommended Test | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Moisture in oil | BDV Test, DGA | Water lowers dielectric strength |
| Winding short between turns | Turns Ratio Test | Ratio deviates from nameplate |
| Loose bolted connection | Winding Resistance | High contact resistance |
| Core insulation failure | Megger (core-to-tank) | Earth fault in core |
| Tap changer wear | Resistance + DGA | Eroded contacts → black gas |
| Oil contamination | BDV + DGA + Moisture | Multi-prong diagnosis |
| Bushing failure | Megger + Visual + Tan-Delta | Surface creepage |
| Buchholz alarm | Gas collection + DGA | Identifying fault type |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Sequential Flow Diagram of Pre-Commissioning Tests

The following Mermaid diagram shows the *logical sequence* in which pre-commissioning tests are conducted on a power transformer at site. Note: Visual inspection comes first, followed by non-energized tests, then energized tests, and finally protection tests before charging.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Visual Inspection<br>and Mechanical Check] --> B[Step 2: Insulation Resistance<br>Megger Test HV-LV, HV-E, LV-E]
    B --> C[Step 3: Oil BDV Test<br>and Moisture Check]
    C --> D[Step 4: Turns Ratio TTR<br>and Polarity Test]
    D --> E[Step 5: Winding Resistance<br>at All Tap Positions]
    E --> F[Step 6: Earth Resistance<br>Test on Tank and Neutral]
    F --> G[Step 7: Phase Sequence<br>and Phasing Out]
    G --> H[Step 8: Energize Transformer<br>at No-Load]
    H --> I[Step 9: No-Load Loss<br>and Current Measurement]
    I --> J[Step 10: Short-Circuit<br>Impedance Test]
    J --> K[Step 11: Protection Tests<br>Buchholz, OTI, WTI, PRD]
    K --> L[Step 12: Temperature Rise<br>and Cooling Check]
    L --> M[Step 13: Final Phasing Out<br>and Parallel Operation]
    M --> N[Step 14: Charging Approval<br>and Energization]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Transformer Pre-Commissioning Test Architecture

This diagram maps the **functional relationship** between the test instruments, the transformer under test, and the recording / acceptance authority.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS[Measurement Inputs]
        A1[5 kV DC<br>Megger]
        A2[TTR Set<br>Low Voltage AC]
        A3[BDV Kit<br>Oil Sample]
        A4[Micro-Ohm<br>Meter]
        A5[Earth Tester]
    end

    subgraph CORE[Transformer Under Test]
        B1[HV Winding<br>11 kV Class]
        B2[LV Winding<br>433 V Class]
        B3[Insulating Oil<br>in Tank]
        B4[Tank and Earthing<br>System]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUTS[Outputs and Decisions]
        C1[Test Report<br>Numerical Values]
        C2{Acceptance<br>vs IS Standard}
        C3[Pass Certificate]
        C4[Rejection and<br>Rectification]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A1 --> B2
    A2 --> B1
    A2 --> B2
    A3 --> B3
    A4 --> B1
    A4 --> B2
    A5 --> B4

    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    B4 --> C1

    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C2 --> C4
```

## 4.3 Industrial Load Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Industrial Load] --> B[Resistive]
    A --> C[Inductive]
    A --> D[Capacitive]
    A --> E[Non-Linear]

    B --> B1[Electric Ovens]
    B --> B2[Incandescent Lighting]
    B --> B3[Resistance Furnaces]

    C --> C1[Induction Motors]
    C --> C2[Transformers at No-Load]
    C --> C3[Welding Reactors]

    D --> D1[Capacitor Banks]
    D --> D2[Synchronous Motors<br>Over-Excited]
    D --> D3[Underground Cables<br>Long Lengths]

    E --> E1[VFD Drives]
    E --> E2[UPS Systems]
    E --> E3[LED Lighting]
    E --> E4[Rectifiers and<br>Smelters]
```

## 4.4 Load Curve Schematic (Daily Industrial Demand)

A typical KTU question presents a 24-hour load curve and asks for the Load Factor, Energy Consumed, or Peak Demand. Below is a block-level representation of how the calculation logic flows from a measured load curve to the design parameters.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[24-Hour<br>Load Curve] --> B[Integrate Area<br>under Curve]
    B --> C[Total Energy<br>kWh]
    C --> D[Energy / Time<br>Average Load]
    A --> E[Identify Peak<br>Maximum Demand]
    D --> F[Load Factor<br>Average / Peak]
    E --> F
    A --> G[Identify Connected<br>Load from Inventory]
    E --> H[Demand Factor<br>MD / CL]
    G --> H
```
