# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Part A questions are direct, definition-based, or short-explanation questions. They test the student's ability to recall specific facts, instruments, or standard values.

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**List any six pre-commissioning tests carried out on a power transformer. State the purpose of each test in one line.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Test | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Insulation Resistance (Megger) Test | To check the integrity of insulation between windings and earth |
| 2 | Turns Ratio (TTR) Test | To verify the voltage ratio matches the nameplate |
| 3 | Polarity Test | To check correct relative polarity of HV and LV terminals |
| 4 | Oil BDV Test | To measure the dielectric strength of insulating oil |
| 5 | Winding Resistance Test | To detect loose connections or broken strands |
| 6 | Earth Resistance Test | To verify effective earthing of tank and neutral |

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution (3 M):** 6 tests × 0.5 M = 3 M. Each test name + purpose in one line is sufficient.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define (i) Demand Factor, (ii) Diversity Factor, and (iii) Load Factor. State one practical significance of each.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Demand Factor (DF):**
$$
DF = \frac{\text{Maximum Demand of Load}}{\text{Connected Load}}
$$
*Significance:* Used to estimate the realistic kVA demand from a group of loads, avoiding over-design of transformers and cables.

**(ii) Diversity Factor (DivF):**
$$
DivF = \frac{\text{Sum of Individual Maximum Demands}}{\text{Simultaneous Maximum Demand of Group}}
$$
*Significance:* Indicates how diverse load timings are. A higher DivF permits a smaller upstream transformer.

**(iii) Load Factor (LF):**
$$
LF = \frac{\text{Average Load}}{\text{Peak Load}}
$$
*Significance:* Indicates the efficiency of energy utilization. A low LF signals opportunities for load shifting and energy cost reduction.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse "Demand Factor" and "Diversity Factor." Memorize: **DF operates on a SINGLE load; DivF operates on a GROUP of loads.** A classic KTU board trap.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Internal Choice Mandate:** Every 14-mark question in the KTU 2024 ESE provides an **internal choice**: students must answer either OR part. We provide two completely independent question alternatives below.

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Alternative 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**A.** (a) **Explain in detail the pre-commissioning tests performed on a power transformer with reference to:**
   (i) Insulation Resistance Test
   (ii) Turns Ratio Test
   (iii) Oil BDV Test

   For each test, state the **instrument used**, the **procedure**, and the **acceptance criterion** as per relevant IS standard. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A 1000 kVA, 11 kV / 433 V, 50 Hz, Dyn11 transformer has its HV winding insulation resistance measured as **80 MΩ** at an average winding temperature of **50°C**. Calculate the IR corrected to the standard reference temperature of **30°C**. Comment on the suitability of the transformer for charging. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Detailed Explanation:**

**(i) Insulation Resistance Test (Megger Test):**
- **Instrument:** 2.5 kV or 5 kV DC Megger for HV transformers; 500 V for LV.
- **Procedure:** With transformer de-energized, short HV terminals together and LV terminals together. Apply megger voltage between HV-Earth, LV-Earth, and HV-LV for 60 seconds. Record values.
- **Acceptance:** IR value should be ≥ $C \times kV / kVA$ formula value, and **PI = IR(60s)/IR(15s) > 1.5** for transformers > 1 MVA.

**(ii) Turns Ratio Test (TTR Test):**
- **Instrument:** Transformer Turns Ratio Meter (TTR Set), applying low-voltage AC to the HV winding.
- **Procedure:** Measure LV induced voltage. Calculate ratio $R = V_{HV} / V_{LV}$.
- **Acceptance:** Deviation from nameplate ratio ≤ **± 0.5%** for distribution transformers (IS 2026 Part 1).

**(iii) Oil BDV Test:**
- **Instrument:** Oil BDV Test Kit with mushroom electrodes, 2.5 mm gap.
- **Procedure:** Fill test cell with oil sample. Raise AC voltage at **2 kV/sec** until breakdown. Repeat 6 times. Average last 5 readings.
- **Acceptance:** Minimum **30 kV (RMS)** for transformers up to 72.5 kV class (IS 335).

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $IR_T = 80 \text{ M}\Omega$ at $T = 50°C$.

**Step 1:** Apply the temperature correction formula

$$
IR_{30} = IR_T \cdot 2^{(30 - T)/10}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute

$$
IR_{30} = 80 \times 2^{(30 - 50)/10} = 80 \times 2^{-2}
$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate

$$
2^{-2} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25
$$

**Step 4:** Final value

$$
IR_{30} = 80 \times 0.25 = 20 \text{ M}\Omega
$$

**Step 5:** Acceptance Check (IS 2026 formula):

$$
IR_{min} = \frac{C \times kV}{kVA}
$$

For an 11 kV transformer, using $C = 160$ (oil-immersed standard):

$$
IR_{min} = \frac{160 \times 11}{1000} = 1.76 \text{ M}\Omega
$$

Since **20 MΩ >> 1.76 MΩ**, the insulation is **healthy and the transformer is fit for charging**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Many students write the formula with the **wrong sign** in the exponent. Remember: the formula is $2^{(30 - T)/10}$, **not** $2^{(T - 30)/10}$. A sign error here will cost **2 of the 7 marks** instantly.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**B.** (a) **Discuss the different types of industrial loads with examples. Explain the significance of Demand Factor and Diversity Factor in sizing a distribution transformer for an industrial consumer. State typical IS 732 values for any three load types. **[7 Marks]****

**(b)** A factory has the following load data:
   - Induction Motors (connected): **300 kW**, PF 0.75 lag, DF 0.70
   - Lighting Load (connected): **50 kW**, PF 0.90 lag, DF 0.85
   - Welding Sets (connected): **100 kVA**, PF 0.50 lag, DF 0.30

   Compute the **kVA rating** of the transformer to be installed, assuming a Diversity Factor of **1.3** between motor group and the rest, and a future growth margin of **20%**. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Conceptual Discussion:**

Industrial loads are classified as:
1. **Resistive Loads** — heaters, ovens (pf = 1.0).
2. **Inductive Loads** — induction motors, transformers (pf lagging).
3. **Capacitive Loads** — capacitor banks, long cables (pf leading).
4. **Non-Linear Loads** — VFDs, rectifiers (cause harmonics).

**Significance of Demand Factor (DF):**
Since not all connected loads run simultaneously at full rating, DF < 1.0. It allows realistic sizing of transformers, preventing over-investment.

**Significance of Diversity Factor (DivF):**
Loads in different parts of a factory peak at different times. DivF > 1.0 allows the upstream transformer to be sized **smaller** than the sum of individual max demands.

**Typical IS 732 Values:**
- Motors (group): DF = 0.60–0.70
- Lighting: DF = 0.80–1.0
- Welding Sets: DF = 0.25–0.50

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute Maximum Demand for each load group**

Induction Motors:
$$
MD_1 = CL \times DF = 300 \times 0.70 = 210 \text{ kW}
$$

Convert to kVA:
$$
kVA_1 = \frac{210}{0.75} = 280 \text{ kVA}
$$

Lighting:
$$
MD_2 = 50 \times 0.85 = 42.5 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
kVA_2 = \frac{42.5}{0.90} = 47.22 \text{ kVA}
$$

Welding Sets (already in kVA):
$$
MD_3 = 100 \times 0.30 = 30 \text{ kVA}
$$

**Step 2: Sum of Individual Max Demands**

$$
\sum MD_i = 280 + 47.22 + 30 = 357.22 \text{ kVA}
$$

**Step 3: Apply Diversity Factor**

$$
MD_{group} = \frac{\sum MD_i}{DivF} = \frac{357.22}{1.3} = 274.78 \text{ kVA}
$$

**Step 4: Apply 20% Growth Margin**

$$
S_{final} = 274.78 \times 1.20 = 329.74 \text{ kVA}
$$

**Step 5: Select Standard Rating**

Next standard size: **400 kVA** (per IS 1180 / IEC 60076 standard sizes: 315, 400, 500 kVA).

$$
\boxed{\text{Select a 400 kVA transformer.}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning:** The most common mistake in this type of question is mixing **kW and kVA** in the same summation. **You must convert all to kVA BEFORE summing.** Also, do not apply Diversity Factor on the connected load — DivF is applied on the **sum of maximum demands**, not on the connected load.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Read this 1 hour before the exam.**

### Pre-Commissioning Tests
- [ ] **Megger Test** uses 5 kV DC for HV transformers. IR value corrected to 30°C using $IR_{30} = IR_T \times 2^{(30-T)/10}$.
- [ ] **PI Ratio** = IR(60s) / IR(15s). Must be **> 1.5** for healthy insulation.
- [ ] **TTR Test** tolerance is **± 0.5%** for distribution transformers (IS 2026).
- [ ] **BDV Test** uses 2.5 mm gap, 2 kV/sec rate of rise, average of last 5 of 6 readings.
- [ ] **Minimum BDV** is **30 kV** for ≤ 72.5 kV class, **50 kV** for 72.5–245 kV class.
- [ ] **Winding Resistance** is corrected to 75°C using $R_{75} = R_T \times (309.5 / (234.5 + T))$.
- [ ] **Buchholz Test** — yellow gas = paper decomposition; black gas = oil decomposition.
- [ ] **Earth Resistance** must be < **1 Ω** for HV substations.
- [ ] **%Z** of distribution transformer is typically **4% – 7.5%**; of power transformer is **8% – 12.5%**.

### Industrial Loads
- [ ] **DF** is always ≤ 1; **DivF** is always ≥ 1.
- [ ] **Load Factor = Average / Peak** — high LF is good (efficiency).
- [ ] **Induction Motors** draw **6× to 8×** full-load current during starting (must be considered in transformer sizing).
- [ ] **Welding sets** have very low DF (0.25–0.50) but very high instantaneous kVA.
- [ ] **Non-linear loads** (VFD, UPS) generate harmonics → THD must be < 5% (IEEE 519) at PCC.
- [ ] Standard Indian transformer sizes: 100, 160, 200, 250, 315, 400, 500, 630, 800, 1000, 1250, 1600, 2000, 2500 kVA.
- [ ] Always apply **20% design margin** above calculated maximum demand for industrial transformers (Kerala IS 1180 practice).
- [ ] All summations of demand must be in **kVA** (not kW) before applying DivF.

### Universal Exam Tips
- [ ] Always state the **IS / IEC standard reference** in your answer (IS 2026, IS 335, IS 3043, IS 732, IS 1180).
- [ ] Always include **units** in numerical answers.
- [ ] Always draw the **circuit / connection diagram** (Mermaid or hand-drawn) — it carries 1–2 marks in 14-mark questions.
- [ ] For Megger questions, mention the **voltage rating** of the Megger used.
- [ ] For BDV questions, specify the **electrode gap and rate of voltage rise**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selection of Starters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT752 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Selection of Starters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Starter:** A starter is an electro-mechanical or electronic switching and control device used for the safe acceleration of an electric motor from rest to its rated operating speed, by limiting the **inrush starting current**, regulating the **starting torque**, and providing essential **overload and short-circuit protection** during the starting transient.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEEOT752 - Electrical System Design, Module 3)**, the **Selection of Starters** is a design-level decision in which the designer matches the motor's starting characteristics (current, torque, duration, load profile) with an appropriate switching topology, voltage-reduction technique, and protection philosophy, while complying with **IS 13947 / IEC 60947** standards and utility supply regulations.

### Classification of Starters (As per KTU Syllabus)

> [!NOTE]
> Starters are classified based on the **voltage applied to the stator winding** during starting.

| Category | Type | Mechanism |
|----------|------|-----------|
| Full-Voltage | Direct-On-Line (DOL) Starter | Full supply voltage applied to stator |
| Reduced-Voltage | Star-Delta (Y-Δ) Starter | Stator reconnected from Y → Δ |
| Reduced-Voltage | Auto-Transformer Starter | Tapped transformer steps down voltage |
| Reduced-Voltage | Primary Resistor / Reactor Starter | Series impedance drops voltage |
| Electronic | Soft Starter (SCR based) | Phase-angle control of voltage |
| Electronic | Variable Frequency Drive (VFD) | Variable V/f control |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Bicycle Clutch:**
> Think of a starter like the **clutch system of a heavy truck**. When the truck is stationary, connecting the engine directly to the wheels would cause a violent jerk (similar to a motor's inrush current). The clutch *gradually engages*, transmitting torque smoothly. Similarly, a starter "gradually engages" the supply with the motor, limiting the electrical and mechanical stress.

- **DOL Starter** → Like *engaging clutch instantly* (works only for small motors).
- **Star-Delta Starter** → *Two-stage clutch engagement*: first soft (Y), then full (Δ).
- **Auto-Transformer Starter** → *Clutch with a gear-reduction* (taps adjust reduction ratio).
- **Soft Starter / VFD** → *Modern hydraulic clutch* with precise electronic control.

### Physical Constants & Standards

- **Standard Reference Voltage:** **415 V, 3-Phase, 50 Hz** (Indian LV system).
- **Standard Motor Rating Boundary for DOL:** Typically up to **5 HP (3.7 kW)** for industrial premises; utility norms often restrict DOL above **5 kW** to avoid voltage dip.
- **Key Standards:** **IS 13947-4-1** (Low-voltage switchgear), **IEC 60947-4-1** (Contactors & Motor Starters), **IS 325** (Three-phase induction motors).
- **Typical Allowed Starting Voltage Dip:** **15 %** of nominal system voltage (per CBIP guidelines).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of Major Starter Types

### A. Direct-On-Line (DOL) Starter
- **How it works:** The contactor connects the stator windings **directly to the supply** in a single step.
- **Why it is used:** Simplest, cheapest, and provides the **highest starting torque** (100 % of rated).
- **Drawback:** Starting current is **6 to 8 times** the Full-Load Current (FLC).
- **Application:** Small motors, pumps, fans, conveyors with low inertia.

### B. Star-Delta (Y-Δ) Starter
- **How it works:** Motor first connected in **Star** (line voltage reduced by **1/√3** ≈ **57.7 %** per phase). After acceleration, reconnected in **Delta** for normal running.
- **Why it is used:** Reduces starting current to **1/3** and starting torque to **1/3** of DOL values.
- **Limitation:** The motor MUST be **delta-connected** at rated voltage (i.e., the stator winding's rated phase voltage = line voltage).
- **Application:** Centrifugal pumps, compressors, fans — light starting loads.

### C. Auto-Transformer Starter
- **How it works:** A tapped autotransformer supplies **reduced voltage** (typically at **50 %, 65 %, 80 %** taps) during starting. After the motor accelerates, the autotransformer is bypassed and full voltage is applied.
- **Why it is used:** Flexible tap selection allows **optimal balance** between starting current and starting torque. Current drawn from supply is **k² times** the motor current.
- **Application:** Heavy-duty starting where torque is critical (large compressors, crushers).

### D. Soft Starter (Electronic)
- **How it works:** Uses **back-to-back SCRs (Thyristors)** to ramp up the voltage via phase-angle control. The voltage rises gradually, limiting the inrush.
- **Why it is used:** Smooth, stepless acceleration with programmable **ramp time, initial voltage, and current limit**. Reduces mechanical shock.
- **Application:** Belt conveyors, escalators, mixers, HVAC systems.

### E. Variable Frequency Drive (VFD)
- **How it works:** Rectifier → DC bus → Inverter produces variable **V/f ratio** to control speed AND torque.
- **Why it is used:** Full speed control with **soft start and soft stop**, energy savings (for variable torque loads).
- **Application:** Pumps, fans, HVAC, process industries.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA BANK** for the topic "Selection of Starters" as per KTU 2024 Scheme evaluation. Memorize all expressions and the **derivation logic** behind them.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variable Definition | Units |
|---|----------|---------|---------------------|-------|
| 1 | DOL Starting Current | $I_{sc} \;=\; \dfrac{V_{L}}{\sqrt{R_s^2 + X_s^2}}$ | $V_L$ = Line voltage, $R_s, X_s$ = Stator resistance & reactance per phase | A |
| 2 | DOL Starting Current Ratio | $I_{sc} \;\approx\; (6 \text{ to } 8) \times I_{FLC}$ | $I_{FLC}$ = Full-load current | A |
| 3 | Starting Torque (DOL) | $T_{sc} \;\propto\; V^2$ | $V$ = Applied voltage | N·m |
| 4 | Star-Delta Line Current | $I_{Y} \;=\; \dfrac{I_{\Delta}}{3}$ | $I_\Delta$ = DOL starting current | A |
| 5 | Star-Delta Starting Torque | $T_{Y} \;=\; \dfrac{T_{\Delta}}{3}$ | $T_\Delta$ = DOL starting torque | N·m |
| 6 | Auto-Transformer Tap Ratio | $k \;=\; \dfrac{V_{\text{tap}}}{V_{\text{Line}}}$ | $V_{\text{tap}}$ = Reduced voltage, $V_{\text{Line}}$ = Nominal | unitless |
| 7 | Auto-Transformer Motor Current | $I_m \;=\; k \cdot I_{\Delta}$ | Reduced motor current | A |
| 8 | Auto-Transformer Line Current | $I_{\text{line}} \;=\; k^2 \cdot I_{\Delta}$ | Current drawn from supply | A |
| 9 | Auto-Transformer Starting Torque | $T_{\text{start}} \;=\; k^2 \cdot T_{\Delta}$ | Reduced starting torque | N·m |
| 10 | Reactor Starter Motor Voltage | $V_m \;=\; V_s - I_s \cdot X_r$ | $V_s$ = Supply, $I_s$ = Starting current, $X_r$ = Reactance | V |
| 11 | Stator Copper Loss Ratio | $\dfrac{P_{\text{loss,red}}}{P_{\text{loss,DOL}}} \;=\; k^2$ | For auto-transformer starting | unitless |
| 12 | Starting Voltage Dip (Utility) | $\Delta V\% \;=\; \dfrac{Z_{\text{source}}}{Z_{\text{source}} + Z_{\text{motor}}} \times 100$ | $Z_{\text{source}}$ = Source impedance | % |
| 13 | Number of Starts Allowed | $N_{\text{allowed}} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Thermal limit of rotor}}{\text{Energy per start}}$ | Empirical | starts/hr |
| 14 | Motor Slip (general) | $s \;=\; \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | $N_s$ = Synchronous, $N_r$ = Rotor speed | unitless |
| 15 | Synchronous Speed | $N_s \;=\; \dfrac{120 \cdot f}{P}$ | $f$ = Frequency, $P$ = Number of poles | RPM |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The selection of starters is a **decision-critical step** in the design of:

- **Industrial Plants** (Cement, Steel, Textile, Paper) — Motor-driven machinery forms 60-70 % of the electrical load.
- **HVAC Systems in Commercial Buildings** — Large chillers and AHUs need soft starting to prevent pipe water hammer.
- **Water Pumping Stations** — Municipal pumps often require auto-transformer starting to manage heavy water column load.
- **Oil & Gas** — Compressors and pumps in hazardous areas require explosion-proof (Ex d) starters per **IS 5571**.
- **Smart Manufacturing (Industry 4.0)** — Integration of VFDs with PLC / SCADA enables predictive motor health monitoring.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The following derivations use a common reference frame and are written exhaustively to satisfy the KTU valuation scheme's demand for **complete logical progression**.

## 3.1 Derivation: Starting Current Reduction in Star-Delta Starter

**Given:**
- A 3-phase induction motor with stator winding rated for **delta connection** at line voltage $V_L$.
- DOL starting current (in delta) per phase = $I_{\Delta,\text{ph}}$.
- DOL starting line current = $I_{\Delta,L} = \sqrt{3} \cdot I_{\Delta,\text{ph}}$.

**Step 1 — Identify the per-phase voltage in Star mode.**

In star connection, the phase voltage is the line voltage divided by $\sqrt{3}$:

$$
V_{Y,\text{ph}} \;=\; \dfrac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the per-phase current in Star mode.**

Since the per-phase impedance of the motor $Z_s$ is constant, Ohm's law gives:

$$
I_{Y,\text{ph}} \;=\; \dfrac{V_{Y,\text{ph}}}{Z_s} \;=\; \dfrac{V_L}{\sqrt{3} \cdot Z_s}
$$

**Step 3 — Express the DOL per-phase current.**

For the same motor connected in delta (DOL starting), the per-phase voltage equals the line voltage:

$$
I_{\Delta,\text{ph}} \;=\; \dfrac{V_L}{Z_s}
$$

**Step 4 — Form the ratio of per-phase currents.**

$$
\dfrac{I_{Y,\text{ph}}}{I_{\Delta,\text{ph}}} \;=\; \dfrac{V_L / (\sqrt{3} \cdot Z_s)}{V_L / Z_s} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to line current ratio.**

In star, the line current equals the phase current: $I_{Y,L} = I_{Y,\text{ph}}$.
In delta, $I_{\Delta,L} = \sqrt{3} \cdot I_{\Delta,\text{ph}}$.
Hence, the line current ratio is:

$$
\dfrac{I_{Y,L}}{I_{\Delta,L}} \;=\; \dfrac{I_{Y,\text{ph}}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot I_{\Delta,\text{ph}}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{3}
$$

**Step 6 — Final Result.**

$$
\boxed{\,I_{Y,L} \;=\; \dfrac{I_{\Delta,L}}{3} \;\approx\; 0.333 \cdot I_{\Delta,L}\,}
$$

**Step 7 — Torque Derivation (since $T \propto V^2$):**

$$
\dfrac{T_Y}{T_\Delta} \;=\; \left(\dfrac{V_{Y,\text{ph}}}{V_{\Delta,\text{ph}}}\right)^2 \;=\; \left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)^2 \;=\; \dfrac{1}{3}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,T_Y \;=\; \dfrac{T_\Delta}{3} \;\approx\; 0.333 \cdot T_\Delta\,}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Both current and torque are reduced to **1/3** in star-delta starting. This is the **most frequently asked** derivation in KTU Module-3 questions.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Auto-Transformer Starting — Line Current and Torque

**Given:**
- Tap ratio $k = V_{\text{tap}} / V_{\text{Line}}$.
- DOL starting current = $I_{\Delta}$ (line), DOL starting torque = $T_{\Delta}$.

**Step 1 — Voltage across motor stator.**

The autotransformer delivers a reduced voltage to the motor stator:

$$
V_m \;=\; k \cdot V_{\text{Line}}
$$

**Step 2 — Motor current at reduced voltage.**

Since $T \propto V^2$ and $I \propto V$ for fixed impedance, the motor current becomes:

$$
I_m \;=\; k \cdot I_{\Delta}
$$

**Step 3 — Current transformation in autotransformer.**

In an ideal autotransformer, the current ratio is the inverse of the voltage ratio (KCL at primary):

$$
I_{\text{line}} \cdot V_{\text{Line}} \;=\; I_m \cdot V_{\text{tap}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{I_{\text{line}}}{I_m} \;=\; \dfrac{V_{\text{tap}}}{V_{\text{Line}}} \;=\; k
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $I_m$:**

$$
I_{\text{line}} \;=\; k \cdot I_m \;=\; k \cdot (k \cdot I_{\Delta}) \;=\; k^2 \cdot I_{\Delta}
$$

**Step 5 — Torque at reduced voltage:**

$$
T_{\text{start}} \;=\; k^2 \cdot T_{\Delta}
$$

**Step 6 — Comparative Analysis (Numerical Example):**

Let motor FLC = **50 A**, DOL starting current = **6 × 50 = 300 A**, DOL starting torque = **2 × Full-Load Torque**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
k &= 0.65 \quad (65\% \text{ tap}) \\
I_{\text{line}} &= (0.65)^2 \times 300 \;=\; 0.4225 \times 300 \;=\; 126.75 \text{ A} \\
T_{\text{start}} &= (0.65)^2 \times 2 \cdot T_{FL} \;=\; 0.4225 \times 2 \cdot T_{FL} \;=\; 0.845 \cdot T_{FL}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Compare with Star-Delta at same effective voltage ($k = 1/\sqrt{3} \approx 0.577$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{Y} &= (0.577)^2 \times 300 \;=\; 100 \text{ A} \\
T_Y &= (0.577)^2 \times 2 \cdot T_{FL} \;=\; 0.667 \cdot T_{FL}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** For the **same reduction in line current**, the auto-transformer provides **MORE starting torque** than the star-delta starter. This is the primary reason auto-transformer starters are preferred for **high-inertia / heavy-load** applications.

---

## 3.3 Selection Decision Matrix (Analytical)

The following matrix is used by KTU examination problems and is directly applicable to real engineering design.

| Selection Parameter | DOL | Star-Delta | Auto-Transformer | Soft Starter | VFD |
|---------------------|-----|------------|------------------|--------------|-----|
| Motor Power Range (kW) | 0.37 – 5 | 5 – 30 | 10 – 150 | 1 – 500 | 0.5 – 500+ |
| Starting Current (% FLC) | 600 – 800 | 200 – 250 | 150 – 400 | 100 – 300 | 100 – 150 |
| Starting Torque (% FLT) | 100 – 200 | 33 – 50 | 40 – 80 | 10 – 100 | 0 – 200 |
| Cost (Relative) | Very Low | Low | Medium | High | Very High |
| Speed Control | No | No | No | Limited (start/stop) | Yes (full) |
| Energy Saving | No | No | No | Marginal | High (V² profile) |
| Typical Application | Pumps, fans | Compressors | Crushers | Conveyors | HVAC, process |
| Maintenance | Low | Medium | Medium | Low | Medium |
| Harmonics Injected | None | None | None | High | High (needs filter) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Starter Selection Decision Flow

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the **decision logic** a designer follows when selecting a starter for a given motor-load combination.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Motor-Load Specification] --> B[Identify Motor Power Rating kW]
    B --> C{Power less than 5 kW}
    C -- Yes --> D[Select DOL Starter]
    C -- No --> E{Is starting torque greater than 50 percent FLT}
    E -- No --> F[Select Star-Delta Starter]
    E -- Yes --> G{Is fine speed control required}
    G -- Yes --> H[Select VFD]
    G -- No --> I{Is smooth acceleration required}
    I -- Yes --> J[Select Soft Starter SCR based]
    I -- No --> K[Select Auto-Transformer Starter]
    D --> L[Apply Protection: Overload Relay + MCB + Contactor]
    F --> L
    H --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[End: Finalize BOM and Wiring Diagram]
```

## 4.2 Schematic — Power and Control Circuit Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M[3-Phase Supply 415V 50Hz] --> MCB[Molded Case Circuit Breaker Short-Circuit Protection]
    MCB --> CT1[Main Contactor K1]
    CT1 --> OL[Thermal Overload Relay Bimetallic]
    OL --> STR[Starter Topology DOL / Y-D / Auto / Soft]
    STR --> MOT[3-Phase Induction Motor]
    
    MCB --> CC[Control Circuit 230V AC]
    CC --> PB1[Start Push Button NO]
    CC --> PB2[Stop Push Button NC]
    CC --> AUX[Auxiliary Contact of Contactor Holding]
    PB1 --> CR1[Control Relay Logic]
    PB2 --> CR1
    AUX --> CR1
    CR1 --> CT1
```

> [!TIP]
> **Note for KTU students:** The Mermaid block above maps the **functional architecture** of a motor starter power and control circuit. In the KTU board exam, drawing a standard schematic with all protective devices (MCB, contactor, overload relay, start/stop push buttons) fetches **full marks (7/7)** in Part-B questions on starters.

## 4.3 Comparative Block — Soft Starter Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SU[3-Phase Supply] --> R1[Thyristor Pair SCR1 Phase R]
    SU --> S1[Thyristor Pair SCR2 Phase Y]
    SU --> T1[Thyristor Pair SCR3 Phase B]
    R1 --> JCT[Common Junction to Motor]
    S1 --> JCT
    T1 --> JCT
    JCT --> M2[3-Phase Induction Motor]
    
    R1 --> CTR[Microcontroller / DSP Control Unit]
    S1 --> CTR
    T1 --> CTR
    CTR --> DSP[Display and Keypad HMI]
    CTR --> SIG[Analog and Digital I/O Signals to PLC]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
**State the main advantage of an auto-transformer starter over a star-delta starter for the same starting current reduction. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> The auto-transformer starter is preferred over a star-delta starter because **for the same reduction in starting line current, the auto-transformer provides a higher starting torque**. The relationship is:
>
> $$\dfrac{T_{\text{auto}}}{T_{Y}} \;=\; \dfrac{k^2 \cdot T_{\Delta}}{(1/3) \cdot T_{\Delta}} \;=\; 3k^2$$
>
> For typical tap $k = 0.65$, $T_{\text{auto}} = 3 \times 0.4225 = 1.27 \times T_Y$, which is **27 % higher torque** than star-delta. Hence, auto-transformer starters are ideal for **high-inertia heavy loads** like crushers and large compressors where starting torque demand is high.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Sample Paper]**
**List any three criteria used in the selection of a starter for a three-phase induction motor. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The three primary criteria for selecting a starter are:

1. **Motor Power Rating (kW / HP):** DOL is used for motors ≤ 5 kW; star-delta for 5–30 kW; auto-transformer for > 30 kW.
2. **Starting Torque Requirement:** Loads with high starting torque (compressors) need auto-transformer or VFD, while light loads (fans) can use star-delta.
3. **Permissible Starting Current / Voltage Dip:** If the utility allows only a 15 % voltage dip, the starter must reduce the inrush to within that limit.

> Additional criteria: **Number of starts per hour**, **cost**, **availability of space**, and **harmonics limit** (per IEEE 519).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified]** A 3-phase, 415 V, 50 Hz, 15 kW induction motor has a full-load current of **28 A** and a DOL starting current of **6 × FLC = 168 A**. The supply authority restricts the starting current to a maximum of **60 A**.

**(a)** Select a suitable starter and justify. (7 Marks)
**(b)** For the selected starter, calculate the starting torque as a percentage of the full-load torque, given that DOL starting torque is **1.8 × FLT**. (7 Marks)

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Selection and Justification (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Compute required current reduction ratio.**

$$
k^2 \;=\; \dfrac{I_{\text{allowed}}}{I_{\text{DOL}}} \;=\; \dfrac{60}{168} \;=\; 0.357
$$

$$
k \;=\; \sqrt{0.357} \;=\; 0.598 \;\approx\; 0.60
$$

**Step 2: Identify available starters:**

- **DOL Starter:** Starting current = 168 A → **NOT acceptable** (exceeds 60 A).
- **Star-Delta Starter:** Starting current = $168 / 3 = 56$ A → **Acceptable**, but $k = 1/\sqrt{3} = 0.577$, giving $k^2 = 0.333$. Since $0.333 < 0.357$, the resulting current is lower (56 A), which is fine.
- **Auto-Transformer Starter:** Tap selection flexibility allows $k = 0.65$ (closest standard tap to 0.60) → Starting current = $0.65^2 \times 168 = 71$ A, which slightly exceeds 60 A. Tap $k = 0.55$ gives $0.55^2 \times 168 = 50.8$ A, which is acceptable.

**Step 3: Decision Logic.**

- Star-delta gives 56 A (within 60 A limit) and is the **most economical** for a 15 kW motor.
- Auto-transformer provides **higher torque** but costs more.

**Conclusion:** **Select Star-Delta Starter** for this 15 kW motor. (Auto-transformer could be an alternative if torque is critical.)

**Part (b) — Starting Torque Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Star-Delta starting torque formula.**

$$
T_{Y} \;=\; \dfrac{T_{\Delta}}{3}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute $T_\Delta = 1.8 \times T_{FL}$.**

$$
T_{Y} \;=\; \dfrac{1.8 \cdot T_{FL}}{3} \;=\; 0.6 \cdot T_{FL}
$$

**Step 3: Express as percentage.**

$$
T_{Y} \;=\; 60\% \text{ of Full-Load Torque}
$$

**Step 4: Verify torque adequacy.**

If the load requires ≥ 60 % FLT at start (typical for centrifugal pumps, fans), the star-delta starter is sufficient. If the load requires > 60 % FLT, an auto-transformer at $k = 0.65$ must be chosen, giving $T_{\text{start}} = 0.65^2 \times 1.8 \times T_{FL} = 0.76 \times T_{FL} = 76\%$ of FLT.

**Final Answer:** $T_Y = 60\%$ of FLT (Star-Delta) or $T_{\text{start}} = 76\%$ of FLT (Auto-transformer at 65% tap).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified]** Discuss the **selection criteria for starters** used with 3-phase induction motors. Compare the **DOL, Star-Delta, Auto-Transformer, and Soft Starter** types in a tabular form based on at least **six parameters**.

**(a)** Explain the working of an **auto-transformer starter** with a neat sketch and derive the expressions for **starting current** and **starting torque** in terms of the tap ratio. (7 Marks)
**(b)** A **22 kW, 400 V, 50 Hz, 6-pole** induction motor has a full-load slip of **4 %**. The motor is started using an auto-transformer starter with a **65 % tap**. Calculate the **(i)** synchronous speed, **(ii)** full-load speed, and **(iii)** starting torque as a percentage of FLT, given DOL starting torque = **2.0 × FLT**. (7 Marks)

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Auto-Transformer Starter Working & Derivation (7 Marks):**

*Working:* The auto-transformer starter consists of a **3-phase tapped autotransformer** with tappings at **50 %, 65 %, and 80 %** of the line voltage. During starting, the motor is connected to the selected tap, and reduced voltage is applied. After the motor accelerates to about **80 % of rated speed**, the autotransformer is **bypassed** (using a changeover contactor), and the motor receives full line voltage.

*Derivation:*

Let $k = V_{\text{tap}} / V_{\text{Line}}$.

Motor current (reduced):

$$
I_m \;=\; k \cdot I_{\Delta}
$$

Line current (drawn from supply, by transformer action):

$$
I_{\text{line}} \;=\; k \cdot I_m \;=\; k^2 \cdot I_{\Delta}
$$

Starting torque (since $T \propto V^2$):

$$
T_{\text{start}} \;=\; k^2 \cdot T_{\Delta}
$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Synchronous speed.**

$$
N_s \;=\; \dfrac{120 \cdot f}{P} \;=\; \dfrac{120 \times 50}{6} \;=\; 1000 \text{ RPM}
$$

**Step 2: Full-load speed.**

$$
N_r \;=\; N_s \cdot (1 - s) \;=\; 1000 \times (1 - 0.04) \;=\; 1000 \times 0.96 \;=\; 960 \text{ RPM}
$$

**Step 3: Auto-transformer starting torque.**

Given: $T_\Delta = 2.0 \cdot T_{FL}$, tap $k = 0.65$.

$$
T_{\text{start}} \;=\; k^2 \cdot T_{\Delta} \;=\; (0.65)^2 \times 2.0 \cdot T_{FL}
$$

$$
T_{\text{start}} \;=\; 0.4225 \times 2.0 \cdot T_{FL} \;=\; 0.845 \cdot T_{FL}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,T_{\text{start}} \;=\; 84.5\% \text{ of Full-Load Torque}\,}
$$

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Mistakes in KTU Board Exams (Module 3 - Starters):**
> 
> 1. **Formula Confusion (–2 Marks):** Writing $I_{Y} = I_{\Delta}/\sqrt{3}$ instead of $I_{Y} = I_{\Delta}/3$. The **3** is for line current (after applying the $\sqrt{3}$ factor in delta), not $\sqrt{3}$. Always derive step-by-step.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the Torque Relation (–1 Mark):** When asked about starting current, students often ignore the **torque reduction** as a side effect. Always mention that $T \propto V^2$.
> 
> 3. **Wrong Starter Justification (–2 Marks):** Stating "DOL is best because it's cheap" — this loses marks. Justification must include **motor rating, current limit, torque demand, and application**.
> 
> 4. **Missing Protection Devices (–2 Marks):** In schematic questions, omitting the **overload relay** or **short-circuit protection (MCB/MPCB)** is a common error. Always include all three: **Contactor + Overload Relay + Short-Circuit Protection (MCB)**.
> 
> 5. **Mixing Soft Starter with VFD (–1 Mark):** Soft starters are for **start/stop only**; VFDs provide **continuous speed control**. Examiners penalize this conceptual blur.
> 
> 6. **Unit Errors (–1 Mark):** Writing $N_s$ in rps instead of RPM, or omitting units for slip (dimensionless).

---

## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Selection of Starters:**

- ☐ **DOL Starter** is the simplest, gives **100 % starting torque** but **6–8 × FLC inrush**; used up to **5 kW** only.
- ☐ **Star-Delta Starter** reduces starting current and torque to **1/3** of DOL values; motor stator must be **delta-rated**.
- ☐ **Auto-Transformer Starter** offers **flexible tap selection** (50 %, 65 %, 80 %); line current and torque both reduce by **$k^2$** factor.
- ☐ **For the same starting current, auto-transformer provides HIGHER starting torque** than star-delta — preferred for heavy loads.
- ☐ **Soft Starter** uses **SCRs** for phase-angle voltage ramping; ideal for **smooth acceleration** without speed control.
- ☐ **VFD** = **Soft start + speed control + energy savings**; most expensive; introduces **harmonics** (use line filter per IEEE 519).
- ☐ **Selection Criteria (in priority order):** Motor rating (kW) → Starting current limit (utility) → Starting torque demand → Load type → Number of starts/hr → Cost.
- ☐ **Synchronous Speed Formula:** $N_s = 120f/P$. Slip: $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$.
- ☐ **Stator Copper Loss** during reduced-voltage starting is **$k^2$ times** the DOL loss (for auto-transformer); **$1/3$ times** for star-delta.
- ☐ **Protection Triad:** Every starter MUST have (i) **Short-circuit protection (MCB/MPCB)**, (ii) **Overload relay**, (iii) **Contactor for switching**.
- ☐ **Key Standards:** IS 13947-4-1 / IEC 60947-4-1 (Contactors & Starters), IS 325 (Motors), IEEE 519 (Harmonics).
- ☐ **Threshold Voltage Dip Allowed by Indian Utilities:** Typically **15 %** of nominal during motor starting.
- ☐ **Soft Starters and VFDs** must be **bypassed** in case of harmonic-sensitive loads; or **line reactors / filters** must be installed.
- ☐ **Open-Transition vs Closed-Transition Star-Delta:** Open (break-before-make) has a momentary open circuit; Closed (make-before-break) is smoother but costlier.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cable and Switchgears
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT752 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cable and Switchgears

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Power Cables — Formal Definition

A **power cable** is an assembly of one or more electrical conductors (typically copper or aluminium) enclosed within a protective sheath, designed for the safe transmission and distribution of electrical energy at medium or low voltages. As per **IS 7098 (for XLPE cables)** and **IS 1554 (for PVC cables)**, a cable is a complete flexible high-capacity conductor that can be laid underground, in trenches, on cable trays, or in air, as opposed to overhead bare conductors.

### 1.1.1 Cable Construction (Layer-by-Layer from Inside Out)

| Layer | Function | Typical Material |
|---|---|---|
| **Conductor** | Carries current | Annealed Copper / Aluminium |
| **Conductor Screen** | Smooths electric stress | Semi-conducting tape |
| **Insulation** | Withstands voltage stress | **XLPE**, **PVC**, **EPR**, **PILC** |
| **Insulation Screen** | Earths leakage currents | Semi-conducting layer |
| **Metallic Screen / Armour** | Carries fault current + mechanical protection | Copper tape / Steel wire |
| **Bedding** | Cushions inner layers | PVC / Hessian tape |
| **Serving / Outer Sheath** | Environmental protection | PVC / HDPE |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must remember the **two-letter cable designation code**, e.g., `A2XFY` (Aluminium conductor, XLPE insulated, Steel strip armoured, PVC sheathed — single core) versus `A2XWY` (Aluminium, XLPE, Steel wire armoured, PVC sheathed — multi-core).

## 1.2 Switchgear — Formal Definition

**Switchgear** is the collective term encompassing all devices used for **switching, controlling, protecting, and isolating** electrical circuits and equipment. As per **IS 13947 / IEC 60947** (for low-voltage switchgear) and **IEC 62271** (for high-voltage switchgear), switchgear ensures operational safety, fault interruption, and reliable power distribution.

### 1.2.1 Major Categories of Switchgear

- **LV Switchgear (≤ 1000 V AC):** MCB, MCCB, ACB, Contactors, Fuses, SFUs
- **MV Switchgear (1 kV – 36 kV):** VCB, SF₆ breakers, Oil circuit breakers
- **HV Switchgear (> 36 kV):** SF₆ GIS, Air blast breakers, Vacuum interrupters

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Plumbing System

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of an electrical distribution system like a building's plumbing network.
> - **Cables** are the **pipes** carrying water (current) from a reservoir (source) to homes (loads). Thicker pipes carry more water; similarly, **larger conductor cross-sections** carry more current.
> - **Switchgear** is the **valve and safety valve system** — normal valves (isolators) control flow, while safety valves (circuit breakers) slam shut the instant a burst (short circuit) is detected.
> - **Fuses** are like one-time-use rupture discs that sacrifice themselves to save the rest of the system.

## 1.4 Key Physical & Standard Metrics

- **Standard Reference Ambient Temperature:** **30°C** (for air), **25°C** (for ground) — as per IS 3961.
- **Standard Soil Thermal Resistivity (g):** **1.5 K·m/W** (tropical Indian conditions).
- **Depth of Laying (standard):** **0.75 m** to **1.0 m** for LT cables; **1.0 m to 1.2 m** for HT cables.
- **Power Frequency Withstand Voltage (1 min):** **3 kV** for 1.1 kV grade XLPE cables.
- **Maximum permissible conductor temperature:** **90°C** for XLPE; **70°C** for PVC.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Cables Need Multiple Layers — The Physics Behind It

When current flows through a conductor, two physical phenomena occur:
1. **Electric Stress Concentration** at the conductor surface (higher for small radii, multi-core geometry)
2. **Heat Dissipation** through surrounding media (governed by thermal Ohm's law)

The **insulation** is graded such that the dielectric withstand stress ($E_{max}$) at the conductor surface remains within safe limits:

$$E_{max} = \frac{V}{r \cdot \ln(R/r)}$$

where:
- $V$ = phase voltage (V)
- $r$ = conductor radius (mm)
- $R$ = insulation outer radius (mm)

> **Why this matters:** The **insulation screen (semiconducting layer)** ensures no air pockets form between insulation and metallic sheath, which would otherwise cause **partial discharge** and eventual insulation failure.

## 2.2 Cable Selection Criteria — The Engineering Logic

A cable is selected by satisfying **four simultaneous constraints**:

1. **Current Carrying Capacity (Ampacity):** $I_b \leq I_z$ (Installed load current ≤ cable rated current)
2. **Voltage Drop:** $V_d \leq 3\%$ (lighting) or $\leq 5\%$ (power) of declared voltage
3. **Short Circuit Withstand:** $I_{sc}^2 \cdot t \leq K^2 \cdot S^2$ (adiabatic equation)
4. **Earth Fault Loop Impedance:** $Z_s \leq U_0 / I_a$ (disconnection time compliance)

## 2.3 Switchgear Operating Principle — How a Breaker Interrupts Faults

A circuit breaker must extinguish the **arc** drawn between its contacts during contact separation. Arc extinction relies on:
- **Lengthening the arc** (arc chutes with magnetic blast)
- **Cooling the arc** (oil, SF₆ gas, vacuum)
- **De-ionizing the arc path** (gas blast)
- **Zero-current crossing** (in AC, natural current zero every half cycle = 10 ms at 50 Hz)

### 2.3.1 Ratings Every KTU Student Must Memorize

- **Breaking Capacity (in kA):** Maximum rms fault current a breaker can interrupt safely.
- **Making Capacity (in kA peak):** Typically **2.55 × Breaking Capacity** (asymmetrical peak factor for 50 Hz).
- **Short-Time Withstand (I²t):** Thermal capacity of breaker for 1s/3s fault duration.
- **Operating Duty:** O – 3 min – CO – 3 min – CO (as per IEC 62271).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | DC Resistance of Conductor | $R_{dc} = \rho \cdot L / A$ | $\Omega$ | $\rho_{Cu} = 1.72 \times 10^{-8}$ $\Omega\cdot m$ |
| 2 | AC Resistance | $R_{ac} = R_{dc} \cdot (1 + y_s + y_p)$ | $\Omega$ | $y_s$ = skin, $y_p$ = proximity effect |
| 3 | Cable Current Rating (derated) | $I_z = I_{tab} \cdot C_g \cdot C_a \cdot C_s$ | A | Product of derating factors |
| 4 | Voltage Drop (3-$\phi$, 50 Hz) | $V_d = \sqrt{3} \cdot I \cdot L \cdot (R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)$ | V | Per phase voltage drop |
| 5 | % Voltage Drop | $\%V_d = (V_d / V) \cdot 100$ | % | Limit: 3% (lighting), 5% (power) |
| 6 | Short Circuit Current (at load end) | $I_{sc} = V / (Z_s + Z_c)$ | kA | $Z_c$ = cable impedance |
| 7 | Adiabatic Short Circuit Withstand | $t = (K \cdot S / I_{sc})^2$ | s | $K_{Cu}=143$, $K_{Al}=94$ (XLPE, 1s) |
| 8 | Symmetrical Breaking Capacity | $I_b = I_{sc} \cdot e^{-R/X \cdot \tan^{-1}(X/R)}$ | kA | Decaying DC component |
| 9 | Peak Making Current | $I_{peak} = 2.55 \cdot I_{sc,rms}$ | kA peak | Standard factor |
| 10 | Earth Fault Loop Impedance | $Z_s = Z_e + (R_1 + R_2) \cdot L$ | $\Omega$ | $Z_e$ = external earth fault |
| 11 | Earth Electrode Resistance | $R = \rho / (2 \cdot \pi \cdot L)$ | $\Omega$ | Rod electrode approximation |
| 12 | Power Loss in Cable (3-$\phi$) | $P_{loss} = 3 \cdot I^2 \cdot R \cdot L \cdot 10^{-3}$ | kW | Copper loss per phase |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbol Convention Reminder:** In the table above, $\vert$ for absolute value is written as `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity. The KTU 2024 paper may use $|Z_s|$ form; both notations are equivalent.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

These calculations drive **every industrial, commercial, and residential electrical design** in India:
- **Cable sizing** ensures the **Tata Steel Jamshedpur plant's** 11 kV feeders don't melt during faults.
- **Switchgear selection** ensures **KSERC-regulated** safety in Kerala's 110 kV substations during monsoon faults.
- **Breaking capacity** calculation prevents catastrophic **arc-flash incidents** in switchboards (a leading cause of industrial electrical fatalities).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Symmetrical Short Circuit Current at the Far End of a Cable

**Given:** A 3-phase, 11 kV / 433 V transformer (500 kVA, $Z = 5\%$) feeds a 50 m long, 3-core 95 mm² XLPE aluminium cable ($R = 0.32$ $\Omega$/km, $X = 0.07$ $\Omega$/km). Find the short circuit current at the cable far end.

### Step 1: Calculate Full Load Current of Transformer

$$I_{FL} = \frac{S}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V} = \frac{500 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times 433}$$

$$I_{FL} = \frac{500000}{750.03} = 666.67 \text{ A}$$

### Step 2: Calculate Source Impedance in Ohms

The percentage impedance $Z\% = 5\%$ means the short circuit current is $100/5 = 20$ times full load current.

$$I_{sc,source} = 20 \times 666.67 = 13333.33 \text{ A} \approx 13.33 \text{ kA}$$

To express as impedance:

$$Z_{source} = \frac{V_{LV}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot I_{sc,source}} = \frac{433}{\sqrt{3} \times 13333.33}$$

$$Z_{source} = \frac{433}{23094.01} = 0.01875 \text{ }\Omega$$

### Step 3: Calculate Cable Impedance for 50 m Length

Cable resistance:

$$R_{cable} = 0.32 \times (50/1000) = 0.016 \text{ }\Omega$$

Cable reactance:

$$X_{cable} = 0.07 \times (50/1000) = 0.0035 \text{ }\Omega$$

Total cable impedance:

$$Z_{cable} = \sqrt{R^2 + X^2} = \sqrt{0.016^2 + 0.0035^2}$$

$$Z_{cable} = \sqrt{0.000256 + 0.0000122} = \sqrt{0.0002682}$$

$$Z_{cable} = 0.01638 \text{ }\Omega$$

### Step 4: Calculate Total Fault Loop Impedance

$$Z_{total} = Z_{source} + Z_{cable} = 0.01875 + 0.01638$$

$$Z_{total} = 0.03513 \text{ }\Omega$$

### Step 5: Calculate Short Circuit Current at Cable End

$$I_{sc,end} = \frac{V_{LV}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot Z_{total}} = \frac{433}{\sqrt{3} \times 0.03513}$$

$$I_{sc,end} = \frac{433}{0.06085} = 7115.85 \text{ A} \approx 7.12 \text{ kA}$$

### Step 6: Result Interpretation

> **Engineering Conclusion:** The downstream circuit breaker must have a **breaking capacity of at least 7.5 kA** (next standard rating above 7.12 kA). Selecting a 10 kA MCCB would provide a **40% safety margin**, satisfying KTU examiner expectations.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Cable Cross-Section from Voltage Drop Constraint

**Given:** A 3-$\phi$, 415 V, 50 Hz motor load of 75 kW at 0.85 p.f. lagging is located 120 m from the panel. Permissible voltage drop = 3%. Find minimum cable size (Cu, XLPE, $X = 0.08$ $\Omega$/km).

### Step 1: Calculate Full Load Current

$$I = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V \cdot \cos\phi} = \frac{75000}{\sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 0.85}$$

$$I = \frac{75000}{611.55} = 122.62 \text{ A}$$

### Step 2: Calculate Allowable Voltage Drop (Volts)

$$V_{d,allow} = 0.03 \times 415 = 12.45 \text{ V}$$

### Step 3: Use Voltage Drop Formula and Solve for $R$

$$V_d = \sqrt{3} \cdot I \cdot L \cdot (R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)$$

Substituting values:

$$12.45 = \sqrt{3} \times 122.62 \times 0.120 \times (R \times 0.85 + 0.08 \times 0.1)$$

$$12.45 = 25.50 \times (0.85R + 0.008)$$

$$0.85R + 0.008 = \frac{12.45}{25.50} = 0.4882$$

$$0.85R = 0.4802$$

$$R = 0.5650 \text{ }\Omega$$

This is the **maximum total resistance**. Since $R = r \cdot L$ where $r$ is per km:

$$r_{max} = 0.5650 / 0.120 = 4.71 \text{ }\Omega/\text{km}$$

### Step 4: Compute Required Cross-Section

For copper, $R = \rho L / A$:

$$A = \frac{\rho \cdot 1000}{r_{max}} = \frac{0.0172 \times 1000}{4.71}$$

$$A = 3.65 \text{ mm}^2$$

> **However**, the **current carrying capacity** constraint dominates here. From IS 3961 Part V, a 3-core XLPE Cu cable needs **25 mm²** to carry 122.62 A (3 runs in parallel of 6 mm² not allowed by IS; minimum per run = 16 mm² derated).

### Step 5: Final Selection

- **Ampacity-driven selection:** 25 mm² Cu XLPE 3-core cable (rating ≈ 134 A in air).
- **Voltage drop check:** $V_d = \sqrt{3} \times 122.62 \times 0.120 \times (0.727/1000 \times 0.85 + 0.0001) = 2.85\%$ ✓ (within 3%).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Trick:** Always check **both** ampacity AND voltage drop — the **larger** size governs. Many students lose 2–3 marks by skipping the ampacity check.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Adiabatic Short Circuit Withstand of Cable

**Given:** A 3-core 95 mm² XLPE Cu cable must withstand a fault current of 12 kA. Find the maximum permissible fault duration.

### Step 1: Recall the Adiabatic Equation

$$t = \left(\frac{K \cdot S}{I_{sc}}\right)^2$$

where for XLPE Cu cable: **K = 143** (initial temp 90°C, final 250°C), $S$ = 95 mm².

### Step 2: Substitute Values

$$t = \left(\frac{143 \times 95}{12000}\right)^2$$

$$t = \left(\frac{13585}{12000}\right)^2 = (1.132)^2$$

$$t = 1.281 \text{ seconds}$$

### Step 3: Compare with Breaker Clearing Time

Standard MV breaker clearing time = 3 cycles = **0.06 s** (much less than 1.28 s) → **Cable is SAFE**.

> **Rule of thumb:** If $t_{calc} \geq 0.1$ s, the cable is considered short-circuit rated. If the breaker operates faster (typical 0.05 s), the actual energy let-through is much lower.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Switchgear Selection Logic

| Step | Question to Ask | If YES → Action | If NO → Next Step |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Is system voltage > 1 kV? | Select MV switchgear (VCB/SF₆) | Go to Step 2 |
| 2 | Is load current < 100 A? | Use MCB | Go to Step 3 |
| 3 | Is short circuit current < 10 kA? | Use MCCB 10 kA | Go to Step 4 |
| 4 | Is breaking capacity > 25 kA? | Use ACB 25–50 kA | Use special HV breaker |
| 5 | Is frequent operation required? | Use SF₆ or VCB (not oil) | Use oil/Minimum Oil CB |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State and explain the function of any THREE layers of an underground power cable.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Conductor (1 mark):** Carries the actual current. Made of stranded Cu or Al to provide flexibility and reduce skin effect.

2. **Insulation (1 mark):** Provides dielectric strength to withstand system voltage. XLPE (cross-linked polyethylene) is preferred due to 90°C operating temperature, low dielectric loss, and high impulse strength.

3. **Metallic Sheath / Armour (1 mark):** Carries capacitive charging current and provides earth fault return path. Also provides mechanical protection during laying.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the term "Breaking Capacity" of a circuit breaker. State its standard unit.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Breaking Capacity (2 marks):** It is the maximum rms value of fault current (symmetrical or asymmetrical, depending on the standard) that a circuit breaker can interrupt safely at a specified recovery voltage and power factor, without any damage to itself or the system.

It is expressed in **kA (kiloamperes)** (0.5 marks) and is determined under the standard **operating duty cycle O – 3 min – CO – 3 min – CO** as per IEC 62271 (0.5 marks).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Expected]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the construction of an 11 kV XLPE underground power cable with a neat sketch. List any FIVE advantages of XLPE over PVC insulation.

**(b) [7 marks]** A 3-phase, 11 kV / 433 V, 500 kVA transformer with 5% impedance feeds a 100 m long 3-core 70 mm² aluminium XLPE cable ($R = 0.443$ $\Omega$/km, $X = 0.08$ $\Omega$/km). Calculate:
   (i) The short circuit current at the far end of the cable.
   (ii) The percentage voltage drop when feeding a balanced load of 400 A at 0.8 p.f. lagging.

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) Solution:

**Construction (4 marks):**
- **Conductor:** Stranded aluminium (since Al is asked), compacted circular, with conductor screen.
- **Conductor Screen:** Extruded semi-conducting compound.
- **Insulation:** XLPE, dry-cured, with thickness as per IS 7098 Part 2.
- **Insulation Screen:** Semi-conducting + metallic (copper tape).
- **Inner Sheath:** PVC bedding.
- **Armour:** Galvanised steel wire (multi-core) for mechanical strength.
- **Outer Sheath:** PVC, fire-retardant grade.

**Five Advantages of XLPE over PVC (3 marks — 0.6 each):**
1. Higher continuous operating temperature (90°C vs 70°C).
2. Higher short-circuit temperature (250°C vs 160°C).
3. Lower dielectric loss (tan δ = 0.0005 vs 0.008 for PVC).
4. Higher current carrying capacity for same cross-section.
5. Longer service life (40+ years vs 25 years).
6. Better moisture resistance.

#### Part (b) Solution:

**(i) Short Circuit Current at Cable End (4 marks):**

Transformer full load current on LV side:

$$I_{FL} = \frac{500 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times 433} = 666.67 \text{ A}$$

Source short circuit current:

$$I_{sc,src} = \frac{100}{5} \times 666.67 = 13333.33 \text{ A}$$

Source impedance:

$$Z_{source} = \frac{433}{\sqrt{3} \times 13333.33} = 0.01875 \text{ }\Omega$$

Cable impedance for 100 m:

$$R_c = 0.443 \times 0.1 = 0.0443 \text{ }\Omega$$

$$X_c = 0.08 \times 0.1 = 0.008 \text{ }\Omega$$

$$Z_c = \sqrt{0.0443^2 + 0.008^2} = \sqrt{0.001962 + 0.000064} = 0.0450 \text{ }\Omega$$

Total impedance:

$$Z_{total} = 0.01875 + 0.0450 = 0.06375 \text{ }\Omega$$

Short circuit current at far end:

$$I_{sc,end} = \frac{433}{\sqrt{3} \times 0.06375} = \frac{433}{0.1104} = 3922.0 \text{ A} \approx 3.92 \text{ kA}$$

**(ii) Percentage Voltage Drop (3 marks):**

$$V_d = \sqrt{3} \cdot I \cdot L \cdot (R_c \cos\phi + X_c \sin\phi)$$

$$\cos\phi = 0.8, \sin\phi = 0.6$$

$$V_d = \sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 0.1 \times (0.0443 \times 0.8 + 0.008 \times 0.6)$$

$$V_d = 69.28 \times (0.03544 + 0.0048) = 69.28 \times 0.04024$$

$$V_d = 2.788 \text{ V}$$

$$\% V_d = \frac{2.788}{433} \times 100 = 0.644\%$$

> **Result:** This is well within the 5% permissible limit for power loads (3 marks).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Compare MCB, MCCB, ACB, and VCB based on: voltage class, breaking capacity, operating mechanism, and typical applications.

**(b) [7 marks]** Derive the adiabatic equation for the short-circuit current withstand of a cable. A 50 mm² XLPE Cu cable (K = 143) is subjected to a fault current of 5 kA. Calculate the maximum fault duration the cable can withstand.

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) Solution: Comparison Table (7 marks)

| Parameter | MCB | MCCB | ACB | VCB |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Voltage Class (1 m)** | ≤ 415 V | ≤ 690 V | ≤ 690 V | 3.3 kV – 36 kV |
| **Breaking Capacity (1 m)** | 3 kA – 10 kA | 10 kA – 100 kA | 25 kA – 100 kA | 16 kA – 40 kA |
| **Operating Mechanism (2 m)** | Thermal magnetic | Thermal magnetic / Electronic | Stored energy (spring/motor) | Stored energy spring |
| **Trip Setting (1 m)** | Fixed | Adjustable | Adjustable | Adjustable |
| **Typical Application (2 m)** | Domestic distribution | Motor feeders, sub-distribution | Main LV incomers | 11 kV / 22 kV substation feeders |

#### Part (b) Solution: Adiabatic Derivation (7 marks)

**Derivation (3 marks):**

Consider a cable carrying fault current $I_{sc}$ for time $t$ seconds. The heat generated is:

$$H = I_{sc}^2 \cdot R \cdot t \text{ joules}$$

The temperature rise $\Delta\theta$ in the conductor relates to heat as:

$$H = m \cdot s \cdot \Delta\theta = (A \cdot L \cdot d) \cdot s \cdot \Delta\theta$$

where:
- $A$ = cross-section (m²)
- $L$ = length (m)
- $d$ = density (kg/m³)
- $s$ = specific heat (J/kg·K)

Resistance: $R = \rho L / A$

Equating heat generated to heat absorbed:

$$I_{sc}^2 \cdot \frac{\rho L}{A} \cdot t = A \cdot L \cdot d \cdot s \cdot \Delta\theta$$

Rearranging:

$$\left(\frac{I_{sc}}{A}\right)^2 \cdot t = \frac{d \cdot s \cdot \Delta\theta}{\rho}$$

Define the constant: $K^2 = \dfrac{d \cdot s \cdot \Delta\theta}{\rho}$

This gives the **adiabatic equation**:

$$\boxed{I_{sc}^2 \cdot t = K^2 \cdot S^2}$$

**Numerical Calculation (4 marks):**

Given: $S = 50$ mm², $K = 143$ (XLPE Cu), $I_{sc} = 5000$ A.

$$t = \left(\frac{K \cdot S}{I_{sc}}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{143 \times 50}{5000}\right)^2$$

$$t = \left(\frac{7150}{5000}\right)^2 = (1.43)^2$$

$$t = 2.0449 \text{ s}$$

> **Conclusion:** The cable can safely withstand a 5 kA short-circuit for approximately **2.04 seconds**. If the upstream breaker clears in 0.1 s, the cable has a **20× safety margin** in time domain (3 marks for numerical + 1 mark for comment).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose 3–7 Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert % impedance to ohms** — A $Z\% = 5\%$ is meaningless on its own; you MUST use $I_{sc,base} = I_{FL} / (Z\%/100)$. Loss of 2 marks.
>
> 2. **Using line-to-line voltage instead of phase voltage** in voltage drop formula for single-phase — and vice versa for 3-phase. The formula $V_d = \sqrt{3} I L (R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)$ is for 3-phase only.
>
> 3. **Mixing up K values:** For PVC-insulated Cu cable, $K = 115$ (not 143). For XLPE Cu, $K = 143$. For XLPE Al, $K = 94$. Wrong K = -1 to -2 marks.
>
> 4. **Not including cable impedance** in the fault loop calculation when the cable is long (> 50 m). Examiner expects $Z_{total} = Z_{source} + Z_{cable}$.
>
> 5. **Selecting next standard size incorrectly:** If calculation gives 17.2 mm², you must round up to **25 mm²**, not 16 mm². Always pick the next higher standard size.
>
> 6. **Skipping derating factors** for grouped cables, ambient temperature, soil resistivity. If 4 cables in a trench, $C_g = 0.65$ — examiners specifically test this.
>
> 7. **Writing 'breaking capacity' without units** (kA) — KTU paper evaluators deduct 0.5 marks for missing units in derivations.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 3**

- [ ] **Cable Designation Codes:** A2XFY (Al, XLPE, Flat steel strip armoured, PVC sheathed); A2XWY (Al, XLPE, Steel wire armoured, PVC sheathed); 2XFY / 2XWY (Cu, XLPE versions).

- [ ] **XLPE K-values for Adiabatic:** $K_{Cu, XLPE} = 143$, $K_{Al, XLPE} = 94$ (initial 90°C, final 250°C, t ≤ 5s).

- [ ] **Standard Reference Conditions (IS 3961):** Ambient air 30°C, ground 25°C, depth 1.0 m, soil resistivity 1.5 K·m/W.

- [ ] **Voltage Drop Limits:** 3% for lighting, 5% for power, 5% combined for sub-circuits (as per IS 732).

- [ ] **Breaking Capacity → Making Capacity Conversion:** $I_{peak, make} = 2.55 \times I_{sc, rms, break}$ (50 Hz asymmetry factor, IEC standard).

- [ ] **Switchgear Selection Hierarchy:** MCB (≤ 100 A, 10 kA) → MCCB (≤ 1600 A, 100 kA) → ACB (≤ 6300 A, 100 kA) → VCB (MV range).

- [ ] **Standard Short-Time Withstand:** 25 kA for 1 s, 40 kA for 3 s are typical for MV switchboards.

- [ ] **Cable Ampacity Derating:** $I_z = I_{tab} \times C_g \times C_a \times C_s \times C_d$ (grouping, ambient, soil, depth).

- [ ] **Earth Fault Loop:** $Z_s \leq U_0 / I_a$ (BS 7671 / IS 732) — must disconnect within 5 s for TN systems and 0.4 s for final circuits ≤ 32 A.

- [ ] **Remember the 4-Step Cable Selection:** Ampacity → Voltage Drop → Short Circuit Withstand → Earth Loop Impedance. The LARGEST size wins.

- [ ] **Why VCBs are preferred over OCBs in modern Indian substations:** No fire hazard, no oil maintenance, silent operation, 10,000+ mechanical operations.

- [ ] **Cable Drum Handling:** Maximum recommended pull = 5 × cable weight; minimum bend radius = 12 × OD for XLPE multi-core (per IS 1255).

- [ ] **Armour Resistance Test Value:** Should be < 1 ohm per km for fault loop integrity (post-installation testing).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Factor Improvement - kVAR Calculation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT752 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Power Factor Improvement - kVAR Calculation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Power Factor (PF)** is defined as the cosine of the angle between the supply voltage and the line current in an AC electrical system, and is mathematically expressed as the ratio of **Real Power (kW)** to **Apparent Power (kVA)**.

$$\cos\phi \;=\; \frac{P_{\text{real}}}{S_{\text{apparent}}} \;=\; \frac{\text{kW}}{\text{kVA}}$$

**Power Factor Improvement** is the deliberate engineering process of reducing the reactive power demand (kVAR) drawn from the supply by installing **shunt-connected capacitors** (or synchronous condensers) at the load end, so that the overall power factor seen by the utility rises toward unity (1.0), without altering the actual useful working power (kW) consumed by the load.

The **kVAR Calculation** refers to the precise quantitative evaluation of the size (rating) of the capacitor bank that must be installed to achieve a target power factor, given the original (uncorrected) power factor, the target (corrected) power factor, and the active load in kW.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A "leading" power factor is created by over-correction using capacitors, which is **NOT permitted** in industrial practice. The standard KTU answer must always show correction **up to (but not beyond) unity**, typically targeting **0.95 to 0.98 lag** for industrial consumers.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Beer Glass Analogy"

Imagine you are at a restaurant and you order a large **pint of beer (kVA — Apparent Power)**. The actual drinkable **liquid beer (kW — Real/Active Power)** is what satisfies your thirst — this is the **useful** part. The rest of the glass is filled with **useless foam (kVAR — Reactive Power)** that just takes up space and makes the glass look fuller than it really is.

- A **low power factor** = a glass with **lots of foam, very little beer** (you paid for a full pint but got mostly air).
- A **high power factor** = a glass with **mostly beer, just a thin layer of foam** (efficient use).
- **kVAR Calculation** is exactly the process of figuring out **how much "anti-foam" (capacitor kVAR)** you need to add so that the foam (reactive current) is suppressed, leaving the glass full of pure, billable beer (active current).

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants / Standard Metrics (Bolded):**
> - **Power Triangle Hypotenuse:** $S$ in **kVA** or **MVA**
> - **Adjacent Side:** $P$ in **kW** or **MW**
> - **Opposite Side:** $Q$ in **kVAR** or **MVAR**
> - **Standard Industrial Target PF:** **0.95 lagging to 0.98 lagging**
> - **System Frequency for Capacitor Rating:** **50 Hz** (India / Kerala)

### 1.3 The Power Triangle — Foundation of kVAR Calculation

Every kVAR calculation problem is solved by manipulating the **right-angled power triangle**, whose three sides are inter-related by Pythagoras' theorem and the basic trigonometric ratios:

$$S^{2} = P^{2} + Q^{2} \quad\Rightarrow\quad Q = P\,\tan\phi$$

where:
- $S$ = Apparent Power
- $P$ = Real (Active) Power  
- $Q$ = Reactive Power
- $\phi$ = Phase angle between voltage and current

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** Whenever a question says *"find the capacitor kVAR rating"*, the examiner expects the student to first draw the **before-correction** and **after-correction** power triangles, label both angles, and then subtract. Skipping the diagram typically costs **2 marks** in ESE.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Power Factor Improvement is Necessary — The "Why"

The KTU syllabus specifically expects students to justify *why* industries are penalized for low power factor. The reasons fall into four engineering categories:

1. **Utility Penalty (Direct Financial Loss):** Kerala State Electricity Board (KSEB) and most DISCOMs in India levy a **low power factor surcharge / penalty** on consumers whose average PF falls below **0.90 lag** (and in some HT industrial tariffs, below **0.95 lag**). A PF bonus is also offered if the consumer maintains PF > 0.95.
2. **Increased Line Current (I²R Losses):** For a fixed real power $P$ and supply voltage $V$, the line current is $I = \dfrac{P}{V\cos\phi}$. A lower $\cos\phi$ means higher $I$, which produces **quadratically higher copper losses** ($I^{2}R$) in cables, busbars, and transformer windings.
3. **Reduced Equipment Capacity:** The same transformer / cable / alternator must be physically larger (higher kVA rating) to deliver the same useful kW when the PF is poor. This increases **capital cost**.
4. **Excessive Voltage Drop:** Higher line current causes a larger voltage drop ($IZ$) in the distribution network, which may lead to **under-voltage at the consumer end**, affecting motor starting and lighting.

### 2.2 Methods of Power Factor Improvement (Conceptual Ranking)

| Method | Typical Application | PF Range | KTU Exam Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Static Capacitor Bank** (Shunt) | Industrial LT/HT plants | 0.85 → 0.98 | **Highest (14-mark derivation questions)** |
| **Synchronous Condenser** | Large HT substations, steel plants | 0.80 → 0.95 | Medium (descriptive 7-mark) |
| **Phase Advancer** | Induction motor slip-ring type | 0.70 → 0.90 | Low (definition only) |
| **Active Power Filters** | Modern harmonic-rich loads | 0.90 → 1.00 | Mention only |

> [!NOTE]
> For the **kVAR Calculation** topic, the KTU 2024 Module 3 syllabus exclusively focuses on the **Shunt Capacitor Bank** method. This is also the most practical and economical method, and is the basis of all numerical problems.

### 2.3 Working Principle of a Shunt Capacitor

A capacitor connected in **parallel** (shunt) with the inductive load draws a **leading current** that is 180° out of phase with the lagging magnetising current drawn by the inductive load. The leading kVAR supplied by the capacitor **cancels** the lagging kVAR demanded by the load, leaving the source to supply only the **net (smaller) reactive current** plus the unchanged real current.

$$Q_{\text{capacitor}} \;=\; Q_{\text{original (lagging)}} \;-\; Q_{\text{after correction}}$$

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — every formula below has appeared (or will appear) in a KTU ESE paper.**

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Units | Boundary / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Power Factor | $\cos\phi = \dfrac{P}{S} = \dfrac{\text{kW}}{\text{kVA}}$ | dimensionless | $0 \le \cos\phi \le 1$ |
| 2 | Reactive Power (Q) | $Q = P\tan\phi$ | kVAR | $Q > 0$ for lagging |
| 3 | Apparent Power (S) | $S = \sqrt{P^{2} + Q^{2}}$ | kVA | Always positive |
| 4 | Original kVAR (before correction) | $Q_{1} = P\tan\phi_{1}$ | kVAR | Lagging, $\cos\phi_{1}$ given |
| 5 | Target kVAR (after correction) | $Q_{2} = P\tan\phi_{2}$ | kVAR | Lagging, $\cos\phi_{2}$ given |
| 6 | **Capacitor kVAR Required** | $\mathbf{Q_{C} \;=\; P\,(\tan\phi_{1} - \tan\phi_{2})}$ | **kVAR** | **This is THE master formula** |
| 7 | Leading kVAR (over-correction, not allowed) | $Q_{C} > P\tan\phi_{1}$ | kVAR | Causes leading PF — rejected |
| 8 | New Line Current | $I_{\text{new}} = \dfrac{P}{\sqrt{3}\,V\cos\phi_{2}}$ | A | Used to show reduction |
| 9 | % Reduction in Line Current | $\%\Delta I = \dfrac{\cos\phi_{2} - \cos\phi_{1}}{\cos\phi_{2}} \times 100$ | % | Bonus KTU sub-part |
| 10 | kVAR per kW Lookup Ratio | $k = \tan\phi_{1} - \tan\phi_{2}$ | kVAR/kW | Tabulated in KTU data sheet |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Notation Rule (Anti-Table-Breaker):** When writing $|\tan\phi|$ or absolute values inside a markdown table, you **MUST** use `\vert \tan\phi \vert` or `\mid \tan\phi \mid` — never the raw pipe `|`, as that breaks the column delimiter.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility of kVAR Calculation

In **practical electrical design** (the subject of this course PEEOT752), the kVAR calculation is the **stepping stone** to:

- **Sizing the APFC Panel** (Automatic Power Factor Correction) — used in every modern industrial LT panel.
- **Selecting capacitor duty contactors** with pre-insertion resistors.
- **Calculating harmonic de-tuning reactor ratings** (e.g., 7% detuned for 189 Hz notch).
- **Energy audit billing** — Kerala KSEB HT consumers are billed on **kVArh** consumed, so under-sized capacitor banks directly inflate the electricity bill.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Master kVAR Formula: $Q_{C} = P(\tan\phi_{1} - \tan\phi_{2})$

This is the **single most important derivation** in Module 3. The KTU 2024 ESE paper has asked for this derivation (or its direct numerical application) in at least **3 of the last 5 university examinations**.

**Given:** A load of $P$ kW operates at original power factor $\cos\phi_{1}$ (lagging). A shunt capacitor bank is to be added so that the new power factor becomes $\cos\phi_{2}$ (lagging), where $\cos\phi_{2} > \cos\phi_{1}$.

**Step 1 — Express the original reactive power:**
The active power $P$ is unchanged by the capacitor (capacitors consume **zero** real power in steady state). The original reactive power drawn from the source is:

$$Q_{1} \;=\; P\,\tan\phi_{1}$$

> *Logic:* From the right-angled power triangle, $\tan\phi = \dfrac{\text{Opposite}}{\text{Adjacent}} = \dfrac{Q}{P}$. Rearranging, $Q = P\tan\phi$.

**Step 2 — Express the target (corrected) reactive power:**
After connecting the capacitor, the new reactive power drawn from the source is:

$$Q_{2} \;=\; P\,\tan\phi_{2}$$

> *Logic:* Same trigonometric relation, applied to the *new* (steeper, narrower) power triangle, since the real power $P$ is unchanged.

**Step 3 — Apply the principle of leading–lagging cancellation:**
The capacitor supplies a **leading** kVAR of magnitude $Q_{C}$, which cancels an equal portion of the **lagging** reactive power of the load. Hence:

$$Q_{2} \;=\; Q_{1} \;-\; Q_{C}$$

> *Logic:* The net reactive current is the phasor difference between the lagging inductor current and the leading capacitor current. Magnitude-wise, the source no longer "sees" the full $Q_{1}$ — only the residual $Q_{2}$.

**Step 4 — Solve for $Q_{C}$:**

$$Q_{C} \;=\; Q_{1} \;-\; Q_{2}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the expressions from Steps 1 and 2:**

$$Q_{C} \;=\; P\,\tan\phi_{1} \;-\; P\,\tan\phi_{2}$$

**Step 6 — Factor out the common $P$:**

$$\boxed{\;Q_{C} \;=\; P\,(\tan\phi_{1} - \tan\phi_{2})\;}$$

**Units Check:** $P$ is in kW, $\tan\phi$ is dimensionless, therefore $Q_{C}$ is in **kVAR** ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Marking Insight:** The examiner awards **1 mark each** for Steps 1, 2, 3, and the final boxed answer. The "principle of cancellation" in Step 3 carries **2 marks** because it is the *physical insight* — students who memorize the formula without understanding this step lose marks if a follow-up conceptual question is asked.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Walkthrough

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example A** (This is the *complete* KTU 14-mark style solution. Read every line — do not skip.)

**Problem Statement:**
A 3-phase, 400 V, 50 Hz industrial load draws **100 kW** at a power factor of **0.70 lagging**. Calculate (a) the rating of the shunt capacitor bank in kVAR to improve the power factor to **0.95 lagging**, and (b) the reduction in line current. Assume star-connected load for current calculation purposes.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Extract given data and identify unknowns:**

$$P \;=\; 100\;\text{kW}, \quad \cos\phi_{1} \;=\; 0.70\;\text{lag}, \quad \cos\phi_{2} \;=\; 0.95\;\text{lag}, \quad V_{L} \;=\; 400\;\text{V}$$

**Step 2 — Find the original phase angle $\phi_{1}$:**

$$\cos\phi_{1} \;=\; 0.70 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \phi_{1} \;=\; \cos^{-1}(0.70) \;=\; 45.57^{\circ}$$

**Step 3 — Find the target phase angle $\phi_{2}$:**

$$\cos\phi_{2} \;=\; 0.95 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \phi_{2} \;=\; \cos^{-1}(0.95) \;=\; 18.19^{\circ}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the trigonometric ratios:**

$$\tan\phi_{1} \;=\; \tan(45.57^{\circ}) \;=\; 1.020$$

$$\tan\phi_{2} \;=\; \tan(18.19^{\circ}) \;=\; 0.3287$$

**Step 5 — Apply the master formula:**

$$Q_{C} \;=\; P\,(\tan\phi_{1} - \tan\phi_{2})$$

$$Q_{C} \;=\; 100 \times (1.020 - 0.3287)$$

$$Q_{C} \;=\; 100 \times 0.6913$$

$$\boxed{\;Q_{C} \;\approx\; 69.13\;\text{kVAR}\;}$$

**Step 6 — Calculate the original line current (before correction):**

$$I_{1} \;=\; \frac{P}{\sqrt{3}\,V_{L}\cos\phi_{1}}$$

$$I_{1} \;=\; \frac{100 \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 0.70}$$

$$I_{1} \;=\; \frac{100\,000}{484.97}$$

$$\boxed{\;I_{1} \;\approx\; 206.2\;\text{A}\;}$$

**Step 7 — Calculate the new line current (after correction):**

$$I_{2} \;=\; \frac{P}{\sqrt{3}\,V_{L}\cos\phi_{2}}$$

$$I_{2} \;=\; \frac{100 \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 0.95}$$

$$I_{2} \;=\; \frac{100\,000}{658.18}$$

$$\boxed{\;I_{2} \;\approx\; 151.93\;\text{A}\;}$$

**Step 8 — Calculate the % reduction in line current:**

$$\%\Delta I \;=\; \frac{I_{1} - I_{2}}{I_{1}} \times 100$$

$$\%\Delta I \;=\; \frac{206.2 - 151.93}{206.2} \times 100$$

$$\%\Delta I \;=\; \frac{54.27}{206.2} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\;\%\Delta I \;\approx\; 26.32\%\;}$$

**Result Summary:**

| Quantity | Before Correction | After Correction |
|---|---|---|
| Power Factor | 0.70 lag | 0.95 lag |
| Real Power (kW) | 100 | 100 |
| Line Current (A) | 206.2 | 151.93 |
| Reactive Power (kVAR) | 102.0 | 32.87 |
| Capacitor kVAR Supplied | — | **69.13** |

### 3.3 Worked Example B — Cap Size in kVAr AND Microfarads

**Problem:** A 3-phase, 415 V, 50 Hz factory draws 80 kVA at 0.6 PF lag. Design the capacitor bank (total kVAR and per-phase capacitance in $\mu F$) to raise the PF to 0.95 lag.

**Step 1 — Compute active power:**

$$P \;=\; S\cos\phi_{1} \;=\; 80 \times 0.6 \;=\; 48\;\text{kW}$$

**Step 2 — Find $\phi_{1}$ and $\phi_{2}$:**

$$\phi_{1} \;=\; \cos^{-1}(0.6) \;=\; 53.13^{\circ}, \quad \phi_{2} \;=\; \cos^{-1}(0.95) \;=\; 18.19^{\circ}$$

**Step 3 — Compute tan values:**

$$\tan(53.13^{\circ}) \;=\; 1.333, \quad \tan(18.19^{\circ}) \;=\; 0.3287$$

**Step 4 — Total kVAR required:**

$$Q_{C} \;=\; 48 \times (1.333 - 0.3287) \;=\; 48 \times 1.004 \;=\; 48.20\;\text{kVAR (3-\phi total)}$$

**Step 5 — Per-phase kVAR (delta connection of capacitor bank is standard):**

$$Q_{C,\text{per phase}} \;=\; \frac{48.20}{3} \;=\; 16.07\;\text{kVAR/phase}$$

**Step 6 — Convert kVAR per phase to capacitance in Farads (delta-connected, so phase voltage = line voltage = 415 V):**

$$Q_{C,\text{per phase}} \;=\; \frac{V_{L}^{2}}{X_{C}} \;=\; V_{L}^{2} \cdot \omega C \;=\; V_{L}^{2} \cdot 2\pi f C$$

$$C \;=\; \frac{Q_{C,\text{per phase}}}{2\pi f V_{L}^{2}}$$

$$C \;=\; \frac{16.07 \times 1000}{2\pi \times 50 \times (415)^{2}}$$

$$C \;=\; \frac{16\,070}{2\pi \times 50 \times 172\,225}$$

$$C \;=\; \frac{16\,070}{54\,103\,136}$$

$$\boxed{\;C \;\approx\; 297\;\mu F \text{ per phase (delta)}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world note:** In commercial APFC panels, capacitors are usually available in ratings of **5, 10, 12.5, 15, 20, 25, 50 kVAR** at 415/440 V. The student must select the **next higher standard size** to avoid under-correction.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Phasor Diagram — Before and After PF Correction (Conceptual)

The phasor diagram is **non-negotiable** in KTU answers. The voltage phasor $V$ is taken as reference (horizontal), and current phasors are drawn with respect to it.

> Since physical phasor diagrams are difficult to render in Mermaid, the following is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture** that maps the *logical flow of the calculation* as if it were a phasor-state machine:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source: 3-phase 400V, 50Hz] --> B[Load Bus: Industrial Plant]
    B --> C[Inductive Load: Motors + Transformers]
    C --> D[Draws I_lag at angle phi1 behind V]
    B --> E[Shunt Capacitor Bank]
    E --> F[Draws I_cap leading V by 90 deg]
    D --> G[Phasor Sum at Source]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Net Current I_new at angle phi2 behind V]
    H --> I{phi2 less than phi1 ?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Power Factor Improved]
    I -- No --> K[Over-correction: REJECTED]
    J --> L[Target PF 0.95 lag achieved]
```

### 4.2 Power Triangle Schematic — Before and After Correction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TRIANGLE_BEFORE["BEFORE CORRECTION (phi1 = 45.57 deg)"]
        P1[Real Power P = 100 kW] -->|adjacent| ORIG[(Hypotenuse S1)]
        Q1[Reactive Q1 = 102 kVAR] -->|opposite| ORIG
        ORIG -->|angle phi1 lag| P1
    end
    subgraph TRIANGLE_AFTER["AFTER CORRECTION (phi2 = 18.19 deg)"]
        P2[Real Power P = 100 kW] -->|adjacent| NEW[(Hypotenuse S2)]
        Q2[Reactive Q2 = 32.87 kVAR] -->|opposite| NEW
        NEW -->|angle phi2 lag| P2
    end
    subgraph CAP_BLOCK["SHUNT CAPACITOR SUPPLIES"]
        QC[Leading Q_C = 69.13 kVAR]
    end
    Q1 -->|minus QC| Q2
    CAP_BLOCK -.supplies.-> Q2
```

### 4.3 kVAR Calculation Procedure — Block Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start: Problem Given] --> S1[Step 1: Extract P, cos phi1, cos phi2]
    S1 --> S2[Step 2: Compute phi1 and phi2 using cos inverse]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Look up tan phi1 and tan phi2]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Apply Q_C = P times tan phi1 minus tan phi2]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Convert to per-phase kVAR if 3-phase bank]
    S5 --> S6{Step 6: Need capacitance in microfarads ?}
    S6 -- Yes --> S7[Use C = Q_C per phase divided by 2 pi f V squared]
    S6 -- No --> S8[Select standard kVAR rating from APFC table]
    S7 --> S8
    S8 --> S9[End: Specify capacitor bank rating]
```

### 4.4 Component-Level Block Diagram of an APFC Panel (Industry Standard)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MAIN[Main LT Incomer 400V] --> BUS[Copper Bus Bar 800A]
    BUS --> CT[CT for kWh and kVArh metering]
    BUS --> CONT[Capacitor Duty Contactor with PIR]
    CONT --> CAP1[5 kVAR Capacitor Unit]
    CONT --> CAP2[10 kVAR Capacitor Unit]
    CONT --> CAP3[25 kVAR Capacitor Unit]
    CONT --> CAP4[50 kVAR Capacitor Unit]
    BUS --> PFR[Automatic PF Controller Relay]
    CT --> PFR
    PFR -->|Switch ON/OFF stages| CONT
    PFR -->|Display cos phi| DISP[Digital PF Display]
    CAP1 --> BUS
    CAP2 --> BUS
    CAP3 --> BUS
    CAP4 --> BUS
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
