## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**Q1. Define power factor. Why is power factor improvement necessary in industrial installations? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
Power factor is the cosine of the angle between the alternating voltage and current, defined as $\cos\phi = \dfrac{P}{S} = \dfrac{\text{kW}}{\text{kVA}}$.

Power factor improvement is necessary in industrial installations for the following reasons:

1. **To avoid utility penalty** — Kerala KSEB penalizes HT industrial consumers operating below 0.90 PF lag by levying a low PF surcharge on the energy bill.
2. **To reduce line current and I²R losses** in cables, busbars, and transformer windings, thereby improving system efficiency.
3. **To release transformer / cable capacity** — the same kVA-rated equipment can now supply more useful kW.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for penalty, 1 mark for I²R loss OR capacity release. A 2-reason answer is the **minimum acceptable** length.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**Q2. A load of 50 kW operates at 0.6 PF lag. Find the reactive power drawn from the supply. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1:** Find phase angle.

$$\phi_{1} \;=\; \cos^{-1}(0.6) \;=\; 53.13^{\circ}$$

**Step 2:** Compute $\tan\phi_{1}$.

$$\tan(53.13^{\circ}) \;=\; 1.333$$

**Step 3:** Apply $Q = P\tan\phi$.

$$Q_{1} \;=\; 50 \times 1.333 \;=\; 66.65\;\text{kVAR (lagging)}$$

**Result:** The load draws **66.65 kVAR** of lagging reactive power from the supply.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write $Q = P \times \sin\phi$ instead of $Q = P \times \tan\phi$. Since the power triangle has $P$ as **adjacent** and $Q$ as **opposite**, the correct ratio is $\tan\phi$, **not** $\sin\phi$. This error costs **full 3 marks** if the examiner follows a strict step marking.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Calculation of kVAR and Current Reduction*

**(a)** A 3-phase, 400 V, 50 Hz induction motor load draws **75 kW** at **0.75 PF lagging**. **(7 Marks)**

**(i)** Calculate the original apparent power, reactive power, and line current.

**(ii)** Determine the kVAR rating of the capacitor bank needed to improve the PF to **0.95 lag**.

**(b)** For the corrected system, **(7 Marks)**

**(i)** Calculate the new line current and the **percentage reduction** in line current.

**(ii)** If the capacitors are connected in **delta** and rated at 415 V, 50 Hz, calculate the capacitance per phase in microfarads.

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**(a)(i) Original Apparent Power, Reactive Power, and Line Current:**

Phase angle: $\phi_{1} = \cos^{-1}(0.75) = 41.41^{\circ}$, $\tan\phi_{1} = 0.8819$

$$S_{1} \;=\; \frac{P}{\cos\phi_{1}} \;=\; \frac{75}{0.75} \;=\; 100\;\text{kVA}$$

$$Q_{1} \;=\; P\tan\phi_{1} \;=\; 75 \times 0.8819 \;=\; 66.14\;\text{kVAR}$$

$$I_{1} \;=\; \frac{P}{\sqrt{3}\,V\cos\phi_{1}} \;=\; \frac{75 \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 0.75} \;=\; 144.34\;\text{A}$$

**(a)(ii) Capacitor kVAR:**

$\phi_{2} = \cos^{-1}(0.95) = 18.19^{\circ}$, $\tan\phi_{2} = 0.3287$

$$Q_{C} \;=\; P(\tan\phi_{1} - \tan\phi_{2}) \;=\; 75 \times (0.8819 - 0.3287) \;=\; 75 \times 0.5532$$

$$\boxed{\;Q_{C} \;=\; 41.49\;\text{kVAR (3-phase total)}\;}$$

**(b)(i) New Line Current and % Reduction:**

$$I_{2} \;=\; \frac{75 \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 0.95} \;=\; 113.95\;\text{A}$$

$$\%\Delta I \;=\; \frac{144.34 - 113.95}{144.34} \times 100 \;=\; 21.05\%$$

**(b)(ii) Capacitance per Phase (Delta Connection):**

Per-phase kVAR: $Q_{\text{phase}} = 41.49/3 = 13.83\;\text{kVAR/phase}$

For delta, $V_{\text{phase}} = V_{\text{line}} = 400\;\text{V}$

$$C \;=\; \frac{Q_{\text{phase}} \times 1000}{2\pi f V^{2}} \;=\; \frac{13\,830}{2\pi \times 50 \times 160\,000}$$

$$\boxed{\;C \;\approx\; 275.1\;\mu F \text{ per phase}\;}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Conceptual + Calculation*

**(a)** Explain with a neat power triangle diagram **why** the addition of a shunt capacitor bank improves the power factor of an inductive load. State **three** advantages of PF improvement. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A factory operates on a 3-phase, 11 kV, 50 Hz HT supply and draws a load of **2000 kW** at **0.72 PF lag**. The HT consumer is billed on kVA maximum demand. **(7 Marks)**

**(i)** Calculate the present kVA demand and the line current.

**(ii)** Find the kVAR of the capacitor bank to be installed to improve PF to **0.95 lag**, and the **new kVA demand** after correction.

**(iii)** Comment on the impact on the maximum demand charges.

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**(a) Explanation:**

In an inductive load, the current lags the voltage by angle $\phi_{1}$, and the power triangle has a long opposite side (reactive $Q_{1}$). When a shunt capacitor (which draws current **leading** voltage by 90°) is connected in parallel, the leading capacitive kVAR **cancels** an equal portion of the lagging inductive kVAR. The new triangle has a smaller opposite side ($Q_{2}$) and a smaller angle ($\phi_{2}$), hence $\cos\phi_{2} > \cos\phi_{1}$.

**Three Advantages:**
1. Reduction in line current and I²R losses in the distribution system.
2. Reduced kVA demand → lower maximum demand charges in HT billing.
3. Improved voltage profile at the consumer end.

**(b)(i) Present kVA Demand and Line Current:**

$$S_{1} \;=\; \frac{P}{\cos\phi_{1}} \;=\; \frac{2000}{0.72} \;=\; 2777.78\;\text{kVA}$$

$$I_{1} \;=\; \frac{2000 \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \times 11\,000 \times 0.72} \;=\; 145.83\;\text{A}$$

**(b)(ii) Capacitor kVAR and New kVA Demand:**

$\phi_{1} = \cos^{-1}(0.72) = 43.95^{\circ}$, $\tan\phi_{1} = 0.9638$

$\phi_{2} = 18.19^{\circ}$, $\tan\phi_{2} = 0.3287$

$$Q_{C} \;=\; 2000 \times (0.9638 - 0.3287) \;=\; 2000 \times 0.6351 \;=\; 1270.2\;\text{kVAR}$$

$$S_{2} \;=\; \frac{P}{\cos\phi_{2}} \;=\; \frac{2000}{0.95} \;=\; 2105.26\;\text{kVA}$$

**(b)(iii) Impact on Maximum Demand Charges:**

$$\text{Reduction in kVA demand} \;=\; 2777.78 - 2105.26 \;=\; 672.52\;\text{kVA}$$

$$\%\text{Reduction} \;=\; \frac{672.52}{2777.78} \times 100 \;\approx\; 24.21\%$$

The consumer saves **24.21% of the monthly maximum demand charges**, which at typical Kerala KSEB HT industrial tariff of approximately **₹400 per kVA per month** translates to a saving of about **₹2.69 lakh per month** — a very strong payback argument for the capacitor bank.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch (–2 marks):** If $P$ is given in **watts** and the formula is applied directly, the answer will be in **VAR**, not **kVAR**. Always convert $P$ to **kW** before using the master formula, OR convert the final kVAR to VAR if $P$ was in W.
> 2. **Confusing delta and star capacitor connection (–2 marks):** In **delta**, $V_{\text{phase}} = V_{\text{line}}$; in **star**, $V_{\text{phase}} = V_{\text{line}}/\sqrt{3}$. This changes the capacitance value by a factor of **3** — a costly mistake.
> 3. **Forgetting to draw the power triangle (–1 mark):** Even in purely numerical answers, the KTU evaluator expects a labelled power triangle before the calculation. A diagram is **not optional**.
> 4. **Using $\sin\phi$ instead of $\tan\phi$ (–3 marks):** This is the **#1 mistake** observed in valuation. Memorize: $Q$ is **opposite**, $P$ is **adjacent**, therefore $\tan\phi = Q/P$.
> 5. **Over-correction:** Installing a capacitor bank larger than $P\tan\phi_{1}$ will produce a **leading** PF, which the utility **refuses to accept** and may even penalize (export of reactive power is not rewarded in Indian tariffs).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this section — read it the night before the exam.**

- [x] **Power Factor = $\cos\phi = P/S$** — ratio of real to apparent power. **Always lagging** for industrial loads.
- [x] **Master Formula:** $\mathbf{Q_{C} = P(\tan\phi_{1} - \tan\phi_{2})}$ — learn this **by heart**; derive it once so you understand it.
- [x] **$P$ is invariant** during correction — only $Q$ and $S$ change. If $P$ changes, you must recalculate $Q_{C}$.
- [x] **Standard target PF for industries:** **0.95 lag** (most common) or **0.98 lag** (premium plants). Never target unity — the marginal kVAR is too expensive.
- [x] **Always extract $\tan\phi$ from $\cos\phi$** using the angle first, then tangent. Do not use $\tan(\cos^{-1}(x))$ trick shortcuts — they are error-prone.
- [x] **Capacitor connection:** **Delta** is preferred for **low-voltage (415 V)** systems (lower capacitance, higher kVAR per unit); **Star** is preferred for **HT (11 kV / 33 kV)** capacitor banks (lower voltage rating per unit).
- [x] **Per-phase capacitance formula:** $C = \dfrac{Q_{\text{phase}}}{2\pi f V_{\text{phase}}^{2}}$ — remember $Q$ must be in **VAR** (not kVAR) and $V$ in **volts**.
- [x] **Reduction in line current** is a direct KTU sub-question — always compute $I_{1}$ and $I_{2}$ to show the engineering benefit.
- [x] **Penalties apply below 0.90 lag** in most Indian DISCOMs; **bonus applies above 0.95 lag** in some progressive tariffs.
- [x] **Three losses reduced by PF correction:** I²R losses in cables, iron losses in transformers (due to reduced magnetising), and slip losses in motors (due to better voltage).
- [x] **Capacitor bank must NEVER be switched without a discharge resistor** — the residual voltage on a switched-off capacitor can be lethal (KTU safety question).
- [x] **APFC = Automatic Power Factor Correction** — uses a controller relay that switches capacitor stages in/out to maintain target PF dynamically.
- [x] **Detuned reactors** (typically 7% — i.e., $X_{L} = 0.07\,X_{C}$) are added in series with capacitors when **non-linear loads (VFDs, UPS, SMPS)** are present, to avoid harmonic resonance.
- [x] **Don't forget units:** kW for $P$, kVAR for $Q$, kVA for $S$, A for $I$, V for $V$, F or $\mu F$ for $C$.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 3 Topic Notes — Power Factor Improvement: kVAR Calculation**
> *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern. Aligned with Course Outcomes CO3 (Design of PF correction systems for industrial loads).*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Correction Methods, Design of MSB & SSB including Motor Control Centre (MCC), Selection of Bus Bars (CU & Al) and Switchgears, Specifications of LT Breakers and Other LT Panel Components (Basics Only), Selection of Industrial UG Cables - Calculation of Ampacity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEOT752 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Correction Methods, Design of MSB & SSB including Motor Control Centre (MCC), Selection of Bus Bars (CU & Al) and Switchgears, Specifications of LT Breakers and Other LT Panel Components (Basics Only), Selection of Industrial UG Cables - Calculation of Ampacity # MODULE 3: LV SWITCHBOARD DESIGN, PROTECTION & CABLE AMPACITY ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Correction (Derating) Methods for Cables **Formal Definition:** A *correction factor* (or *derating factor*, $K$) is a numerical multiplier applied to the **tabulated base ampacity** of a cable to account for real-world installation conditions that differ from the standard reference conditions (typically 30 °C ambient ground/air temperature, single circuit in free air or direct buried at standard depth, and standard soil thermal resistivity of **1.5 K·m/W**). The *corrected ampacity* $I_z$ is the actual permissible continuous current the cable may carry safely. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition:** > $$I_z = I_{tab} \times K_1 \times K_2 \times K_3 \times \cdots \times K_n$$ > where $I_{tab}$ is the tabulated current from IS 3961 / IEC 60364-5-52 / IS 1554 / IS 7098. **Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):** Imagine a highway with a **speed limit of 80 km/h** (this is your tabulated ampacity). But if it is **raining** (rain = ambient temperature derating), the safe speed drops to 60 km/h. If there is **construction work** (construction = grouping derating), speed drops to 50 km/h. If the road is **muddy and slippery** (muddy = soil resistivity derating), speed drops further to 40 km/h. The *final safe speed* (corrected ampacity $I_z$) is the product of all the reduction multipliers. Each real-world condition makes the cable "less able" to dissipate heat, so we must de-rate it. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Reference Conditions (IS / IEC):** > - Ambient air temperature: **30 °C** (for air) / **40 °C** (for some LT tables) > - Ground temperature: **30 °C** > - Soil thermal resistivity: **1.5 K·m/W** > - Depth of laying (for UG): **0.5 m to 0.8 m** > - Single circuit, trefoil formation (for 3-core cables) > - Frequency: **50 Hz** ### 1.2 Main Switch Board (MSB), Sub Switch Board (SSB) & Motor Control Centre (MCC) **MSB (Main Switch Board):** The **primary LV distribution board** that receives power directly from the transformer LV terminals (or main generator bus) and feeds downstream SSBs and large feeders. It is the "**spinal cord**" of an industrial LT distribution system. **SSB (Sub Switch Board):** A **secondary distribution board** fed from the MSB, supplying lighting, small power, and localised loads. Each SSB serves a **zone or floor** of the plant. **MCC (Motor Control Centre):** A **specialised switchboard assembly** containing motor starters, contactors, overload relays, and short-circuit protective devices for **group of motors** in a process plant. MCCs are factory-assembled, modular, multi-section cubicles. **Conceptual Analogy:** - **MSB = Main water tank** receiving supply from the pump. - **SSB = Local distribution headers** (smaller tanks) feeding individual floors/rooms. - **MCC = Motorised valve control panel** controlling the pumps and motors that move the water. ### 1.3 Bus Bars (Copper & Aluminium) **Bus bars** are **rigid, solid conductors** (usually rectangular in cross-section) used inside switchboards, panel boards, and substations to carry large currents between incoming and outgoing devices. They are the **"highways"** inside the panel. | Property | Copper (Cu) | Aluminium (Al) | |----------|-------------|----------------| | **Conductivity** (% IACS) | **100 %** | **61 %** | | **Density** | 8.96 g/cm³ | 2.70 g/cm³ | | **Cost** | High | Low (~1/3 of Cu) | | **Current density (typical)** | 1.0–1.6 A/mm² | 0.6–0.8 A/mm² | ### 1.4 LT Switchgears and Panel Components **Switchgear:** A general term for devices used to **switch, protect, isolate, and control** electrical circuits. - **MCB** (Miniature Circuit Breaker) – up to 100 A, 10 kA - **MCCB** (Moulded Case Circuit Breaker) – 100 A to 1600 A, 25–70 kA - **ACB** (Air Circuit Breaker) – 630 A to 6300 A, 50–100 kA - **SDF** (Switch Disconnector Fuse) – up to 800 A - **Contactor** – for remote switching of motors - **Overload Relay (OLR)** – bimetallic / electronic, for motor protection - **MPCB** (Motor Protection Circuit Breaker) – combined short-circuit + overload ### 1.5 Industrial UG (Underground) Cables **Underground cables** are insulated, armoured, and sheathed power cables laid directly in trenches or ducts, used for **industrial, urban, and substation** LV/MV interconnections. The most important design parameter is the **ampacity (current-carrying capacity)**, which is limited by the **maximum permissible conductor temperature** (e.g., 70 °C for PVC, 90 °C for XLPE). > [!NOTE] > **Standard Industrial LT Cables (IS 7098 / IS 1554):** > - **XLPE (Cross-Linked Polyethylene):** 90 °C conductor temperature, preferred. > - **PVC (Polyvinyl Chloride):** 70 °C, cheaper but lower rating. > - **Armouring:** Galvanised steel (for multi-core) / Aluminium (for single-core). > - **Sheath:** PVC / FRLS (Fire Retardant Low Smoke). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Correction (Derating) Factors in Detail A cable installed in actual field conditions rarely matches the standard reference. We must apply **multiplicative correction factors**: ### A. Temperature Correction Factor ($K_1$) When the ambient temperature differs from the standard reference (30 °C for ground, 40 °C for air — depends on standard): $$K_1 = \sqrt{\frac{\theta_{cond,max} - \theta_{a,actual}}{\theta_{cond,max} - \theta_{a,ref}}}$$ where: - $\theta_{cond,max}$ = max permissible conductor temperature (90 °C XLPE, 70 °C PVC) - $\theta_{a,actual}$ = actual ambient temperature - $\theta_{a,ref}$ = reference ambient temperature > [!TIP] > **KTU Memory Trick:** "Hotter ambient → smaller $K_1$". If $K_1 > 1$, ambient is **cooler** than reference (cable can carry more current). If $K_1 < 1$, ambient is **hotter** (cable carries less). ### B. Grouping / Circuit Multiplication Factor ($K_2$) When **multiple circuits** run together in the same trench, duct, or on the same tray, mutual heating reduces the dissipation. The **grouping factor** depends on the number of circuits and their spacing. | No. of Circuits | Touching / Trefoil | Spaced (one dia.) | Spaced (>2 dia.) | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 1 | 1.00 | 1.00 | 1.00 | | 2 | 0.80 | 0.88 | 0.94 | | 3 | 0.70 | 0.82 | 0.90 | | 4 | 0.65 | 0.77 | 0.90 | | 6 | 0.57 | 0.73 | 0.88 | | 9 | 0.50 | 0.68 | 0.85 | > [!IMPORTANT] > **For UG cables laid direct in ground in trefoil formation:** Use IS 3961 Table 5 / IEC 60364-5-52. ### C. Soil Thermal Resistivity Correction ($K_3$) For underground cables, the soil's ability to conduct heat away matters. **Standard** is **1.5 K·m/W**. | Soil Thermal Resistivity (K·m/W) | $K_3$ | |:---:|:---:| | 0.8 (wet soil) | 1.25 | | 1.0 | 1.18 | | **1.5 (standard)** | **1.00** | | 2.0 | 0.86 | | 2.5 | 0.76 | | 3.0 | 0.70 | ### D. Depth of Laying ($K_4$) Deeper laying → poorer heat dissipation → smaller factor. | Depth of Laying (m) | $K_4$ | |:---:|:---:| | 0.50 | 1.00 | | 0.70 | 0.96 | | 0.90 | 0.93 | | 1.05 | 0.91 | | 1.20 | 0.89 | ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $I_z = I_{tab} \cdot K_1 \cdot K_2 \cdot K_3 \cdot K_4$ | Corrected ampacity | A | | 2 | $I_b \le I_n \le I_z$ | Coordination (where $I_b$=design current, $I_n$=protective device rating) | A | | 3 | $K_1 = \sqrt{\frac{\theta_{max} - \theta_{a,act}}{\theta_{max} - \theta_{a,ref}}}$ | Temperature correction | dimensionless | | 4 | $A = \frac{I_{b}}{\delta}$ | Bus bar cross-section ($A$=area, $\delta$=current density) | mm² | | 5 | $I_{sc} = \frac{k \cdot A}{\sqrt{t}}$ | Short-time current capacity (cable) | kA·s | | 6 | $V_d = \sqrt{3} \cdot I_b \cdot L \cdot (R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)$ | Voltage drop (3-phase) | V | | 7 | $R_{ac} = R_{dc} \cdot (1 + y_s + y_p)$ | AC resistance correction | Ω/km | | 8 | $I_{b} = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V \cdot \cos\phi}$ | Line current (3-phase load) | A | | 9 | $I_{b} = \frac{P}{V}$ | Line current (1-phase load) | A | | 10 | $M_{d} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V \cdot I_{sc}$ | Short-circuit MVA at LV side | MVA | | 11 | $\text{Let-through Energy} I^2 t = I_{sc}^2 \cdot (t_{breaker} + t_{relay})$ | Discrimin. check | A²s | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Current Density (δ) — KTU Standard Reference:** > - **Copper bus bars:** **1.2 to 1.6 A/mm²** (indoor, painted) > - **Aluminium bus bars:** **0.7 to 0.8 A/mm²** (indoor, painted) > - **Tinned Copper:** 1.0 A/mm² > - **Bare Copper in panel:** 1.6 A/mm² ## 2.3 Why Correction Factors? (The "Why" Behind It) Every factor arises from a single physical law — **Fourier's heat conduction**: $$P_{heat} = \frac{\theta_{cond} - \theta_{ambient}}{R_{thermal}}$$ If $\theta_{ambient}$ rises → less temperature headroom → less allowable $I^2R$ loss → smaller $K_1$. If circuits are grouped → mutual heating → higher ambient for each → smaller $K_2$. If soil resistivity rises → higher $R_{thermal}$ → less heat removal → smaller $K_3$. If depth rises → longer conduction path → higher $R_{thermal}$ → smaller $K_4$. All factors = 1 at standard conditions; deviation reduces the permissible ampacity. ## 2.4 Bus Bar Selection Procedure **Step 1:** Compute full-load current $I_b$ of the bus. **Step 2:** Select current density $\delta$ (depending on material, painting, location). **Step 3:** Compute cross-section $A = I_b / \delta$. **Step 4:** Select standard size: thickness ($t$) × width ($w$). **Step 5:** Check temperature rise (≤ 65 K above 40 °C ambient per IS 8623). **Step 6:** Check short-time current withstand: $$I_{cw} \ge I_{sc,rms} \cdot \sqrt{t_{sc}}$$ where $I_{sc,rms}$ is the short-circuit current and $t_{sc}$ is the duration (typically 1 s). ## 2.5 Selection of Industrial UG Cables — Ampacity Workflow ``` 1. Determine load type → 1-φ / 3-φ 2. Compute design current I_b 3. Reference standard → IS 1554 (PVC) / IS 7098 (XLPE) 4. Look up I_tab from tables (standard conditions) 5. Identify field conditions: - Ambient T, Soil T, Soil ρ - Grouping, Depth, Laying method 6. Apply K1 × K2 × K3 × K4 ... 7. Compute I_z = I_tab × ∏K_i 8. Select cable size such that I_z ≥ I_b 9. Verify voltage drop ≤ 3% (motor) / 5% (lighting) 10. Verify short-circuit withstand ``` # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Temperature Correction Factor $K_1$ The heat dissipated by a current-carrying conductor is: $$P = I^2 R$$ The conductor temperature $\theta_c$ is governed by the heat balance with the surrounding ambient temperature $\theta_a$ through thermal resistance $R_{th}$: $$\theta_c = \theta_a + I^2 R \cdot R_{th}$$ The maximum **permissible** conductor temperature is $\theta_{max}$ (90 °C XLPE, 70 °C PVC). Thus the maximum current under any ambient is: $$I_{max}^2 = \frac{\theta_{max} - \theta_a}{R \cdot R_{th}}$$ Taking the ratio of $I_{max}$ at actual ambient to $I_{max}$ at reference ambient: $$\frac{I_{max,actual}^2}{I_{max,ref}^2} = \frac{\theta_{max} - \theta_{a,act}}{\theta_{max} - \theta_{a,ref}}$$ $$\therefore \quad \boxed{K_1 = \sqrt{\frac{\theta_{max} - \theta_{a,act}}{\theta_{max} - \theta_{a,ref}}}}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation of Corrected Ampacity $I_z$ If a cable's tabulated ampacity $I_{tab}$ corresponds to **standard reference conditions**, then under a set of $n$ derating conditions, each contributing a factor $K_i$, the new ampacity is: $$I_z = I_{tab} \times \prod_{i=1}^{n} K_i$$ **Justification:** Each factor independently reduces the permissible current. Since they act **multiplicatively** (as I²R losses compound with thermal limitations): $$I_z = I_{tab} \cdot K_1 \cdot K_2 \cdot K_3 \cdot K_4$$ ## 3.3 Derivation of the Short-Circuit Withstand for Bus Bars When a short-circuit current $I_{sc}$ flows for a time $t$, the heat generated is: $$E = I_{sc}^2 \cdot t$$ This heat raises the bus bar temperature. Using the adiabatic heating equation (assuming no heat loss during the short duration): $$E = m \cdot c \cdot \Delta\theta = (A \cdot L \cdot \rho_{m}) \cdot c \cdot \Delta\theta$$ Equating and solving for $A$: $$A = \frac{I_{sc} \cdot \sqrt{t}}{\sqrt{\rho_{m} \cdot c \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{\theta_f + \beta}{\theta_i + \beta}\right)}}$$ In simplified form (per IS 8623): $$A_{min} = \frac{I_{cw}}{\alpha \cdot \sqrt{t}}$$ where $\alpha$ is a material constant (Copper: 142, Aluminium: 94) and $I_{cw}$ is the rated short-time current. ## 3.4 Worked Example 1 — Derating of UG Cable > **Problem:** A 3-core **XLPE insulated, 95 mm²** copper cable (armoured, PVC sheathed) is to be installed **directly buried in ground**. Conditions: > - **Ambient ground temperature:** 35 °C > - **Soil thermal resistivity:** 2.0 K·m/W > - **Number of circuits in trench:** 4 > - **Depth of laying:** 0.7 m > - **Conductor:** Cu, XLPE → $\theta_{max} = 90$ °C > - Reference ground temperature: **30 °C** > > Standard **$I_{tab}$** for 95 mm² 3C XLPE Cu (from IS 7098 Table 4): **$I_{tab} = 240$ A** > > **Find:** Corrected ampacity $I_z$. **Step 1 — Apply Temperature Correction $K_1$:** $$K_1 = \sqrt{\frac{90 - 35}{90 - 30}} = \sqrt{\frac{55}{60}}$$ $$K_1 = \sqrt{0.9167} = 0.957$$ **Step 2 — Apply Soil Thermal Resistivity Correction $K_3$:** For $\rho = 2.0$ K·m/W, from IS 3961: $$K_3 = 0.86$$ **Step 3 — Apply Grouping Factor $K_2$:** For 4 circuits in trefoil in ground, touching: $$K_2 = 0.65$$ **Step 4 — Apply Depth of Laying $K_4$:** For depth = 0.7 m: $$K_4 = 0.96$$ **Step 5 — Compute Corrected Ampacity:** $$I_z = I_{tab} \times K_1 \times K_2 \times K_3 \times K_4$$ $$I_z = 240 \times 0.957 \times 0.65 \times 0.86 \times 0.96$$ **Step 6 — Numerical Evaluation:** $$I_z = 240 \times 0.957 = 229.68$$ $$I_z = 229.68 \times 0.65 = 149.29$$ $$I_z = 149.29 \times 0.86 = 128.39$$ $$I_z = 128.39 \times 0.96 = 123.26 \text{ A}$$ $$\boxed{I_z \approx 123.3 \text{ A}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** A cable that could nominally carry **240 A** is restricted to **123 A** under actual field derating — a **48.6 % reduction**! This is why KTU always asks cable sizing with full derating. ## 3.5 Worked Example 2 — Bus Bar Selection for MSB > **Problem:** A 3-phase, 415 V, 50 Hz MSB must carry a balanced load of **800 kVA** at 0.8 p.f. Find the **required copper bus bar size** (indoor, painted, single-bar configuration). > Take allowable current density $\delta = 1.2$ A/mm². **Step 1 — Compute Full-Load Current:** $$I_{b} = \frac{S}{\sqrt{3} \times V_L} = \frac{800 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times 415}$$ $$I_b = \frac{800\,000}{718.8} = 1112.97 \text{ A}$$ **Step 2 — Compute Cross-Sectional Area:** $$A = \frac{I_b}{\delta} = \frac{1112.97}{1.2} = 927.5 \text{ mm}^2$$ **Step 3 — Select Standard Size:** A standard copper bus bar of **80 mm × 12 mm = 960 mm²** (or 100 mm × 10 mm = 1000 mm²) is chosen. > [!NOTE] > **Standard bus bar sizes (mm):** width: 12, 16, 20, 25, 30, 40, 50, 60, 80, 100, 120; thickness: 5, 6, 8, 10, 12 mm. ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Selection of LT Switchgear | Load (A) | Voltage Level | Recommended Switchgear | |:---:|:---:|:---| | ≤ 63 A | 240/415 V | **MCB** (Miniature Circuit Breaker) | | 63 – 250 A | 415 V | **MCB** (industrial) / **MCCB** | | 250 – 800 A | 415 V | **MCCB** (Moulded Case) | | 800 – 1600 A | 415 V | **MCCB** (large) / **ACB** | | > 1600 A | 415 V / 11 kV | **ACB** (Air Circuit Breaker) / **VCB** | | Motor loads (5–100 kW) | 415 V | **Contactor + OLR + MPCB / MCB** | ## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — SLD Components for MSB / SSB / MCC | Component | In MSB? | In SSB? | In MCC? | |---|:---:|:---:|:---:| | **ACB (Main Incomer)** | ✔ | – | – | | **MCCB (Bus Coupler / Feeders)** | ✔ | ✔ | – | | **MCB (Outgoing final circuits)** | – | ✔ | ✔ | | **CT / PT / Metering** | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | | **Contactor + OLR (DOL Starter)** | – | – | ✔ | | **Star-Delta / Soft Starter** | – | – | ✔ | | **SDF (Switch Disconnector Fuse)** | ✔ (large feeders) | – | ✔ (incoming) | | **Power Factor Correction Capacitor Bank** | ✔ | – | – | | **Bus Bar Trunking** | ✔ | – | – | | **APFC Relay** | ✔ | – | – | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Industrial LT Distribution Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB TRANS[Transformer 11kV / 415V]:::source MSB[MSB Main Switch Board
ACB Incomer 1600A]:::main BUSBUS[Copper Bus Bar 3P N E
100mm x 10mm]:::bus BC[Bus Coupler MCCB 1600A]:::main SSB1[SSB Floor 1
MCCB 400A]:::sub SSB2[SSB Floor 2
MCCB 400A]:::sub MCC1[MCC Process Plant A
MCCB 630A]:::mcc MCC2[MCC Process Plant B
MCCB 630A]:::mcc CAP[Capacitor Bank
300 kVAr]:::aux L1[Lighting Load]:::load L2[Small Power]:::load M1[Motor 75 kW DOL]:::load M2[Motor 30 kW StarDelta]:::load TRANS --> MSB MSB --> BUSBUS BUSBUS --> BC BUSBUS --> SSB1 BUSBUS --> SSB2 BUSBUS --> MCC1 BUSBUS --> MCC2 BUSBUS --> CAP SSB1 --> L1 SSB1 --> L2 MCC1 --> M1 MCC2 --> M2 classDef source fill:#ffd700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef main fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef sub fill:#4ecdc4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef mcc fill:#a8d8ea,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef bus fill:#ffffff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef aux fill:#f7b801,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef load fill:#95e1d3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Single Line Diagram (SLD) of a Typical MCC Section ```mermaid flowchart LR INCM[Incoming MCCB
630 A]:::mcc BUSB[Three Phase Bus Bar
Aluminium 80x8 mm]:::bus F1[Feeder 1: 75 kW Motor]:::mcc F2[Feeder 2: 30 kW Motor]:::mcc F3[Feeder 3: 15 kW Motor]:::mcc F4[Feeder 4: Spare]:::mcc CT1[CT 600/5 A Metering]:::aux M1A[Contactor + OLR
DOL Starter]:::load M2A[Contactor + OLR
Star Delta]:::load M3A[MPCB
Direct Online]:::load INCM --> BUSB BUSB --> CT1 BUSB --> F1 BUSB --> F2 BUSB --> F3 BUSB --> F4 F1 --> M1A F2 --> M2A F3 --> M3A classDef mcc fill:#a8d8ea,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef bus fill:#ffffff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef aux fill:#f7b801,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef load fill:#95e1d3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Cable Derating Cascade Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Start: Design Current I_b]:::startNode B[Look up Tabulated Current I_tab
from IS 1554 / IS 7098]:::processNode C[Apply Temperature Correction K1]:::processNode D[Apply Grouping Correction K2]:::processNode E[Apply Soil Resistivity K3
if UG]:::processNode F[Apply Depth Correction K4
if UG]:::processNode G{I_z greater than or equal to I_b?}:::decisionNode H[Select Next Higher
Cable Size]:::processNode I[Select this Cable Size]:::endNode J[Check Voltage Drop
less than 3 percent]:::processNode K[Verify Short Circuit
Withstand]:::processNode L[Final Cable Approved]:::endNode A --> B B --> C C --> D D --> E E --> F F --> G G -- No --> H H --> B G -- Yes --> I I --> J J --> K K --> L classDef startNode fill:#90ee90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef processNode fill:#87ceeb,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px classDef decisionNode fill:#ffeb3b,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef endNode fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff ``` ## 4.4 Functional Block: LT Switchboard Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph INCOMER[INCOMER SECTION] ACB1[ACB / MCCB 1600A
With Microprocessor Release] end subgraph METER[METERING SECTION] MTR1[Multi Function Meter
V A Hz kW kWh PF] CTPT1[CTs 1600/5A and PTs] end subgraph BUSBAR[BUSBAR SECTION] B1[3P + N + E Cu Bus
100 x 10 mm] end subgraph FEEDERS[FEEDER SECTION] F1A[MCCB 400A to SSB 1] F1B[MCCB 400A to SSB 2] F1C[MCCB 630A to MCC A] F1D[MCCB 630A to MCC B] F1E[SDF 800A to Capacitor] end subgraph CAPBANK[CAPACITOR BANK] CAP1[300 kVAr APFC Bank
12 Stage] end INCOMER --> METER METER --> BUSBAR BUSBAR --> FEEDERS F1E --> CAPBANK ``` ## 4.5 Protection Coordination Hierarchy (Discrimination) ```mermaid flowchart TD U[Utility / Transformer]:::source ACB[ACB 1600A 50kA
Adjustable Release]:::lv1 MCCB1[MCCB 630A 35kA
Adjustable]:::lv2 MCCB2[MCCB 250A 25kA]:::lv3 MCB1[MCB 100A 10kA]:::lv4 MCB2[MCB 63A 10kA]:::lv5 MPCB[MPCB 32A Motor]:::lv6 LOAD[Motor / Final Load]:::load U --> ACB --> MCCB1 --> MCCB2 --> MCB1 --> MCB2 --> MPCB --> LOAD classDef source fill:#ffd700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef lv1 fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef lv2 fill:#ffa07a,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px classDef lv3 fill:#ffeb3b,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px classDef lv4 fill:#90ee90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px classDef lv5 fill:#87ceeb,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px classDef lv6 fill:#dda0dd,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px classDef load fill:#95e1d3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define "correction factor" as applied to power cable sizing. List any four standard correction factors used in LT cable design.** **Model Answer:** A correction factor (also called a derating factor) is a **multiplicative coefficient** applied to the tabulated ampacity of a cable to account for installation conditions differing from the standard reference. **Four standard correction factors:** 1. **$K_1$** — Ambient temperature correction 2. **$K_2$** — Grouping / bunching of circuits correction 3. **$K_3$** — Soil thermal resistivity correction (for UG cables) 4. **$K_4$** — Depth of laying correction (for UG cables) ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between MSB, SSB and MCC in an industrial LV distribution system.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | MSB | SSB | MCC | |---|---|---|---| | **Full form** | Main Switch Board | Sub Switch Board | Motor Control Centre | | **Position** | Top of hierarchy (fed by transformer) | Fed from MSB | Fed from MSB / SSB | | **Function** | Receives power, feeds SSBs & MCCs | Feeds lighting, small power, sub-circuits | Controls motors (starters, contactors, OLR) | | **Incomer device** | ACB / MCCB | MCCB | MCCB / SDF | | **Outgoing** | MCCBs to SSBs, MCCs, capacitors | MCBs to final circuits | Contactors + OLR to motors | ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — With Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) > **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** > **(a)** A 3-core **XLPE aluminium cable** of size 150 mm² has a tabulated current rating of **220 A** (for direct burial in ground, standard conditions). It is to be installed with the following derating conditions: > - Ground temperature = 40 °C (Reference: 30 °C) > - Soil thermal resistivity = 2.5 K·m/W > - 6 circuits grouped in the same trench, touching > - Depth of laying = 0.9 m > - $\theta_{max}$ for XLPE = 90 °C > > Determine the **corrected ampacity** of the cable. **(7 Marks)** > **(b)** Design the **bus bar** for a 3-phase, 415 V MSB delivering **1250 kVA** at 0.85 p.f. lagging. Use copper, painted, with $\delta = 1.4$ A/mm². State the size in mm and verify short-circuit withstand for $I_{sc} = 35$ kA, $t = 1$ s. Take material constant $\alpha = 142$ for copper. **(7 Marks)** --- ### Model Solution for Question A #### Part (a) — Corrected Ampacity **Step 1 — Temperature Correction $K_1$:** $$K_1 = \sqrt{\frac{90 - 40}{90 - 30}} = \sqrt{\frac{50}{60}} = \sqrt{0.8333}$$ $$K_1 = 0.9129$$ **Step 2 — Soil Thermal Resistivity $K_3$ (for $\rho = 2.5$ K·m/W):** $$K_3 = 0.76$$ **Step 3 — Grouping $K_2$ (6 circuits, touching):** $$K_2 = 0.57$$ **Step 4 — Depth of Laying $K_4$ (0.9 m):** $$K_4 = 0.93$$ **Step 5 — Corrected Ampacity $I_z$:** $$I_z = 220 \times 0.9129 \times 0.57 \times 0.76 \times 0.93$$ $$I_z = 220 \times 0.9129 = 200.84 \text{ A}$$ $$I_z = 200.84 \times 0.57 = 114.48 \text{ A}$$ $$I_z = 114.48 \times 0.76 = 87.00 \text{ A}$$ $$I_z = 87.00 \times 0.93 = 80.91 \text{ A}$$ $$\boxed{I_z \approx 80.9 \text{ A}}$$ **Conclusion:** The 150 mm² Al XLPE cable, though rated 220 A nominally, can only carry **~81 A** under the given field conditions. #### Part (b) — Bus Bar Design **Step 1 — Full-Load Current:** $$I_b = \frac{S}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L} = \frac{1250 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times 415} = \frac{1\,250\,000}{718.8} = 1738.9 \text{ A}$$ **Step 2 — Required Cross-Section:** $$A = \frac{I_b}{\delta} = \frac{1738.9}{1.4} = 1242.1 \text{ mm}^2$$ **Step 3 — Select Standard Size:** Standard copper bar: **100 mm × 12.5 mm = 1250 mm²** (closest standard) or **120 mm × 12 mm = 1440 mm²**. Use **100 mm × 12 mm = 1200 mm²** (one bar per phase) or two bars per phase 80 mm × 10 mm = 1600 mm². **Step 4 — Short-Circuit Withstand Check:** $$A_{min} = \frac{I_{cw}}{\alpha \cdot \sqrt{t}} = \frac{35 \times 10^3}{142 \times \sqrt{1}} = \frac{35\,000}{142} = 246.5 \text{ mm}^2$$ Since selected $A = 1200 \text{ mm}^2 \gg 246.5 \text{ mm}^2$ → **Safe**. **Step 5 — Conclusion:** $$\boxed{\text{Select Cu bus bar } 100 \times 12 \text{ mm} \text{ per phase, } A = 1200 \text{ mm}^2, \text{ withstand verified.}}$$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) > **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** > **(a)** A 75 kW, 3-phase, 415 V, 50 Hz squirrel-cage induction motor (efficiency 92 %, p.f. 0.85) is to be supplied by a **4-core PVC insulated, armoured, Cu cable** buried directly in ground. Tabulated ampacity of 50 mm² Cu PVC cable is **170 A**. Derating factors are: > - $K_1 = 0.94$ (ground temp = 35 °C) > - $K_2 = 0.80$ (2 circuits touching) > - $K_3 = 0.86$ (soil $\rho = 2.0$ K·m/W) > - $K_4 = 0.96$ (depth 0.7 m) > > Check whether the **50 mm² cable is suitable**. **(7 Marks)** > **(b)** Explain with a neat SLD, the **constituents of an MCC** section for three motors of 30 kW, 22 kW and 15 kW. List the protection and starting arrangements. **(7 Marks)** --- ### Model Solution for Question B #### Part (a) — Cable Suitability Check **Step 1 — Compute Full-Load Current of Motor:** $$I_b = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \eta} = \frac{75 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 0.85 \times 0.92}$$ $$I_b = \frac{75\,000}{562.9} = 133.2 \text{ A}$$ **Step 2 — Compute Corrected Ampacity $I_z$:** $$I_z = 170 \times 0.94 \times 0.80 \times 0.86 \times 0.96$$ $$I_z = 170 \times 0.94 = 159.8 \text{ A}$$ $$I_z = 159.8 \times 0.80 = 127.84 \text{ A}$$ $$I_z = 127.84 \times 0.86 = 109.94 \text{ A}$$ $$I_z = 109.94 \times 0.96 = 105.5 \text{ A}$$ $$\boxed{I_z \approx 105.5 \text{ A}}$$ **Step 3 — Check Coordination $I_b \le I_n \le I_z$:** The protective device rating $I_n$ must satisfy $I_b \le I_n \le I_z$. - $I_b = 133.2$ A - $I_z = 105.5$ A Since $I_b > I_z$, the **50 mm² cable is NOT suitable**. We need a **higher cross-section**. **Step 4 — Try 70 mm² (next standard size, $I_{tab} = 210$ A):** $$I_z = 210 \times 0.94 \times 0.80 \times 0.86 \times 0.96 = 210 \times 0.621 = 130.4 \text{ A}$$ Now $I_b = 133.2$ A still slightly exceeds $I_z = 130.4$ A. **Step 5 — Try 95 mm² ($I_{tab} = 255$ A):** $$I_z = 255 \times 0.621 = 158.4 \text{ A}$$ Now $I_b = 133.2 < I_z = 158.4$ A ✔ — **Suitable**. $$\boxed{\text{Select 95 mm}^2 \text{ 4C Cu PVC cable}}$$ #### Part (b) — MCC Section Constituents A **Motor Control Centre (MCC) section** for three motors contains: | Module | Device | Function | |---|---|---| | **Incoming** | MCCB / SDF (250–400 A) | Isolation + short-circuit protection for entire MCC | | **Bus bar** | TPN aluminium/copper | Distributes power to all feeders | | **Feeder 1 (30 kW)** | MPCB/Contactor + OLR (DOL) | Direct-on-line start; overload + short-circuit | | **Feeder 2 (22 kW)** | MPCB/Contactor + Timer (Star-Delta) | Reduced-voltage start | | **Feeder 3 (15 kW)** | MPCB/Contactor + OLR (DOL) | Direct-on-line start | | **Metering** | MFM (V, A, kW, kWh, Hz) | Energy monitoring | | **Indication** | RYB lamps + ON/OFF pushbuttons | Status indication & control | > **Block-level MCC architecture:** > ``` > [Incomer MCCB] → [Bus Bar 3P+N] → [Feeder 1: DOL + OLR] → 30 kW Motor > → [Feeder 2: Star-Δ + OLR] → 22 kW Motor > → [Feeder 3: DOL + OLR] → 15 kW Motor > → [Spare Feeder: MCCB/Contactor] > ``` > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:** > 1. **Forgetting to apply all four derating factors** — KTU expects students to write each one step-wise. Half-mark deduction per missed factor. > 2. **Confusing $I_b$ vs $I_z$** — Students often equate motor FLC with cable rating, ignoring derating. Always write: *"Design current $I_b$ must be ≤ corrected ampacity $I_z$."* > 3. **Using $I_{tab}$ directly without derating** — Loses 3-4 marks immediately. > 4. **Wrong unit in current density** — Always use **A/mm²**, not A/m². > 5. **Selecting non-standard bus bar sizes** — Stick to standard 12, 16, 20, 25, 30, 40, 50, 60, 80, 100, 120 mm widths and 5, 6, 8, 10, 12 mm thicknesses. > 6. **Forgetting short-circuit withstand** — IS 8623 mandates $I_{cw}$ check for 1 s. Marks lost if omitted. > 7. **Mixing up ACB/MCCB/MCB application ranges** — Examiner expects a clear table. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **🔑 Rapid Revision Checklist (Exam Must-Know):** - [x] **Correction Factor Formula:** $I_z = I_{tab} \times K_1 \times K_2 \times K_3 \times K_4$ (always list all four factors explicitly). - [x] **Standard Reference Conditions:** Ground T = 30 °C, Air T = 40 °C, Soil ρ = 1.5 K·m/W, Depth = 0.5 m, Single circuit. - [x] **$K_1 < 1$** when actual ambient is **higher** than reference; $K_1 > 1$ when cooler. - [x] **XLPE $\theta_{max} = 90$ °C**; **PVC $\theta_{max} = 70$ °C**. - [x] **Coordination rule:** $I_b \le I_n \le I_z$ (design current ≤ device rating ≤ cable ampacity). - [x] **Voltage drop limits:** ≤ **3 %** for motors, ≤ **5 %** for lighting (from transformer LV to consumer terminals). - [x] **Bus bar current densities:** Cu = 1.2–1.6 A/mm²; Al = 0.7–0.8 A/mm² (indoor, painted). - [x] **Bus bar short-circuit check:** $A_{min} = I_{cw}/(\alpha \sqrt{t})$ — α = 142 (Cu), 94 (Al). - [x] **Standard MSB structure:** Incomer (ACB) → Metering → Bus bar → Feeders (MCCBs) → Outgoing. - [x] **MCC contains:** Incomer MCCB, bus bar, contactors, OLRs, MPBs, starters (DOL/Star-Δ/Soft). - [x] **ACB** is for **>800 A**; **MCCB** for **100–1600 A**; **MCB** for **<100 A**. - [x] **Copper** has 100 % IACS conductivity, Al has 61 % — Al needs ~1.6× larger cross-section for same current. - [x] **LT cables preferred:** XLPE > PVC (better ampacity, longer life, higher temp). - [x] **When in doubt, always size up** — round up to the next standard cross-section, never round down. - [x] **Three-phase FLC formula:** $I_b = P/(\sqrt{3} \cdot V \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \eta)$ for motors (include efficiency). - [x] **Derating can reduce ampacity by 40–60 %** in dense groupings — never skip it. - [x] **For UG cables in trefoil, single circuit, 1.5 K·m/W, 0.5 m depth = reference** (K = 1.0 each). - [x] **Always state the standard** (IS 1554, IS 7098, IEC 60364) when quoting tabulated values — earns examiner's goodwill marks.

topics.title: Voltage Drop
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEOT752 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Voltage Drop # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Voltage Drop** is defined as the reduction in electric potential (voltage) along the path of a current flowing through an electrical circuit, caused by the **impedance** (resistance + reactance) of the conducting wires/cables connecting the source to the load. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition (Adopt this verbatim in 2-mark answers):** > *"Voltage drop is the loss of voltage across a conductor due to its resistance and reactance when current flows through it, expressed as the difference between the voltage at the sending end and the voltage at the receiving end of a feeder/distribution line."* Mathematically, it is given by: $$V_{drop} = V_{sending\ end} - V_{receiving\ end}$$ For practical engineering design, it is expressed as a **percentage voltage drop**: $$\% V_{drop} = \frac{V_{sending} - V_{receiving}}{V_{rated}} \times 100$$ ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **"Water in a Leaky Hose" Analogy** 🚰 > > Imagine water flowing from an overhead tank (voltage source) to your kitchen tap (load) through a long, narrow garden hose (cable conductor). Even though the tank has 10 meters of head pressure, the water reaches the tap at much lower pressure. Why? Because the **friction inside the hose walls** (analogous to conductor resistance) and the **narrowing/roughness** of the pipe (analogous to reactance) consume some of that energy as heat before the water reaches the tap. > > - **Longer hose = bigger pressure drop** → Longer cable length = higher voltage drop > - **Narrower hose = bigger pressure drop** → Smaller cable cross-section = higher resistance = higher voltage drop > - **Higher water flow rate = bigger pressure drop** → Higher current = higher voltage drop > > This is exactly what happens in electrical cables! The "pressure" lost in the hose is the **voltage** lost across the cable. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Resistivity of Copper ($\rho_{Cu}$)** = **$1.724 \times 10^{-8}\ \Omega \cdot m$ at 20°C** - **Resistivity of Aluminium ($\rho_{Al}$)** = **$2.826 \times 10^{-8}\ \Omega \cdot m$ at 20°C** - **Temperature coefficient of Copper ($\alpha_{Cu}$)** = **$0.00393\ /^{\circ}C$ at 20°C** - **Temperature coefficient of Aluminium ($\alpha_{Al}$)** = **$0.00403\ /^{\circ}C$ at 20°C** - Standard reference temperature for voltage drop calculation = **20°C** (or 30°C as per IS 694) - Permissible voltage drop limit (IS 732 / NEC / IEC 60364) = **3% for power circuits, 5% total combined (feeder + branch)** - For lighting circuits: **3%** is the recommended limit > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** > Voltage drop calculations are essential for proper **cable sizing**, ensuring that the voltage at the load terminals remains within acceptable limits (typically ±5% of nominal voltage), and that equipment operates efficiently without performance degradation or overheating. ## 1.4 Why is Voltage Drop Critical in Design? If the voltage drop is excessive at the load: 1. **Motors** draw higher current, leading to overheating, reduced torque, and premature failure 2. **Incandescent lamps** become dim and have reduced lifespan 3. **Electronic equipment** may malfunction, reset, or get damaged 4. **Power factor** deteriorates, increasing electricity bills 5. **Star-delta starters** may fail to operate correctly This is why every electrical design MUST include a **voltage drop verification** as a mandatory step before finalizing conductor cross-section. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Components Causing Voltage Drop In an AC electrical circuit, the total opposition to current flow is the **impedance (Z)**, which has two components: | Component | Symbol | Cause | Significance | |-----------|--------|-------|--------------| | **Resistance** | R | Material property of conductor (opposition to current) | Dominant in DC and low-voltage AC systems | | **Reactance** | X | Magnetic field effects (inductance of cable) | Dominant in HV transmission lines and large conductors | The total impedance: $$Z = \sqrt{R^2 + X^2}\ \ \Omega$$ The voltage drop in a single-phase AC system is the **vectorial (phasor) sum** of: - $V_R = I \cdot R \cdot \cos\phi$ (in-phase component) - $V_X = I \cdot X \cdot \sin\phi$ (quadrature component) > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** The voltage drop is NOT simply $I \times Z$ (which is the **apparent voltage drop**). The **effective voltage drop** (which equals the difference between sending and receiving end voltages) is the phasor projection: > $$V_{drop} = I \cdot (R \cos\phi + X \sin\phi)$$ > This is the formula that KTU expects you to use. ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Flow for Calculation **Step 1: Identify circuit type** - Is it **DC**, **single-phase AC**, or **three-phase AC**? - This determines which formula to apply. **Step 2: Identify conductor material** - **Copper (Cu)** — for indoor wiring, switchgear, small cables - **Aluminium (Al)** — for overhead lines, large power cables (cheaper) **Step 3: Determine conductor resistance** $$R = \frac{\rho \cdot L}{A}\ \ \Omega$$ Where: - $\rho$ = resistivity ($\Omega \cdot m$) - $L$ = **total length** of conductor (go + return path) - $A$ = cross-sectional area ($m^2$) **Step 4: Determine conductor reactance** $$X = 2\pi f L_{inductance}\ \ \Omega$$ - For LV cables: $X \approx 0.08\ \Omega/km$ (typical for 3-core armoured cables) - For overhead lines: $X \approx 0.4\ \Omega/km$ (higher due to spacing) **Step 5: Apply correct formula (see table below)** **Step 6: Check permissible limits** - $\%V_{drop} = \dfrac{V_{drop}}{V_{rated}} \times 100$ - Must be $\leq$ 5% (IS 732) for total system ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Circuit Type | Voltage Drop Formula | Key Variables | Typical Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **DC (2-wire)** | $V_d = \dfrac{2 \cdot I \cdot \rho \cdot L}{A}$ | $\rho$, $L$ (one-way), $A$, $I$ | Battery, DC drives, solar | | 2 | **DC (3-wire)** | $V_d = \dfrac{I \cdot \rho \cdot L}{A}$ | 3-wire system, $L$ one-way | Traction, large DC loads | | 3 | **Single-phase AC** | $V_d = 2 \cdot I \cdot (R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)$ | Factor of 2 for go+return | Domestic supply, lighting | | 4 | **Three-phase AC** | $V_d = \sqrt{3} \cdot I \cdot (R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)$ | $\sqrt{3}$ for 3-φ, $I$ = line current | Motors, feeders, large loads | | 5 | **% Voltage Drop** | $\%V_d = \dfrac{V_d}{V_{rated}} \times 100$ | $V_{rated}$ = system voltage | Compliance check | | 6 | **Resistance per km** | $R_{per\ km} = \dfrac{\rho \times 1000}{A \times 10^{-6}}\ \Omega/km$ | $A$ in $mm^2$ | Cable catalog lookup | | 7 | **% VD with load** | $\%V_d = \dfrac{\sqrt{3} \cdot I \cdot L \cdot (r\cos\phi + x\sin\phi)}{10 \cdot V_{rated}}$ | $L$ in km, $V$ in kV | Direct % calculation | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Table Notation Convention:** The angle bracket $\vert x \vert$ should never be used in markdown tables. In this context, for example, $\vert \vec{V} \vert$ should be written as $\vert \vec{V} \vert$ using `\vert` in LaTeX when needed in a table. ## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility - **Cable Sizing Selection:** Voltage drop is the **second-most important criterion** (after current-carrying capacity) for selecting cable cross-section. KTU often asks: "Select a cable for given load and distance, then verify by voltage drop." - **IS 732 / NEC Compliance:** Indian and international standards mandate a maximum **5% total drop** (3% feeder + 2% branch, or similar split). - **Cost Optimization:** Designers balance cable cost (larger cross-section = costlier) against energy loss penalty (smaller cross-section = more I²R losses). - **Renewable Integration:** Solar PV systems require strict voltage drop limits to maximize energy yield. - **EV Charging Stations:** Long cable runs demand careful voltage drop analysis to ensure proper charging. > [!NOTE] > **Why do we need the factor "2" in single-phase and "$\sqrt{3}$" in three-phase?** > - In **single-phase**, current must travel through both the **phase conductor** and the **neutral conductor** (go + return path) → length doubles. > - In **three-phase**, the voltage drop is taken as **line-to-line**, which is $\sqrt{3}$ times the line-to-neutral drop, and only one-way path length is considered. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Voltage Drop in Single-Phase AC Circuit Consider a single-phase AC circuit delivering load current $I$ at lagging power factor $\cos\phi$ over a distance $L$ (one-way). **Step 1: Define the system parameters** Let sending end voltage be $V_S$ and receiving end voltage be $V_R$. Load current phasor: $\vec{I} = I \angle -\phi$ (lagging) **Step 2: Express total loop impedance** For single-phase, the loop includes both the line and neutral conductors: $$Z_{loop} = R_{loop} + jX_{loop} = 2R + j2X$$ where $R$ and $X$ are the resistance and reactance of **one conductor**. **Step 3: Apply KVL around the loop** $$\vec{V_S} = \vec{V_R} + \vec{I} \cdot Z_{loop}$$ **Step 4: Substituting values** $$\vec{V_S} = \vec{V_R} + I\angle{-\phi} \cdot (2R + j2X)$$ **Step 5: Take the phasor diagram approach** Drawing the phasor diagram, with $\vec{V_R}$ along the reference axis, the voltage drop across the impedance is: $$\vec{V_d} = \vec{I} \cdot (R + jX) \times 2 = 2I(R + jX)$$ **Step 6: Resolve into components** Resolving $\vec{V_d}$ into in-phase (with $V_R$) and quadrature components: - **In-phase component** (along $V_R$): $2I(R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)$ - **Quadrature component** (perpendicular): $2I(X\cos\phi - R\sin\phi)$ **Step 7: For small voltage drops (< 10%)** Since the quadrature component only causes a small phase shift, the **effective voltage drop** (algebraic difference between sending and receiving end magnitudes) is approximately: $$V_{drop} = \vec{V_S} - \vec{V_R} \approx 2I(R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)$$ **Final Result — Single Phase:** $$\boxed{V_{drop} = 2 \cdot I \cdot (R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)\ \ \text{Volts}}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Voltage Drop in Three-Phase AC Circuit **Step 1: Consider a balanced 3-phase system** Three identical conductors carry balanced currents $I_A$, $I_B$, $I_C$ at lagging power factor $\cos\phi$. **Step 2: Take one phase as reference** Phase A: $\vec{I_A} = I\angle 0°$ (taking $-30°$ shift from line voltage) The phase-to-neutral voltage at receiving end: $V_{RN} = \dfrac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}$ **Step 3: Voltage drop in phase A** $$\vec{V_{drop,A}} = \vec{I_A} \cdot Z = I(R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi) + jI(X\cos\phi - R\sin\phi)$$ **Step 4: Line-to-line voltage drop** Since $V_{AB} = V_{AN} - V_{BN}$, and by symmetry, the line-to-line voltage drop magnitude is: $$V_{drop,LL} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{drop,phase}$$ **Step 5: Effective magnitude (assuming small drop)** $$V_{drop,LL} \approx \sqrt{3} \cdot I \cdot (R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)$$ **Final Result — Three Phase:** $$\boxed{V_{drop} = \sqrt{3} \cdot I \cdot (R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)\ \ \text{Volts}}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Conductor Resistance from First Principles **Step 1: Start with the resistance formula** $$R = \rho \cdot \dfrac{L}{A}$$ Where: - $\rho$ = resistivity in $\Omega \cdot m$ - $L$ = length in meters - $A$ = cross-sectional area in $m^2$ **Step 2: Convert to convenient engineering units** Let $L$ be in meters and $A$ in $mm^2$. Since $1\ m^2 = 10^6\ mm^2$: $$R = \rho \cdot \dfrac{L}{A \times 10^{-6}}\ \Omega$$ **Step 3: For length in km ($L_{km}$)** $$R = \rho \cdot \dfrac{L_{km} \times 1000}{A \times 10^{-6}} = \rho \cdot \dfrac{10^9 \cdot L_{km}}{A}\ \Omega$$ But this gives resistance in $\Omega$ for the total length. **Step 4: Resistance per km (most useful for cable selection)** $$R_{per\ km} = \dfrac{\rho \times 10^9}{A}\ \Omega/km$$ For Copper with $\rho = 1.724 \times 10^{-8}\ \Omega \cdot m$: $$R_{Cu,per\ km} = \dfrac{0.01724}{A}\ \Omega/km$$ For Aluminium with $\rho = 2.826 \times 10^{-8}\ \Omega \cdot m$: $$R_{Al,per\ km} = \dfrac{0.02826}{A}\ \Omega/km$$ ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style — 14 Mark Question) > **Problem:** > A 3-phase, 400 V, 50 Hz induction motor draws 80 A at a power factor of 0.8 lagging. The supply cable is 3-core copper, with conductor cross-section 35 mm². The cable run length is 80 m. The cable has resistance $R = 0.524\ \Omega/km$ and reactance $X = 0.08\ \Omega/km$. Calculate: > (a) The total voltage drop in the cable > (b) The percentage voltage drop > (c) The receiving end voltage **Solution:** **Given Data:** - System: 3-phase, 4-wire - Rated voltage: $V_L = 400$ V - Line current: $I = 80$ A - Power factor: $\cos\phi = 0.8$ lagging → $\sin\phi = 0.6$ - Length: $L = 80$ m $= 0.08$ km - $R = 0.524\ \Omega/km$, $X = 0.08\ \Omega/km$ **Part (a): Total Voltage Drop** Using the 3-phase formula: $$V_d = \sqrt{3} \cdot I \cdot (R \cdot \cos\phi + X \cdot \sin\phi) \cdot L_{km}$$ Substituting: $$V_d = \sqrt{3} \times 80 \times (0.524 \times 0.8 + 0.08 \times 0.6) \times 0.08$$ Calculate each component: - $R\cos\phi = 0.524 \times 0.8 = 0.4192\ \Omega/km$ - $X\sin\phi = 0.08 \times 0.6 = 0.048\ \Omega/km$ - $R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi = 0.4192 + 0.048 = 0.4672\ \Omega/km$ Then: $$V_d = \sqrt{3} \times 80 \times 0.4672 \times 0.08$$ $$V_d = 1.732 \times 80 \times 0.4672 \times 0.08$$ $$V_d = 5.180\ V$$ **Part (b): Percentage Voltage Drop** $$\%V_d = \dfrac{V_d}{V_L} \times 100 = \dfrac{5.18}{400} \times 100 = 1.295\%$$ **Part (c): Receiving End Voltage** $$V_R = V_S - V_d = 400 - 5.18 = 394.82\ V$$ **Inference:** $\%V_d = 1.295\%$ is well within the IS 732 permissible limit of 5%. ✅ ## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix: How to Select Cable by Voltage Drop | Step | Action | Criterion | |------|--------|-----------| | 1 | Compute the load current $I$ | From nameplate or load list | | 2 | Assume power factor $\cos\phi$ | 0.8 for motors, 1.0 for resistive loads | | 3 | Determine permissible $\%V_d$ | 3% feeder + 2% branch = 5% total | | 4 | Compute $V_{d,max} = \dfrac{\%V_d \times V}{100}$ | Maximum allowed drop in volts | | 5 | Calculate required resistance | $R = \dfrac{V_{d,max}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi \cdot L}$ (3-φ) | | 6 | Determine minimum $A$ | $A = \dfrac{\rho \cdot L_{km} \cdot 10^3}{R_{per\ km}}$ | | 7 | Select next **standard cable size** | 1.5, 2.5, 4, 6, 10, 16, 25, 35, 50, 70, 95, 120, 150, 185, 240, 300 mm² | | 8 | Verify by current carrying capacity | Must satisfy both criteria (ampacity + VD) | | 9 | Apply derating factors if any | Grouping, ambient temp, depth of laying | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Tip:** When a question asks to "design" a cable, you must check **both** (i) current carrying capacity (ampacity) **and** (ii) voltage drop. The larger of the two selected cross-sections is the final answer. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram — Voltage Drop in a Feeder ```mermaid flowchart LR SourceBus["SOURCE / SENDING END
V = 415 V"] --> CableA["CABLE IMPEDANCE
Z = R + jX"] CableA --> LoadBus["LOAD / RECEIVING END
V' = 410 V"] SourceBus -. "Current I flows
cos phi = 0.8 lag" .-> CableA CableA -. "Voltage Drop
Vd = 5 V" .-> LoadBus LoadBus --> MotorLoad["3-PHASE MOTOR
Drawing 80 A"] style SourceBus fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff style CableA fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff style LoadBus fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff style MotorLoad fill:#f4a300,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Phasor Diagram — Voltage Drop Components ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PHASOR_DIAG["PHASOR DIAGRAM OF VOLTAGE DROP"] VR["Receiving End Voltage VR
(Reference Axis)"] IRcosphi["Component: I x R x cos phi
(In-phase with VR)"] IXsinphi["Component: I x X x sin phi
(Leading by 90 deg)"] VS["Sending End Voltage VS
(Phasor Sum Resultant)"] end VR --> IRcosphi IRcosphi --> VS VR --> IXsinphi IXsinphi --> VS style VR fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff style IRcosphi fill:#4472c4,stroke:#000,color:#fff style IXsinphi fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff style VS fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture — Voltage Drop Calculation Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD A["STEP 1: Input Circuit Parameters
Voltage, Current, Power Factor, Length"] --> B["STEP 2: Identify Conductor
Material: Cu or Al"] B --> C["STEP 3: Look up Cable Specs
R per km, X per km from Catalog"] C --> D["STEP 4: Choose Formula
DC, 1-Phase, or 3-Phase"] D --> E["STEP 5: Calculate Vd
Vd = sqrt 3 I R cos phi + X sin phi L"] E --> F["STEP 6: Compute % Vd
percent = Vd/V rated x 100"] F --> G{"STEP 7: Check Limit
Is percent Vd less than 5 percent?"} G -- YES --> H["STEP 8: DESIGN ACCEPTED
Receiving end voltage within tolerance"] G -- NO --> I["STEP 9: Increase Cable Size
Go back to Step 3 with larger area"] I --> C style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff style E fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff style G fill:#f4a300,stroke:#000,color:#000 style H fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff style I fill:#7b1f1f,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` ## 4.4 Diagram — Single-Phase vs Three-Phase Voltage Drop Comparison ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SP["SINGLE PHASE 2 WIRE"] SP1["Line Conductor
Current I"] --> SP2["Load"] SP2 --> SP3["Neutral Conductor
Return Current I"] SP3 --> SP4["Source"] end subgraph TP["THREE PHASE 3 WIRE"] TP1["Phase A Conductor"] --> TP2["3-Phase Load"] TP3["Phase B Conductor"] --> TP2 TP4["Phase C Conductor"] --> TP2 end style SP1 fill:#4472c4,stroke:#000,color:#fff style SP2 fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff style SP3 fill:#4472c4,stroke:#000,color:#fff style TP1 fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff style TP3 fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff style TP4 fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff style TP2 fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Schematic Note:** Single-phase uses **2 conductors** (loop length = 2L), while three-phase uses **3 conductors** carrying balanced currents (loop length = 1L per phase). This is the geometric reason for the factor "2" vs "$\sqrt{3}$" in their respective formulas. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (2 / 3 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam - May 2023]** **Q1. Define voltage drop in an electrical circuit. Mention its permissible limit as per IS standards.** **Model Answer:** Voltage drop is the reduction in voltage between the sending end and the receiving end of a feeder/distribution line caused by the impedance (resistance and reactance) of the conductor. $$V_{drop} = V_{sending} - V_{receiving}$$ As per **IS 732** and **NEC**, the permissible voltage drop is: - **3%** for the feeder portion alone - **5%** total combined (feeder + branch) when measured at the most remote outlet - For lighting loads, **3%** is recommended to prevent visible flicker. --- > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]** **Q2. Why is voltage drop important in cable sizing? List two consequences of excessive voltage drop.** **Model Answer:** Voltage drop is critical in cable sizing because it ensures that equipment receives voltage within its rated tolerance (±5% of nominal). **Two consequences of excessive voltage drop:** 1. **Motors:** Draw higher current, leading to overheating, reduced starting torque, and shortened life. 2. **Lamps (Incandescent/LED):** Reduced brightness and shortened lifespan; LEDs may flicker or fail to operate. --- ## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Internal Choice Pattern]** ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1** **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for voltage drop in a 3-phase balanced AC system. State the assumptions made. **(b) [7 Marks]** A 3-phase, 415 V, 50 Hz induction motor has a full-load current of 60 A at 0.85 power factor lagging. The distance from the MCC to the motor is 75 m. Calculate the size of the 3-core copper cable required to limit the voltage drop to 3%. Assume cable data: $R = 0.524\ \Omega/km$ for 35 mm², $R = 0.387\ \Omega/km$ for 50 mm², $R = 0.268\ \Omega/km$ for 70 mm², $X = 0.08\ \Omega/km$ for all sizes. ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Alternative)** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a phasor diagram the difference between apparent voltage drop and effective voltage drop in an AC circuit. Why do we use the effective value in design? **(b) [7 Marks]** A single-phase, 230 V, 5 kW heater is connected through a 30 m long copper cable. The cable resistance is $1.2\ \Omega$ per km per conductor. Calculate (i) the voltage drop in the cable, (ii) the percentage voltage drop, (iii) the receiving end voltage, and (iv) verify whether the cable meets the IS 732 limit. --- ### **Solutions** #### **Question A Solutions:** **(a) [7 Marks] Derivation of 3-Phase Voltage Drop:** The derivation is covered in detail in **Section 3.2** above. Key steps for writing: 1. **State the assumptions:** - Balanced 3-phase load - Sinusoidal steady-state - Voltage drop < 10% (so the approximation is valid) - Same impedance per phase 2. **Write the KVL equation per phase:** $$\vec{V_{SN}} = \vec{V_{RN}} + \vec{I_A} \cdot (R + jX)$$ 3. **Resolve into components using the power factor angle $\phi$:** $$V_d = I(R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)$$ 4. **Multiply by $\sqrt{3}$ for line-to-line:** $$\boxed{V_d = \sqrt{3} \cdot I \cdot (R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)\ \ \text{Volts}}$$ **(b) [7 Marks] Cable Sizing Calculation:** **Given:** $V = 415$ V (3-φ), $I = 60$ A, $\cos\phi = 0.85$ lag → $\sin\phi = 0.527$, $L = 75$ m = 0.075 km, $X = 0.08\ \Omega/km$ **Maximum allowable voltage drop:** $$V_{d,max} = 3\% \times 415 = 12.45\ V$$ **For 35 mm² cable:** $$V_d = \sqrt{3} \times 60 \times (0.524 \times 0.85 + 0.08 \times 0.527) \times 0.075$$ $$= 1.732 \times 60 \times (0.4454 + 0.04216) \times 0.075$$ $$= 1.732 \times 60 \times 0.4876 \times 0.075 = 3.80\ V$$ $$\%V_d = \dfrac{3.80}{415} \times 100 = 0.916\%\ \checkmark \text{ (within 3%)}$$ **Conclusion:** 35 mm² cable is **adequate** for voltage drop (0.916% < 3%), but the designer must also check the **ampacity** (current carrying capacity) — for 60 A in 3-core Cu, 35 mm² XLPE cable can carry ~100 A, so ampacity is also satisfied. **Final selection: 35 mm².** --- #### **Question B Solutions:** **(a) [7 Marks] Phasor Diagram Explanation:** The phasor diagram should show: - $\vec{V_R}$ along the reference (horizontal) axis - Current $\vec{I}$ lagging by angle $\phi$ - Voltage drop $\vec{I} \cdot R$ in phase with $\vec{I}$ - Voltage drop $\vec{I} \cdot X$ leading $\vec{I}$ by 90° **Apparent voltage drop** $= I \cdot Z$ (the magnitude of the phasor $I \cdot \sqrt{R^2 + X^2}$) **Effective voltage drop** $= I(R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)$ (the projection of the voltage drop phasor on the receiving end voltage axis) The **effective** value is used because it represents the **actual algebraic difference** between $V_S$ and $V_R$ magnitudes, which determines whether the load gets adequate voltage. **(b) [7 Marks] Single-Phase Heater Calculation:** **Given:** $V_S = 230$ V, $P = 5$ kW (resistive load → $\cos\phi = 1.0$), $L = 30$ m (one-way), $R_{conductor} = 1.2\ \Omega/km$ **Step 1: Calculate load current** $$I = \dfrac{P}{V} = \dfrac{5000}{230} = 21.74\ A$$ **Step 2: Calculate total loop resistance (go + return)** $$R_{loop} = 2 \times 1.2 \times 0.030 = 0.072\ \Omega$$ **Step 3: Calculate voltage drop (resistive load, no reactance effect)** $$V_d = I \cdot R_{loop} = 21.74 \times 0.072 = 1.565\ V$$ **Step 4: Percentage voltage drop** $$\%V_d = \dfrac{1.565}{230} \times 100 = 0.68\%$$ **Step 5: Receiving end voltage** $$V_R = 230 - 1.565 = 228.43\ V$$ **Step 6: IS 732 verification** $\%V_d = 0.68\% \ll 3\%$ → **Cable meets the IS 732 limit** ✅ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Forgetting the factor "2" in single-phase:** Many students use $I \cdot R$ directly without doubling the resistance for the neutral return path. **Lose 2-3 marks instantly.** > 2. **Using $I \cdot Z$ instead of effective drop:** Some students compute $\sqrt{R^2 + X^2}$ and multiply, which gives the apparent drop, not the effective drop. **The KTU board expects the projection formula.** > 3. **Mixing up units:** $R$ must be in $\Omega/km$ and $L$ in km for direct multiplication. Converting $A$ from $mm^2$ to $m^2$ requires $\times 10^{-6}$, and $L$ from m to km requires $\times 10^{-3}$. **Loses 1-2 marks on unit conversions.** > 4. **Forgetting to convert $L$ in cable sizing:** When the answer is in mm² but you used $L$ in meters, the area will be off by 1000×. **Always work in km for $L$ when using $\Omega/km$ values.** --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for Voltage Drop** - **Definition:** Voltage drop = $V_{sending} - V_{receiving}$, caused by conductor impedance. - **Three Must-Know Formulas:** - **DC:** $V_d = \dfrac{2 I \rho L}{A}$ - **1-φ AC:** $V_d = 2 I (R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)$ - **3-φ AC:** $V_d = \sqrt{3} I (R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)$ - **Key Resistivity Values (20°C):** - $\rho_{Cu} = 1.724 \times 10^{-8}\ \Omega \cdot m$ - $\rho_{Al} = 2.826 \times 10^{-8}\ \Omega \cdot m$ - Use $R_{per\ km} = \dfrac{0.01724}{A}\ \Omega/km$ for copper, $\dfrac{0.02826}{A}$ for aluminum - **IS 732 / NEC Permissible Limits:** - **3%** for feeder only - **5%** total (feeder + branch) - **3%** for lighting - **Temperature Effect:** Resistance increases with temperature. For hot cables (70°C for XLPE), multiply 20°C resistance by approximately **1.2** for copper. - **Cable Selection Rule (KTU):** Select cable based on **maximum** of (i) ampacity requirement and (ii) voltage drop requirement. - **Order of Importance in Design:** 1. Current carrying capacity (ampacity) 2. Voltage drop 3. Short-circuit rating 4. Earth fault loop impedance - **Standard Cable Sizes to Memorize (in mm²):** 1.5, 2.5, 4, 6, 10, 16, 25, 35, 50, 70, 95, 120, 150, 185, 240, 300, 400, 500. - **Effect of Power Factor:** Lower $\cos\phi$ (more lagging) increases the voltage drop. For $\cos\phi = 0.5$ vs $1.0$, the $X\sin\phi$ term becomes significant. - **Reactive Component:** For cables < 50 mm², $X$ is small and often ignored. For cables > 50 mm² or long runs, $X$ must be included. - **Final Memory Aid:** *"VD = k × I × (R cos φ + X sin φ) × L (in km)"* where k = 2 (1-φ) or $\sqrt{3}$ (3-φ) or 2 (DC).

topics.title: Short Circuit Withstand Capacity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT752 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Short Circuit Withstand Capacity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Short Circuit Withstand Capacity (SCWC)** is defined as the maximum value of prospective short-circuit current (both peak and r.m.s. symmetrical) that an electrical equipment, busbar, cable, or switching device can carry for a specified duration (typically **1 second** or **3 seconds**) without suffering any **thermal damage**, **electromechanical deformation**, or **loss of operational capability** that would prevent its continued safe service.

In KTU 2024 scheme context, this is fundamentally a **two-fold verification criterion** that every piece of equipment in an LV/MV switchboard must satisfy:

1. **Thermal Withstand Capacity** — quantified by the *I²t let-through energy* that the device's mass and material can absorb as heat.
2. **Mechanical (Dynamic) Withstand Capacity** — quantified by the *peak short-circuit current* (also called the *making peak* or *electrodynamic force limit*) that produces unacceptable bending, deflection, or contact separation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference:** IS 8623 / IEC 61439 (Low-voltage switchgear assemblies) explicitly state that busbars and structural parts shall be capable of carrying the prospective short-circuit current at the supply terminals for a duration of at least **1 s** without exceeding the allowable temperature rise.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Hammer" Effect

Imagine a long, pressurized water pipeline carrying water at a steady flow. If someone suddenly slams a downstream valve shut, the kinetic energy of the moving water column has nowhere to go — this creates a violent pressure shock wave known as **water hammer**, which can rupture the pipe or burst fittings.

A short circuit in an electrical system is the exact electrical twin of this phenomenon:

| Hydraulic Analogy | Electrical Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Steady water flow | Normal load current |
| Valve slam shut | Short circuit (near-zero impedance path) |
| Water hammer pressure spike | Peak short-circuit current ($i_p$) |
| Pipe wall heating | $I^2 R$ joule heating of busbars |
| Pipe structural failure | Busbar deformation / contact blow-apart |
| Pipe rated pressure | **Short Circuit Withstand Capacity** |

The lesson: just as the pipe manufacturer specifies a *maximum surge pressure rating*, every electrical apparatus must be **rated and verified** to survive the worst-case fault current at its terminals for the *shortest* time it takes for the upstream protective device (breaker/fuse) to clear the fault.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following values are **codified in IEC 60909 / IS 13234** and must be memorized:

- **Standard thermal verification duration:** $t = 1\text{ s}$ (sometimes $3\text{ s}$ for MV systems)
- **Peak-to-RMS ratio** of short-circuit current (factor $n$): $n = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$ (asymmetric) up to $n = 2.69$ (with DC component)
- **DC component decay factor:** $\kappa = 1.02 \text{ to } 1.8$ depending on $R/X$ ratio
- **Peak short-circuit current formula:** $i_p = n \cdot \sqrt{2} \cdot I_k''$
- **Conductor resistivity at final temperature:** calculated at short-circuit final temperature, **not** at $20^{\circ}\text{C}$ or $75^{\circ}\text{C}$ operating temperature.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of a Short Circuit — Step-by-Step Logic

A short circuit occurs when an abnormally low-impedance connection is established between two points of differing potential (phase-to-phase, phase-to-neutral, or phase-to-earth). The resulting current is limited only by the **source impedance** (transformer + cabling) upstream of the fault.

The transient short-circuit current can be decomposed into two orthogonal components:

**Step 1 — Symmetrical (AC) Component ($i_{ac}$)**
This is the steady-state symmetrical RMS current that would flow if the DC transient had decayed to zero:
$$I_k'' = \frac{c \cdot U_n}{\sqrt{3} \cdot Z_k}$$
where $c$ is the IEC 60909 **voltage factor** (1.05 for LV systems > 1 kV nominal, 1.10 for systems 380–1000 V).

**Step 2 — Asymmetrical (DC) Component ($i_{dc}$)**
The DC offset arises because current cannot change instantaneously through an inductance. It decays exponentially with the time constant $\tau = L/R$:
$$i_{dc}(t) = \sqrt{2} \cdot I_k'' \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$

**Step 3 — Total Instantaneous Current**
$$i(t) = \sqrt{2} \cdot I_k'' \left[ \sin(\omega t + \alpha - \phi) + e^{-t/\tau} \sin(\alpha - \phi) \right]$$

**Step 4 — Peak (Making) Current**
At $t = T/2$ (half-cycle after fault inception), the DC component has barely decayed, producing a peak that is **2.0 to 2.5 times** the symmetrical RMS:
$$i_p = \kappa \cdot \sqrt{2} \cdot I_k''$$

**Step 5 — Thermal Energy Dissipated**
The heat generated in a conductor of resistance $R$ during a fault is:
$$Q = \int_0^{t_k} i^2(t) \cdot R \, dt \approx I_k''^2 \cdot t_k \cdot R$$
This is the famous **$I^2 t$ (let-through energy)** in Ampere-squared-seconds.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters:** The upstream circuit breaker has a *break time* (typically 30–80 ms) + *opening time* (10–25 ms). If the busbar or cable downstream cannot absorb the energy released before the breaker fully clears, the **entire switchboard can be destroyed** in under 100 ms. This is why SCWC verification is non-negotiable.

## 2.2 Two-Stage Verification Approach

### A) Thermal Verification (Energy Criterion)

The conductor final temperature $\theta_f$ at the end of the short circuit must remain below the maximum admissible value for the insulation class:

**For PVC insulated cables (Class 70°C):**
$$\theta_f = \left( \frac{I_k''^2 \cdot t_k}{K^2} \right) \cdot \text{conductor property term} + \theta_i$$

**Simplified IEC 60865 formula for busbars:**
$$\theta_f - \theta_i = \left( \frac{I_{th}}{A} \right)^2 \cdot t_k \cdot \frac{\rho_0}{c_w \cdot \delta}$$

where:
- $I_{th}$ = thermal equivalent short-circuit current (A)
- $A$ = cross-section of the conductor ($\text{mm}^2$)
- $K$ = material-dependent constant (e.g., **115 for Cu with PVC**, **74 for Al with PVC**, **142 for Cu with XLPE**)
- $t_k$ = fault duration (s)
- $\rho_0$ = resistivity at $20^{\circ}\text{C}$ (Cu: $1.724 \times 10^{-8}\ \Omega\cdot\text{m}$)
- $c_w$ = specific heat (Cu: 3.45 J/g·K)
- $\delta$ = density (Cu: $8.96\ \text{g/cm}^3$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The cable/conductor's **$K$ factor** is the single most-tested value in KTU 2024 scheme examinations. Memorize the table below.

### B) Mechanical (Dynamic) Verification (Force Criterion)

The electrodynamic force between two parallel conductors carrying current in opposite directions is given by **Ampere's Force Law**:
$$F = \frac{\mu_0}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{I_1 \cdot I_2 \cdot L}{d} = 0.2 \cdot \frac{i_p^2 \cdot L}{d}$$
where:
- $F$ = force in Newtons (N)
- $L$ = span length between insulators (m)
- $d$ = distance between conductor centers (m)
- $i_p$ = peak short-circuit current (kA)

The **bending stress** on the busbar must be less than the allowable stress of the material:
$$\sigma = \frac{M}{W} = \frac{F \cdot L}{10 \cdot W} \leq \sigma_{allow}$$
where $W$ is the section modulus ($\text{mm}^3$).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield for Exam)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Values/Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_k'' = \dfrac{c \cdot U_n}{\sqrt{3} \cdot Z_k}$ | Initial symmetrical SC current | kA |
| 2 | $i_p = \kappa \cdot \sqrt{2} \cdot I_k''$ | Peak making current | kA, $\kappa = 1.02$ to $1.8$ |
| 3 | $F = 0.2 \cdot \dfrac{i_p^2 \cdot L}{d}$ | Electrodynamic force | N |
| 4 | $I_{th} = I_k'' \cdot \sqrt{m+n}$ | Thermal equivalent SC current | kA, $m, n$ = heat factors |
| 5 | $A_{min} = \dfrac{I_{th} \cdot \sqrt{t_k}}{K}$ | Minimum conductor cross-section | $\text{mm}^2$ |
| 6 | $Q = I_k''^2 \cdot t_k$ | Let-through energy | $\text{A}^2\text{s}$ or $\text{kA}^2\text{s}$ |
| 7 | $\sigma = \dfrac{F \cdot L}{10 \cdot W}$ | Bending stress | $\text{N/mm}^2$ |
| 8 | $t_{min} = \left( \dfrac{K \cdot A}{I_k''} \right)^2$ | Min. safe fault clearance time | s |
| 9 | $\theta_f = \theta_i + \left( \dfrac{I_k''}{K \cdot A} \right)^2$ | Final temperature | °C |
| 10 | $Z_k = \dfrac{U_{HV}^2}{S_{sc,HV}} \cdot \left( \dfrac{U_{LV}}{U_{HV}} \right)^2$ | Referred SC impedance | $\Omega$ |

## 2.4 K Factor Table (Material + Insulation) — Must Memorize

| Conductor | Insulation | Initial Temp $\theta_i$ (°C) | Final Temp $\theta_f$ (°C) | $K$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Copper | PVC | 70 | 160 | **115** |
| Copper | XLPE/EPR | 90 | 250 | **143** |
| Copper | Bare | 75 | 200 | **159** |
| Aluminium | PVC | 70 | 160 | **74** |
| Aluminium | XLPE | 90 | 250 | **92** |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Switchgear Manufacturing (L&T, Schneider, Siemens, ABB):** Every busbar chamber is rated in **kA peak / 1 s**. A typical 415 V PCC panel: 50 kA peak for 1 s.
- **Cable Selection in Industrial Plants:** Proper SCWC prevents cable insulation melting and prevents toxic PVC fume release in sub-station fires.
- **Power Plants & Refineries:** API/NFPA standards demand SCWC verification with **3-phase short circuit** at every bus for safe evacuation.
- **Renewable Integration:** With increasing inverter-based generation, SCWC re-evaluation is critical as fault current characteristics have changed.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Peak Short-Circuit Current ($i_p$)

**Given:** A three-phase bolted fault occurs on the secondary of a transformer with per-unit impedance $Z_k$. The pre-fault voltage is $U_n / \sqrt{3}$ per phase.

**Step 1:** Express the source voltage phasor:
$$\underline{U} = \frac{U_n}{\sqrt{3}} \angle 0^{\circ}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the IEC 60909 voltage factor $c$ to account for voltage variations:
$$\underline{U}_0 = \frac{c \cdot U_n}{\sqrt{3}} \angle 0^{\circ}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the initial symmetrical short-circuit current:
$$\underline{I}_k'' = \frac{\underline{U}_0}{\underline{Z}_k} = \frac{c \cdot U_n}{\sqrt{3} \cdot Z_k}$$

**Step 4:** In the time domain, this becomes (with switching angle $\alpha - \phi$ as worst case of $\pi/2$):
$$i_{ac}(t) = \sqrt{2} \cdot I_k'' \cdot \sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 5:** Add the DC component $i_{dc}$ (which is maximum at switching angle $\pi/2$):
$$i_{dc}(t) = \sqrt{2} \cdot I_k'' \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$

**Step 6:** Total current:
$$i(t) = \sqrt{2} \cdot I_k'' \left( \sin \omega t + e^{-t/\tau} \right)$$

**Step 7:** Maximum occurs at $t = T/2 = \pi/\omega$. Substituting:
$$i_p = \sqrt{2} \cdot I_k'' \left( 1 + e^{-\pi / (\omega \tau)} \right)$$

**Step 8:** Define the peak factor $n = 1 + e^{-\pi / (\omega \tau)}$. For typical $R/X$ ratios, $n$ lies between 1.414 and 2.69.

**Step 9:** Final form:
$$\boxed{i_p = n \cdot \sqrt{2} \cdot I_k'' = \kappa \cdot \sqrt{2} \cdot I_k''}$$

where $\kappa$ is the IEC 60909 peak factor: $\kappa = 1.02 + 0.98 e^{-3R/X}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Minimum Cross-Section for Thermal Withstand

**Given:** A cable of cross-section $A$ and material constant $K$ must withstand a short-circuit current $I_k''$ for time $t_k$.

**Step 1:** Heat energy balance — Joule heating equals rise in thermal energy:
$$I_k''^2 \cdot t_k \cdot R = m \cdot c_w \cdot (\theta_f - \theta_i)$$
