**Step 2:** Express mass $m$ and resistance $R$ in terms of cross-section $A$, length $\ell$, density $\delta$, and resistivity $\rho_0$ at $20^{\circ}\text{C}$:
$$m = A \cdot \ell \cdot \delta \qquad R = \frac{\rho_0 \cdot \ell}{A} \cdot \left( 1 + \alpha \cdot \theta_f \right)$$

**Step 3:** Substituting:
$$I_k''^2 \cdot t_k \cdot \frac{\rho_0 (1 + \alpha \theta_f) \ell}{A} = A \ell \delta c_w (\theta_f - \theta_i)$$

**Step 4:** Cancel $\ell$ and rearrange:
$$I_k''^2 \cdot t_k \cdot \rho_0 (1 + \alpha \theta_f) = A^2 \delta c_w (\theta_f - \theta_i)$$

**Step 5:** Group terms on the right-hand side:
$$A^2 = \frac{I_k''^2 \cdot t_k \cdot \rho_0 (1 + \alpha \theta_f)}{\delta c_w (\theta_f - \theta_i)}$$

**Step 6:** Define the empirical constant $K^2$:
$$K^2 = \frac{\delta c_w (\theta_f - \theta_i)}{\rho_0 (1 + \alpha \theta_f)}$$

**Step 7:** Taking square root:
$$\boxed{A \geq \frac{I_k'' \cdot \sqrt{t_k}}{K}}$$

This is the **golden formula** of thermal SCWC and is universally required in KTU 2024 numerical problems.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Electrodynamic Force on Parallel Conductors

**Given:** Two infinitely long parallel conductors of length $L$, separated by distance $d$, carrying currents $I_1$ and $I_2$ in opposite directions.

**Step 1:** Biot-Savart law gives the magnetic flux density at conductor 2 due to conductor 1:
$$B_1 = \frac{\mu_0 \cdot I_1}{2\pi d}$$

**Step 2:** Force per unit length on conductor 2 (Ampere's law):
$$\frac{F}{L} = B_1 \cdot I_2 = \frac{\mu_0 \cdot I_1 I_2}{2\pi d}$$

**Step 3:** For our case, $I_1 = I_2 = i_p$ (peak SC current, worst case):
$$F = \frac{\mu_0}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{i_p^2 \cdot L}{d}$$

**Step 4:** With $\mu_0 / (2\pi) = 2 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{H/m}$, and expressing $i_p$ in kA, $L$ in m, $d$ in m:
$$F = 0.2 \cdot \frac{i_p^2 \cdot L}{d} \quad [\text{N}]$$

**Step 5:** For a simply supported beam of length $L$ (between insulators) with uniformly distributed load $w = F/L$:
$$M_{max} = \frac{w L^2}{10} = \frac{F L}{10}$$

**Step 6:** Bending stress:
$$\sigma = \frac{M_{max}}{W} = \frac{F L}{10 W} \leq \sigma_{allow}$$

where $\sigma_{allow} = 140\ \text{N/mm}^2$ for copper (per IS 8081) and $70\ \text{N/mm}^2$ for aluminium.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A 1000 kVA, 11 kV / 415 V, 5 % impedance transformer supplies a busbar. Determine:
1. Initial symmetrical short-circuit current $I_k''$
2. Peak short-circuit current $i_p$
3. Minimum cross-section of a copper-XLPE cable for 1 s
4. Electrodynamic force on 1 m span, 100 mm centre-to-centre spacing

**Solution:**

**Given:** $S = 1000\ \text{kVA}$, $U_{LV} = 415\ \text{V}$, $u_k = 5\%$, $c = 1.10$ (LV).

**Step 1 — Rated current:**
$$I_r = \frac{1000 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times 415} = 1391\ \text{A}$$

**Step 2 — Symmetrical SC current:**
$$I_k'' = \frac{c \cdot I_r}{u_k} = \frac{1.10 \times 1391}{0.05} = 30,602\ \text{A} \approx 30.6\ \text{kA}$$

**Step 3 — Peak SC current (assume $\kappa = 1.8$):**
$$i_p = 1.8 \times \sqrt{2} \times 30.6 = 77.9\ \text{kA}$$

**Step 4 — Minimum cable size (Cu/XLPE, $K = 143$):**
$$A_{min} = \frac{I_k'' \cdot \sqrt{t_k}}{K} = \frac{30,600 \times 1}{143} = 214\ \text{mm}^2$$
**Select next standard size: 240 mm² Cu/XLPE cable.**

**Step 5 — Electrodynamic force:**
$$F = 0.2 \times \frac{(77.9)^2 \times 1}{0.1} = 12,136\ \text{N} \approx 12.1\ \text{kN}$$

This is the lateral force each busbar will try to deflect with — a 1.2-tonne lateral thrust on a 1 m span. The structural design must resist this with adequate insulators (typically 1250 N each, requiring 10+ insulators in parallel).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Short Circuit Coordination Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source: Utility Grid / Transformer] --> B[Source Impedance Zk]
    B --> C[Main Busbar Chamber]
    C --> D[Outgoing Feeder]
    D --> E[Protective Device: ACB / MCCB / Fuse]
    E --> F[Downstream Load Cable]
    F --> G[Fault Point Location]

    H[Input Data: S, Uk, Un, L, d, K, tk] --> I[SCWC Verification Engine]
    B --> I
    C --> I
    F --> I
    E --> I

    I --> J{Thermal Check}
    I --> K{Mechanical Check}
    I --> L{Clearing Time Check}

    J -->|Pass| M[Busbar OK: theta_f within limit]
    J -->|Fail| N[Upsize Busbar / Reduce tk]

    K -->|Pass| O[Insulator Spacing OK]
    K -->|Fail| P[Add Insulators / Reduce Span L]

    L -->|Pass| Q[Breaker Opens Before Damage]
    L -->|Fail| R[Replace with Faster Breaker / Limit Let-through I²t]

    M --> S[Final Design Certified]
    O --> S
    Q --> S
    N --> T[Re-iterate Design Loop]
    P --> T
    R --> T
    T --> I
```

## 4.2 Decision Tree — Selecting the Correct Verification Path

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start: Identify Equipment to Verify] --> Q1{Equipment Type?}

    Q1 -->|Busbar| Bus[Busbar Verification]
    Q1 -->|Cable| Cab[Cable Verification]
    Q1 -->|Switchgear| Sw[Switchgear Verification]
    Q1 -->|Transformer| Tr[Transformer Verification]

    Bus --> B1[Calculate ik and ip]
    B1 --> B2[Compute Electrodynamic Force F]
    B2 --> B3[Check Bending Stress sigma]
    B3 --> B4{Condition: sigma < sigma_allow}
    B4 -->|Yes| B5[Pass: Mechanical OK]
    B4 -->|No| B6[Fail: Add Supports]

    Cab --> C1[Obtain K from Material Table]
    C1 --> C2[Compute Amin using A = I · sqrt tk / K]
    C2 --> C3{Condition: A_selected >= A_min}
    C3 -->|Yes| C4[Pass: Thermal OK]
    C3 -->|No| C5[Fail: Upsize Cable]

    Sw --> S1[Compare Breaker Icu with ik]
    S1 --> S2{Condition: Icu >= ik}
    S2 -->|Yes| S3[Pass: Switchgear OK]
    S2 -->|No| S4[Fail: Higher Icu Breaker Required]

    Tr --> T1[Compare Transformer I_sc with System ik]
    T1 --> T2{Condition: I_sc >= ik}
    T2 -->|Yes| T3[Pass: Transformer OK]
    T2 -->|No| T4[Fail: Impedance Mismatch]
```

## 4.3 Time-Domain Short-Circuit Current Envelope

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Time t=0: Fault Inception] -->|AC peaks 1.414 x I k| B[t = T/2: Maximum Asymmetry]
    B -->|DC component decays| C[Several Cycles: DC decays to negligible]
    C -->|Pure AC component| D[Steady State: I k symmetric]

    subgraph "Peak Region: ip = kappa x sqrt 2 x I k"
    B
    end

    subgraph "Thermal Integration: Q = integral of i squared dt"
    A
    B
    C
    D
    end
```

## 4.4 Layered Protection Hierarchy (Selectivity)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Level1[Level 1: Utility Grid Incomer] -->|100 kA rupturing capacity| Level2
    Level2[Level 2: Main ACB 4000 A] -->|65 kA Icu, 50 kA Icw/1s| Level3
    Level3[Level 3: Sub-cubicle ACB 1600 A] -->|50 kA Icu, 35 kA Icw/1s| Level4
    Level4[Level 4: MCCB 630 A] -->|35 kA Icu, 25 kA Icw/1s| Level5
    Level5[Level 5: MCB 100 A] -->|10 kA Icu| Level6
    Level6[Level 6: Final Load 32 A]

    Level1 -.->|Clears in 5 cycles| Level1
    Level2 -.->|Clears in 3 cycles| Level2
    Level3 -.->|Clears in 2 cycles| Level3
    Level4 -.->|Clears in 1 cycle| Level4
    Level5 -.->|Clears in <1 cycle| Level5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Read the diagram:** Each downstream device has **lower Icu** but **faster clearing**. The SCWC of the *busbar between any two levels* must exceed the **let-through I²t of the upstream device** for the time it takes the downstream device to clear. This is the essence of discrimination.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the term "Short Circuit Withstand Capacity" of a busbar. Mention the two criteria on which it is verified.**

**Model Answer:**
Short Circuit Withstand Capacity (SCWC) is the ability of an electrical equipment, particularly a busbar or switchgear assembly, to carry the maximum prospective short-circuit current for a specified duration (typically 1 s) without suffering thermal damage or mechanical deformation.

It is verified on the basis of two criteria:
1. **Thermal withstand** — the conductor temperature at the end of the fault must remain below the permissible limit for its insulation class (e.g., 160°C for PVC, 250°C for XLPE).
2. **Mechanical (dynamic) withstand** — the electrodynamic force due to the peak short-circuit current must not produce a bending stress exceeding the allowable stress of the busbar material.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is meant by the "peak factor" $\kappa$ in short-circuit calculations? State its typical range.**

**Model Answer:**
The peak factor $\kappa$ is the ratio of the peak (making) short-circuit current $i_p$ to the peak of the steady-state symmetrical current $\sqrt{2} \cdot I_k''$:

$$i_p = \kappa \cdot \sqrt{2} \cdot I_k''$$

It accounts for the DC offset component that arises due to the sudden establishment of a fault in an inductive circuit. Per IEC 60909:
$$\kappa = 1.02 + 0.98 \, e^{-3R/X}$$

**Typical range:** $\kappa = 1.02$ (highly resistive networks) to $\kappa = 1.8$ (typical LV systems) and a theoretical maximum of **2.69** at $R/X = 0$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

**A 1500 kVA, 11 kV / 433 V, 6% impedance transformer feeds a 415 V switchboard. The length of the busbar chamber is 4 m with four 1 m spans. The busbars are spaced 100 mm centre-to-centre. A 3-phase fault occurs at the outgoing terminal.**

**(a)** Calculate the initial symmetrical short-circuit current and the peak short-circuit current. Assume $c = 1.1$ and $\kappa = 1.8$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the minimum cross-section of a copper-XLPE cable required to withstand this fault for 1 s, and check the mechanical (bending) stress on the busbar with section modulus $W = 4.5\ \text{cm}^3$. Take $K = 143$, $\sigma_{allow,Cu} = 140\ \text{N/mm}^2$. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution**

**Part (a):**

**Step 1:** Rated full-load current on LV side:
$$I_r = \frac{S}{\sqrt{3} \cdot U_{LV}} = \frac{1500 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times 433} = 2000.6\ \text{A}$$

**Step 2:** Initial symmetrical SC current:
$$I_k'' = \frac{c \cdot I_r}{u_k} = \frac{1.1 \times 2000.6}{0.06} = 36,677\ \text{A} \approx 36.68\ \text{kA}$$

**Step 3:** Peak SC current:
$$i_p = \kappa \cdot \sqrt{2} \cdot I_k'' = 1.8 \times \sqrt{2} \times 36.68 = 93.34\ \text{kA}$$

**Part (b):**

**Step 4:** Minimum cable size (Cu/XLPE):
$$A_{min} = \frac{I_k'' \cdot \sqrt{t_k}}{K} = \frac{36,680 \times 1}{143} = 256.5\ \text{mm}^2$$
**Select next standard size: 300 mm² Cu/XLPE.**

**Step 5:** Force per span ($L = 1\ \text{m}$, $d = 0.1\ \text{m}$):
$$F = 0.2 \times \frac{i_p^2 \times L}{d} = 0.2 \times \frac{(93.34)^2 \times 1}{0.1} = 17,425\ \text{N} \approx 17.4\ \text{kN}$$

**Step 6:** Bending moment for simply supported beam:
$$M = \frac{F \cdot L}{10} = \frac{17,425 \times 1}{10} = 1,742.5\ \text{N·m}$$

**Step 7:** Bending stress:
$$\sigma = \frac{M}{W} = \frac{1,742.5 \times 10^3}{4.5 \times 10^3} = 387.2\ \text{N/mm}^2$$

**Step 8:** Comparison with allowable stress:
$$\sigma = 387.2\ \text{N/mm}^2 \gg \sigma_{allow} = 140\ \text{N/mm}^2$$

**Conclusion: The mechanical strength is INADEQUATE.** The busbar geometry must be redesigned — either reduce span to 0.35 m, increase spacing to 200 mm, or use a double-channel section with higher $W$.

**Revised span required** (for $\sigma = 140$):
$$L_{new} = \sqrt{\frac{10 \cdot W \cdot \sigma_{allow}}{0.2 \cdot i_p^2 / d}} = \sqrt{\frac{10 \times 4500 \times 140}{0.2 \times 8712.5 / 0.1}} = \sqrt{\frac{6.3 \times 10^6}{17,425}} = 0.60\ \text{m}$$
So use **insulators at 0.6 m spacing** for the busbar chamber.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the minimum cross-sectional area of a cable required to withstand a short-circuit current of $I_k''$ for a duration of $t_k$ seconds. Define all symbols used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-core aluminium cable with XLPE insulation has a cross-section of 120 mm². It feeds a 250 kVA, 415 V load. The upstream transformer is 11 kV / 433 V, 315 kVA, 5% impedance. Compute:
- (i) The maximum short-circuit current the cable can withstand for 1 s and 3 s.
- (ii) Verify whether the cable is thermally adequate for the prospective fault at the load end. Take $K = 92$ for Al/XLPE, $c = 1.1$. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution**

**Part (a):** See **Derivation 2 in Section 3.2** above. The final expression is:
$$A_{min} = \frac{I_k'' \cdot \sqrt{t_k}}{K}$$
where $K^2 = \dfrac{\delta \cdot c_w \cdot (\theta_f - \theta_i)}{\rho_0 (1 + \alpha \theta_f)}$, incorporating material density, specific heat, resistivity, and the permissible temperature rise.

**Part (b):**

**Step 1:** Rated current of load:
$$I_r = \frac{250 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times 415} = 347.7\ \text{A}$$

**Step 2:** Rated current of transformer LV side:
$$I_{r,Tr} = \frac{315 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times 433} = 419.9\ \text{A}$$

**Step 3:** Prospective SC current at transformer secondary (worst case at cable end ≈ transformer secondary):
$$I_k'' = \frac{c \cdot I_{r,Tr}}{u_k} = \frac{1.1 \times 419.9}{0.05} = 9,238\ \text{A} \approx 9.24\ \text{kA}$$

**Step 4 (i):** Maximum SC current the cable can withstand:

For $t_k = 1\ \text{s}$:
$$I_{th,max} = \frac{K \cdot A}{\sqrt{t_k}} = \frac{92 \times 120}{1} = 11,040\ \text{A} = 11.04\ \text{kA}$$

For $t_k = 3\ \text{s}$:
$$I_{th,max} = \frac{92 \times 120}{\sqrt{3}} = 6,374\ \text{A} = 6.37\ \text{kA}$$

**Step 5 (ii):** Compare $I_{th,max}$ with prospective $I_k''$:

- For 1 s clearing: $11.04\ \text{kA} > 9.24\ \text{kA}$ → **CABLE IS ADEQUATE** ✓
- For 3 s clearing: $6.37\ \text{kA} < 9.24\ \text{kA}$ → **CABLE IS NOT ADEQUATE** ✗

**Conclusion:** The 120 mm² Al/XLPE cable is suitable only if the upstream protective device clears the fault within 1 s. For slower protection (e.g., fuses with 3 s time-lag), the cable must be upsized.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Most Common Mark Losers**
> 1. **Forgetting the voltage factor $c$:** Many students compute $I_k'' = I_r / u_k$ directly. This loses 1–2 marks. Always use $c = 1.10$ for LV (415 V) and $c = 1.05$ for MV.
> 2. **Using wrong $K$ value:** The $K$ factor depends on **both** the conductor material **and** the insulation class. KTU papers deliberately swap "Cu/PVC" with "Cu/XLPE" to test this.
> 3. **Mixing up $I_{th}$ and $i_p$:** Thermal verification uses the *RMS* symmetrical current over time. Mechanical verification uses the *peak* current. Using $I_k''$ for force calculation gives a **non-conservative** (dangerous) answer.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert units:** $i_p$ must be in **kA** when using the simplified force formula $F = 0.2 \cdot i_p^2 L / d$.
> 5. **Neglecting DC decay factor:** The factor $\kappa$ is often assumed as 2 (worst case), but in real systems it depends on the source $R/X$ ratio. The 1.8 value is common in KTU problems.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ⚡ **SCWC = Thermal + Mechanical** — always verify both, never one alone.
- ⚡ **Golden formula for cable sizing:** $A_{min} = I_k'' \cdot \sqrt{t_k} / K$
- ⚡ **Golden formula for busbar force:** $F = 0.2 \cdot i_p^2 \cdot L / d$ (with $i_p$ in kA, $L$ in m, $d$ in m)
- ⚡ **Peak current:** $i_p = \kappa \cdot \sqrt{2} \cdot I_k''$, with $\kappa = 1.02$ to $1.8$ typical.
- ⚡ **Symmetrical SC current:** $I_k'' = c \cdot I_r / u_k$ — never forget $c$!
- ⚡ **Memorize the $K$ table:** Cu/PVC = 115, Cu/XLPE = 143, Al/PVC = 74, Al/XLPE = 92.
- ⚡ **Bending stress check:** $\sigma = M / W \leq \sigma_{allow}$ (140 N/mm² for Cu, 70 N/mm² for Al).
- ⚡ **Voltage factor $c$:** 1.10 for LV (≤ 1000 V), 1.05 for MV (> 1 kV).
- ⚡ **Thermal limit temperatures:** PVC = 160°C max, XLPE = 250°C max during SC.
- ⚡ **Standard SCWC duration:** 1 s for LV assemblies, 3 s for some MV applications.
- ⚡ **Let-through energy** $I^2 t$ is the unifying concept between breaker and cable — both must be coordinated.
- ⚡ **Discrimination principle:** Downstream device must clear first; upstream busbar must withstand the energy of all downstream $I^2 t$ contributions.
- ⚡ **Most-tested concepts in KTU 2024:** (1) Cable cross-section calculation, (2) Busbar force calculation, (3) $K$ factor identification, (4) Selection of standard cable size.
- ⚡ **Common fail mode in exam:** Using $I_r$ instead of $I_k''$ — the question always asks for **short-circuit** current, not rated current.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Standby DG Systems with AMF Panel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEOT752 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Standby DG Systems with AMF Panel # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Standby Diesel Generator (DG) System** is an independent, self-contained emergency power generation unit designed to automatically supply electrical energy to predefined "essential" loads when the **normal utility supply (EB/grid)** fails, gets disconnected, or falls outside the prescribed voltage/frequency tolerances. The standby DG is NOT a continuous power source — it operates only during emergencies, fire scenarios, or planned shutdowns, and is governed by **IS 14654:1999** and **IEEE 446 (Orange Book)** for Emergency and Standby Power Systems. An **Automatic Mains Failure (AMF) Panel** is an intelligent microprocessor / relay-based control panel that continuously monitors the incoming mains (utility) supply and, on detecting a mains failure, automatically performs a sequenced start of the DG set, allows it to stabilize, and then transfers the load from mains to DG via a **Power Changeover Switch (PCC/MTS)**. When the mains is restored, the AMF panel reverses the sequence (cooling, off, mains retransfer). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEEOT752 / Module 4)** > The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to design a complete standby DG system including: (i) DG kVA sizing, (ii) AMF panel architecture and logic sequence, (iii) battery sizing for DG cranking, (iv) cabling from DG to AMF to load, and (v) compliance with earthing and neutral isolation rules. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of the standby DG system like a **fire brigade on standby**: - The **EB (Electricity Board) supply** is your main water pipeline supplying a building. As long as it works, nobody calls the brigade. - The moment the pipeline bursts (mains fails), the **fire brigade (DG set)** is automatically dispatched by an **emergency alarm system (AMF panel)**. - The alarm first checks that the failure is real (voltage/frequency sensing), then signals the brigade to **start its engine**, **warm up**, and **connect its hoses to the building's plumbing (load transfer)**. - When the main pipeline is repaired, the brigade **disconnects**, **cools down its engine**, and **shuts off**, going back to standby. Similarly, the AMF panel acts as the "intelligent dispatcher," ensuring the DG only starts when needed, stabilizes before taking load, and is properly cooled before shutdown to avoid engine seizure. ## 1.3 Key Standard Reference Values - **System Voltage**: 415 V (3-phase, 4-wire) for LT standby systems. - **Standard DG ratings**: 7.5 kVA, 15 kVA, 30 kVA, 62.5 kVA, 125 kVA, 250 kVA, 500 kVA, 1000 kVA. - **Power Factor**: 0.8 lagging (industry standard for DG sets). - **Voltage Regulation**: ±5% (steady state), ±10% (transient). - **Frequency**: 50 Hz ± 3% (steady state). - **Cranking Voltage**: 12 V DC / 24 V DC for starter batteries. - **AMF Transfer Time**: Typically **10 to 30 seconds** (including DG startup). - **Load Transfer Time (MTS)**: < 500 ms (break-before-make). - **Acceptable mains parameters** (per IS 14654): Voltage = **±10%** of nominal; Frequency = **±5%** of nominal. > [!NOTE] > The **emergency load** (lifts, fire pumps, ICU, stairwell lighting) MUST be served within **15 seconds** of mains failure per the National Building Code (NBC 2016, Part 8, Section 6.5). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Components of a Complete Standby DG System A fully functional standby DG installation consists of **six major subsystems**, all of which must be understood for KTU design questions: ### 2.1.1 The Diesel Generator Set (Mechanical + Electrical) - **Diesel Engine**: 4-stroke, water-cooled (radiator) for ratings > 30 kVA; air-cooled for smaller sets. - **Alternator**: Self-excited, self-regulated, brushless, synchronous machine with AVR (Automatic Voltage Regulator). - **Coupling**: Direct flexible disc coupling between engine and alternator. - **Fuel System**: Day service tank (8-hour capacity) + integral fuel tank in skid. - **Governor**: Electronic / hydraulic for ISO 8528-G3 class performance. - **Acoustic Enclosure**: Reduces noise to **≤ 75 dB(A) at 1 meter** (per CPCB norms). ### 2.1.2 The AMF Panel (Control + Power Section) - **Control Section**: Microprocessor-based controller (e.g., DSE 7320, Woodward easYgen) with sensors for mains voltage, frequency, DG voltage, current, oil pressure, water temperature, fuel level, and battery voltage. - **Power Section**: Power Contactors or Motorized Changeover Switch (MTS) — typically 4-pole for full neutral isolation, with mechanical and electrical interlocks. - **Indications**: Mains ON, Mains OFF, DG Running, Load on Mains, Load on DG, Fault indications. ### 2.1.3 The Battery Bank (Cranking Battery) - Lead-acid, tubular positive plate type, typically **12 V DC** (for engines < 30 kVA) or **24 V DC** (for larger engines). - Charged continuously by an integrated **battery charger** powered from mains (and/or DG when running). ### 2.1.4 The Cabling Network - From **EB meter → Main LT panel → AMF panel input**. - From **AMF output → Essential load panel (sub-distribution)**. - **Control cables** from AMF to DG control panel (multi-core, shielded). ### 2.1.5 Earthing System - **Two distinct earth pits** are mandatory: - **Body earth** for DG frame and AMF panel chassis (typically < 1 Ω). - **Neutral earth** at the alternator neutral point (typically < 1 Ω). - These two earths are **interconnected** through an earth electrode and the system neutral. ### 2.1.6 The Exhaust and Ventilation System - Black smoke exhaust pipe routed upward with rain cap. - Adequate ventilation to dissipate heat rejection (typically ~ 3 kW heat per 10 kVA rating). ## 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Sequence The AMF panel follows a **fixed sequenced logic** during a mains failure event: 1. **Monitoring Stage**: AMF continuously samples mains voltage and frequency. 2. **Failure Detection**: If voltage < 10% of nominal OR frequency < 47.5 Hz OR complete blackout for a preset delay (typically 3–5 seconds, to avoid nuisance starting during transient dips), the panel declares **MAINS FAILURE**. 3. **Cranking Command**: AMF energizes the starter solenoid, cranking the engine for 5–10 seconds. 4. **Stabilization**: After engine fires, AMF allows warm-up (5–10 seconds at idle). 5. **Voltage/Frequency OK**: AMF verifies DG output is within tolerance. 6. **Load Transfer**: The motorized changeover switch disconnects the mains contactor and connects the DG contactor (with **break-before-make** to prevent paralleling). 7. **Running Stage**: DG supplies essential loads indefinitely. 8. **Mains Restoration**: When mains returns and is stable for the preset time (typically 30 seconds to 60 seconds), the AMF initiates a **retransfer sequence**: load → mains → DG cooling (1–3 minutes) → DG stop. 9. **Standby**: AMF returns to monitoring mode. > [!NOTE] > **Why "break-before-make" transfer?** > If the mains contactor and DG contactor close simultaneously, the grid and DG are briefly paralleled, causing massive circulating currents and possible mechanical damage to the alternator. KTU examiners frequently test this concept. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet The following table consolidates **every formula** the KTU 2024 scheme demands for Module 4 questions on Standby DG with AMF. | # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Description of Variables | Typical Unit | |---|----------------------|---------|--------------------------|--------------| | 1 | DG Rated kVA | $S_{DG} = \dfrac{\sum P_{L} \cdot D_f}{\eta_{alt} \cdot \cos\phi \cdot 1000}$ | $\sum P_L$ = total connected essential load; $D_f$ = diversity factor (0.6–0.8); $\eta_{alt}$ = alternator efficiency (~0.92); $\cos\phi$ = 0.8 | kVA | | 2 | DG Rated kW (electrical output) | $P_{DG} = S_{DG} \cdot \cos\phi \cdot 1000$ | $S_{DG}$ in kVA; $\cos\phi$ = 0.8 | kW | | 3 | Engine Brake Power (BHP) | $P_{BHP} = \dfrac{P_{DG}}{\eta_{alt} \cdot \eta_{m}}$ | $\eta_m$ = mechanical efficiency (~0.92) | HP | | 4 | Starting Current for Motors | $I_{start} = 6 \cdot I_{FL}$ (DOL) | DOL = Direct-On-Line starting; $I_{FL}$ = full load current | A | | 5 | Voltage Drop (3-phase cable) | $V_d = \dfrac{\sqrt{3} \cdot I \cdot L \cdot \cos\phi}{\sigma \cdot A} \cdot 1000$ | $L$ = length (m); $A$ = area (mm²); $\sigma$ = conductivity (Cu = 58, Al = 36) | Volts | | 6 | Cable Cross-Section | $A = \dfrac{\sqrt{3} \cdot I \cdot L \cdot \cos\phi \cdot 1000}{\sigma \cdot V_{d,\text{allow}}}$ | $V_{d,\text{allow}}$ = 2.5% of system voltage (per IS 732) | mm² | | 7 | Battery Capacity | $C = \dfrac{I_{crank} \cdot t_{crank} \cdot F_a}{\eta_{T}}$ | $I_{crank}$ = starter current (A); $t_{crank}$ = crank time (s); $F_a$ = aging factor (1.25); $\eta_T$ = temperature factor (0.85) | Ah | | 8 | Cranking Current | $I_{crank} = 3 \cdot C_{20}$ (rule of thumb) | $C_{20}$ = 20-hour rated capacity of battery | A | | 9 | Full Load Current | $I_{FL} = \dfrac{S_{DG} \cdot 1000}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{LL}}$ | $V_{LL}$ = 415 V for LT system | A | | 10 | Frequency of Engine | $n = \dfrac{120 \cdot f}{P}$ | $f$ = 50 Hz; $P$ = number of poles | rpm | | 11 | Alternator Cooling Air | $Q = \dfrac{P_{loss}}{1.005 \cdot \Delta T}$ | $P_{loss}$ in kW; $\Delta T$ = 15 K | m³/s | | 12 | Fuel Consumption | $FC = 0.22 \cdot P_{kW} + 2.7$ | Empirical formula for standby rating | L/hr | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Trick Question Pattern:** > Examiners often ask — *"Why is the DG rated in kVA, not kW?"* > **Answer**: Because the **alternator's thermal limit depends on apparent power (kVA)**, while the **engine's mechanical limit depends on active power (kW)**. The more conservative of the two governs, and since $\cos\phi = 0.8$, the kVA rating is always 25% higher than the kW rating. Hence the DG nameplate shows **kVA** but actual usable load depends on **kW × power factor**. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Standby DG systems are mandatory by code in: - **Hospitals (NABH standards)** — uninterrupted ICU/OT supply. - **High-rise buildings (NBC 2016)** — fire lifts, fire pumps, stairwell pressurization. - **Data centers (TIA-942)** — Tier III/IV redundancy. - **Airports, Metro stations, Hotels, Banks** — process-critical loads. - **Industrial plants** — process continuity, safety interlocks. > [!NOTE] > KTU expects the student to know that **DG is NOT a substitute for a UPS**. A UPS has transfer time < 10 ms (electronic), whereas a DG + AMF takes 10–30 seconds. Hence sensitive electronics are often protected by **UPS + DG** in cascade — UPS bridges the gap until DG stabilizes. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Design Problem Statement (Typical KTU Module 4 Question) A multistorey office building has the following **essential loads** that must be supplied by a standby DG with AMF panel upon mains failure: | S.No | Load Description | Quantity | Rating (kW each) | Total (kW) | Type | |------|------------------|----------|------------------|------------|------| | 1 | Lift motors (5 kW each, DOL start) | 2 | 5.0 | 10.0 | Motor | | 2 | Fire hydrant pump (15 kW, star-delta) | 1 | 15.0 | 15.0 | Motor | | 3 | Staircase & corridor lighting | — | — | 6.0 | Lighting | | 4 | Server room (UPS-fed) | — | — | 8.0 | Mixed | | 5 | Building management system (BMS) | — | — | 2.0 | Mixed | | 6 | Emergency sockets (1-phase) | — | — | 3.0 | Mixed | | | **TOTAL CONNECTED LOAD** | | | **44.0** | | Assume: - Diversity factor $D_f = 0.75$ - Alternator efficiency $\eta_{alt} = 0.92$ - Power factor $\cos\phi = 0.8$ - System voltage = 415 V, 3-phase - Cable length from DG to AMF panel = 25 m - Cable length from AMF to essential panel = 40 m - Ambient temperature = 45 °C - Allowable voltage drop = 2.5% - DG starter battery: 24 V DC, crank current 300 A, crank time 10 s - Use copper conductors ($\sigma = 58$) --- ## 3.2 Step-by-Step Design Solution ### Step 1: Compute the diversified load in kW $$ P_{diversified} = \sum P_L \cdot D_f $$ $$ P_{diversified} = 44.0 \cdot 0.75 = 33.0 \text{ kW} $$ *Explanation*: The diversity factor of 0.75 accounts for the fact that **not all connected loads operate simultaneously** at full rating during a mains failure. --- ### Step 2: Account for motor starting inrush (DOL lift motor) The 5 kW DOL lift motors draw approximately **6× their full-load current** during starting. Although the DG must tolerate this inrush, it does NOT increase the kW demand. However, it **does reduce the usable capacity** (typically derate DG by ~10% for DOL starting). $$ P_{derated} = P_{diversified} \cdot 1.10 $$ $$ P_{derated} = 33.0 \cdot 1.10 = 36.3 \text{ kW} $$ *Explanation*: The DG alternator's transient voltage dip during motor starting must be limited to ~15%; hence a 10% safety margin is applied to the running load. --- ### Step 3: Convert to kVA demand $$ S_{demand} = \dfrac{P_{derated}}{\cos\phi} $$ $$ S_{demand} = \dfrac{36.3}{0.8} = 45.375 \text{ kVA} $$ *Explanation*: DG nameplates are in kVA. With $\cos\phi = 0.8$, the apparent power exceeds real power by 25%. --- ### Step 4: Apply alternator efficiency correction and select standard rating $$ S_{DG,calc} = \dfrac{S_{demand}}{\eta_{alt}} $$ $$ S_{DG,calc} = \dfrac{45.375}{0.92} = 49.32 \text{ kVA} $$ *Explanation*: 8% of mechanical energy is lost in conversion to electrical energy inside the alternator. We divide by efficiency because we need the alternator to **deliver** 45.4 kVA after losses. **Standard rating selection** (next higher standard value): $S_{DG} = 62.5$ kVA (industry standard size). *Rationale*: KTU expects students to round up to the next standard kVA. Going below 49.32 kVA would overload the alternator. --- ### Step 5: Determine full-load current on the DG $$ I_{FL} = \dfrac{S_{DG} \cdot 1000}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{LL}} $$ $$ I_{FL} = \dfrac{62.5 \cdot 1000}{\sqrt{3} \cdot 415} = \dfrac{62500}{718.91} = 86.94 \text{ A} $$ *Explanation*: This is the line current the cabling and the AMF contactor must be rated for. --- ### Step 6: Size the cable from DG to AMF panel Allowable voltage drop $V_d = 2.5\% \text{ of } 415 \text{ V} = 10.375$ V. $$ A = \dfrac{\sqrt{3} \cdot I_{FL} \cdot L \cdot \cos\phi \cdot 1000}{\sigma \cdot V_d} $$ $$ A = \dfrac{1.732 \cdot 86.94 \cdot 25 \cdot 0.8 \cdot 1000}{58 \cdot 10.375} $$ $$ A = \dfrac{3011685.6}{601.75} = 5005.5 \text{ mm}^2 $$ *Explanation*: This is a HUGE area! Clearly, using the actual current at full rated kVA over the entire circuit length is impractical. In practice, the cable is sized for **actual diversified running current**, not the full nameplate. Let's recalculate using diversified current. **Diversified running current**: $$ I_{diversified} = \dfrac{P_{diversified} \cdot 1000}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{LL} \cdot \cos\phi} = \dfrac{33000}{1.732 \cdot 415 \cdot 0.8} = 57.4 \text{ A} $$ **Recompute area for diversified current**: $$ A = \dfrac{1.732 \cdot 57.4 \cdot 25 \cdot 0.8 \cdot 1000}{58 \cdot 10.375} = \dfrac{1989060}{601.75} = 3305.4 \text{ mm}^2 $$ *Explanation*: Even this is unrealistic. The voltage drop of 2.5% over the **entire circuit length** (DG to essential load terminal) must be split. Allocate **1%** (4.15 V) to DG→AMF, and **1.5%** (6.225 V) to AMF→load. **Recalculate with 1% drop on DG→AMF segment**: $$ A = \dfrac{1.732 \cdot 57.4 \cdot 25 \cdot 0.8 \cdot 1000}{58 \cdot 4.15} = \dfrac{1989060}{240.7} = 8263.5 \text{ mm}^2 $$ *Wait — this is still enormous.* Let me reconsider. KTU students must use **mm²** properly. The issue is that 25 m at 57.4 A with copper at 58 m/Ω·mm² should give reasonable values. Let me re-derive using the correct formula form: **Correct 3-phase voltage drop formula**: $$ V_d = \dfrac{\sqrt{3} \cdot I \cdot L}{\sigma \cdot A} \cdot \cos\phi $$ Solving for A: $$ A = \dfrac{\sqrt{3} \cdot I \cdot L \cdot \cos\phi}{\sigma \cdot V_d} \text{ (units: m and mm² give Volts)} $$ Plugging in with $V_d = 4.15$ V: $$ A = \dfrac{1.732 \cdot 57.4 \cdot 25 \cdot 0.8}{58 \cdot 4.15} = \dfrac{1989.06}{240.7} = 8.26 \text{ mm}^2 $$ *Explanation*: 8.26 mm² is reasonable. **Select standard 10 mm² copper cable** (XLPE insulated, armoured). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Common Student Mistake**: Forgetting to convert cable length and area units consistently. In SI: $\sigma$ for Cu at 58 m/$\Omega\cdot$mm², $L$ in metres, $A$ in mm², gives $V_d$ directly in Volts (no factor of 1000 needed when using these conventions). --- ### Step 7: Battery sizing for DG cranking The battery must crank the engine for 10 s at 300 A. Apply aging factor and temperature derating. $$ C = \dfrac{I_{crank} \cdot t_{crank} \cdot F_a}{\eta_T \cdot 3600} $$ *Explanation*: 3600 converts seconds → hours for Ah units. Alternatively, we use the 5-second current rule and convert directly. $$ C = \dfrac{300 \cdot 10 \cdot 1.25}{0.85 \cdot 3600} = \dfrac{3750}{3060} = 1.225 \text{ Ah} $$ *Explanation*: This is the **minimum** battery capacity. Standard practice is to oversize by 2× for safety, and to also support the battery charger maintenance cycle. **Standard selection**: Two 12 V, 100 Ah tubular lead-acid batteries in series (24 V, 100 Ah). The 5-second current rating of such a battery is ~ 300 A at 25 °C, which is borderline. For cold cranking reliability, **150 Ah** is preferred. --- ### Step 8: Verify engine power requirement (BHP) $$ P_{kW,elec} = S_{DG} \cdot \cos\phi = 62.5 \cdot 0.8 = 50 \text{ kW} $$ $$ P_{BHP} = \dfrac{P_{kW,elec}}{\eta_{alt} \cdot \eta_{mech}} = \dfrac{50}{0.92 \cdot 0.92} = 59.07 \text{ kW} $$ Converting: $P_{BHP} = 59.07 \cdot 1.341 = 79.2$ BHP *Explanation*: The diesel engine must produce ~80 BHP (mechanical brake power) to deliver 50 kW (50 kVA at 0.8 pf) electrical output, after mechanical and electrical losses. --- ### Step 3 Summary Table (Analytical Matrix) | Design Step | Computed Quantity | Value | Selection | |-------------|-------------------|-------|-----------| | Diversified load | 33.0 kW | — | — | | Motor derated load | 36.3 kW | — | — | | kVA demand | 45.4 kVA | — | — | | Corrected for efficiency | 49.3 kVA | — | **62.5 kVA** selected | | Full-load current | 86.9 A | — | 100 A MCCB | | Running diversified current | 57.4 A | — | — | | Cable DG → AMF | 8.26 mm² (req.) | — | **10 mm² XLPE Cu** | | Cable AMF → essential load | recompute with 6.225 V | — | **16 mm² XLPE Cu** | | Battery capacity | 1.23 Ah min | — | **2 × 12 V, 150 Ah** | | Alternator output | 50 kW | — | 62.5 kVA | | Engine BHP | 79.2 BHP | — | 80 BHP engine | --- ## 3.3 Worked Mini-Example — Voltage Drop on AMF → Essential Load Cable Given: $I = 57.4$ A, $L = 40$ m, allowable drop $V_d = 6.225$ V (1.5% of 415 V), Cu, $\cos\phi = 0.8$. $$ A = \dfrac{1.732 \cdot 57.4 \cdot 40 \cdot 0.8}{58 \cdot 6.225} $$ $$ A = \dfrac{3183.18}{361.05} = 8.81 \text{ mm}^2 $$ **Select 10 mm² XLPE Cu cable** (next standard). *Verification*: With 10 mm², actual voltage drop becomes: $$ V_d = \dfrac{1.732 \cdot 57.4 \cdot 40 \cdot 0.8}{58 \cdot 10} = \dfrac{3183.18}{580} = 5.49 \text{ V} $$ Percentage drop: $5.49 / 415 \cdot 100 = 1.32\%$, which is within the 1.5% allowance. ✓ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Standby DG + AMF System The following Mermaid diagram shows the **complete functional architecture** of a Standby DG system with AMF panel, illustrating every signal and power flow path. This is the schematic a KTU examiner expects when "draw the block diagram" appears in a 14-mark question. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[EB Utility Supply
11 kV or 415 V] --> B[Transformer
11 kV to 415 V] B --> C[Main LT Panel
MCCB 1 Incomer] C --> D[AMF Panel

62.5 kVA, 415 V] F --> G[DG Contactor

24 V DC, 150 Ah] F --> I[Fuel Tank
Day Service 8 hr] F --> J[Battery Charger

Final Distribution] E --> K G --> K K --> L1[Lift Motors 2 x 5 kW] K --> L2[Fire Pump 15 kW] K --> L3[Staircase Lighting] K --> L4[Server Room UPS] K --> L5[BMS and Emergency Sockets] D --> M[AMF Controller Logic
Voltage and Frequency Sensing] M --> N[Signal to Crank Solenoid] M --> O[Signal to Changeover Motor] M --> P[Fault Annunciation
Buzzer and Indications] D --> Q[Body Earth Pit
Less than 1 ohm] D --> R[Neutral Earth Pit
Less than 1 ohm] ``` ## 4.2 Sequence Flowchart of AMF Logic Operation This second diagram captures the **temporal logic** the controller executes during a mains outage — directly answering the KTU question *"Explain with a flowchart the working of AMF panel."* ```mermaid flowchart TD S1([Start of Sequence]) --> S2{Mains Healthy?
V and f within range} S2 -- Yes --> S3[Load on Mains
DG in Standby] S3 --> S2 S2 -- No --> S4[Wait 3 to 5 seconds
Confirm failure] S4 --> S5{Mains Still Failed?} S5 -- No --> S3 S5 -- Yes --> S6[Crank DG Engine
10 seconds max] S6 --> S7{DG Started?} S7 -- No --> S8[Crank Rest 10 sec
Max 3 attempts] S8 --> S7 S7 -- Yes --> S9[Warm Up Idle
5 to 10 seconds] S9 --> S10{DG V and f OK?} S10 -- No --> S11[Alarm DG Fault
Annunciate] S10 -- Yes --> S12[Open Mains Contactor
Break before Make] S12 --> S13[Close DG Contactor
Load on DG] S13 --> S14[Monitor DG Run
Oil Pressure and Temp] S14 --> S15{Mains Restored
and stable 30 s?} S15 -- No --> S14 S15 -- Yes --> S16[Open DG Contactor
Close Mains Contactor] S16 --> S17[Cool Down DG
1 to 3 minutes idle] S17 --> S18[Stop DG Engine] S18 --> S3 ``` ## 4.3 Earthing & Neutral Isolation Diagram This schematic shows the **critical earthing topology** that KTU examiners specifically test for understanding of fault safety in DG systems. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[DG Alternator Neutral] --> N1[Neutral Link Isolator
4 Pole Contactor] N1 --> N2[Neutral Bus at AMF] N2 --> N3[Load Neutral] N2 -.-> NE[Neutral Earth Pit
Cu Rod 25 mm x 3 m
Less than 1 ohm] A --> NE DG[Diesel Generator Frame] --> BE1[Body Earth Conductor
50 x 6 mm Cu Strip] BE1 --> BE[Body Earth Pit
Less than 1 ohm] AMF[AMF Panel Body] --> BE LB[Load Panel Body] --> BE ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Critical Concept**: When the DG is running, the **neutral must be earthed only at the alternator neutral point**. Simultaneously, when mains is restored, the **EB neutral earth** takes over. **The two neutral earths must NEVER be connected simultaneously** — this creates a parallel neutral return path, which can lead to dangerous touch voltages and incorrect ELCB operation. This is why a **4-pole changeover** (with neutral break) is used in TN-S systems for DG applications. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (Part A, 3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]** > *"Define an AMF panel and list its main functions."* **Model Answer (3 Marks Allocation):** An **Automatic Mains Failure (AMF) panel** is a microprocessor-based controller that continuously monitors the utility (mains) supply and, on detecting a sustained failure, automatically starts the standby diesel generator and transfers the essential load from mains to the DG. It also re-transfers the load back to mains once the utility supply is restored and stable. *(2 marks)* **Main functions:** 1. Sensing mains voltage and frequency; declaring a "mains failure" if values fall outside preset limits. *(0.5 mark)* 2. Automatic cranking, starting, and stabilizing the DG set. *(0.25 mark)* 3. Operating the motorized changeover switch (MTS) to transfer load — with **break-before-make** logic. *(0.25 mark)* --- ### Question 2 (Part A, 3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]** > *"Why is a 4-pole changeover switch used in DG installations instead of a 3-pole switch?"* **Model Answer (3 Marks Allocation):** A **4-pole changeover switch** breaks **all three phases AND the neutral** during transfer. *(1 mark)* This is essential because: 1. **Neutral isolation**: When the DG takes over, the neutral earth reference shifts from the EB neutral to the DG alternator neutral. Simultaneous connection of both neutrals creates a parallel earth-return path, leading to **false tripping of ELCBs/RCCBs** and **dangerous touch voltages**. *(1 mark)* 2. **Preventing grid back-feed**: A 4-pole switch prevents the DG from back-feeding the EB network if the mains re-closes on a fault. *(1 mark)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A (Part B, 14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model Question]** > **(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the working of an Automatic Mains Failure (AMF) panel used with a standby diesel generator set. *(7 marks)* > > **(b)** A standby DG system must serve the following essential loads on mains failure: Lighting 8 kW, Lift 7.5 kW (DOL start), Fire pump 11 kW (star-delta), Server UPS input 5 kW. Compute the suitable standard kVA rating of the DG set. Assume diversity factor = 0.8, alternator efficiency = 0.9, power factor = 0.8, and apply a 10% margin for motor starting. *(7 marks)* --- #### Model Solution for Part A(a) — 7 Marks **Block Diagram** (3 marks): Draw a labelled block diagram showing: - **EB supply → Main LT Panel → AMF Panel (Controller + Changeover) → Essential Load**. - **AMF → Crank signal → DG Engine**. - **DG → Battery Charger → Battery (24 V)**. - **Two earth pits** (body and neutral). - **Sensors** for mains V, f; DG V, f; oil pressure; temperature; fuel level. **Working Explanation** (4 marks): 1. **Monitoring mode**: The AMF controller continuously samples mains voltage and frequency. *(0.5 mark)* 2. **Mains failure detection**: If mains voltage drops below **+10% / -15%** of nominal OR frequency deviates beyond **±5%**, and persists for a preset delay (3–5 s, to ride through transient dips), the AMF declares mains failure. *(1 mark)* 3. **DG cranking**: The AMF energizes the starter solenoid for 5–10 s. If the engine fails to start, up to 3 crank attempts are made with rest intervals. *(0.5 mark)* 4. **Stabilization**: After successful start, the engine idles for 5–10 s to stabilize oil pressure, then ramps to rated speed. *(0.5 mark)* 5. **Voltage/frequency check**: AMF verifies DG output is within tolerance (±5% voltage, ±3% frequency steady-state). *(0.5 mark)* 6. **Load transfer**: The motorized changeover switch opens the mains contactor (break), then closes the DG contactor (make) — a **break-before-make** sequence to prevent paralleling. *(0.5 mark)* 7. **Reverse sequence on mains restoration**: After mains is stable for 30–60 s, load is transferred back to mains. DG runs unloaded for 1–3 min (cooling), then stops. Battery charger resumes trickle charging. *(0.5 mark)* --- #### Model Solution for Part A(b) — 7 Marks **Given data** (1 mark): - Lighting = 8 kW - Lift (DOL) = 7.5 kW - Fire pump (star-delta) = 11 kW - Server UPS input = 5 kW - Total connected load $\sum P_L = 8 + 7.5 + 11 + 5 = 31.5$ kW - $D_f = 0.8$, $\eta_{alt} = 0.9$, $\cos\phi = 0.8$ **Step 1: Diversified load** (1 mark): $$ P_{div} = 31.5 \cdot 0.8 = 25.2 \text{ kW} $$ **Step 2: Apply 10% motor-starting margin** (1 mark): $$ P_{derated} = 25.2 \cdot 1.10 = 27.72 \text{ kW} $$ **Step 3: Convert to kVA** (1 mark): $$ S_{demand} = \dfrac{27.72}{0.8} = 34.65 \text{ kVA} $$ **Step 4: Apply alternator efficiency correction** (1 mark): $$ S_{calc} = \dfrac{34.65}{0.9} = 38.5 \text{ kVA} $$ **Step 5: Select standard rating** (1 mark): Next higher standard rating = **40 kVA** (or 45 kVA in some catalogues; 50 kVA is the conservative next choice). **Recommended selection**: **45 kVA DG set** with prime rating 36 kW at 0.8 pf. **Justification** (1 mark): Standard sizes available: 7.5, 15, 25, 30, 40, 45, 50, 62.5, 82.5, 100, 125, 250 kVA. The 38.5 kVA calculated value requires us to pick the next higher standard size to avoid overload, and 45 kVA provides a 17% margin which is healthy for motor-starting transients. --- ### Question B (Part B, 14 Marks, Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model Question]** > **(a)** Explain, with a labeled block diagram, the complete architecture of a standby diesel generator system including the AMF panel, earthing, battery, and cabling. List the major IS/IEC standards applicable. *(7 marks)* > > **(b)** For the DG system designed in Part A, compute the size of (i) the cranking battery in Ah, and (ii) the copper cable from DG to AMF panel (25 m run), assuming an allowable voltage drop of 1.5% and a running diversified current of 45 A. *(7 marks)* --- #### Model Solution for Part B(a) — 7 Marks **Block Diagram Description** (3 marks): The student must sketch a diagram (similar to Section 4.1) showing: 1. EB supply → Transformer → Main LT panel → AMF panel. 2. AMF → DG set (with fuel tank, battery, exhaust). 3. AMF → Essential load panel → Final loads. 4. Two earth pits (body & neutral). 5. Battery charger loop. **Component explanation** (2 marks): Briefly explain the function of: - AMF controller (DSE/Woodward type). - Motorized changeover switch (MTS, 4-pole). - DG set (engine + alternator + AVR). - Battery + charger. - Acoustic enclosure (75 dB(A) limit). **Applicable standards** (2 marks): - **IS 14654:1999** — AC generators for standby applications. - **IS 4722:1992** — Rotating electrical machines (performance). - **IEEE 446** — Orange Book for emergency/standby power. - **IS 732:1989** — Wiring installations (cabling, voltage drop). - **IEC 60947-6-1** — Automatic transfer switches. - **CPCB norms** — Noise (< 75 dB(A)) and emission standards. - **NBC 2016, Part 8** — Building services, mandatory standby power. --- #### Model Solution for Part B(b) — 7 Marks **Given** (1 mark): - DG output: 62.5 kVA, 415 V, 3-phase, $\cos\phi = 0.8$ - Cranking current $I_{crank}$ = 300 A (assumed standard for engines up to 80 BHP) - Cranking time $t_{crank}$ = 10 seconds - Aging factor $F_a = 1.25$, temperature factor $\eta_T = 0.85$ - Cable length $L = 25$ m, running current $I = 45$ A, allowable $V_d = 1.5\% \text{ of } 415 \text{ V} = 6.225$ V - $\sigma_{Cu} = 58$ m/$\Omega \cdot$mm² **(i) Battery Sizing** (3 marks): $$ C_{Ah} = \dfrac{I_{crank} \cdot t_{crank} \cdot F_a}{\eta_T \cdot 3600} $$ $$ C_{Ah} = \dfrac{300 \cdot 10 \cdot 1.25}{0.85 \cdot 3600} $$ $$ C_{Ah} = \dfrac{3750}{3060} = 1.225 \text{ Ah} $$ *Reasoning*: This is the **minimum** capacity to crank once. However: 1. Standard lead-acid tubular batteries for 24 V systems come in 100 Ah / 150 Ah sizes. 2. The 5-second current rating of a 100 Ah battery is ~ 300 A only at 25 °C; with derating at higher ambient and over lifetime, a **150 Ah battery** is selected. 3. Therefore: **2 × 12 V, 150 Ah batteries in series → 24 V, 150 Ah**. **(ii) Cable Sizing** (3 marks): $$ A = \dfrac{\sqrt{3} \cdot I \cdot L \cdot \cos\phi}{\sigma \cdot V_d} $$ $$ A = \dfrac{1.732 \cdot 45 \cdot 25 \cdot 0.8}{58 \cdot 6.225} $$ $$ A = \dfrac{1558.8}{361.05} = 4.32 \text{ mm}^2 $$ **Select standard 6 mm² XLPE Cu cable** (next higher size, 3.5-core armoured). *Verification*: With 6 mm², actual voltage drop is: $$ V_d = \dfrac{1.732 \cdot 45 \cdot 25 \cdot 0.8}{58 \cdot 6} = \dfrac{1558.8}{348} = 4.48 \text{ V} $$ Percentage drop = $4.48 / 415 \cdot 100 = 1.08\%$ ✓ (within 1.5%) **Final selection: 3.5-core × 6 mm² XLPE Cu armoured cable** (1 mark) --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students LOSE marks in DG + AMF questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting to apply the motor-starting margin (10%)**: When DOL motors are part of the load, simply summing diversified kW is INSUFFICIENT. The DG must handle transient inrush, which is why a derating factor of 1.10–1.15 is applied. > > 2. **Choosing the wrong DG size**: Students frequently pick the calculated kVA as the standard rating, forgetting that kVA must always be **rounded UP** to the next standard size (e.g., 38.5 kVA → 45 kVA, not 40 kVA). > > 3. **Confusing DOL and star-delta starting**: DOL = 6× inrush; star-delta = 2× inrush. The DG derating margin differs. > > 4. **Not mentioning break-before-make**: AMF transfer MUST be break-before-make. Writing "the panel transfers load" without this qualifier costs at least 1 mark. > > 5. **Mixing up earth pits**: Students frequently state that "the neutral is earthed at the DG only" — which is correct ONLY when the DG is supplying the load. When mains is healthy, the **EB neutral earth** is the reference. The 4-pole isolator achieves this transition. > > 6. **Battery sizing units error**: Students often write "Ah = I × t" without aging factor, temperature derating, or unit conversion (seconds → hours). KTU examiners specifically deduct marks for missing $F_a$ and $\eta_T$. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid Revision Checklist for Standby DG with AMF** ### A. Conceptual Understanding - ☐ Standby DG is **NOT** a continuous power source; it is emergency-only. - ☐ AMF panel = automatic sensing + cranking + load transfer logic. - ☐ Transfer is **break-before-make** — never parallel mains and DG. - ☐ 4-pole changeover is used for neutral isolation in TN-S systems. - ☐ Two distinct earth pits: **body earth** (< 1 Ω) and **neutral earth** (< 1 Ω). - ☐ **DOL starting = 6× full-load current** inrush. - ☐ **Star-delta starting = 2× full-load current** inrush. ### B. Numerical Must-Knows - ☐ DG sizing formula: $S_{DG} = \dfrac{\sum P_L \cdot D_f \cdot \text{margin}}{\eta_{alt} \cdot \cos\phi}$. - ☐ Always **round UP** to standard kVA. - ☐ Battery sizing: $C = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot F_a}{\eta_T \cdot 3600}$. - ☐ Cable sizing: $A = \dfrac{\sqrt{3} \cdot I \cdot L \cdot \cos\phi}{\sigma \cdot V_d}$ (3-phase). - ☐ Allowable voltage drop for power circuits = **2.5%** (per IS 732). - ☐ Allowable voltage drop from DG to essential load = split: 1% DG→AMF, 1.5% AMF→load. - ☐ DG noise limit = **75 dB(A) at 1 m** (CPCB norms). ### C. Standards & Codes (Frequently Tested) - ☐ **IS 14654** — AC generators for standby. - ☐ **IS 4722** — Performance of rotating machines. - ☐ **IEEE 446 (Orange Book)** — Emergency and standby power systems. - ☐ **IEC 60947-6-1** — Automatic transfer switches. - ☐ **IS 732** — Wiring and voltage drop. - ☐ **NBC 2016 Part 8** — Mandatory standby for high-rise buildings. - ☐ **CPCB norms** — Noise and emissions. ### D. Logic Sequence Mnemonic — **"MWTCR"** - **M** — Monitor mains (continuous). - **W** — Wait for confirmation of failure (3–5 s). - **T** — Transfer via cranking (with 3 attempts max). - **C** — Cool down (1–3 min) after retransfer. - **R** — Return to standby monitoring. ### E. Frequently Confused Pairs | Item 1 | Item 2 | Key Difference | |--------|--------|----------------| | DOL start | Star-delta start | 6× vs 2× inrush | | 3-pole isolator | 4-pole isolator | 4-pole breaks neutral | | Prime rating | Standby rating | Prime = unlimited hrs; Standby = 200 hrs/yr with 10% overload 1 hr/12 hr | | UPS | DG | UPS < 10 ms transfer; DG = 10–30 s | | Body earth | Neutral earth | Body for safety; Neutral for system reference |

topics.title: Essential Protections, UPS System and its Design for Residential Application, Selection and Installation of Elevators and Lifts, Earthing and Soil Resistivity Calculation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT752 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Essential Protections, UPS System and its Design for Residential Application, Selection and Installation of Elevators and Lifts, Earthing and Soil Resistivity Calculation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Essential Protections in Electrical Systems

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
Essential protections in an electrical system refer to the coordinated set of devices and schemes designed to detect abnormal operating conditions (such as overcurrent, earth faults, under/over voltage, surges, and lightning strikes) and to isolate the faulty section automatically without endangering personnel, equipment, or the continuity of supply to healthy sections.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per **IS 732:2019** and **IE Rules 1956 (Amended)**, every low-voltage installation in India **must** incorporate protection against overcurrent, earth faults, and overvoltages. KTU examiners treat this as a guaranteed question.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of your home's electrical wiring as a network of **water pipes**:
- **Overload** is like a pipe being asked to carry far more water than it was designed for (too many taps open). The pipe gets hot.
- **Short circuit** is like a pipe bursting — water rushes out uncontrollably.
- **Earth fault** is like a leak from the pipe into the surrounding soil (earth). Dangerous!
- **MCB/MCCB/Fuse** are the **automatic shut-off valves** that close the moment danger is detected.
- **ELCB/RCD** are the **leak detectors** — they trip the moment even a tiny current escapes to earth (as little as 30 mA can be lethal to a human).

> [!NOTE]
> The human body begins to feel shock at approximately **1 mA**, becomes painful at **5–15 mA**, and ventricular fibrillation (often fatal) starts at just **30 mA for 1 second**. This is why residual current devices trip at **30 mA**.

### The Five Essential Protections in a Residential Installation

| # | Protection Type | Device Used | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Overload Protection** | MCB (C-curve), MCCB | Protects cables from sustained overcurrent |
| 2 | **Short-Circuit Protection** | MCB (B-curve), HRC Fuse | Clears high fault currents in milliseconds |
| 3 | **Earth-Fault Protection** | RCCB / ELBC / RCBO | Detects leakage to earth, prevents electrocution |
| 4 | **Overvoltage / Surge Protection** | SPD (Type 1, 2, 3) | Protects from lightning & switching transients |
| 5 | **Under-Voltage / Phase Loss** | UV relay, Phase-sequence relay | Protects 3-phase motors from single-phasing |

---

## 1.2 UPS System and its Design for Residential Application

**Formal Definition:**
An **Uninterruptible Power Supply (UPS)** is a static power-conditioning device that provides **continuous, regulated, and instantaneous backup power** to a connected load during mains failure, voltage sags, surges, or frequency variations, by drawing energy from a stored DC source (typically a battery bank) through an inverter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A UPS differs from an **Inverter** in one critical aspect: the UPS has a **transfer time of < 10 ms** (typically 2–4 ms), whereas a standby inverter may take 20–100 ms — long enough to reset a computer or trip a PLC. KTU often asks this difference.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are drinking water from a municipal tap:
- The **EB grid** is the municipal tap.
- The **UPS battery** is a small tank kept on the roof, always full.
- A **non-return valve with a sensor** is the static switch — the moment the tap (grid) goes dry, the valve opens and you get water from the tank within milliseconds.
- For a **computer** (your CPU/laptop), even a 20 ms gap causes a reboot and data loss. The UPS closes that gap.

### UPS Architecture Types (Must-Know for KTU)

1. **Offline / Standby UPS** — Load is normally on mains; battery+inverter kicks in only on failure. Cheapest, transfer time ~5–10 ms. Suitable for PCs, CCTV.
2. **Line-Interactive UPS** — Has an automatic voltage regulator (AVR) that boosts/trims mains without switching to battery. Smoother. Suitable for homes with frequent voltage fluctuations.
3. **Online / Double-Conversion UPS** — Load is *always* fed through the inverter; mains only charges the battery. Zero transfer time. Used for servers, medical equipment, and high-end home offices.

---

## 1.3 Selection and Installation of Elevators and Lifts

**Formal Definition:**
An **elevator (or lift)** is a vertical transportation device that moves people or goods between floors of a building using a hoisting mechanism driven by an electric motor, governed by **IS 14665 (Parts 1–5)** in India and previously referred to in the **National Building Code of India (NBC 2016, Part 8, Section 5)**.

> [!NOTE]
> The terms "elevator" (American English) and "lift" (British English) are used interchangeably. KTU questions on this topic almost always revolve around **sizing a lift for a residential building** — you must know the formula for **lift motor rating**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

A lift is essentially a **rope-pulled balance scale**:
- The **car (cab)** hangs on one end of a steel rope.
- A **counterweight** equal to (car weight + 40–50% of rated load) hangs on the other end.
- The **motor** is the "hand" that pulls either side up or down.
- The **traction sheave** (pulley with grooves) is the pivot point.
- **Brakes** are your safety hand — they engage the moment power fails (fail-safe spring-applied, electrically released).

### Types of Lifts (KTU Favorite)

| Type | Drive | Speed | Residential Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Traction (Geared)** | Gearbox + Motor | 0.5–1.5 m/s | Common in mid-rise |
| **Traction (Gearless)** | Direct-drive PMSM | 1.0–2.5 m/s | High-rise |
| **Hydraulic** | Oil pump + Cylinder | 0.25–1.0 m/s | Low-rise (≤ 4 floors) |
| **Machine-Room-Less (MRL)** | Compact PM motor in shaft | 1.0–2.0 m/s | Modern compact homes |

---

## 1.4 Earthing and Soil Resistivity Calculation

**Formal Definition:**
**Earthing** is the process of creating a low-resistance electrical connection between a non-current-carrying metallic part of an electrical system and the general mass of earth, so that fault currents dissipate safely into the ground without causing dangerous touch voltages.

**Soil resistivity (ρ)** is the electrical resistance of a unit cube of soil, measured in **ohm-metres (Ω·m)**. It is the single most important parameter in designing any earthing system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per **IS 3043:2018**, the maximum permissible earth-electrode resistance for a residential installation is **≤ 5 Ω** (some utilities demand ≤ 1 Ω for transformer neutrals).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the earth as a giant **sponge soaked in salty water (electrolyte)**:
- A **dry rocky soil** is like a dried-out sponge — almost no conduction. **ρ is high (1000+ Ω·m)**.
- A **moist clay soil** is like a wet sponge — conduction is excellent. **ρ is low (10–100 Ω·m)**.
- A **copper plate buried in the sponge** is the earth electrode. The lower the ρ and the bigger the plate, the easier current flows.
- The **"resistance to ground"** is like the effort required to push a thumb into the sponge — easier if the sponge is soft (low ρ).

> [!WARNING]
> Soil resistivity **changes seasonally** — it spikes in dry summer and drops in monsoon. KTU problems often state "worst-case (summer) value" — always design for that.

### Types of Earthing (Indian Practice)

1. **Plate Earthing** — Copper/GI plate of size 60 cm × 60 cm × 6.3 mm (Cu) or 60 cm × 60 cm × 6 mm (GI), buried vertically in alternate layers of charcoal and salt.
2. **Pipe Earthing** — GI pipe of 40 mm dia, 2.5 m long, with holes drilled near the bottom, treated similarly. Most common for residential.
3. **Rod Earthing** — Solid copper rod or copper-clad steel rod, 1.2–3 m long, driven into soil. Used in rocky terrain.

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Essential Protections — Deep Analysis

### 2.1.1 Overload vs Short-Circuit Protection (Conceptual Distinction)

- **Overload** = small but sustained overcurrent (e.g., 1.2–1.5 × rated). Heats up the cable over minutes/hours. MCB trips on its **thermal bimetal strip**.
- **Short circuit** = massive instantaneous current (5–20 kA). The **electromagnetic solenoid** inside the MCB reacts in **< 10 ms**.

> [!NOTE]
> The **I²t characteristic** of a protective device must be **less than the I²t withstand** of the cable — this is called **device-to-cable coordination**. KTU often asks: "Verify that the MCB protects the cable."

### 2.1.2 Discrimination (Selectivity)

Discrimination is the ability of upstream and downstream protective devices to coordinate such that **only the device closest to the fault trips**, leaving the rest of the installation healthy.

**Achieved by:**
- **Current discrimination** — Upstream device set to a higher current band.
- **Time discrimination** — Upstream has intentional time delay.
- **Energy discrimination (zone-selective interlocking)** — Communication between devices.

### 2.1.3 Surge Protection Devices (SPDs)

Classified by **IEC 61643-11**:
- **Type 1** — At the service entrance, for direct lightning strikes. **I_imp ≥ 12.5 kA (10/350 μs)**.
- **Type 2** — At sub-distribution boards. **I_n = 20 kA (8/20 μs)**.
- **Type 3** — Near sensitive equipment (TV, PC). **Uoc = 6–10 kV**.

---

## 2.2 UPS System — Deep Analysis

### 2.2.1 Sizing a UPS (Three Critical Parameters)

When you size a UPS for a residence, you need three numbers:

1. **Total Connected Load (W)** — Sum of all appliance wattages you want to back up.
2. **Power Factor (PF)** of the load — Modern SMPS loads have PF ≈ 0.9–1.0; older inductive loads may be 0.6–0.8.
3. **Required Backup Time (minutes)** — Determines battery capacity.

### 2.2.2 Battery Sizing Logic

Battery capacity (Ah) is governed by Peukert's Law and discharge rate. For a sealed lead-acid (VRLA) or lithium battery:

$$\text{Required Ah} = \frac{\text{Load (W)} \times \text{Backup Time (h)}}{\text{Battery Voltage (V)} \times \text{Inverter Efficiency} \times \text{Discharge Factor}}$$

For a typical **12 V lead-acid** battery discharging over 1 hour at 0.85 efficiency:
$$\text{Ah} = \frac{P \times t}{12 \times 0.85 \times 0.8}$$

### 2.2.3 VA vs Watts (Common KTU Trap)

$$\text{VA Rating of UPS} = \frac{\text{Watt Load}}{\text{Power Factor}}$$

A 1000 W load at 0.8 PF requires a **1250 VA** UPS, not a 1000 VA one.

---

## 2.3 Elevators and Lifts — Deep Analysis

### 2.3.1 Lift Selection Criteria (KTU Design Steps)

A KTU problem typically gives you:
- Number of floors
- Population per floor
- Building height
- Waiting time target

And asks for: **car capacity, speed, motor rating, counterweight**.

### 2.3.2 Motor Power Calculation

The driving motor must overcome the **net unbalanced load** plus friction and acceleration.

$$P_{motor} = \frac{(Q + C_w) \times v}{\eta}$$

Where:
- $Q$ = Net unbalanced load (kg) = Rated Load × 0.4 (typically)
- $C_w$ = Car weight (kg)
- $v$ = Contract speed (m/s)
- $\eta$ = Drive efficiency (0.75–0.85 geared; 0.85–0.92 gearless)

### 2.3.3 Counterweight Philosophy

The counterweight balances the car **plus 40–50% of rated load** — this minimises the peak motor power, because in normal operation the car is either empty (going down) or full (going down), and counterweight assists in both.

$$C_{cw} = C_w + (0.4 \text{ to } 0.5) \times Q_{rated}$$

---

## 2.4 Earthing and Soil Resistivity — Deep Analysis

### 2.4.1 The Wenner Four-Pin Method (Most Tested)

Four equally-spaced electrodes are driven into the soil in a straight line, with the two outer electrodes carrying current and the two inner electrodes measuring potential.

$$\rho = 2\pi a R$$

Where:
- $a$ = spacing between adjacent electrodes (metres)
- $R$ = measured resistance (ohms)
- $\rho$ = apparent soil resistivity (Ω·m)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The depth of measurement = electrode spacing $a$. So if you set $a$ = 5 m, you are measuring the average resistivity of the top 5 m of soil — exactly what matters for a plate earthing.

### 2.4.2 Earth Electrode Resistance Formulas

For a **plate electrode** of area $A$ (m²) buried at depth $h$ (m):
$$R_e = \frac{\rho}{4} \sqrt{\frac{\pi}{A}} \left(1 + \frac{1}{2h}\sqrt{\frac{A}{\pi}}\right)$$

Simplified (when $h \gg \sqrt{A/\pi}$):
$$R_e \approx \frac{\rho}{4r} \quad \text{where } r = \sqrt{A/\pi}$$

For a **pipe/rod electrode** of length $L$ and diameter $d$ (m):
$$R_e = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L} \ln\left(\frac{4L}{d}\right)$$

For a **horizontal strip** of length $L$ at depth $h$:
$$R_e = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L} \ln\left(\frac{2L}{h}\right)$$

### 2.4.3 Resistance of Multiple Electrodes in Parallel

For $n$ identical electrodes:
$$R_n = \frac{R_1}{n} \times F$$

Where $F$ is a **usage factor** (0.7–0.9) accounting for proximity — electrodes too close interfere with each other.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Topic | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | UPS VA rating | $S_{VA} = \dfrac{P_W}{PF}$ | $P_W$ in W, $S_{VA}$ in VA |
| 2 | Battery capacity | $Ah = \dfrac{P \cdot t}{V_{bat} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot k_d}$ | $t$ in h, $V_{bat}$ in V, $k_d$ ≈ 0.8 |
| 3 | Lift motor power | $P = \dfrac{(Q + C_w) \cdot v}{\eta}$ | $Q$ in kgf (= kg), $v$ in m/s, $P$ in W |
| 4 | Lift counterweight | $C_{cw} = C_w + 0.45 \cdot Q_{rated}$ | All in kg |
| 5 | Soil resistivity (Wenner) | $\rho = 2\pi a R$ | $a$ in m, $R$ in Ω, $\rho$ in Ω·m |
| 6 | Plate electrode resistance | $R_e \approx \dfrac{\rho}{4r}$ | $r = \sqrt{A/\pi}$ in m |
| 7 | Pipe/rod electrode | $R_e = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}\ln\left(\dfrac{4L}{d}\right)$ | $L$, $d$ in m |
| 8 | Multiple electrodes | $R_n = \dfrac{R_1 \cdot F}{n}$ | $F$ = 0.7–0.9 |
| 9 | Trip current for shock | $I \le \dfrac{30 \text{ mA}}{t \text{ (s)}}$ | Human body limit |
| 10 | MCB rating rule | $I_{b} \le I_{n} \le I_{z}$ and $I_{f} \le 1.45 \cdot I_{z}$ | IEC 60364 / IS 732 |
| 11 | Touch voltage limit | $V_{touch} \le 50 \text{ V (AC)}$ | Dry indoor (25 V for wet) |
| 12 | Step voltage limit | $V_{step} \le 50 \text{ + } \rho_s$ | ρ_s = surface layer resistivity |

> [!NOTE]
> Memorise formulas 5, 6, 7, 2, 3, 4 — these six appear in **90% of KTU ESE problems** on this module.

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Wenner Soil Resistivity Formula

We will derive the expression $\rho = 2\pi a R$ from first principles using the geometry of current flow from a point source.

### Setup

Consider four electrodes $C_1$, $P_1$, $P_2$, $C_2$ placed in a straight line in homogeneous soil of resistivity $\rho$, with spacing $a$ between adjacent electrodes.

- Outer electrodes $C_1$, $C_2$ carry current $I$ (in and out).
- Inner electrodes $P_1$, $P_2$ measure potential difference $V$.

### Step 1: Potential Due to a Current Source in Infinite Soil

For a single hemispherical electrode of effective radius $r$ injecting current $I$ into soil of resistivity $\rho$, the current density at distance $x$ is:

$$J = \frac{I}{2\pi x^2}$$

(Factor $2\pi$ because current flows through a hemisphere, not a full sphere.)

The electric field at distance $x$:
$$E = \rho J = \frac{\rho I}{2\pi x^2}$$

The potential at distance $x$ (from infinity):
$$V = \int_x^{\infty} E \, dx = \int_x^{\infty} \frac{\rho I}{2\pi x^2} dx = \frac{\rho I}{2\pi x}$$

### Step 2: Apply Superposition at Inner Electrode $P_1$

At $P_1$:
- Distance from $C_1$ = $a$ (current $I$ enters)
- Distance from $C_2$ = $3a$ (current $I$ leaves, so subtract)

$$V_{P_1} = \frac{\rho I}{2\pi a} - \frac{\rho I}{2\pi (3a)} = \frac{\rho I}{2\pi a}\left(1 - \frac{1}{3}\right) = \frac{\rho I}{2\pi a} \cdot \frac{2}{3}$$

### Step 3: Apply Superposition at Inner Electrode $P_2$

At $P_2$:
- Distance from $C_1$ = $2a$ (current $I$ enters)
- Distance from $C_2$ = $a$ (current $I$ leaves)

$$V_{P_2} = \frac{\rho I}{2\pi (2a)} - \frac{\rho I}{2\pi a} = \frac{\rho I}{2\pi a}\left(\frac{1}{2} - 1\right) = -\frac{\rho I}{2\pi a} \cdot \frac{1}{2}$$

### Step 4: Potential Difference

$$V = V_{P_1} - V_{P_2} = \frac{\rho I}{2\pi a}\left(\frac{2}{3} + \frac{1}{2}\right) = \frac{\rho I}{2\pi a} \cdot \frac{7}{6}$$

Hmm, that is not yet the standard form. The standard derivation uses the **mutual resistance** method; let us restart with the cleaner form.

### Step 4 (Corrected): General Potential Form

Potential at $P_1$ from $C_1$ at distance $a$ and from $C_2$ at distance $2a$ (measuring from $P_1$ to $C_2$ along the line of four electrodes):

Taking origin at $P_1$: $C_1$ is at $x = -a$, $C_2$ is at $x = 2a$, $P_2$ is at $x = a$.

$$V_{P_1} = \frac{\rho I}{2\pi a} - \frac{\rho I}{2\pi (2a)} = \frac{\rho I}{2\pi a}\left(1 - \frac{1}{2}\right) = \frac{\rho I}{4\pi a}$$

$$V_{P_2} = \frac{\rho I}{2\pi (2a)} - \frac{\rho I}{2\pi a} = -\frac{\rho I}{4\pi a}$$

$$V = V_{P_1} - V_{P_2} = \frac{\rho I}{4\pi a} - \left(-\frac{\rho I}{4\pi a}\right) = \frac{\rho I}{2\pi a}$$

### Step 5: Solve for ρ

$$V = \frac{\rho I}{2\pi a}$$

Since $R = V/I$:
$$R = \frac{\rho}{2\pi a}$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{\rho = 2\pi a R}$$

This is the **Wenner four-pin formula**, the most-cited equation in KTU earthing problems.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Pipe Electrode Earth Resistance

For a rod or pipe electrode of length $L$ and diameter $d$ driven vertically in soil of resistivity $\rho$, we treat the rod as a thin line source.

### Step 1: Total Resistance from a Hemispherical Shell Model

The resistance of the soil surrounding the rod can be considered as a series of concentric cylindrical shells of radius $x$ and thickness $dx$ along the rod length $L$.

The resistance of one such shell (length $L$, radius $x$):
$$dR = \frac{\rho \, dx}{2\pi x L}$$

### Step 2: Integrate from Surface of Rod to Infinity

Integrate from the rod surface (radius $d/2$) to the outer limit. As the radius grows, current spreads out further; mathematically, the integration limit is chosen to match the field of an equivalent hemisphere of radius $L$:

$$R = \int_{d/2}^{L} \frac{\rho \, dx}{2\pi x L} \cdot 2 = \frac{\rho}{\pi L} \int_{d/2}^{L} \frac{dx}{x}$$

(We multiplied by 2 to account for the fact that current flows radially on **all** sides.)

### Step 3: Evaluate the Logarithm

$$R = \frac{\rho}{\pi L} \left[\ln x\right]_{d/2}^{L} = \frac{\rho}{\pi L} \ln\left(\frac{L}{d/2}\right) = \frac{\rho}{\pi L} \ln\left(\frac{2L}{d}\right)$$

### Step 4: Standard Form

$$\boxed{R_e = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L}\ln\left(\frac{4L}{d}\right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor inside the log changes from $\frac{2L}{d}$ to $\frac{4L}{d}$ when we adopt the "average potential" boundary condition (Schwarz's formula). KTU textbooks use the **2π** form, so memorise the boxed version.

---

## 3.3 Solved Numerical: Residential UPS Sizing

**Problem (KTU-style):**
A residential user wants to back up the following load during a power cut: 4 LED bulbs × 20 W, 2 ceiling fans × 75 W, 1 refrigerator × 200 W, 1 Wi-Fi router × 15 W, 1 laptop × 90 W, 1 TV (50") × 120 W. Required backup time: **45 minutes**. Inverter efficiency: 0.85. Battery voltage: 12 V. Discharging factor: 0.8. Power factor of total load: 0.8. Determine: (a) total load in W and VA, (b) battery capacity in Ah, (c) battery configuration.

### Part (a): Total Load

| Appliance | Qty | Wattage | Sub-total |
|---|---|---|---|
| LED bulbs | 4 | 20 W | 80 W |
| Ceiling fans | 2 | 75 W | 150 W |
| Refrigerator | 1 | 200 W | 200 W |
| Wi-Fi router | 1 | 15 W | 15 W |
| Laptop | 1 | 90 W | 90 W |
| TV | 1 | 120 W | 120 W |
| **Total** | | | **655 W** |

**VA rating of UPS** (with 25% safety margin):
$$S_{VA} = \frac{P_W}{PF} \times 1.25 = \frac{655}{0.8} \times 1.25 = 1023.4 \text{ VA}$$

➡ **Select next standard size: 1.5 kVA (1500 VA) UPS.**

### Part (b): Battery Capacity

$$Ah = \frac{P \times t}{V_{bat} \times \eta_{inv} \times k_d} = \frac{655 \times 0.75}{12 \times 0.85 \times 0.8}$$

$$Ah = \frac{491.25}{8.16} = 60.2 \text{ Ah}$$

➡ **Select next standard size: 65 Ah or 75 Ah battery.**

### Part (c): Battery Configuration

For 1.5 kVA online UPS: usually single 12 V battery.
For higher capacity: 2 × 12 V in series for 24 V, etc.

**Recommendation: 1 × 12 V, 75 Ah C10 rated VRLA battery.**

---

## 3.4 Solved Numerical: Lift Motor Power

**Problem:**
Design a residential lift for a 6-storey building (G+5). Floor-to-floor height = 3.2 m. Rated passenger load = 6 persons (assume 75 kg per person). Car weight = 600 kg. Contract speed = 1 m/s. Drive efficiency = 0.85. Find: (a) counterweight, (b) motor power, (c) minimum motor size.

### Part (a): Counterweight

$$C_{cw} = C_w + 0.45 \times Q_{rated} = 600 + 0.45 \times (6 \times 75) = 600 + 202.5 = 802.5 \text{ kg}$$

### Part (b): Motor Power (Maximum Unbalanced Load)

The **maximum unbalanced load** occurs when the car is fully loaded and moving up (or empty and moving down). The net unbalanced load = rated load (because the counterweight already balances 45% of rated load, but the car is 100% full, leaving 55% as net).

Wait — more precisely, the net unbalanced load at worst is when car is **100% loaded going up**:

$$Q_{net} = Q_{rated} - 0.45 Q_{rated} = 0.55 Q_{rated} = 0.55 \times 450 = 247.5 \text{ kg}$$

But to accelerate and overcome friction, design margin is applied:

$$Q_{design} = 1.5 \times Q_{net} \approx 370 \text{ kg (round up)}$$

Motor power:
$$P = \frac{(Q_{design} + C_w) \times v}{\eta} = \frac{(370 + 600) \times 1}{0.85} = \frac{970}{0.85} \approx 1141 \text{ W}$$

Wait — this formulation is conservative. The standard KTU textbook formula is:

$$P = \frac{(C_w + Q_{net}) \cdot v}{\eta \cdot 1000} \text{ kW}$$

$$P = \frac{(600 + 247.5) \times 1}{0.85 \times 1000} = \frac{847.5}{850} \approx 0.997 \text{ kW}$$

➡ **Select a 1.1 kW (or 1.5 kW with margin) motor.**

### Part (c): Travel

Total travel = 5 × 3.2 = 16 m. (G to 5th floor = 5 floor intervals.)

---

## 3.5 Solved Numerical: Soil Resistivity & Plate Earthing

**Problem:**
A plate earthing system is installed at a residential site. The Wenner four-pin test gave the following readings: electrode spacing $a = 5$ m, measured resistance $R = 12 \Omega$. A 60 cm × 60 cm copper plate is buried vertically at 3 m depth. The soil is moist clay. Compute: (a) soil resistivity, (b) plate earth resistance, (c) number of plates needed for $\le 5 \Omega$ in parallel (with usage factor 0.8).

### Part (a): Soil Resistivity

$$\rho = 2\pi a R = 2 \times 3.1416 \times 5 \times 12 = 377.0 \text{ Ω·m}$$

### Part (b): Plate Resistance

Plate area: $A = 0.6 \times 0.6 = 0.36$ m²

Equivalent radius: $r = \sqrt{A/\pi} = \sqrt{0.36/\pi} = 0.338$ m

$$R_e = \frac{\rho}{4r} = \frac{377}{4 \times 0.338} = \frac{377}{1.354} \approx 278.5 \text{ Ω}$$

This is way too high. So a single plate is insufficient. Let's try multiple plates.

### Part (c): Number of Plates

We need $R_n \le 5$ Ω. For $n$ plates in parallel with usage factor $F$:

$$R_n = \frac{R_1 \cdot F}{n} = \frac{278.5 \times 0.8}{n} \le 5$$

$$n \ge \frac{278.5 \times 0.8}{5} = \frac{222.8}{5} = 44.56$$

➡ **Use 45 plates in parallel.** 

This is impractical! Hence, for $\rho > 100$ Ω·m, **pipe earthing with chemical treatment (salt + charcoal)** is the practical solution in Kerala.

### Re-Design Using Pipe Earthing

Try a **GI pipe** of 40 mm dia, 2.5 m long.

$$R_e = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L}\ln\left(\frac{4L}{d}\right) = \frac{377}{2\pi \times 2.5}\ln\left(\frac{4 \times 2.5}{0.04}\right)$$

$$R_e = \frac{377}{15.708} \ln(250) = 24.0 \times 5.521 = 132.5 \text{ Ω}$$

Still high. Use **salt + charcoal treatment** to reduce effective $\rho$ by factor of 5–10:

Effective $\rho_{eff} = 377 / 8 \approx 47$ Ω·m:
$$R_e = \frac{47}{2\pi \times 2.5}\ln(250) = 2.99 \times 5.521 = 16.5 \text{ Ω}$$

For $\le 5 \Omega$, with 4 pipes in parallel and $F = 0.85$:
$$R_n = \frac{16.5 \times 0.85}{4} = 3.51 \text{ Ω} \quad \checkmark$$

> [!TIP]
> KTU answer: **4 pipe electrodes of 40 mm dia × 2.5 m length, treated with salt and charcoal, spaced at least 3 m apart, gives an earth resistance of about 3.5 Ω — well within the 5 Ω limit.**

---

```mermaid

    STEP3 --> STEP4{R1 less than 5 ohm?}
    STEP4 -->|Yes| DONE[Single Electrode Sufficient]
    STEP4 -->|No| STEP5[Step 4: Add Chemical Treatment salt/charcoal]

    STEP7 -->|Yes| DONE2[Acceptable Design]
    STEP7 -->|No| STEP8[Step 6: Use n Electrodes in Parallel]
    STEP8 --> STEP9[Step 7: Apply Rn = R1 x F / n]
    STEP9 --> STEP10{Rn less than 5?}
    STEP10 -->|Yes| DONE3[n Electrodes Confirmed]
    STEP10 -->|No| STEP11[Use Deep Bore / Multiple Holes]
```
