The following Mermaid block-diagram illustrates the **functional architecture and assembly sequence** of a pipe earthing system, mapped from surface level down to the electrode tip. The diagram is rendered as a **block-level flow** to comply with Mermaid compilation safeguards (no reserved keywords used as node IDs; labels are raw uppercase alphanumeric text only).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["SURFACE LEVEL GROUND PLATE 30 CM X 30 CM CI COVER"] --> B["FUNNEL ASSEMBLY GI FUNNEL WITH WIRE MESH 10 CM BELOW SURFACE"]
    B --> C["EARTH LEAD CONNECTION GI STRIP 25 MM X 3 MM BOLTED TO PIPE"]
    C --> D["GI PIPE ELECTRODE LENGTH 2.44 M DIAMETER 38 MM VERTICAL ORIENTATION"]
    D --> E["LAYER 1 CHARCOAL 15 CM THICK HYGROSCOPIC MOISTURE RETAINER"]
    E --> F["LAYER 2 SALT NACL 15 CM THICK IONIC CONDUCTIVITY ENHANCER"]
    F --> G["LAYER 3 CHARCOAL 15 CM THICK ALTERNATING FILL"]
    G --> H["LAYER 4 SALT NACL 15 CM THICK BOTTOM RETENTION LAYER"]
    H --> I["PIT FLOOR 1.25 M TO 4.0 M BELOW SURFACE"]
    D --> J["PERFORATIONS 12 MM DIA HOLES 7.5 CM CENTERS ALONG LOWER PIPE LENGTH"]
    J --> K["MOISTURE SEEPAGE PATH WATER FROM FUNNEL ENTERS SOIL THROUGH HOLES"]
    F --> L["ELECTROLYTIC ZONE DISSOLVED NA AND CL IONS CREATE CONDUCTIVE SOIL"]
    L --> M["EQUIPOTENTIAL HEMISPHERICAL SHELLS OF CURRENT DISSIPATION"]
    M --> N["BULK EARTH INFINITE SOIL MASS FAULT CURRENT NEUTRALIZED"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Starting from the topmost node (ground surface) and following the downward arrows, you traverse the physical layers in installation order. The lateral branch (D → J → K → L → M → N) shows the **functional flow of fault current and water**, illustrating how the perforated pipe allows moisture to seep outward into the salt-charcoal layers, which in turn reduce soil resistivity and create a low-impedance path to the bulk earth.

## 4.2 Equivalent Electrical Circuit Analogy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["LIVE FAULT SOURCE V 230V"] --> R1["CONDUCTOR RESISTANCE R_C 0.5 OHM"]
    R1 --> R2["CONTACT RESISTANCE R_K 1.0 OHM"]
    R2 --> R3["PIPE TO SOIL RESISTANCE R_E TARGET LESS THAN 5 OHM"]
    R3 --> G["BULK EARTH 0V REFERENCE"]
    G -.-> P
```

> **Insight:** The total fault-loop impedance is $R_{total} = R_C + R_K + R_E$. For a 230 V system, the prospective fault current is $I_f = V / R_{total}$. To ensure the protective device (MCB/fuse) operates within its breaking time, $R_{total}$ must be low enough to drive sufficient current — typically $R_E \le 5$ Ω for domestic and $R_E \le 1$ Ω for substation installations.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**List the essential components of a pipe earthing system and state the function of charcoal and salt in it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The essential components of a pipe earthing system are:
1. **GI/CI Pipe Electrode** (length 2.44 m, diameter 38 mm minimum) — acts as the earth electrode.
2. **Earth Lead** (GI strip 25 mm × 3 mm or copper conductor) — connects the pipe to the installation's main earth.
3. **Cast Iron Cover Plate** — protects the top of the pipe and the pit.
4. **GI Funnel with Wire Mesh** — for periodic watering of the pit.
5. **Charcoal and Salt Layers** — fill the annular space around the pipe in alternating layers.

**Function of Charcoal and Salt:**
- **Charcoal** is porous and acts as a moisture-retaining sponge, absorbing rainwater and atmospheric humidity.
- **Salt (NaCl)** is hygroscopic and dissolves in the retained water to form a conductive electrolyte, drastically reducing the **effective soil resistivity** around the electrode from 100–1000 Ω·m down to 10–50 Ω·m.

*(Marking: 1 mark for component list, 1 mark for charcoal function, 1 mark for salt function.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**State and explain the formula for the earth resistance of a vertical pipe electrode.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The earth resistance of a vertical pipe electrode of length $L$ and diameter $d$ buried in soil of resistivity $\rho$ is given by:

$$R_e = \frac{\rho}{2 \pi L} \left[ \ln\left(\frac{4L}{d}\right) - 1 \right]$$

where:
- $R_e$ = earth resistance in ohms (Ω)
- $\rho$ = soil resistivity in ohm-metres (Ω·m)
- $L$ = length of pipe electrode in metres (m)
- $d$ = diameter of pipe electrode in metres (m)

**Explanation (1 mark):** The resistance is **directly proportional to soil resistivity** (better soil → lower $R_e$) and **inversely proportional to pipe length** (longer pipe → lower $R_e$). The logarithmic term accounts for the geometric spreading of current from a cylindrical electrode into the hemispherical soil mass.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** In pipe earthing numerical problems, students frequently lose marks for the following reasons:
> 1. **Unit inconsistency** — using diameter in mm without converting to metres. Always convert $d$ to metres.
> 2. **Forgetting the `-1` term** in the logarithmic bracket — full marks require the complete expression.
> 3. **Using $\log_{10}$ when the formula expects $\ln$** — both are acceptable only if used consistently. State the base.
> 4. **Not commenting on the safety/adequacy** of the final $R_e$ value with respect to IS:3043 norms (≤ 5 Ω for domestic, ≤ 1 Ω for substation).
> 5. **Skipping the derivation/physical reasoning** — KTU examiners award at least 3–4 marks for "explanation" components.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **pipe earthing system**. List the standard dimensions of the pipe as per **IS: 3043**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A GI pipe of **2.5 m length and 40 mm diameter** is used as a pipe electrode in soil of resistivity **150 Ω·m**. Calculate:
- (i) The earth resistance of the electrode.
- (ii) The earth resistance if **two such pipes are connected in parallel**, spaced 3 m apart.
- (iii) Comment on whether the combined resistance meets IS: 3043 norms. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Construction (4 marks):**

A pit of size **30 cm × 30 cm** is dug to a depth of approximately **1.5 m to 4.0 m**. A **GI pipe of length 2.44 m and diameter 38 mm** is placed vertically at the centre of the pit. The pipe is **perforated with 12 mm holes at 7.5 cm centres** along its lower length to allow moisture seepage. The annular space around the pipe is filled with **alternating layers of charcoal (coke) and common salt (NaCl)**, each layer approximately **15 cm thick**, with charcoal at the bottom and salt above, repeating. A **GI funnel with wire mesh** is fitted at the top of the pipe, projecting above ground level, to allow periodic watering. A **CI cover plate** is placed over the pit. The **earth lead** (GI strip 25 × 3 mm) is bolted to the top of the pipe using **GI nut, bolt, and washers**.

**Working (2 marks):**

During a fault, current from the equipment flows through the earth lead into the pipe, then into the moist, salt-laden soil. The electrolyte created by dissolved salt reduces the soil's effective resistivity, allowing the fault current to dissipate into the bulk earth with minimal resistance. Periodic watering through the funnel replenishes the moisture, ensuring long-term stability of earth resistance.

**Standard Dimensions (1 mark):** As per **IS: 3043**, pipe diameter ≥ 38 mm, length ≥ 2.44 m for ordinary soil; for dry/rocky soil, length 3.0–4.0 m and diameter up to 100 mm.

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Given:**
- $L = 2.5$ m, $d = 40$ mm $= 0.04$ m, $\rho = 150$ Ω·m

**(i) Single pipe resistance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{4L}{d} &= \frac{4 \times 2.5}{0.04} = \frac{10}{0.04} = 250 \\[6pt]
\ln(250) &= 5.5215 \\[6pt]
\left[ \ln(250) - 1 \right] &= 4.5215 \\[6pt]
R_{e1} &= \frac{150}{2 \pi \times 2.5} \times 4.5215 \\[6pt]
&= \frac{150}{15.708} \times 4.5215 \\[6pt]
&= 9.549 \times 4.5215 \\[6pt]
&\approx 43.18 \ \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Two pipes in parallel:**

Assuming each pipe has the same resistance $R_{e1} = 43.18$ Ω, the parallel combination is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{parallel} &= \frac{R_{e1}}{2} = \frac{43.18}{2} \approx 21.59 \ \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

However, due to **mutual resistance effects** (when two electrodes are within 1× their length, their resistance zones overlap), the actual combined resistance is higher than the simple parallel value. Using the empirical rule for two rods in parallel at spacing $S = 3$ m, the effective resistance is approximately:

$$
R_{e,eff} \approx R_{parallel} \times 1.15 \approx 24.83 \ \Omega
$$

**(iii) Comment:** For domestic installations, IS: 3043 recommends $R_e \le 5$ Ω, and for large installations $R_e \le 1$ Ω. The achieved value of **~24.83 Ω is well above the safe limit**. To meet norms, the designer should:
- Increase pipe length (e.g., to 4.0 m),
- Use **salt-charcoal treatment** (reducing $\rho_{eff}$ to 30 Ω·m would give $R_{e1} \approx 8.6$ Ω and $R_{parallel} \approx 4.3$ Ω × 1.15 ≈ 5.0 Ω — borderline),
- Use **3 or more parallel pipes**,
- Use **chemical earth enhancers** like Marconite or bentonite.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Explain the **factors affecting earth electrode resistance**. Discuss how the **length, diameter, depth of burial, and soil resistivity** influence the effectiveness of a pipe earthing system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **3.0 m long, 50 mm diameter** GI pipe is installed in soil of resistivity **250 Ω·m**. After installation, salt-charcoal treatment reduces the effective resistivity to **40 Ω·m**.
- (i) Calculate the initial and post-treatment earth resistance.
- (ii) If the **soil temperature rises by 25 °C** after treatment, and the soil resistivity temperature coefficient is **−0.02 per °C**, find the new earth resistance.
- (iii) Suggest **two engineering methods** to bring the resistance below 5 Ω. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

The earth resistance of a pipe electrode depends on the following factors:

1. **Soil Resistivity ($\rho$):** $R_e \propto \rho$. Lower resistivity → lower resistance. Resistivity depends on **moisture content, salt content, temperature, and grain size**. Clay and marshy soil: 10–100 Ω·m; sandy/dry soil: 100–1000 Ω·m; rocky: 1000+ Ω·m.

2. **Length of Electrode ($L$):** $R_e \propto 1/L$. Doubling the pipe length reduces resistance by approximately **half**. For instance, increasing $L$ from 2.44 m to 4.0 m reduces $R_e$ by ~40%.

3. **Diameter of Electrode ($d$):** $R_e$ has only a **weak logarithmic dependence** on $d$ (via $\ln(4L/d)$). Doubling the diameter reduces $R_e$ by only ~10–15%. Hence, **increasing length is more effective than increasing diameter**.

4. **Depth of Burial:** For pipe electrodes (which are themselves vertical), depth of burial is already maximized. The principle is that the electrode should reach **permanently moist subsoil** below the water table.

5. **Soil Moisture & Temperature:** Moisture reduces resistivity; freezing or drying increases it. Salt-charcoal treatment stabilizes the moisture content.

6. **Number and Spacing of Electrodes (if multiple):** Connecting multiple pipes in parallel reduces resistance, but only if spaced **at least equal to their length apart** to avoid overlap of resistance zones.

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Given:** $L = 3.0$ m, $d = 50$ mm $= 0.05$ m, $\rho_1 = 250$ Ω·m, $\rho_2 = 40$ Ω·m (post-treatment).

**(i) Initial and post-treatment resistance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{4L}{d} &= \frac{4 \times 3.0}{0.05} = 240 \\[6pt]
\ln(240) &= 5.4806 \\[6pt]
\left[ \ln(240) - 1 \right] &= 4.4806
\end{aligned}
$$

Initial:

$$
R_{e1} = \frac{250}{2 \pi \times 3.0} \times 4.4806 = \frac{250}{18.8496} \times 4.4806 = 13.263 \times 4.4806 \approx 59.43 \ \Omega
$$

Post-treatment:

$$
R_{e2} = \frac{40}{18.8496} \times 4.4806 = 2.122 \times 4.4806 \approx 9.51 \ \Omega
$$

**(ii) Effect of Temperature Rise:**

Temperature coefficient $\alpha = -0.02$ per °C, $\Delta T = +25$ °C.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_3 &= \rho_2 \left[ 1 + \alpha \cdot \Delta T \right] \\[6pt]
&= 40 \times \left[ 1 + (-0.02) \times 25 \right] \\[6pt]
&= 40 \times \left[ 1 - 0.50 \right] \\[6pt]
&= 40 \times 0.50 = 20 \ \Omega\cdot\text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** A **negative** temperature coefficient means resistivity **decreases** with rising temperature (typical for soils — moisture conductivity improves with warmth). With $\Delta T = +25$ °C, the resistivity drops by 50%.

New resistance:

$$
R_{e3} = \frac{20}{18.8496} \times 4.4806 = 1.061 \times 4.4806 \approx 4.75 \ \Omega
$$

**(iii) Two engineering methods to bring $R_e < 5$ Ω:**

1. **Use of Chemical Earth-Enhancing Compounds (Marconite / Bentonite / GEM):** Replace the salt-charcoal fill with a permanent low-resistivity backfill. Marconite, a calcareous cement-based compound, achieves $\rho \le 2$ Ω·m when set, reducing $R_e$ by an order of magnitude.

2. **Multiple Parallel Pipes with Adequate Spacing:** Install **3 to 4 pipes** of 3.0 m length in parallel, spaced **≥ 6 m apart**, interconnected by a 50 × 6 mm GI strip. This reduces the combined resistance to $R_{combined} \approx R_{e2}/N \times 1.1 \approx 9.51/3 \times 1.1 \approx 3.5$ Ω.

(Alternative methods accepted: longer pipe to 4.5 m, deep-bore electrode in rocky soil, copper-bonded earth rods, etc.)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Board Exam Quick-Recall:**

- ☐ **Definition:** Pipe earthing uses a **vertical GI/CI pipe** as the earth electrode, with salt-charcoal fill.
- ☐ **Standard dimensions:** Length **≥ 2.44 m**, diameter **≥ 38 mm** (for ordinary soil).
- ☐ **Salt:** Hygroscopic, provides ionic conductivity by forming Na⁺/Cl⁻ electrolyte.
- ☐ **Charcoal:** Porous, retains moisture, prevents rapid drying of the soil.
- ☐ **Main formula:** $R_e = \dfrac{\rho}{2 \pi L} \left[ \ln\left(\dfrac{4L}{d}\right) - 1 \right]$
- ☐ **Always convert diameter from mm to m** before substituting in the formula.
- ☐ **Dominant factor:** Length $L$ (inversely proportional); diameter $d$ has only a logarithmic effect.
- ☐ **Soil resistivity order (low to high):** Marshy < Clay < Loam < Sand < Gravel < Rock.
- ☐ **IS: 3043 safe limits:** ≤ **5 Ω** for domestic, ≤ **1 Ω** for HV substations.
- ☐ **Parallel electrodes:** Must be spaced **at least equal to their length** to avoid mutual resistance overlap.
- ☐ **Temperature effect:** Negative coefficient — soil resistivity **decreases** with temperature rise.
- ☐ **Periodic maintenance:** Water the pit through the funnel **every 2–3 months** in dry seasons.
- ☐ **Material preference:** GI is preferred over CI (cast iron) because GI is corrosion-resistant and mechanically strong.
- ☐ **Perforations:** 12 mm holes at 7.5 cm centres along the lower portion of the pipe to allow water seepage.
- ☐ **Cover plate:** Cast iron cover at the top prevents accidental stepping and debris entry.
- ☐ **Earth lead:** GI strip 25 × 3 mm (domestic) or 50 × 6 mm (substation) bolted to pipe.
- ☐ **Funnel purpose:** Allows watering without removing the cover plate.
- ☐ **Key pitfall in KTU exams:** Not stating the base of the logarithm ($\ln$ vs. $\log_{10}$) — pick one and state it explicitly.
- ☐ **KTU 2024 CO mapping:** This topic maps to **CO4** — *Design electrical installations with appropriate earthing and lightning protection systems.*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rod Earthing, Methods of Improving Earth Resistance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEOT752 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Rod Earthing, Methods of Improving Earth Resistance ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Rod Earthing — Formal Definition **Rod Earthing** is the most commonly employed method of earthing in which a metallic rod (made of copper, galvanized iron (GI), or mild steel) is driven vertically into the ground (or buried in a pit) such that it makes intimate physical contact with the surrounding soil mass. The earth lead (conductor) from the electrical installation is securely bolted to the top of the rod using a nut-bolt-clamp arrangement, thereby providing a low-impedance path for fault/leakage currents to dissipate into the general body of earth. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** As per IS 3043:1987 (Reaffirmed 2018), the rod electrode is classified as a **"vertical driven type" earth electrode**, and is the preferred choice for sandy, rocky, or limited-area soils where horizontal plate electrodes are impractical. **Standard KTU-Accepted Rod Specifications:** | Parameter | Copper Rod | GI (Galvanized Iron) Rod | |---|---|---| | **Standard Length (L)** | **1.0 m – 3.0 m** (typical **2 m**) | **1.5 m – 3.0 m** (typical **2.5 m**) | | **Diameter (d)** | **12.5 mm – 25 mm** | **25 mm – 50 mm** | | **Surface Treatment** | Copper-bonded (to prevent galvanic corrosion) | Hot-dip galvanized | | **Typical Application** | Substations, sensitive electronic equipment | Domestic, commercial buildings | --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Imagine a straw in a glass of water.** > > - The **straw** = the metal rod. > - The **water** = the soil mass (the "earth"). > - The **amount of water that can flow through the straw per second** = the inverse of earth resistance (i.e., more flow = lower resistance). > > Now, what happens if you use a **longer straw**? → More contact with water → Lower resistance. > What happens if you use a **thicker straw**? → More cross-section → Lower resistance. > What happens if you use **multiple straws side-by-side**? → Even more water flows → Even lower resistance. > > This is *exactly* how rod earthing works. The rod is just a "straw" dipped into the "ocean of soil" to provide a safe leakage path for fault currents. --- ### 1.3 Why Rod Earthing? — Engineering Justification - **Ease of Installation:** A rod can be driven into the ground using a sledgehammer or pile driver — no major excavation required. - **Deep Earth Penetration:** Reaches permanently moist sub-soil layers, ensuring **stable, low earth resistance throughout the year**. - **Cost-Effective for Limited Spaces:** Ideal for urban installations and rocky terrain. - **Standardization:** KTU/IS specifications make the design calculations deterministic and repeatable. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Resistance of a Single Vertical Rod Electrode When a fault current **I** flows from the rod into the earth, the current does **not** flow in a straight line; rather, it **diverges radially outward** in all directions from the rod surface. The total resistance offered to this current is the sum of infinitesimal resistances of successive concentric cylindrical shells of soil surrounding the rod. #### Derivation Logic — Step-by-Step Bullets - **Step 1:** Consider a thin cylindrical shell of soil at radius **x** and thickness **dx** surrounding the rod. - **Step 2:** The cross-sectional area through which current flows at radius **x** is $A = 2\pi x L$ (lateral surface of the cylinder). - **Step 3:** The resistance of this thin shell of soil resistivity **ρ** is $dR = \dfrac{\rho \, dx}{2\pi x L}$. - **Step 4:** Integrate from the rod surface (radius = d/2) to infinity (theoretically the remote earth). - **Step 5:** This yields the **classical Dwight's Formula** (modified form, valid for L > d). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Dwight's Empirical Formula for Vertical Rod Resistance** is the **#1 High-Yield Formula** for KTU board exams on this topic. --- ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description / Variables | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $R = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[ \ln\left(\dfrac{4L}{d}\right) - 1 \right]$ | **Resistance of a single vertical rod (Dwight's Formula)** — valid when $L \gg d$ | $R$ in $\Omega$ | | 2 | $R = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L} \ln\left(\dfrac{4L}{d}\right)$ | **Approximate (Simon's) Formula** — for quick board exam calculations | $R$ in $\Omega$ | | 3 | $\dfrac{1}{R_{eq}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{1}{R_i}$ | **n Rods in Parallel** (general case) | $R_{eq}$ in $\Omega$ | | 4 | $R_{eq} = \dfrac{R}{n}$ | **n Identical Rods in Parallel** (if all rods have the same resistance R and are far apart, $\ge 2L$ spacing) | $R_{eq}$ in $\Omega$ | | 5 | $R_{eq} = \dfrac{R}{n} \left[1 + k \cdot \alpha\right]$ | **Practical Parallel Rods (with mutual resistance)** — k = mutual resistance coefficient, $\alpha$ = spacing factor | $R_{eq}$ in $\Omega$ | | 6 | $R_{total} = R_{rod} + R_{soil-layer}$ | Total earth resistance = rod-soil contact + soil layer resistance | $\Omega$ | | 7 | $\rho_{apparent} = 2\pi a R$ | **Wenner's Method** for soil resistivity (a = probe spacing) | $\rho$ in $\Omega \cdot m$ | **Key Variables Explained:** - **$\rho$** = Soil resistivity (typically **10 – 1000 $\Omega \cdot m$**; moist soil ≈ 100 $\Omega \cdot m$) - **L** = Length of rod (m) - **d** = Diameter of rod (m) - **R** = Resistance ($\Omega$) - **n** = Number of identical rods in parallel --- ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Domestic Earthing:** GI rod of **2 m length, 25 mm diameter** is the most common configuration in Kerala households. - **Substation Earthing:** Multiple **copper-bonded rods of 3 m length** are driven in a grid pattern, paralleled together to achieve **< 1 $\Omega$** earth resistance. - **Telecom Towers:** Rod earthing combined with chemical backfill compounds to maintain **< 5 $\Omega$** in dry, rocky terrain. - **Lightning Protection:** Rod electrodes form the down-conductor termination into the earth for LPS (Lightning Protection System) per IS/IEC 62305. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Resistance of a Vertical Rod (Dwight's Formula) We start with the resistance of an elemental soil shell at radius **x** from the axis of the rod. **Step 1 — Elemental Resistance of a Soil Shell** The current diverges radially through a cylindrical shell of radius **x**, length **L**, and thickness **dx**. The area available for current flow is the lateral surface area of the shell: $$A_x = 2\pi x L$$ The differential resistance of this thin shell, having soil resistivity **$\rho$**, is given by Ohm's law applied to the elemental volume: $$dR = \frac{\rho \, dx}{A_x} = \frac{\rho \, dx}{2\pi x L}$$ **Step 2 — Setting Up the Integration Limits** The total resistance is the integral of these elemental resistances from the **surface of the rod** (where $x = d/2$) to the **remote earth** (theoretically, $x = \infty$): $$R = \int_{d/2}^{\infty} dR = \int_{d/2}^{\infty} \frac{\rho \, dx}{2\pi x L}$$ **Step 3 — Evaluating the Integral** Pulling out the constants $\rho$, $2\pi$, and $L$: $$R = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L} \int_{d/2}^{\infty} \frac{dx}{x}$$ **Step 4 — Applying the Standard Logarithmic Integral** Using the standard result $\int \dfrac{dx}{x} = \ln(x)$: $$R = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[ \ln(x) \right]_{d/2}^{\infty}$$ **Step 5 — Substituting the Limits** Evaluating the antiderivative at the upper and lower limits: $$R = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[ \ln(\infty) - \ln(d/2) \right]$$ Since $\ln(\infty)$ diverges, we apply a finite-domain correction (this is where Dwight's empirical correction comes in). The actual current effectively returns to the surface of the earth at a finite distance (approximately equal to the rod length L), leading to the **empirical upper limit** $x \approx 2L$: $$R = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[ \ln(2L) - \ln(d/2) \right]$$ **Step 6 — Simplifying the Logarithmic Difference** Using the logarithmic identity $\ln(a) - \ln(b) = \ln(a/b)$: $$R = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L} \ln\left(\frac{2L}{d/2}\right) = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L} \ln\left(\frac{4L}{d}\right)$$ **Step 7 — Final Empirical Correction (Dwight's Adjustment)** Field-calibrated experiments show a small correction factor of **−1** must be subtracted (to account for the non-ideal current return path through the top soil surface): $$\boxed{R = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[ \ln\left(\frac{4L}{d}\right) - 1 \right]}$$ This is the **final, board-exam-ready Dwight's Formula** for a single vertical rod. --- ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Each Parameter on Rod Resistance | Parameter | Change Made | Effect on Resistance (R) | Magnitude of Effect | KTU Exam Tip | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Length (L) ↑** | Double L | R approximately **halves** (≈ 50% reduction) | **STRONGEST** effect | Doubling length is the *cheapest* way to reduce R | | **Diameter (d) ↑** | Double d | R decreases by only ≈ **8–10%** (logarithmic) | **WEAK** effect | Doubling diameter is *not economical* | | **Soil Resistivity (ρ) ↓** | Add salt/charcoal | R decreases **proportionally** to ρ | **STRONG** effect | ρ can drop by 5–10× with chemical treatment | | **Depth of Burial ↑** | Bury top deeper | R decreases marginally (already buried vertically) | **MODERATE** effect | Helps only if top soil is dry | | **Number of Rods (n) ↑** | Add n parallel rods | R reduces by factor of n (ideal case) | **STRONG** effect | Use **2L** spacing between rods for efficiency | > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight for KTU Board:** Doubling the **length** of the rod is **far more effective** than doubling its **diameter**. This is why deep-driven 3 m rods are preferred over short fat rods. --- ### 3.3 Worked Example — KTU Standard Numerical **Problem:** A copper rod of **3 m length** and **25 mm diameter** is driven vertically into soil of resistivity **100 $\Omega \cdot m$**. Calculate the earth resistance. If two such identical rods are connected in parallel with a spacing of **6 m**, find the equivalent earth resistance (assume negligible mutual resistance). **Solution:** **Given:** - $L = 3 \text{ m}$ - $d = 25 \text{ mm} = 0.025 \text{ m}$ - $\rho = 100 \, \Omega \cdot m$ - $n = 2$ rods in parallel **Step 1 — Resistance of a Single Rod (Dwight's Formula)** $$R = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[ \ln\left(\frac{4L}{d}\right) - 1 \right]$$ $$R = \frac{100}{2\pi \times 3} \left[ \ln\left(\frac{4 \times 3}{0.025}\right) - 1 \right]$$ $$R = \frac{100}{18.849} \left[ \ln(480) - 1 \right]$$ $$R = 5.305 \left[ 6.1738 - 1 \right]$$ $$R = 5.305 \times 5.1738$$ $$\boxed{R = 27.45 \, \Omega}$$ **Step 2 — Two Identical Rods in Parallel** $$R_{eq} = \frac{R}{n} = \frac{27.45}{2}$$ $$\boxed{R_{eq} = 13.72 \, \Omega}$$ **Step 3 — Verify Spacing Adequacy** Required spacing ≥ 2L = 2 × 3 = **6 m** ✓ (Spacing of 6 m is just sufficient to minimize mutual resistance). ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Rod Earthing System (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Electrical Installation
LV Panel / Equipment] -->|Earth Lead via GI/Copper Wire| B[Earth Clamp at Top of Rod] B --> C[Vertical Rod Electrode
Cu or GI, 2-3 m] C --> D[Soil Resistance Region
Concentric Cylindrical Shells] D --> E[Moist Sub-Soil Layer
Stable Resistivity Zone] E --> F[Remote Earth
Zero Potential Reference] C -.->|Optional: Backfill| G[Charcoal + Salt Layer
Reduces rho] G --> D ``` **Description of Flow:** - The **Electrical Installation** (Panel/Equipment) connects via an earth lead to a **clamp** at the top of the rod. - Current flows down the **Vertical Rod Electrode** into the soil. - It diverges radially through **concentric cylindrical shells** of soil (each shell adds a small resistance). - The current eventually reaches the **Remote Earth** (zero-potential reference). - The **charcoal-salt backfill** (optional) lowers the local soil resistivity, improving the dissipation efficiency. --- ### 4.2 Block-Level Flow — Methods to Improve Earth Resistance ```mermaid flowchart LR M1[Method 1
Increase Rod Length L] -->|Most Effective| R1[Earth Resistance Reduced] M2[Method 2
Increase Rod Diameter d] -->|Marginal Effect| R1 M3[Method 3
Use Multiple Rods in Parallel] -->|Strong Effect| R1 M4[Method 4
Soil Treatment
Salt + Charcoal + Water] -->|Strong Effect| R1 M5[Method 5
Use Earth Plate
Alternative to Rod] -->|Moderate Effect| R1 M6[Method 6
Chemical Compounds
Bentonite / Marconite] -->|Strong Effect| R1 M7[Method 7
Keep Soil Moist
Regular Watering] -->|Moderate Effect| R1 M8[Method 8
Deep Bore Well
with Pipe Electrode] -->|Strong Effect| R1 M1 --> R2[Target: R_earth less than 1 ohm
for substations, less than 5 ohm
for domestic] M2 --> R2 M3 --> R2 M4 --> R2 M5 --> R2 M6 --> R2 M7 --> R2 M8 --> R2 ``` --- ### 4.3 Cross-Section of a Typical Rod Earthing Pit (Functional Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart TB P1[Ground Level Surface] --> P2[Top Soil Layer
Dry, High Resistivity] P2 --> P3[Moist Sub-Soil Layer
Low, Stable Resistivity] P3 --> P4[Deep Water Table Zone
Very Low Resistivity] R1[Rod Top with Clamp
and Earth Lead] -.->|Stays Above| P1 R2[Rod Section
L 1] -.->|Embedded in| P2 R3[Rod Section
L 2] -.->|Embedded in| P3 R4[Rod Tip / Pointed End] -.->|Embedded in| P4 B1[Charcoal Layer
50 mm thick] -.->|Surrounds Rod| P3 B2[Salt Layer NaCl
50 mm thick] -.->|Surrounds Rod| P3 ``` **Reading the Schematic:** The rod penetrates through the dry top soil into the moist sub-soil where resistivity is low and stable year-round. The charcoal-salt backfill further reduces local resistivity and retains moisture. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **State Dwight's formula for the earth resistance of a vertical rod electrode and explain the significance of each term.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > **Dwight's Formula:** > > $$R = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[ \ln\left(\frac{4L}{d}\right) - 1 \right]$$ > > Where: > - **R** = Earth resistance of the rod ($\Omega$) — *1 mark* > - **$\rho$** = Soil resistivity ($\Omega \cdot m$) — *0.5 mark* > - **L** = Length of the rod (m) — *0.5 mark* > - **d** = Diameter of the rod (m) — *0.5 mark* > - The factor **$\frac{1}{2\pi L}$** represents the cylindrical geometry of current divergence. > - The term $\left[\ln\left(\frac{4L}{d}\right) - 1\right]$ is the **geometric correction factor** (empirically adjusted by Dwight). — *0.5 mark* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **List any four methods of improving the earth resistance of a rod electrode.** **Model Answer (3 Marks — 0.75 each):** 1. **Increasing the length of the rod** — drives it deeper into moist sub-soil. 2. **Using multiple rods in parallel** — adds parallel conduction paths. 3. **Soil treatment with salt (NaCl) and charcoal** — reduces soil resistivity locally. 4. **Increasing the diameter of the rod** — provides more contact area (marginal effect). 5. *(Bonus, if asked)*: Regular watering of the pit, using chemical compounds like bentonite. --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Pattern --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – July 2024]` **(a)** Derive the expression for the earth resistance of a vertical rod electrode using the method of cylindrical shells. State clearly all assumptions. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A copper rod of **2 m length** and **20 mm diameter** is used for earthing in soil of resistivity **80 $\Omega \cdot m$**. Calculate: - (i) The resistance of the single rod. - (ii) The resistance when **three** such identical rods are connected in parallel (assume spacing ≥ 4 m, mutual resistance negligible). **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation (7 Marks):** Refer to the exhaustive derivation in **Section 3.1** above. Key steps to be written in the answer sheet: 1. Diagram of a vertical rod with cylindrical soil shells — **1 Mark** 2. Expression for elemental resistance: $dR = \dfrac{\rho \, dx}{2\pi x L}$ — **1 Mark** 3. Setting up the integral: $R = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L} \int_{d/2}^{\infty} \dfrac{dx}{x}$ — **1 Mark** 4. Evaluating the integral with empirical upper limit (Dwight's correction) — **2 Marks** 5. Final boxed formula: $R = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[\ln\left(\dfrac{4L}{d}\right) - 1\right]$ — **1 Mark** 6. Assumptions: (i) Homogeneous soil, (ii) Current diverges radially, (iii) Rod length >> diameter — **1 Mark** --- **(b) Numerical (7 Marks):** **Given:** - $L = 2 \text{ m}$, $d = 20 \text{ mm} = 0.02 \text{ m}$, $\rho = 80 \, \Omega \cdot m$, $n = 3$ **Part (i) — Single Rod Resistance (3.5 Marks):** $$R = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[ \ln\left(\frac{4L}{d}\right) - 1 \right]$$ $$R = \frac{80}{2\pi \times 2} \left[ \ln\left(\frac{4 \times 2}{0.02}\right) - 1 \right]$$ $$R = \frac{80}{12.566} \left[ \ln(400) - 1 \right]$$ $$R = 6.366 \times [5.9915 - 1]$$ $$R = 6.366 \times 4.9915$$ $$\boxed{R = 31.78 \, \Omega} \quad \text{— 3.5 Marks}$$ **Part (ii) — Three Rods in Parallel (3.5 Marks):** Spacing check: 4 m ≥ 2L = 4 m ✓ (mutual resistance neglected) $$R_{eq} = \frac{R}{n} = \frac{31.78}{3}$$ $$\boxed{R_{eq} = 10.59 \, \Omega} \quad \text{— 3.5 Marks}$$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam – July 2024]` **(a)** Explain in detail **six different methods** of improving earth resistance, with sketches wherever possible. Discuss which method is the most cost-effective. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A substation requires an earth resistance of **less than 1 $\Omega$**. The soil resistivity is **300 $\Omega \cdot m$**. Design a suitable rod earthing system using: - Copper rods of length **3 m** and diameter **25 mm**. - Determine the **number of rods** required (in parallel) and the **minimum spacing** between them. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Methods to Improve Earth Resistance (7 Marks — ≈ 1 Mark each for first 5 + 2 Marks for cost analysis):** 1. **Increase Rod Length:** Doubling L reduces R by ~50%. Deep-boring into the moist stratum is the single most effective method. 2. **Increase Rod Diameter:** Effect is logarithmic, so doubling d reduces R by only ~10%. Not economical. 3. **Use Multiple Rods in Parallel:** n identical rods in parallel give R/n. Spacing must be ≥ 2L to avoid mutual interference. 4. **Soil Treatment (Salt + Charcoal):** A 50 mm layer of alternate salt (NaCl) and charcoal around the rod reduces local ρ by up to 5–10×. 5. **Use Earth Plates / Pipe Electrodes:** Substitute rod with a plate (typically 60 cm × 60 cm) for areas with very high resistivity. 6. **Chemical Compounds:** Use bentonite, marconite, or conductive cement as backfill for long-term low resistance. 7. **Most Cost-Effective Method:** A combination of **soil treatment (salt + charcoal)** with **moderate increase in length** offers the best cost-to-performance ratio for domestic and small commercial installations. --- **(b) Substation Earth Resistance Design (7 Marks):** **Given:** - Required: $R_{eq} \leq 1 \, \Omega$ - Soil: $\rho = 300 \, \Omega \cdot m$ - Rod: $L = 3 \text{ m}$, $d = 0.025 \text{ m}$ **Step 1 — Resistance of a Single Rod (3 Marks):** $$R = \frac{300}{2\pi \times 3} \left[ \ln\left(\frac{4 \times 3}{0.025}\right) - 1 \right]$$ $$R = \frac{300}{18.849} \left[ \ln(480) - 1 \right]$$ $$R = 15.915 \times [6.1738 - 1]$$ $$R = 15.915 \times 5.1738$$ $$\boxed{R = 82.34 \, \Omega} \quad \text{— 3 Marks}$$ **Step 2 — Number of Rods in Parallel Required (2 Marks):** $$n = \frac{R}{R_{required}} = \frac{82.34}{1} = 82.34$$ Rounding up for safety margin: $$\boxed{n = 83 \text{ rods}} \quad \text{— 2 Marks}$$ **Step 3 — Minimum Spacing Between Rods (2 Marks):** $$S_{min} = 2L = 2 \times 3 = 6 \text{ m}$$ **Engineering Note:** In practice, rods are arranged in a **grid pattern** with 6 m spacing. The grid is interconnected using a **40 mm × 6 mm GI/Cu strip** to ensure equipotential bonding. The 83 rods in parallel yield $R_{eq} \approx 0.99 \, \Omega$ ✓. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:** > > - **Forgetting unit conversion of diameter (mm → m).** A 25 mm rod is **0.025 m**, not 25. This single error propagates through the entire log term and **loses ~3 marks**. > - **Confusing Simon's Approximate Formula $\left(\frac{\rho}{2\pi L}\ln\frac{4L}{d}\right)$ with Dwight's Exact Formula $\left(\frac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\frac{4L}{d}-1\right]\right)$.** Board answers MUST clearly state which formula is used. > - **Ignoring spacing constraint.** Writing "$R_{eq} = R/n$" without checking the 2L spacing rule is considered an **incomplete answer** and loses 1–2 marks. > - **Wrong sign in Dwight's formula.** Writing "+1" instead of "−1" inside the bracket is a **fatal sign error** — verify before submitting. > - **Not stating assumptions** in derivation questions — KTU board examiners explicitly allocate **1 mark for "Assumptions"**. --- ### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ⭐ **Dwight's Formula is the BIBLE formula** for this topic: $R = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\left(\dfrac{4L}{d}\right) - 1\right]$. - ⭐ **Length is the most powerful parameter** — not diameter. Always prioritize **increasing L** over **increasing d**. - ⭐ For **n identical rods in parallel**: $R_{eq} = \dfrac{R}{n}$ — but only if spacing $\geq 2L$. - ⭐ **Standard KTU dimensions:** Copper rod → 12.5–25 mm dia, 2–3 m length. GI rod → 25–50 mm dia, 2.5 m length. - ⭐ **Soil treatment recipe:** Alternate layers of **charcoal (50 mm)** and **salt (50 mm)** around the rod, topped with water → reduces ρ by 5–10×. - ⭐ **Recommended earth resistance:** Domestic = **< 5 $\Omega$**, Substation = **< 1 $\Omega$**, Telecom tower = **< 5 $\Omega$**. - ⭐ **Assumption list to memorize:** Homogeneous soil, radial current flow, $L \gg d$, point source of current, isothermal conditions. - ⭐ **Code reference (no need to memorize page numbers, but know the name):** IS 3043:1987 (Code of Practice for Earthing) and IEEE Std 80 (Substation Grounding). - ⭐ **Don't confuse:** Rod Earthing (vertical) vs. Plate Earthing (horizontal) — different formulas, different applications. - ⭐ **In a board numerical:** Always show **unit conversion**, **substitution**, and **final boxed answer** — each step earns independent marks.

topics.title: Size of Earth Continuity Conductor, Substation Earthing and Design (Theory Only), Substation Lightning Protection (Theory Only), Solar PV Power Generation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT752 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Size of Earth Continuity Conductor, Substation Earthing and Design (Theory Only), Substation Lightning Protection (Theory Only), Solar PV Power Generation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Earth Continuity Conductor (ECC)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):**
> An **Earth Continuity Conductor (ECC)** is the protective conductor that connects the metallic body of an electrical appliance or equipment to the earth electrode, ensuring that any leakage current or fault current safely flows to the ground. It forms the low-impedance return path between the equipment frame and the main earthing terminal.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine a skyscraper fire escape slide. The slide (ECC) is rarely used, but in case of an emergency (electrical fault), it provides a safe path for people (current) to escape to the ground. If a live wire accidentally touches the metal body of your washing machine, the ECC whisks that dangerous current harmlessly into the ground instead of through your body when you touch the machine. Without it, every appliance would be a potential death trap.

### Sizing Basis of Earth Continuity Conductor (As per IS 732 & IE Rules)

The cross-sectional area of the ECC is **never** designed for full load current. It carries only **fault current** for a very short duration (typically **0.2 to 5 seconds**, depending on protective device clearing time). The size is therefore governed by **thermal withstand capacity** (adiabatic equation) and **mechanical strength**.

#### Empirical Sizing Rules (KTU High-Yield):

| Phase Conductor Material | Phase Conductor Size (S) | ECC Size (Minimum) | Rationale |
|--------------------------|--------------------------|--------------------|-----------|
| Copper | Any | **Half of S** (min 1.5 mm²) | High conductivity, lower resistance |
| Aluminium | Any | **Same as S** | Lower conductivity compensates by larger area |
| If ECC is buried in earth | — | Min **2.5 mm² Cu / 4 mm² Al** | Corrosion protection factor |
| If ECC is part of flexible cord | — | Min **0.75 mm² Cu** | Mechanical flexibility limit |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Pearl:** When the size of the phase conductor exceeds **25 mm²**, the size of the protective (earth) conductor need not exceed **25 mm²** if it is copper, and **35 mm²** if it is aluminium. This is the "25 mm² saturation rule" frequently asked in exams.

### Adiabatic Equation for Precise Calculation

$$S = \frac{I}{\sqrt{\left(\frac{k^2 \cdot A^2 \cdot 10^4}{t}\right)}} = \frac{I \sqrt{t}}{k}$$

Where:
- $S$ = Cross-sectional area of ECC in **mm²**
- $I$ = RMS fault current in **Amperes**
- $t$ = Disconnecting time of protective device in **seconds**
- $k$ = Material constant (**143** for copper with PVC, **94** for copper with XLPE, **76** for aluminium)

---

## 1.2 Substation Earthing — The Foundation of Safety

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> **Substation Earthing** is the intentional connection of all non-current carrying metallic parts of substation equipment (transformer tanks, CB enclosures, support structures, operating handles, fencing) and the neutral point of the system to a low-resistance earth electrode through a robust conductor system. Its purpose is to limit touch/step potentials, provide stable reference potential, and ensure fast operation of protective relays during earth faults.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of a substation as a giant bathtub full of dangerous electrical water. The earth electrode is the floor drain — it must be wide and clean enough to drain the water (fault current) rapidly without overflowing onto humans (step/touch potentials). A poorly designed earthing system is like a clogged drain in a flooding bathroom; everything outside it becomes dangerously live.

---

## 1.3 Substation Lightning Protection

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> **Lightning Protection System (LPS)** is an integrated network of air terminations, down conductors, and earth terminations designed to safely intercept direct lightning strikes, conduct the enormous current (~30 kA typical, up to 200 kA) to the earth, and dissipate it without causing flashover, fire, injury, or equipment damage. The components include **lightning finials (rods)**, **shield wires**, **surge arresters**, and **down conductors**.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
A lightning protection system is a designated "lightning parking lot." By placing tall pointed rods (Franklin rods) at strategic locations, we make them the *preferred* target for the lightning bolt. Once hit, the current is funneled through a thick copper highway (down conductor) and dumped into a massive underground plate (earth pit). The building itself is the "no-parking zone" — too well-protected to be hit.

---

## 1.4 Solar PV Power Generation

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> **Solar PV Power Generation** is the direct conversion of sunlight (photons) into electricity via the **photovoltaic effect** discovered by Becquerel (1839) and commercially realized using semiconductor junctions (typically monocrystalline/polycrystalline silicon). A PV system comprises PV modules (DC source), mounting structures, inverters (DC→AC), charge controllers, batteries (for off-grid), protection devices, and grid interconnection (for grid-tied).

**Conceptual Analogy:**
A solar PV cell works like a pinball machine made of sand (silicon). Sunlight photons are the balls; the cell's electric field is the bumper that propels them; the external wires are the gutters that collect the balls and roll them down to do useful work. No fuel, no moving parts, no noise — just light converted directly to electrons in motion.

---

|----------|----------------|--------------|
| Earth resistivity ($\rho$) — typical clay | **100 Ω·m** | Substation design |
| Earth resistivity — wet sand | **200–500 Ω·m** | Electrode design |
| Lightning stroke current (typical) | **30 kA** | Arrester rating |
| Lightning stroke current (max) | **200 kA** | Direct stroke shielding |
| Solar insolation (peak sun hours, Kerala) | **4.5–5.5 kWh/m²/day** | PV sizing |
| Silicon band gap | **1.12 eV** | Cell physics |
| Standard Test Conditions (STC) | **1000 W/m², 25°C, AM 1.5** | PV module rating |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 Detailed Sizing of Earth Continuity Conductor

### 2.1.1 Governing Principles

The ECC sizing follows a dual-criterion approach:

**Criterion 1 — Thermal Capacity (Adiabatic Heating):**
During a fault, all heat generated ($I^2Rt$) is assumed trapped in the conductor (no dissipation to surroundings). The temperature rise must not damage the insulation.

**Criterion 2 — Mechanical Strength:**
The conductor must not break under its own weight, short-circuit magnetic forces, or corrosion over the design life (typically 25–40 years).

### 2.1.2 Derivation of the Adiabatic Equation

Starting from heat balance:

$$I^2 R t = m S_{h} \Delta\theta$$

With $R = \rho L / S$ and $m = \delta L S$, this simplifies to:

$$I^2 \frac{\rho L}{S} t = \delta L S \cdot c \cdot \Delta\theta$$

$$I^2 t = \frac{S^2 \delta c \Delta\theta}{\rho} = k^2 S^2$$

$$\boxed{S = \frac{I \sqrt{t}}{k}}$$

### 2.1.3 Standardized k-Values (IS 732 / IEC 60364-5-54)

| Conductor Material | Insulation Type | **k** value |
|--------------------|-----------------|-------------|
| Copper | PVC | **143** |
| Copper | XLPE / EPR | **94** (143/√2.3) |
| Copper | Bare | **159** |
| Aluminium | PVC | **76** |
| Aluminium | XLPE | **76** |
| Steel | — | **50** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** For the same fault current, aluminium conductors need roughly **2× the cross-section** of copper because of the inverse relationship in the adiabatic equation. This is why copper is preferred for ECC despite cost.

---

## 2.2 Substation Earthing — Theoretical Foundation

### 2.2.1 Functions of Substation Earthing

1. **Safety of personnel** — limits touch & step potentials to safe values (≤ **50 V** for human body under fault).
2. **Stable reference potential** — defines 0 V reference for the system neutral.
3. **Fault current return path** — enables reliable operation of earth-fault relays.
4. **Overvoltage protection** — dissipates switching surges and lightning impulse energy.
5. **Electromagnetic compatibility** — provides low-impedance reference for shielding and communication.

### 2.2.2 Earth Electrode Resistance Formulas

#### A) Pipe Electrode (Most Common in Substations)

$$R = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[ \ln\left(\frac{4L}{d}\right) - 1 \right]$$

Where:
- $R$ = Earth resistance in **Ω**
- $\rho$ = Soil resistivity in **Ω·m**
- $L$ = Length of pipe (typically **2.5–3 m**)
- $d$ = Diameter of pipe (typically **40–100 mm**)

#### B) Plate Electrode (Vertical Burial)

$$R = \frac{\rho}{2A}\left[1 + \frac{a}{\sqrt{A}}\right] \quad \text{or simplified} \quad R = \frac{0.08 \rho}{a}$$

Where $a$ = side of square plate (m), or radius for circular plate.

#### C) Rod Electrode (Driven Vertical)

$$R = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[ \ln\left(\frac{8L}{d}\right) - 1 \right]$$

#### D) Horizontal Strip / Mat (Common in HV Substations)

$$R = \frac{\rho}{\pi L} \left[ \ln\left(\frac{2L}{\sqrt{w \cdot t}}\right) \right]$$

Where $w$ = width, $t$ = depth of burial.

### 2.2.3 Multiple Electrodes in Parallel

When $n$ identical electrodes are connected in parallel, the combined resistance is **never** simply $R/n$ due to mutual interference (overlapping resistance areas).

$$R_{total} = \frac{R_{single}}{n} \cdot \eta$$

Where $\eta$ = **utilization factor** (0.55 to 0.85), depends on spacing/dimensions.

| Electrode Spacing | $\eta$ (approx.) |
|-------------------|------------------|
| Equal to length | **0.6** |
| 2 × length | **0.75** |
| 3 × length | **0.85** |

---

## 2.3 Substation Lightning Protection — Deep Theory

### 2.3.1 Mechanism of Lightning

A lightning stroke is the discharge of a **stepped leader** (downward) meeting an **upward streamer** from ground objects. When they connect, the main return stroke propagates upward carrying currents of **2–200 kA** at temperatures of **~30,000 K**.

### 2.3.2 Components of Lightning Protection System

1. **Air Termination** — Franklin rod, mesh conductor, or overhead shield wire.
2. **Down Conductor** — Heavy copper/aluminium strip carrying current from air termination to earth.
3. **Earth Termination** — Earth electrodes, ground rods, or earth mats.
4. **Surge Arrester** — Voltage-clamping device at equipment terminals.
5. **Equipotential Bonding** — Connecting all metallic parts to a common earth.

### 2.3.3 Methods of Determining Zone of Protection

#### A) Cone of Protection (Empirical, IS 2309)

For a single vertical rod of height $h$, the protected zone forms a cone of **45° apex angle** (some codes use 60°). Height of protected object $H$ at distance $D$ from rod base:

$$H = h - D \quad \text{(for 45° cone)}$$

#### B) Rolling Sphere Method (Modern, IS/IEC 62305)

A sphere of radius $r$ (function of class of LPS) is rolled over the structure. Spaces the sphere can touch are vulnerable; spaces it cannot touch are protected.

| LPS Class | Sphere Radius $r$ (m) | Min. Peak Current (kA) |
|-----------|----------------------|------------------------|
| I (highest) | **20** | **3** (99% protection) |
| II | **30** | **5** (97%) |
| III | **45** | **10** (91%) |
| IV (lowest) | **60** | **16** (84%) |

### 2.3.4 Types of Surge Arresters

| Type | Working Principle | Application |
|------|-------------------|-------------|
| **Rod Gap** | Air breakdown at fixed distance | HV transmission lines, basic protection |
| **Horn Gap** | Arc rises and stretches to extinguish | Outdoor HV switchyards |
| **Expulsion Gap** | Arc heat de-ionizes gas & ejects | Older systems, 11 kV class |
| **Valve Type (SiC)** | Non-linear resistance blocks | Up to 400 kV historically |
| **Metal Oxide (ZnO, Gapless)** | Highly non-linear V-I characteristic | Modern EHV, 1100 kV |

**ZnO V-I characteristic:**

$$I = k V^{\alpha} \quad \text{where } \alpha \approx 25\text{–}40$$

A high $\alpha$ means the arrester conducts negligibly at normal voltage but heavily at overvoltage.

---

## 2.4 Solar PV Power Generation — Detailed Theory

### 2.4.1 Photovoltaic Effect & Cell Construction

A silicon PV cell is a **PN junction diode** with:
- **Emitter (N+)** — thin, heavily doped, exposed to light.
- **Base (P)** — thicker, lightly doped.
- **Anti-Reflection Coating (ARC)** — reduces reflection losses.
- **Front & Back Contacts** — collect current.

When photon energy $h\nu \geq E_g$ (band gap = 1.12 eV for Si) strikes the junction, electron-hole pairs are generated and separated by the built-in electric field, producing DC current.

### 2.4.2 Cell → Module → Array Hierarchy

$$\text{1 Cell} \approx 0.5\text{–}0.6 \text{ V, 3–8 A} \rightarrow \text{36 cells in series} = \sim 18 \text{ V module}$$

$$\text{Modules in series} \rightarrow \text{String} \quad ; \quad \text{Strings in parallel} \rightarrow \text{Array}$$

### 2.4.3 Equivalent Circuit & V-I Equation

$$I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[ e^{\frac{q(V + IR_s)}{n k T}} - 1 \right] - \frac{V + IR_s}{R_{sh}}$$

Where $I_{ph}$ is light-generated current, $I_0$ is dark saturation current, $R_s$ is series resistance (low), $R_{sh}$ is shunt resistance (high).

### 2.4.4 Maximum Power Point (MPP) & MPPT Algorithms

The MPP is at the "knee" of the I-V curve. Common MPPT methods:

| Algorithm | Principle | Speed / Accuracy |
|-----------|-----------|-------------------|
| **P&O (Perturb & Observe)** | Increment V, observe $\Delta P$ | Slow but simple |
| **Incremental Conductance** | $\frac{dI}{dV} = -\frac{I}{V}$ at MPP | Faster, more accurate |
| **Fuzzy Logic / Neural** | AI-based tracking | Highest performance |

### 2.4.5 Solar PV System Topologies

**Stand-Alone (Off-Grid):**
PV Array → Charge Controller → Battery Bank → Inverter → AC Load

**Grid-Tied (On-Grid):**
PV Array → DC-DC Boost → Grid-Tie Inverter → AC Bus → Grid + Load

**Hybrid:**
PV + Battery + Diesel/Wind backup, with bidirectional grid interface

### 2.4.6 Key Performance Parameters

$$\text{Capacity Utilization Factor (CUF)} = \frac{\text{Actual Energy Output (kWh)}}{\text{Installed Capacity (kWp)} \times 8760 \text{ h}}$$

$$\text{Performance Ratio (PR)} = \frac{\text{Actual kWh}}{\text{Peak kWh} \times \text{Irradiance ratio}}$$

$$\text{System Efficiency} = \eta_{module} \times \eta_{inverter} \times \eta_{wiring} \times \eta_{temp} \times \eta_{soiling}$$

Typical values: $\eta_{module}$ 15–22%, $\eta_{inverter}$ 95–98%, PR 0.7–0.85.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Module 4)

| Formula | Expression | Use / Domain |
|---------|------------|--------------|
| ECC area (adiabatic) | $S = I\sqrt{t}/k$ | Sizing earth conductor |
| Earth resistance of pipe | $R = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln(4L/d) - 1\right]$ | Substation earth pit |
| Earth resistance of plate | $R = 0.08\rho/a$ | Square plate electrode |
| Earth resistance of rod | $R = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln(8L/d) - 1\right]$ | Driven earth rod |
| Multiple electrodes | $R_n = R_1 / (n \cdot \eta)$ | Parallel electrode banks |
| Touch potential limit | $E_t = (R_b + R_{foot}/2) \times I_{body}$ | Safety assessment |
| Step potential limit | $E_s = (R_b + 2R_{foot}) \times I_{body}$ | Safety assessment |
| Cone of protection (45°) | $H = h - D$ | LPS layout |
| Rolling sphere | $r$ based on LPS class | Modern LPS design |
| PV module V | $V_{mp} = 36 \times 0.5 = 18$ V (Si) | 12 V system |
| PV cell efficiency | $\eta = (P_{out} / P_{in}) \times 100\%$ | Module performance |
| CUF | $E_{out} / (P_{inst} \times 8760)$ | Plant performance |
| PV sizing (energy) | $E_{req} = P_{load} \times h_{use}$ | Energy balance |

**Safety Limits (IEEE 80):**
- Body resistance $R_b$ = **1000 Ω** (assumed)
- Foot resistance $R_{foot}$ = **3 $\rho_s$** (where $\rho_s$ = surface soil resistivity, $\Omega$·m)
- Safe touch/step potential = **few hundred volts** at substation, but design target is **less than tolerable limits** based on body weight & fault duration.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Adiabatic Equation for ECC Sizing

### Statement
Derive the expression for the minimum cross-sectional area of an earth continuity conductor subjected to fault current.

### Step 1: Energy balance (no heat loss)
During the short fault duration, all electrical energy converts to heat within the conductor.

$$W_{electrical} = W_{thermal}$$

### Step 2: Express electrical energy
Electrical energy dissipated:

$$W_{electrical} = I^2 R t$$

For a conductor of length $L$ and cross-section $S$:

$$R = \frac{\rho L}{S}$$

Therefore:

$$W_{electrical} = I^2 \cdot \frac{\rho L}{S} \cdot t$$

### Step 3: Express thermal energy (mass × specific heat × temperature rise)
Mass of conductor:

$$m = \delta L S$$

where $\delta$ is density (kg/m³). Then:

$$W_{thermal} = m \cdot c \cdot \Delta\theta = \delta L S \cdot c \cdot \Delta\theta$$

### Step 4: Equate and simplify
Setting $W_{electrical} = W_{thermal}$:

$$I^2 \cdot \frac{\rho L}{S} \cdot t = \delta L S \cdot c \cdot \Delta\theta$$

Cancel $L$ from both sides:

$$I^2 \cdot \frac{\rho}{S} \cdot t = \delta S \cdot c \cdot \Delta\theta$$

$$I^2 t = \frac{\delta c \Delta\theta}{\rho} \cdot S^2$$

### Step 5: Define material constant
Define the constant:

$$k^2 = \frac{\delta c \Delta\theta}{\rho}$$

Then:

$$I^2 t = k^2 S^2$$

$$\boxed{S = \frac{I \sqrt{t}}{k}}$$

This is the **adiabatic equation** used in IS 732 and IEC 60364-5-54.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Resistance of a Pipe Electrode

### Setup
A vertical pipe electrode of length $L$ and diameter $d$ is buried such that its top is flush with the ground. Soil of uniform resistivity $\rho$ surrounds it. Find the resistance between the pipe and remote earth.

### Step 1: Consider as hemispherical equipotentials
For a vertical rod, current flows radially into the soil. The resistance can be approximated as the resistance of coaxial cylindrical shells of soil from radius $x$ to $x + dx$:

$$dR = \frac{\rho \, dx}{2\pi x \cdot L_{eff}}$$

Where $L_{eff}$ is the effective length.

### Step 2: Integrate for the resistance
Integrating from the surface of the pipe to remote earth:

$$R = \int_{d/2}^{\infty} \frac{\rho \, dx}{2\pi x L}$$

### Step 3: Approximation for practical electrodes
For finite length pipe, a standard empirical formula (Dwight's equation) is:

$$R = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[ \ln\left(\frac{4L}{d}\right) - 1 \right]$$

### Numerical Example — Worked Problem
**Given:** Pipe length $L = 2.5$ m, diameter $d = 50$ mm = 0.05 m, soil resistivity $\rho = 100\ \Omega\cdot$m.
**Find:** Earth resistance.

**Solution:**

$$R = \frac{100}{2\pi \times 2.5} \left[\ln\left(\frac{4 \times 2.5}{0.05}\right) - 1\right]$$

Compute the term in brackets:
- $\frac{4 \times 2.5}{0.05} = \frac{10}{0.05} = 200$
- $\ln(200) = 5.2983$
- $5.2983 - 1 = 4.2983$

Front factor:
- $\frac{100}{2\pi \times 2.5} = \frac{100}{15.708} = 6.366$

Therefore:

$$R = 6.366 \times 4.2983 = 27.36\ \Omega$$

This is the resistance of **one pipe** alone. To achieve the target earth resistance (typically < **1 Ω** for HV substations), we need multiple pipes in parallel.

### Combined Resistance with Multiple Pipes
Suppose we need 8 pipes. Spacing = 5 m (> 2L). So $\eta \approx 0.85$.

$$R_{8} = \frac{27.36}{8 \times 0.85} = \frac{27.36}{6.8} = 4.02\ \Omega$$

Still high. Increase to 16 pipes:

$$R_{16} = \frac{27.36}{16 \times 0.85} = \frac{27.36}{13.6} = 2.01\ \Omega$$

For 32 pipes (4 rows × 8):

$$R_{32} = \frac{27.36}{32 \times 0.85} = 1.005\ \Omega \approx 1\ \Omega$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Lesson:** Earth resistance halves only when number of electrodes is doubled — not when added linearly. This is the key insight for KTU substation design problems.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Touch & Step Potential Formulas

### Touch Potential ($E_t$)
Voltage between an energized object and the foot of a person touching it:

$$E_t = I_{body} \cdot (R_b + 1.5\ \rho_s)$$

where:
- $I_{body}$ = body current (assumed **1000 mA** fatal threshold)
- $R_b$ = body resistance (assume **1000 Ω** for dry conditions)
- $1.5\ \rho_s$ = approximate foot resistance (foot is a disc, two feet in parallel ≈ $1.5\rho_s$)

### Step Potential ($E_s$)
Voltage between the feet of a person standing near a faulted structure:

$$E_s = I_{body} \cdot (R_b + 6\ \rho_s)$$

**Tolerable values** (IEEE 80) for **50 kg** body weight and 1 s fault duration:

$$E_{touch,tol} = (1000 + 1.5\ \rho_s C_s) \cdot \frac{0.116}{\sqrt{t}}$$

$$E_{step,tol} = (1000 + 6\ \rho_s C_s) \cdot \frac{0.116}{\sqrt{t}}$$

where $C_s$ = surface layer derating factor (1 for crushed stone ~ 3000 Ω·m).

### Numerical Example
- $\rho_s = 3000\ \Omega\cdot$m (crushed stone)
- $t = 0.5$ s

$$E_{touch,tol} = (1000 + 1.5 \times 3000) \cdot \frac{0.116}{\sqrt{0.5}}$$

$$= (1000 + 4500) \cdot \frac{0.116}{0.7071}$$

$$= 5500 \times 0.1640 = 902\ \text{V}$$

> **Interpretation:** Touch potential must be less than 902 V to be safe under these conditions.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Solar PV Array Sizing

### Problem
A house in Kerala needs **3 kW** of connected load, used **6 hours/day**. Autonomy requirement: 2 days. Available 12 V battery system. Design battery and PV array.

### Step 1: Daily energy requirement
$$E_{daily} = P_{load} \times h_{use} = 3000 \times 6 = 18{,}000\ \text{Wh} = 18\ \text{kWh/day}$$

### Step 2: Battery capacity
With **50% Depth of Discharge (DOD)** for lead-acid:

$$C_{Ah} = \frac{E_{daily} \times \text{Autonomy}}{V_{sys} \times \text{DOD}} = \frac{18{,}000 \times 2}{12 \times 0.5} = \frac{36{,}000}{6} = 6000\ \text{Ah}$$

In kWh: $12 \times 6000 = 72$ kWh battery bank.

### Step 3: PV array sizing
- Peak sun hours in Kerala: **5 h/day**
- System derating factor: 0.77 (combines inverter, wiring, temperature, soiling losses)

$$P_{PV} = \frac{E_{daily}}{\text{PSH} \times \text{derating}} = \frac{18{,}000}{5 \times 0.77} = \frac{18{,}000}{3.85} = 4675\ \text{W}_p$$

Round up to next available size: **5 kWp**.

### Step 4: Number of modules
Assume 330 Wp mono-crystalline module.

$$N_{modules} = \frac{5000}{330} = 15.15 \rightarrow 16\ \text{modules}$$

Arrangement: 2 strings × 8 modules in series (each string = 8 × 41 Vmp ≈ 328 V, suitable for MPPT inverter).

### Step 5: Inverter sizing
For grid-tied systems: inverter = 0.8 × PV array kWp = **4 kW**.
For off-grid: inverter = 1.25 × continuous load = **3.75 kW** (peak handling).

---

|-----------|------|-------|-----|-----------|
| **Best use** | Substations, transformer neutrals | Power stations, HV yards | Distribution substations, small installations | Switchyards, large EHV |
| **Typical size** | 2.5 m × 50 mm dia | 600×600×6.3 mm (Cu) or 600×600×10 mm (GI) | 1.2 m × 20 mm dia | 25×4 mm GI strip, 50 m long |
| **Cost** | Low | Medium | Lowest | Medium |
| **Mechanical strength** | Excellent | Excellent | Limited | Excellent |
| **Ease of installation** | Easy | Difficult (excavation) | Easiest (driven) | Moderate |
| **Maintenance** | Watering needed | Watering needed | Minimal | Minimal |
| **Resistance achievable (single)** | 5–50 Ω | 5–30 Ω | 10–100 Ω | 1–5 Ω (mat) |

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Table of Surge Arresters

| Property | Rod Gap | Horn Gap | Expulsion | SiC Valve | ZnO (Gapless) |
|----------|---------|----------|-----------|-----------|---------------|
| Voltage limiting | Poor | Moderate | Good | Good | Excellent |
| Follow current | High | High | High | Moderate | **Negligible** |
| Surge handling | Low | Moderate | Good | Good | **Excellent** |
| Aging | — | — | — | Yes | Very low |
| Used in EHV | No | No | No | Rarely | **Yes (dominant)** |
