## 3.7 Comprehensive Worked Example: Design an 11 kV/433 V Distribution Substation Earthing

**Specifications:**
- Fault level: **250 MVA**
- Earth resistance required: **≤ 1 Ω**
- Soil resistivity: **200 Ω·m**
- Available GI pipe: **50 mm OD, 2.5 m long**

**Step 1: Pipe resistance (single)**

$$R = \frac{200}{2\pi \times 2.5}\left[\ln\left(\frac{4 \times 2.5}{0.05}\right) - 1\right]$$

$$= \frac{200}{15.708}[5.298 - 1] = 12.732 \times 4.298 = 54.72\ \Omega$$

**Step 2: Number of pipes needed**
Assume spacing = 5 m (≥ 2L), so $\eta = 0.75$ (more conservative for moderate spacing).

$$R_n = \frac{54.72}{n \times 0.75} \leq 1\ \Omega$$

$$n \geq \frac{54.72}{0.75} = 72.96 \rightarrow 73\ \text{pipes}$$

**Step 3: Practical arrangement**
To reduce count, we add **moisture & salt** treatment (reduces $\rho$ by ~30%) and **charcoal/coke** bedding (also reduces $\rho$).

If $\rho_{eff} = 0.7 \times 200 = 140\ \Omega\cdot$m:

$$R_{single} = \frac{140}{15.708} \times 4.298 = 38.3\ \Omega$$

$$n \geq \frac{38.3}{0.75} = 51.1 \rightarrow 52\ \text{pipes}$$

**Step 4: Final layout**
- 52 pipes arranged in **4 rows of 13**
- Interconnected by **50×6 mm GI strip** at 0.5 m depth
- All connections **welded** (cadweld preferred)
- Treated with **charcoal + salt** mixture
- Periodic watering provided

**Step 5: Verification**
$$R_{total} = \frac{38.3}{52 \times 0.75} = \frac{38.3}{39} = 0.98\ \Omega \leq 1\ \Omega \quad \checkmark$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Pattern:** Such substation earthing design problems with multi-step calculation are common in **14-mark Part B questions**. Practice the systematic layout (single resistance → required count → derating factor → verification).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

---

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Earth Continuity Conductor Sizing Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given Phase Conductor Size and Fault Current] --> B[Determine Material Type: Cu or Al]
    B --> C{Phase Area > 25 mm²?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Apply Saturation Rule: ECC = 25 mm² Cu or 35 mm² Al]
    C -- No --> E[Apply Ratio Rule: ECC = Half of Phase Size]
    D --> F[Check Thermal Capacity: S = I√t / k]
    E --> F
    F --> G{Area Adiabatic ≥ Area Ratio?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Select ECC Area = Adiabatic Result]
    G -- No --> I[Select ECC Area = Ratio Result]
    H --> J[Verify Mechanical Strength: Min 1.5 mm² Cu]
    I --> J
    J --> K[End: Final ECC Cross-Section]
```

**Description:** This decision tree implements the dual-criterion selection: empirical ratio rule first, then check against thermal (adiabatic) limit. The final selection is the **larger** of the two to ensure both criteria are met.

---

## 4.2 Architecture: Substation Earthing System

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Above_Ground
        TR[Transformer Tank] -->|Body| EB[Earth Bus Bar]
        CB[Circuit Breaker Frame] -->|Body| EB
        CT_CT[CT / PT Frame] -->|Body| EB
        SW[Switchgear Panels] -->|Body| EB
        FENCE[Substation Fence] -->|Body| EB
        PNL[Operating Handles] -->|Body| EB
        LT[Lightning Mast] --> EB
    end
    EB -->|GI Strip 50x6 mm| EP[Earth Pit Network]
    EP -->|Welded| P1[Pipe Electrode 1]
    EP -->|Welded| P2[Pipe Electrode 2]
    EP -->|Welded| P3[Pipe Electrode n]
    P1 -->|in soil| SOIL[Earth Soil with Charcoal-Salt Layer]
    P2 --> SOIL
    P3 --> SOIL
    NEUT[System Neutral of Transformer] -->|Neutral| EP
    style EB fill:#ffcc66
    style EP fill:#66ccff
    style SOIL fill:#99cc99
```

**Description:** All non-current-carrying metallic parts (transformer, breaker, CT/PT, fence, panels) are bonded to a common earth bus, which is then connected via heavy GI/Cu strip to multiple parallel pipe electrodes buried in soil treated with charcoal-salt. The neutral point of the transformer is also connected here.

---

## 4.3 Lightning Protection System Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    SKY[Lightning Cloud] -->|Stepped Leader| LR[Air Termination - Franklin Rod]
    LR -->|Intercepts| DOWN[Down Conductor - Cu or GI Strip]
    DOWN -->|Carries Stroke Current| ET[Earth Termination Network]
    ET -->|Dissipates to Soil| GROUND[Earth Mass]
    subgraph Protected_Volume
        BLDG[Substation Building / Equipment]
        GO[Mesh Conductor / Shield Wire]
    end
    LR -.->|Zone of Protection| BLDG
    GO -.->|Catches Side Strokes| BLDG
    SA[Surge Arrester at Equipment Terminal] -->|Clamps Overvoltage| BLDG
    SA -->|Connection| ET
    style LR fill:#ff9966
    style DOWN fill:#ccccff
    style ET fill:#99cc99
    style SA fill:#ff6666
```

**Description:** Lightning rod intercepts the descending leader, down conductor carries the enormous current safely to ground, while a separate surge arrester (connected to same earth) clamps any overvoltage that reaches sensitive equipment terminals.

---

## 4.4 Solar PV System Topology (Grid-Tied)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SUN[Sunlight Photons] -->|Light| PV[PV Array - Multiple Modules]
    PV -->|DC, e.g., 400 V| DCDC[DC-DC MPPT Boost Converter]
    DCDC -->|Variable DC| INV[Grid-Tie Inverter DC to AC]
    INV -->|415V 3-phase AC| AC_BUS[AC Distribution Bus]
    AC_BUS --> LOAD[Local AC Loads]
    AC_BUS --> METER[Bidirectional Net Meter]
    METER -->|Export| GRID[Utility Grid]
    METER -->|Import| GRID
    MPPT[MPPT Controller] -.->|Set Point| DCDC
    PRO[DC Protection: MCB + SPD] -.-> PV
    AC_PRO[AC Protection: MCB + SPD] -.-> INV
    SCADA[Monitoring / SCADA] -.-> INV
    style PV fill:#ffcc66
    style INV fill:#66ccff
    style GRID fill:#99cc99
```

**Description:** Photons → DC electricity in PV array → MPPT-controlled DC-DC boost → grid-tie inverter → AC bus → serves local loads with surplus exported to grid via net meter.

---

## 4.5 Solar PV Off-Grid System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SUN[Sunlight] --> PV[PV Array]
    PV -->|DC| CC[Charge Controller with MPPT]
    CC -->|Controlled DC| BAT[Battery Bank Lead Acid or Li-ion]
    BAT -->|DC| INV2[Standalone Inverter]
    INV2 -->|AC 230 V| LOAD[AC Loads Lights, Fan, TV]
    BAT -->|DC| DCL[DC Loads LED, Mobile Charging]
    DC_PROT2[Fuse + SPD] -.-> PV
    BAT_PROT[Battery Protection BMS] -.-> BAT
    style PV fill:#ffcc66
    style BAT fill:#cc99ff
    style INV2 fill:#66ccff
```

**Description:** Off-grid PV charges a battery via MPPT charge controller. Battery provides DC directly for DC loads and feeds a standalone inverter for AC loads. Includes battery management and protection.

---

## 4.6 Zone of Protection Comparison (Conceptual)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Cone_Method
        A1[Vertical Rod h m] -->|45° cone| Z1[Protected Cone of Height h on Ground]
    end
    subgraph Rolling_Sphere
        A2[Structure Outline] -->|Sphere r rolls over| Z2[Protected Volume = Envelope where Sphere Touches Only Rods]
    end
    subgraph Mesh_Method
        A3[Horizontal Mesh Conductors] -->|Faraday Cage Effect| Z3[Protected Volume Below Mesh]
    end
    style A1 fill:#ff9966
    style A2 fill:#66ccff
    style A3 fill:#99cc99
```

**Description:** Three different methods to define the protected volume of a lightning protection system: Cone (old, simple), Rolling Sphere (modern, accurate for complex structures), Mesh (for plane surfaces like roofs).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]**
**Explain the function of Earth Continuity Conductor (ECC) in an electrical installation. State the empirical rules for its sizing with respect to phase conductor.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Earth Continuity Conductor (ECC)** is the protective conductor that connects the metallic body of an electrical appliance to the main earth terminal, providing a low-impedance return path for fault currents. Its primary function is to ensure that under fault conditions, sufficient current flows to operate the protective device (fuse/MCB) quickly, thereby isolating the faulty equipment and preventing electric shock to humans.

**Empirical Sizing Rules:**
- If the **phase conductor area S ≤ 25 mm²**: ECC = **half of S** (for copper), or **equal to S** (for aluminium).
- If the **phase conductor area S > 25 mm²**: ECC can be limited to a **maximum of 25 mm² (copper) or 35 mm² (aluminium)**, this is the saturation rule.
- **Minimum mechanical size**: 1.5 mm² (Cu) for non-buried, 2.5 mm² (Cu) for buried.

(3 marks: 1 for definition/function, 2 for sizing rules)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 (Model)]**
**What is the significance of "rolling sphere method" in lightning protection design? Compare it with the cone of protection method.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Rolling Sphere Method (RSM)** is a modern technique (per IS/IEC 62305) to determine the zone of protection of a lightning air termination system. A sphere of radius $r$ (depending on LPS class) is conceptually rolled over the structure. Any point that the sphere can **touch** is vulnerable to direct strike; any point the sphere **cannot touch** (because a rod or conductor intercepts it) is protected.

**Significance:**
- Accurate for **complex, multi-level** structures (where cone method fails).
- Accounts for **side strokes** to lower parts of the structure.
- Provides scientifically defensible protection level (e.g., 99% for Class I).

**Comparison with Cone Method:**
| Aspect | Cone of Protection | Rolling Sphere |
|--------|--------------------|----------------|
| Shape | 45° cone from rod top | Spherical envelope |
| Application | Simple structures | Complex structures |
| Accuracy | Approximate, empirical | Geometrically rigorous |
| Standard | IS 2309 (older) | IS/IEC 62305 (modern) |
| Cost of design | Low | Higher (CAD needed) |

(3 marks: 1 for rolling sphere, 1 for significance, 1 for comparison)

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question is **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model)]**

**(a)** Derive the adiabatic equation for sizing an earth continuity conductor. What are the standard $k$ values used for copper and aluminium conductors with PVC insulation? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An 11 kV substation has a single line-to-ground fault current of **5000 A**. The protective relay operates in **0.5 s**. The copper ECC is insulated with PVC. Determine the minimum cross-sectional area of the ECC using the adiabatic equation. Verify whether it satisfies IS 732 rules given that the phase conductor is 150 mm² copper. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

The **adiabatic equation** assumes that during a short-circuit, all electrical energy is converted to heat within the conductor (no heat loss to surroundings). This gives the **worst-case temperature rise** and the minimum size to avoid conductor melting/insulation damage.

**Step 1: Energy balance**
$$W_{electrical} = W_{thermal}$$

**Step 2: Electrical energy**
$$W_{electrical} = I^2 R t = I^2 \cdot \frac{\rho L}{S} \cdot t$$

**Step 3: Thermal energy**
$$W_{thermal} = m \cdot c \cdot \Delta\theta = \delta L S \cdot c \cdot \Delta\theta$$

**Step 4: Equating and cancelling $L$**
$$I^2 \cdot \frac{\rho t}{S} = \delta S c \Delta\theta$$

$$I^2 t = \frac{\delta c \Delta\theta}{\rho} S^2 = k^2 S^2$$

$$\boxed{S = \frac{I \sqrt{t}}{k}}$$

**Standard k-values (IS 732 / IEC 60364-5-54):**
- Copper with PVC: $k = \mathbf{143}$
- Copper with XLPE: $k = \mathbf{94}$
- Aluminium with PVC: $k = \mathbf{76}$
- Bare copper: $k = \mathbf{159}$

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:** $I = 5000$ A, $t = 0.5$ s, copper with PVC, so $k = 143$.

**Step 1: Adiabatic calculation**

$$S = \frac{I \sqrt{t}}{k} = \frac{5000 \times \sqrt{0.5}}{143}$$

$$S = \frac{5000 \times 0.7071}{143} = \frac{3535.5}{143} = 24.72\ \text{mm}^2$$

**Step 2: IS 732 Rule (Empirical)**

Phase conductor = 150 mm². Since **150 > 25 mm²**, apply saturation rule: **ECC = 25 mm²**.

**Step 3: Comparison**

| Method | Result |
|--------|--------|
| Adiabatic | **24.72 mm²** |
| IS 732 Rule | **25 mm²** |

**Step 4: Final Selection**

Since the **empirical rule (25 mm²) > adiabatic (24.72 mm²)**, the **ECC is sized as 25 mm² copper** to satisfy both criteria.

$$\boxed{S_{ECC} = 25\ \text{mm}^2\ \text{Cu with PVC insulation}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **Cone of Protection** method and the **Rolling Sphere method** for determining the zone of protection of a lightning rod. State the standard sphere radius for LPS Class I and Class II. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **33/11 kV substation** is to be designed in a soil of resistivity **150 Ω·m**. Determine the number of **50 mm diameter, 2.5 m long GI pipe electrodes** required to achieve an earth resistance of **1 Ω or less**, assuming a spacing of **5 m** between pipes. Take utilization factor $\eta = 0.75$. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — LPS Methods (7 marks)

**Cone of Protection Method:**

A vertical lightning rod of height $h$ creates a protected conical volume beneath it. The standard 45° cone extends from the tip to the ground. The protected height at distance $D$ from the base is:

$$H = h - D$$

This is a simple empirical method suitable for small, isolated structures like buildings and small substations. Standard apex angles: **45°** (most common), 60° (more conservative for HV).

**Rolling Sphere Method (IS/IEC 62305):**

Imagine a sphere of radius $r$ rolling over the structure. Any part of the structure that the sphere **can touch** is vulnerable to a direct lightning strike. Air terminations (rods, mesh wires) are placed so the sphere **cannot touch** the protected equipment.

| LPS Class | Sphere Radius $r$ (m) | Min. Peak Current (kA) | Protection Efficiency |
|-----------|----------------------|------------------------|----------------------|
| I (highest) | **20** | 3 | 99% |
| II | **30** | 5 | 97% |
| III | 45 | 10 | 91% |
| IV | 60 | 16 | 84% |

**Application:** Used for tall, complex structures (transmission towers, wind turbines, EHV substations). The rolling sphere method is geometrically rigorous and can identify vulnerable zones on lower parts of structures that the cone method misses.

#### Part (b) — Substation Earth Pit Design (7 marks)

**Given:** $\rho = 150\ \Omega\cdot$m, $L = 2.5$ m, $d = 50$ mm = 0.05 m, $R_{target} \leq 1\ \Omega$, spacing = 5 m, $\eta = 0.75$.

**Step 1: Resistance of single pipe**

$$R = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\left(\frac{4L}{d}\right) - 1\right]$$

$$R = \frac{150}{2\pi \times 2.5}\left[\ln\left(\frac{4 \times 2.5}{0.05}\right) - 1\right]$$

$$\frac{4L}{d} = \frac{10}{0.05} = 200 \quad ; \quad \ln(200) = 5.298$$

$$R = \frac{150}{15.708} \times (5.298 - 1) = 9.549 \times 4.298 = 41.04\ \Omega$$

**Step 2: Number of pipes required**

$$R_n = \frac{R}{n \cdot \eta} \leq 1\ \Omega$$

$$n \geq \frac{R}{1 \times \eta} = \frac{41.04}{0.75} = 54.72$$

**Step 3: Final count**

$$n = 55\ \text{pipes (round up to next whole number)}$$

**Step 4: Verification**

$$R_{55} = \frac{41.04}{55 \times 0.75} = \frac{41.04}{41.25} = 0.995\ \Omega \leq 1\ \Omega \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 5: Practical Layout**

- 55 pipes arranged in **5 rows × 11 pipes** (with extra contingency)
- Spacing: **5 m × 5 m** grid
- Connected by **50×6 mm GI strip** at 0.6 m depth
- All joints **cadwelded**
- Treated with **charcoal and salt** (further reduce $\rho$ by ~20%)

$$\boxed{n = 55\ \text{pipes, giving } R_{total} \approx 0.995\ \Omega}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Adiabatic equation:** Don't forget to use **$k = 143$** for Cu-PVC (not 94 or 76). Wrong $k$ → 50% marks lost.
> 2. **Saturation rule:** Many students apply "ECC = half of S" blindly, ignoring the **25 mm²** cap when $S > 25$ mm². This costs 1–2 marks in 14-mark questions.
> 3. **Multiple electrodes:** Never write $R_n = R/n$. Always include **utilization factor $\eta$** — losing 1 mark for this is common.
> 4. **Cone of protection:** Some texts use 60° (more conservative) instead of 45°. State the angle explicitly in your answer.
> 5. **ZnO vs SiC:** Modern answers should mention **metal oxide (ZnO) gapless arresters** for EHV; SiC is largely obsolete.
> 6. **PV Sizing:** Show all intermediate steps (daily energy, battery capacity, derating factor). Marks are awarded per step.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### ⚡ Earth Continuity Conductor (ECC)
- ECC = protective conductor carrying **fault current only** (not load current).
- Empirical rule: **ECC = ½ × phase area (Cu)** for $S \leq 25$ mm², capped at **25 mm² Cu** above.
- Precise sizing: **$S = I\sqrt{t}/k$** (adiabatic equation).
- Material constants ($k$): **143** (Cu-PVC), **94** (Cu-XLPE), **76** (Al-PVC).

### ⚡ Substation Earthing
- Functions: **safety + reference + fault path + overvoltage dissipation**.
- **Pipe electrode:** $R = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln(4L/d) - 1\right]$.
- **Plate electrode:** $R = 0.08\rho/a$ (square plate).
- **Multiple electrodes:** $R_n = R/(n \cdot \eta)$, $\eta$ = 0.55–0.85.
- Target resistance: **< 1 Ω** for HV substations, **< 5 Ω** for LV.
- Use **charcoal-salt-coke** treatment to lower soil resistivity.
- **Touch potential** must be < 902 V (typical), **step potential** similarly bounded (IEEE 80).

### ⚡ Lightning Protection
- Components: **air termination + down conductor + earth termination + surge arrester**.
- **Cone method** (45° apex) — simple, old.
- **Rolling sphere** (IS/IEC 62305) — modern, accurate. Class I sphere radius = **20 m**.
- **Surge arrester types:** rod gap → horn gap → expulsion → SiC valve → **ZnO (gapless)**. ZnO is the modern EHV standard.
- Down conductor minimum: **25 × 4 mm GI or 50 mm² Cu**.
- Earth resistance for lightning protection: **< 10 Ω** (relaxed compared to power earthing).

### ⚡ Solar PV Power Generation
- **1 cell ≈ 0.5 V, 3–8 A**. **36 cells in series = 12 V module ≈ 18 Vmp**.
- **Array = strings in series × strings in parallel**.
- **V-I equation:** $I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[ e^{\frac{q(V+IR_s)}{nkT}} - 1 \right] - \frac{V+IR_s}{R_{sh}}$.
- **MPPT algorithms:** P&O (simple), Incremental Conductance (accurate).
- **Components:** PV array, MPPT charge controller, inverter, battery (off-grid), protection (MCB, SPD), net meter (grid-tied).
- **Sizing formula:** $P_{PV} = \frac{E_{daily}}{\text{PSH} \times \eta_{system}}$; for Kerala PSH = 5 h/day.
- **CUF** = actual kWh / (kWp × 8760 h) — typically 15–20%.
- **PR** (Performance Ratio) = 0.7–0.85 typical.

### 🛠️ Standards to Remember
- **IS 732** — ECC sizing
- **IS 3043** — Code of practice for earthing
- **IEEE 80** — Substation earthing design
- **IS 2309 / IEC 62305** — Lightning protection
- **IEC 60364-5-54** — Earthing arrangements

### 📝 KTU High-Yield Keywords to Use in Answers
- **Adiabatic equation**, **utilization factor**, **soil resistivity**
- **Cone of protection**, **rolling sphere**, **surge arrester**
- **Photovoltaic effect**, **MPPT**, **Performance Ratio**
- **Touch potential**, **step potential**, **cadweld**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design and Installation of Standalone and Grid Interactive Solar PV System - Smart Meter/Net Meter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT752 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design and Installation of Standalone and Grid Interactive Solar PV System - Smart Meter/Net Meter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Solar PV System — Classification

A **Solar Photovoltaic (PV) System** converts sunlight directly into electricity using semiconductor cells (typically crystalline silicon). For the KTU 2024 scheme, these systems are broadly classified by their relationship to the utility grid.

### A. Standalone (Off-Grid) Solar PV System
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024):** A *Standalone Solar PV System* is an autonomous power generation architecture that operates independently of the public utility grid, relying on **battery energy storage** to supply load during periods of low or zero solar irradiance (night, cloudy days).

**Key Characteristics:**
- Requires **Battery Bank** (Lead-acid / Lithium-ion) for energy storage.
- Needs a **Charge Controller** to prevent battery overcharge/deep discharge.
- Inverter is required if AC loads are present.
- **Critical Loads Only** — sized for autonomy of 1–5 days (Days of Autonomy, DoA).

### B. Grid-Interactive (On-Grid / Grid-Tied) Solar PV System
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024):** A *Grid-Interactive Solar PV System* is a synchronized power generation architecture that remains permanently connected to the utility grid. The grid acts as an infinite "virtual battery," absorbing excess generation and supplying deficits.

**Key Characteristics:**
- **No battery** is mandatory (Net-metered system).
- Uses a **Grid-Tie Inverter (GTI)** with anti-islanding protection.
- Exports surplus power → **Net Metering** concept.
- Cannot operate during grid outage (unless hybrid with battery).

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of the **Standalone system** as a *self-sufficient farmhouse with its own well and water tank* — you must size the tank to survive dry seasons. The **Grid-Interactive system** is like a *city apartment connected to municipal water* — you draw from the grid when needed and push surplus (from your rooftop rainwater harvesting) back into it. The **Net Meter** is the *calibrated flow meter that records both directions*.

## 1.2 Smart Meter vs Net Meter

| Feature | **Smart Meter** | **Net Meter** |
|---|---|---|
| **Function** | Bi-directional digital energy meter with communication capability | Bi-directional meter that records exported vs imported units separately |
| **Communication** | GSM / RF / PLC / IoT enabled | Mechanical reversal or two-register electronic |
| **Data** | Real-time, time-of-use (ToU), load profile | Cumulative export/import kWh |
| **KTU Emphasis** | Smart grid integration, AMI (Advanced Metering Infrastructure) | Billing settlement, net energy export |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** For grid-interactive systems ≤ **1 MW**, **Net Meters** are the regulatory standard under the **Kerala State Electricity Regulatory Commission (KSERC) Net Metering Regulations, 2020** (and subsequent amendments). The Net Meter and the existing consumer meter are typically integrated into a single bi-directional unit.

---

## 1.3 System Voltage & Capacity Limits (As per KSERC / CEA Norms)

- **LT (Low Tension) Consumer** — Net metering allowed up to **1 MWp** (after 2022 amendment).
- **HT (High Tension) Consumer** — Net metering allowed up to the sanctioned contract demand.
- **Voltage of Synchronization** — must match grid (e.g., **230 V / 415 V** for LT, **11 kV** for HT).

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic is Critical for KTU

- The subject **PEEOT752 – Electrical System Design** is application-oriented; Module 4 directly maps to **sustainable energy design** and **smart grid metering**.
- Examination questions typically ask: *system architecture, sizing of array/battery, single-line diagram (SLD), net-meter settlement calculations.*

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Standalone PV System — Architecture & Component Logic

The standard architecture follows this logical flow:

**PV Array → Charge Controller → Battery Bank → Inverter → AC Load**

### Step-by-Step Component Logic

1. **PV Array (DC Source)**
   - Series-parallel combination of modules to achieve desired **Vmpp (Maximum Power Point Voltage)** and **Impp**.
   - Module count in series: $N_s = \dfrac{V_{system,DC}}{V_{module,mpp}}$
   - Strings in parallel: $N_p = \dfrac{I_{array,required}}{I_{module,mpp}}$

2. **Charge Controller (MPPT / PWM)**
   - **MPPT (Maximum Power Point Tracking)** — efficiency **95–98 %**, extracts up to **30 %** more energy.
   - **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)** — efficiency **70–80 %**, cheaper.
   - Protects battery from overcharge (High Voltage Disconnect, HVD) and deep discharge (Low Voltage Disconnect, LVD).

3. **Battery Bank (Energy Storage)**
   - Rated in **Ah (Ampere-hour)** at a specified C-rate (e.g., C/10).
   - Types: **VRLA (Valve-Regulated Lead-Acid)**, **Tubular Lead-Acid**, **Li-ion (LFP/NMC)**.

4. **Inverter (DC → AC)**
   - Pure Sine-Wave preferred; THD < **3 %**.
   - Surge capacity ≥ **2× continuous rating** for motor loads.

5. **Protection Devices**
   - DC fuses, DC MCB/MCCB, surge protection device (SPD), Earthing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Telecom towers in remote Kerala districts (e.g., Wayanad, Idukki) deploy standalone PV systems with **3–5 days of autonomy** because grid extension is uneconomical.

---

## 2.2 Grid-Interactive PV System — Architecture & Component Logic

**PV Array → DC Combiner → String/Grid-Tie Inverter → ACDB → Bi-directional Net Meter → Grid (and Load)**

### Step-by-Step Component Logic

1. **PV Array** — sized for annual energy yield that matches (or partially matches) consumer demand.
2. **String Inverter / Central Inverter** — synchronizes with grid voltage and frequency (**50 Hz ± 0.5 Hz** in India), injects current at near-unity power factor.
3. **Anti-Islanding Protection** — inverter must trip within **≤ 2 seconds** of grid outage (per IEEE 1547 / IEC 62116).
4. **Net Meter / Bi-directional Meter** — installed at consumer premises; records import and export.
5. **AC Distribution Board (ACDB)** and **DC Distribution Board (DCDB)** with isolators.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Residential rooftops in Kerala (1–10 kWp) registered under the **KSEB (Kerala State Electricity Board) rooftop solar programme** are grid-interactive net-metered systems.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Daily Energy Demand** | $E_d = \sum (P_L \times t_L)$ | kWh/day | Sum of all load × hours |
| 2 | **PV Array Size (W)** | $P_{PV} = \dfrac{E_d}{\eta_{sys} \times PSH \times D_d}$ | Wp | $D_d$ = derating factor |
| 3 | **Peak Sun Hours (PSH)** | $PSH = \dfrac{\text{Daily Irradiation (kWh/m}^2\text{)}}{1 \text{ kW/m}^2}$ | h | Kerala avg ≈ **4.5–5.0 h** |
| 4 | **Battery Capacity (Ah)** | $C_{bat} = \dfrac{E_d \times DoA}{V_{bat} \times \eta_{bat} \times DoD}$ | Ah | $DoA$ = days of autonomy |
| 5 | **Depth of Discharge (DoD)** | $DoD = 1 - SoC_{min}$ | fraction | Lead-acid: 0.5, Li-ion: 0.8 |
| 6 | **Inverter Size** | $P_{inv} \geq 1.25 \times \sum P_{L,continuous}$ | VA | Safety factor 1.25 |
| 7 | **Charge Controller** | $I_{CC} = 1.25 \times I_{sc,array}$ | A | 25 % safety margin |
| 8 | **Net Energy Settlement** | $E_{net} = E_{export} - E_{import}$ | kWh | Per billing cycle |
| 9 | **Payback Period** | $PB = \dfrac{C_{install}}{A_{savings} - A_{O\&M}}$ | years | |
| 10 | **Series Modules** | $N_s = \dfrac{V_{DC,bus}}{V_{module,mpp}}$ | — | Round up |
| 11 | **Parallel Strings** | $N_p = \dfrac{I_{DC,bus}}{I_{module,mpp}}$ | — | Round up |
| 12 | **Annual Yield** | $E_{annual} = P_{PV} \times PSH \times 365 \times \eta_{sys}$ | kWh | |

**Standard Constants (KTU-favoured):**
- **Derating factor $D_d$ = 0.75–0.85** (accounts for temperature, dust, wiring, inverter efficiency).
- **Battery round-trip efficiency $\eta_{bat}$ ≈ 0.80** (Lead-acid), **0.95** (Li-ion).
- **Overall system efficiency $\eta_{sys}$ ≈ 0.75–0.80** for standalone.

---

## 2.4 Sizing Logic — The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why divide by PSH?** Because PV produces rated power only under **1 kW/m² STC irradiance**; real average energy is the integral of solar hours.
- **Why include DoA in battery sizing?** Because cloudy stretches must be sustained without PV generation.
- **Why 1.25× safety on inverter?** To handle **inductive surge currents** (motors, transformers) without tripping.
- **Why 1.25× on charge controller?** To avoid thermal failure at peak irradiance.
- **Why anti-islanding?** To prevent back-feeding a "dead" grid during maintenance — a **lethal hazard** to linemen.

---

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Standalone PV System Sizing (7-Mark Type)

> **Problem (KTU pattern):** A standalone PV system must supply a daily load of **3.2 kWh/day** at **48 V DC bus** with **3 days of autonomy**. Site PSH = **4.5 h**, $D_d$ = **0.80**, $DoD$ = **0.70**, $\eta_{bat}$ = **0.80**, $\eta_{sys}$ = **0.75**. Module specs: $V_{mpp}$ = **34 V**, $I_{mpp}$ = **8.5 A**, $V_{oc}$ = **41 V**, $I_{sc}$ = **9.2 A**. Design the array and battery.

### Step 1 — Daily Energy Demand
$$
E_d = 3.2 \text{ kWh/day} \quad \text{(given)}
$$

### Step 2 — Required PV Array Power (Watt-peak)
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{PV} &= \dfrac{E_d}{\eta_{sys} \times PSH \times D_d} \\
P_{PV} &= \dfrac{3.2}{0.75 \times 4.5 \times 0.80} \\
P_{PV} &= \dfrac{3.2}{2.7} \\
P_{PV} &= 1.185 \text{ kWp}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Number of Modules in Series (per string)
$$
\begin{aligned}
N_s &= \dfrac{V_{DC,bus}}{V_{module,mpp}} = \dfrac{48}{34} \\
N_s &= 1.411 \rightarrow \textbf{2 modules in series}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Total Number of Modules
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{module} &= V_{mpp} \times I_{mpp} = 34 \times 8.5 = 289 \text{ Wp} \\
N_{total} &= \dfrac{P_{PV}}{P_{module}} = \dfrac{1185}{289} = 4.10 \rightarrow \textbf{5 modules}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Number of Parallel Strings
$$
N_p = \dfrac{N_{total}}{N_s} = \dfrac{5}{2} = 2.5 \rightarrow \textbf{3 parallel strings (6 modules total)}
$$

### Step 6 — Battery Bank Capacity
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{bat} &= \dfrac{E_d \times DoA}{V_{bat} \times \eta_{bat} \times DoD} \\
C_{bat} &= \dfrac{3.2 \times 3}{48 \times 0.80 \times 0.70} \\
C_{bat} &= \dfrac{9.6}{26.88} \\
C_{bat} &= 357.14 \text{ Ah} \rightarrow \textbf{360 Ah}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7 — Charge Controller Sizing
$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{array,sc} &= 3 \times 9.2 = 27.6 \text{ A} \\
I_{CC} &= 1.25 \times 27.6 = \textbf{34.5 A}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 8 — Inverter Sizing
Assume continuous load power $P_L$ = **800 W** (with $\sum P_L \times t_L = 3.2$ kWh across 4 h use):
$$
P_{inv} = 1.25 \times 800 = \textbf{1000 VA}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Grid-Interactive Net Metering Settlement (7-Mark Type)

> **Problem:** A residential consumer in Kerala installs a **5 kWp** rooftop PV system. Annual generation = **6500 kWh**. Annual consumption = **7200 kWh**. Net-meter settlement to be computed.

### Step 1 — Net Energy
$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{net} &= E_{export} - E_{import} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Since **net-metered billing is annual** in Kerala:
- If **generation < consumption** → pays for deficit only: $(7200 - 6500) = 700 \text{ kWh billed}$.
- If **generation > consumption** → excess carried forward to next billing cycle (or settled at **APPC rate** as per KSERC norms).

### Step 2 — Bill Calculation (Generation < Consumption)
$$
\text{Energy Charge} = 700 \text{ kWh} \times \text{Rate} \quad (\text{slab-wise as per KSEB tariff})
$$

### Step 3 — Surplus Settlement
If generation = **7600 kWh** and consumption = **7200 kWh**:
$$
E_{surplus} = 400 \text{ kWh} \rightarrow \text{Carry forward OR settled at APPC rate}
$$

### Step 4 — Annual Savings
$$
A_{savings} = (7200 \times R_{avg}) - (700 \times R_{avg}) = 6500 \times R_{avg}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this is "Net" Metering:** The *net* (export − import) is what the consumer pays, not the gross generation or consumption.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flow

| **If PV Generation > Load** | **If PV Generation < Load** | **If Grid Outage** |
|---|---|---|
| Excess exports to grid → Net Meter records **EXPORT kWh** (counter A) | Deficit imported from grid → Net Meter records **IMPORT kWh** (counter B) | Inverter trips (anti-islanding) → Load disconnected (unless hybrid battery present) |
| Inverter operates at **MPPT**, PF ≈ 1.0 | Inverter operates at **MPPT**, grid supplies balance | Inverter self-checks grid parameters (V, f) before re-connection |

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Smart Meter Communication Architecture

A **Smart Meter** in AMI (Advanced Metering Infrastructure) uses:
- **HAN (Home Area Network)** — Zigbee / Wi-Fi / BLE.
- **NAN (Neighbourhood Area Network)** — RF Mesh / PLC.
- **WAN (Wide Area Network)** — GSM/GPRS / LTE / NB-IoT → to utility DCU (Data Concentrator Unit) → HES (Head-End System) → MDMS (Meter Data Management System).

This enables:
- **Time-of-Use (ToU)** billing.
- **Remote connect/disconnect**.
- **Outage detection**.
- **Theft detection** (tamper logs).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note:** The KSEB has begun deploying smart meters under the **RDSS (Revamped Distribution Sector Scheme)** — likely to be an exam topic.

---

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a Standalone Solar PV System. List its main components.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A Standalone (Off-Grid) Solar PV System is an autonomous electricity generation system that operates independently of the utility grid and uses battery storage to supply load during non-sunshine hours.
**Main Components (1 Mark each, any 3):**
- PV Array
- Charge Controller (MPPT/PWM)
- Battery Bank
- Inverter (DC → AC)
- AC/DC Distribution and Protection

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### Choice A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Question:** A household in Kerala has the following loads:
- 5 × LED bulbs (9 W each) — 5 h/day
- 2 × Fans (60 W each) — 8 h/day
- 1 × Refrigerator (150 W) — 10 h/day (duty cycle 0.4)
- 1 × TV (80 W) — 4 h/day

Design a **Standalone PV System** for this household. Assume:
- System voltage = **48 V DC**, PSH = **4.5 h**, $D_d$ = **0.80**, $\eta_{sys}$ = **0.75**, $DoA$ = **2 days**, $DoD$ = **0.70**, $\eta_{bat}$ = **0.80**.
- Module: 330 Wp, $V_{mpp}$ = **34 V**, $I_{mpp}$ = **9.7 A**.

**(a) Compute daily energy demand, PV array size, number of modules, and their series-parallel configuration. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Daily Energy Demand**
$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{LED} &= 5 \times 9 \times 5 = 225 \text{ Wh} \\
E_{Fan} &= 2 \times 60 \times 8 = 960 \text{ Wh} \\
E_{Fridge} &= 150 \times 10 \times 0.4 = 600 \text{ Wh} \\
E_{TV} &= 80 \times 4 = 320 \text{ Wh} \\
E_d &= 225 + 960 + 600 + 320 = \textbf{2105 Wh/day} = 2.105 \text{ kWh/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — PV Array Power**
$$
P_{PV} = \dfrac{2.105}{0.75 \times 4.5 \times 0.80} = \dfrac{2.105}{2.7} = \textbf{779.6 Wp}
$$

**Step 3 — Number of Modules**
$$
N_{total} = \dfrac{779.6}{330} = 2.36 \rightarrow \textbf{3 modules}
$$

**Step 4 — Series-Parallel Configuration**
$$
N_s = \dfrac{48}{34} = 1.41 \rightarrow \textbf{2 modules in series} \Rightarrow N_p = \dfrac{3}{2} = 1.5 \rightarrow \textbf{2 parallel strings (4 modules total, 1.32 kWp)}
$$

**Conclusion:** Use **2S × 2P = 4 modules** (1320 Wp), which is slightly oversized → acceptable design margin.

---

**(b) Design the battery bank and inverter. Draw the single-line diagram (SLD). (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Battery Capacity**
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{bat} &= \dfrac{E_d \times DoA}{V_{bat} \times \eta_{bat} \times DoD} \\
C_{bat} &= \dfrac{2.105 \times 2}{48 \times 0.80 \times 0.70} \\
C_{bat} &= \dfrac{4.21}{26.88} = 156.6 \text{ Ah} \rightarrow \textbf{160 Ah @ 48 V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Inverter Sizing**
$$
P_{continuous} = 5(9) + 2(60) + 150 + 80 = 45 + 120 + 150 + 80 = 395 \text{ W}
$$
$$
P_{inv} = 1.25 \times 395 = \textbf{500 VA} \quad (\text{use 1 kVA standard rating for surge headroom})
$$

**Step 3 — Single Line Diagram (SLD)**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[PV Array 2S x 2P 1320 Wp] --> B[DCDB DC MCB SPD]
    B --> C[MPPT Charge Controller 60 A]
    C --> D[Battery Bank 48 V 160 Ah]
    C --> E[Inverter 1 kVA Pure Sine]
    E --> F[ACDB MCB ELCB]
    F --> G[Loads LED Fan Fridge TV]
    D -.parallel.-> E
    style A fill:#FFD700
    style C fill:#FFA500
    style D fill:#90EE90
    style E fill:#87CEEB
```

---

### Choice B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Question:** Explain the **Grid-Interactive (On-Grid) Solar PV System** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the concept of **Net Metering** and compute the annual energy bill for a 4 kWp system with given parameters.

**(a) Describe the architecture, components, and the function of a Net Meter. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A Grid-Interactive Solar PV System is permanently synchronized to the utility grid through a **bi-directional interface** at the consumer premises. The system architecture comprises:

1. **PV Array** — converts solar energy into DC electricity. Sized to match (or partially match) the consumer's annual load.
2. **DC Combiner Box (DCDB)** — aggregates strings and houses DC isolators + SPD.
3. **String/Grid-Tie Inverter** — converts DC → AC, performs **MPPT**, synchronizes with grid V & f, ensures anti-islanding.
4. **ACDB (AC Distribution Board)** — supplies local loads and feeds the grid through the meter.
5. **Bi-directional Net Meter** — installed at the point of common coupling (PCC) between consumer and grid.

**Net Meter Function:**
The Net Meter has **two registers (Counter A and Counter B)**:
- **Counter A** → records **EXPORT** (kWh sent to grid).
- **Counter B** → records **IMPORT** (kWh drawn from grid).
- **Net Energy = Export − Import** is computed per billing cycle (annually for LT consumers in Kerala).

**Regulatory framework (KSERC 2020):**
- Up to **1 MWp** for LT, contract demand for HT.
- Settlement: net energy, surplus carry-forward.

---

**(b) A 4 kWp grid-tied PV system is installed in a residence at Thiruvananthapuram. Annual generation = **5200 kWh**; Annual consumption = **6000 kWh**. Electricity tariff = **₹7.50/kWh** (flat). Compute the annual bill and savings. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Net Energy**
$$
E_{net} = 6000 - 5200 = 800 \text{ kWh imported}
$$

**Step 2 — Annual Bill**
$$
\text{Bill} = 800 \times 7.50 = \textbf{₹6000}
$$

**Step 3 — Comparison Without PV (Baseline Bill)**
$$
\text{Baseline Bill} = 6000 \times 7.50 = \textbf{₹45,000}
$$

**Step 4 — Annual Savings**
$$
\text{Savings} = 45,000 - 6,000 = \textbf{₹39,000}
$$

**Step 5 — Payback (optional bonus)**
If installation cost = ₹1,80,000 and O&M = ₹2,000/yr:
$$
PB = \dfrac{1,80,000}{39,000 - 2,000} = \dfrac{1,80,000}{37,000} = 4.86 \text{ years}
$$

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (KTU Examiner Insights):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting DoA (Days of Autonomy) in battery sizing** → direct loss of 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing $\eta_{sys}$ with $\eta_{inverter}$** — $\eta_{sys}$ is a *combined* factor including wiring, temperature, dust, mismatch.
> 3. **Inverter size = continuous load**, NOT daily energy/24 h. Students often compute $E_d/24$ — this is *average*, not *peak*, and is wrong for inverter sizing.
> 4. **Net Meter = bi-directional, not two separate meters** — students sometimes claim "one meter for export, one for import."
> 5. **Anti-islanding is mandatory for grid-tied inverters** — failing to mention this loses 1 mark in grid-interactive design.
> 6. **Series vs Parallel calculation:** always round **up** $N_s$ and $N_p$ to the next integer (and verify array $V_{oc} < 1.2 \times V_{DC,system,max}$ for safety).
> 7. **DoD ≠ 1.0** for lead-acid batteries — using 100 % DoD will give a dangerously undersized battery (mark loss + real-world failure).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Standalone System** = PV + Battery + Inverter + Charge Controller → **autonomous**, used in remote/off-grid sites.
- **Grid-Interactive System** = PV + Grid-Tie Inverter + Net Meter → **synchronized to grid**, no battery (in pure net-metered topology).
- **Net Meter** = bi-directional meter with two registers (Export / Import); net energy is billed per **KSERC Net Metering Regulations**.
- **Smart Meter** = adds AMI capability — ToU, remote reading, outage detection — but is *not* a synonym for net meter.
- **Sizing Equations (must memorize):**
  - $P_{PV} = \dfrac{E_d}{\eta_{sys} \cdot PSH \cdot D_d}$
  - $C_{bat} = \dfrac{E_d \cdot DoA}{V_{bat} \cdot \eta_{bat} \cdot DoD}$
  - $P_{inv} = 1.25 \times \sum P_{L,cont}$
  - $I_{CC} = 1.25 \times I_{sc,array}$
- **Key Standards:** IEEE 1547, IEC 62116, IS 16221, KSERC 2020, MNRE.
- **Anti-islanding** is **mandatory** for grid-interactive inverters (safety for grid workers).
- **MPPT efficiency (95–98 %)** > PWM (70–80 %) — always preferred in modern designs.
- **Kerala PSH** ≈ 4.5–5.0 h/day (one of the highest in India).
- **Net-metering limit (KSERC):** up to 1 MWp for LT consumers.
- **Surplus energy settlement:** carry-forward or APPC rate (per KSERC norms).
- **DCDB / ACDB** terminology, **MCB/MCCB/SPD/ELCB** protection — common in SLD questions.

---

**End of Module 4 Topic Notes — Smart Meter / Net Meter**
